routes.overview: Southwest glacier and couloir approach to a narrow, exposed summit ridge in the remote Northern Pickets. Combines moderate snow climbing with Class 2-3 rock scrambling and exposed ridge traversing on solid granitic rock.
routes.beta: The standard approach to Phantom Peak uses the Southwest Glacier and South Couloir with a snow ramp at approximately 40-45 degrees. Climbers cross a bergschrund, navigate a steep snow finger, then move onto broken rock with Class 2-3 scrambling before traversing the sharp, exposed summit ridge. The summit consists of solid granitic rock with thrilling exposure on both sides. A hand line or belay may be useful for the narrowest sections.
routes.approach: From Hannegan Campground, follow the Whatcom Pass Trail 16 miles and 2,740 feet of elevation gain to Whatcom Pass (approximately 7-8 hours). From Whatcom Pass, traverse to Phantom Peak's southwest face. The route involves off-trail alpine travel across glaciers and loose terrain. Expect to descend to approximately 5,200-5,300 feet as you traverse west toward Phantom Peak's cirque.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent via the Southwest Ridge, South Couloir, and Southwest Glacier. Use snow flukes for protection on the 40-45 degree snow slopes, particularly above the bergschrund. On earlier season ascents when snow is more continuous, descent may be more straightforward down the southeast face. Loose rock hazard exists on descent across snowfields.
routes.descentText: The descent is accomplished by reversing the up-route along the summit ridge, broken rock slope, and snow finger. Teams should coordinate movement through exposed sections and consider simul-climbing with snow flukes for protection on steeper sections. Early in the season, the southeast face may offer a more direct descent on continuous snow.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through mid-September offers the most stable snow conditions. Early season (June-July) may have more continuous snow for descent. Late season (August-September) will have more exposed rock and less snow coverage, making the exposed ridge traverses more challenging but reducing avalanche hazard.
objHaz: Exposed ridge traversing with multi-thousand-foot exposure
knownHazards: Cascade Range steep terrain and spires
Crevassed glaciers in Luna Cirque approach region
Narrow ridges with multi-thousand-foot drops
watchOut: Summit ridge extremely narrow and exposed
Fog and low visibility common in region
Weather deteriorates rapidly in the Pickets
Late-season ascents have more exposed rock sections
Rockfall hazard from fractured rock above route
routes.detailedRack: Minimal gear typically used: selection of nuts (black diamond size range), several slings. Ice climbing gear unnecessary. Approach boots with good traction essential.
routes.proNeeds: Snow flukes for bergschrund area and steep slopes. Small emergency kit given remote location and lack of cell service.
proTips: Summit register dates to 1958; sign in for peak history
Ascents beyond first are relatively rare; expect minimal trail
Distinctive white dikes create dramatic visual appeal from distance
Use pickets for snow protection rather than ice tools
Phantom Peak Fence indicates variant route

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.fees: $35 vehicle pass
access.permit: Required year-round for overnight backcountry camping
access.passRequired: North Cascades National Park pass
access.closures: Avalanche paths may close access in winter. Whatcom Pass Trail open seasonally.

climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North Cascades and Passes (NOAA)
climate.typical: High alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Afternoon thunderstorms common. Winds frequently exceed 30 mph.
climate.spring: Heavy snow cover, avalanche risk. Occasional fair days by May.
climate.summer: Most stable weather with afternoon storms. Snow gradually recedes. Freezing levels near 13,000 feet by mid-July.
climate.fall: September offers best window with fewer storms, clearing skies, mild temperatures.
climate.winter: Heavy snow, sub-zero temperatures, extreme avalanche hazard.

emergency.nearestHospital: Haggen Family Emergency Center, PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Visitor Center or Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount
emergency.notes: No cell service in Pickets. Satellite communicator essential. Helicopter evacuation may take 2-4+ hours.

routes.comms: No cellular reception throughout Picket Range. Satellite communicator (SPOT or Garmin inReach) essential. Line of sight to sky required.

road.name: Highway 542 to Hannegan Campground Road
road.status: Seasonal - typically open May through October
road.seasonalGate: Hannegan Pass Road may close near Hannegan Campground in winter
road.driveNote: Approximately 2-3 hours from Bellingham. Highway 542 east to terminus. Narrow road with limited turnouts.

routes.corrections: ROUTE CLASSIFICATION DISCREPANCY: Given data lists 'Grade III, Class 4' but sources document Class 2-3 with 40-45 degree snow slopes. Alpine Grade III / PD appears correct for first-ascent Southwest Route. ELEVATION: Sources cite 8,015-8,207 feet; given 8,016 feet is within documented range.

hierarchyNote: Peak verified as genuine: Sinister Peak (8,440+ ft) is located in Glacier Peak Wilderness at coordinates 48.2998°N, 121.0098°W, straddling Skagit/Chelan counties in the North Cascades. Almost 1 mile east of Dome Peak. Elevation matches input data (8444 ft ~ 8440+ ft); hierarchy placement in North Cascades/Glacier Peak Wilderness region is correct.
blurb: Sinister Peak (8,440 ft) is a sharp, granodiorite alpine summit in the remote Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, notable as the first ascent by Lloyd Anderson, Clint Kelley, and Jim Crooks on May 29, 1939. An ice-clad peak bordered by the Chickamin Glacier on the north and Garden Glacier on the southeast, it is accessed via the Glacier Peak High Route or as a standalone objective from the Downey Creek approach, requiring glacier travel and mixed rock/snow climbing across multiple route options.

routes.overview: Alpine climb via southwest snowfield and gully to the west ridge, combining moderate snow travel, loose rock scrambling, and exposed ridge climbing to the summit. A classic Cascades alpine objective accessing the Dome-Sinister col via glacier terrain.
routes.beta: Approach via Downey Creek Trail to high camp at Cub Lake or Itswoot Ridge (5,400-6,400 ft). Traverse Chickamin Glacier to Dome-Sinister col at approximately 8,450 feet. Contour around Sinister's south side via easy snowfield, then climb a prominent loose gully (Class 3, loose rock) to the ridge. Ascend the upper west ridge via Class 2-3 snow and scrambling to the roomy summit. Total time from camp approximately 3-4 hours. The southwest gully is notably loose—one well-documented party described it as 'one of the loosest, most unpleasant gullies we've ever had the displeasure of climbing.' Descent can reverse via the gully or traverse to the lower west ridge (white-knuckle Class 3-4 scrambling with fixed sling protection at one crux).
routes.approach: From Darrington, drive north 8 miles to Suiattle River Road 26, then east 20 miles to Downey Creek trailhead parking (fee required: $5 day-use or Northwest Forest Pass). Follow Downey Creek Trail 768 6.5 miles on maintained trail to Bachelor Creek, then ascend steeply through brush and patchy snow. Continue to Bachelor Creek pass (6,000 ft, ~12.5 miles from trailhead), then descend to Cub Lake (5,338 ft, ~14 miles). Camp at Cub Lake Meadows or ascend 1,600 ft to Itswoot Ridge (6,400-6,900 ft) for higher camp. From high camp, traverse east along snow and talus to Dome Glacier (7,600 ft), then ascend to the Dome-Sinister col at approximately 8,450 feet via the Chickamin Glacier (heavily crevassed). Total approach: 15-16 miles with 5,500+ feet elevation gain over 2-3 days.
routes.descentText: From the summit, most parties descend the upper west ridge via Class 2-3 scrambling and snow, then reverse through the upper gully system back to the Dome-Sinister col. Some parties opt for the lower west ridge, which features exposed white-knuckle Class 3-4 scrambling on semi-solid granodiorite rock with considerable exposure; a fixed sling is present at one difficult spot. Return to camp via Chickamin Glacier traverse, watching for crevasses and bergschrund changes. Total descent time: 2-3 hours depending on route choice and snow/ice conditions.
routes.descent: Via upper west ridge and southwest gully to Dome-Sinister col, or exposed lower west ridge (Class 3-4 scramble with fixed anchor), then Chickamin Glacier descent.
routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the most stable snow and ice conditions. Early season (late June) preferred for bergschrund conditions before it opens fully. September can be climbed but bergschrund hazard increases. Avoid early June (excessive snowpack) and late September (deteriorating conditions).
routes.proNeeds: Alpine rock protection: single set of nuts and hexes (0.5-2 inches), small rack (6-8 pieces), and 50-meter rope for belay anchors on exposed ridge sections. Crampons required for glacier and snow sections.
routes.detailedRack: Crampons, ice tools/axes (2), ice screws (4-6, 12-16 cm), snow flukes/pickets (2-3), rock nuts (half set from 0.5-2 inches), hexes (2-3), carabiners (8-10, mix of lockers and non-lockers), 50m single or 60m double rope, webbing and slings, harness, belay devices.
watchOut: The southwest gully is notably loose with significant rockfall hazard—move quickly and watch for falling rock from above.
Bergschrund beneath the Dome-Sinister col can be impassable in late season; early season ascents preferred.
Heavily crevassed Chickamin Glacier requires rope team and careful route-finding; bergschrund just below the col is often the crux.
Exposed Class 3-4 scrambling on lower west ridge with considerable drop—fixed sling present at one spot, may be inadequate.
Icy conditions on upper west ridge, especially in morning hours; late-start summits may encounter ice.
knownHazards: Loose, unstable gully rock (primary objective hazard)
Crevasse field and bergschrund on Chickamin Glacier
Rockfall from steep loose gully
Exposure on ridge scrambling (not protected)
Avalanche potential on steep snow sections (50-degree north face; less common on SW approach but possible in heavy snowpack)
Variable bergschrund conditions making glacier crossing problematic in late season
proTips: Climb the southwest gully early in the day to minimize rockfall hazard and loose rock destabilization.
Early season (late June through July) offers most reliable snow bridges for bergschrund crossing.
Some parties opt to skirt the worst of the gully by gaining the ridge via Class 4 south face scrambling, though this is more exposed.
The upper west ridge near the summit can ice up overnight; start descent early if attempting a one-day push from intermediate camps.
A fixed sling at the lower west ridge crux suggests others have found that spot particularly exposed; verify its condition and test all anchors carefully.
The roomy summit allows for large parties and prolonged time.
routes.itinerary:
Day 1: Drive to Downey Creek trailhead (1,440 ft). Hike 6.5 miles on Downey Creek Trail 768 to Bachelor Creek area, establishing camp at lower elevation (2,500-3,000 ft). 7-8 hours of hiking.
Day 2: Continue upward past Bachelor Creek pass (6,000 ft) to Cub Lake (5,338 ft) or push to Itswoot Ridge (6,400-6,900 ft) for high camp. 6-8 hours depending on route choice and snow conditions.
Day 3 (Summit Day): From high camp, traverse east to Dome Glacier, ascend to Dome-Sinister col via Chickamin Glacier (5-6 hours). Locate Southwest Gully, contour around lower south side via snowfield, climb gully to ridge, ascend upper west ridge to summit (3-4 hours). Descent back to high camp (2-3 hours). 10-13 hours total from camp.
Day 4: Return to trailhead via Cub Lake and Downey Creek Trail. 7-8 hours downhill.
routes.turnaround: If bergschrund on Chickamin Glacier appears impassable by early afternoon on summit day, turn around and descend to camp to reassess. If upper ridge becomes dangerously icy mid-morning, abandon summit bid and return to camp to wait for night freeze. Weather deterioration (whiteout conditions, heavy rain, high winds) should trigger immediate descent.
routes.comms: No cell service in Glacier Peak Wilderness or along approach. Satellite communication device (InReach, Spot) recommended for emergency contact. The remote nature of this peak means self-rescue is essential; groups should be proficient in crevasse rescue and rock scramble safety.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00-7:00 AM from high camp to maximize daylight on exposed ridge sections and minimize afternoon weather risk.

road.name: Suiattle River Road 26 (FR 26)
road.status: Partially washout-prone; confirm accessibility with Darrington Ranger District before trip. As of December 2025, multiple washouts reported.
road.seasonalGate: Generally open late May through November, but verify with ranger station. Winter snow may close access.
road.driveNote: From Darrington, drive north 8 miles, then east on FR 26 for 20 miles to Downey Creek Trailhead. Road is gravel and can become impassable with heavy rain or winter weather. Contact Darrington Ranger District at (360) 436-1155 to confirm access.

climate.forecastZone: Cascade West South (NWAC avalanche forecast zone)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; heavy orographic precipitation on west slopes, drier on east. Highly variable daily conditions.
climate.spring: Wet, snow-heavy (persistent snowpack), rapidly changing weather, avalanche risk.
climate.summer: Clearest window July-August with occasional thunderstorms; September becomes wetter. Early morning clear, afternoon weather window.
climate.fall: Increasing precipitation, earlier storms, snow line drops rapidly. Snow possible above 6,000 ft by late September.
climate.winter: Inaccessible; heavy snowpack, avalanche danger, extreme cold.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District) and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. Glacier Peak Wilderness boundary enforces party size limit of 12 persons.
access.fees: $5 day-use fee per vehicle or Northwest Forest Pass ($30 annual). Washington State Discover Pass NOT accepted.
access.permit: Free self-issuing wilderness permit required (available at Downey Creek trailhead). No quota system for Glacier Peak Wilderness.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee. Recreation pass required for parking.
access.closures: As of December 2025, Suiattle River Road has multiple washouts causing temporary closure. Confirm current road and trail status with Darrington Ranger District before planning trip. Road closures due to winter weather possible November-April.

emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District, 1405 Emens Avenue North, Darrington, WA 98241. Phone: (360) 436-1155. Hours: Monday-Friday 8:00 AM - 4:00 PM (closed 12:00-12:30 PM for lunch). Closed weekends.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (approximately 60 miles from Darrington). Emergency Department available. Address: 1415 E. Kincaid St, Mount Vernon, WA 98274. Phone: (360) 424-4111.
emergency.notes: Extremely remote peak; helicopter evacuation unlikely due to steep terrain and limited landing zones. Self-rescue capability essential. Satellite communication device strongly recommended. Evacuation would require multi-day packout or helicopter rescue in life-threatening emergency. Contact ranger station or county sheriff immediately if emergency occurs.

pitchDetail.notes: Loose, poorly consolidated granodiorite rock. Described as 'one of the loosest, most unpleasant gullies.' Prone to rockfall. Width and steepness variable by season. Fast party movement essential.
Moderate snow climbing ending in easy scramble to roomy summit. May ice up overnight; better in afternoon after sun exposure. Fixed anchor present at lower ridge crux spot.

blurb: East Twin Needle is one of a pair of rugged spires in the Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, composed of metamorphic gneiss characterized by parallel crack systems and steep terrain. The peaks were first climbed on August 17, 1932, by William Degenhardt, James Martin, and Herb Strandberg via a diagonal chimney system. These remote 7,936+ foot peaks offer committing alpine rock routes with significant objective hazards including rockfall and volatile weather.

routes.overview: Classic 1932 first-ascent route ascending a long diagonal chimney system through steep gneiss terrain on the south-facing aspect of East Twin Needle with multiple chockstone obstacles and route-finding challenges.
routes.beta: The South Route follows a long diagonal chimney that runs up the south face. The face is broken with chimneys running in multiple directions, all extremely steep and full of chockstones. After entering from the glacier, climbers ascend the continuous chimney, trusting route-finding skills as the summit approach cannot be planned from below. Expect occasional steep grass slopes, exposure, and sustained class 4-5 climbing.
routes.rappels: Multiple rappels on descent (two 60-meter ropes recommended)
routes.approach: From Lower Goodell Creek trailhead, hike approximately 6-7 miles with 6,000 feet of elevation gain to reach Terror Basin where East Twin Needle is located. The approach follows Goodell Creek drainage through steep terrain to the basin. Multi-day backpacking expedition required; typical approach involves camping near Terror Basin or at a col near Chopping Block after first day of hiking. Peak is accessed from the Mustard Glacier on the north side of the formation.
routes.descent: From the summit, downclimb and rappel approximately 1,200 feet down the south face through complex terrain to Eye Col (notch between East and West Twin Needles). Use two 60-meter ropes to execute rappels and bypass vertical slabs. From Eye Col, descend via fourth-class down-scrambling through steep south-facing gullies with additional rappelling and snow travel if present to regain Crescent Creek Basin.
routes.descentText: Descent from East Twin Needle summit begins with downclimbing the class 2-3 terrain near the summit, then rappelling twice using two full-length 60-meter ropes to bypass large vertical slabs and bypass most of the complex gully system. From Eye Col, navigate steep south-facing gullies via fourth-class down-scrambling with additional rappelling sections and potential snow travel depending on season. The final rappel delivers climbers back to Crescent Creek Basin. Total descent time is approximately 2-3 hours depending on snow conditions and climbing speed.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August typically offers the most consistent weather windows in the Picket Range, though conditions remain highly variable. June is possible with lingering snowpack making route-finding relatively efficient. Late summer (July-August) sees some exceptionally warm periods that can last about a week. September starts reliably, though increasing variability near month's end can bring rain. Avoid the peak of August glacier melt if crevasse crossings are a concern.
routes.turnaround: At least 2-3 hours of daylight remaining for descent; turn around by early afternoon given the complex descent and potential for afternoon weather deterioration in the Pickets.
knownHazards: Rockfall from unstable terrain and ice-coated rock
Chockstone hazards in chimney systems
Steep exposure on south face
Volatile weather patterns typical of North Cascades
Objective hazards from rockfall on adjacent peaks
Large crevasses in glacier travel zones during late summer
Lichen and moisture-slicked rock surfaces
watchOut: Route-finding is challenging due to multiple chimney systems; careful observation needed to identify correct line
South face approach is more technical and poorly documented than other routes; consider North Buttress alternative
The peak sheds rock regularly due to geological instability
Weather can deteriorate rapidly - have contingency bail-out plan
Crockstone obstacles can shift and jam protection
routes.comms: The Picket Range is a remote wilderness area with no reliable cell phone coverage. Satellite messaging devices (garmin inReach, Zoleo, or smartphones with satellite features) are essential for emergency communication. Reception for satellite devices may be limited by steep terrain and narrow gullies.
routes.detailedRack: Recommended rack includes a single set of BD Camalots from .3 to #2 (gneiss climbing favors cams due to parallel crack systems), a selection of passive protection (wedges/nuts), approximately 10-15 quickdraws, slings, cordelette, and two full-length 60-meter dynamic ropes. Given the gneiss rock type and mixed terrain, emphasis should be placed on camming devices that can handle variable crack widths.
routes.proNeeds: The South Route requires solid rock climbing skills at 5.7 grade with competence in multi-pitch trad climbing. Leaders should be experienced at route-finding on complex alpine faces and comfortable with modest class 4 scrambling. Ability to place protection quickly and accurately in shifting rock and gneiss terrain is essential. This is an alpine expedition-style climb requiring self-rescue competence and remote wilderness mountaineering experience.
proTips: The 1932 first ascent route is feasible but requires careful attention to correct chimney selection
Early starts are critical given the 6000+ feet of approach elevation gain
The North Buttress (Thread of Gneiss) may offer a better-protected alternative route with established modern first-ascent documentation
Bring extra protection as gneiss can be unreliable for passive gear; cams dominate the protection strategy
Consider the South Route as a descent option from a summit reached via North Buttress, rather than as a primary ascent line

timing.recommendedStart: 3:00 AM - 4:00 AM from base camp to ensure daylight for complex descent

climate.typical: Highly variable Pacific Northwest alpine weather with frequent rapid transitions between clear skies and severe storms
climate.summer: July-August typically offer longest consistent weather windows with occasional very warm periods (high 80s to low 100s in lowlands). Late July to early August may see significant crevasse opening in glaciers.
climate.fall: September generally reliable with warm, sunny rain-free days early in the month; increasing variability and rain chances toward month's end
climate.winter: Generally not climbed; heavy snow and avalanche danger

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for North Cascades National Park
access.fees: North Cascades National Park entrance fee applies

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Ranger Station (located near park visitor centers)
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (approximately 60-80 miles from trailhead); United General Hospital in Sedro-Woolley, WA also available
emergency.notes: This is a very remote wilderness location. Any rescue operation will be complex and time-consuming. Satellite communication devices are essential. Evacuation via helicopter is possible in good weather but may take many hours to coordinate.

blurb: Liberty Bell Mountain is a 7,720-foot granite spire in Washington's North Cascades, featuring world-class alpine climbing with routes ranging from 5.6 to 5.13b. First ascended September 27, 1946, by Fred Beckey, Jerry O'Neil, and Charles Welsh, the peak offers 18+ established routes on excellent rock.

North Peak (8,160 ft) is one of four granite summits in the remote Gunsight Range of the North Cascades, located above the Chickamin Glacier east of Dome Peak. The peak features some of the Cascades' cleanest white granite and hosts one of the region's premier hard free climbs, the West Face, which offers exceptional crack and face climbing in an extraordinarily remote alpine setting requiring a strenuous 2-day glacier approach.

routes.fa: 1986 - Jim Nelson and Carl Dietrich (5.10 A1); Free first ascent 2007 - Jens Holsten and Max Hasson (5.11c)
routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the most stable and driest weather conditions in the North Cascades, with temperatures averaging 63-70°F during the day and 41-45°F at night. June sees slightly more precipitation (2.2 inches) while July-August drops to 1.7 inches. Occasional afternoon thunderstorms and rapid weather changes are always a possibility at this remote alpine location. Early season (June) can have more heavily crevassed glaciers, while late summer (August-September) offers better snow bridge conditions.

climate.typical: Alpine rock and ice climbing in remote North Cascades; high exposure to rapidly changing weather; frequent afternoon showers possible
climate.spring: Heavy snow coverage at high elevations; glaciers heavily crevassed; poor rock conditions
climate.summer: Best climbing season; July-August ideal with most stable weather; occasional afternoon storms; daytime temps 63-70°F; precipitation 1.7-2.2 inches; blue skies interspersed with showers
climate.fall: Increasingly unstable weather; rising avalanche danger; early season storms
climate.winter: Heavy snow accumulation; alpine rock not generally climbed; approach not feasible
climate.forecastZone: North Cascades/Mount Baker area

routes.overview: The West Face of North Peak is one of the Cascades' sheerest and cleanest granite faces, offering world-class alpine rock climbing. The route ascends the center of the face with excellent crack and face climbing on solid white granite. Originally established as an aid route in 1986, the first free ascent in 2007 demonstrated the exceptional quality of the line, with sustained 5.11 climbing featuring hard crack work, bulbous knobs, lieback sections, and wild exposure.
routes.beta: A premier alpine testpiece featuring five pitches of high-quality granite climbing. The route climbs splitter cracks and exposed face climbing over 600 vertical feet. Pitch 2 contains the crux with thin cracks, bulbous knobs, and a small roof. The climbing features varied terrain including incipient flakes, stemming, and lieback sections. Protection is generally reliable on white granite. The onsight first free ascent in 2007 was completed with no falls. Descent via four double-rope rappels from the summit.
routes.approach: Start at the Downey Creek trailhead (1,400 ft) on the Suiattle River Road. Follow Downey Creek trail for approximately 6.6 miles to Bachelor Creek/Bachelor Flats, reaching the first bivy site (Camp 1) after 9.7 hours of hiking with full alpine pack. From Bachelor Flats, continue cross-country off-trail gaining approximately 3,500 feet over approximately 11.6 hours to reach the Chikamin-Dome Col at 8,500 feet (Camp 2/high camp). From the col, access the base of the West Face by crossing the Chickamin Glacier. Route-finding requires careful attention to crevasse hazards and may require glacier travel equipment. NOTE: As of April 2026, the Suiattle River Road had multiple washouts from December 2025 storms, potentially affecting trailhead access.
routes.descent: From the summit, descend via four double-rope rappels (approximately 30m each) directly from the peak, utilizing existing anchors and natural features on the granite. This brings climbers back to the base of the route. From base camp, descend the approach route in reverse. Some ascents have descended via traversal south to Middle Gunsight and South Gunsight, adding complexity and additional days to the descent.
routes.descentText: Rappel down the center of the West Face via four consecutive double-rope rappels of approximately 30 meters each, using natural anchor features and existing bolt/gear anchors established on the granite. Ensure proper rope management to avoid tangling on the steep granite face. Once back at the base of the route (approximately 9,000 feet), descend to the Chikamin-Dome Col via the Chickamin Glacier approach, watching carefully for crevasses and potential moat crossings. From the col, descend the cross-country route back to Bachelor Flats (taking approximately 8-10 hours depending on snow coverage and conditions). From Bachelor Flats, follow the Downey Creek trail back to the trailhead (6-8 hours depending on fitness and pack weight). Total descent time from summit to vehicle is typically 3-4 days.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00 AM from high camp (Chikamin-Dome Col)

gear: Climbing rack for granite crack climbing (cams #0.5-#3, wires #1-#8, stoppers)
Insulating layers, alpine rock shoes, approach shoes
Helmet required for exposed alpine terrain
routes.detailedRack: For the West Face on North Peak, bring a complete rack of cams from #0.5 to #3 for the thin crack climbing on pitches 1-2 and the crux, supplemented with wires #1-#8 for protection on flakes and knobs. Small stoppers are useful for incipient crack sections. Quickdraws for both natural protection and any bolted anchors. The rock is solid white granite with reliable placements. Bring a 60m single dynamic rope and basic alpine rock climbing gear. Thin hands to finger cracks are essential for pitches with incipient placements.
routes.proNeeds: This is a serious alpine rock route requiring expert-level crack climbing protection skills. Protection is generally available but requires solid understanding of gear placement in granite. The crux pitches (especially pitch 2) demand confident lead climbing and protection placement at 5.11 difficulty. Pitches with thin cracks and bulbous knobs require precision protection. The onsight nature of the first free ascent speaks to the quality of the granite and availability of natural protection.
proTips: Start early from the high camp to maximize daylight and avoid afternoon thunderstorms
Pitch 2 contains the technical and psychological crux; focus and confidence critical
The approach requires serious glacier travel skills; crevasse rescue knowledge mandatory
Carry minimal water on the climb itself as it adds weight during sustained steep climbing
Plan for a 2-3 day approach and 3-4 day descent; rushing this objective has led to accidents
July offers the most stable weather window; plan accordingly
The onsight FFA demonstrates clean rock and reliable protection; trust the granite
watchOut: Rapidly changing alpine weather with afternoon thunderstorms common; storms can appear within minutes
Significant crevasse hazard on Chickamin Glacier approach; use proper glacier travel techniques and equipment
Remoteness means rescue is extremely difficult and time-delayed; every team member must be self-sufficient
Route Finding: cross-country and glacier navigation required; detailed map and compass/GPS essential
The approach involves heavy load carrying (rock shoes, crampons, ice axe, glacier gear, bivouac kit) over steep terrain; fitness critical
Objective hazard: loose rock on descent from summit; descenders and those on rappel must remain aware
The 2-day approach means weather exposure builds throughout the climb; turnaround decisions must be made early
knownHazards: Exposed granite climbing on remote face with significant consequences for falls
Glacier travel on Chickamin Glacier with crevasse rescue required if roped team breaks through
Severe altitude and remote location (50+ miles from nearest road) means helicopter rescue unlikely
Rapid onset afternoon thunderstorms common in July; exposure to lightning hazard above treeline
Bivouacking in sub-zero temperatures at 8,500 feet with minimal shelter options
Approach trail washouts have occurred (2025-2026); access may be seasonally restricted
routes.bail: From the base of the route or mid-route, descend back to the Chikamin-Dome Col and reverse the approach route via Bachelor Flats to the Downey Creek trailhead. If weather deteriorates mid-climb, retreat down the same route via rappel anchors (or natural features if no anchors present). Once back at high camp, wait for safe weather window to descend approach. In emergency situations, signal with mirror/whistle; however, response time may exceed 2+ days due to remoteness. Self-rescue and route-finding in whiteout conditions should be expected as primary contingency.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time of 1:00 PM from the start of climbing from high camp (typically 6:00 AM start suggests 1:00 PM go/no-go decision). If crux pitches (pitch 1-2) are not completed and protected by 10:00-11:00 AM, consider descending to avoid rappelling in darkness. In any conditions worse than broken clouds with visibility over 1 mile, abort the climb. Do not delay descent to tag the summit if weather is deteriorating.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
access.permit: Wilderness permit required for overnight camping in Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.closures: Suiattle River Road experienced multiple washouts in December 2025; confirm current trailhead access status with Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest before traveling (as of April 2026, road was closed)

road.name: Suiattle River Road
road.status: Closed as of April 2026 due to washouts from December 2025 storms; verify current status with Forest Service before trip
road.seasonalGate: Road typically open June-October depending on weather
road.driveNote: Drive to Downey Creek trailhead (1,400 ft) on Suiattle River Road near Darrington, WA. Road is narrow, unpaved in sections, and can be rough. High-clearance vehicles recommended. Allow 2+ hours driving time from Seattle. Confirm road status before committing to approach.

emergency.rangerStation: Mount Baker Ranger District (Glacier Peak Wilderness office), approximately 50 miles from peak (very remote)
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Sedro-Woolley) or Cascade Medical Center (Cascade), 60+ miles away
emergency.notes: Extreme remoteness means rescue response can take 2+ days. Helicopter rescue unlikely due to terrain and weather. Party must be capable of self-rescue and descent with injuries. Satellite messenger (InReach/Garmin) recommended for emergency communication. Cell phone coverage: none in the Gunsight Range area.

whatToBring: Waterproof jacket and pants (afternoon thunderstorm risk)
Lightweight sleeping bag rated to 0-10°F for high camp
Water bottles/hydration system (refill at streams on approach)
First aid kit with blister treatment
Map and compass or GPS device
Helmet for both climbing and approach
Rope and rappel devices (personal backup descenders)

pitchDetail.notes: Thin cracks and flakes in a shallow corner with delicate climbing. Requires small gear placements. Belay with good anchor at horn.

Technical crux featuring thin cracks, bulbous knobs, and small roof with overlap. Exceptional granite with solid protection available but demanding lead climbing. Aesthetically striking climbing with hard crack work.
Committing moves on tricky gear transitioning to wild climbing up flakes. Challenging protection requiring careful placement.
Continuation along flake and crack systems with knobs. Granular rock but solid climbing with good natural protection.
Stemming over steep terrain, then corner with final mantle move onto terminal terrace. Includes chimney system (5.6) leading to summit. Traversal required to reach actual summit register.

routes.corrections: Original data showed pitches=0; research indicates 5 pitches (Alpinist 2007 article). Original FA attribution correct (Jim Nelson and Carl Dietrich 1986) but missing context: 1986 ascent was 5.10 A1 grade; free first ascent 2007 by Jens Holsten and Max Hasson at 5.11c. Peak elevation was null; confirmed 8,160 feet from multiple sources. Mountain Project lists 5 pitches; StephAbegg detailed trip report may describe additional sub-pitches but Alpinist magazine (authoritative source) and Mountain Project confirm 5 main pitches.

hierarchyNote: Peak coordinates (47.36741, -121.45564) and elevation (4,585 ft) confirmed from Mountain Project. Located in The Citidel alpine area, which is documented at https://www.mountainproject.com/area/120477448/the-citidel. Located on NW side of Silver Basin's east wall, north of Silver Peak in Snoqualmie Pass area. Hierarchy placement and geography verified as plausible.
blurb: Sofa King Buttress is an emerging alpine sport climbing area on the northwest side of Silver Basin's east wall, north of Silver Peak in the Snoqualmie Pass region of Washington's Central Cascades. The area features volcanic metamorphic rock similar to World Wall, with both established sport and developing traditional climbing. Recently developed by first-ascensionist Wylie Moreshead starting in 2020.

routes.overview: Alpine sport climb at Sofa King Buttress in The Citidel area.
routes.approach: From Snoqualmie Pass Exit 54, take Hyak Drive to FS Road NF-9070. Continue to Ollallie Meadow where the PCT crosses (approximately 5.5 miles from I-90). Take the left fork of NF-9070 after the PCT intersection and follow it to the large turnaround below Silver Basin. Approximately 20-30 minutes approach from the road end to the base of the climbing area. High-clearance vehicle recommended due to washouts and potholes.
routes.bestSeason: June through October when FS Road 9070 is open and snow is minimal

routes.fa: Leland Winham, James Mallory, and David Wolfe
routes.beta: One pitch route starting on a ledge about 20 feet up the wall near a large black block. Located on the right side of the East Face area. Sustained climbing with jugs and small features.
routes.overview: 5.10c sport route on the right side of Keechelus Ridge's East Face, featuring sustained jug climbing and a spectacular position on the climbing area.
routes.approach: From the main access point at Keechelus Ridge, hike to the East Face crag. The route is located on the right side of the main wall on a ledge approximately 20 feet up, identifiable by a large black block nearby.
routes.descent: Lower from anchors or rappel down to the belay ledge and hiking descent.
routes.proNeeds: Sport climbing with bolted protection.

pitchDetail.notes: Bolted route with sustained jug hauling and spectacular position

routes.beta: Single pitch sport climb featuring increasingly overhanging jugs leading to a crux mantle move. After a no-hands rest ledge, a second mini crux around 5.10c difficulty, followed by easier slab climbing to anchor on a large ledge. Route passes through some orange-streaked rock.
routes.overview: 5.11b sport route on Keechelus Ridge's East Face near Snoqualmie Pass, featuring powerful jug climbing, a crux mantle, and spectacular position.
routes.approach: From the East Face area at Keechelus Ridge, locate the route by walking downhill from Black Systems Solar route.
routes.descent: Lower from anchors.
routes.detailedRack: 10 bolts with chain anchor
routes.proNeeds: Sport climbing route with full bolt protection.

pitchDetail.notes: 30 feet of increasingly overhanging jugs to crux mantle. No-hands rest ledge. Second mini crux (5.10c). Easier slab climbing to anchor on big ledge.

routes.beta: Two-pitch sport climb on a lower west-facing wall section of Keechelus Ridge. Can be climbed as one continuous pitch or split into two pitches using mid-route anchors. Described as one of the best 5.9s in Washington with commanding views from the belay stations. Belay ledge provides excellent sun protection during morning and midday hours.
routes.overview: 5.9 sport route on Keechelus Ridge, featuring accessible climbing with excellent views and described as one of the best 5.9s in Washington.
routes.approach: From the main Keechelus Ridge access, walk down from west wall past Lizard Ledge area and follow the wall as it bends to locate the first west-facing route on the lower section.
routes.descent: Lower from anchors. Note: a 70 meter rope will not be sufficient to lower from the upper anchors.
routes.detailedRack: 17 bolts with 2 chain anchors. Alpine draws recommended.
routes.proNeeds: Sport climbing with full bolt protection. Mid-route anchors allow for two-pitch option.

pitchDetail.notes: First pitch on lower west-facing wall section
Second pitch with mid-route anchor to upper anchors

routes.routeName: With Love Lie Ancients Nestled Within
routes.beta: One of the most popular routes at Keechelus and possibly the best 5.10 here. Features easy initial bolts followed by sustained jug hauling with a spectacular position.
routes.overview: 5.10b/c sport route on Keechelus Ridge's East Face, featuring sustained jug climbing and described as possibly the best 5.10 at the crag with a spectacular climbing position.
routes.approach: From the main Keechelus Ridge East Face area, locate the rest area with trees about 30 feet back from the wall. The climb starts directly above these trees. Alternatively, identify the tiers of ledges and look for the first route left of two climbs that start 20 feet up on a ledge near a large black block.
routes.descent: Lower from anchors.
routes.detailedRack: 13 bolts, chain anchor
routes.proNeeds: Sport climbing route with full bolt protection.

pitchDetail.notes: Easy initial bolts followed by sustained jug hauling with spectacular position

blurb: Keechelus Ridge's East Face is a single-pitch sport climbing crag near Snoqualmie Pass in Washington's Central-West Cascades, featuring blocky andesite formations with 17 routes ranging from 5.3 to 5.12+, primarily concentrated between 5.8-5.11. The wall sits at approximately 4,700 feet on a ridge dividing Lake Keechelus and Kachess Lake, offering commanding views of the lakes, Mt. Rainier, and Mt. Stuart. Developed primarily through sport climbing bolting by climbers including Leland Windham, the area provides easily accessible high-elevation crag climbing within the Snoqualmie Pass area.",
hierarchyNote: The 'East Face' area name refers to the East Face section of Keechelus Ridge crag, not a standalone peak. Keechelus Ridge itself is the geographic feature (approximately 5,151 ft). The climbing area is located at coordinates 47.3651, -121.31359 near Snoqualmie Pass. The hierarchy structure in the database treats 'East Face' as a parent area, which is accurate for organizing the sport climbing routes in this section.

blurb: Ingalls Peak is a 7,662-foot triple-summit mountain in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, located 2 miles west of Mount Stuart in the North Cascades. The peak features sound peridotite and serpentine rock offering excellent alpine scrambling and rock climbing; the popular South Ridge (Grade II 5.4) was first ascended in 1941 and is a classic gateway alpine rock climb.
hierarchyNote: Given elevation 7,666 ft differs slightly from authoritative sources (Wikipedia, PeakBagger) showing North Peak at 7,662 ft. Peak identity confirmed: Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Kittitas/Chelan County border, coordinates 47.473°N 120.946°W. Minor elevation discrepancy does not affect route validity.

routes.overview: Classic 3-pitch alpine rock climb on the North Peak via the south-facing ridge. Well-protected crack systems with moderate exposure on good peridotite and serpentine rock. Excellent first alpine rock climb combining scrambling approach with technical climbing.
routes.beta: The South Ridge ascends south-facing aspect of North Peak via mixed terrain and crack systems. Hike broken rock and slabs to broad bench below the large red slab. Two main crack variations: traditional 5.4 on left, 5.6 Beckey variation in middle. Pitches are bolted at anchors (pitches 2-3) with additional slings at pitch 1. Serpentine rock sections are notably slick; helmets recommended above 6,900 ft due to rockfall hazard. Parties of 3-4 fit comfortably on small belay ledges.
routes.approach: Drive I-90 to 1 mile east of Cle Elum, northeast on SR-970 for 5 miles past Teanaway River Bridge. Turn left on Teanaway River Road and drive 23 miles to Esmeralda/Teanaway trailhead parking at 4,243 ft (Northwest Forest Pass required). Hike abandoned mine road/trail 0.25 mile to fork, then right onto Ingalls Way Trail 1390. Stay left at fork at 5,600 ft. Hike 3 miles to Ingalls Pass (6,500 ft). Continue on trail to Ingalls Lake (6,400 ft) in 4 miles total. From lake, ascend large talus gully toward saddle below climbing route (additional 1-2 hours). Total approach: 3-4 hours from trailhead.
routes.descent: Three rappels with 60-meter ropes (double rope). First rappel: both 60m ropes from summit bolts to large ledge at top of pitch 2. Second rappel: single rope to top of pitch 1. Third rappel: single 60m rope off anchors. Alternatively, two 60m rope rappels with proper positioning at anchor (walk to east side of face to avoid rope jamming). Note: single 60m rope falls about 15 feet short on certain pitches.
routes.descentText: Rappel descent is standard and straightforward with established bolted anchors at pitches 2 and 3. Most parties use two 60-meter ropes rigged together for efficiency. Ensure all rappellers are tied in and use carabiners/safeguards on all rappel anchors. Be aware that one 60m rope alone is insufficient for lower pitches; coordinate rope management carefully to avoid jamming in cracks below summit anchor.
routes.fa: Keith Lankin and Ken Solberg, May 30, 1941
routes.bestSeason: Peak climbing season is July through September when rock is dry and snow-free. Late June is viable if post-melt conditions permit; early October offers excellent weather and fewer crowds with potential early morning frost on serpentine sections. Avoid May-early June (snow) and November onward (unpredictable weather, icy conditions). Weather windows can be short; monitor forecasts.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage generally unreliable at peak and base of route; intermittent coverage possible near Ingalls Lake. Satellite communication devices recommended for backcountry travel. Contact Kittitas County Sheriff (509-962-7525) for emergencies via 911 in developed areas only.
routes.proNeeds: Standard alpine rock rack from 0.2 inch to 2 inch pieces. Nuts, larger hexes work well in serpentine cracks. Slings useful for anchors (some rap anchors may need replacement). 60-meter rope (two required for efficient descent). Most parties carry single rock climbing rack; bolted anchors on pitches 2-3 limit pro requirements.
routes.detailedRack: Bring one standard single-pitch rack: cams 0.2 to 2.5 inches (or equivalent nuts). Hexes effective in wider cracks. Include 6-8 alpine draws, 4-6 slings for anchor backup. Both 60-meter ropes are essential; many parties prefer two identical ropes for rappel simplicity. Approach may require microspikes for early season snow.

pitchDetail.notes: Mixed 3rd/5th class with short 5.7 crux moves. Low-angle slab with class 4 scrambling; slings at base. About 40 feet of 3rd class terrain, then technical section. Moderate exposure.

Full 60-meter pitch, mostly 5.4 in crack systems. Follows large red slab with broken rock and slabs. Well-protected by natural placements and bolts. This is the main technical pitch; quality climbing on good peridotite.
Short pitch with mantel move; mostly easy 3rd/4th class terrain to summit. Bolted anchor at top. Small ledge accommodates 3-4 climbers. Exposure continues but terrain is straightforward.

road.name: Teanaway River Road (23 miles from SR-970)
road.status: Well-maintained gravel/dirt road passable to high-clearance vehicles in summer; rutted in spring
road.seasonalGate: Typically accessible June-October; winter closure varies with snow
road.driveNote: Rough road with some washboard sections; 1-1.5 hours from highway to trailhead. Four-wheel drive recommended early season; standard vehicles viable mid-summer onward.

climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer. Temperature ranges 50-65F during climbing season.
climate.spring: Highly variable. May has significant snowpack above 6,500 ft; risk of avalanche on approach. By late May/early June, route becomes viable as snow retreats.
climate.summer: Warmest, most stable season. July-August: daily highs 60-70F at peak, lows 35-45F. Low precipitation. Excellent visibility. Most crowded.
climate.fall: September-early October: similar to summer with clear skies and slightly cooler temps (highs 55-65F). Fewer crowds. Early morning frost on serpentine sections. Risk of sudden weather deterioration by mid-October.
climate.winter: Route inaccessible. Heavy snow, icing, avalanche danger. Road often gated by November.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (USDA Forest Service); area managed as Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: $5 day pass or $30 annual Northwest Forest Pass
access.permit: Free Wilderness Permit required May 15-Oct 31
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: Wilderness permit system closes outside May 15-Oct 31 but day-use access remains (no permit needed). Road may be gated Nov-May due to snow.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Kittitas County Sheriff: 509-962-7525; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Healthcare, Cle Elum, WA 98926; phone 509-674-6944
emergency.notes: Helicopter evacuation possible; cell reception unreliable at peak. Satellite messenger recommended. Emergency response time from Cle Elum approximately 90-120 minutes depending on weather and terrain access.

timing.recommendedStart: 6-7 am to complete climb in daylight and avoid afternoon thunderstorms

routes.corrections: Database elevation listed as 7,666 ft; authoritative sources (Wikipedia, PeakBagger) document North Peak as 7,662 ft. Database pitches listed as 3; Mountain Project data shows 4 anchor stations (can be climbed as 3 or 4 pitches depending on style). First ascent: Keith Lankin and Ken Solberg confirmed via multiple authoritative sources on May 30, 1941.
proTips: Start early (6-7 am) to avoid afternoon storms and crowds on popular weekends
Scout the two crack variations before committing; conditions and personal preference may favor 5.4 left or 5.6 middle
Sling natural anchors in addition to bolts for redundancy; some bolts are aged
Helmets essential above 6,900 ft due to occasional rockfall from other parties
Use slings at belay ledges to minimize exposure while managing rope
Two 60m ropes rigged together for rappel is most efficient; single rope is marginal
Pre-arrange signal system with partners if cell coverage is absent
In early season, check snow conditions on approach talus; soft snow can require additional time
watchOut: Rockfall hazard increases dramatically on crowded weekends; stay alert and wear helmet
Serpentine rock on upper pitches is notoriously slick when dry; brushing improves friction but holds are tenuous
Afternoon thunderstorms can form rapidly; an early descent is prudent if clouds build
Crowding on small belay ledges can create communication and safety issues; manage party size and expectations
One 60m rope is insufficient for safe rappel descent; two ropes are mandatory
Rope can jam in cracks below summit; coordinate anchor position to direct rope away from crack systems
knownHazards: Rockfall from above becomes significant hazard during busy climbing conditions, especially on weekends with multiple parties
Serpentine rock polish reduces friction on slabs; use-worn sections become increasingly slick; small holds require precise footwork
Early season lingering snow on approach talus slopes can delay ascent and increase rockfall risk from melt activity
Afternoon thunderstorm formation is common in summer; lightning exposure on exposed ridge and summit is genuine concern
Crowded conditions and small belay ledges increase risk of communication failure and accidental anchor overloading

blurb: The Aiguille de l'M is a distinctive M-shaped cockscomb in Boston Basin that divides the Forbidden Basin from Torment Basin in North Cascades National Park. Named by a mountain guide for its striking resemblance to a much larger peak in the French Alps, this small alpine feature offers two exposed ridge routes combining fourth-class scrambling with short 5th-class sections, accessible as half-day objectives from the Boston Basin campsites.

routes.overview: Exposed ridge climb combining three to four pitches of fourth-class scrambling with a final 40-foot 5.5 pitch to a short tower. Easily completed as a half-day climb from Boston Basin campsites.
routes.beta: Approach as for Forbidden Peak and camp at lower (5,300 ft) or upper (6,400 ft) bivy sites. From camp, navigate toward Forbidden Gully or Cat Scratch Gullies and traverse toward the notch to the right of the North Ridge. Make three to four fourth-class pitches to tower base, then climb the crux 40-foot 5.5 pitch to the tower top. For descent, make one 40-foot rappel and reverse the ridge climb.
routes.approach: Drive WA 20 to Marblemount, then 21.7 miles down Cascade River Road to mile marker 22 (3,200 ft) opposite Johannesburg Mountain. Follow abandoned road 3/4 mile to old mine site, then ascend steep climber's path with four stream crossings to treeline at 5,300 ft (low camp). Total approach time: 3-4 hours.
routes.descent: One 40-foot rappel from tower top, followed by down-climbing ridge.
routes.descentText: From tower summit, establish anchor and rappel 40 feet. Reverse fourth-class scrambling pitches and traverse back toward notch before descending to camp.
routes.bestSeason: July-August most stable, though snow may persist at high camp into July. August typically driest. September dry but larger crevasses possible. June early season with significant lingering snow.
routes.comms: Cell and satellite coverage limited or absent in Boston Basin backcountry. Carry personal locator beacon or satellite communicator.
routes.bail: Short nature makes retreat straightforward. If weather deteriorates or conditions unsafe, descend via same route to camp.
routes.turnaround: Start early from camp to complete climb and rappel with safe daylight margin. If not on tower by mid-afternoon, retreat to preserve daylight for descent.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from Boston Basin camps

gear: Small alpine rock rack to 2 inches
routes.detailedRack: Small to medium alpine rack with emphasis on cams. Bring 6-8 cams from 0.5 to 2 inches, 6-8 alpine draws, multiple slings, and standard biners. Use only removable protection as fixed anchors prohibited in wilderness.
routes.proNeeds: Protection light with most climbing fourth-class scrambling. Crux 5.5 pitch requires basic pro placement. Set anchors with removable protection only.
proTips: Start very early to allow ample daylight
Scout notch location before committing
Tower summit notably loose; true summit to right should be avoided
Test holds carefully; rockfall is hazard
Practice rappelling if unfamiliar with 40-foot pitch
Weather deteriorates rapidly; be prepared to retreat
watchOut: Rockfall: test holds by tapping, protect head, watch for climbers above
Exposure: requires confident footwork on unprotected fourth-class scrambling
Weather volatility: retreat if lightning or heavy precipitation
Rappel anchor failure: test anchor redundantly
True summit instability: do not climb true summit block to right
knownHazards: Rockfall from ridge and tower is primary hazard
Inclement weather strikes rapidly; afternoon thunderstorms common
Short tower summit presents exposure; most parties turn around before absolute top

pitchDetail.notes: Exposed fourth-class scrambling toward tower base. Mostly solid rock with occasional loose blocks.
Final 40-foot pitch to tower top. Good quality rock with clear crack systems.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Seasonal; may close in spring/late summer
road.seasonalGate: Check conditions; closures possible May-June and August-September
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive 21.7 miles down Cascade River Road to mile marker 22. Park at pullout opposite Johannesburg Mountain (3,200 ft).

climate.typical: Alpine environment with volatile weather. Afternoon thunderstorms common.
climate.spring: May-June: lingering snow, variable conditions
climate.summer: July-August: most stable, sunny, mild. Snow into July at high camp.
climate.fall: September: dry conditions, large crevasses possible
climate.winter: October-April: typically inaccessible

emergency.nearestHospital: Marblemount area
emergency.notes: Limited cell/satellite coverage. Carry personal locator beacon. Rescue may take hours or days.

routes.overview: A cool 5.6 romp up exposed ridge combining fourth-class scrambling with short 5th-class sections. Similar commitment to North Ridge but slightly more technical. Half-day alpine objective from Boston Basin camps.
routes.beta: Approach as for Forbidden Peak from low camp (5,300 ft) or high camp (6,400 ft). Navigate toward South Ridge approach facing Forbidden Glacier. Route-finding less obvious than North Ridge; scout carefully. Follow exposed ridge combining fourth-class scrambling with short pitches of 5.6 climbing.
routes.approach: Drive WA 20 to Marblemount, then 21.7 miles down Cascade River Road to mile marker 22 (3,200 ft) opposite Johannesburg Mountain. Follow abandoned road 3/4 mile to old mine site, then ascend steep climber's path with four stream crossings to treeline at 5,300 ft (low camp). Total approach time: 3-4 hours.
routes.descent: Descend via same ridge or similar route depending on descent planning. Likely requires belayed sections and possible rappels.
routes.descentText: Descend exposed ridge via Class 4 scrambling and short 5.6 downclimbing. Depending on party confidence, rappel may be used for crux section. Reverse route-finding back to camp.
routes.bestSeason: July-August most stable. August typically driest. Snow may linger at high camp into July; September dry with possible large crevasses.
routes.comms: Cell and satellite coverage limited or absent in Boston Basin backcountry. Carry personal locator beacon or satellite communicator.
routes.bail: Exposed nature makes retreat more committing than North Ridge. If weather deteriorates, descend carefully via ridge to rejoin approach.
routes.turnaround: Start very early to ensure completion with safe daylight margin. If not at summit by mid-afternoon, retreat for safe descent.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from Boston Basin camps

gear: Small to medium alpine rock rack to 2.5 inches
routes.detailedRack: Small to medium alpine rack with good distribution of cams for thin cracks. Bring 7-8 cams from 0.5 to 2.5 inches, 8 alpine draws, multiple slings, and standard biners. Use only removable protection as fixed anchors prohibited.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needed throughout 5.6 sections. Good crack climbing skills and reliable pro placement essential. Set all anchors with removable protection only.
proTips: Start very early to maximize daylight
South Ridge approach requires careful route-finding
Be confident in 5.6 climbing before attempting
Test holds carefully; rockfall hazard on exposed ridges
Establish solid anchors for crux sections
Watch cloud development; weather deteriorates rapidly
watchOut: Rockfall: exposed ridge means falling rock is hazard
Exposure: more sustained than North Ridge
Route-finding: South Ridge approach less obvious
Weather volatility: afternoon thunderstorms common
Descent exposure: plan carefully as you ascend
knownHazards: Rockfall from ridge due to exposed terrain and looser sections
Inclement weather develops rapidly, particularly afternoon thunderstorms
Sustained 5.6 climbing with exposure requires strong skills; fall protection essential

pitchDetail.notes: Exposed ridge scrambling and light climbing. Follow ridge line toward summit cone.
Most technical section. Good quality rock with crack systems. Place solid protection and belay carefully.
