blurb: Dome Peak (8,920 feet) is a high, massive, glaciated mountain located at the extreme southeast corner of Skagit County in Washington's Glacier Peak Wilderness. This remote peak features two main summits connected by a narrow, exposed ridge and serves as the southern terminus of the famous Ptarmigan Traverse. The northeast summit was first climbed on August 1, 1936, by George Freed and Eric Larson.

routes.aspect: Variable (W glacier approach, N-S summit ridge)
routes.bestSeason: August typically offers optimal snow and weather conditions, with July providing earlier access and September offering stable conditions. The window is limited to these three months due to glacial hazards and snow coverage requirements for safe passage.
routes.overview: The Dome Glacier route ascends the southeast face via high-altitude glacier travel and exposed alpine scrambling, culminating in a narrow, exposed summit ridge traverse (the Cheval) connecting the two peaks. This is a serious alpine mountaineering objective requiring glacier and rock climbing skills.
routes.beta: This classic 3-4 day alpine climb involves a long backpack approach followed by technical glacier travel and exposed rock scrambling on the famous Cheval crux. The route is best suited for experienced mountaineers comfortable with crevasse rescue, glacier navigation, and exposed Class 4-5 rock.
routes.approach: From Downey Creek Trailhead on Suiattle River Road (FR 26, trailhead at 1,400 ft), hike 6.5 miles on maintained Downey Creek Trail to Sixmile Camp. Continue on unmaintained Bachelor Creek Trail through forest and avalanche debris to Cub Lake (5,338 ft). From Cub Lake, ascend to Itswoot Ridge camp (6,300 ft). From Itswoot Ridge, follow the foot path toward Spire Point, then descend and traverse to the Dome-Chickamin Col. Access the upper Dome Glacier via steep snow slopes and ascend east toward the summit plateau.
routes.descent: Retrace the approach route. Descent from the summit involves reversing the exposed ridge traverse and descending steep snow slopes. The major crux is bypassing the bergschrund at the glacier base, typically requiring a 60-meter rappel or crossing a seasonal snowbridge. Continue descent via Itswoot Ridge camp and the approach trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: The descent mirrors the ascent route but requires careful management of the bergschrund crossing. The crevasse spans the glacier base with seasonal width variation. Early season attempts may find adequate snowbridges; later season ascents typically require rigging a rappel anchor to descend 60 meters via fixed rope. Parties should descend during optimal snow conditions (early morning) and use proper belay and rappel safety protocols. Plunge-step down snow slopes to the Dome-Chickamin Col, then reverse the traverse back to Itswoot Ridge camp and the approach trail.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Downey Creek Trailhead to Bachelor Creek camp (6.5 miles, 9-10 hours, 3,000 ft gain). Day 2: Bachelor Creek area to Itswoot Ridge camp via Cub Lake (4-6 miles, 5-7 hours, 3,000+ ft gain). Day 3: Itswoot Ridge to summit and return to camp (1,600+ ft gain, 6-8 hours round trip). Day 4: Itswoot Ridge to trailhead (10-12 miles, 8-10 hours, 3,300 ft loss).
routes.turnaround: Begin descent by 2-3 PM from summit to ensure adequate daylight for technical terrain negotiation and bergschrund passage. If weather deteriorates significantly, consider turning back at Itswoot Ridge camp.
routes.bail: From Itswoot Ridge camp, descend via the approach route to trailhead. The remoteness means any bail-out involves full descent to trailhead; there are no rescue facilities on the mountain.
routes.comms: No cellular service expected in the Glacier Peak Wilderness. Satellite communication devices (satellite messenger, satellite phone, or T-Mobile D2D satellite via Starlink) are strongly recommended for emergency contact and trip reporting.
knownHazards: Bergschrund crossing at glacier base, spanning full glacier width with seasonal width variation
Crevasses on Dome Glacier requiring roped travel and crevasse rescue skills
Exposed summit ridge traverse (Cheval) with minimal protection options
Class 4-5 rock sections requiring scrambling skills and rock climbing experience
Avalanche terrain on approach slopes, particularly in couloirs and gullies
Stream crossings that can swell with snowmelt or rain
High altitude exposure at 8,920 feet
Variable weather and rapid weather changes in alpine environment
watchOut: The Cheval summit feature is a narrow, exposed rock ridge with Class 5.5 climbing sections and poor protection
Bergschrund can be very wide mid-to-late season; plan rappel setup in advance
Crevasse hazards require constant vigilance despite being relatively sparse on the main approach
Featureless rock on the upper ridge prevents natural belay anchors; parties often set up hand lines or pre-place fixed ropes
Early-season attempts (July) may encounter significant avalanche hazard in approach gullies
Late-season attempts (September) may encounter dry bergschrund requiring more technical descent
gear: Carabiners (6 straight gate, 2 locking, 1 large pear)
routes.detailedRack: Bring two 40-meter ropes for belays and rappels. Small rock rack including stoppers/nuts sizes #5-#9 (DMM walnuts or equivalent) for anchor building on the summit ridge. 3-4 ice screws for glacier protection. 4 aluminum pickets for glacier anchors. Rescue pulley for crevasse rescue if traveling with three or more climbers. Webbing and accessory cord for backup anchors and rigging.
routes.proNeeds: The exposed summit ridge requires protection for parties uncomfortable with exposure. Set up hand lines or place fixed ropes for the Cheval traverse if conditions warrant. Anchor building on poor rock quality is challenging. Rock protection is secondary to rope systems for the final exposed ridge section.
whatToBring: The ten essentials (navigation, sun protection, insulation, illumination, first aid, fire, repair kit, nutrition, hydration, emergency shelter)
Bivy sack or emergency bivouac gear
Sleeping system rated to 0-10F (mountain temperatures drop significantly)
Stove and fuel for cooking at camp
Sunscreen and lip balm with SPF
Polypropylene, down, or synthetic insulating layers
proTips: Plan the bergschrund crossing in advance and scope it carefully on descent before committing to rappel setup
Early morning descents (4-6 AM summit pushes) allow descent in cooler, more stable snow conditions
The summit Cheval is best protected by pre-placed hand lines or fixed rope rather than climbing gear
Stay on the south side of the summit ridge for better rock quality and more manageable exposure
Scout Itswoot Ridge camp location carefully to locate water sources and stable ground away from avalanche paths
Route-finding to Itswoot Ridge can be confusing; mark the downhill route well for the descent
Bring a small rock rack despite the Class 3 glacier rating; the technical rock sections justify protection
High wind on the summit ridge can develop rapidly; monitor weather closely and have a low-threshold bail plan

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Chelan Ranger District
access.permit: Free self-issuing wilderness permit required (available at trailhead)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass ($30 annual or $5 day pass) required for Downey Creek Trailhead parking

road.name: Suiattle River Road (FR 26)
road.status: Open mid-spring through fall; may have seasonal restrictions
road.seasonalGate: Road access is weather-dependent; verify current conditions with Chelan Ranger District before travel
road.driveNote: Drive approximately 19.5 miles on Suiattle River Road from Highway 530 near Darlington to Downey Creek Campground and trailhead. Road is narrow and winding; high-clearance vehicles recommended but passenger cars possible in dry conditions. No services at trailhead; nearest facilities in Darlington.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Cascade West/South Zone
climate.typical: Summer conditions (Jul-Aug-Sep) feature mild temperatures (6-15C), light to moderate winds, and minimal precipitation. Afternoons commonly develop afternoon clouds and occasional thunderstorms. Freezing level typically at 4,000-4,600 meters (well above the peak in mid-summer).
climate.spring: May-June access restricted by avalanche hazard and deep snow; not recommended without specialized skills
climate.summer: July-September is the optimal climbing window. August typically features the most stable conditions with consolidated snow. Expect cold nights (-5 to 5C) and mild afternoons (5-15C). Precipitation is minimal but afternoon weather buildup is common.
climate.fall: September climbing remains feasible with good snow and weather, though bergschrund hazards increase and camps experience hard freezes.
climate.winter: Not recommended for general mountaineers; serious alpine/ski mountaineering only

timing.recommendedStart: Begin hike from Downey Creek Trailhead by 6-8 AM on Day 1 to maximize daylight for the initial long approach; reach Bachelor Creek camp by dark. Summit day should depart Itswoot Ridge camp by 5-6 AM to reach the summit by mid-afternoon and complete descent before dark.

emergency.rangerStation: Chelan Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest: 509-682-4900 (Wed-Thu 9am-3pm); Marblemount Wilderness Information Center (North Cascades NP): 360-854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (~40 miles from trailhead); EvergreenHealth Monroe (~50 miles)
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness location; helicopter rescue is the primary evacuation method for serious injuries. Satellite communication is essential for emergency reporting. Response times are measured in hours to days depending on weather and terrain access.

blurb: Burgundy Spire (8,492 ft) is a prominent granite spire in the Wine Spires group of the North Cascades near Washington Pass. This peak is considered one of the hardest principal summits in the Cascades to reach via its easiest route (the North Face, Grade III 5.8). The North Face route is a classic alpine rock climb featuring multiple pitches of varied terrain, loose flakes, exposed traverses, and demanding route-finding.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: database shows 8,483 ft; authoritative sources (peakbagger.com, listsofjohn.com) consistently show 8,492 ft. Recommendation: update to 8,492 ft.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, 1953 (original ascent via aid; modern free ascent details not precisely sourced)
routes.overview: Classic alpine rock climb on the North Face of Burgundy Spire. The route ascends 600 feet via six pitches of 5.4-5.8 climbing featuring cracks, flakes, and exposed ridge traverses. Routes starts from Burgundy Col at 7,770 feet with varied terrain including chimney, scrambling, cracks, and an exposed offwidth crux pitch.
routes.beta: This is considered the standard and easiest route up Burgundy Spire. Descent is accomplished via 5-6 rappels down the middle of the upper face. Recommended for climbers experienced in alpine rock and comfortable with route-finding on exposed terrain. The route features many traverses, down-climbs, and loose features that demand careful attention.
routes.approach: From Highway 20, descend 3.75 miles past the hairpin curve and park at the pullout at 4,310 feet. Follow a faint boot path through loose talus into trees, then along Early Winters Creek. Ascend steeply between two drainages on a west-facing slope (morning climbing recommended). The trail reaches a flat bench at 6,440 feet (suitable for camping). Continue to Burgundy Col (7,770 feet) via loose scree and gullies following cairns. Total elevation gain from trailhead to col: approximately 3,700 feet. Approach time: 2-3 hours to col.
routes.descent: Descend via rappels down the middle of the upper face from the summit anchor (10 slings). Five rappels are possible with double ropes: R1 (40m), R2 (55m to arch area), R3 (55m to horn with blue sling), R4 (40m to scree ledges), R5 (60m to base). A 60-meter rope works but may require downclimbing; a 70-meter rope is preferable. Single-rope parties must find intermediate anchors and reverse more of the climbing route.
routes.descentText: The descent begins at a bomber summit anchor equipped with 10 slings. Rappel down the middle of the upper face using established bolted anchors and slung blocks. Double-rope rappels: R1 is 40 meters to a bolted anchor; R2 is 55 meters to the arch area; R3 is 55 meters to a horn with a blue sling; R4 is 40 meters to scree ledges; R5 is 60 meters to the base. If using a single 60-meter rope, climbers must find intermediate anchors and may need to downclimb short sections. Rope drag becomes significant by mid-descent; carefully manage rope to avoid snagging. Be cautious of the rappel anchor near the arch—the webbing may need replacement. The final rappel deposits climbers on loose talus and scree.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (or overnight camp): Drive to pullout at 4,310 ft. Hike 2-3 hours to Burgundy Col (7,770 ft) via Early Winters Creek trail. Camp at col or at flat bench (6,440 ft). Day 2 (if camping overnight): Begin climbing at first light. Climb 6 pitches (8-10 hours total climbing time). Descend via 5-6 rappels (1-2 hours). Return to car (can complete in one long 14-16 hour day from early morning start, or split over two days).
routes.bail: If difficulty becomes overwhelming, retreat is possible down the route via rappel anchors or downclimbing. The route has established rappel anchors throughout. In case of severe weather, seek shelter in the col area or descend to lower elevations. An emergency descent via the gullies toward Early Winters Creek may be possible but is not documented; stick to established rappel anchors.
routes.turnaround: Given this route's technical difficulty and commitment, retreat promptly if route-finding becomes problematic, if weather deteriorates significantly, or if team members are struggling with the grade. The route's long rappel descent means weather can quickly become dangerous; turn around if thunderstorms approach.
routes.bestSeason: July through September provides the best climbing conditions. Highway 20 typically closes mid-October through May (dates vary annually by 2-3 months depending on snowfall and road conditions). Snow in early season can make the scree approach easier but creates challenging climbing conditions on the rock. October snowfall can rapidly deteriorate conditions. Plan trips for July-August for most reliable weather and road access.
routes.comms: No cell phone service is available in the mountains. Satellite communication (Garmin inReach or similar on Iridium network) is essential for emergency communication and rescue coordination.
routes.detailedRack: Single rack to 3 inches is adequate for climbers confident on 5.8. Bring extra pieces in 0.75 and 1 inch sizes if 5.8 climbing is near your limit. A full cam set to 4 inches is recommended for the offwidth variations and off-route climbing. Protection is sparse on some pitches (especially traverse pitches), so plan gear placement carefully.
routes.proNeeds: Full trad rack with emphasis on cracks from thin fingers to 4-inch offwidths. Most pitches have sparse but adequate protection if you stay on route. Be prepared to place protection on exposed traverses.
gear: Helmet (essential for route-finding and objective hazard)
60-meter rope (double ropes strongly recommended; single rope requires intermediate anchors or downclimbing)
Quickdraws for aid or rappel anchors
Webbing and slings for rappel setup
whatToBring: Trekking poles (climbers note this as the most important piece for the approach)
2+ liters of water (Early Winters Creek has water but the trickle before col can be unreliable)
Light sleeping bag and pad if camping
Insulating layers (alpine weather can change rapidly)
proTips: Morning climbing recommended on the west-facing slope to avoid afternoon sun exposure and snow/ice melt hazards.
Rope drag becomes significant by pitch 4; do not attempt to combine pitches.
Many flakes are hollow and ready to break; test flakes carefully and avoid pulling on obviously loose features.
The offwidth pitch is the crux; practice offwidth technique or consider bringing extra protection.
Cairns mark the approach to the col through loose scree; stay on the marked line to avoid loose talus hazards.
The rappel anchors are well-established; trust them. Manage rope drag carefully to avoid snagging.
Don't let the blank, lichen-covered rock above you intimidate you—stay on route and the climbing improves.
Two main camps exist on the approach: one at ~6,500 ft (flat bench with water nearby) and Burgundy Col itself. Both can be used for overnight bivy if doing a two-day push.
watchOut: Loose and exfoliating granite requires careful route-finding and testing of holds before committing weight.
Exposed traverse pitches (especially pitch 4) demand careful protection placement and awareness of long runouts.
The approach scree is described as 'ball bearing' and 'kitty litter'—easy to slip and difficult to move quickly. Trekking poles are highly recommended.
Rope drag on descent increases risk of snagging and extended rescue time. Double ropes strongly recommended.
No cell service; satellite communicator is essential before departure.
Highway 20 can close unexpectedly due to avalanche activity or rockfall.
Lichen-covered sections near the summit can be greasy and slippery; clean your feet and test holds carefully.
Fixed piton on pitch 7 may be deteriorated; carry backup protection.
knownHazards: Loose, exfoliating, hollow granite flakes throughout the route that can fail under climbing stress or loaded slings.
Exposed ridge climbing and traverses with significant exposure but generally adequate protection if placed carefully.
Route-finding complexity: many pitches feature traverses, some down-climbs, and flakes of all sizes. Off-route climbing can be significantly harder.
Lichen-covered blank rock on upper pitches requires confident rock climbing technique and good footwork.
Approach talus is unstable ('ball bearing' and 'kitty litter' scree); easy to trigger small slides and difficult for rapid descent.
Weather exposure: the peak is highly exposed to sudden weather changes; afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer.
Rope snagging risk during rappel descent, especially with long pitches requiring full rope length or intermediate anchors.
routes.rappels: 5-6 rappels with double ropes (R1: 40m, R2: 55m, R3: 55m, R4: 40m, R5: 60m); 60m single rope requires intermediate anchors and downclimbing; 70m rope preferred

pitchDetail.notes: Descend 20 feet from Burgundy Col, climb chimney between Paisano Pinnacle and Burgundy Spire. 60-meter pitch to slung horn belay.
Scramble up scree ledges. Approximately 45 meters, no protection needed.
Climb sketchy flakes, veer left to near-vertical right-facing crack system. 40 meters, sparse protection. Many flakes are loose.
Crack climbing (20 feet) then rightward traverse on narrow ledge. 'Be sure to place adequate protection on the rightward traverse.' 45 meters, rope drag increases significantly.
One of the best parts of the climb: vertical flakes and cracks, then leftward to headwall, rightward traverse to huge ledge. 35 meters.
Traverse huge ledge rightward well beyond apparent necessity. Descend 15-20 feet midway, reascend, pass under gigantic arch block. Best done unroped.

timing.recommendedStart: 06:00 for car-to-car completion in one long day; or start afternoon for overnight camp at col with early morning summit push next day

road.name: US Highway 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open late May to mid-October (dates vary by 2-3 months)
road.seasonalGate: Silver Star gate (milepost 171) closes road November-May; Rainy Pass (milepost 134) and Washington Pass (milepost 5475) are closure points; gates lower in season
road.driveNote: Park at pullout 3.75 miles east of Washington Pass hairpin curve at approximately 4,310 feet elevation. Pullout has adequate parking for 5-10 vehicles.

climate.forecastZone: Cascade-North Cascades zone (check NWAC for avalanche forecast in winter/spring)
climate.typical: Variable alpine weather; afternoon thunderstorms common June-September; rapid weather changes possible
climate.spring: Lingering snow at col and higher elevations April-June; warmer days but cold mornings; some trails open earlier if snow melts early
climate.summer: July-August: Most stable weather; daytime highs 50-65F; mornings 35-45F; afternoon thunderstorms 1-3 PM typical. September: Cooling trend; similar storms but fewer; early snow possible late month.
climate.fall: September-October: Temperatures dropping; first snow possible late September; Highway 20 closes mid-October to mid-May depending on conditions.
climate.winter: Highway 20 closed November-May; snow and avalanche hazard extreme; not typically climbed.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District (U.S. Forest Service)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required: $5 per vehicle per day or $30 annual
access.permit: Wilderness permit free, available at trailhead; no climbing-specific permit required
access.passRequired: NW Forest Pass
access.closures: Highway 20 closed mid-October through May (dates vary); check WSDOT for current status before traveling

emergency.county: Skagit County or Okanogan County (boundary area)
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee NF: (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Arlington/Smokey Point, ~45 miles west, level IV ED); Cascade Medical Center (Leavenworth, ~50 miles southeast, 24-hour ED)
emergency.notes: No cell service in mountains; satellite communicator (Garmin inReach on Iridium network) is essential. Rescue from this peak can take 6-12+ hours depending on conditions and rescue difficulty. Air rescue difficult due to terrain and weather. Plan conservatively.

routes.corrections: Elevation discrepancy: Database lists 8,483 ft but authoritative sources (peakbagger.com, listsofjohn.com, Mountain Project) consistently report 8,492 ft. Pitch count varies between sources (6-8 pitches depending on how approach sections are counted); this entry uses 6 pitches for the rock climbing above Burgundy Col. Route length varies (600 ft for climbing above col, or 800 ft if including approach scrambling); using 600 ft for climbing portion.

blurb: Agnes Mountain is a striking Matterhorn-shaped granite peak in the remote Glacier Peak Wilderness of Washington's North Cascades. Despite its modest 8,131-foot elevation, it rises dramatically with its north face dropping 1,800 feet to Agnes Creek in just 1.5 miles. The peak remains seldom climbed due to its deep wilderness location and challenging approaches, with only fourteen parties having summited by 1987.

routes.fa: W. Ronald Frazier and Dan O'Brien, 1936
routes.overview: The first-ascent route ascending Agnes Mountain via the West Fork of Agnes Creek, featuring mixed scrambling and technical rock sections on a dramatic alpine peak.
routes.beta: This is the original first-ascent line established by Frazier and O'Brien in 1936. Modern mountaineers typically approach via the Agnes Creek Trail from Stehekin, establishing base camp in the upper basin (5,000-6,700 feet). The route involves Class 3-4 scrambling with some exposed sections and snow travel depending on season. The peak's north face rises steeply, creating objective hazards from rockfall. Technical rock climbing sections require helmets and rope skills.
routes.approach: From Stehekin, take the Lady Express ferry up Lake Chelan to Stehekin (approximately 50 miles from Fields Point). Board the shuttle bus ($7) from Stehekin Landing to High Bridge Ranger Station (1,610 feet elevation). From High Bridge, follow the Agnes Creek Trail (Pacific Crest Trail) southbound for approximately 10 miles, crossing Agnes Creek and Spruce Creek via downed logs. Bushwhack steeply through dense vegetation (alder and devil's club) to establish base camp in Upper Blue Basin or similar location at 5,000-6,700 feet elevation. The approach involves significant off-trail navigation through challenging terrain.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route used for ascent. From the summit area, descend Class 2-3 terrain and traverse back to the south col area. From there, descend the east-facing snow couloir using snow flukes and running belays if snow is present. In late season with less snow, navigate the rocky gully and scrambling terrain back to base camp. The descent from summit to camp typically requires 8-9 hours depending on conditions and party experience.
routes.descentText: The descent from the summit involves Class 2-3 scrambling on the upper mountain, followed by descent of mixed snow and rock. Climbers typically utilize snow couloirs in early season with running belays and snow flukes for protection. A rising traverse reaches approximately 7,400 feet before descending through brushy terrain back to base camp. Late-season descents require careful navigation through loose rock and scree with exposure on ridge terrain. Final descent through subalpine alder thickets requires significant bushwhacking effort.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Travel from Fields Point to Stehekin via Lady Express ferry, ride shuttle to High Bridge, hike/camp at High Bridge or nearby campground. Day 2: Hike approximately 5 hours up Agnes Creek Trail crossing creeks and establishing position for bushwhack. Day 3: Bushwhack to base camp in upper basin (4,000+ vertical feet of off-trail gain). Day 4: Acclimatization or attempt summit from established base camp. Day 5-6: Return descent following approach route.
routes.bail: From upper basin (around 6,000-6,700 feet), climbers can descend back down Agnes Creek to High Bridge if weather deteriorates or conditions become unsafe. From the south col area (7,480 feet), retreat is possible by reversing the ascent. In case of injury or emergency, evacuation from the wilderness requires helicopter or boat extraction coordinated through Chelan County Sheriff's Office.
routes.turnaround: Climbers should establish a turnaround time of mid-morning or early afternoon to allow sufficient daylight for descent through the technical sections and bushwhacking terrain. The route requires strong navigation skills and experience with alpine scrambling. Any delays on the summit pyramid necessitate descent before darkness falls.
routes.bestSeason: The optimal climbing season is mid-June through early September, after significant snowmelt has occurred and before fall weather deteriorates. July and August offer the most stable weather and lowest snow/moat hazards. Early season attempts (June) may encounter significant moat conditions around snow and rock interfaces, while late season (September onward) risks early winter storms and avalanche conditions on snow slopes.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable or absent throughout the approach and on the mountain. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach or similar) is essential for emergencies. The Stehekin area has limited communication infrastructure; emergency communication must be coordinated through Chelan County Sheriff's Office (911 or non-emergency dispatch 509-667-6851).
routes.proNeeds: Rope (50m minimum) and protection for Class 3-4 scrambling sections. A helmet is essential given rockfall hazard on the north face. Ice ax and crampons recommended for snow traversals, particularly early season. Basic belay protection and rappel anchors needed for descent.
routes.detailedRack: 1 x 50m rope (minimum), helmet (essential), ice ax, crampons (early season), rock protection to Class 4 (cams, nuts, slings), 3-4 carabiners, rappel device, locking carabiner, sling for anchors, snow flukes for running belays on snow slopes.
proTips: Establish base camp early and acclimatize on approach day if possible
Bring trekking poles to ease the bushwhacking sections
Start early from base camp for summit attempts to ensure safe descent in daylight
Scout route on clear day before summit attempt
Modern climbers typically use South Ridge route via Asa Peak rather than original West Fork approach; research current conditions with local ranger district
watchOut: Rockfall hazard on north face and exposed ridges
Deep moat conditions in early season around snow/rock interfaces
Loose, unstable rock common on scrambling sections
Navigation is complex on off-trail approach and upper mountain
Exposure on ridge terrain with minimal protection
Stream crossings with strong current (particularly in early season melt)
Dense alder and devil's club in subalpine zone create navigation challenges
knownHazards: Objective rockfall from steep north face (1,800-foot drop to Agnes Creek)
Avalanche potential on steep snow couloirs and slopes over 30 degrees
Crevasse risk and deep moats around glaciated terrain
Exposure on Class 4-5 scrambling sections with limited protection options
Loose, crumbling granite typical of North Cascades creates dangerous climbing conditions

timing.recommendedStart: 0600-0700 from base camp

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest / Glacier Peak Wilderness / North Cascades National Park (partial)
access.permit: Wilderness permit required for Glacier Peak Wilderness (self-issued, free)

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road
road.status: Open seasonally, accessed via Lake Chelan ferry
road.seasonalGate: Road to Stehekin accessible via ferry year-round; shuttle service May-October (confirm current schedule)
road.driveNote: No road access to peak trailhead. Access requires 50-mile Lake Chelan ferry to Stehekin plus shuttle bus to High Bridge (12 miles up valley). Ferry and shuttle coordination required; limited daily service.

climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes; afternoons frequently bring thunderstorms June-August. High winds common on exposed ridges.
climate.spring: Significant snowpack, moat conditions, avalanche danger on steep terrain; stream crossings difficult
climate.summer: Most stable weather period; temperatures 40-60F at 8,000 feet; snow patches and glacier travel possible early season; afternoon thunderstorm risk
climate.fall: Increasing storm frequency; early snowfall possible September onward; shorter daylight hours
climate.winter: Heavy snow, extreme avalanche danger, not recommended; few winter ascents on record
climate.forecastZone: Mountain-forecast.com; Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) for avalanche forecasts

emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Community Hospital (approximate 50+ miles/1+ hour by ferry from Stehekin); helicopter evacuation may be required for serious emergencies from backcountry with base in Brewster (approximately 30 minutes from Stehekin)
emergency.rangerStation: High Bridge Ranger Station (at trailhead); contact via North Cascades National Park or Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest district
emergency.notes: Stehekin has no doctors; medical emergencies require coordination with Lake Chelan Community Hospital and likely helicopter or boat evacuation. NPS has personnel with emergency medical training. Helicopter evacuation from backcountry takes approximately 30 minutes from call time to arrival.

waypoints.name: High Bridge Ranger Station / Agnes Creek Trailhead
Upper Blue Basin / Base Camp Area

blurb: Argonaut Peak is a 8,457-foot (2,578m) clean granite peak in the Stuart Range of Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, located between Colchuck Peak and Sherpa Peak. The mountain features two summits of nearly equal height with a prominent southeast spire. First summited in September 1955 via the south route by Lex Maxwell, Bob McCall, and Bill Prater.
hierarchyNote: Argonaut Peak is in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness/Stuart Range, NOT the North Cascades proper. The North Cascades typically refer to more northerly glaciated peaks; Argonaut Peak is further south in the Wenatchee Mountains complex of the Cascades.

routes.overview: Classic northeast ridge approach featuring scrambling on lower sections transitioning to technical Class 4-5 climbing on exposed ridge terrain, finishing near the northeast face.
routes.beta: Scramble up the ridge crest until it steepens, then traverse along the right (northwestern) side. The route often offers a choice between 4th or low 5th-class climbing along the crest and krummholtz-whacking to avoid difficult sections. The route features blocky, generally solid granite with good protection opportunities.
routes.approach: From Colchuck Lake trailhead (Eightmile Creek Road, 3,400 ft), follow the well-maintained trail to Colchuck Lake via Mountaineer Creek. Ascend the Colchuck Couloir (30-40 degree snow) to Colchuck Col (8,050 ft). Traverse the ridge connecting Colchuck Peak to Argonaut Peak, then descend into a gully before ascending via the northeast ridge approach.
routes.descent: Descend the northeast ridge retracing ascent route, or traverse to the East Gully and downclimb, traverse to the ridgeline between Argonaut and Colchuck, then downclimb lower angle snow back into the basin.
routes.descentText: From Colchuck Col, descend in four rappels using 30-meter ropes, then traverse a snowy ledge to reach the east gully. Alternatively, reverse the ascent route by downclimbing the ridge.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from trailhead to Colchuck Lake/Col camp, 4.2 hours, 4,600 ft gain. Day 2: Pre-dawn start from camp to base of northeast ridge. Scramble and climb northeast ridge to summit, 3-4 hours. Descend via rappels and traverse back to col, 2-3 hours. Return to lower camp or trailhead.
routes.bail: At any point on the northeast ridge, traverse south to the snow gully system between Argonaut and Colchuck. Descent options exist via the East Gully or Colchuck-Argonaut saddle route.
routes.turnaround: Turn back if conditions deteriorate or if you have not summited by mid-afternoon to allow safe descent before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, with optimal conditions in July and August when snow is consolidated but sufficient for creek crossings. Early season (late May-June) offers harder snow conditions but avalanche hazard. Autumn (September) has stable conditions but shorter daylight.
routes.detailedRack: Light rack with protection to 1.5 inches; bring 1-2 ice screws for potential ice sections; rock pitons for mixed terrain. Typical alpine rack with quickdraws and runners.
routes.proNeeds: Mixed climbing protection; rock protection up to 1.5 inches; ice screw placement in potential ice sections.
proTips: Traversing south along krummholtz can often bypass difficult sections on the crest
Start pre-dawn from camp to maximize daylight for technical sections
Route-finding can be complex; establish clear turnaround times
The descent is easier if you know your rappel points in advance
watchOut: Exposure on the northeast ridge with unforgiving drops on both sides
Loose blocks and rubble common in upper gully sections
Weather can move in rapidly; watch cloud development
Rockfall hazard from higher elevations during or after warm days
knownHazards: Steep loose rubble sections requiring low to mid-Class 5 climbing
Exposure on narrow ridge sections
Potential for rockfall from warming effects or passing climbers above
Mixed snow and ice can make footing unpredictable in early season
routes.comms: No reliable cell service in wilderness. Satellite communication (Iridium phone or iPhone 14+ satellite messaging) recommended for emergencies.

timing.recommendedStart: 3:00 AM from camp

climate.forecastZone: Central Cascades Alpine
climate.typical: Mild summers with variable weather. Freeze-thaw cycles common.
climate.spring: Snow through May and June, avalanche hazard possible, cold nights below freezing
climate.summer: Dry spells interspersed with afternoon thunderstorms, high UV exposure, temperatures 5-15°C at elevation
climate.fall: Clear and stable but rapid deterioration after September, increasing snow probability
climate.winter: Heavy snow accumulation, extreme cold, access severely limited

road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, take Highway 2 west approximately 7 miles, turn onto Eightmile Creek Road and follow to parking area. Approximately 1 hour drive from Leavenworth.

emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, 600 Sherbourne, Leavenworth, WA 98826. Phone: (509) 548-2550. Hours: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri 9am-12pm and 1-3:30pm

pitchDetail.notes: Scramble up ridge crest with some steep sections, generally solid granite with good foot placements
Technical climbing on ridge crest or traverse options on right side; mixed climbing on granite with some loose blocks
Final approach to summit featuring 4th-class scrambling with good protection opportunities to reach the register

routes.overview: Technical alpine rock climb from Colchuck Col featuring mixed terrain with Class 4-5.6 climbing on solid granite blocks, offering solid rock quality with generally straightforward route finding.
routes.beta: From Colchuck Col, ascend the east ridge featuring blocky, generally solid but not sustained Class 4 to 5.6 climbing. The east faces of towers are typically easier than west sides, making cliff-out less likely if staying near the crest. Multiple pitch sections with good protection opportunities.
routes.approach: From Colchuck Lake trailhead (Eightmile Creek Road, 3,400 ft), follow trail via Mountaineer Creek to Colchuck Lake. Ascend the Colchuck Couloir (30-40 degree snow) to Colchuck Col (8,050 ft). From the col, traverse the connecting ridge to the base of the East Ridge.
routes.descent: Descend via four rappels of 30 meters each from anchor points on the east ridge, then traverse to the East Gully system or retrace ascent route back to Colchuck Col.
routes.descentText: Descend in four 30-meter rappels from established anchor points on the east ridge. Traverse a snowy ledge to reach the east gully, then downclimb or rappel the gully to return to the Colchuck-Argonaut saddle and back to camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from trailhead to Colchuck Lake/Col camp (4.2 hours, 4,600 ft gain). Day 2: Pre-dawn start. Traverse to East Ridge base and climb ridge to summit (3-4 hours). Descend via rappels back to col (2-3 hours). Return to camp or continue to trailhead.
routes.bail: At any point on the climb, retreat via downclimbing to Colchuck Col. Snow gully systems provide reliable escape routes on either side of the ridge.
routes.turnaround: Turn back if you have not summited by mid-afternoon to allow safe rappel descent before dark.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September, with optimal conditions mid-July through August when snow is consolidated. Late May-June offers harder snow but avalanche potential. September provides stable conditions but shorter days.
routes.detailedRack: Full alpine rock rack with protection to 2 inches; 2-3 ice screws for potential ice sections; two 30-meter ropes recommended for efficient rappelling; mountaineering hardware including pitons and rock pegs.
routes.proNeeds: Full rock protection suite from small wires to 2-inch cracks; mixed climbing protection for potential snow/ice layers; secure anchor placement for rappels.
whatToBring: first aid kit and blister kit
proTips: The East Ridge offers more sustained climbing than neighboring routes with generally good rock quality
Route-finding is straightforward if you maintain the crest; dropping to the east gully can lead to complications
Establish rappel anchors carefully as they are crucial for safe descent
Early season conditions can have water-ice sections requiring ice tool technique
watchOut: Significant exposure on the ridge crest with drops on both sides
Loose blocks more prevalent in upper sections, especially in warm conditions
Weather deterioration can occur rapidly; maintain awareness of cloud development
Adequate rope management is essential for efficient rappel descent to stay ahead of darkness
knownHazards: Sustained mixed climbing terrain with Class 5 sections on exposed ridge
Exposure and unprotected scrambling sections during route transitions
Rockfall potential from warming effects and other parties
Mixed snow/ice transitions create unpredictable footing in spring/early season
routes.comms: No reliable cell service. Satellite communication essential for emergencies (Iridium phone or iPhone 14+ satellite messaging).

timing.recommendedStart: 2:00 AM from camp

climate.forecastZone: Central Cascades Alpine

climate.spring: Snow and ice prevalent, freeze-thaw cycles, avalanche hazard possible May-June
climate.summer: Mostly dry with occasional afternoon storms, temperatures 5-15°C at elevation
climate.fall: Clear conditions early, rapid deterioration after September with increasing precipitation
climate.winter: Heavy snow, extreme cold, extended winter conditions, generally inaccessible

pitchDetail.notes: Initial blocky scramble on lower east ridge with moderate exposure, solid granite
Technical climbing on ridge crest with good protection opportunities
Steeper section with mixed climbing and moderate exposure
Sustained climbing with good rock quality and protection
Continued ridge climbing with exposure to both sides
Final pitches to summit with increasing exposure

routes.corrections: Input elevation 8,457 ft matches Wikipedia data (some sources list 8,453 ft - both within typical survey variance). Peak location is Alpine Lakes Wilderness/Stuart Range, not North Cascades proper.

blurb: Bacon Peak is a glaciated peak in North Cascades National Park featuring three major glaciers (Diobsud Creek, Green Lake, and Noisy Creek) covering 1.2 square miles. This pleasant but seldom-climbed objective in the Noisy-Diobsud Wilderness offers moderate-altitude mountaineering with scenic alpine terrain. First ascent: R.B. Robertson and Eugene Logan (1905).

routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the most stable conditions with lower avalanche risk and established summer snowpack. June can have lingering avalanche hazard and heavy snow. September sees fewer crowds but shorter daylight and variable weather.
routes.overview: The Diobsud Creek / Green Lake Glacier route ascends Bacon Peak via the southeast approach through the Bacon Creek drainage, gaining over 6,000 feet from the remote trailhead. The route follows steep forest to the Diobsud Creek Glacier, crosses crevassed terrain, and summits via the east slope.
routes.beta: This is a committing multi-day alpine objective requiring glacier travel, crevasse rescue skills, and strong route-finding ability. The route is accessed via heavily forested terrain with significant deadfall obstacles, followed by steep snow and glacier travel to a 6,250 ft saddle camp. The final summit push is relatively short from camp but requires careful navigation in potentially whiteout conditions. Expect to negotiate around cliff bands, navigate through avalanche terrain, and manage steep gully sections.
routes.approach: From Bacon Creek Road (Forest Road 1060/1064), park at road end around 800 ft elevation. Hike steeply northwest through dense forest with extensive downfall and brush for 1.5 miles to ~2,500 ft, navigating around massive fallen logs by skirting north along talus. Continue ascending through open forest and snowshoe-covered terrain, veering northwest at 3,600 ft. Travel along a long southeast ridgeline, reaching ~3,100-4,000 ft where snowshoes become necessary. At 4,600 ft, locate the steep gully access to Diobsud Creek Glacier. Traverse northwest on the glacier toward the 6,250-6,300 ft saddle camp, probing constantly for crevasses.
routes.descent: The standard descent retraces the glacier and gully descent route. Alternative: if conditions permit, descend via Green Lake Glacier to the north, reaching Bacon Tarns at a rock bench below the glacier, then continue down heather meadows to Hidden Lake before traversing back to the gully base.
routes.descentText: Descend via Diobsud Creek Glacier in reverse, carefully navigating the same crevassed terrain on descent where conditions may have changed. The steep gully exit at 4,600 ft is particularly exposed and requires careful foot placement. Alternative longer descent: via Green Lake Glacier descending 2,200 feet over roughly 1.5 miles to Bacon Tarns alpine ponds, then continuing through heather meadows to Hidden Lake drainage. This alternate descent adds approximately 3-4 hours but may be safer during unstable snow conditions.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Park at Bacon Creek Road (~800 ft). Hike through forest and climb to 3,000-4,000 ft, establish camp in snowshoe-accessible terrain. 5-7 hours. Day 2: Continue to Diobsud Creek Glacier, traverse to 6,250 ft saddle camp. 3-5 hours. Day 3: Summit push via east slope (1-2 hours), return to camp for descent decision based on conditions. Day 4: Descend via Diobsud Creek Glacier or Green Lake Glacier to lower elevations, exit to trailhead. 5-8 hours depending on descent route.
routes.bail: From Diobsud Creek Glacier saddle camp, retreat via the same gully and forest descent route. If avalanche danger becomes severe in the basin area, consider descending back to camp and exiting via Green Lake Glacier alternate route if conditions permit. Below 4,000 ft terrain flattens and exit is straightforward to road.
routes.turnaround: Turnaround time should be set conservatively at 2 PM from the 6,250 ft saddle camp to ensure safe glacier descent in daylight. Avalanche danger in the basin is highest during afternoon heating. If not on summit by mid-afternoon, retreat to camp and try next morning with fresh snow conditions.
routes.comms: Cell service is not available in the remote Bacon Creek drainage or alpine areas. Carry satellite communication device (modern iPhones with iOS 18 have satellite SOS capability via Globalstar). North Cascades dispatch: (360) 873-4500 ext. 37. Whatcom County Sheriff dispatch: (360) 676-6911 or 911.
proTips: Flag your route through the dense deadfall approach section with visible flagging tape to aid descent navigation
Scout the steep gully at 4,600 ft carefully before committing with a heavy pack; momentum and careful footwork are essential
Camp on the 6,250 ft saddle on the glacier to acclimatize and scout the final summit push in good light
Start summit push before dawn to cross avalanche terrain during frozen conditions and ensure daylight descent
Probe constantly for crevasses even when they appear filled; snow bridges are deceptive
The Green Lake Glacier alternate descent is safer during unstable snow but adds significant time; choose based on conditions and daylight
watchOut: Avalanche hazard is extreme in the 'Amphitheater of a Thousand Avalanches' basin below the gully; multiple slides observed in May-June trip reports
Crevasses on Diobsud Creek Glacier require constant probing; even small cracks can hide dangerous voids
The steep gully (4,600-5,400 ft) has 35+ degree slopes with avalanche potential; cross during stable snow conditions
Extensive deadfall in the lower approach can cause injury and significant time loss; wear sturdy boots and protective gear
Exposure on the southeast ridge and final push to summit is significant with Class 3-4 scrambling terrain
Wet avalanche debris and unconsolidated snow at gully base during warm periods creates difficult, dangerous travel
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain dominates the basin and steep gully sections; timing and weather assessment are critical to safe passage
Crevasse hazard on Diobsud Creek Glacier requires rope team and rescue equipment; the glacier maintains crevasse danger even during early summer
Class 3-4 rock scrambling terrain near summit with potential exposure and loose rock requires careful foot placement
Navigation in whiteout conditions is extremely difficult; GPS and compass essential due to complex terrain and multiple glaciers
Exposure to hypothermia and altitude effects on a ~13,000+ feet roundtrip ascent with forced high camp
Steep gully descent with overloaded pack is physically demanding and error-prone; rest breaks are essential
gear: Helmet (critical for objective hazard and rockfall exposure)
Crevasse rescue kit (pickets, ice screws, pulley system)
Sleeping bag (-10 degree C rated minimum)
First aid kit (extensive for remote location)
routes.detailedRack: Rope system: 50m dynamic rope with backup cord. Crevasse rescue: 3+ ice screws (minimum 18cm), snow pickets or deadman anchors, pulley system, locking carabiners (6+), webbing slings (4+), for Z-pulley or similar mechanical advantage systems. Belay: locking carabiners, dynamic rope anchors. The Diobsud Creek Glacier section typically does not require technical rock gear, but Class 3-4 terrain near summit may benefit from a small rack of passive protection (stoppers, hexes) if rope-protecting scrambling sections.
routes.proNeeds: Comprehensive belay and anchor systems for glacier travel and potential emergency rappels. Full crevasse rescue setup with mechanical advantage is essential for the party to self-rescue. Competency in snow and ice anchor placement, rope team management, and emergency descent techniques is required.
whatToBring: Personal medications and high-altitude medications (Diamox consideration)
Sun protection: sunscreen, goggles, glacier glasses, sun hat
Waterproof and windproof jacket and pants
Gaiters (to keep snow out of boots)
Repair kit: duct tape, nylon, adhesive

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM from saddle camp for summit push

road.name: Bacon Creek Road (NF-1060 to 1064)
road.status: Passable to Road 1064 end; high-clearance vehicle recommended due to potholes and washouts
road.seasonalGate: Open year-round; winter snow may limit access December-March
road.driveNote: Road 1060 requires crossing Steelhead Creek and navigating significant potholes. Road conditions deteriorate after summer rains. High-clearance 4WD vehicle recommended. Park at road end before the major washout area (approximately 800 ft elevation). Road is unmaintained and subject to washouts; check current conditions locally.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades Summit Zone (2100-2200m)
climate.typical: Highly variable Pacific Northwest maritime climate. Expect cool, wet conditions with frequent fog and clouds. Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer. Freezing level typically 11,500-14,750 ft, meaning rain-snow transition zone at 10,000-11,000 ft. Wind 5-20 kph from south to southwest.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack present through May; avalanche hazard high. Late-season snow is wet and dense. Freezing temperatures overnight, thaw during day creating unstable snow conditions. Expect rain-snow mix at trailhead elevations.
climate.summer: Most stable conditions July-August. Daytime temperatures 16-18 degrees C at peak elevation. Light winds (5-15 kph). Occasional drizzle or afternoon showers. Early summer (June) retains avalanche hazard; late summer (September) approaches wetter conditions.
climate.fall: Weather becomes more unsettled September onward. Increasing precipitation frequency. Earlier freezing levels. Shorter daylight hours limiting window for safe travel.
climate.winter: Peak receives heavy snow. Avalanche conditions extreme. Access via Bacon Creek Road may be blocked. Approach not recommended; objective hazard unmanageable.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.closures: Check with North Cascades National Park for current closures; no permanent restrictions on this approach but seasonal trail closures are possible. Park Service may restrict access during extreme avalanche conditions.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park dispatch: (360) 873-4500, ext. 37
emergency.nearestHospital: Sedro-Woolley hospital approximately 90 minutes from trailhead. Concrete has EMTs with 1-hour response; Bellingham has full medical facilities 90+ minutes away.
emergency.notes: Cell service unavailable in remote areas. Carry satellite communication (modern iPhone with iOS 18 satellite SOS, InReach, or similar). Self-rescue capability is essential. Evacuation from high altitude on Bacon Peak requires helicopter response which may be delayed by weather.

routes.corrections: Elevation values in sources vary slightly (7,061-7,070 ft); using Wikipedia NAVD88 value of 7,070 ft as authoritative. Coordinates confirmed consistent across multiple sources: 48 degrees 39 minutes 46 seconds N 121 degrees 31 minutes 14 seconds W. Route name 'Diobsud Creek / Green Lake Glacier' is descriptive of actual glaciers on peak rather than a formal established route name from guidebooks.

objHaz: Avalanche hazard from ski resort blasting
knownHazards: High objective rockfall danger: November 2021 rockfall event destroyed major section of route with 30x40ft section falling off
Traverse pitch now requires 5.9-5.10 downclimbing ~20 feet from old piton with 30+ feet of additional unprotected climbing
Very loose and crumbly rhyolite rock throughout route; poor rock quality except for traversing ledges
Intense afternoon sun exposure on west-facing wall intensifies rockfall danger and afternoon weather deterioration
Avalanche blasting from nearby Mount Snoqualmie ski resort in winter (contact 425-434-7669 for alerts)
proTips: The iconic traverse pitch (5.8) is the crux with good ledges for feet and several old fixed pitons providing protection
Lunch Ledge at ~300 feet provides good rest/belay ledge before committing to upper pitches
Watch for extremely loose sections throughout; much of route is crumbly and unstable
Use slings to extend placements around flakes and poor rock
Start early to finish before afternoon sun intensity and weather changes
November 2021 rockfall significantly damaged the route—many parties now recommend descending via alternatives
watchOut: Rockfall danger is EXTREME and increasing due to November 2021 damage; route condition is marginal and getting worse
Traverse pitch now involves exposed 5.9-5.10 downclimbing with inadequate protection—many consider it not recommended
Loose rock and dirt throughout; expect to encounter unstable blocks and flakes requiring careful footwork
High consequence scrambling on approach and upper pitches
Afternoon wind, sun, and storm development rapid in July-September
routes.overview: A technical alpine rock climb on Guye Peak's prominent west face offering a classic 5.8 traverse pitch with stellar views from Interstate 90. Once popular, the route was heavily damaged by rockfall in November 2021; current conditions are marginal and downclimbing on the traverse is now required.
routes.beta: Start from Alpental parking lot and hike up talus cone via Ober Strasse Road (controversial private access; use new non-established approach avoiding private roads as of 2021). Scramble ~200 feet of loose, mossy, dirty slabs to reach first anchor. Climb 6 pitches of mixed alpine rock through crumbly terrain to Lunch Ledge at ~300 feet. The iconic traverse (pitches 4-5) traverses right on good ledges with old fixed pitons; now requires careful downclimbing due to 2021 damage. Upper pitches climb Class 4 ramp terrain with trees and exposure to summit. Route is sustained and exposed throughout; not a beginner alpine rock climb.
routes.approach: From Alpental Ski Area parking lot (3,120 ft), walk through residential area on Ober Strasse Road to talus cone base beneath west face (note: as of 2021, private road access is contested; climbers must use new non-established approach avoiding Alpental Community Club private roads). Approach time ~30 minutes to 1 hour from parking.
routes.descent: Traverse north over subsummits with climbers' trail; descent varies seasonally. Alternative: rappel from tree anchors on east side of summit to bypass exposed terrain and avoid reverse-traversing dangerous sections. Some descend via Southeast Couloir toward Cave Ridge and Snow Lake trail.
routes.descentText: After summiting via Improbable Traverse, most climbers traverse north over Guye's north and middle summits to descend via established gully systems. A climbers' trail descends from the Guye-Snoqualmie saddle area. For faster/safer descent, fixed anchors (tree with rap tatters) provide rappel opportunity on east side of summit to bypass exposure. Southeast Couloir descent toward Cave Ridge parking is viable alternative.
routes.detailedRack: Mid-size alpine rock rack with 6-8 cams and nuts (mostly cams for Guye's crumbly rock); 60-meter rope minimum (bring two ropes for safe rappel options); full rack of medium to large chocks; several double runners or slings for extending placements around flakes; helmet (mandatory); climbing shoes; approach shoes or boots.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is generally intermittent and poor-quality due to loose flakes and crumbly rock. Many dirt-filled cracks yield marginal placements. Several old fixed pitons remain on the traverse pitch itself. Plan for lots of sling extensions around features rather than clean gear placements. Tree anchors available for rappel.
whatToBring: Water (minimum 2-3L due to altitude and exposure)
Headlamp (long approach in summer daylight, but plan for changes)

pitchDetail.notes: Initial low to mid-5th class climbing through loose, crumbly rhyolite terrain; lots of holds but quality poor and exposed
Continuation of crumbly terrain leading toward Lunch Ledge; climbers should carefully test all holds
Final approach to Lunch Ledge (~300 ft from start); large comfortable belay ledge provides good rest point before upper pitches

The iconic Improbable Traverse pitch(es): traverse right on good foot ledges with several old fixed pitons for protection. CRITICAL: November 2021 rockfall destroyed ~30x40ft section; now requires downclimbing 5.9-5.10 from old pin with 30+ feet of unprotected traversing. Many now recommend this route is not safe.
Class 4 ramp climbing through alpine trees and rock slabs to summit ridge approach; short but exposed

routes.bestSeason: June through October offer best alpine rock climbing conditions at Guye Peak. July-September provide most stable weather and driest conditions. Avoid winter due to avalanche danger, heavy snow, and rockfall hazards. Spring (April-May) has variable conditions with lingering snow.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (6-7am) to finish descent before afternoon weather deterioration

routes.comms: Cell phone service generally available at Alpental parking area (AT&T, Verizon coverage). No reliable service on route itself. Contact Mount Snoqualmie ski resort at 425-434-7669 for avalanche conditions and ski resort operations status.

waypoints.name: Lunch Ledge belay (~300 ft on route)

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, U.S. Forest Service
access.fees: Parking fee varies by season: Northwest Forest Pass ($30 annual or $5 day pass) required summer season. Winter season (Nov 25 - Apr 30): $55 daily parking at Alpental. Snoqualmie season pass holders park free in winter.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent day pass
access.closures: Private road access via Alpental Community Club roads restricted as of 2021; use established alternatives

road.name: Alpental Road (WA-906)
road.status: Open year-round
road.seasonalGate: Alpental ski area operations drive road use; check ski resort schedule for winter closures
road.driveNote: From Seattle: Take I-90 eastbound to Exit 52 at Snoqualmie Pass (elevation ~2,318 ft). Turn northwest onto Alpental Road (WA-906) and drive to parking area at summit (elevation 3,120 ft). Approximately 1 hour from Seattle. Winter conditions may affect access.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center - Snoqualmie Pass/Central Cascades Zone
climate.typical: Cool, wet maritime climate typical of Cascade Range. Temperatures range 40-70F in summer. Rapid weather changes common; afternoon thunderstorms and wind typical July-September.
climate.spring: Variable conditions April-May; lingering snow and wet conditions; avalanche danger moderate to high; transitions from winter to summer conditions unpredictably
climate.summer: Best climbing season. June-September: temperatures 50-70F daytime, 40-50F early morning/evening. Morning clear conditions typical, afternoon clouds/wind/thunderstorms common. Mossy/wet rock early season. Driest July-August.
climate.fall: September offers continued good conditions but increasing cloud cover and weather instability; snow possible by late September at higher elevations
climate.winter: November-March: Heavy snow, avalanche danger extreme, ice/mixed terrain. Avalanche blasting from ski resort. Temperatures 20-40F. Popular for winter mountaineering but very serious objective hazards.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for life-threatening emergencies; King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: (206) 296-3311
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District (North Bend Office), USFS: 902 S.E. North Bend Way, North Bend, WA 98045; (425) 888-1421. Hours: Mon, Tues, Thurs 9am-3pm (closed Wed, Fri, Sat, Sun). Snoqualmie Pass Visitor Center: (425) 434-6111
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital: 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA 98065; (425) 831-2300 (24-hour emergency). Level 5 Trauma Center, Level 2 Cardiac, Level 3 Stroke facility. Helicopter evacuation available.
emergency.notes: Alpental area is close to Snoqualmie Pass; emergency response times generally 30-60 minutes depending on conditions. Winter avalanche control and ski patrol activity may impact response capabilities. Contact Mount Snoqualmie ski resort for avalanche/weather information: 425-434-7669.

routes.corrections: Original dataset listed: rockGrade 5.6 (should be 5.8), alpineGrade II (should be III), pitches 5 (should be 6), routeFt 600 (should be 800). Authoritative sources (Mountain Project, Mount Stuart Climb Project, trip reports on CascadeClimbers.com and NWHikers) confirm correct grades are 5.8 YDS, Grade III overall, 6 pitches, ~800 feet.

knownHazards: Objective rockfall danger from loose, crumbly rhyolite throughout
Poor rock quality with many loose blocks and flakes
High consequence exposure on upper sections with significant fall potential
Afternoon sun exposure and rapid weather deterioration common
Private road access issues as of 2021 (must use alternate approach)
proTips: Large ramp provides good foot ledges and steady climbing angle
Trees in upper sections provide natural protection and psychological holds
Crux crack section (30 feet of low 5th class) is the most technical; take time here
Class 4 scrambling sections require careful footwork on loose rock
Many old pitons and gear placements available for protection
Start early to finish in daylight
watchOut: Rock quality is poor and loose throughout; all holds must be tested
High consequence exposure with little protection in some sections
Loose blocks and flakes common; watch for rockfall from above
Not recommended for climbers uncomfortable with serious scrambling
Weather changes rapidly; be prepared to descend
routes.overview: A moderate alpine rock climb featuring a rising traverse across Guye Peak's prominent west face visible from Interstate 90. The crux involves a steep crack section (low 5th class) that transitions to Class 4 scrambling through alpine trees. Popular with intermediate alpine climbers for its direct approach and good views.
routes.beta: From Alpental parking, make a rising traverse up talus to the far left (north) side of the west face (note private road access restrictions as of 2021). Ascend a very large ramp that traverses the entire face from north to south. The climbing starts up a steep crack/face (crux) for approximately 30 feet of low 5th class, then transitions to Class 4 for about half a rope length through rocks and alpine trees with exposure. Climbing continues via mixed scrambling and rock sections to the summit. Route involves significant high-consequence scrambling.
routes.approach: From Alpental Ski Area parking lot (3,120 ft), make a rising traverse up talus to the far left (north) side of west face. Note: As of 2021, private road access via Alpental Community Club is restricted; use established alternate approaches that avoid private roads.
routes.descent: Traverse north over Guye's subsummits to established gully/trail systems. Alternative: descend via Southeast Couloir toward Cave Ridge and Snow Lake trailhead area.
routes.descentText: Multiple descent options available. Most common: traverse north over Guye's north and middle summits following climbers' trails to gully systems descending toward Cave Ridge saddle area and Snow Lake trailhead. Southeast Couloir provides alternative descent toward Snow Lake parking. All descents involve significant scrambling; move carefully and test all holds.
routes.detailedRack: Standard rock equipment and alpine rack with medium chocks and cams. Bring a full range of gear including nuts, cams, and slings for extending placements in loose rock.
routes.proNeeds: Rock quality is generally poor with many loose features. Protect carefully using trees, chocks, and cams wherever possible. Plan to use slings extensively for extending placements.
routes.bestSeason: June through October offer best conditions. July-September most stable. Avoid winter due to avalanche danger, snow, and rockfall.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (6-7am)

routes.comms: Cell service at Alpental parking area. No reliable service on route.

access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or day pass
access.closures: Private road access issues as of 2021

climate.typical: Cool, wet maritime climate; temps 40-70F summer; rapid weather changes; afternoon thunderstorms July-September

emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital: (425) 831-2300 (24hr, Level 5 Trauma)
emergency.notes: Response times 30-60 minutes. Helicopter evacuation available.

routes.corrections: CRITICAL: Route 'North Rib' cannot be verified in any primary or secondary climbing sources after exhaustive research of Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, The Mountaineers, Washington Trails Association, CascadeClimbers.com, and multiple trip report databases. All documented technical rock climbing routes on Guye Peak are: (1) Improbable Traverse (5.8, Grade III), (2) West Face (5.0-5.4, Grade II-III), (3) South Rib (5.6, Grade II), (4) South Gully/South Spur (snow/ice), (5) Southeast Gully (winter), and (6) North Ridge/North Route (Class 3/4 scramble, not technical rock climb). The 'North Rib' may be a historical/obsolete route name, local variant name, or data entry error. Recommend verifying route name against original source data.

blurb: Guye Peak, located at 5,168 feet in Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Snoqualmie Pass, is a highly fractured rhyolite mountain offering popular alpine rock climbs visible from Interstate 90. Named after Francis M. Guye's 1880s iron mining claim, the peak was first ascended October 6, 1912 by Charles Hazlehurst, Hector Abel, Paul Dubuar, and Mary Hard. The peak features sustained technical climbing on its west face (the iconic Improbable Traverse, 5.8, Grade III) alongside moderate alpine rock options, with excellent summer conditions June through October but serious winter avalanche and rockfall hazards.
hierarchyNote: CRITICAL DATA ISSUE: The 'North Rib' route (wa_guye_peak_r2) listed in provided data cannot be verified in any authoritative climbing sources despite exhaustive research across Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, The Mountaineers, CascadeClimbers.com, WTA, and regional guidebooks. All documented technical routes on Guye Peak are Improbable Traverse, West Face, South Rib, South Gully/South Spur (variants), Southeast Gully, and North Ridge (Class 3/4 scramble only). The 'North Rib' may be historical/obsolete nomenclature, a local variant name, or a data entry error. Recommend verification against original source. GRADE CORRECTIONS: Improbable Traverse data in original dataset (5.6 YDS, Grade II, 5 pitches, 600 ft) conflicts with authoritative sources (confirmed: 5.8 YDS, Grade III, 6 pitches, ~800 ft). Elevation in original data (5,169 ft) aligns well with Wikipedia/Mountain Project (5,168 ft)—negligible difference. Peak is located in Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Cascade Range, King County, WA (47.4415°N, 121.4093°W).

blurb: Hozomeen Mountain is a double-summited rock peak (North Peak 8,071 ft) on the east side of Ross Lake in the North Cascades of Washington, characterized by steep faces, loose friable rock, and dramatic exposure. Named from Salish meaning "sharp like a sharp knife," it was first climbed September 6, 1904 by Sledge Tatum and George E. Loudon Jr. during boundary survey work.

routes.overview: A mixed alpine route on the eastern aspect of North Peak involving snow/ice and Class 3-4 rock scrambling to reach the broad summit. This lower-angle approach is less committing than technical face climbs on the South Peak.
routes.beta: The Southeast Face offers a third-class approach to the summit, though grading varies by route variant and seasonal conditions. Most parties traverse the northeast ridge where it transitions to mixed terrain. Previous climbing experience on exposed, loose rock is essential.
routes.approach: From Hozomeen Ranger Station (1,600 ft), cross-country traverse west through forest on the western flank, targeting heathery knolls at 5,900 ft. Alternatively, from Skyline II Trailhead in BC (1,800 ft), hike 8 miles on Skyline II Trail and Hozomeen Ridge Trail gaining 4,500 ft to camp at 6,350 ft ridge, then approach southeast face from there. The northern Skyline II approach is longer (24.7 mi round trip) but avoids extensive forest travel. Border crossing required.
routes.descent: Descend via the climbing route, reversing exposed Class 4 sections carefully. Route-finding can be challenging due to loose rock and eroded sections. Estimated descent time 3-4 hours depending on party size and experience.
routes.descentText: Descend the same route, paying careful attention to loose rock and eroded sections. A short but very exposed Class 4 scramble near mid-descent requires careful footwork and should be reversed in controlled, methodical fashion. Class 3 terrain below the ledge is generally straightforward but still requires caution on loose, down-sloping rock.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with July and August offering the most stable conditions and minimal snow. June may have significant snow on upper reaches requiring crampons and ice axe. September weather becomes increasingly unstable.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Skyline II Trailhead (34 miles from Hope, BC) via Skyline II and Hozomeen Ridge trails to ridge camp at 6,350 ft (8 miles, ~4 hours). Day 2: Ascend from camp to Southeast Face base, climb face to summit, reverse to camp (6-8 hours technical time). Day 3: Return via same trail to trailhead (8 miles, ~4 hours).
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or technical sections prove beyond party capability, descend back toward camp via well-defined ridge. Do not attempt difficult terrain in poor visibility. Retreat is straightforward until committed to technical sections; always maintain escape routes via reverse ascent.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if: conditions deteriorate to poor visibility, weather shows threatening signs (darkening clouds, lightning), party is moving slower than planned indicating fatigue, or snow/ice conditions require ongoing step-kicking above 7,500 ft. Allow at least 4 hours descent time and establish a firm turn-around time before committing to upper terrain.
routes.comms: No cell phone reception at Hozomeen Mountain or surrounding backcountry. Satellite communication (Garmin InReach, SPOT beacon) recommended for emergency contact. Remote location near Canadian border adds communication/evacuation delays.
proTips: Wear a helmet throughout due to loose, friable rock and rockfall hazard
Establish camp near 6,350 ft to minimize daily elevation gain and allow early start
Scout approach to Southeast Face base day before climbing to optimize route-finding
Early start (dawn) critical for weather avoidance and to clear exposure before afternoon conditions deteriorate
Manage timing to avoid night descent on loose rock
watchOut: Loose, friable rock throughout the route with poor quality, unreliable holds
Eroded Class 4 ledge near mid-route can be wet and slippery requiring careful footwork
Rockfall hazard from other climbing parties above; stay alert and move efficiently through exposed terrain
Exposure on northeast ridge with several Class 4 moves on sharp, down-sloping holds where falling is not an option
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; afternoon thunderstorms common in summer
knownHazards: Friable, decomposing rock throughout approach and climb requiring hardhat use
Eroded sections on northeast ridge with loose, wet, down-sloping holds
Rockfall potential from other climbing parties or loose talus fields
Steep snowfields in early season (June) on upper reaches requiring crampons
Exposure on narrow ridges with minimal protection for falls
routes.fa: September 6, 1904 (Sledge Tatum and George E. Loudon Jr., Boundary Survey)
routes.detailedRack: Lightweight rack: cordelette or 5.0mm dynamic rope for simul-belaying exposed Class 3-4 sections; 12-15 quickdraws; selection of wired nuts (sizes 0.4-1.5); 3-4 cams (0.75-2 inch); 2-3 slings for natural anchors. Most climbers move together on Class 3 terrain without rope. Helmets mandatory due to rockfall.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is sporadic and poor quality; most climbing is protected by running belays or simul-climbing. Expect 'ornamental gear' quality with few solid placements. Confidence in third-class scrambling and ability to place protection on demand necessary.
whatToBring: fleece jacket, rain jacket, gloves, beanie
map and compass or GPS device

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: $10/person + $6 fee (summer, May-October)
access.fees: Backcountry permit required for overnight stays
access.closures: Gate at Hozomeen border crossing on US/Canada boundary closed as of Feb 2025; use designated ports of entry or alternative access via East Bank Trail (31 mi) or Ross Lake water taxi

road.name: Silver-Skagit Road / BC Forest Service Road
road.status: Seasonal; passable May-October, winter closures possible
road.seasonalGate: Winter gate closures possible; confirm road access before traveling
road.driveNote: 34 miles on Silver-Skagit Road from Hope, BC to Skyline II Trailhead. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Alternative: East Bank Trail from SR 20 (31 miles one way) or water taxi via Ross Lake Resort

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Mount Baker zone
climate.typical: High alpine; cold, wet; frequent precipitation. Summer avg high 50-60F, low 35-45F. Evening thunderstorms common.
climate.spring: May: Heavy snow above 6,500 ft; avalanche danger. Late May viable with crampons.
climate.summer: Jun-Aug: Snow recedes mid-late June. July-August most stable but afternoon T-storms frequent. Aug-Sep: Mosquitoes peak.
climate.fall: Sep: Early season storms; frost possible. October: Likely snow above 7,000 ft.
climate.winter: Nov-April: Extensive snow and ice; not recommended except for experienced winter mountaineers

emergency.sheriffDispatch: (360) 360-428-3211 (non-emergency); Emergency: 911. Skagit 911 dispatch serves eastern area.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Visitor Center (~60 miles south); Hozomeen Campground ranger station (access limited)
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley WA (~50 miles); PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham WA (~70 miles)
emergency.notes: Extremely remote; helicopter evacuation costly and weather-dependent. Excellent fitness, navigation, and self-rescue capability essential. No cell service; satellite communication strongly recommended.

routes.corrections: DATA DISCREPANCY: The database lists this route as 'Southeast Face (Standard)' with grade 'Class 4 / low 5th'. Multiple authoritative sources consistently describe the Southeast Face of North Peak as Class 3 (straightforward third-class scramble). The standard route to North Peak is typically the Northeast Buttress/Northeast Ridge (Class 3-4). The Class 4 section is an eroded ledge feature on the northeast approach that varies by conditions. The peak first ascent (1904, Sledge Tatum and George E. Loudon Jr.) was via the Northeast route, not Southeast Face. This grade and route name combination warrants verification against the original source data.

blurb: Klawatti Peak (8,485 ft) is a nunatak surrounded by glaciers in North Cascades National Park, composed of Eldorado Orthogneiss. The peak was first ascended on July 7, 1940 by Lloyd Anderson, Karl Boyer, and Tom Gorton. Its name is a misspelling of the Chinook word for swan. Located in the heart of the North Cascades, Klawatti is surrounded by the Klawatti Glacier to the east, McAllister Glacier to the northwest, and Inspiration Glacier to the south.

routes.fa: Lloyd Anderson, Karl Boyer, Tom Gorton - July 7, 1940
objHaz: Loose rock requiring care in holds
routes.overview: Grade III alpine rock and snow climb on the southeast aspect of Klawatti Peak via Klawatti Glacier. This route combines glacier travel with technical mixed rock and ice climbing featuring 2 pitches of low 5th class rock and steep snow sections.
routes.beta: Grade III alpine route requiring mixed climbing technique. Approach via Klawatti Glacier from the west with 2 pitches of Class 4-5 rock climbing and 45-50 degree snow slopes. Lower sections feature Class 4 rock; upper sections Class 3 scrambling. Crampons, ice axe, pickets, 60m rope, and single rack to 2 inch are essential. Allow 1-2 hours for climbing depending on amount of roped climbing.
routes.approach: From Marblemount on SR-20, drive 19 miles east on Cascade River Road to parking lot at 2,150 ft elevation. Cross Cascade River following climber trail (look for flagging near three trees or large log crossing river) to Eldorado Creek trail. Ascend forested trail and steep talus to reach Eldorado Glacier. Climb eastern fringe of Eldorado Glacier approximately 1,300 ft to snow dome immediately south-southeast of Eldorado Peak (approximately 7,500 ft). Traverse north from the dome across glacier to access Klawatti Glacier field. Establish high camp on rocks near Klawatti Col (7,800 ft) on peak's west side before accessing the southeast face.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route, down-climbing the 2-3 low 5th class rock pitches with extreme care to test all holds due to frequent loose rock. Descend steeper snow sections in controlled manner using crampons and ice axe for security. Traverse back south through Klawatti Glacier to return to high camp, then retrace approach to base camp.
routes.descentText: Carefully down-climb the 2-3 low 5th class rock pitches, testing every hold as the rock is often loose. Move deliberately and place protection if needed. Descend the steeper snow slopes (45-50 degrees) in a controlled manner, using crampons and ice axe for security. Reverse the exposed traverses and gully sections on the way down. Descend the Klawatti Glacier using standard crevasse avoidance and rope team procedures if snow bridges are questionable. Pace descent to avoid fatigue.
routes.bail: From Klawatti Col, descend southwest back down to the glacier camp to retrace descent route. If conditions deteriorate while ascending the rocks, descend back to col and camp.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time based on sunset time (June has 14-15 hour days; September has 11-12 hour days) and headlamp availability. Plan to be descending upper rock pitches by mid-afternoon to ensure safe conditions and daylight for glacier descent.
