routes.bestSeason: July through September offer optimal climbing conditions. Early season (July) provides longer daylight and more stable snow conditions on glaciers. Late season (September) has shorter daylight but potentially better rock conditions as ice retreats. Avoid winter and spring due to excessive snow, crevasse hazards, and avalanche risk. The marine west coast climate brings frequent weather fronts; storms can develop quickly.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the North Cascades backcountry; carry satellite communication device (Garmin InReach or PLB) for emergencies. Northwest Cascades region is served by NWAC avalanche forecasts.
routes.detailedRack: Single set of rock protection to 2 inches: several pieces in 0.5-1.5 inch range for small cracks, a few pieces 1.5-2 inch. Alpine slings and cordelettes for anchor building. 60m dynamic rope (consider 70m for rappels). Crampons (modern hybrid or strap-on), ice axe (70-80cm for mixed terrain), 2-3 pickets for snow/ice anchors.
routes.proNeeds: This is a mixed alpine rock and ice climb requiring proficiency in glacier travel, rope team management, rock scrambling up to 5.2, and winter/alpine skills. Climbers should be comfortable with crevasse rescue and have experience identifying safe glacier routes.
gear: Rock climbing rack (single set to 2 inch)
proTips: Test rock holds extensively during ascent as loose rock is common, especially on the lower pitches
Start early to maximize daylight for complex terrain
Establish camp on rocks near Klawatti Col rather than on exposed glacial areas
Monitor weather carefully; storms develop rapidly in the marine west coast climate
Use a systematic glacier travel rope team approach to manage crevasse risk efficiently
watchOut: Loose rock throughout route; test every hold carefully
Hidden crevasses can be covered by thin snow bridges; probe ahead with ice axe
Steep south-facing snow slopes (45-50 degrees) can be hazardous; avoid climbing in heat of day
Exposure on narrow ridge sections requires careful footwork and focus
knownHazards: Seasonal crevasse routefinding varies significantly throughout the climbing season
Steep south-facing snow slopes become unstable in afternoon heat and are prone to sloughing
Loose rock throughout the route, especially on lower sections, requires careful hold testing
Exposure on scrambling sections with significant cliff drops nearby

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.fees: Backcountry permit $10 per person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee
access.permit: North Cascades backcountry permit required; 60% available for advance reservation (minimum 2 days ahead), 40% first-come first-served (1 day ahead)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass recommended for parking

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open seasonally; last 10 miles gravel, washes out periodically
road.seasonalGate: Typically open June-October; verify current conditions at North Cascades National Park
road.driveNote: From I-5 near Mount Vernon, take SR-20 east to Marblemount, then right on Cascade River Road for 19 miles to parking lot. Last 10 miles are rough gravel; high-clearance vehicle recommended after wet weather.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Cascades (NWAC Cascade West South zone)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation, especially winter months. Summer brings fewer storms but weather can change rapidly.
climate.spring: Still heavy snowpack at elevation; frequent storms and avalanche danger; not recommended for climbing
climate.summer: July-August optimal for climbing with longer daylight (14-15 hours), warmer temperatures, and more stable conditions. Still cold at 8,000+ ft; expect freezing at night.
climate.fall: September climbing possible with 11-12 hour days; earlier storms and shorter weather windows. Increasingly unstable climbing conditions.
climate.winter: Excessive snowpack, crevasse hazards, avalanche risk, and limited daylight make winter climbing not recommended

timing.recommendedStart: 2:00 AM from base camp to reach upper pitches during optimal conditions; adjust based on season daylight (earlier in July, later in September)

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness District, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245; email noca_wilderness@nps.gov
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III ED), 300 Hospital Parkway, Mount Vernon, WA 98274; approximately 45 miles south via SR-20 and I-5
emergency.notes: Satellite communication device (Garmin InReach, PLB) essential for backcountry emergencies. Helicopter rescue is possible but weather-dependent and extremely costly. Cell coverage unreliable at higher elevations.

routes.fa: 1920 by Fairman B. Lee and party of 13
routes.overview: The standard mountaineering route on Mount Anderson's East Peak, ascending via Anderson Glacier and Flypaper Pass to access Eel Glacier, then traversing and scrambling to summit with Class 3 rock and moderate glacier travel.
routes.beta: Grade I alpine climb requiring glacier travel experience, crampons, ice axe, rope, and proper avalanche awareness. Spring conditions ideal. Anderson Glacier has minimal crevasse hazard due to 50 percent recession since 1920. Late-season bergschrund can be difficult. Olympic rock is notably loose and crumbly, requiring careful footwork. Flypaper Pass snow finger runs about 30 degrees, 300 vertical feet.
routes.approach: From Brinnon on US-101, take Dosewallips River Road 8.5 miles (closed to vehicles since 2002). Hike or bike 5 miles on closed road to Dosewallips Ranger Station (1,611 ft). Hike Dosewallips Trail 1.4 mi to junction with West Fork Dosewallips Trail. Take West Fork Trail 8 miles to Honeymoon Meadows camp (3,527 ft), crossing river ford. Continue 1.2 miles to Anderson Pass (4,464 ft). From pass, ascend moraine to glacier at 5,200 ft. Rope up and traverse right side of Anderson Glacier to steep snow finger below Flypaper Pass (6,500 ft). Ascend 30-degree snow finger 300 ft to Flypaper Pass saddle.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent route. Descend snow finger and traverse back across Anderson Glacier and moraine. Return via Anderson Pass to West Fork Dosewallips Trail and Dosewallips Road. Total descent from summit 5-7 hours.
routes.descentText: From summit, descend Class 3 rock and snow south to saddle north of summit. Traverse northwest across upper Eel Glacier to Flypaper Pass saddle, descending the steep 30-degree snow finger carefully (300 vertical feet). Continue across moraine and traverse back to Anderson Glacier. Descend to moraine at 5,200 ft and retrace the glacier approach to Anderson Pass. Return via West Fork Dosewallips Trail to Honeymoon Meadows (ford river crossing), then retrace to Dosewallips Ranger Station and bike/hike back down Dosewallips Road.
routes.turnaround: If conditions deteriorate at Flypaper Pass with loss of snow coverage, turn around. If bergschrund at glacier head becomes impassable, retreat. Afternoon deterioration, clouds obscuring route, or unstable snow require retreat.
routes.bestSeason: May through June optimal for stable freeze-thaw snow and passable Flypaper Pass snow finger. July-August feasible but bergschrund issues and rock exposure increase. June has proven most reliable.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike/bike 5 miles on Dosewallips Road, then hike 10+ miles to Honeymoon Meadows camp (7-9 hours total). Day 2: Hike 3+ miles to Anderson Pass and camp on moraine (3-4 hours). Day 3: Pre-dawn start, summit attempt via Flypaper Pass and Eel Glacier (5-7 hours to summit, 5-7 hours descent). Day 4: Descend to base camp. Day 5: Return to trailhead (8-9 hours including bike section).
routes.proNeeds: Rope and harness recommended though glacier is low-risk for crevasses due to heavy recession. Self-rescue skills desirable.
routes.detailedRack: Ice axe (one per person, 45-55cm), crampons with good ankle support, helmet, rope (30-50m for team), harness, belay device, 2-3 quickdraws, climbing helmet, 2+ snow pickets or ice screws for glacier anchors. Consider lighter approach boots.
proTips: Start the climb pre-dawn to gain maximum daylight for descent
Establish fixed ropes on Flypaper Pass snow finger if party size warrants it
Scout camp location below moraine to ensure snowmelt access for water
May offers best freeze-thaw cycle; start early season climbs before June 15
Watch for cornice development on ridge above Flypaper Pass
The loose Olympic rock requires more careful footwork than typical alpine scrambles
Eel Glacier retreat has created new rocky terrain; verify current conditions with recent trip reports
watchOut: Loose, crumbly Olympic rock creates rockfall hazard especially in afternoon
Flypaper Pass snow finger becomes steeper than guidebook elevations suggest (40+ degrees reported)
Late-season bergschrund at glacier head becomes impassable without cutting
Wet loose avalanches common in spring thaw conditions
Sun exposure on south slopes of peak accelerates softening and increases scree instability
Afternoon sun softens glacier making crevasse discovery more likely
knownHazards: Objective hazard: Loose, fractured meta-sedimentary rock prone to rockfall, especially in spring thaw and afternoon sun
Steep snow finger at Flypaper Pass averages 30 degrees, can exceed 40 degrees, avalanche risk if unstable snow layer present
Eel Glacier subject to calving and rockfall from surrounding peaks; north-facing aspect provides some mitigation
Wet loose avalanche activity typical in May-June melt cycle
Bergschrund at glacier head becomes prominent late season, difficult or impossible to cross

pitchDetail.notes: 5 miles on closed road from parking to Ranger Station (1,611 ft). Road in good hiking condition despite washouts further up.

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach 2: Dosewallips River Trail to West Fork junction
pitchDetail.notes: 1.4 miles of trail through old-growth forest alongside river to junction (1,750 ft). Well-maintained tread.

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach 3: West Fork Dosewallips River Trail to Honeymoon Meadows
pitchDetail.notes: 8 miles through montane forest with elevation gain to Honeymoon Meadows camp (3,527 ft). Includes river ford. Trail moderately steep in upper sections.

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach 4: Honeymoon Meadows to Anderson Pass
pitchDetail.notes: 1.2 miles with 937 ft of gain to Anderson Pass (4,464 ft). Trail becomes steeper and more exposed, gaining alpine meadows.

pitchDetail.pitch: Glacier traverse: Anderson Glacier moraine to Flypaper Pass
pitchDetail.notes: From Anderson Pass, ascend moraine to glacier at 5,200 ft. Rope up and traverse right side of Anderson Glacier to Flypaper Pass (6,500 ft). Main feature: 30-degree snow finger (300 vertical ft).

pitchDetail.pitch: Upper Eel Glacier to summit ridge
pitchDetail.notes: From Flypaper Pass, drop 200 ft onto Eel Glacier, traverse north around rock buttress, climb east on upper glacier to saddle north of summit (7,200 ft).
Final 121 ft to summit via loose meta-sedimentary rock and snow patches. Exposed scramble with poor quality holds. Rock improves near summit block.

waypoints.name: Moraine camp (high camp), upper Anderson Glacier

road.name: Dosewallips River Road
road.status: Closed to vehicles since 2002, open to foot and bike traffic
road.seasonalGate: Road blocked by washout; accessible year-round on foot/bike but winter snow/flooding may impede access
road.driveNote: Drive US-101 south 11 miles from Quilcene to Brinnon, turn onto Dosewallips River Road, drive 8.5 miles and park (road washed out further up). Road washout adds ~10 miles to approach.

climate.forecastZone: Olympic Mountains Mount Anderson marine west coast
climate.typical: Cool, wet maritime climate dominated by Pacific weather systems. Significant precipitation year-round, especially winter/spring snowfall.
climate.spring: May-June: Freeze-thaw cycles create stable snow for climbing. Afternoon warming, rapid runoff, potential wet loose avalanches. Days 10-14 hours.
climate.summer: July-August: Warmer days (40-55F), cooler nights. Snow melting rapidly, glacier retreat, bergschrund exposure increases. Lower elevation sections snow-free.
climate.fall: September-October: Shortening days, increasing cloud and precipitation. First snow at altitude by late September. Increasingly unstable travel conditions.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy precipitation, deep snowpack at altitude. Road and lower trails often impassable or very difficult. Not recommended for this route.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park / Olympic National Forest
access.fees: No entrance fee for day hikes to ranger station. Wilderness permit required: $6 permit fee plus $8 per night backcountry camping fee.
access.permit: Wilderness permit self-issued at Dosewallips Ranger Station, no advance reservation required (first-come, first-served).
access.closures: Dosewallips Road closed to vehicles since 2002 (washout). Road accessible via foot/bike. Winter conditions may block trail sections. Check National Park Service conditions before departure.

emergency.rangerStation: Dosewallips Ranger Station (decommissioned but available); Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center: (360) 565-3100
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson Healthcare, Port Townsend, WA (24-hr Emergency Department): (360) 385-2200, approximately 35 miles from trailhead
emergency.notes: Cell service limited or absent in wilderness area. Satellite communicator or satellite phone strongly recommended. Most rescues require multi-day response time due to remote location. Party size of 4+ recommended.

routes.comms: No cell service in wilderness area. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, SPOT) strongly recommended for emergency communication. Park rangers patrol via helicopter only in summer season.
routes.corrections: Mount Anderson is located in the Olympic Mountains, Olympic National Park, NOT in the North Cascades as database hierarchy initially suggested. This is a major geographic distinction affecting regional resource, weather, and emergency response considerations.

hierarchyNote: CRITICAL: Mount Anderson (7,321 ft elevation) is located in the Olympic Mountains, Olympic National Park, Jefferson County, Washington. It is NOT in the North Cascades/Cascade Range as initial database context suggested. Mount Anderson sits at the hydrographic center of the Olympic Peninsula where waters flow to three distinct drainages (Dosewallips River to Puget Sound, Quinault River to Pacific Ocean, and Elwha River to Strait of Juan de Fuca). This geographic distinction is critical for regional resource management, weather forecasting, and emergency response planning. Parent hierarchy should be: World > North America > United States > Washington > Olympic Mountains > Anderson Massif (not Cascades)."

blurb: Mount St. Helens is an active stratovolcano in Skamania County, Washington's Cascade Range, standing at 8,363 feet after the cataclysmic 1980 eruption reduced it from 9,677 feet. Composed of volcanic dacite and andesite, the mountain features a distinctive horseshoe-shaped crater and is climbed via Monitor Ridge in summer and Worm Flows in winter, making it one of the Pacific Northwest's most popular alpine objectives.

routes.overview: The most popular summer route to Mount St. Helens' summit, Monitor Ridge is a non-technical scramble gaining 4,500 feet over 5 miles. Named for weather monitoring instruments positioned along the route, this route follows the Ptarmigan Trail through forest to timberline, then ascends the rocky Monitor Ridge via marked wooden posts through loose pumice and ash to the crater rim.
routes.beta: Begin at Climbers' Bivouac (3,700 ft) on Ptarmigan Trail #216A. Hike 2.1 miles through forest and meadows to the Loowit Trail junction at approximately 4,800 feet, gaining 1,000 feet. Above timberline, follow wooden cairn markers up rocky Monitor Ridge through boulder fields covered in abrasive ash pumice. The final section features sandy terrain nicknamed the 'Vertical Beach' before reaching the crater rim at 8,363 feet.
routes.approach: From I-5 exit 21 near Woodland, WA, drive to Cougar. Continue east on Lewis River Road (Forest Road 90, paved) just 1 mile beyond Swift Dam, then turn left (north) on FR 83 (gravel). Drive on FR 83 to the junction with FR 81, turn left for 1.6 miles, then turn right on FR 830. Follow FR 830 (2.7 miles of winding gravel road) to Climbers' Bivouac parking lot at 3,700 feet elevation. The trailhead has composting toilets and ample parking. From the parking area, take Ptarmigan Trail #216A north, climbing 1,000 feet over 2.1 miles through forest and meadows to reach the Loowit Trail junction at timberline around 4,800 feet elevation.
routes.descent: Return via Monitor Ridge, retracing the same route down to Climbers' Bivouac.
routes.descentText: Descend Monitor Ridge, retracing the ascent route. From the crater rim, head southeast off the ridge and work carefully down through the ash and pumice fields. Watch for the wooden posts marking the descent route (they become more sparse lower on the ridge). Continue down through the boulder fields, being cautious of the abrasive ash pumice which can cause skin injury in a fall. At approximately 4,800 feet, rejoin the Loowit Trail and return via Ptarmigan Trail #216A through the forest back to Climbers' Bivouac.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Start at Climbers' Bivouac parking (3,700 ft). Ascend Ptarmigan Trail 2.1 miles to Loowit Trail junction (4,800 ft, ~1 hour). Continue up Monitor Ridge through boulder fields and ash terrain (3-5 hours). Reach crater rim summit (8,363 ft). Descend via same route (3-4 hours). Total round trip 7-12 hours depending on fitness and conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through September, with optimal conditions June through August. Permits are required April 1 through October 31 and must be obtained in advance via recreation.gov. Winter climbing (November 1–March 31) is possible but requires crampons, ice axe, and experience; use the Worm Flows route instead for winter. Snow typically clears the access road by early to mid-July.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or parties are moving slowly, turnaround at the Loowit Trail junction (4,800 ft) or at any point. Descend immediately if lightning threatens.
routes.turnaround: Standard turnaround is reaching the crater rim at 8,363 feet. Do not attempt to enter or traverse the crater itself; the rim is extremely unstable with frequent rockfall. Allow 7-12 hours total for the round trip depending on fitness and trail conditions.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is required. This is a non-technical scramble suitable for hikers in good physical condition. No ropes, belay devices, or rock climbing gear are necessary.
whatToBring: Two to three liters of water

pitchDetail.notes: Forest trail gaining 1,000 feet through mixed forest and open meadows to timberline at Loowit Trail junction (4,800 ft). Well-maintained trail with minimal scrambling.
Transition zone from forest to open ridge. Scattered rocks and loose material. Wooden posts mark the route.
Main ridge ascent through boulder fields covered in ash and pumice. Loose, abrasive material requires caution. Wooden posts guide the route to approximately 7,000 feet elevation.

pitchDetail.pitch: Upper Monitor Ridge to Crater Rim (1.0 mile)
pitchDetail.notes: Final section through sand and small rocks nicknamed 'Vertical Beach.' Very steep loose terrain; feels like two steps forward, one step back. Final push to 8,363 feet crater rim.

road.name: Lewis River Road / Forest Road 90 + Forest Road 83 + Forest Road 830
road.status: Open (seasonal closure possible in winter)
road.seasonalGate: April 1 - October 31 permit season; winter access via Worm Flows route
road.driveNote: From Cougar, drive east on Lewis River Road (paved) which becomes Forest Road 90. Turn left onto FR 83 (gravel) 1 mile beyond Swift Dam. Continue on FR 83 to FR 81 junction, turn left 1.6 miles, then right on FR 830. The final 2.7 miles on FR 830 to Climbers' Bivouac may be rough or closed in winter/early spring.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Cascades (Skamania County, WA)
climate.typical: Variable high-altitude conditions with rapid weather changes. Summit winds 20-30+ mph common, especially afternoon. High UV exposure due to reflection from light-colored volcanic rock.
climate.spring: May-June: Temperatures 30-50°F at summit, frequent afternoon storms and wind. Snow possible but usually melting.
climate.summer: July-September: Daytime summit temperatures 40-60°F, overnight 30-40°F. Low precipitation but afternoon thunderstorms common, especially July-August. Morning conditions best.
climate.fall: September-October: Summit temperatures 35-50°F. Increased precipitation, early snow possible. Permit season ends October 31.
climate.winter: November-March: Winter climbing route (Worm Flows) in effect. Summit temperatures 0-30°F. Heavy snow. Typically inaccessible via Monitor Ridge.

access.landManager: U.S. Department of Agriculture Forest Service, Gifford Pinchot National Forest, Mount St. Helens District
access.fees: Climbing permit: $15/person/day (April 1 - October 31); free self-issued November 1 - March 31. Northwest Forest Pass: $5/day or $30/annual.
access.permit: Climbing permit required year-round for travel above 4,800 feet. April 1 - October 31: obtained via recreation.gov in advance, first-come, first-served. November 1 - March 31: free and self-issued at trailhead.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or $30/annual) OR valid climbing permit displayed in vehicle waives day-use fee.
access.closures: Monitor Ridge typically closed by snow November through early July. Climbers' Bivouac parking and road may close seasonally; check current conditions.

emergency.rangerStation: Mount St. Helens District, Gifford Pinchot National Forest, 42218 NE Yale Bridge Rd, Amboy, WA 98601
emergency.nearestHospital: Legacy Salmon Creek Hospital (Vancouver, WA) ~40 miles; Morton General Hospital (Morton, WA) ~40 miles
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable above timberline. Helicopter evacuation possible for serious injuries.

routes.overview: The standard winter and spring route on Mount St. Helens, Worm Flows is the most direct approach to the summit, gaining nearly 5,700 feet over approximately 6 miles. Named for the rivers of rocky lava flows surrounding the route, Worm Flows is accessed from Marble Mountain Sno-Park and requires winter mountaineering skills including avalanche awareness, crampons, and ice axe technique.
routes.beta: Start at Marble Mountain Sno-Park (2,800 ft). Follow Swift Ski Trail #244 north through dense forest to timberline. Cross to the west side of Swift Creek above Chocolate Falls (3,700 ft). Navigate rocky lava flows and mudflows marked by wooden cairn posts up to approximately 4,800 feet. Continue ascending marked route to a seismic station at 6,200 feet. Final snowfield ascent leads to crater rim at 8,363 feet.
routes.approach: From Cougar, drive east on Lewis River Road (Forest Road 90, paved). Turn left onto Forest Road 83. Drive approximately 6 miles on FR 83 to Marble Mountain Sno-Park parking area (2,800 ft). From Sno-Park, follow Swift Ski Trail #244 north through forest for approximately 2 miles. Navigate to Chocolate Falls (3,700 ft), a 40-foot seasonal waterfall along Swift Creek. From Chocolate Falls, pick up climbing route marked by wooden posts and ascend rocky lava flows and mudflows.
routes.descent: Return via Worm Flows, retracing the same route. Descend from crater rim to seismic station, then through lava flows to Chocolate Falls, then via Swift Ski Trail #244 to Marble Mountain Sno-Park.
routes.descentText: From the summit crater rim, carefully descend the final snowfield to the seismic station at 6,200 feet. Follow marked posts through rocky lava flows and mixed snow/rock terrain down to 4,800 feet. Continue down through mudflows and rocky terrain marked by wooden posts. At 3,700 feet, reach Chocolate Falls area and Swift Ski Trail #244. Follow ski trail through forest to Marble Mountain Sno-Park. Use skis or snowshoes for efficiency on descent if snow present.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Start at Marble Mountain Sno-Park (2,800 ft). Follow Swift Ski Trail #244 through forest to timberline (1.5-2 hours). Cross to west side of Swift Creek at Chocolate Falls (3,700 ft). Ascend marked lava flows to 4,800 feet (2-3 hours). Continue to seismic station at 6,200 feet (2-3 hours). Final snowfield ascent to crater rim (8,363 ft, 1-2 hours). Descend via same route (3-4 hours). Total round trip 7-10 hours.
routes.bestSeason: December through June, with optimal conditions typically December through April when snow is stable. Permits required year-round; December 1 - March 31 permits free and self-issued at Sno-Park kiosk. April 1 - November 30 permits via recreation.gov. Washington State Sno-Park pass required December 1 - March 31.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or parties moving slowly, turnaround at seismic station (6,200 ft). Descend immediately if avalanche danger increases or whiteout conditions develop.
routes.turnaround: Standard turnaround is reaching the crater rim at 8,363 feet. Do not enter or traverse the crater itself; rim is extremely unstable. Allow 7-10 hours total for round trip depending on snow conditions and fitness.
routes.proNeeds: Avalanche awareness and safety skills essential. Basic mountaineering competency including self-arrest with ice axe and crampon technique required. No technical rock climbing protection needed.
gear: Skis with climbing skins OR snowshoes with cleats
whatToBring: Two to three liters of water (insulated or thermos)
Sunscreen and lip balm with SPF
Map and GPS device with downloaded route

pitchDetail.pitch: Swift Ski Trail forest section (1.5-2 miles)
pitchDetail.notes: Travel through dense forest on ski trail, gaining ~900 feet to timberline. May require skis or snowshoes depending on snow depth.

pitchDetail.pitch: Chocolate Falls area to lower lava flows (1-1.5 miles)
pitchDetail.notes: Cross Swift Creek west of Chocolate Falls (3,700 ft). Transition from forest to rocky lava flows and mudflows. Wooden posts begin marking route.

pitchDetail.pitch: Lava flows and mudflows ascent (2-2.5 miles)
pitchDetail.notes: Navigate through lava flows and mudflows marked by cairns. Ascend through mixed terrain (rock and snow) to approximately 4,800 feet where posts become more sparse.

pitchDetail.pitch: Upper lava flows and steep section (1-1.5 miles)
pitchDetail.notes: Continue ascending through rocky lava flows toward seismic station at 6,200 feet. This section has avalanche exposure on steep slopes.

pitchDetail.pitch: Final snowfield to crater rim (0.5-1.0 mile)
pitchDetail.notes: Final ascent on snow from seismic station (6,200 ft) to crater rim (8,363 ft). May require ice axe and crampons. Watch for cornices near rim.

road.name: Lewis River Road / Forest Road 90 + Forest Road 83
road.status: Open year-round for winter route; typically passable December-June
road.seasonalGate: No seasonal gate; heavy snow may impact road access in extreme winters
road.driveNote: From Cougar, drive east on Lewis River Road (paved), which becomes Forest Road 90. Turn left onto Forest Road 83. Drive ~6 miles on FR 83 to Marble Mountain Sno-Park. Road passable year-round but may have snow/ice in winter; carry chains.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Cascades (Skamania County, WA)
climate.typical: Heavy snow typical at elevation; unpredictable and rapidly changing weather. Afternoon warming and cloud development common. Wind at higher elevations 20-40+ mph.
climate.spring: April-May: Increasing sun exposure and warming (25-45°F at summit). Significant avalanche danger from wet-loose conditions, especially afternoon.
climate.summer: June-August: Rapid snow melt makes Worm Flows less viable; Monitor Ridge becomes preferred route.
climate.fall: September-October: Increasing chance of early snow; cooling. Weather unstable.
climate.winter: November-March: Peak Worm Flows season. Summit temperatures 0-25°F. Heavy snow (4-6+ feet possible). Stable snow in early December and late February/March.

access.landManager: U.S. Department of Agriculture Forest Service, Gifford Pinchot National Forest, Mount St. Helens District
access.fees: Climbing permit: $15/person/day (April 1 - November 30); free December 1 - March 31. Washington State Sno-Park Pass: $8-10 daily or $80 seasonal (required December 1 - March 31).
access.permit: Climbing permit required year-round for travel above 4,800 feet. December 1 - March 31: free and self-issued at Marble Mountain Sno-Park kiosk. April 1 - November 30: purchased via recreation.gov in advance.
access.passRequired: Washington State Sno-Park Pass required December 1 - March 31 (must be purchased in advance, not available at trailhead). Climbing permit satisfies day-use requirements.
access.closures: No official seasonal closures, but route intended for December-June use. Heavy avalanche danger and deep snow may make route impractical after mid-May. Extreme winter weather may temporarily close access.

emergency.rangerStation: Mount St. Helens District, Gifford Pinchot National Forest, 42218 NE Yale Bridge Rd, Amboy, WA 98601
emergency.nearestHospital: Legacy Salmon Creek Hospital (Vancouver, WA) ~40 miles; Morton General Hospital (Morton, WA) ~40 miles
emergency.notes: Helicopter evacuation likely for serious injuries. Avalanche burial rescue requires immediate party response. Cell service unreliable; carry personal locator beacon (PLB) or satellite communicator.

blurb: Mount Tom is a remote 7,076-foot glacier-clad peak in Olympic National Park's western range. It supports White Glacier, the westernmost glacier in the contiguous United States, and stands as the westernmost summit higher than 7,000 feet. First ascended in 1914 by Edmond S. Meany's party, the peak is typically accessed as an addition to Mount Olympus expeditions via glacier and scramble terrain.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: database lists 7,084 ft; Wikipedia and mountain-forecast.com list 7,076 ft (8-foot difference, likely survey method variation). Peak location and parent hierarchy (Olympic Mountains, Olympic National Park, Jefferson County) verified as accurate.

routes.fa: 1914 by Edmond S. Meany (party); Thomas Martin first to summit
routes.overview: The standard and only documented route to Mount Tom combines glacier travel and Class 3 rock scrambling. Parties typically access it as an addition to Mount Olympus expeditions, traversing from Snow Dome across the Olympus Massif to connect with White Glacier on the northeast aspect, then scrambling to summit via rock.
routes.beta: Mount Tom, Mount Mathias, and Mount Mercury are recommended additions to Mount Olympus expeditions. The route requires solid glacier mountaineering skills, ice axe and crampon proficiency, and comfort with steep, loose rock. Early season (late June-July) may require more ice/snow technique; late summer (August-September) emphasizes scrambling skills on exposed Class 3 terrain.
routes.approach: Access via Hoh Ranger Station trailhead (located 12.5 miles south of Forks, WA on US-101, then 19 miles up Hoh River Road). Hike 17.2 miles up Hoh River Trail to Glacier Meadows (4,200 ft), with established campsites at 5-Mile Island (5 mi), Happy Four (5.8 mi), Olympus Guard Station (9+ mi), Elk Lake (15 mi), and Caltech Rocks/Snow Dome camps. From Glacier Meadows, ascend Blue Glacier, navigate through rock islands and snow slopes to Snow Dome (6,600 ft). Make horizontal traverse along snowy Olympus Massif staying below the Olympus peaks, heading west toward Mount Tom, to reach White Glacier's upper reaches. Note: descent from Olympus Massif to White Glacier is steep and loose; this barrier has caused multiple expeditions to abandon Mount Tom plans.
routes.descent: Reverse the approach: descend scrambling and snow to White Glacier, traverse back to Olympus Massif, retrace path to Snow Dome, descend Blue Glacier via ascent route, return to Glacier Meadows and Hoh River Trail trailhead.
routes.descentText: Scramble down Class 3 terrain via the northeast side to White Glacier. Traverse the glacier and negotiate the steep, loose rock descent back to the Olympus Massif. Follow the horizontal traverse back toward Snow Dome, descending snow slopes and navigating rock islands. Descend Blue Glacier (watch for crevasses and bergschrunds, particularly on upper sections). Retrace Hoh River Trail back to trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hoh Ranger Station to Olympus Guard Station or Elk Lake (9-15 miles, 6-10 hrs). Day 2: Elk Lake to Glacier Meadows via Jemrod Gully ladder (2-4 miles, 4-6 hrs). Day 3: Glacier Meadows to Snow Dome or lower Olympus Massif camp (4-6 hrs). Day 4: Summit Mount Tom traverse and descent to camp. Day 5: Return to Glacier Meadows. Day 6-7: Hike out via Hoh River Trail.
routes.bail: From White Glacier: retreat via Olympus Massif to Snow Dome if conditions deteriorate. From Snow Dome: return down Blue Glacier to Glacier Meadows. From any point on glacier: descend to established campsites (Snow Dome, Caltech Rocks, or lower). Early descent via Hoh River Trail to Glacier Meadows is always an option; ranger station is reachable in 1-2 days from Glacier Meadows.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if: (1) Weather prevents safe glacier travel or crevasse rescue capability; (2) Loose rock conditions on Olympus Massif descent are worsening; (3) Group hasn't reached White Glacier by mid-afternoon on summit day; (4) Any member shows signs of altitude, fatigue, or cold injury.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the most favorable conditions, with July-August providing more stable snow and ice for glacier travel, and September offering clearer rock on scrambling sections. Late June is possible but may require additional ice axes and crampons. Winter travel above Elk Lake is not recommended due to avalanche hazard.
routes.comms: No cell service above the trailhead. Satellite communicators (inReach, SPOT, or equivalent) recommended for expedition groups. Ranger stations at Olympus Guard Station and ranger presence at Hoh area provide some contact capability during business hours.
objHaz: Crevasses and bergschrunds on Blue Glacier and White Glacier
Avalanche hazard on steep snow slopes and approaches to Olympus Massif
Steep, loose, unstable rock on descent from Olympus Massif
Exposure and fall hazard on Class 3+ scrambling terrain
knownHazards: The steep and loose descent from Olympus Massif to White Glacier has caused experienced parties to abandon Mount Tom summit attempts due to rockfall and instability risk.
White Glacier has retreated significantly since the Little Ice Age (retreated 10,500 feet by 1952); current conditions may differ from older guidebook descriptions.
Jemrod Gully Ladder (0.25 miles before Glacier Meadows) is a prominent, exposed feature requiring safe descent/ascent technique.
Hoh River Trail washout zones require navigation care; consult ranger station before departure.
Avalanche terrain surrounds approaches; NWAC forecasts are essential for late spring/early summer travel.
watchOut: The Olympus Massif descent to White Glacier is steeper and looser than many climbers anticipate; scout from Snow Dome before committing
Blue Glacier has multiple bergschrunds; post-hole hazard is significant in warmer months
Weather changes rapidly; afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer; always be off high terrain by afternoon
White Glacier is often corniced on the upper east edge; approach from west side when possible
Recent parties report loose, pointy volcanic talus on Olympus Massif; protect rope team carefully
routes.detailedRack: Full glacier mountaineering rack: ice axe and crampons per climber (most parties use half-rope or simul-roped techniques on Blue Glacier). For White Glacier ascent, bring standard rock protection (nuts, cams size 0-2) for potential runner anchors in Class 3 sections, though many parties climb unroped on well-established scramble terrain. Pickets or snow anchors for glacier belays.
routes.proNeeds: Solid understanding of self-rescue on glaciers, ability to identify and cross crevasse fields, competence with ice axe braking, and proficiency with Class 3 rock. Parties should include at least one person experienced with crevasse rescue and rope management.
proTips: Scout the Olympus Massif descent from Snow Dome in good light before committing to Mount Tom
White Glacier is best approached from the west side via lower Olympus Massif
Plan extra time for the scramble terrain in late summer when snow is melted
Carry extra sling/cordelette for emergency anchors on loose rock
Camp at Snow Dome or Caltech Rocks for Mount Tom summit day
Bring trekking poles for Hoh River Trail; the 34-mile round trip is long

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM from Snow Dome or Caltech Rocks camp

road.name: Hoh River Road (Upper Hoh Road)
road.status: Open; check with ranger station for washout updates
road.seasonalGate: Open year-round; access may be limited in winter
road.driveNote: From US-101 south of Forks (12.5 miles), turn east onto Upper Hoh Road and drive 19 miles to Hoh Ranger Station and Hoh Rain Forest Visitor Center. Road is paved but narrow in places; allow 30-40 minutes from Forks.

climate.forecastZone: Olympics (NWAC Zone coverage); mountain-forecast.com provides Mount Tom-specific 6-day forecasts
climate.typical: Cool and wet; precipitation common year-round. Summit conditions below freezing except mid-summer. Strong winds at ridge elevation.
climate.spring: Late May-June: heavy snow melt, increased avalanche hazard, frequent precipitation, frequent storms. Temperatures near or below freezing at summit.
climate.summer: July-September: Most stable period; daytime highs near 13-16C at summit, lows 5-7C. Afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August. Freezing level 3,300-4,450 ft.
climate.fall: October-November: Rapid deterioration; heavy precipitation, early season snow, avalanche danger increases, daylight decreases, temperatures drop rapidly
climate.winter: December-March: Not recommended. Heavy snowfall, sustained avalanche hazard, severe cold, limited daylight, whiteout conditions frequent.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Olympic National Park)
access.fees: Olympic National Park entrance: $30/vehicle (7-day pass). Wilderness camping permit: $8/person/night + $6 permit fee (youth under 16 free).
access.permit: Wilderness overnight permit required; reserved via Recreation.gov. Permits open April 15 for summer season (May 15-Oct 15).
access.passRequired: Recreation.gov wilderness permit required; park entrance pass required
access.closures: Winter travel above Elk Lake not recommended due to avalanche danger. Hoh River Trail washout sections may require temporary closure; check with ranger station.

emergency.rangerStation: Hoh Ranger Station: (360) 374-6534 (business hours). Olympus Guard Station staffed intermittently in summer.
emergency.nearestHospital: Forks Community Hospital (Level IV Trauma, 24-hour ER): ~30 miles, (360) 374-6271. Jefferson Healthcare (Port Townsend, Critical Access Hospital, 24-hour ER): ~50 miles, (360) 385-2200
emergency.notes: No cell service above trailhead. Satellite communicators (inReach/SPOT) strongly recommended. Helicopter rescue possible but weather-dependent; evacuation via hiking to Glacier Meadows and ranger assistance preferred. Jefferson Search and Rescue (JSAR) covers County and assists Olympic NP.

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend via established route, navigating crevasse fields. Simul-rope or belay depending on hazard. Early season more icy; late season may have postholing.
Steep snow slopes with rock islands. Late summer may be mixed terrain. Bootpacks typical in early season.

pitchDetail.pitch: Olympus Massif traverse to White Glacier
pitchDetail.notes: Horizontal traverse staying below Olympus peaks. Steep descent (45+ deg) to White Glacier is crux: loose, unstable rock, poor protection, significant rockfall hazard.
Approach from west side when possible. Watch for crevasses and bergschrunds. Final approach to scramble terrain may involve steep snow/ice.
Loose, volcanic rock. Hand-over-hand terrain with exposure. Rock quality poor; protection sparse. Final 200 ft to summit is key crux.

blurb: Storm King is one of Washington's 100 highest peaks located in North Cascades National Park at the junction of Park Creek, North Fork Bridge Creek, and Thunder Creek. Composed of Skagit Gneiss metamorphic rock, the peak forms a rugged set of rock towers arranged in an arc around an amphitheater of talus. First ascended in 1933 by Forrest Farr and Art Winder, Storm King offers dramatic summit views of three neighboring 8,000+ foot peaks: Bucknor, Logan, and Goode.

routes.overview: A classic alpine scramble on Skagit Gneiss towers with exposed Class 4 terrain and technical sections. The route ascends loose talus slopes and ledge systems to access the summit tower via a short but steep crack.
routes.beta: Approach the peak from Park Creek campground by ascending a loose talus slope, bearing left toward a cirque below the towers. Route-finding requires staying on the correct gully (right-side route preferred over left). Traverse a ledge across the backside of the southern tower with Class 3-4 moves and occasional exposure. Navigate a slight overhang with a squeeze-through section (Class 3), then traverse the summit tower's backside to a small spur. Final approach involves either climbing a 15-foot Class 4 solid face or wrapping around the left side on Class 3 blocks to reach the summit register. Slings are in place near the summit for rappel anchors.
routes.approach: Access requires traveling from Seattle to Chelan (3.5+ hours), then taking the Lady of the Lake ferry or Chelan Seaplanes to Stehekin (1-2 hours). From Stehekin, board the shuttle service to High Bridge Camp (11 miles). From High Bridge, follow the old road/Old Wagon Trail for approximately 2 miles, then sign for 'Old Wagon Trail/PCT' to bypass a washout. Stay on this trail for 3 miles to Bridge Creek campground. Continue 2.5 miles to Park Creek campground (approximately 7.5 miles total from High Bridge with +700 ft elevation gain). From Park Creek campground, ascend the climber's trail marked with cairns for approximately 1-2 miles through talus and scree to reach the base of the towers at approximately 7,400 feet.
routes.descent: Standard descent involves rappelling from slings installed near the summit. Two rappels with approximately 30-meter rope segments are typical, though rope drag can occur. Downclimbing is an alternative but exposed.
routes.descentText: Rappel from fixed slings near the summit using two 30-35 meter rope segments, managing rope drag carefully. Alternatively, downclimb the ascent route via the same technical sections (Class 4 crack descent, then Class 3 ledge traverse). Descent to Park Creek campground via the same talus slopes and climber's trail, being careful of loose rock and potential rockfall.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Ferry/seaplane to Stehekin, shuttle to High Bridge Camp, hike to Park Creek campground (7.5 miles, +700 ft, 3-4 hours). Day 2: Day trip from Park Creek: ascend to base cirque (~1-2 miles), climb Southwest Scramble to summit and descend (8-10 hours round trip from camp). Day 3: Descend to High Bridge for return shuttle and ferry/seaplane.
routes.turnaround: Descend from the notch before the summit pitch if weather deteriorates, rope systems fail, or party members become fatigued. Descent to Park Creek campground in daylight to avoid navigating loose talus in darkness.
routes.bestSeason: The climb is generally in shape from July through the first significant snowfalls of autumn (typically October). Snow lingers on the approach and lower sections until early-to-mid August, even in good years. July offers the most stable climbing conditions, though higher elevations may still retain patches of steep snow. August is more snow-free but can see sudden weather changes. September-early October provides late-season climbing with excellent weather but shorter daylight.
routes.comms: No cell service in the North Cascades backcountry. Satellite communicators (e.g., Garmin InReach, personal locator beacons) are strongly recommended for emergencies. Weather changes rapidly; consider filing a float plan with rangers at Stehekin.
routes.fa: First ascent: 1933, Forrest Farr and Art Winder
routes.face: North towers and south tower systems
whatToBring: Hat and gloves (even in July/August for early mornings)
routes.detailedRack: Small alpine rack: 2-3 cams (0.5-1.5 inch), 4-5 nuts (micro to medium), 4-6 slings (various lengths), 4-6 locking carabiners for anchors. The climbing is more about protection for rappel anchors and occasional belay points than continuous technical climbing, so a minimalist approach is suitable.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is limited to natural features (rock horns, flakes) for belays and rappel anchors. Rope systems appear to be pre-established at the summit; climbers should verify sling condition and anchor integrity. Most parties set up fixed lines for the exposed ledge traverse and summit pitch rather than continuously building anchors.
proTips: Start early from Park Creek (pre-dawn or first light) to maximize daylight for the climb and descent
Study route-finding: the right-side gully is easier and more popular than the left-side approach
The summit register exists—sign it if present for trip documentation
Test all rope systems and slings before committing weight; gneiss is solid but anchors can deteriorate
Descent via rappel is standard; make sure you are proficient before attempting the climb
Route-finding is the crux; cairns help but dissipate in wind; stay on the correct gully to avoid dead-ends
Bring extra slings; refix loose rope systems if needed to leave the route in good condition for others
watchOut: Loose talus slopes present continuous rockfall hazard, especially when multiple parties are on the route
Class 4 ledge traverse is exposed; a single mistake or dislodged rock can be fatal
Rapid weather changes common in North Cascades; fog and thunderstorms can develop suddenly
Snow lingers on north-facing sections into August; cramponing technique is essential
Gneiss is solid but slopes at angle that sheds rock; avoid climbing beneath other parties
Rappel failure is fatal; inspect and test anchors carefully
Descent in darkness on talus is extremely hazardous; do not descent in bad light
knownHazards: Objective rockfall from talus slopes and exposed towers; wear helmet at all times
Spontaneous rock/ice shedding from Class 4+ terrain above the main route
Sudden weather deterioration with lightning risk on exposed upper mountain
Lingering snow and ice patches creating unexpected ski-like conditions or slipping hazards
Exposure on north-side ledge: exposed to Class 4 terrain with significant drop-off; fixed line recommended
Loose anchor systems or deteriorated rope at summit slings; always test before use
Fatigue and altitude exposure after long approach hike and climbing day; turnaround times essential
Limited bail options once committed to upper technical sections; retreat via rappel is standard but risky in bad weather

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn or first light from Park Creek campground to maximize daylight

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road from Stehekin townsite to High Bridge shuttle (11 miles via shuttle)
road.status: Seasonal, ferry dependent
road.seasonalGate: Mid-June through November (ferry and shuttle service operate in summer; routes may be closed in winter or after major storms)
road.driveNote: No road access to High Bridge or Park Creek; access requires ferry/seaplane to Stehekin and mandatory shuttle service. The valley road is shared with pedestrian/bike traffic; shuttle schedules and pricing vary by year. Confirm ferry departure times and shuttle availability before departing Seattle. The ferry is operated by Lady of the Lake, Chelan Seaplanes, or a combination depending on season.

climate.forecastZone: Skagit/Snohomish county mountains in North Cascades National Park; forecast: NPS North Cascades weather zone
climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Temperatures drop 3-4 degrees per 1000 feet elevation. Expect afternoon thunderstorms July-August. Wind can be significant on exposed ridges.
climate.spring: Not recommended; heavy snow and avalanche danger
climate.summer: July-August: daytime highs 50-65F at 8500 ft, lows 25-35F. Morning/evening conditions can be freezing; afternoon storms common. August slightly warmer and drier than July. September: cooler, more stable weather; temps 40-55F daytime, 15-30F night.
climate.fall: September-October: decreasing temperatures and daylight. First significant snow typically October. Rapid weather deterioration possible.
climate.winter: Not recommended; heavy snow and mountaineering-only conditions

access.landManager: National Park Service, North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Backcountry permit required year-round for overnight stays
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or National Park Annual/Discover America Pass recommended for ferry/Stehekin access; verify current requirements
access.fees: Backcountry permit fee ($10/person + $6 reservation fee) or walk-up permits available at ranger stations. Ferry/seaplane to Stehekin ($10-$20+) and shuttle fees ($8-$15) vary by season. Park entrance fees may apply.
access.closures: Backcountry campsites and high alpine terrain may be closed seasonally due to snow, avalanche danger, or trail damage. Check with North Cascades NPS visitor centers or recreation.gov before planning trip. Park roads and passes may be closed winter-to-spring (November-June) due to snow.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff Emergency Services: 911 (no cell service in backcountry; use satellite communicator or hike out for emergency calls). Chelan County Sheriff: 509-667-4000 (land line for pre-trip notification).
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Visitor Center (North District): (360) 854-7200. Stehekin Ranger Station (seasonal, summer only): available for permit verification and emergency liaison. Closest ranger station to Park Creek area is at Stehekin.
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center (Marblemount, WA): ~1.5 hours by vehicle from Sedro-Woolley ranger station, then additional time for evacuation from backcountry. Chelan Hospital (Wenatchee, WA): ~3 hours by road from Stehekin. No helicopter rescue service guaranteed; difficult terrain and remote location may require ground evacuation.
emergency.notes: Cell service is non-existent in the North Cascades backcountry. File a float plan with rangers at Stehekin before departure. Consider carrying a personal locator beacon (PLB) or satellite messenger (InReach, Spot) for emergency communication. Weather is the primary hazard; prepare for rapid deterioration and self-rescue capability. Distance from trailhead to hospital is 5-10+ hours depending on evacuation method.

pitchDetail.notes: 1-2 miles of loose talus slopes from Park Creek campground/base cirque. Ascend right-bearing toward cirque. Path of least resistance; cairns mark climber's trail. ~1000 ft elevation gain. Expect loose rock and rockfall hazard. Take ice axe and crampons for any remaining snow patches (common into August).

pitchDetail.pitch: South tower ledge traverse and squeeze
pitchDetail.notes: Traverse ledge across backside of southern tower (~70 feet). Fairly exposed; 2-3 Class 4 moves on ledge. Squeeze under slight overhang (Class 3). Some parties set up fixed line. Ledge widens and contains loose rock; move carefully.

pitchDetail.pitch: Summit tower access and final crack
pitchDetail.notes: Traverse summit tower's backside to small spur. Ascend a 15-foot solid Class 4 face crack, or wrap around left side on Class 3 blocks (easier). Fixed anchors (slings) present near summit for rappel. Either climb the solid Class 4 crack or traverse Class 3 blocks; both lead to register and rappel slings. Short but sustained exposure.

blurb: Tenpeak Mountain is an 8,312-foot alpine peak in Washington's Glacier Peak Wilderness featuring dramatic north-facing topography with a 4,200-foot rise above Suiattle Valley. The peak's Cretaceous granitic rock and surrounding hanging glaciers provide technical mountaineering objectives accessible via the White River approach. Lloyd Anderson and Tom Campbell achieved the first ascent on September 21, 1940.

routes.fa: Lloyd Anderson and Tom Campbell, September 21, 1940
routes.overview: The standard ascent via Thunder Basin combines glacier travel, Class 4 scrambling, and low Class 5 rock climbing to the summit via a loose gully and broken chimney system on the southeast slope.
routes.beta: From Thunder Basin at 5,180 feet, ascend snowfields and rocky slopes toward the summit block. The final two pitches involve Class 4 scrambling up a gully left of the summit col (approximately 85 feet to a ledge), followed by a longer pitch up a face toward a broken chimney containing a good jam crack that leads directly to the summit. Crampons and ice axe essential for snowfield sections. A fixed pin exists for rappel descent assistance. The route sees minimal traffic and requires solid route-finding skills.
routes.approach: From Lake Wenatchee, drive Highway 207 north 10.5 miles to Coles Corner, then right onto White River Road for 10.1 miles to parking area (2,300 feet). Hike 6 miles up White River Trail through forest and multiple creek crossings to Thunder Creek junction. Beyond Thunder Creek, locate the old sheep herders trail (difficult to find) ascending west of Thunder Creek through dense vegetation and slide alder to Lower Thunder Basin (4,175 feet). Continue steeply up rocky slopes and past waterfalls to Upper Thunder Basin (5,180 feet), where good campsites exist on granite slabs. Total approach distance approximately 8-10 miles and 8-10 hours.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route. Rappel from the summit using existing anchor or establish one on the upper pitch. Down-climb Class 4 terrain in the gully, then return across snowfields and rocky terrain to Thunder Basin. Exercise caution on descent in poor visibility due to cliff bands and complex terrain. Loose rock hazard increases on descent.
routes.descentText: From the summit, carefully descend approximately 85 feet via the broken chimney, testing all holds due to loose rock. Rappel from the upper pitch (30-50 meter rope recommended for single rap; 60-meter rope preferred for two-stage descent). Down-climb Class 4 scrambling in the gully left of col. Once clear of technical terrain, descend rocky slopes to snowfield sections. Return across snowfields to the base of the scramble, then descend loose scree and rocky slopes toward Thunder Basin camp. Retrace the White River Trail descent, managing multiple creek crossings and brush sections.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Trailhead (2,300 ft) to Thunder Basin camp (5,180 ft). Hike 8-10 hours via White River Trail and Thunder Creek to establish camp. Day 2: From camp, summit push approximately 6 hours round trip, gaining 3,100 feet to peak and returning. Day 3: Descend to trailhead, approximately 8-10 hours including 6 miles on trail.
routes.bail: From Thunder Basin, descend via the approach route (White River Trail) to trailhead. Safe descent is straightforward via the established trail. If unable to proceed beyond Lower Thunder Basin (4,175 ft), retreat directly to this point and descend to the trailhead—this is not a remote location and escape is possible at any point on the approach.
routes.turnaround: Parties should plan 6-hour round-trip summit push from Thunder Basin base camp. Turn around by mid-afternoon to ensure safe descent before dark; in summer this typically means summit departure by 1-2 p.m. Approaching the technical terrain after 2 p.m. is not recommended. Parties should descend from camp by day three; extended stays above 5,000 feet increase altitude-related risk and weather exposure.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers optimal conditions. July provides the most reliable snow coverage on the snowfields below the summit, while August and September offer mostly snow-free scrambling on stable scree and rock. June is possible but involves more brush and route-finding difficulty at lower elevations. Winter ascents are extremely rare and require full winter mountaineering skills; late spring (May-June) can involve significant avalanche hazard on approach slopes.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable in the valley but may be intermittent at higher elevations with certain carriers. Satellite communicators (InReach, Spot) are strongly recommended for this remote location. White River Valley terrain and dense forest create significant RF attenuation at low elevations.
routes.proNeeds: Protection and rappel capability essential: 60-meter rope (or 30-meter minimum for single-stage rappel), standard rock rack (cams sizes 0.5-2 inches, nuts), and at least three reliable anchor placements or slings for rappel setup. The broken chimney pitch offers natural protection and jamming opportunities.
whatToBring: High-quality climbing boots with ankle support
First aid kit with blister treatment
Water bottles or hydration reservoir (2-3 liters capacity)
Map and compass; GPS device recommended
Microspikes or light crampon alternative for approach
watchOut: Loose, unstable rock throughout approach and summit block—test all holds before committing weight
Rockfall hazard on upper pitches especially after snowmelt or freeze-thaw cycles; wear helmet
Avalanche debris and active runout zones on approach to Thunder Basin; assess stability before crossing
Complex terrain and poor visibility can lead to route-finding errors; GPS and good map reading essential
Rapidly changing weather; afternoon thunderstorms common July-September
Multiple creek crossings on approach often running high from snowmelt; early morning crossing safer
Extensive brush and slide alder on lower approach causes delays and disorientation
knownHazards: Loose scree and rock on slopes; frequent rockfall events observed after freeze-thaw cycles
Avalanche terrain on approach slopes and around Thunder Basin—evaluate slab stability and weather patterns
Steep Class 4 terrain with significant exposure; falls would likely be fatal
Crevasse danger minimal in summer but Honeycomb Glacier and snowfields below summit present rockfall hazard
Extreme exposure (Class 4 terrain with 1,000+ feet of drop nearby); rope management essential
Rapidly deteriorating weather windows; route closed by storm systems within 12-24 hours
Route-finding complexity above Thunder Basin; lack of established trail; disorientation risk in poor visibility
proTips: Scout the sheep trail location at Thunder Creek junction before committing; GPS waypoint or marker recommended if returning
Camp at 5,180 feet (Upper Thunder Basin) rather than lower options to shorten summit day and reduce fatigue risk on technical pitches
Start summit attempt by 5-6 a.m. to allow adequate daylight for both climbing and descent; late starts often force dangerous night descents
The broken chimney on the final pitch offers surprisingly good holds and is more straightforward than it appears from below—thorough inspection recommended before committing
A fixed pin exists for rappel anchor; check current condition via recent trip reports before relying on it; always back up with own anchor sling
Approach in July when snowfields are more consolidated; late August-September involves more loose rock hazard due to melt and freeze-thaw
Reverse the climb (summit-down) if darkness is approaching; do not attempt navigation of complex terrain by headlamp if avoidable

pitchDetail.notes: 85-foot ascent from col ledge up gully left of summit block; loose rock throughout; good holds available but require testing; crux of technical difficulty
Long pitch up face toward broken chimney; chimney contains good jam crack and natural protection; straightforward climbing with good handholds for grade; approximately 50-100 feet to summit

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (west side) and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (east side)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking ($30)
access.permit: Self-issued wilderness permit available at trailhead (no fee)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass for vehicle parking
access.closures: White River Road may be seasonally gated due to winter snow; check current conditions with Methow Valley Ranger District (509-996-4000) before trip. April 2026 note: severe access limitations reported due to December 2025 storm damage.

road.name: White River Road (Road 6400)
road.status: Seasonal gate closure during winter; typically open late May through October
road.seasonalGate: Winter closure November-April approximately; verify current status with USFS before trip
road.driveNote: From Lake Wenatchee Highway at Coles Corner, turn north on Highway 207 for 10.5 miles. Fork right onto White River Road (Road 6400) and drive 10.1 miles to trailhead parking at 2,300 feet elevation. Road is gravel; high-clearance vehicle recommended but not required in summer.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC forecast zone: Cascades (North)
climate.typical: High mountain environment with rapid weather changes. Summer afternoon thunderstorms common. Significant precipitation year-round from Pacific weather systems. Temperatures on ridge vary 20-30 degrees F below surrounding valleys.
climate.spring: May-June sees snowmelt; waterfalls run high. Avalanche hazard elevated. Temperatures range 35-55 degrees F at summit. Snow may linger above 6,000 feet into June.
climate.summer: July-September offers most stable conditions. Daytime temps 45-65 degrees F at summit; nighttime 30-45 degrees F. Freeze-thaw cycles create consolidated snowfields in early July; increasing melt by late August-September. Afternoon thunderstorms occur 3-4 days per week on average.
climate.fall: September-October weather increasingly unstable. First significant snows possible by late September at higher elevations. Temps drop to 30-50 degrees F range. Transition period with fewer dry weather windows.
climate.winter: November-April dominated by deep snow and winter storms. Route is not typically climbed; extreme avalanche hazard, whiteout conditions common, and technical ice climbing required on exposed terrain. Temperatures well below freezing.

emergency.county: Straddles Snohomish County (west) and Chelan County (east)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County: 911 or (425) 407-3999 non-emergency. Chelan County: 911 or Rivercom (509) 663-9911.
emergency.rangerStation: Chelan Ranger District: 428 W. Woodin Avenue, Chelan, WA 98816, (509) 682-4900. Open Wed-Thu 9am-3pm. Methow Valley Ranger District (509-996-4000) also covers this area.
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence Regional Medical Center Everett (Level III trauma center) in Everett, WA approximately 70-80 miles southwest. Snohomish County Level I Trauma: Harborview Medical Center, Seattle (90+ miles south). Cascade Valley Hospital in Concrete (~35 miles) is closest regional facility.
emergency.notes: No cell coverage in Thunder Valley; satellite communicator (InReach, Spot) essential. Helicopter evacuation possible in life-threatening emergency; weather dependent and delays are common.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning 5-6 a.m. from camp to ensure adequate daylight for technical climbing and descent by 5-6 p.m.

routes.corrections: Elevation in given data (8310 ft) is accurate per USGS surveys (documented as 8312 ft by Wikipedia, essentially equivalent within survey tolerance). No major factual conflicts detected between given data and reputable sources. First ascent (Lloyd Anderson/Tom Campbell, 1940) applies to the peak generally; specific Southeast Route FA not separately documented in available sources.

routes.fa: William A. Degenhardt and Herbert V. Strandberg, August 1, 1931
routes.overview: Standard glacier approach to The Needle ascends the Neve Glacier from the east via the Pyramid Lake approach, traversing past Horseman's Pack and The Horseman before ascending the southeast ridge via a steep snow arete. The route features glacier travel with crevasse hazards followed by exposed Class 3 scrambling and one unavoidable low 5th class move on the knife-edge summit ridge.
routes.beta: Follow the standard Snowfield Peak approach via Pyramid Lake to reach the Colonial-Neve col (approximately 6,800-6,900 feet). Traverse westward along the Neve Glacier staying near the glacier edge to avoid steep snow. Pass Horseman's Pack (8,156 ft) and The Horseman (7,760 ft), then turn north following the glacier edge to the southeast ridge of The Needle. Ascend the snow arete, bypassing the bergshrund, to gain the rocky ridge. Climb sustained Class 3 scrambling with full exposure, turning west around a corner. The final knife-edge ridge section before the summit can be crossed straddled (à cheval) or via an awkward mantle traverse. Exposed moves throughout.
routes.approach: Begin at Pyramid Lake Trailhead (state Route 20, mile post 126.8, small pullout on north side). Hike the easy 2.1-mile trail to Pyramid Lake (1,500 ft gain). From Pyramid Lake, follow the steep climber's trail up the east ridge of Pyramid Peak with relentless switchbacks gaining significant elevation. Reach the ridge notch under Pyramid Peak at approximately 5,800 feet (total approximately 6 hours from trailhead). Traverse the east side of the Colonial Glacier tarn. Ascend to the Colonial-Neve col. From the col, pick your way westward across the Neve Glacier staying close to its edge to avoid hazardous steep snow slopes. Navigate crevasses (typically 15-20+ small crevasses reported). Pass Horseman's Pack and The Horseman summits staying on defined paths. Continue north to reach the southeast ridge base of The Needle.
routes.descent: From the summit, rappel down the north face approximately 25 feet to a large flake anchor and established ledge. Walk back over to the dirty ledges below the gendarme (rocky pinnacle) and downclimb to the glacier. Return to camp via the Neve Glacier, retracing the approach route. Total descent time typically 1-1.5 hours.
routes.descentText: Rappel north face to big ledge, walk back to glacier, retrace approach down Neve Glacier. May require short simul-climbing sections on Class 3 terrain. Afternoon snow conditions can degrade the approach; descend early to minimize avalanche risk.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Pyramid Lake Trailhead to camp at or below Colonial-Neve col (approximately 6-7 hours total). Day 2: Pre-dawn start from camp, ascend Neve Glacier to The Needle southeast ridge base (1-2 hours), climb to summit (1.5-2 hours), descend to camp (1.5 hours), possible descent to lower camp or trailhead if doing long day. Day 3 (if overnight): Descend to trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat down the Neve Glacier to the col; well-established route with many parties using it. In bad weather or fatigue, do not continue past the snow arete; descend via the approach route. Can descend to lower camps or trailhead at any time.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by mid-morning (10-11 AM) if cloud cover develops, weather deteriorates, or team is moving slower than planned. Afternoon avalanche danger and verglas conditions worsen throughout the day. Do not attempt the summit ridge in high winds or with poor visibility.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through August offers optimal conditions with overnight freezing levels that preserve glacier integrity and consolidated snow for travel. Daytime temperatures average 63-70°F with nights at 41-45°F. July is the driest month with approximately 10 days of rain. June is possible with extended approach times due to deeper snow coverage above 5,500 feet. September can be viable in early month but weather becomes increasingly unstable. October through April is not recommended due to extreme avalanche danger, heavy snow accumulation, and winter road closures on State Route 20.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is non-existent in the backcountry areas surrounding The Needle. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, Spot, or similar) is strongly recommended for emergency communication. Park dispatch can be reached at (360) 873-4500 ext. 37 in emergency situations, though response times may be slow. The Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount at (360) 854-7200 can provide emergency coordination.
routes.detailedRack: Light glacier rack consisting of 50-60 cm straight-shaft ice axe with leash, 10-12 point steel crampons, mountaineering helmet, 50-60 meter rope (minimum 7-8mm dynamic), rock protection including medium and large cams, wired nuts, 8-10 quickdraws, several slings (120cm and 60cm), pitons (if available), 4 screw-gate carabiners, 2 snapgate carabiners, 2 prusik cords (3-4mm), 2 ice screws (for glacier rescue), harness, rappel device (ATC or equivalent). Glacier travel kit should include 2 ice screws for belaying, pickets for anchor backup if needed.
routes.proNeeds: Rope and protection essential for glacier travel to manage crevasse hazard; short-rope party of 3-4 individuals reduces crevasse fall severity. Rock protection optional but recommended for summit ridge; many parties protect the final low 5th class move with quickdraws and nuts. Helmet mandatory for both objective hazard (falling ice/rock) and subjective risk (falls). Rappel anchor essential for descent (large flake on summit).
proTips: Climb as part of a larger Snowfield group traverse including Snowfield Peak and optionally Horseman's Pack for a full experience.
Start pre-dawn (5-6 AM) to minimize afternoon snow conditions and avalanche danger.
Use trekking poles for the initial steep approach; save energy for the technical sections.
Descend by mid-afternoon to avoid afternoon thaw and verglas formation on the descent.
Scout crevasses carefully on Neve Glacier; use established boot packs and stay on defined paths.
The snow arete crossing is the crux technical section; take extra time and care here.
More cautious parties protect the final knife-edge ridge with rock protection despite terrain being Class 3-4.
Plan for a 2-3 day trip if this is your first alpine mountaineering climb; the terrain exposure is significant.
watchOut: The final knife-edge ridge is extremely exposed despite being Class 3-4 terrain; this is not a beginner-friendly scramble.
At least one unavoidable low 5th class move exists on the summit ridge; protection recommended.
The Needle has limited flat summit area (room for only one person at a time).
Verglas (surface icing) can form on snow/ice in early season morning hours, creating slippery conditions.
Afternoon avalanche danger increases throughout the day on steep approach slopes.
Exposure on the final ridge section can be psychologically intimidating; assess party comfort level.
Bergshrund crossing requires careful evaluation; snow bridges can be unstable.
knownHazards: Crevasse falls on Neve Glacier, particularly in mid-summer when bergschrunds and newer crevasses open up.
Avalanche danger on approach slopes during warming cycles or after new snow.
Falling seracs or ice blocks from hanging glaciers adjacent to approach.
Exposure and fall hazard on final knife-edge summit ridge.
Objective rock fall from summit area during afternoon sun and warming cycles.
Verglas formation on approach in early season creating slippery conditions.
Exposure to weather on high alpine terrain with limited shelter.
Bear activity reported in adjacent basins; practice proper food storage.

pitchDetail.pitch: Neve Glacier traverse to southeast ridge base
pitchDetail.notes: Crevasse navigation required; 1-2 hours of glacier travel passing Horseman's Pack and The Horseman; stay near glacier edge to avoid steep snow
Steep snow ridge with bergshrund crossing; crux section where technical climbing becomes necessary; 40-50 feet of steep terrain
Exposed scrambling with full drop exposure; ridge turns west around a corner; 40+ feet of steep Class 3 leading to dirty ledges below gendarme

pitchDetail.grade: Class 4 exposed ridge, low 5th class moves unavoidable
pitchDetail.notes: Knife-edge section can be crossed à cheval (straddled) or via awkward mantle traverse; very exposed; small summit block accommodates only one person; large flake visible for rappel anchor

access.landManager: National Park Service, North Cascades National Park
access.fees: $10 per person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee
access.permit: Backcountry permit required
access.passRequired: Backcountry permit

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open summer and fall; may have winter closures
road.seasonalGate: Route 20 typically closes late November through April due to heavy snow
road.driveNote: Trailhead located at mile post 126.8 on north side of Highway 20 near Pyramid Creek. Parking is a small pullout on the left side of the highway. No facilities at trailhead; closest services in Concrete or Marblemount.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park / Snowfield-Colonial zone
climate.typical: Maritime mountain climate with frequent unpredictable weather changes; west side receives higher precipitation than east side
climate.spring: May-June: Snow persists above 5,500 feet; transitional season with warming trends; afternoon thunderstorms possible
climate.summer: July-August: Best season with relatively stable weather; daytime highs 63-70F, night lows 41-45F; July is driest month with approximately 10 days of rain; overnight freezing levels remain low enough to preserve glacier travel conditions
climate.fall: September: Similar temperatures to June with increasing precipitation; early September often viable but weather becomes less predictable by late month; potential for early season snow
climate.winter: October-April: Heavy snow and rain at all elevations; areas above 2,000 feet remain snow-covered; extreme avalanche danger in steep terrain; Route 20 closes

emergency.rangerStation: Wilderness Information Center, North Cascades National Park, 7280 Ranger Station Road, Marblemount, WA 98267; (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (approximately 45 miles); Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (approximately 40 miles)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is non-existent in backcountry; satellite communicator essential. For emergencies, contact Park Dispatch at (360) 873-4500 ext. 37; calling 911 works but response times are slower.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (5-6 AM from camp)

blurb: Mount Olympus, at 7,980 feet (2,430 m), is the tallest and most prominent mountain in the Olympic Mountains and one of the most heavily glaciated peaks in the continental United States outside the Cascade volcanoes. Located on the Olympic Peninsula within Olympic National Park, it features seven named glaciers (including Blue, Hoh, Humes, Jeffers, Hubert, Black, and White) and is notable for its extensive ice coverage and remote wilderness setting. The mountain's West Peak was first successfully summited on August 13, 1907, by Lorenz A. Nelson and party via the Blue Glacier route, and it remains the premier alpine objective in the Olympic Range.

routes.routeName: Blue Glacier / Snow Dome — East Face Ramps Finish (Standard)
routes.overview: The standard and most popular route on Mount Olympus, ascending the Blue Glacier and Snow Dome with a Class 4 scramble and one Class 5 step on the final loose ramps of the East Face to the West Peak summit.
routes.beta: From Glacier Meadows, descend to the Blue Glacier lateral moraine and rope up for glacier travel. Cross the Blue Glacier for approximately 1 mile with crevasse avoidance and careful snow bridge assessment. Ascend the broad Snow Dome to 6,600-7,000 feet. From Snow Dome, ascend toward Crystal Pass at 7,250 feet. After crossing Crystal Pass and traversing Five Fingers Ridge, encounter the false summit before the final push. The crux involves Class 4-5 scrambling on loose rocky ramps on the East Face, with one Class 5 step. The final 50-60 feet involves several Class 5.3-5.4 moves on mostly solid rock to reach the summit block belay ledge, then 50 feet of Class 5.3-5.4 rock to the true summit.
routes.approach: Drive to the Hoh Ranger Station at the end of Upper Hoh Road, approximately 31 miles south of Forks, WA off Highway 101. Hike the Hoh River Trail for 17.5 miles through temperate rainforest (typically snow-free through mid-summer) to Glacier Meadows at 4,200 feet. From Glacier Meadows, follow a way trail 0.8 miles to the top of the Blue Glacier's lateral moraine. Descend and traverse along the moraine for 0.2 miles before descending onto the glacier itself.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route. From the summit, rappel 30m on the north side or northwest edge using a double rope or single rope setup to a belay ledge, then reversing the summit block. Descend the East Face ramps carefully (loose rock and falling stone hazards). Carefully descend Snow Dome, watching for crevasses and maintaining rope team protocols. Cross the Blue Glacier downslope to the moraine. Navigate back up the lateral moraine and return to Glacier Meadows via the way trail. Complete the long 17.5-mile hike out via the Hoh River Trail, typically a full day from Glacier Meadows.
routes.descentText: The descent mirrors the approach but requires careful management of fatigue after summit day. From the summit, establish a solid rappel anchor and descend the steep summit block in one or two rappels using 40-60m ropes (late season may require longer ropes due to expanded crevasses on the approach). Carefully descend the loose East Face ramps, staying alert for rockfall and maintaining 3-point contact. From the base of the ramps, descend the broad Snow Dome, maintaining distance between rope team members and probing snow bridges as you descend. Cross the Blue Glacier on descent, carefully choosing a safe route through crevasse fields. The glacier becomes more heavily crevassed late season. Once at the base of the glacier, retrace your approach to Glacier Meadows. The hike out from Glacier Meadows takes 8-10 hours, making this typically an 10-14 hour descent day from the summit.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Hoh Ranger Station. Hike 17.5 miles on Hoh River Trail to Glacier Meadows (9 miles to Olympus Guard Station, 8.5 miles to Glacier Meadows). Establish camp, organize gear, acclimate to 4,200 feet. Day 2: Rest day or glacier reconnaissance; acclimate and review route plan. Day 3: Summit day from Glacier Meadows; depart pre-dawn, cross Blue Glacier and ascend Snow Dome, pass Crystal Pass, negotiate summit block via East Face ramps, summit, descend to Snow Dome camp. Day 4: Descend from Snow Dome to Glacier Meadows, complete hike out via Hoh River Trail to Olympus Guard Station. Day 5: Hike from Olympus Guard Station to trailhead and drive home.
routes.bail: Early retreat is safest from Glacier Meadows; parties can descend to lower camps in 3-4 hours. From Snow Dome and Crystal Pass, descent to Glacier Meadows is 4-5 hours. Parties reaching the summit block but encountering dangerous conditions (very loose rock, icing, extreme weather) should descend the same route immediately. Avoid traversing between peaks if weather deteriorates.
