routes.turnaround: Turn around and descend if you reach the base of the summit block and conditions are worse than observed on ascent, if weather deteriorates to poor visibility or lightning risk, if any team member shows signs of altitude sickness or exhaustion beyond safe recovery, if the rock is significantly iced or wet, if daylight is running short and you haven't summited by late afternoon, or if crevasse rescue has been needed.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through mid-August offers the most reliable climbing window with consolidated snow bridges, reduced crevasse hazard, and stable weather patterns. Late June provides firmer snow and intact bridges; July offers longer days and warmer conditions but opening crevasses; mid-to-late July to early August is the prime window. August brings warmer temperatures and more crevasse exposure, but crowds diminish late August into September. Early season (late June) is preferred for snow bridge integrity. Late season (September and beyond) features widely open crevasses and significant rockfall risk on the summit block as ice melts, making climbing significantly more technical and dangerous.
routes.rappels: 1-2 rappels of 30m from summit block (60m rope total recommended for late season)
objHaz: Crevasses and bergschrunds on active glaciers
Collapsing snow bridges, particularly late season
Rockfall on East Face ramps and summit block approach
Loose summit rock requiring careful footwork
Avalanche terrain on approach to Snow Dome, particularly early season on steep slopes
Exposure on summit block Class 5 climbing
knownHazards: The Blue Glacier is active and heavily crevassed; late season presents wider crevasses and weakened snow bridges requiring expert assessment
Loose rock on East Face ramps is a primary hazard; falling stone and rockfall from climbers above are serious concerns
The summit block features poor rock quality with frequent loose holds; protection is essential for the Class 5 pitches
Mount Olympus receives some of the highest precipitation in mainland North America (approximately 259 inches annually on the West Peak); weather can deteriorate with minimal warning at any season
The remote location and long approach mean rescue is extremely difficult and time-consuming; self-reliance and conservative decision-making are critical
watchOut: Crevasse rescue proficiency is mandatory, not optional; every team member must carry prusiks, carabiners, and ascenders
Snow bridges weaken daily in warm weather; cross them quickly and in single-file order with the heaviest pack crossing first
Climb the east face ramps early and quickly; afternoon sun triggers rockfall and wet rock
Do not traverse between peaks on the descent if weather is marginal; descend to Glacier Meadows via the known route
Do not attempt the summit block at night or in poor visibility; retreat to Snow Dome camp if darkness or weather intervenes
Late season (August-September) glacier conditions change rapidly; expect much more difficult crevasse terrain
proTips: Start summit day in pre-dawn darkness (3-4 AM) to minimize rockfall exposure on the East Face and avoid afternoon thunderstorms
Carry a 60-meter rope late season (July-August onward) to avoid multiple rappel stations; 40-50m may require intermediate anchors and extra time
Use a small, lightweight alpine rack (6-10 cams, slings, quickdraws); most teams climb the summit block with minimal gear and good footwork
The Snow Dome is a broad, gently sloping platform; in whiteout conditions, use a GPS waypoint or compass bearing to navigate toward Crystal Pass to avoid wandering
Acclimatize at Glacier Meadows for at least one full day before summit attempt; the 4,200-7,980 ft push in a single day is taxing
Bring extra layers and a windproof shell; the summit is severely exposed and weather can change in minutes
whatToBring: Ice axe (55-75 cm mountaineering axe with adze and pick)
Crampons (12-point steel with anti-balling plates for wet snow)
Alpine harness with adjustable leg loops
Helmet (mandatory for rockfall hazard on East Face)
Rope (40-60m, 8-9mm dynamic rope for glacier and summit block climbing)
Crevasse rescue kit: prusiks (120 cm and 240 cm cord), mechanical ascenders (Micro Traxion or Nano Traxion), locking carabiners (at least 4), ice screws
Small rock climbing rack: 6-10 cams (sizes 0.4-1.5 BD or equivalent), quickdraws, slings (various lengths), rappel anchors (cordellette)
Glacier glasses or goggles with UV protection and side shields
Map, compass, GPS device, and altimeter watch
Insulated water bottle (32 oz) with insulation to prevent freezing at altitude
Down jacket or synthetic insulation layer
Windproof and waterproof outer shell jacket and pants
Insulated, waterproof gloves and expedition mittens
Lightweight base layer (merino wool or synthetic)
Gaiters to keep crampons and snow out of boots
Emergency/repair kit: duct tape, cordage, knife, first aid supplies
Food: high-calorie snacks for 4-5 days (nuts, energy bars, dried fruit, electrolyte supplements)
Sleeping bag rated to at least 10F (-12C)
Tent with four-season rating for Olympic weather
Stove and fuel (canisters, or liquid fuel for cold temperatures)
Water treatment (tablets or filter for glacial meltwater)
Bear canister (required by NPS for overnight stays above Glacier Meadows)
Blue bags for human waste (required by NPS on Mount Olympus)
gear: Crevasse rescue kit (prusiks, ascenders, carabiners, ice screws)
Rock climbing rack with cams and slings
routes.detailedRack: The summit block requires a small rack of 6-10 cams in sizes ranging from 0.4 to 1.5 inches (or equivalent). Include 2-3 quickdraws, multiple slings (60 cm and 120 cm), and lockers for anchor building. The East Face ramps are often ascended with minimal protection due to poor rock quality; protection is intermittent and bomber placements are rare. Bring 2-3 ice screws (10 cm length) for ice/névé anchors on Snow Dome. Rappel anchors typically use natural rock features or pre-existing slings, but climbers should build solid anchors as backups.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is essential for the Class 5 pitches on the summit block, with solid rock only in sections. Expect variable protection quality due to loose or fractured stone. Place all protection carefully, test holds, and be prepared to retreat if protection opportunities are poor or nonexistent. The ramps offer minimal protection; many parties choose to simulclimb the East Face ramps.

pitchDetail.pitch: Glacier Crossing (Blue Glacier to Snow Dome base)
pitchDetail.notes: Approximately 1 mile of crevassed glacier; rope teams of 3-4, spaced 6-8m apart. Use deliberate footwork and probe snow bridges before weighting. This section is the crux of crevasse rescue risk. Estimated time: 1-1.5 hours.

pitchDetail.pitch: Snow Dome ascent to Crystal Pass
pitchDetail.notes: Broad, slope-angled snowfield to 6,600 ft. No technical protection required. Watch for crevasse fields near pass. Estimated time: 1.5-2 hours.

pitchDetail.pitch: Crystal Pass to false summit traverse
pitchDetail.notes: Traverse along ridgeline, stay alert for exposure and falling rocks. Estimated time: 45 minutes.

pitchDetail.pitch: False summit to true summit block base
pitchDetail.notes: Climb via Five Fingers Ridge area, then descend slightly to approach summit block. Loose and exposed. Estimated time: 30-45 minutes.

pitchDetail.pitch: East Face ramps to summit block base
pitchDetail.notes: The crux of the route. Approximately 60-80 feet of steep, loose ramps. Watch for rockfall from above and careful footwork on friable stone. Several moves are Class 5.3-5.4. Time: 45 minutes to 1.5 hours depending on protection strategy.
Ascend mostly solid rock with several Class 5.3-5.4 moves to the summit. Requires careful movement and moderate protection. Establish anchor and rappel on descent. Time: 45 minutes to 1 hour.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (3-4 AM) from Glacier Meadows to minimize rockfall exposure and afternoon thunderstorms

road.name: Upper Hoh Road (to Hoh Ranger Station trailhead)
road.status: Maintained year-round (seasonal conditions in winter)
road.driveNote: Drive from Forks, WA south on Highway 101 approximately 31 miles to Upper Hoh Road. Turn east and follow the winding paved road to the Hoh Ranger Station and Campground at road end. Parking is available at the trailhead. Road is paved and suitable for standard passenger vehicles. In winter, road may have snow/ice; chains may be required.

climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North Cascades (NWAC) / Olympic Mountains (Cascade West South)
climate.typical: Mount Olympus is located in one of the wettest locations in mainland North America, receiving approximately 259 inches of precipitation annually. Expect frequent rain and snow; weather can change rapidly with minimal warning.
climate.spring: Late spring (May-June) features lingering snow at high elevations, frequent rain in the valleys, avalanche hazard on steep slopes, and unreliable weather.
climate.summer: Summer (June-August) is the primary climbing season with relatively stable high-pressure systems providing longer dry windows. July and August are the driest months but still see frequent afternoon thunderstorms. Daytime temperatures near sea level are 55-70F; summit temperatures often 20-35F. Afternoon thunderstorms are common.
climate.fall: Fall (September-October) brings increasing precipitation and a return to autumn storms; crevasses are widely opened, and rockfall is significant.
climate.winter: Winter (November-April) features extreme precipitation, deep snow accumulation, avalanche hazard, and extremely short daylight hours. Not recommended for climbing.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Olympic National Park)
access.fees: No entrance fee for backcountry camping in wilderness areas (day-use visitors to Olympic National Park pay entrance fee)
access.permit: Wilderness permit required for overnight backcountry camping. Reserve via Recreation.gov (online) or in person at the Port Angeles Wilderness Information Center or Hoh Visitor Center. Permits are limited, particularly for popular sites like Glacier Meadows; reserve early for summer season.
access.passRequired: No special pass required beyond wilderness permit (America the Beautiful annual pass or daily entrance fee for vehicle entry to Olympic National Park)
access.closures: No specific closures for Mount Olympus climbing, though early-season avalanche hazard may make the approach inadvisable before late June. Winter conditions above Elk Lake are not recommended due to avalanche terrain. Check current conditions with the Hoh Visitor Center or Port Angeles Wilderness Information Center before departure.

emergency.rangerStation: Hoh Visitor Center: 360-374-6925; Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center (Port Angeles): 360-565-3100
emergency.nearestHospital: Forks Community Hospital, 530 Bogachiel Way, Forks, WA 98331; (360) 374-6271. Hospital is approximately 45-60 minutes from the trailhead by vehicle. Rescue from high altitude is extremely difficult; self-reliance and conservative decision-making are critical.
emergency.notes: Mount Olympus is extremely remote and rescue is time-consuming and expensive. Cell phone coverage is sporadic; satellite communication (InReach, SPOT) is recommended. Evacuation typically involves helicopter rescue in good weather; bad weather may delay rescue for days. All team members should have wilderness first aid training.

routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is unreliable throughout the Hoh River valley and Olympic backcountry. Service may be available at the trailhead but not in the wilderness. Satellite communicators (Garmin InReach, SPOT device) are highly recommended for emergency communication and tracking. Radio frequencies are not reliably available in the Olympics.
routes.fa: Lorenz A. Nelson and party (Mountaineers Club), August 13, 1907

routes.overview: A variation finishing up the solid northwest edge of the summit block, offering better rock quality than the East Face ramps but with more exposure.
routes.beta: After reaching the base of the summit block (via any approach), this variation climbs the northwestern edge of the block directly. The rock is generally more solid than the East Face, with several Class 5.3-5.4 moves. The exposure is significant but the climbing is more straightforward.
routes.approach: Same as Blue Glacier/East Face route approach until reaching the summit block base. Approach time is identical.
routes.descent: Rappel from summit anchor down the northwest edge using a single rope to a belay ledge below, then downclimb and traverse to rejoin the standard descent route.
routes.descentText: Rappel 25-30m down the northwest edge using a single rope to a belay ledge, then scramble/downclimb to rejoin standard descent route via East Face ramps.
routes.itinerary: Same as Blue Glacier standard route; variation only on summit block.
routes.bail: Downclimb or rappel the northwest edge if protection becomes difficult or exposure is unacceptable. Retreat to lower belay ledge and traverse to East Face route.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if rock quality is poor, protection is difficult to find, or weather deteriorates.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through mid-August
objHaz: Rock fall from climbers on adjacent routes
Loose rock and poor quality stone
knownHazards: The northwest edge offers more solid rock than the East Face ramps but is steeper and more exposed, requiring good climbing ability and protection skills
watchOut: Place protection carefully due to variable rock quality
Exposure is significant; not for climbers uncomfortable with heights
Rockfall hazard from climbers above on other variations
proTips: This variation is more challenging but offers better rock quality than the East Face ramps
Most protection placements will be found in cracks and corners on the northwest edge
Consider bringing slightly larger cams (up to 2.5 inch) for this variation
whatToBring: Standard glacier climb gear plus small rock rack
routes.detailedRack: Small rock rack of 6-10 cams, sizes 0.4-2.0 inches, with quickdraws and slings. The northwest edge offers more consistent protection placements than the East Face ramps.
routes.proNeeds: Good protection placement and climbing technique required. This variation is more technical and exposed than the East Face ramps.

pitchDetail.notes: Climb directly up the northwest edge with several Class 5.3-5.4 moves. Rock quality is better than East Face ramps. Moderate protection available in cracks and corners. Time: 1-1.5 hours.

timing.recommendedStart: Same as standard route; reach summit block base mid-to-late morning

road.driveNote: Same as Blue Glacier standard route

routes.routeName: Summit Block — North Face Direct Finish (variation)
routes.overview: A direct finish up the steeper North Face of the summit block, offering a more technical alternative with Class 5.4 climbing.
routes.beta: Approach the summit block from the north side (from Snow Dome and/or Crystal Pass traverse), then climb directly up the North Face instead of via the East Face ramps or northwest edge. This line is steeper and more exposed, with Class 5.4 climbing throughout.
routes.approach: Same as Blue Glacier standard approach to summit block base. Approach time identical.
routes.descent: Rappel from summit anchor down the north face using a double rope setup (50-60m) to avoid multiple rappel stations, then traverse to rejoin standard descent.
routes.descentText: Rappel 40-50m down the north face on double rope to base of North Face, then traverse to standard descent via East Face ramps.
routes.itinerary: Same as Blue Glacier standard route; variation only on summit block.
routes.bail: Rappel the north face or traverse to the northwest edge or East Face for retreat if conditions become unsafe.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if rock is wet or iced, protection is poor, or party is moving too slowly.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through mid-August
objHaz: Ice or wet rock early and late season
knownHazards: The North Face is steeper and more exposed than other summit block variations; early or late season may feature ice, making this variation significantly more dangerous
watchOut: Check rock for icing or wet conditions; abort if frozen
The exposure is greater than other variations; climbers must be comfortable with significant height and exposure
This variation is not recommended for parties inexperienced with exposure or low Class 5 rock climbing
proTips: This variation offers more direct climbing but is more committing than the East Face ramps or northwest edge
Early season (late June-early July) offers the firmest snow and iced rock; late season (August-September) offers bare rock but more crevasses below
Bring extra protection gear for this more technical line
whatToBring: Standard glacier climb gear plus small rock rack for technical climbing
routes.detailedRack: Rock rack of 8-10 cams, sizes 0.4-1.5 inches, quickdraws, and slings. The North Face is steeper and protection placements may be smaller and more sporadic.
routes.proNeeds: Solid rock climbing ability and protection placement skills required. This is the most technical summit block variation.

pitchDetail.notes: Direct ascent of the steep north face with sustained Class 5.4 climbing. Rock quality is variable; protection placements are smaller and less frequent. More exposed and committing than other variations. Time: 1-1.5 hours.

timing.recommendedStart: Same as standard route

road.status: Maintained year-round
road.driveNote: Same as standard route

routes.overview: An ambitious alpine traverse of all three main summits of Mount Olympus (West Peak, Middle Peak, and East Peak), connecting multiple glacier systems and technical scrambles. Only for experienced mountaineers with strong fitness and solid decision-making.
routes.beta: After summiting the West Peak via the standard Blue Glacier route or a variation, descend to the saddle between West and Middle Peaks (this descent is not technical but involves careful route-finding and downclimbing). Cross to Middle Peak (7,929 feet), which has Class 3-4 scrambling. Continue to East Peak (7,762 feet) with additional Class 3-4 scrambling and potential crevassed terrain. Reverse the approach from the East Peak to descend back to Glacier Meadows via an alternative glacier system or retrace the standard route.
routes.approach: Same as standard Blue Glacier approach to West Peak. Trailhead, drive, and approach to Glacier Meadows are identical (17.5 miles).
routes.descent: This is where the traverse becomes complex: parties can descend from the East Peak via Glacier Pass (less commonly used) back to the base of the peaks and traverse back to Blue Glacier, or retrace the entire traverse back to West Peak and descend via the standard Blue Glacier route. Most parties opt for retracing.
routes.descentText: From the East Peak, carefully descend Class 3-4 scramble terrain to the saddle between East and Middle Peaks. Descend Middle Peak via Class 3 scrambling to the West-Middle saddle. Carefully descend to the base of the West Peak summit block and retrace the standard Blue Glacier descent route, or attempt a descent via an alternative glacier (Hoh Glacier) if conditions permit (not recommended for most parties).
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive and hike 17.5 miles to Glacier Meadows. Day 2: Acclimate/rest. Day 3: Depart pre-dawn from Glacier Meadows, climb to West Peak summit, traverse to Middle and East Peaks, and return to a high camp below the peaks or descend to Glacier Meadows (long day, 14-16+ hours). Day 4: If camped high, descend to Glacier Meadows. Complete the 17.5-mile hike out to trailhead. Fit parties may compress to 3 days; less fit or cautious parties should plan 4-5 days.
routes.bail: The primary bailout is to abandon the traverse at Middle Peak and descend back to West Peak, then descend via the standard route. Attempting to complete the traverse in marginal weather or darkness is dangerous; turn around at any peak if conditions worsen.
routes.turnaround: Turn around at Middle Peak if weather deteriorates, daylight is running short (should summit Middle Peak by mid-afternoon), if party members show fatigue or altitude symptoms, or if snow/ice makes safe passage between peaks unlikely.
routes.bestSeason: July-early August (peak season with most stable weather and longest daylight)
routes.rappels: Multiple rappels on descent if needed
objHaz: Crevasses between peaks and on glacier systems
Loose scrambling terrain on Middle and East Peaks
Exposure and fall hazard on narrow ridges
Fatigue and decision-making on long summit day
knownHazards: This is an advanced alpine objective requiring strong fitness, solid rock scrambling ability, and excellent weather judgment
The traverse requires 14-16+ hours of climbing from Glacier Meadows, making cumulative fatigue a significant hazard
Route-finding between peaks is not straightforward; poor visibility makes navigation difficult
watchOut: Do not attempt this route unless all party members are strong alpinists comfortable with exposure and scrambling
Commit to a turnaround time at Middle Peak (typically mid-afternoon) and stick to it, even if the East Peak looks close
The return trip down from East Peak is tiring and dangerous in poor light or marginal conditions; abort the traverse early if daylight is limited
proTips: Start very early (2-3 AM from Glacier Meadows) to maximize daylight hours
Identify waypoints on the route between peaks before starting (use binoculars from West Peak to study the route)
Consider caching lightweight gear at high camp below the peaks to minimize weight on traverse day
Most parties do not carry rock climbing gear for the peak traverse; the scrambling is 3rd and 4th class, not technical rock climbing
Watch the weather closely; a marginal forecast should result in canceling the traverse in favor of summiting West Peak only
whatToBring: Standard glacier climb gear (ice axe, crampons, rope, harness, crevasse rescue kit)
Minimal rock climbing gear (optional; most scrambling does not require roped belays)
Extra water (3L minimum) due to long summit day
High-calorie food for 16+ hours of climbing
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rock climbing gear; most scrambling is unroped. Bring 3-4 slings for potential rappel anchors or protection on steep sections if needed.
routes.proNeeds: Self-reliance and route-finding ability are critical. Parties should be proficient in crevasse rescue and comfortable with unroped scrambling on exposed terrain.

pitchDetail.notes: Same as standard route. Time: 2-2.5 hours to summit from Glacier Meadows base.

pitchDetail.pitch: West to Middle Peak traverse and descent
pitchDetail.notes: Descend to saddle between peaks, then ascend Middle Peak. Loose rock and exposure. Time: 1.5-2 hours.

pitchDetail.pitch: Middle Peak summit and traverse to East Peak
pitchDetail.notes: Traverse the narrow ridge from Middle to East Peak. Significant exposure on both sides. Time: 1.5-2 hours.

pitchDetail.pitch: East Peak descent and return traverse
pitchDetail.notes: Descend East Peak and retrace to West Peak. Time: 2-3 hours.

pitchDetail.pitch: West Peak descent via standard route
pitchDetail.notes: Descend summit block and East Face ramps via standard descent. Time: 3-4 hours to Glacier Meadows.

timing.recommendedStart: 2-3 AM from Glacier Meadows for maximum daylight

routes.overview: The standard and most popular route on Mount Olympus, ascending via the Blue Glacier and Snow Dome to reach the West Peak summit. This is an intermediate alpine climb with extensive glacier travel and moderate rock scrambling at the summit.
routes.beta: From Glacier Meadows, descend to and cross the Blue Glacier for approximately 1 mile with crevasse avoidance. Ascend the broad Snow Dome (6,600 feet) with careful snow bridge assessment. Navigate toward Crystal Pass at 7,250 feet, then traverse to the summit block base and complete the final Class 5.3-5.4 climbing to the true summit.
routes.approach: Drive to the Hoh Ranger Station at the end of Upper Hoh Road (31 miles south of Forks, WA off Highway 101). Hike the Hoh River Trail for 17.5 miles through old-growth rainforest to Glacier Meadows at 4,200 feet. The approach is snow-free much of the year and typically takes 6-8 hours for the first day.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route: rappel the summit block, descend Snow Dome and Crystal Pass, cross the Blue Glacier carefully, navigate back to the moraine, and complete the 17.5-mile hike out via Hoh River Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, establish a rappel anchor and descend 30-50m on the north side using a 40-60m rope. Carefully descend the Snow Dome, probing snow bridges and maintaining rope team protocols. Navigate through the crevasse field on the Blue Glacier with extreme caution, particularly late season when crevasses are widely opened. Once at Glacier Meadows, hike the Hoh River Trail for 8-10 hours to reach the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Hoh Ranger Station, hike 17.5 miles to Glacier Meadows, camp. Day 2: Rest/acclimate at Glacier Meadows. Day 3: Summit day from Glacier Meadows (10-13 hours); camp at Glacier Meadows or lower camp. Day 4: Descend Glacier Meadows to Olympus Guard Station. Day 5: Hike trailhead and drive home.
routes.bail: Bail from Glacier Meadows before summit attempt if weather is marginal. From Snow Dome, descend to Glacier Meadows in 4-5 hours. From the summit block, retreat via the same route if conditions deteriorate.
routes.turnaround: Turn around at Snow Dome if weather worsens, daylight is running short, or any team member shows significant fatigue or altitude symptoms.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through mid-August (peak season); late June for consolidated snow; late July to early August for stable weather and lower avalanche hazard.
objHaz: Crevasses and bergschrunds on Blue Glacier
Loose summit rock requiring careful footwork
knownHazards: The Blue Glacier is active and heavily crevassed; crevasse rescue proficiency is mandatory
Mount Olympus receives extreme precipitation; weather can deteriorate with minimal warning
The remote location means rescue is extremely difficult and time-consuming
watchOut: Crevasse rescue skills are essential, not optional
Do not attempt this route without proper glacier travel and crevasse rescue training and equipment
proTips: Start summit day very early (3-4 AM) to minimize rockfall hazard and afternoon weather
Carry a 60-meter rope late season to reduce rappel anchors
This is the most straightforward route to the summit, suitable for experienced intermediate alpinists
whatToBring: Full glacier mountaineering kit: ice axe, crampons, rope, harness, crevasse rescue gear
Alpine helmet and rock climbing shoes
Sleeping bag, pad, tent with winter rating
Bear canister and blue bags (NPS required)
routes.detailedRack: Small rock climbing rack of 6-10 cams and slings for the summit block rappel anchors and protection. Minimal gear needed.
routes.proNeeds: Solid glacier travel and crevasse rescue skills are required. All team members must carry prusiks, carabiners, and ascenders.

pitchDetail.notes: Approximately 1 mile of crevassed ice. Rope team of 3-4 spaced 6-8m apart. Time: 1-1.5 hours.
Broad snowfield to 6,600 feet. Watch for crevasses near Crystal Pass. Time: 1.5-2 hours.
Navigate ridgeline to summit block base. Time: 1-1.5 hours.
Final technical scramble to true summit. Time: 30 minutes to 1 hour.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn from Glacier Meadows

routes.overview: A scenic alpine route ascending the broad West Ridge to Mount Olympus's West Peak summit. This route offers a Class II alpine climb with moderate snow/ice climbing and lower crevasse hazard than the Blue Glacier route. First ascended by Gary Maykut, Len Miller, and Joe Witte in 1964.
routes.beta: The West Ridge route approaches from the southern side of the mountain, ascending a series of seven pitches along the broad ridge crest. Snow slopes and ice climbing dominate with Class 5.4 rock scrambles on the final pitches to the summit block. This route is less frequently done than the Blue Glacier standard but offers excellent alpine climbing and less crevasse hazard.
routes.approach: Approach via an alternative drainage on the south side of the mountain (specifics require detailed local knowledge or guidebook data; generally accessed from the lower Hoh River valley via bushwhacking or established way trails). Approach time is estimated at 1-2 days with significant elevation gain through rainforest terrain.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route or descend to connect with the Blue Glacier descent if retreat is necessary. Rappel stations are necessary on several pitches.
routes.descentText: Descend the West Ridge via rappels (6 rappels of up to 30m each are typical) or downclimbing on easier sections. Navigate back to base camp via the approach route.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Approach via southern drainage to high camp on West Ridge (4,000-5,000 feet). Day 2: Climb seven pitches of West Ridge to summit (8-10 hours from high camp). Day 3: Descend via rappels and hike out.
routes.bail: Bail from any high camp on the ridge by descending via the same route (rappels on descent).
routes.turnaround: Turn around at any pitch if weather worsens, rock or ice conditions are dangerous, or party is moving too slowly for daylight retreat.
routes.bestSeason: July-August (stable weather, moderate snow consolidation)
routes.rappels: 6 rappels of approximately 30m each on descent
objHaz: Ice and mixed terrain on ridge pitches
knownHazards: The West Ridge is less frequently climbed than the standard Blue Glacier route, so conditions may be less established and less documented
Route-finding may be more difficult without recent ascent information
watchOut: This route requires good alpine rock climbing and mixed terrain skills
Weather exposure on the ridge is significant; marginal forecasts should result in choosing the Blue Glacier route instead
proTips: This is an excellent alternative to the heavily traveled Blue Glacier route if you have solid mixed terrain skills
The seven-pitch format means breaking the climb into logical sections with potential rest/weather windows
whatToBring: Full alpine climbing kit: ice axe, crampons, rope, harness, helmet
Rock climbing rack for mixed terrain (cams, nuts, slings, quickdraws)
gear: Rock climbing rack with cams and nuts
routes.detailedRack: Mixed terrain rack: 10-15 cams (sizes 0.4-2.0 inches), 6-8 nuts, 8-10 quickdraws, multiple slings (60 and 120 cm), locking carabiners. Bring ice screws (2-3) for ice and névé anchor protection.
routes.proNeeds: Solid mixed terrain climbing ability and protection placement skills required. Knowledge of managing rappel anchors and descent under load is essential.

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend from high camp to establish ridge line. Time: 1-1.5 hours.
Climb ridge crest with Class 4 scrambles and snow/ice patches. Time: 1-1.5 hours.
Mix of rock scramble and 30-40 degree ice sections. Time: 1.5-2 hours.
Steeper mixed terrain with more sustained climbing. Time: 1.5-2 hours.
Steep ridge climbing with good protection placements. Time: 1.5-2 hours.
Climb to false summit area, more technical rock. Time: 1-1.5 hours.
Final push to true summit. Time: 1-1.5 hours.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from high camp on ridge

Philip Dickert, Jack Hossack & George MacGowan, 1936
routes.overview: A glacier-based alpine climb on the north slopes of Mount Challenger, involving 4+ miles of glacier traversal with crevasses and a bergshrund, leading to a steep final rock pitch with fixed protection.
routes.beta: Access via Hannegan Pass or Big Beaver Creek trailheads with multi-day approach through remote wilderness. The route gains the glacier via Easy Ridge or Wiley Ridge, then traverses moderate snowfields, crosses a bergschrund, and finishes with a Class 5 rock pitch on the summit horn. Fixed anchors exist for protection and rappels. The route sees relatively stable conditions July through early September; later season snowbridge degradation can make the bergshrund impassable.
routes.approach: From Hannegan Campground via Hannegan Pass and Easy Ridge (approximately 10-12 miles and 5+ hours to high camp) or from Big Beaver trailhead via water taxi and Wiley Ridge (approximately 13.5 miles trail plus 3-4 miles off-trail; 8-10 hours to high camp).
routes.descent: Reverse the route of ascent. Descend the Class 5 summit pitch via rappel from fixed anchors (approximately 50 feet). Cross back over the bergshrund and traverse the glacier at 7,000-7,200 feet to exit slopes. Either return via Perfect Pass and Easy Ridge (if using Hannegan approach) or descend via Wiley Ridge back to Beaver Pass then Big Beaver Trail to Ross Lake landing.
routes.descentText: From the summit horn, rappel approximately 50 feet to the fixed anchor station, which contains old pitons and fixed cams. Class 5 descent moves lead to easier terrain. Reverse the approach gully (loose rock present, exercise caution). Traverse the heavily crevassed Challenger Glacier in a rope team, downclimbing or descending carefully depending on conditions. At the glacier terminus, remove crampons and continue downhill to the saddle or pass exit. If taking the Wiley Ridge descent, follow the climbers' path downward through talus and heather back to high camp, then onward to Beaver Pass (4-5 hours from glacier exit). From Beaver Pass, return on the maintained Big Beaver Trail to the water taxi landing (6-7 hours) or Ross Lake trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (Hannegan approach): Hike 4 miles on good trail from Hannegan Campground to Hannegan Pass (gaining 1,900 ft), then descend to Chilliwack River and camp in valley (approximately 8-10 hours total). Alternatively (Big Beaver approach): Water taxi to Big Beaver Landing, hike 13.5 miles on trail to Beaver Pass, establishing base camp (6-7 hours). Day 2: From valley camp, ascend Easy Ridge to Perfect Pass and scout glacier approach, or if based at Beaver Pass, climb Wiley Ridge to high camp at 7,100 feet (4-6 hours depending on terrain and camps). Day 3: Alpine start (3-4 am), approach glacier base, rope up, traverse glacier to bergschrund (1-2 hours), climb snow arête to summit rock (1-2 hours), execute 5.5 final pitch (30-45 minutes), summit and descend summit pitch via rappel (30 minutes), return to high camp (2-3 hours total descent). Day 4: Descend Wiley Ridge or Easy Ridge to pass camp, then onward (4-6 hours). Day 5: Exit to landing or trailhead (6-8 hours).
routes.bail: If bergshrund is impassable or conditions deteriorate, retreat down the approach slopes to high camp (1-2 hours). If severe weather develops, descend immediately to the pass or lower camps. Bergschrund becomes increasingly problematic in late season (August+) when snowbridges degrade; early turnaround is critical. If the summit pitch cannot be safely climbed, descend the glacier and retreat via approach route. The remote location and limited escape routes make early commitment to retreat essential.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm time turnaround (typically 1-2 pm on summit day) to ensure safe descent before afternoon weather deterioration and to clear the glacier before dark. Do not wait for ideal conditions if approach to summit becomes time-consuming. The exposed glacier and bergschrund require careful assessment; if more than 2 hours behind typical progress by mid-morning, consider retreat. Rope team size and member fitness are limiting factors; turn back if safe movement speeds exceed 100 vertical feet per hour on approach slopes.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with early July most favored. By early July, snow has melted sufficiently in forested approach terrain, and the bergschrund typically retains a solid snowbridge. Mid to late July offers stable glacier conditions and lowest avalanche risk. August through early September remain viable, though bergshrunds become progressively impassable as seasonal snowbridges degrade, and some years the route becomes completely blocked by August. Plan 5-6 days total for the expedition. Winter ascents are possible but involve significantly higher hazard from snow stability, navigation difficulty, and extreme exposure to weather.
routes.comms: No cell phone coverage throughout the approach or on the peak. The remote wilderness location means satellite communicators (Garmin inReach, Zoleo) or personal locator beacons are essential for emergency contact. The National Park ranger dispatch is contactable through emergency channels (911 initially triggers ranger response), but satellite communication is the only reliable method for non-emergency contact from the wilderness.
routes.detailedRack: Minimal rock protection is required. Carry approximately 4 alpine cams ranging from 0.5 to 2 inches to supplement the fixed protection on the summit pitch. Bring alpine draws and a few runner slings for anchors on snow. One 50-60 meter dynamic rope is standard for glacier and summit rock travel. Crampons and ice axes are essential for all party members.
routes.proNeeds: The fixed protection on the summit pitch (old pitons and fixed cams) is generally sound but should not be relied upon as a single anchor. Carry a small rock rack to place additional pieces and build redundant anchors for belay and rappel stations. Glacier travel with crevasse rescue capability is required; all team members must be competent in self-rescue and hauling techniques.
whatToBring: Thermal base layers (long sleeves, long pants)
Stove and fuel for camp meals
First aid kit (extensive, for remote rescue)
proTips: Start very early (3-4 am) on summit day to maximize daylight for the glacier and summit rock, and ensure ample time for safe descent.
Scout the bergshrund approach on Day 2 if possible to assess snow condition and plan the crossing; late-season snowbridges degrade progressively.
Travel in a rope team on the glacier at all times; crevasse rescue skills are mandatory.
The fixed protection on the summit pitch is useful but not infallible; place independent anchors for belay and rappel.
The final 5.5 rock pitch is short but exposed and psychologically committing; bring confidence and secure footing.
Descending crampons are often unnecessary if the snow is consolidated; observe first ascensionists and adjust technique accordingly.
Insect pressure (hornets and yellow jackets) can be intense in the approach valleys during July-August; head nets and DEET are recommended.
Water is abundant from meltwater and streams during the approach; camp placement near water simplifies logistics.
Weather deterioration is rapid in the Pickets; maintain flexibility and a low commitment to summit.
Use trip reports from The Mountaineers or CascadeClimbers.com for current conditions before starting; alpine conditions change seasonally.
watchOut: Bergshrund variability: The snowbridge degrades significantly from early July through August; if it collapses or becomes unsafe, the route is impassable.
Steep exposed snow: Sections between the glacier and summit ridge involve 40-50 degree slopes with long fall potential if a climber slips; self-arrest must be practiced and reliable.
Class 5 technical rock: The final pitch to the summit is sustained Class 5 (5.5) on exposure; experience with protection placement and rappelling is essential.
Crevasse hazard: The Challenger Glacier features numerous crevasses with variable snow cover; crevasse rescue training is mandatory.
Moat crossings: Late-season moats (gaps between glacier and rock) may be 5+ feet wide, requiring careful navigation or traversing of loose snow/rock.
Loose rock on descent: The alternative descent gully contains loose, chossy rock; stay on the primary descent route.
Weather exposure: The Pickets have a reputation for rapid and severe weather; afternoon thunderstorms and winds are common; retreat if dark clouds approach.
Route finding: Off-trail sections (Wiley Ridge approach) require map/compass skill; poor visibility can lead to becoming cliffed out or lost in gullies.
Insects: Yellow jackets and hornets are aggressive in mid-summer; consider head nets and avoid wearing dark colors that attract insects.
Limited bailout: The remote location offers few emergency egress options once committed above high camp; weather and injury response times are delayed.
knownHazards: Bergshrunds present a significant seasonal hazard; the snowbridge supporting the bergschrund deteriorates rapidly mid-season and may become impassable by late July.

Objective avalanche hazard exists on the steep snow arête approaching the summit; generally low probability due to the north aspect and typically consolidated snow, but the line offers minimal runout protection.
Rockfall and icefall risk is low on the standard approach but present near seracs on the lower glacier; travel during stable conditions (morning/cool hours).
Climber-induced rappel hazards: The summit rappel uses old fixed anchors (pitons) that are legacy equipment; supplementary protection should always be placed.
The 5.5 rock pitch features exposed moves with high consequences for a slip; solid belay and backup systems are essential.
Crevasse falls remain the primary accident hazard on the glacier; poor rope management or inadequate team competency can be fatal.
Exhaustion and altitude (8,207 ft) combine with remote location to increase risk of poor decision-making; maintain hydration and nutrition throughout the climb.
Hypothermia risk is ever-present; the north aspect and glacier environment remain cold even in summer, and exposure to wind or wet conditions accelerates heat loss.

pitchDetail.notes: Approximately 50 feet to the summit horn. Fixed pitons and an old fixed cam are present but should not be relied upon as the sole anchor. The crux involves low-5.5 moves on reasonably solid rock. Belayed ascent is standard; an inverted V rappel anchor is established at the top by modern parties. Excellent protection available from both natural features and fixed gear. The moves are sustained and exposed.

road.name: Highway 542 to Hannegan Campground (Ruth Creek Road) or Highway 20 to Ross Lake trailhead
road.status: Open mid-June through late October; may close earlier/later depending on snow
road.seasonalGate: Ruth Creek Road typically gated November-May; check with Marblemount Wilderness Info Center
road.driveNote: From Highway 542 near Glacier, WA, turn on Road 32, then Ruth Creek Road 1.3 miles, continue 5.4 miles to Hannegan Campground. Alternative (Big Beaver): Highway 20 to Ross Lake Dam trailhead parking.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades, Cascade West (Northwest Avalanche Center zone); high alpine exposure, frequent weather changes
climate.typical: Rapidly changing conditions with frequent afternoon thunderstorms July-August. Morning clear; afternoon clouds and precipitation common. Temperatures range 30-50F depending on elevation and time of day. Wind often increases afternoon onward.
climate.spring: Significant snowpack persists through June; route may not be fully accessible until early July. Avalanche hazard elevated from snowmelt and sun exposure.
climate.summer: Most stable season (July-August). Early morning clear, afternoon thunderstorms common. Temperatures 40-60F on glacier, 50-65F at lower camps. Bergshrund undergoes rapid seasonal degradation. Wind variable.
climate.fall: September through early October sees more stable (less daily precipitation) but increasingly cold conditions. First frost common by early October. Shorter daylight requires earlier starts. Bergshrund likely impassable by mid-September.
climate.winter: November through May: Extremely heavy snowfall, avalanche hazard, and profound cold (5-20F). Route rarely attempted; hazard level is very high. Not recommended for most parties.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park, with adjacent Ross Lake National Recreation Area)
access.permit: Backcountry camping permit required ($10 per person + $6 non-refundable reservation fee per group; youth 15 and under free). Mandatory orientation video must be watched before permit issuance. Permits available online via Recreation.gov or in-person at Marblemount Wilderness Information Center.
access.passRequired: National Park Pass (America the Beautiful annual or daily) or Northwest Forest Pass (daily or annual). Entry fee approximately $30/vehicle (day use) or $80 annual pass.
access.fees: $10 per person + $6 permit fee (summer season, mid-May through early October); free off-season
access.closures: Park typically fully open June-October; North Cascades Highway (542) may close November-April depending on snow. Upper backcountry may be inaccessible before July due to snow. Some passes and ridges may be snow-blocked through June.

timing.recommendedStart: Early July for optimal conditions; mid-July through early August acceptable. Avoid late August+ due to bergshrund degradation.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (primarily); parts also in Skagit County
emergency.rangerStation: Marblemount Wilderness Information Center, (360) 854-7245; North Cascades Visitor Center in Newhalem, (206) 386-4495 ext. 11
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA, (360) 734-5400, approximately 60-70 miles from trailhead; evacuation by helicopter is the most likely emergency response
emergency.notes: The remote Picket Range location means rescue response times are extended (helicopter rescue only in good weather). Satellite communication is strongly recommended for emergency contact. North Cascades/Mount Rainier/Olympic parks share a dedicated short-haul rescue helicopter; response is weather-dependent.

blurb: Mount Challenger (8,207 ft) is the northern anchor of Washington's Picket Range in North Cascades National Park, a remote alpine massif known for steep cascading ridges and active glaciers. The peak sports five major summits and is accessed via multi-day approaches through dense wilderness, making it one of the most remote technical climbs in the lower 48 states. The normal route ascends the Challenger Glacier on the north face, involving glacier travel with crevasses, bergshrund passage, and a striking Class 5.5 rock pitch to the main summit, with first ascent dating to September 1936 by Dickert, Hossack, and MacGowan.

Amphitheatre Mountain is an 8,358-foot granite peak in the Pasayten Wilderness of Washington's Okanogan Range, renowned for its 3/4-mile north wall hosting numerous established and potential multipitch rock routes. The peak was first ascended in 1901 and has become a destination for alpinists seeking classic Grade II-III alpine trad climbs with approaches exceeding 20 miles through remote Cascade terrain.

routes.beta: Exceptional granite fingers-to-thin-hands splitter with a series of left-leaning roofs surmounted by hand cracks and jugs. Onsight first ascent; considered one of the finest modern additions to the north face.
routes.overview: A striking steep crack climb on the north face's Middle Finger Buttress featuring excellent granite splitter cracks and powerful roof sequences.
A scenic alpine scramble up the blunt north ridge with several pitches of easy rock climbing through gully steps between rock formations.
routes.beta: Start from Cathedral Pass and scramble up to where roping is possible. Ascend the steepest sections of the blunt North Ridge through multiple steps (short steep gully notches between). Sections of loose boulders and route-finding challenges are common.
routes.approach: From Cathedral Pass, scramble up to where it's possible to rope up at approximately 7,600 feet.
routes.descent: Two options: (1) Walk off to the southwest (long walk around Amphitheatre back to Upper Cathedral Lake) or (2) descend via a small col southwest of the North Ridge through a 3rd class gully down the north slope.
routes.descentText: From the summit plateau, descend north via a 3rd class gully to a col southwest of the North Ridge, then work your way down the north slope using a mix of rock steps and scree. Alternatively, traverse south along the ridge crest and walk off to the southwest for a longer but less steep descent.
watchOut: Loose rock throughout
Route-finding can be challenging
Sections between steps require careful climbing
routes.detailedRack: Light rack: nuts and long slings for protection on the pitches

routes.overview: A substantial multipitch crack climb on the right side of the Middle Finger Buttress, ascending steep slabs and crack systems on the northwest aspect.
routes.beta: Ascends the cracks on the northwest side of the Middle Finger Buttress above Upper Cathedral Lake. Begin on a steep slab with a thin vertical crack. The short crux section occurs on the 3rd pitch. Route finding is straightforward once on the rock.
routes.approach: From Upper Cathedral Lake, walk around the lake on the west side and approach the buttress. Scramble approximately 15 minutes to reach the starting slab.
routes.descent: Hike east along the ridge crest to a small col southwest of the North Ridge, then descend via a 3rd class gully down the north slope.
routes.detailedRack: Bring a full range of protection including nuts and cams for the varied crack widths
watchOut: Crux on pitch 3 requires solid 5.9 climbing
Exposed sections above loose ground

routes.overview: A classic 4-pitch alpine trad climb on the left side of the Middle Finger Buttress, featuring exceptional hand crack climbing and exposed granite pitches.
routes.beta: The largest and more western buttress above Upper Cathedral Lake. Begins in a deep chimney on the left side (with optional belay at top) before undertaking an amazing hand crack. Pitches 3-4 include moderate terrain with non-sustained climbing and perhaps a fifth pitch dependent on route choice.
routes.approach: From Upper Cathedral Lake, walk around the lake on the west side and up to the buttress. The scramble to reach the starting chimney takes approximately 15 minutes.
routes.descent: Hike east along the ridge crest to a col southwest of the North Ridge, then descend via a 3rd class gully down the north slope.

pitchDetail.notes: Begin in a chimney (optional belay at top) before undertaking the amazing hand crack. This is the route's hardest and highest quality section. 60m.
Climb upward then traverse right along a dihedral continuation. 40m.
Moderate terrain on the upper section
Non-sustained climbing, perhaps a fifth pitch dependent on route choice

routes.detailedRack: Stoppers and doubled carabiners from BD #0.3 to #2, plus #3 size pieces for upper pitches
routes.proNeeds: Good hand crack technique essential for pitch 1; solid 5.9 climbing ability required
watchOut: Pitch 1 is the crux and highest quality section
Upper pitches are more moderate

routes.overview: A classic 4-pitch alpine trad climb on the Ka'aba Buttress featuring excellent granite cracks, exposed climbing, and stunning views of the Pasayten Wilderness.
routes.beta: Located on the Ka'aba Buttress (named for Mecca's holiest shrine), an Islamic-themed feature on Amphitheater. The northwestern aspect means the route is in shade most of the day until around 3pm. The route goes quickly on good rock; the FA party completed it in sub-two hours around 5pm and was back in camp by 8pm.
routes.approach: Walk around the lake on the west side of Upper Cathedral Lake and up to the buttress. The scramble to reach the starting block takes approximately 15 minutes from the lake.
routes.descent: Walk east to Amphitheatre saddle, then descend the south trail back to Upper Cathedral Lake (approximately 2 miles).
routes.descentText: Two descent options: (1) A north-side gully providing a short half-mile walk-off, or (2) a longer scenic route circling south around Amphitheater for a 2-mile walk returning to Upper Cathedral Lake.

pitchDetail.notes: Triple crack system leading to a roof
Exposed flake with layback moves to a sandy ledge
The crux, featuring a wild twin handcrack
Low-angle offwidth corner with multiple finish options

routes.detailedRack: Doubles from 0.3 to 3 size for main climbing; one #4 cam, complete nut set, and small cams for gear anchors
routes.proNeeds: Solid hands and crack technique essential; exposed climbing on pitch 2 and 3; comfortable with offwidth moves on pitch 4
watchOut: Pitch 3 crux with thin handcrack is the technical crux
Pitch 4 offwidth requires solid technique
proTips: Start early in the day to get afternoon sun on the lower pitches
Pitch 3 twin handcrack is world-class climbing
Excellent route quality overall

blurb: Kangaroo Temple is a prominent granite spire on Kangaroo Ridge in Washington Pass, part of the North Cascades. First climbed in 1942 by Fred and Helmy Beckey via the Northwest Face, this accessible alpine rock route is a popular multiday climb with three solid moderate routes offering reliable crack climbing on sound granite.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy noted: database lists 7,574 ft, but authoritative sources (Mountain Project, WTA, Peakbagger, SummitPost, Lists of John) consistently cite 7,572 ft. Coordinates 48.5143, -120.6425 confirmed from multiple sources. Peak location verified as Kangaroo Ridge, Washington Pass, North Cascades, Okanogan National Forest.

routes.overview: The most popular and least technical route on Kangaroo Temple, the North Face ascends a moderately steep granite face with three well-protected pitches, bolted anchors, and excellent exposure for a medium-length alpine rock climb.
routes.beta: A solid, short alpine rock route consisting of three pitches on granite with excellent anchor systems. The route is mostly low 5th class climbing with sound rock quality throughout most of the climb, though the first pitch contains some loose material. Multiple fixed anchors and bolts make the route straightforward and relatively safe for well-executed rock climbing skills. The climb features excellent views of Liberty Bell and the surrounding North Cascades.
routes.approach: Drive WA 20 (North Cascades Highway) to Washington Pass, then continue 1 mile east to the hairpin turn (large turnout on the outside of the 180-degree switchback). Park in the large turnout. From the hairpin, walk south through the boulder field following a faint climber's trail (sometimes cairned) leading into the woods. Follow the trail through forest and boulder fields, staying to the right of timber where it widens into a valley. The trail becomes well-defined as it gains elevation. Continue to Kangaroo Pass (approximately 1-1.5 hours from parking). From Kangaroo Pass, traverse left on a faint trail toward the gully between Kangaroo Temple and Little Finger; the actual base of the North Face is approximately 1 hour from the pass.
routes.descentText: Locate the three bolts with hangers at the top of the North Face via exposed scrambling (bolts can be difficult to see until very close). Set up two double-length (50-meter) rappels from the initial bolts and then from either the next set of bolts or a flake and tree located approximately 30 feet below. These two rappels return climbers to the notch where the climb began. Alternatively, use three single-rope rappels (with 60-meter ropes) to the notch. Take care during rappels as the first ledge is blocky and loose, making rope retrieval difficult. A double-length rappel directly from the summit is not recommended due to increased risk of the rope getting stuck and potential for rockfall during rope retrieval.
routes.descent: 2-3 rappels to notch, 50m ropes sufficient
routes.bestSeason: The optimal climbing season is July through August, when snow has melted and weather is most stable. Late June climbing is possible but may have residual snow on the approach. Early season (May-June) requires crampons and an ice ax for persistent snow on the approach, and parking at the hairpin may be blocked by snowbanks. September climbing is feasible but weather becomes less predictable. Access is closed November through April when the pass is shut by heavy snowfall.
routes.detailedRack: A standard rack progressing to 3 inches is required for protection. Bring double-length ropes or two 50-meter ropes for safe descent. Bolted anchors exist at the top and intermediate points, but a trad rack is necessary for climbing pitch protection.
proTips: The route is mostly low 5th class but well-protected; don't be intimidated by the 5.6 rating
The first pitch has some loose rock, so communicate carefully with your belayer and avoid kicking material
The step-across move on pitch 3 is exposed but straightforward with adequate handholds
Rappel anchors can be hard to locate; mark them carefully during the ascent
Two 50m ropes make descent easier than managing three single-rope rappels
Start early to avoid crowds and ensure safe daylight descent
watchOut: Loose rock on first pitch; belayer should position outside the fall line
The approach gully contains loose, dirty rock; move carefully to minimize dislodging material
Blocky, loose ledges on descent create rockfall hazard; retrieve ropes carefully to avoid rock slides
Exposed moves throughout; solid footwork and focus required
Weather changes rapidly at 7,500 feet; be prepared to descend if conditions deteriorate
knownHazards: Loose granite on the first pitch necessitates conservative belay positioning
The approach via the loose gully below the north face is the most dangerous section for rockfall
Significant exposure on all three pitches; a fall during climbing would be serious
Rappel ropes can snag on sharp granite edges during retrieval; use care and consider webbing protection

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend an angled crack through broken rock over multiple ledges. Some loose material on this pitch; belayer should avoid the direct line below the climber. Bolted rap anchors at the top.
Brief traverse right along a ledge system; find a hidden crack shoulder-high at the base of a steep wall for belay anchor.

Ascend with a committing step around a blind corner (very exposed), then navigate upward past a small hand hold on the left wall. Finish at a belay tree or bolts. Remainder of climb is very solid granite.

waypoints.name: North Face Base / Start of Climb

road.name: WA 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open year-round to Concrete; closed Nov-Apr at Washington Pass
road.seasonalGate: Washington Pass gate, typically closed November to April
road.driveNote: Drive to Washington Pass (elevation 5,477 ft), then continue 1 mile east to the hairpin turn. Large parking turnout on the outside of the 180-degree switchback. No permit required for parking or camping in the forest.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (formerly Okanogan National Forest)
access.closures: Washington Pass typically closed Nov-Apr due to heavy snowfall; check WSDOT before driving

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with moisture-laden Pacific fronts. Summer is typically clear and dry; winter brings heavy snowfall.
climate.spring: Snow and ice persist through May; approach requires crampons and ice ax. Snowbanks may block parking.
climate.summer: Typically clear skies with stable weather. July and August offer the best conditions. Occasional afternoon thunderstorms possible.
climate.fall: Weather becomes increasingly unsettled in September. Possible snow at higher elevations by late September.
climate.winter: Pass closed Nov-Apr; heavy snowfall and avalanche hazard.

emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Walla Walla River Ranger District (nearest); Concrete Ranger Station (alternate)
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Sedro-Woolley, ~45 miles southwest) or North Cascades Hospital (Marblemount, ~40 miles southwest)
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable at Washington Pass; consider satellite communicator for emergency response. Nearest ranger station should be contacted before departure if climbing in early season (snow/avalanche risk). Mountain rescue response times can be significant from this remote location.

routes.comms: Cell service unreliable to absent at Washington Pass; expect no signal on approach and peak. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, SPOT) recommended for solo climbers or small parties.

blurb: Half Moon Crag is a granite alpine climbing area visible from Highway 2 near Stevens Pass in Washington's Central Cascades. All routes are approximately 85 feet tall with a mix of slab and crack climbing on trad and sport gear. The south-facing crag offers excellent summer climbing conditions and is documented in David Whitelaw's guidebook Weekend Rock Washington.

routes.overview: The steepest line on Half Moon Crag, featuring sustained slab climbing with positive holds and consistent gear placements.
routes.beta: Predominantly bolted with mixed trad protection. Single 60m rope adequate. South-facing provides good sun exposure.
routes.approach: From Tye Road (old Stevens Pass Highway) parking 1.4 miles downhill from US 2 at the double hairpin turn, head uphill through open old growth forest. Follow faint trail trending left across a drainage to reach the base of the crag. Approximately 1000 feet of elevation gain over 45-60 minutes.
routes.descent: Single rappel down the route using fixed anchors at top of climb.
routes.descentText: Rap down from the bolted anchors at the top of the climb using a single 85-foot rappel to the base.
routes.itinerary: Day trip: 0.5 hour drive from Seattle, 1-1.5 hour approach hike, 1-2 hours for climb and setup, 30 minutes descent.
routes.bail: Rappel off the route at any belay station.
routes.turnaround: Descend if weather deteriorates, if conditions look unsafe, or if you are not comfortable with the difficulty.
routes.bestSeason: June through September provides the most stable and dry conditions. May can be wet, October may have early snow.
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable in crag area beneath Stevens Pass ridge. Satellite communicator recommended for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: Light sport rack with 12+ quickdraws for bolted protection. Bring wired stoppers and medium-sized nuts for occasional slab placements. Single 60m dynamic rope, belay device, 4-6 locking carabiners.
routes.proNeeds: Lead climber comfortable on 5.10b slab with bolted protection. Basic trad skills for occasional nut placements helpful but not essential.
proTips: The huge Refrigerator Crack at the base stays cool even in summer heat
Start early to avoid afternoon crowds and heat
South aspect makes this ideal for spring climbing
watchOut: Watch for rockfall from higher climbing parties
Route can be hot on sunny days despite south exposure
Approach has minimal trail; easy to navigate incorrectly in fog
knownHazards: Rockfall potential from parties climbing above
Sun exposure on south face can lead to dehydration

pitchDetail.notes: Sustained slab climbing with positive edges and consistent crack protection. Mix of bolted anchors and natural gear placements. Ends at fixed anchor at top of crag.

road.name: Tye Road (Old Stevens Pass Highway)
road.status: Open year-round
road.driveNote: From US 2 at Stevens Pass, take Tye Road downhill 1.4 miles toward Iron Horse Trailhead. Park at pullout just uphill of double hairpin turn. Road can have snow/ice in winter.

climate.forecastZone: Stevens Pass area
climate.typical: Continental mountain climate with significant precipitation. Summer highs 70-78F, lows 48-55F. Afternoon winds common.
climate.spring: Cool to cold with possible snow above 4000 feet until June.
climate.summer: Dry and warm with occasional afternoon thunderstorms. 70-78F highs, minimal precipitation.
climate.fall: Cooling trend, increasing precipitation. September still good, October increasingly wet/snowy.
climate.winter: Heavy snow, sub-freezing temperatures. Road access can be compromised.

access.landManager: US Forest Service (Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest)
access.closures: Winter snowpack may restrict access. Check road conditions before driving Tye Road.

emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA 98065
emergency.notes: Approximately 45 minutes to hospital. Helicopter evacuation possible via King County Sheriff Air Support Unit.

routes.overview: A classic moderate crack and slab route with excellent gear placements and straightforward climbing.
routes.beta: Excellent mix of bolted anchors and natural protection points. Slab features positive holds and consistent climbing angle. South-facing receives good sun exposure.
routes.approach: From Tye Road parking at double hairpin turn 1.4 miles from US 2, head uphill through open old growth forest. Follow faint trail trending left across drainage to crag base. Approximately 1000 feet of gain over 45-60 minutes.
routes.descent: Single 85-foot rappel from fixed anchors.
routes.descentText: Rappel directly down the route using anchors at climb exit.
routes.itinerary: Full day outing: 0.5 hour drive from Seattle, 1.25 hour hike, 1.5-2 hours climbing, 0.5 hour descent.
routes.bail: Safe bail points at any belay location via rappel.
routes.turnaround: Descend if weather or conditions change.
routes.bestSeason: June through September for optimal climbing conditions.
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable; satellite communicator recommended for emergencies.

hierarchyNote: CRITICAL: Peak "Ed Wood Memorial Buttress" and route "Plan 9 from Outer Space" cannot be verified to exist in any major climbing databases (Mountain Project, SummitPost, PeakBagger, theCrag) despite comprehensive research. The FA credit lists "M.Layton/E.Wolfe 2006" but verified first ascents by these climbers reference Southeast Mox Peak (The Devil's Club, 2005) in the North Cascades. A "Memorial Buttress" exists in Central-East Cascades near Leavenworth but contains different routes. No coordinates or elevation provided in source data to verify geographic identity. Recommend verifying peak name and route existence before further enrichment.

routes.corrections: Route cannot be verified in any climbing database. First ascent credit (M.Layton/E.Wolfe) verified to have made first ascent of 'The Devil's Club' on Southeast Mox Peak, North Cascades in September 2005 (not 2006).

blurb: Middle Peak is the highest summit in the remote Gunsight Range, a group of white granite spires located in the Glacier Peak Wilderness east of the Cascades crest, surrounded by the Chickamin and Blue glaciers. The peak features some of the cleanest alpine climbing in the North Cascades with excellent granite rock quality. Access requires a rugged 2-day approach.

routes.overview: A major traverse connecting all four Gunsight peaks (Northeast, North, Middle, South), offering sustained alpine crack climbing on excellent granite with breathtaking remote alpine positioning.
routes.beta: Traverse all four major summits on north-facing ascents and south-facing descents. Features 18 pitches of mostly new climbing: three pitches of 5.10 crack climbing with one aid point used to escape a dead-end fist crack near North Peak summit. Rappel descent includes free-hanging rappel over moat onto Blue Glacier in darkness.
routes.approach: From Downey Creek Trailhead on Suiattle River Road (elev. 1400 ft). Follow Downey Creek and Bachelor Creek to Bachelor Flats (4500 ft), then Bachelor Meadows, Cub Pass, Cub Lake, Itswoot Pass, Chikamin-Dome Col to Chikamin Glacier. Total approach distance 12.8 miles with 5,700 ft gain to Itswoot Ridge, then 3.2 miles to Dome-Chickamin Col base camp.
routes.descent: Rappel south-facing sides via 6+ rappels on 50-60m lines. Final rappel is free-hanging over moat onto Blue Glacier, typically in darkness. Total descent 3-4 hours.
routes.descentText: Descend south-facing ridges by rappelling on 50-60 meter lines. Navigate bergschrund and crevasse field on Chickamin Glacier. Final free-hanging rappel over moat onto Blue Glacier. Exercise caution: rappel anchors on granite must be thoroughly checked. Descents often completed in darkness. Total time 3-4 hours depending on snow and crevasse conditions.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Downey Creek Trailhead to Bachelor Flats camp (6 miles, 3,100 ft gain). Day 2: Bachelor Flats to Dome-Chickamin Col base camp (3.2 miles, 2,600 ft gain). Day 3: Climb Gunrunner traverse of four peaks (6-8 hours climbing time, 1,500 ft gain). Day 4: Descend to base camp, then return to Bachelor Flats (5-6 hours). Day 5: Return to trailhead (6 miles, 4,500 ft loss).
routes.bail: Retreat from base camp via same Chikamin Glacier route. Any point on the traverse can bail by descending south-facing slopes to Chikamin Glacier. In bad weather or emergency, descend via Dome Peak bypass route to western slopes.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if: 1) Weather deteriorates with precipitation or lightning risk, 2) Cloud cover reduces visibility below 100 feet, 3) Late afternoon arrival at base of Gunrunner route (after 2pm), 4) Any member shows signs of altitude sickness or exhaustion beyond safety margins, 5) Bergschrund or crevasse field conditions deteriorate during approach.
routes.bestSeason: July and early August offer the best conditions. Early July may have lingering snow and wider bergschrunds; late August risks deteriorating weather and warming patterns. Peak climbing window: July 15-August 15. Bluebird weather most reliable mid-July to early August. Route is rarely done outside July-August due to snow and glacier conditions.
routes.comms: Cell coverage unreliable throughout approach and on peaks. The area is in a deep valley with no reliable signal. Emergency communication may require satellite communicator (InReach, Spot). The nearest ranger station is Chelan Ranger District.
routes.detailedRack: Full range of cracks and aid rack recommended: 1-2x each of 0.3-1.5 inch Alien or TC Pro cams, complete medium-sized stopper set, hexes for medium-sized placements, 0.5-1 inch pitons for aid, 2-3 slings of various sizes, 2 ice axes, crampons, ice tools if early season (possible water ice), 2-3 quickdraws, carabiners, prussiks, cordelette for rappel anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Full trad rack required. Splitter cracks on north faces provide excellent natural protection. Anchors are typically solid granite with bomber placements. Rappel anchors should be redundant and well-tested before weighting. No fixed anchors remain on route.
gear: Full rock climbing rack (cams, stoppers, hexes)
whatToBring: Sun protection (high SPF sunscreen, glacier glasses)
proTips: Scout the notch between North and Middle peaks before dark to identify crevasse crossings and rappel locations.
Mark rappel anchors clearly and double-check all anchors before rappelling.
The dead-end fist crack near North Peak is the only place where aid is necessary; move through it quickly.
Travel on glaciers in early morning before sun softens snow bridges.
Use two ropes for rappels to maintain safety margin and enable quick evacuation if needed.
Coordinate timing to reach major rappel points before darkness; plan for slow descents.
The five-mile traverse is deceptively long; don't underestimate time needed between peaks.
watchOut: Rappel anchors deteriorate with each party; always back-up and test thoroughly.
Free-hanging rappel over moat is exposed and requires full concentration.
Crevasse field changes seasonally; route-finding may differ from previous ascents.
Weather can shift rapidly in this remote location with little warning.
Stream water at base camp can be swift and dangerous; use caution when crossing.
Insects can be overwhelming in early August during base camp stays.
knownHazards: Steep exposed rappels in descent, including free-hanging rappel over moat onto Blue Glacier in darkness
Crevasse field maze on Chickamin Glacier with seasonal variation in crossing points
Bergschrund widens seasonally and may require difficult bypasses or boulder-hopping
Fist crack dead-end on North Peak requires aid to escape; climbers have gotten stuck here
Rockfall hazard on south-facing descent gullies during afternoon warming
Potential icefall hazard on Blue Glacier approaches
Dense vegetation and deadfall on descent through Bachelor Meadows creates tripping hazards
routes.rappels: 6-8 rappels on 50-60m lines; final rappel is free-hanging over moat

climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Afternoon thunderstorms common July-August. Temperatures at 8,200 ft range 40-60F. High UV index due to elevation and glacier reflection.

climate.summer: Best climbing window. July averages highs 65-75F at base camp, 40-50F on peaks. August similar with increasing instability. Frequent afternoon thunderstorms. Most snow gone by late July.
climate.fall: Route becomes difficult in September; snow returns to higher passes. Not recommended outside July-August.
climate.winter: Route is not practical in winter; approach requires advanced winter mountaineering skills.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep splitter granite hand crack; good protection; warm-up pitch on northeast face of Northeast Peak
Twin hand cracks and right-facing flake; excellent protection; sustained climbing
Face climbing on northeast face of Northeast Peak

Crack climbing on North Peak north face

Traverse from North to Middle Peak notch; three pitches of moderate climbing in the notch (only section previously climbed)
Middle Gunsight north face; sustained crack climbing with excellent protection; four pitches of quality granite

South Peak summit approach; short final pitch to highest point of South Gunsight

routes.overview: A stunning 6-7 pitch alpine climb on the east face of Middle Gunsight featuring exceptional granite quality with a hidden line that unfolds piece by piece offering mostly continuous 5.9-5.10+ climbing in a stunning remote setting.
routes.beta: Climb the East Face of Middle Gunsight via a hidden line visible from Blue Glacier. After a short warmup section, climbing is in the 5.9-5.10+ range throughout most of the route. Route features twin hand cracks and flake at the start of pitch 2 visible from the glacier. The route offers mostly continuous climbing with excellent rock quality and exposure.
routes.approach: From Dome-Chickamin Col base camp, traverse and ascend Chickamin Glacier to the base of the east face of Middle Gunsight. Typically reached in 2-3 hours from camp. Route is clearly visible from the glacier; approach via the glacier past crevasse fields to reach the base of the east face rock.
routes.descent: Rappel the south-facing side of the peak (typically 3-4 rappels on 50-60m lines) to avoid the climb-back problem of ascending the east face descent.
routes.descentText: After summiting, traverse south along the summit ridge to the south-facing rappel anchors. Perform 3-4 rappels on 50-60 meter lines to descend the south face, which is protected from rockfall. Navigate the crevasse field on Chickamin Glacier to return to base camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Downey Creek Trailhead to Bachelor Flats camp (6 miles, 3,100 ft gain). Day 2: Bachelor Flats to Dome-Chickamin Col base camp (3.2 miles, 2,600 ft gain). Day 3: Climb East Face of Middle Gunsight (4-6 hours climbing time, 600 ft gain) and descend to base camp (3-4 hours). Day 4: Return to Bachelor Flats (5-6 hours). Day 5: Return to trailhead (6 miles, 4,500 ft loss).
routes.bail: Turn back from any pitch and descend via snow gulley or scramble to the south face for easier exit options. Crevasse field on glacier provides forced exit if base camp is unreachable.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if: 1) Any pitch takes significantly longer than expected (more than 45 minutes), 2) Weather deteriorates with precipitation or wind gusts above 30 mph, 3) Late afternoon arrival at base of rock (after 3pm), 4) Any team member shows signs of altitude sickness or exhaustion.
routes.bestSeason: July through early August. Early July may have lingering snow and ice on lower pitches; late August risks deteriorating weather. Peak climbing window: July 15-August 15. Route best climbed in stable weather windows of 2-3 days.
routes.comms: Cell coverage unreliable throughout approach and on peaks. The area is in a deep valley with no reliable signal. Emergency communication may require satellite communicator. Nearest ranger station is Chelan Ranger District.
routes.detailedRack: Medium-sized rock climbing rack: 1-2x each of 0.5-1.5 inch cams, medium stopper set, 0.5-1 inch pitons for aid (minimal), 2-3 slings, 2-3 quickdraws, ice tools and crampons if early season or water ice present, single 50-60m rope sufficient for climbing, 2x 50m ropes for rappelling.
routes.proNeeds: Full trad protection on well-formed cracks and pockets. Most placements are solid granite with good rock quality. Anchors can be built with cams and slings; no fixed anchors present. Rappel anchors should be redundant with bomber placements.
gear: Medium trad climbing rack (0.5-1.5 inch cams, stoppers)
proTips: The twin hand cracks and flake at pitch 2 are distinctive landmarks visible from the glacier; they mark the best line up the face.
Excellent rock quality throughout; expect good protection on nearly all pitches.
The route unfolds well with minimal route-finding complications.
Consider traversing to North Gunsight West Face (5.11+) if conditions are favorable and time permits; it's nearby.
Early start from camp (dawn) ensures sufficient daylight for climbing and descent.
watchOut: Rappel anchors must be thoroughly checked; no fixed anchors remain from first ascent.
The east face has excellent but exposed rock; falls are serious.
Crevasse field on glacier approach is significant; cross in early morning before snow bridges soften.
Afternoon thunderstorms can develop rapidly; watch for lightning risk on exposed east face.
Descent via south-facing slopes can have rockfall hazard during afternoon warming.
knownHazards: Exposed alpine climbing on east face with significant fall hazard
Crevasse fields on Chickamin Glacier approach and descent
Bergschrund may require boulder-hopping or bypass
Afternoon rockfall hazard on south-facing rappel descent
Rapid weather changes can trap climbers in exposed positions
Icefall hazard on Blue Glacier adjacent to eastern slopes
routes.rappels: 3-4 rappels on 50-60m lines to descend south face
