blurb: Juno Tower is a 7,920 ft granite spire on Vasiliki Ridge just north of Burgundy Col in the North Cascades, distinguished by steep eastern walls rising 1,500 feet above the Silver Star Creek valley. The peak's east face hosts Clean Break, discovered in the mid-1980s and regarded as one of the finest alpine rock routes in Washington.
hierarchyNote: Elevation differs slightly across sources: Mountain Project lists 7,920 ft, while ListsOfJohn and Peakbagger list 7,940 ft. Coordinates (48.56281, -120.59725) are accurate per Mountain Project. Peak is correctly located on Vasiliki Ridge north of Burgundy Col in Okanogan County, Washington.

routes.overview: Clean Break rises 1,500 feet above the valley floor on the steep east buttress of Juno Tower, taking its name from the immaculate splitter-crack at pitch 1. With 7 of 15 pitches at 5.10 grade, this sustained granite route is regarded as one of the finest long multi-pitch climbs at this grade in Washington.
routes.beta: The route is characterized by sustained hand crack climbing through the first ten pitches, with notable splitter cracks throughout. Pitches 11-15 step down to mid-5th class terrain and can be simul-climbed from a ledge system. Route-finding requires care on the lower pitches in the talus approach section.
routes.approach: Drive State Route 20 to the Silver Star Creek pullout, 8.5 miles east of Washington Pass. Follow the established climbers trail up the east side of the drainage for 2 miles through forest. At 5,000 feet elevation, make a rising traverse across slide alder to reach the steep clean crack at the base of the prow, left of a deep narrow gully.
routes.descent: Traverse south to Bench Camp below Burgundy Col and descend via the standard Burgundy Col trail to Highway 20 (approximately 2 hours total). Alternatively, scramble to Sunset Col and descend via Sunset Col trail to Silver Star Creek drainage.
routes.descentText: Two descent options exist: (1) From the summit, traverse south toward Bench Camp below Burgundy Col; this takes approximately 1 hour to reach Bench Camp. From Bench Camp, follow the established trail down to Highway 20 (approximately 1 hour more), then hitch-hike back to the vehicle. (2) Alternatively, scramble northwest to Sunset Col and descend the Sunset Col trail back toward Silver Star Creek drainage, rejoining the approach route.
knownHazards: Pitch 1 requires solid protection skills in splitter crack
Upper portions expose climbers to rockfall
Approach crosses significant talus requiring care
Afternoon thunderstorms common mid-summer
watchOut: Pitch 1 splitter can be over-protected easily; careful placement is key
Pitches 11-15 are deceptively committing despite lower grade
Descent route-finding is critical in semi-wild terrain
proTips: Pitch 1 is one of the best alpine splitters at Washington Pass; savor it
Pack minimal weight for the 10-11 hour round trip; approach shoes work fine
Start very early (before 6 AM) to avoid afternoon thunderstorms
routes.bestSeason: Late June through late September is optimal. June may have residual snow on approach; July and August are ideal with snow-free conditions and stable weather. September sees increasing storm frequency.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable at Washington Pass; plan for no connectivity. Highway 20 corridor has sporadic Verizon coverage only.

timing.recommendedStart: Before 6 AM

gear: Doubles of cams from #0.75 to #3 inches
routes.detailedRack: Bring a full set of nuts in all sizes and doubles of cams from #0.75 to #3. A #4 cam is useful for pitches 6-7 (fixed protection on P7 was present in 2014 but may not be reliable; bring your own). Two 60m ropes or single 70m rope is efficient for descent sections.
routes.proNeeds: Climbers must be proficient with hand and finger crack protection placement. Solid experience on multi-pitch alpine routes at this grade and comfort with route-finding in semi-wild terrain is essential.

pitchDetail.notes: Sustained thin hand crack; one of the best alpine splitters at Washington Pass
Follow 3-inch crack up and right over small roof
Varied climbing with left sweeping crack and thin crack crux
Hand crack climbing
Easy chimney
Mid-5th class terrain (500 feet); can be simul-climbed

routes.overview: A new route on the steep east face of South Peak featuring challenging crack and face climbing on white granite.
routes.beta: The route climbs rad 5.10 hands interspersed with poor rock quality. It is not recommended unless specifically looking for a broken, scary offwidth/chimney/dike pitch.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August when glacier conditions are most stable and weather windows are favorable.
knownHazards: Route features loose, poor quality rock
Contains scary offwidth/chimney sections with sketchy protection
watchOut: Be prepared for poor rock quality
Offwidth climbing sections require solid technique
Avoid if seeking secure, high-quality climbing
routes.approach: From Stehekin, take the ferry (Lady of the Lake II or Lady Express) from Chelan. From Lucerne drop-off, hike the Pacific Crest Trail to Agnes Creek/Spruce Creek confluence (approximately 10 miles). Cross Agnes Creek on logs, then Spruce Creek. Continue bushwhacking northwest to approach South Peak base. Alternatively, access via Downey Creek from the west (2-day approach). The approach requires 5,000 feet of off-trail elevation gain, glacier travel, and bushwhacking through dense vegetation.
routes.descent: Rappel anchors are located on the eastern wall near the summit. South Peak rappel routes drop directly to Upper Broadway area. Use doubled 50-meter ropes for descent (at least two rappels required).
routes.descentText: Descend via established rappel routes on the south/east side. Rappel anchors exist on the eastern wall below the summit area. The descent requires technical rope skills and appropriate anchor setup. Alternative descent is to downclimb the South Ridge route if conditions allow.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning to maximize daylight for climbing and descent

proTips: Start early to maximize daylight
This route is not recommended for the quality of rock; the South Ridge is a better choice
Bring extra protection gear for poor rock
Be prepared for difficult lichen-covered slabs
routes.detailedRack: Small to medium alpine rack (stoppers, cams, hexes). Bring good anchoring equipment for rappels. Extra slings and quickdraws recommended for managing poor rock.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on Accidental Discharge can be problematic due to poor rock quality. Pre-placed rappel anchors on the descent are essential.
whatToBring: Water and food for multi-day approach

routes.overview: The classic route on South Peak following a scenic south-facing ridge. Features clean granite climbing with exposure and memorable features including a notable gap called the 'cannon hole'.
routes.beta: Begin at one of two obvious dihedrals on the west side of the ridge south of the peak (accessible from Blue Lake). The first pitch climbs either of two clean corners to the ridge crest with chickenheads and good face features (5.7). The memorable traverse pitch crosses the gapping cannon hole (5.8), a feature which appeared sometime in the 1980s or 1990s. The final pitch is low 5th class scrambling to the summit block.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through August when the granite is dry and weather windows allow safe climbing.
knownHazards: Cannon hole gap
Moderate exposure
Weather window dependent
watchOut: The cannon hole traverse pitch is very exposed; be confident with your protection and footwork
Weather deterioration is rapid in afternoon; plan early starts
routes.approach: From Stehekin via Lake Chelan ferry, access the PCT to Agnes/Spruce Creek confluence. Bushwhack to Blue Lake approach, then scramble up ledges to the base of the south ridge. Alternatively reach via South Peak plateau and downclimb to the route start. Total approach is 2 days from trailhead.
routes.descent: From the summit, downclimb or rappel the South Ridge (if established anchors exist) or descend via the established South Peak rappel routes on the eastern wall.
routes.descentText: The most common descent is via rappel anchors on the eastern wall below the summit area. Alternatively, the route can be downclimbed (care needed on the cannon hole feature). Doubled 50m ropes recommended.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start to maximize daylight

blurb: South Peak is a prominent granite spire in Washington's remote Gunsight Range, located just east of the Cascades crest near Dome Peak within the Glacier Peak Wilderness. The peak features some of the finest white granite climbing in the North Cascades, with excellent crack systems and face climbing on its ridges. The area remained largely undeveloped until the mid-2000s when the South Ridge was first recorded and new routes like Accidental Discharge were established.
hierarchyNote: South Peak (8,000 ft) is correctly located in the Gunsight Range, Glacier Peak Wilderness, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, North Cascades region of Washington state. Coordinates 48.30571, -120.99439 verified from Mountain Project data. Peak identification confirmed through first ascent records and multiple climbing sources.

blurb: Goose Egg Mountain is a 4,566-foot peak in the southern Cascades located east of Rimrock Lake on Highway 12, roughly 10 miles east of White Pass. Despite its modest elevation, it hosts excellent multi-pitch rock climbing on its 800-foot south wall, featuring popular alpine cragging routes like Dirty Sanchez and Ride the Lightning. The area features characteristic loose, chossy rock typical of the well-known Tieton River climbing area.

routes.overview: A 700-foot, 7-pitch alpine rock climb on the south wall featuring loose chossy rock and the famous Jenga Traverse knife-edge crossing. The route deteriorates in rock quality mid-way but remains a classic Tieton alpine experience.
routes.beta: Multi-pitch alpine rock route with run-out sections requiring careful route-finding. Loose rock throughout with minimal protection on upper pitches, particularly the exposed Jenga Traverse on the final pitch. The route starts on the second obvious rib and ascends primarily bolted sections with gear climbing through corners.
routes.approach: From Highway 12 near Rimrock Lake, access the Tieton climbing area on the south side. Look for the second obvious rib on the south wall. Scramble on loose rock to reach the base, taking care to identify the correct starting rib.
routes.descent: Rappel via rocky gully with one rappel midway down, or descend via the Gangsta Rap route. Multiple rappels or rappel-to-rappel descent with one midway rappel.
routes.descentText: Descend via the rocky gully to climber's right, with one rappel required midway. Alternatively, descend via the Gangsta Rap descent route toward Rimrock Lake. Multiple rappel anchors exist; bring adequate rope for the descent.
routes.bestSeason: May through November, avoiding winter and early spring when snow may linger at higher elevations.
routes.proNeeds: Carry gear to 3 inches and a dozen draws. Traditional protection with bolted anchors on initial pitches, transitioning to natural protection and minimal bolting higher on the route.
routes.detailedRack: Carabiners and draws for bolted sections; cams to 3 inches and nuts for crack sections. Bring slings for anchors and protection. Quick draws recommended for the bolted pitches.
gear: Single rack with cams to 3 inches
Helmet (essential due to loose rock and rockfall potential)
proTips: Identify the correct rib (the second one) before starting—the left rib has poor rock quality
The Jenga Traverse on the final pitch is the crux and features loose blocks; move carefully and minimize weight on any single hold
Lower pitches have bolted anchors; upper pitches rely on gear placement for anchors
Total climbing time is approximately 5 hours; plan for 8-9 hours car-to-car including approach and descent
Watch for lichen on rock which reduces grip; wet conditions significantly increase difficulty
watchOut: Loose chossy rock throughout—a helmet is essential
Run-out sections with minimal protection, especially mid-pitch
The Jenga Traverse (pitch 7) has limited protection and loose blocks that shift under weight
Poor rock quality mid-route; rock fall danger when other parties are climbing above
Lichen-covered surfaces reduce grip significantly
knownHazards: Loose rock and choss are the primary objective hazard throughout the climb
Limited protection options on several pitches increase fall consequence
Rockfall hazard from other climbing parties on adjacent routes
Rattlesnakes present in the lower canyon area (unlikely to encounter at climbing area)
Ticks can be a problem in early season (spring)

pitchDetail.notes: Face and moss slab climbing to tree belay
Bolted climbing with run-out section near top
Trad climbing with poor crack quality; tricky protection
Gear and bolted climbing; anchors can be hard to find
Difficult protection; finger crack section; harder than grade suggests
Sporty climbing; sparse bolts; exposed slab
The Jenga Traverse—knife-edge crossing with loose blocks and minimal protection; the crux of the route; 5.5 climbing but PG protection makes it feel harder

timing.recommendedStart: Dawn/early morning to avoid afternoon rockfall risk and maximize daylight

routes.turnaround: If daylight begins to fade or weather deteriorates, retreat via one of the rappel routes. Do not continue climbing if it will require descending in darkness.
routes.bail: Full retreat is possible at any pitch via rappel. Multiple rappel anchors exist throughout the route. Escape left or right off upper pitches is not viable; descent must be via rappel.

routes.overview: A 7-pitch alpine rock route on the 800-foot south wall featuring face climbing below prominent roof features and a mix of bolted and gear-protected pitches. Initial pitches are the best climbing; the route deteriorates higher with loose rock but improves again on pitch 5.
routes.beta: Sport-and-trad alpine climb on the south face with quality climbing on lower pitches transitioning to chossy rock mid-route before improving toward the summit. Bolted belay anchors on initial pitches; natural protection higher. The opening pitches below the roof are the route's best sections.
routes.approach: From Highway 12, access the Tieton climbing area. Park at 'the well' approximately 1-2 miles before the wall becomes fully visible from the road. Hike to the base following established trail, taking approximately 30 minutes to an hour depending on exact parking spot.
routes.descent: Walk off climber's left via an obvious gully with one rappel required midway. Alternatively, rappel the route via existing anchors.
routes.descentText: From the summit area, walk climber's left to an obvious gully. Descend via this gully with one rappel required midway (approximately 30 feet). This is the standard descent used by most parties. Ensure you have adequate rope for any rappel anchors encountered.
routes.bestSeason: May through November when snow-free; avoid early spring and late fall for best conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Single rack with standard cams and nuts; a dozen draws for bolted sections. Initial pitches have bolted anchors; upper pitches rely on gear placement.
routes.detailedRack: Carry a single set of cams and nuts; this should suffice for the route. Bring a dozen quickdraws or carabiners for the bolted sections. Longer slings useful for natural anchors and equalizing pieces.
gear: Single rock climbing rack (cams and nuts)
Longer slings for anchors and equalization
Helmet (essential due to loose rock hazard)
proTips: The opening pitches below the roof are the best climbing on the route—enjoy them
Pitch 5 has the best rock quality if you want to break up the looser mid-section climbing
Single rack approach means weight and speed; pre-place protection if conditions allow
The upper section has a hanging boulder field directly above; descent to climber's left is key
Summer afternoon heat can be intense; start early for cooler conditions

Upper pitches feature a hanging boulder field directly overhead; rockfall danger
The roof on lower pitches has good protection but is exposed; don't rush
Deteriorating rock quality mid-route requires caution on protection placement

Exposure on lower pitches below the roof feature
Rattlesnakes present in lower canyon (unlikely at climbing area)

Loose rock; deteriorating climbing quality; mixed bolted and gear protection

routes.turnaround: If daylight is running short or weather deteriorates significantly, rappel down from the current position. Do not climb into darkness.
routes.bail: Rappel descent is possible from any pitch via existing anchors or fixed gear. Multiple rap stations exist; ensure adequate rope length for descent.

routes.overview: A moderate alpine rock route featuring Class 3 scrambling to the north summit followed by technical pitches on main gendarmes, with rappels required for descent. Described in the Beckey guide as appearing sensible as a route.

pitchDetail.notes: North Summit approach via stable, lightly vegetated boulder-covered NW ridge; straightforward route finding on good to very good rock
Main gendarmes on ridge; couple of short technical pitches on very nice stable rock, each gendarme requires passage

routes.rappels: 2 rappels on west side, approximately 30m total
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers optimal conditions. Early June possible with lingering snow patches; October excellent with larch colors. Late May through early October window is safest for access.
routes.approach: Approach via Remmel Lake from the north (accessible from Remmel Creek Trail). From Remmel Lake, bushwhack due south approximately 2 miles through mosquito-infested forests and subalpine bogs to reach camp at 7,500 feet elevation on the lower NW ridge. Gain the ridge directly from camp.
routes.descent: From summit, make 2 rappels on the west side of the main gendarmes, then scramble down and around the west slope on good to loose boulders and scree, avoiding the north summit route. Total descent time approximately 2.5 hours from summit to camp.
routes.descentText: Descend via 2 rappels on each of the main gendarmes on the west side of the ridge, each rappel approximately 15m or 30m total. After rappels, scramble down and around the west slope on stable to loose boulders and scree, carefully avoiding the direct descent route via the north summit. Route finding on descent can be straightforward if familiar with the terrain.
routes.beta: Good to very good rock throughout with straightforward route finding. The lower NW ridge is beautiful and wild with wonderful camp spots at 7,500 feet. No established trail to the ridge; route finding from camp to summit is aided by the ridge crest. Most climbing on the two main gendarmes involves short pitches on stable rock with good protection opportunities.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Bushwhack from Remmel Lake south through forests and bogs to reach camp at 7,500 feet on lower ridge. Day 2: Climb from camp to summit (3.5 hours typical climbing time from camp), summit and rappel descent back to camp (2.5 hours), return to Remmel Lake.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward via the same descent route. The ridge offers good camp spots if further descent is needed. If weather deteriorates, retreat to the 7,500 foot camp. The route avoids technical terrain until the gendarmes.
routes.turnaround: If not on the summit by mid-afternoon, consider returning to camp. Weather can deteriorate rapidly on exposed ridge terrain; monitor conditions throughout the climb.
knownHazards: Loose, exposed granite rock on upper ridge
Gendarme passages require scrambling and short rock pitches
Exposure on ridge during scrambling sections
Lightning exposure on exposed summit area; historical fire lookout destroyed by repeated lightning strikes
Mosquito hazard during approach through forested and boggy sections
Steep scree and loose boulder fields on descent, especially on west slope
watchOut: Watch for unstable blocks and flakes on the granite ridge sections
Be cautious on the steep, loose west slope descent - rockfall hazard
Summit area highly exposed to lightning; descend if thunderstorms develop
Mosquitoes can be intense in forested sections of approach; bring bug repellent and head net
proTips: Approach from Remmel Lake one day before climbing to reach camp at 7,500 feet; allows early summit start
The lower NW ridge has excellent camping spots with flat areas; good water sources
Navigate gendarmes by staying on ridge crest; look for established climbing features and cairns marking passage
Double-check rappel anchors before descending from gendarmes; rock may be loose around sling placements
Early morning start from camp enables return to summit and descent before afternoon weather buildup
routes.proNeeds: Light alpine rack with stoppers and cams up to 2-inch size for protection on the gendarme pitches. Two 60m ropes recommended for rappels. Slings for anchor building on granite.
gear: Two 60m ropes or one 70m rope for rappels
Rock climbing shoes or approach boots
Insulating layers (can be cold at 8,685 ft)
whatToBring: Mosquito repellent and head net for approach
Water bottles and water treatment (or camp near water)
Micro-spikes or crampons if ascending before June or after September

timing.recommendedStart: Dawn or early morning from camp at 7,500 feet

climate.summer: July-August most stable with long daylight hours; afternoon thunderstorms common; warm days (60-70F at summit), freezing nights

climate.winter: November-April snow-covered with avalanche hazard and extreme cold; generally not climbed during winter

emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable in Pasayten Wilderness. Satellite messenger or satellite communication device strongly recommended. Emergency evacuation would likely require helicopter rescue from exposed ridge terrain.

routes.comms: Cell phone service unreliable to nonexistent in Pasayten Wilderness. Satellite messenger, inReach, or Personal Locator Beacon recommended for emergency communication. Notify trailhead register of trip plans.

blurb: Mount Formidable (8,325 ft) is a prominent peak in the North Cascades between Cascade Pass and Dome Peak, composed of metamorphic green schist cut by soft diabasic dikes. Named in 1935 by mountaineer Herman Ulrichs for its formidable north face appearance, the peak was first ascended in 1938 by Calder Bessler, Ralph Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers during the inaugural Ptarmigan Traverse. The south and east faces offer scrambling and technical alpine routes, while the glacier-guarded northeast face provides a serious alpine objective for experienced mountaineers.

routes.overview: The primary descent and main access route to Mount Formidable's summit via exposed scrambling terrain on loose metamorphic rock. Characterized by steep slabs, ledges, and airy sections with constant exposure requiring route finding skill and care.
routes.beta: Starting from Spider-Formidable Col (7,380 ft), this route descends east then contours across the south and southeast faces via a system of ledges and slabs. Multiple gullies offer access; climbers must identify the correct gully system to summit as other lines lead to false summits or dead ends. The route involves steep, exposed fourth and low fifth-class scrambling with loose rock throughout, typically requiring a rope and protection. This is the primary summit route and only practical descent.
routes.approach: Approach begins at Cascade Pass Trailhead (end of Cascade River Road, 23 miles from Marblemount). Hike Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 miles gaining 1,700 ft to Cascade Pass (5,560 ft). From the pass, boot path leads south traversing Mix-Up Arm across heather, talus, and snow. Ascend Cache Glacier to Cache Col and descend to Kool-Aid Lake (6,100 ft) campsite. From camp, traverse south across Red Ledge near Art's Knoll, then traverse talus and snow toward Middle Cascade Glacier. Ascend the glacier's lower sections avoiding icefalls, then rope up and cross the crevassed glacier heading south to Spider-Formidable Col (7,380 ft) at the col between Formidable and Spider Mountain. From the col, the south and southeast face systems are accessed.
routes.descent: Reverse the approach: descend south face to Spider-Formidable Col, traverse back across Middle Cascade Glacier northward (most heavily crevassed section - rapid crossing recommended), descend to the glacier's lower sections, return via Red Ledge and Art's Knoll area to Kool-Aid Lake, then retrace the approach trail. Multiple rappels may be necessary on technical sections; climbers have used natural anchors (slung boulders). Approximately 6+ hours summit to camp.
routes.descentText: The descent retraces the ascent route in reverse. From the summit, descend scrambling terrain via the south/southeast face system (Class 4) back to Spider-Formidable Col. At the col, traverse the crevassed Middle Cascade Glacier northward (exercise caution - this section is heavily crevassed and an icefall zone exists; crossing should be swift and deliberate). At approximately 6,600 feet, cross lower glacier sections and head toward Red Ledge near Art's Knoll, traversing heather and talus northward. Pass Red Ledge and continue to Kool-Aid Lake. From camp, retrace the boot path north across Red Ledge, through Cache Col, down Cache Glacier, and back to Cascade Pass. Finish via Cascade Pass Trail to trailhead. Total descent time approximately 6-8 hours depending on conditions and pace.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (Approx. 6 hours): Hike from Cascade Pass Trailhead to Cascade Pass (3.7 miles, 1,700 ft gain). Cache packs and scout or camp at Cascade Pass or lower camp near Cache Glacier. Day 2 (Approx. 6-8 hours): Cache Glacier to Kool-Aid Lake via Cache Col and Red Ledge traverse. Day 3 (Approx. 10-12 hours): Kool-Aid Lake to Middle Cascade Glacier crossing via Red Ledge and Art's Knoll, up glacier to Spider-Formidable Col, ascent of south/southeast face to summit, return to Spider-Formidable Col and descent to Kool-Aid Lake (long day). Alternative: Split summit day into two days, camping at Spider-Formidable Col (7,380 ft, no permit required) for a more moderate outing.
routes.bail: Turnaround point: Spider-Formidable Col (7,380 ft). From any point on the face, retreat to the col and reverse the approach. In poor visibility or hazardous conditions, do not continue beyond Cache Glacier; retreat to Kool-Aid Lake and return via the approach route. The most exposed terrain is the face itself and the crevassed Middle Cascade Glacier crossing; in dangerous conditions (high rockfall risk, deteriorating snow/ice, or poor visibility), retreat to Kool-Aid Lake and do not attempt the glacier or face.
routes.turnaround: Conservative climbers should establish a firm turnaround time based on their start time, fitness, and conditions. A safe guideline: if you have not reached Spider-Formidable Col by mid-morning, or if conditions deteriorate (weather, loose rock, icefall activity), descend and retreat to Kool-Aid Lake. If daylight is running short or energy reserves are depleted, retreat immediately.
routes.bestSeason: June through September is the general climbing season; July is ideal for trail access and conditions. Early season (June) features significant snow cover requiring ice axes and crampons; route finding can be more difficult due to snow coverage. Late summer (August-September) offers better rock and snow conditions, reduced crevasse hazards, and clearer route finding, but weather becomes less stable. July offers a balance of accessible conditions and moderate weather.
routes.rappels: Multiple rappels possible on technical sections using natural anchors
routes.proNeeds: Rope and protection recommended for technical scrambling sections. Ice axes and crampons required for glacier travel and snow/ice sections. Crevasse rescue capability essential for glacier travel.
proTips: Route finding is difficult; multiple gullies exist and some lead to false summits - carefully identify the correct line before committing
The infamous 'chasm' crossing midway up the face presents significant exposure - exercise extreme caution and consider a belay
Red Ledge offers easier passage than the exposed western ridge of Art's Knoll when snow-free; ice axe handy when snowcovered
Travel quickly across Middle Cascade Glacier due to active icefall zone; this is the most dangerous section of the approach
Camp at Kool-Aid Lake (no permit required) rather than higher elevations for safety and practicality
The loose rock quality throughout makes every section serious despite modest grades - stay alert for rockfall from above and from dislodged hold risk
watchOut: Loose, poor-quality metamorphic rock throughout the route; holds are unreliable
Significant exposure on steep slabs and ledges; falls would be serious
Difficult route finding with multiple gullies creating navigation complexity
The 'chasm' feature requires careful assessment and may necessitate a belay or rappel
Constant rockfall hazard; wear helmet and avoid climbing during times when loose rock activity is high
Middle Cascade Glacier is heavily crevassed with active icefalls; crossing is necessary but dangerous
Moats form early in season around glaciers; late-season crossings require careful route finding
No cell reception; SAT communicator strongly recommended
knownHazards: Loose metamorphic rock and poor rock quality create unreliable protection for climbing
High exposure on steep slabs and ledges with serious fall potential
Difficult route finding with risk of mistaking gullies and ascending false summits
Route-finding errors have resulted in climbers reaching only sub-summits instead of the true summit
At least one fatality has occurred on the south scramble route, emphasizing its seriousness
Middle Cascade Glacier is actively crevassed and features an icefall zone; crossing carries risk of crevasse fall or icefall impact
Early season snow-covered terrain masks hazards and complicates navigation
Late-season moats create additional obstacles to glacier travel
gear: Ice axe (required for glacier and snow sections)
Crampons (required for glacier and ice, low-angle late-season ice)
Helmet (essential for rockfall hazard and rope work)
Trad climbing rack (nuts and cams, small to medium sizes for protection in schist)
Cordelette or sling system for anchors
routes.detailedRack: A lightweight alpine trad rack is recommended for the technical scrambling sections. Typical setup: 1-2 sets of nuts (especially small and medium sizes suitable for schist cracks), 1-2 sets of microloaded cams (sizes 0.5-1.5 for schist), 6-8 carabiners (mix of locking and non-locking), slings (2-3, various widths), and cordelette for multi-point anchors. The loose rock quality limits placement reliability, so select anchors conservatively.
whatToBring: Insulated hat and gloves (even in summer)
Map and compass (detailed topo map recommended)
Sat communicator or Personal Locator Beacon (highly recommended)
Food and water (high-calorie; 2+ quarts capacity bladder for glacier travel)
First aid kit with blister treatment

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00-6:00 AM from Kool-Aid Lake to ensure adequate daylight for descent

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open typically June through October; Winter closures possible
road.seasonalGate: Road typically opens end of June; check North Cascades National Park status
road.driveNote: Paved initially then gravel with narrow, steep sections near trailhead. From Marblemount (Highway 20), drive east 23 miles to parking area. Arrive early to secure parking; lot fills quickly in summer. Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass required.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, Washington
climate.typical: Highly variable due to exposure and elevation. Peak sits at 8,325 ft with direct weather exposure to North Cascades weather systems.
climate.spring: May-early June: Heavy snow cover, frequent storms, avalanche risk. Not recommended for most parties.
climate.summer: July-August: Most stable period with lower precipitation and warmer temperatures. Afternoon thunderstorms common. Peak temps 40-50°F at elevation. Ice axe and crampons usually required on glacier well into July.
climate.fall: September: Cooling trend begins, increasing instability. Storms more frequent. Early season snow possible late month.
climate.winter: October-April: Heavy snow, frequent storms, avalanche hazard, extreme cold. Access road typically closed. Not recommended for climbing.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for overnight camping in North Cascades National Park ($10 per person + $6 reservation fee). Permits available through Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount. Some campsites (Kool-Aid Lake, Spider-Formidable Col) do not require permits.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass required for parking at Cascade Pass Trailhead
access.fees: $5 per vehicle (Northwest Forest Pass) or $80 annual America the Beautiful Pass. Backcountry permit $10 per person + $6 reservation fee.
access.closures: Cascade River Road typically closes seasonally (winter); check North Cascades National Park website for current status. Cascade Pass Trail may have snow and ice late into July.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 or Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-3131 for non-emergency (360) 416-1911
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: United General Hospital (PeaceHealth), 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA 98284 (360-856-6021); approximately 45 miles from trailhead. Alternative: Skagit Valley Hospital, 1415 E. Kincaid, Mount Vernon, WA 98273 (360-424-4111)
emergency.notes: No cell reception on or near Mount Formidable. Sat communicator (InReach, SPOT, or Personal Locator Beacon) strongly recommended for emergencies. Rescue is a slow process; assume 12-24+ hours for professional rescue in remote terrain. Self-rescue capability and thorough preparation are essential.

routes.corrections: Given elevation 8,325 ft matches recent survey data (8,352.9 ft from Oct 2023); USGS standard elevation lists 8,271 ft. Route grades (Class 4 / low 5th, 5.0 rock) confirmed via multiple trip reports. Descent time estimates slightly higher than some reports due to glacier hazard and route complexity.
routes.fa: First ascent of peak: July 25, 1938 by Ptarmigan Club members Calder Bessler, Ralph Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers during inaugural Ptarmigan Traverse. This route is likely the first-ascent line used by this party, though the specific route variant (South Face vs. southeast ledges) is not differentiated in historical records.
routes.comms: No cellular reception on or near Mount Formidable. Satellite communicator (InReach, SPOT, or similar) strongly recommended for emergency communication. Check battery life and ensure devices are tested before departure. Digital radio systems may work in limited areas but should not be relied upon as primary communication.
objHaz: Avalanche terrain on steep approach slopes

routes.overview: The most established climbing route on Pernod Spire, ascending the impressive west face which is the tallest wall of all Wine Spires.
routes.approach: From Highway 20 pullout just east of Washington Pass (near milepost 166), follow climbers trail up to bench camp at ~6,500 feet. Approach time typically 1-2 hours to base of west face.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through late September. Snow and ice expected in early season (before mid-June). Approach becomes snow-free by midsummer.
knownHazards: Objective hazards: rockfall on descent
Long commitment route requiring multiple rappels
watchOut: Route finding can be challenging with many traverses and down-climbs
Multiple long rappels required for descent
proTips: Plan for a full day or bivvy depending on climbing pace
Good route finding skills essential due to complex terrain

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning

road.name: Highway 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open mid-summer; seasonal closure November-April
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens April/May, closes November
road.driveNote: Access via pullout on Highway 20 east of Washington Pass, near milepost 166. About 26 miles east of Winthrop.

access.landManager: Okanogan National Forest
access.closures: Highway 20 seasonal closures in winter; check WSDOT for current road status

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades
climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.
climate.spring: Snow at higher elevations through May. Unpredictable storms.
climate.summer: Best weather window. Clear mornings common, afternoon thunderstorms develop. Temps 40-60F at summit.
climate.fall: Good climbing in September. Weather becoming more unsettled by October.
climate.winter: Heavy snow. Route not typically climbed. Avalanche hazard significant.

emergency.rangerStation: Nearest USFS ranger station in Sedro-Woolley area
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital in Mount Vernon, WA (~60 miles)
emergency.notes: Very limited cell service in the area. Satellite communication recommended.

routes.corrections: CRITICAL DISCREPANCY: The provided data lists 'Standard Rock Route' as Grade III, 5.8, 6 pitches, 700 ft with FA 1952 (Don Wilde, Dick McGowan, Fred Beckey). This route does NOT appear in Mountain Project (the authoritative route database for Washington) or any other verifiable climbing source. Mountain Project documents two routes: Direct West Face (5.10+, 20 pitches) and South Face (5.9, 17 pitches). The first ascent attribution could not be verified. The route provided may be misidentified, discontinued, or contain incorrect data. Returning route with best available sourced information from Mountain Project rather than unverifiable data.

blurb: Pernod Spire is the tallest of the Wine Spires, a celebrated granite alpine climbing cluster situated east of Washington Pass in the North Cascades. Located just south of Chianti Spire, it features the tallest walls of any spire in the group, primarily on its impressive west face. This is premier moderate alpine rock climbing terrain offering sustained mid-grade routes (5.9-5.10+) with 15-20 pitch commitments in a stunning high alpine setting.
hierarchyNote: Peak verified as real and identifiable. Located in Wine Spires, North Cascades, Washington. Geographic coordinates and elevation plausible for this range. Hierarchy (peak → Wine Spires → North Cascades → Washington) confirmed through multiple sources. No hierarchy issues detected.

routes.overview: A moderate alpine scramble on Morning Star Peak's accessible slopes, featuring steep rock and snow sections typical of North Cascades Class 2-3 terrain.
routes.beta: The Standard Scramble represents the basic approach to Morning Star Peak's 6,020 ft summit. Multiple scramble options exist on the peak, including the South Ridge and Northwest Face routes, both of which involve Class 3 scrambling with sections of steep rock and snow.
routes.fa: Peak first ascent: July 1961 by Monte Haun, Mark Haun, Dick Hill, and Kenn Carpenter
routes.approach: From Granite Falls on Mountain Loop Highway, drive east approximately 28 miles and turn right onto Sunrise Mine Road (FR 4065). Drive 2.3 miles to the end at Sunrise Mine Trailhead (2,350 ft elev), which requires a Northwest Forest Pass for parking. Follow Sunrise Mine Trail approximately 0.5 mile until just before crossing South Fork Stillaguamish River. Look for an abandoned trail on the east side of the river or follow the path of least resistance along the east side. Initial brush-clearing is heavy. The approach gains roughly 2,700 feet over approximately 2.5 miles, typically taking 2-3 hours to reach the base scramble terrain.
routes.descent: Carefully downclimb the scramble route or retrace your ascent path, taking care on loose rock and steep sections. Avoid staying on steep snow-covered slopes longer than necessary.
routes.descentText: Descend via the same route used for ascent, exercising caution on steep rock sections and any remaining snow. Downclimbing Class 2-3 terrain requires careful footwork. If route finding was difficult on ascent, descent may be equally challenging and should be done in good visibility. Avoid rappelling unless equipped for it.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the most favorable weather conditions for this scramble. June is possible but snow may be deeper. Early summer (June) can have full snow coverage which actually eases brush problems but increases avalanche and glissade hazards. October is possible with clear weather but conditions deteriorate rapidly. Avoid March-May due to heavy snow and avalanche risk; avoid November-February due to extreme conditions and short daylight.
knownHazards: Steep snow-covered slabs present avalanche hazard, especially early season
Thick brush on approach makes progress slow and route-finding challenging in summer months
Rockfall hazard below Headlee Pass on the northwest approaches
Difficult river crossing of South Fork Stillaguamish River early on approach
Exposure and loose rock on upper slopes requires careful footwork
watchOut: Route-finding experience and climbing gear are required; do not underestimate navigation demands on approach
Early-season snowpack can turn Class 2-3 scrambling into more serious alpine terrain
Wet conditions after rain increase rockfall hazard, especially near Headlee Pass
Heavy maritime snow is wet and prone to avalanching; assess slope angles and recent weather carefully
gear: Climbing shoes or sticky approach shoes
routes.detailedRack: For pure scrambling, carry a light rack: helmet mandatory, 30m rope (though rarely needed for Class 2-3), a few slings for rappel anchors if ascending steep sections, ice axe if snow is present, and crampons for icy conditions early season. Most parties climb this unroped but carry rope for emergency rappel capability. Minimize weight given the dense brush and difficult approach.
routes.proNeeds: Self-rescue capability is essential given remote location and route-finding challenges. Carry rope even if not planning to use it; the approach hazards (river crossing, rockfall) are as serious as the summit terrain. Know how to construct rappel anchors on rock.
whatToBring: Trekking poles (optional, can help with brush)
proTips: Winter ascents with full snow coverage can avoid the notorious brush problems but require alpine ice climbing skills and avalanche awareness
The initial 0.5 mile of brush-covered trail is the hardest part of many climbs; early morning starts help beat sun exposure
Route-find carefully on upper slopes; multiple gullies and false summits can trap unwary climbers
Sunrise Mine Trail and the Headlee Pass approach offer alternative access points if South Fork approach becomes impassable
Cell service is unreliable; satellite communication recommended for parties climbing solo
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Early start (ideally 5-6 am) from Sunrise Mine Trailhead. Hike/scramble 2.5 miles and 2,700 feet gain through brush and along Stillaguamish River to base terrain (3-4 hours depending on conditions). Locate the South Ridge or Northwest Face approach and begin scrambling. Gain final 400 feet of Class 2-3 scrambling to 6,020 ft summit (1-2 hours). Summit time: 4-5 hours from trailhead. Descend same route carefully (2-3 hours). Return to trailhead by late afternoon/early evening (7-10 hours total).
routes.turnaround: In marginal weather, turn around by mid-afternoon (2 pm) to ensure descent in daylight. Exposed ridges on upper peak are dangerous in thunderstorms. If river crossing appears impassable or snow conditions are unstable, turn back at lower elevations rather than committing to full ascent.
routes.bail: From the main approach basin (~3,700 ft), descend back down the gully/slabs to the river valley and return via ascent route. If caught on upper peak in poor weather, descend to the southeast basin and traverse down to regain the main gully descent. No formal bailout terrain; retreat via the route of ascent.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the Stillaguamish River Valley and absent above ~4,000 feet. Assume no service; rely on satellite devices (Garmin inReach, PLB) for emergency communication.

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway (WA-530 to FR 4065)
road.status: Open seasonally; typically closed in winter due to snow
road.seasonalGate: May-October; verify status with Darrington Ranger District before travel
road.driveNote: Drive Mountain Loop Highway east from Granite Falls approximately 28 miles to FR 4065 (Sunrise Mine Road). This is a well-maintained paved/gravel highway. FR 4065 is narrow and rough but passable to the Sunrise Mine Trailhead in summer/fall. Road conditions vary; contact Darrington Ranger District (360) 436-1155 for current status, especially in early season.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (NWAC - Northwest Avalanche Center)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate. Frequent precipitation especially October-April. Summer brings more stable weather but afternoon thunderstorms are common. Temperatures at 6,020 ft range from freezing in early season to 50-60 F in mid-summer at summit.
climate.spring: Highly variable. Heavy maritime snowfall March-May with wet, unstable snow. Avalanche danger high. Brush still covered by snow easing approach but increasing hazard level significantly. Not recommended.
climate.summer: Best season. June-September most favorable, with July-August being prime window. Occasional thunderstorms especially afternoons. Temperatures at summit 40-55 F. Approach brush fully exposed, making slow going. Afternoon/evening convection clouds typical.
climate.fall: September-October generally stable before weather deteriorates. Earlier sunsets mean less daylight margin. Early October still viable; late October approaches winter conditions.
climate.winter: November-March: Extreme avalanche hazard, deep snow, short daylight, and very cold. Not recommended for most parties. Specialized winter mountaineering skills required.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking at Sunrise Mine Trailhead (Display Pass)
access.closures: Mountain Loop Highway seasonally closed November-May in some years; check with Darrington Ranger District. Sunrise Mine Trail may be impassable during spring runoff (April-May) due to snow and river conditions.

emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, 315 S. Stillaguamish Ave, Arlington, WA 98223 (operated by Skagit Regional Health, approximately 30-45 minutes from trailhead depending on access). Local clinics: Skagit Regional Health Darrington Family Medicine, 1150 Bridge Street, Darrington, WA 98241 (clinic only, lab services).
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger Station, 1405 Emens Ave N, Darrington, WA 98241, (360) 436-1155, open Mon-Fri 8 am-4 pm (closed 12-12:30 pm lunch). Granite Falls Ranger Station, 33515 Mtn. Loop Hwy, Granite Falls, WA 98252, Friday-Monday 8 am-12 pm and 12:30-4 pm
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable/absent above 4,000 ft. Helicopter rescue not available in poor weather. SAR response times 1-3 hours minimum from Darrington. Most rescues are hiker extractions via trail, not helicopter. Activate your satellite device or PLB immediately if serious injury occurs.

pitchDetail.notes: Approach via Sunrise Mine Trail through brush to river crossing
Follow river valley and ascend main gully/slabs to 3,700 ft basin
Ascend steep gully or adjacent slabs toward southeast ridge
Traverse snow-covered slabs northwest to southeast ridge notch (5,600 ft)
Ascend south ridge via gully or exposed ridge crest to summit block
Final scramble to 6,020 ft summit

routes.corrections: Route name 'Standard Scramble' does not appear in any authoritative climbing guidebooks or records (SummitPost, MountainProject, Mountaineers.org, AAC). Primary named routes on Morning Star Peak are South Ridge (T5/S4) and Northwest Face/North Ridge (Class 3). This entry appears to be a generic designation. Peak elevation in database (6,019 ft) differs slightly from authoritative sources (6,020 ft per Wikipedia and multiple climbing sites). Peak first ascent was July 1961 by Monte Haun, Mark Haun, Dick Hill, and Kenn Carpenter (per AAC Publications), not 1940 as some older sources state.

blurb:
Morning Star Peak is a 6,020 ft granite summit near the western edge of the North Cascades in Snohomish County, Washington, situated 2.5 miles southwest of Barlow Pass along the Mountain Loop Highway. The peak features multiple scramble routes and technical rock climbing options, including the popular seven-pitch Mile High Club route (5.10a), and was first ascended in July 1961 by Monte Haun, Mark Haun, Dick Hill, and Kenn Carpenter. Alpine scrambling with exposure and steep rock sections characterizes the peak's appeal to mountaineers seeking a rugged Cascade experience.</blurb>
<parameter name="hierarchyNote">The route named 'Standard Scramble' does not appear in authoritative climbing references. Named routes on Morning Star Peak are South Ridge (T5/S4 alpine scramble) and Northwest Face/North Ridge (Class 3). This entry may be a generic descriptor or legacy naming; verify if it should reference a specific named route. Peak elevation in database (6,019 ft) is 1 foot less than authoritative sources (6,020 ft).

hierarchyNote: Spire Gully right - Alpenkuhl is a climbing crag area (not a peak) located at ~5,500 ft elevation below South Early Winters Spire (7,807 ft) in the Washington Pass area of the North Cascades, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. Alpenkuhl refers to the sunny south-facing side of Spire Gully. Hierarchy and location are geographically correct and plausible. Note: The unnamed routes on Mountain Project indicate that complete route naming/history data is incomplete in available online sources.
blurb: Spire Gully right - Alpenkuhl is a popular sport climbing crag located below South Early Winters Spire in the Liberty Bell Group at Washington Pass. The south-facing granite wall features bolted routes ranging from 5.8 to 5.10, with good-quality pink and orange granite from the Golden Horn batholith. A quick 7-minute approach from the hairpin turn on Highway 20 makes it an accessible destination for alpine rock climbing in the North Cascades, best visited from late June through September.

routes.overview: Sport climbing route on south-facing granite wall
routes.beta: Bolted sport route in the Alpenkuhl area. Good granite with excellent rock quality.
routes.approach: From Highway 20 at the hairpin turn near Washington Pass, look uphill toward South Early Winters Spire. Spire Gully is visible below and to the left. Follow the trail uphill for approximately 7 minutes to reach the base of the crag.
routes.descent: Walk off or rappel from bolted anchors at route top
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September; May and early June may have snow on approach
routes.comms: Cell service is limited or unavailable at the trailhead; download weather apps while you have service
proTips: This is a quick approach crag accessible year-round on good weather days
The south-facing aspect means good sun exposure but watch for afternoon thunderstorms
The granite is exceptionally clean and well-structured compared to other Cascade areas
watchOut: Thunderstorms are more frequent at Washington Pass than other areas; weather can change rapidly
Rockfall possible during rapid warming or after rain
Lightning risk is significant during storms
knownHazards: Frequent thunderstorms in afternoon hours
Unpredictable snowstorms can occur even in summer months
Exposed position on south-facing wall increases lightning exposure

hierarchyNote: Data geography conflict: Warrior Peak is in the Olympic Mountains (Olympic National Park, Jefferson County, WA), NOT North Cascades. The peak exists at coordinates 47°44'N, 123°8'W. Verify the parent area hierarchy in the database - it may need to be reparented from a "North Cascades" branch to Olympic Mountains.
blurb: Warrior Peak is a striking 7,300-foot double-summited peak on the eastern edge of Olympic National Park, with dramatic views of Mount Constance to the south. Known for excellent alpine climbing on poor-quality but climbable rock, this peak was first ascended solo by Fred Beckey in 1945. The long 9-10 mile approach via Home Lake and Boulder Camp makes this a typical 2-day alpine objective.

routes.face: NW face via couloir between SE and NW summits
routes.overview: Classic alpine climb featuring a long approach to Boulder Camp, then a scramble to the cirque, followed by a steep snow couloir and short Class 4 rock pitch with poor-quality stone. The technical crux is a brief exposed section of 20-50 feet of scrambling and low 5th-class rock near the summit.
routes.beta: This is the primary route to Warrior Peak's Southeast Summit. The route involves approximately 9-10 miles of approach hiking, making it a typical 2-day backpacking objective. From the cirque below the peak, ascend the obvious couloir between the SE and NW summits. If snow-filled (common early season), the couloir is steep and straightforward; if melted out, it's loose scree. Above the couloir, scramble to a saddle at approximately 7,000 feet, then climb steep snow or a snow moat to reach the exposed rock section. Some parties rope this short pitch; others solo it depending on conditions and skill.
routes.approach: Drive Highway 101 to Sequim Bay State Park area, turn left on Louella Road for 1 mile, then left on Palo Alto Road for 1.1 miles to the Forest Boundary. Palo Alto becomes FS Road 28. Turn right onto FS Road 2880, drive past Dungeness Forks Campground for 1 mile, stay left onto FS Road 2870 for 8.7 miles to Upper Dungeness Trailhead (295142 Highway 101 S, Quilcene, WA 98376). Upper Dungeness Trail is the shortest and easiest approach (also called Route 1 from Olympic Climbers Guide). Hike the trail 3.4 miles and 600 feet to Camp Handy, then take the left fork and continue 3 miles and 1,850 feet to Boulder Camp (total: 6.4 miles, 2,450 feet gain). From Boulder Camp, continue south approximately 1.5-2 miles, crossing creeks, until you reach the final larger creek that flows from the cirque SE of Warrior Peak. Continue past this creek to a boulder field, then head up and back left toward the creek, staying under a band of cliffs, and continue up the boulders approximately 1 mile and 1,200 feet to reach the cirque at approximately 5,900 feet.
routes.descent: Descend the climbing route. Most parties downclimb the rock section and snow couloir. A single rope may be useful for rappelling the short rock crux if you lack confidence downclimbing Class 4 terrain. Return via the same approach.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace your steps down the short rock section (downclimb or single-rope rappel as appropriate), descend the couloir (if snow-filled, descending is relatively straightforward; if melted out, loose scree provides quick descent), return to the saddle at 7,000 feet, and retrace the approach back through the cirque and boulder field. The scree does make for a quick and easy descent when melted out, though the return hike to Boulder Camp and then to the trailhead requires careful route-finding through brush and creek crossings. Most parties camp at Boulder Camp on the way down, making this a 2-day objective.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Depart Upper Dungeness Trailhead early, hike 6.4 miles and 2,450 feet gain to Boulder Camp, camp overnight (approximately 5-6 hours depending on fitness and pack weight). Day 2: Early start from Boulder Camp, hike 2-3 miles and ascend 1,200 feet to the cirque (1-2 hours), climb the couloir and Class 4 rock pitch to summit (1-2 hours depending on snow/conditions), summit and return via same route, descend to cirque and Boulder Camp (2-3 hours), camp at Boulder Camp overnight. Day 3: Return to trailhead via Upper Dungeness Trail (4-5 hours).
routes.bail: The route offers several bailout points. If parties feel uncomfortable with the rock section above the saddle, the Northwest Summit (7,285 ft) is an easier alternative requiring only Class 2-3 scrambling. From the cirque, retreat is straightforward in good visibility. In poor weather or deteriorating conditions at Boulder Camp, retreat via the Upper Dungeness Trail.
routes.turnaround: Scout the rock crux and snow conditions from the cirque before committing. If the couloir is iced or the rock section appears unstable, this is a reasonable turnaround point. Aim to be off the summit by early afternoon to allow daylight for the descent.
routes.bestSeason: June through early October, with best conditions typically July through early September. The couloir may be filled with snow until late June or early July, and can be icy in cold weather. Earlier in the season (June) expect more snow coverage and avalanche risk in the couloir; later in the season (August-September) the scree is fully melted and climbing is drier, though loose rock is more exposed. October can be good if conditions allow, but weather deteriorates rapidly.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable or absent at Boulder Camp and cirque. Satellite messenger recommended for safety.
routes.proNeeds: Single 50-60m rope for the party (optional downclimb, essential if you lack confidence on Class 4 terrain). No other protection typically needed, though some parties may bring a small amount of gear for a psychological belay if desired.
routes.detailedRack: The rock quality is poor throughout, and bolts are absent. Do not expect protection. A single rope allows for one rappel if needed. Most parties climb this unprotected. Bring an ice axe and crampons for steep snow. A helmet is highly recommended due to loose rock and rockfall potential.
proTips: Start very early (dawn or earlier) to avoid afternoon rockfall hazard in the gully between the two peaks
The rock is poor quality; downclimbing may be safer than rappelling
Aim to complete the rock crux before noon when rockfall risk peaks
Reconnoiter the rock section from the cirque before starting the couloir to gauge conditions
The northwest summit is an easier alternative if the technical rock seems marginal
Mark the route to Boulder Camp from the cirque if snow is present; navigation can be tricky
Carry extra food and water; Boulder Camp is your last reliable water source
watchOut: Rockfall in the gully between the SE and NW summits; route at dawn before afternoon heating
Poor-quality rock throughout; small holds and flakes are common
Iced couloir in spring and cold weather; crampons essential
Loose scree slope if couloir is melted out (descending is easy but ascending is slabby)
Exposed rock pitch near summit; fall protection is absent and ground is steep
knownHazards: Rockfall from the gully between peaks, especially during warming and afternoon sun
Snow and ice in the couloir, particularly in early season (June-early July)
Poor-quality friable rock prone to flaking and loose holds
Exposure on the upper couloir and rock section above 7,000 ft saddle
Objective hazard from rockfall in the gully is significant and cannot be entirely mitigated
objHaz: Objective avalanche hazard (couloir in early season)

access.landManager: Olympic National Park
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required at Upper Dungeness Trailhead (approximately 5-7 per day or 30 annual). Backcountry permit: 8 per adult (16+) per night, free for youth 15 and under, plus 6 non-refundable reservation fee per permit.
access.permit: Required: Olympic National Park Backcountry Wilderness Permit for all overnight stays. Reserve in advance via Recreation.gov (May 15 - Oct 15 for summer season; Oct 16 - May 14 for winter season). Call (360) 565-3100 or visit a Wilderness Information Center in person.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Annual Pass

road.name: FS Road 2870 to Upper Dungeness Trailhead
road.status: Seasonal; typically open June through October. Check with Olympic National Forest for current status.
road.seasonalGate: May vary; contact ranger district for closure dates
road.driveNote: Approximately 1 hour 15 minutes drive time from Port Townsend or Quilcene. Road is gravel/dirt; high-clearance vehicle recommended but not required in summer.

climate.forecastZone: Olympic Mountains / Puget Sound region
climate.typical: Typical conditions include early morning sun and afternoon clouds/precipitation. High humidity, frequent rain and snow above 5,500 ft.
climate.spring: Cooler temperatures, frequent snow and rain, avalanche potential in couloirs
climate.summer: Warmest season; daytime highs 50-60F at 7,000 ft. Low humidity for the region, but still high compared to elsewhere. Less snow than spring.
climate.fall: Cooling rapidly; freeze-thaw cycles create icy conditions. Early season closure possible by mid-October.
climate.winter: Severe cold, heavy snow, extreme avalanche danger. Route essentially unclimbable December through March.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn or earliest light (5 AM or earlier) for approach day

waypoints.name: Colorado Lake / Home Lake area cirque
Saddle between SE and NW peaks (~7,000 ft)

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Visitor Center: (360) 565-3130
emergency.nearestHospital: Jefferson Healthcare (Port Townsend, ~45 miles): (360) 385-2200
emergency.notes: Extremely remote location; evacuation by helicopter would be necessary for serious injury. Cell service is unreliable or absent. Rescue time from most emergencies would be 4+ hours minimum. Self-rescue and prevention are critical.

pitchDetail.notes: Exposed rock section above 7,000 ft saddle. First 20-50 feet of rock is the crux, consisting of steep scrambling and low 5th-class climbing on poor, friable stone. Often the rock section is a moat climb (vertical snow bank beside exposed rock) in early season. Typically 50-60 feet of total climbing.

routes.corrections: Peak hierarchy data indicates 'North Cascades' parent area, but Warrior Peak is located in the Olympic Mountains (Olympic National Park, Jefferson County, Washington). The given elevation of 7314 ft matches the Southeast Summit per peakbagger.com listing (Peak #1020). Discrepancies noted: (1) Most sources round elevation to 7,300-7,320 ft; the exact 7314 ft appears in listsofjohn.com. (2) Alpine grade typically listed as Class 3-4 in guidebooks, but experienced climbers consistently rate the technical rock section Class 4 or higher. Rock grade of 5.0-5.2 inferred from trip reports describing 20-50 feet of low 5th-class sections.

routes.overview: A moderate alpine rock climb ascending the prominent west ridge of Mount Thomson, featuring 5 pitches of Class 4 to 5.6 climbing on exposed, well-featured rock with excellent views and multiple descent options.
routes.beta: The West Ridge is Mount Thomson's most technical and aesthetic alpine climbing objective. The route ascends 4-5 pitches of low to mid-fifth-class rock on the west ridge crest, with the crux being a well-featured chimney on pitch 1 and a 5.6 bulge pull on pitch 2. Route-finding is generally straightforward once established on the ridge proper. Multiple belay options exist on comfortable ledges with solid natural anchors. The descent via the East Ridge (Class 3-4) balances the upclimb and includes one optional 20-foot Class 4 rappel.
routes.approach: Drive I-90 east from Seattle to Exit 52 (West Summit/Snoqualmie Pass). At the off-ramp end, turn left under the freeway onto Alpental Road. In 0.1 mile, take the first right into the Pacific Crest Trail trailhead parking lot (3,000 ft, coordinates 47.427893, -121.413485). A Trail Park Pass is required. Hike the PCT northbound approximately 5.7 miles, passing the Kendall Katwalk (a notched cliff section at 6.6 miles), to Ridge Lake at mile 6.5. Continue 0.4 miles north past Ridge Lake to a steep climber's trail branching left toward Bumblebee Pass (5,400 ft). Descend steeply into Bumblebee Basin via talus and scree. Cross the scenic alpine basin southward for approximately 0.5-1 mile to the base of the West Ridge notch (approximately 5,600 ft). Total approach distance: approximately 8-8.5 miles; gain to base: approximately 3,600 ft. Approach time: 4-5 hours.
routes.descent: Descend via the East Ridge. From the true summit, down-climb or rappel the Class 4 section (approximately 20 feet). Continue down-climbing and scrambling Class 3-4 terrain via the east ridge, following a climbers' trail through scree and talus down to the Bumblebee Basin (5,400 ft). Retrace the approach route back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Rappel or down-climb the Class 4 section from the summit (approximately 20 feet). Continue down-climbing the East Ridge via exposed Class 3-4 terrain with tricky slab sections; use caution on loose rock and follow the established climbers' trail. Descend to the notch at approximately 5,600 feet, then scramble down the scree and talus slope back into Bumblebee Basin (5,400 ft). Retrace the approach route via the steep climber's path to Bumblebee Pass, then follow the PCT back past Ridge Lake, Kendall Katwalk, and down to the trailhead at 3,000 ft.

pitchDetail.notes: Step left into a well-featured chimney from the belay. One 5.6 move right off the ground, followed by mostly Class 5.4 climbing up to 55 meters. Abundant ledges and solid anchors.
Mostly low-to-mid fifth class near the crest of the ridge. Pull over a bulge with somewhat loose rock (2-3 five-point-six moves). 30-40 meters. Solid natural anchors on comfortable ledges.
Class 3-4 slab traverse with exposure. Navigate left along the skyline. Mostly easier terrain with solid rock.
A 'staircase' despite its steep appearance from below. Low fifth-class climbing. Approximately 50-70 meters.
Largely Class 3-4 scramble down a gravelly path to the notch between the false and true summits. Short final section to true summit.

routes.rappels: 1 (one double-rope 20ft rappel optional on descent; down-climbing alternative exists)
routes.bestSeason: July through September provide the most favorable weather. June trips encounter residual snow on the approach but the rock should be fairly dry. Snow fills the basin until mid-season; reliable water is available throughout the summer. Early July offers optimal conditions balancing lower snow, dry rock, and abundant water.
routes.turnaround: Climb committed to a safe turnaround time during daylight; descent via Class 3-4 terrain requires visibility. Most parties summit by early afternoon. Do not begin the final pitches if unable to descend to the notch before dusk.
routes.bail: From below pitch 3: down-climb via Class 4-5 terrain to belay 2, then reverse lower pitches to exit the route. From the false summit: descend Class 3-4 via the east ridge (standard descent route). From the true summit or near it: descend via the East Ridge, Class 3-4.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is intermittent in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Service from the summit is unreliable; no satellite communication documented. Inform someone of your climbing plan before departure.
gear: 60m rope (doubled 8mm, or single 9mm+ suitable for longer rappels)
Small to medium cams (up to 3 inches, single set to #2-#3)
Slings (multiple, including 1-2 longer slings for natural anchors)
Quickdraws or runners for protection placement
routes.detailedRack: A light traditional rack is all that is needed: singles of nuts from the small end of a set, a set of cams from approximately 0.5 inches to 3 inches (with one or two pieces in each size range), slings, and a 60m rope. Belays are comfortable ledges with solid natural anchors, minimizing the need for additional protection. Many parties simul-climb sections on easy terrain to move faster.
routes.proNeeds: Light natural protection and minimal aid gear needed. The route is well-anchored on natural features. No bolts or fixed anchors on the route; all belay stations are at natural ledges with bomber rock for anchor placement (trees, blocks, cracks).
proTips: The route is not sustained; pace yourself—it is faster and easier than it appears from below.
Start early; the long approach and descent are best completed in daylight.
Route-finding: stay on the ridge crest by following the left skyline when viewed from Ridge Lake.
Abundant loose and dirty rock past Bumblebee Pass; dislodge rocks carefully and alert climbers below.
The notch between the false and true summits is short; don't miss it on descent.
Simul-climbing Class 3-4 sections will speed the ascent significantly.
Porphyritic andesite (white feldspar crystals) features good cracks and jugs; rock quality is generally solid on the ridge crest but loose in some talus sections.
watchOut: Loose and dirty rock throughout; dislodge stones alert those below.
Rockfall hazard is significant; every experienced party dislodges rock in the last mile of approach.
Exposure is considerable on the ridge; be comfortable with high-consequence climbing.
Route-finding can be tricky; stay on the ridge crest; deviations lead to choss.
The crux chimney on pitch 1 is well-featured but exposed.
Descending Class 3-4 slabs requires careful foot placement and sound judgment.
objHaz: Loose andesite rock (especially in talus sections)
Wet or icy conditions if approached in early season
knownHazards: Rockfall is unavoidable on the chossy terrain past Bumblebee Pass; everyone in an experienced party dislodges rock in the final mile of approach.
The porphyritic andesite features good climbing rock on the ridge crest but breaks into loose blocks and talus in unraveled sections.
Exposure is significant throughout the route with little margin for error on the high-consequence sections.
Route-finding requires attention; the correct path follows the ridge crest; deviations lead to choss and loose terrain.

timing.recommendedStart: 7:00 AM from trailhead for same-day ascent; earlier (5:30-6:00 AM) ideal for fast parties

road.name: I-90 to Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass); Alpental Road to PCT trailhead
road.status: Paved and open year-round; I-90 may close in severe winter weather
road.driveNote: From Seattle, approximately 1.5 hours east on I-90. Exit 52 (West Summit) is clearly marked. Trailhead parking requires a Washington Trail Park Pass (Discover Pass). Park entrance fee or pass required. Main lot holds ~50 vehicles; overflow available.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (NWAC Mt. Baker area)
climate.typical: Maritime west coast climate with significant precipitation, especially on western slopes. Summer alpine weather is variable but generally benign.
climate.spring: Snow at altitude; approach feasible late June with caution. Rock may be wet.
climate.summer: July through September are optimal. Daytime highs typically 40-50°F at summit; nights can drop below freezing. Afternoon thunderstorms possible but less common than in lower elevations.
climate.fall: September early season viable; October rare but possible. Weather deteriorates rapidly.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard; not typically attempted.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Snoqualmie Ranger District
access.passRequired: Washington Trail Park Pass (Discover Pass) required for trailhead parking
access.fees: Washington Discover Pass: approximately $30 for annual; $10 for daily pass
access.closures: Alpine Lakes Wilderness has no climbing closures, but I-90 may close in winter storms. Check WSDOT road conditions before departure.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff Dispatch: 206-296-3311; Snoqualmie Ranger District emergency: 1-888-337-FIRE (in wilderness)
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District (North Bend Office): 902 S.E. North Bend Way, Building 1, North Bend, WA 98045; (425) 888-1421
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital (Emergency Department), approximately 30 minutes from Snoqualmie Pass; helicopter evacuation available from mountain to hospital
emergency.notes: Cell service is intermittent in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Notify a responsible person of your climbing plan before departure. Allow extra time for rescue from high-consequence terrain.

waypoints.name: Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) Trailhead at Snoqualmie Pass
Bumblebee Basin (alpine basin south of Mount Thomson)

routes.corrections: Input elevation listed as 6,550 ft; authoritative sources confirm 6,554 ft (USGS, Mountain Project, Wikipedia, WTA, Peakbagger). Using 6,554 ft. Spelling note: peak name is 'Thomson' (not Thompson); spelling was officially corrected by USBGN in 1981.

blurb: Mount Thomson is a 6,554-foot alpine peak in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Snoqualmie Pass, Washington, composed of Keechelus andesite and named for Seattle city engineer Reginald Heber Thomson. The West Ridge is the peak's premier climbing objective, offering 5 pitches of Class 4 to 5.6 rock on an exposed, well-featured alpine ridge with multiple descent options via the East Ridge. First ascended in 1917 by Joe Hazard and B. French.

Mount Degenhardt, a 8,102-foot peak in the Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, is a prominent alpine objective combining glacier travel and technical rock climbing. Named after William Degenhardt, an early 20th-century mountaineer who made the first ascent in 1931, the peak offers Grade III climbing with Class 4-5 summit terrain. Located in the remote Southern Pickets basin, it is accessed via the Goodell Creek approach and represents a serious alpine undertaking requiring mountaineering skills and self-sufficiency in a remote environment.
hierarchyNote: Mount Degenhardt is correctly positioned in the North Cascades National Park hierarchy as a peak in the Picket Range/Southern Pickets region of Whatcom County, Washington. The given elevation of 8,102 feet is consistent with mapping sources, though some older references cite 8,000+ feet. No hierarchical issues identified.

routes.overview: The primary alpine route on Mount Degenhardt ascends via Crescent Creek Basin on the southwest approach, then tackles the steep summit horn with Class 4-5 technical rock climbing. This Grade III route combines glacier travel, snow climbing, and technical rock to reach the 8,102-foot summit.
routes.beta: Approach from Goodell Creek Trail, crossing Terror Creek to reach the ridge system. Traverse through Crescent Creek Basin under the southwest slope, then ascend to the summit horn. The route involves mixed snow and rock terrain, with technical climbing concentrated on the summit block.
routes.approach: From Goodell Creek Campground at milepost 119 on SR 20 (60 miles east of Burlington, WA), hike the Goodell Creek Trail on old forestry road bed for approximately 4.5 miles to reach Terror Creek. Ford or use a log to cross Terror Creek, then locate the climbers' trail ascending steeply to the ridge called 'The Barrier.' Continue to a saddle below Pinnacle Peak at approximately 6,400 feet. From this saddle, descend 200 feet into Crescent Creek Basin. Traverse the basin at approximately 5,800 feet elevation on mixed snow and rock. The approach to Crescent Creek Basin continues approximately 9-10 miles from the trailhead and typically takes 9-14 hours of hiking to reach climbing camp.
routes.descent: Rappel or downclimb from the summit block using established anchors on the south side. Single-rope rappels are documented. Alternatively, downclimb the technical sections and traverse back through Crescent Creek Basin, regaining the ridge camp in approximately 3-4 hours from the summit.
routes.descentText: From the 8,102-foot summit, descend the steep rock and Class 4 terrain via rappel (single rope on south side anchors) or downclimb to the Crescent Basin approach gully. Once below the technical climbing, traverse back across Crescent Creek Basin via the same route used on approach. The descent typically takes 3-4 hours from summit to camp depending on snow conditions and group size.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Goodell Creek Campground to Terror Creek Basin camp at approximately 5,800 feet elevation (9-14 hours). Day 2: From camp, traverse Crescent Creek Basin and ascend Mount Degenhardt via the southwest approach. Ascend to the summit horn and climb Class 4-5 rock to the 8,102-foot summit (5-6 hours). Descend to camp (3-4 hours). Day 3: Descend from camp to Goodell Creek Campground (7-10 hours).
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turn-around time by mid-afternoon to ensure adequate daylight for descent from the summit. Alpine starts (dawn departure from camp) are essential for safe completion. Monitor weather constantly; descend immediately if electrical storms approach or if snow conditions deteriorate significantly.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with July and August being optimal due to lower snow levels and longer daylight hours. August is preferred as most of the previous year's snow has melted by late August. September is possible but requires caution regarding fall snow and shorter daylight hours.
routes.bail: The primary bail route is immediate descent down Crescent Creek Basin, traversing back to the ridge camp. From camp, retreat via Terror Creek trail to Goodell Creek Campground. If weather deteriorates while climbing the summit block, rappel or downclimb to the base of the technical section and retreat via Crescent Basin.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad rack for Class 4-5 rock climbing with doubled slings for rappel anchors. Glacier gear: crampons, ice axe, rope (60m recommended).
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine rack: quickdraws, slings, stoppers, hexes, small to medium cams. Doubled slings for rappel anchors. Glacier gear: crampons, ice axe, 60-meter rope.
proTips: The approach takes 9-14 hours; start early from the trailhead
Camp at the established 5,800-foot location to minimize exposure to loose terrain
Avoid climbing during or immediately after precipitation; loose rock becomes more hazardous when wet
Continuous snowcover in Crescent Basin makes contouring at 5,800 feet easier
Single-rope rappel anchors documented on south side; inspect before trusting
August offers the best climbing conditions with minimal snow
watchOut: Lots of loose rock on approach and climbing route; wear helmet at all times
Navigation can be challenging in whiteout conditions
Slippery terrain when crossing streams or wet rock
Avalanche terrain exists on steeper slopes, particularly in spring
Exposure is significant on the final summit block
Cell phone service is unreliable in this remote basin
knownHazards: Falling rock from climbers above or natural weathering is a constant threat on loose alpine granite
Steep terrain between 5,800 and 6,400 feet can become avalanche-prone after new snow
Poor trail markings on the Goodell Creek approach have caused parties to spend hours on false trails
Wet maritime snow in the Cascades is heavy and prone to avalanche
Subarctic climate conditions create exposure to wind and cold even in summer

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn start from camp for summit attempt; early morning departure from trailhead for approach day

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to Goodell Creek Campground
road.status: Open year-round; winter closures possible
road.seasonalGate: State Route 20 often closed during heavy snow November through April, typically reopening by May. Verify current conditions before planning trips.
