road.driveNote: Goodell Creek Campground at milepost 119 on SR 20, 60 miles east of Burlington, WA. Turn north onto unpaved road across SR 20 from Newhalem campground. Follow signs to trailhead parking.

climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North Cascades, Whatcom County (NOAA zone WAZ567)
climate.typical: Subarctic maritime climate with high precipitation year-round. West-side North Cascades receive heavy snow in winter and moderate precipitation in summer. Weather changes rapidly.
climate.spring: Wet and unstable; persistent snow at higher elevations. Temperatures 30-45°F at sea level, below freezing at altitude. Avalanche danger elevated.
climate.summer: Best climbing season with stable weather windows. Temperatures 45-70°F lower elevations, 25-40°F at 8,000 feet. Afternoon thunderstorms possible.
climate.fall: Weather becomes unstable with early-season snow possible. Temperatures 35-55°F; sudden cold snaps bring snow. Daylight decreases rapidly.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall November-April with severe cold. Not recommended for climbing due to avalanche danger and limited daylight.

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for overnight stays. Reserve online at Recreation.gov or in person at Wilderness Information Center.
access.passRequired: No entry pass required; however, backcountry camping requires permit.
access.fees: Day use free. Overnight backcountry permits $10-20 per night. Goodell Creek Campground car camping has standard NPS fees.
access.closures: Southern Pickets region occasionally closed due to hazardous conditions. Check with North Cascades National Park (360-854-7200) before planning.

emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (Haggen Family Emergency and Trauma Center - Level II Trauma Center)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Visitor Center, Newhalem; Wilderness Information Center at park entrance
emergency.notes: Helicopter rescue possible in good weather but may be delayed several hours. Assume rescue costs of $1,000+ per flight hour. Cell service unreliable. Response times from Whatcom County SAR can exceed 24 hours due to approach complexity.

routes.corrections: Given elevation of 8,102 ft is slightly above some sources citing 8,000+ ft. Wikipedia confirms first ascent as William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg in 1931. Route known locally as either 'Southwest Route' (from Crescent Basin approach on SW side) or 'Corkscrew Route' (spiral path around summit block).

blurb: Boston Peak (8,894 ft) is a prominent alpine peak in the North Cascades National Park's Boston Basin, known for its impressive sweeping views and reputation for loose, friable rock. First summited in 1938 by Calder Bressler, Ray Clough, Bill Cox, and Tom Myers, this 26th highest peak in Washington offers challenging Class 4 scrambling and exceptional panoramic views despite its poor-quality granite composition.

routes.overview: Class 4 scramble on steep, loose rock featuring excellent views but significant rockfall hazard. The summit block appears loose from below but firms up significantly higher on the route.
knownHazards: Boston Peak has a well-documented reputation for poor rock quality with numerous fatalities on the peak
Extremely loose and friable granite throughout the summit block requires careful movement
Rockfall danger is significant, particularly on descent; parties ascend unroped to minimize risk from dislodged rock
Steep exposure with limited protection opportunities on Class 4 sections
watchOut: Assume all rock is unstable; test holds carefully before committing weight
Rockfall is persistent hazard; descend quickly or use rappels to minimize exposure time
Moats and bergschrunds around the Boston-Sahale saddle require careful navigation, particularly in early season
Summit block appears far more unstable than it actually is; rock becomes firmer as you ascend
routes.beta: The standard approach ascends unroped via ledges on the east/southeast face to minimize rockfall danger. Parties typically establish camps at Boston Basin low camp (5,300 ft) or high camp (6,400 ft), then traverse via the Boston-Sahale saddle to access the summit route. Most ascents complete 6-9 hours from high camp to summit and back. Rope is essential for descent via three rappel stations.
routes.approach: From Boston Basin Trailhead (3,200 ft on Cascade River Road): Follow steep trail 2.5 miles gaining 2,100-3,100 ft to low camp at 5,300 ft (3-4 hours). Continue to high camp at 6,400 ft (additional 1-1.5 hours). Alternative approach via Cascade Pass (4.2 miles, well-maintained trail to 5,400 ft) then take Sahale Arm trail to high camps at 7,800 ft. From high camp, traverse glacier and talus to the Boston-Sahale col (7,500-7,600 ft), then traverse loose ridge/talus and ascend the southeast-facing summit block via Class 4 ledges.
routes.descent: Descend via rappels on south face. Standard descent uses three 25-30 meter rappels with 60m rope extending to snow below, or can be done with shorter rappels if using full rope length. Existing anchors should be backed up with webbing. Experienced parties may downclimb, but rappels are safer given loose rock. From snow below rappels, traverse back to Boston-Sahale col and reverse approach route.
routes.descentText: From the summit, locate the rap anchors on the south face below the summit block. Each rappel is approximately 25-30 meters to the next anchor station (three total rappels to reach snow). A 60-meter rope allows full rappel length to each station. Ensure anchors are solid and back up with additional webbing before committing. During descent, watch for rockfall from above and stay alert to rock instability at anchor points. Once at the snow below the final rappel, traverse northeast back to the Boston-Sahale col using care to avoid moats and open bergschrunds, then reverse the approach route.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive from Marblemount to Boston Basin Trailhead (22 miles, ~45 min). Hike to Boston Basin high camp (2.5-3 miles, 3-4 hours, 2,100-3,100 ft gain). Camp at 6,400 ft. Day 2: From camp, traverse glacier and talus to Boston-Sahale col. Ascend southeast face via Class 4 ledges to summit (6-9 hours round trip). Rappel descent with three 25-30m rappels to snow. Return to camp. Day 3: Descend to trailhead (3-4 hours, reverse of Day 1).
routes.bail: The route is easy to bail from the Boston-Sahale col if conditions are unfavorable or time is running short; simply descend via the approach route back to high camp. Once on the summit block, descend via rappels (standard rappel points are established). If rappel anchors are compromised, experienced parties can downclimb, though this is more risky given loose rock. The route offers no emergency shelter; deteriorating weather should trigger immediate descent.
routes.turnaround: Strong parties should plan to start the summit push by 7 am from high camp to ensure adequate daylight for the Class 4 climbing, rappel descent, and return to camp. Turn back by 1-2 pm if you have not reached the summit block to ensure safe light for rappels and traverse back to camp.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through late August offers the most stable conditions. June approach snow may persist until early July at high camps. Late August weather becomes more variable. July and August typically provide the best balance of snow melt (easier glacier travel) and stable weather. Avoid May (high avalanche/crevasse risk) and September+ (shortened daylight, snow/weather increasing).
proTips: Ascend the summit block unroped even though it's Class 4 to minimize rockfall danger; each dislodged rock is a hazard to climbers below
The rock appears far worse than it actually is; though loose-looking, handholds firm up significantly as you climb higher on the block
Use three raps to descend south face rather than downclimbing; rappels are faster and safer despite the commitment
Leave early from camp (dawn if possible) to maximize daylight for climbing and rappel descent
Bring webbing to back up possibly corroded anchor bolts before committing to rappels
whatToBring: Alpine boots with good ankle support
Crampons and ice axe (if snow/ice present in gullies)
Daypack with extra food and water
gear: 60m single or half rope (essential for rappel descent)
Harness and rappel belay device (ATC, GriGri, or equivalent)
Carabiners x8-10 (mix of locking and non-locking)
Webbing or cord for anchor backups
Alpine boots with good ankle support
First aid kit with blister/trauma supplies
routes.detailedRack: This route does not require a trad rack as it is a Class 4 scramble. However, bring 2-4 quickdraws for rappel anchor attachment if necessary. Primary gear is a 60-meter rope (single or half rope), harness, rappel belay device (ATC, GriGri, Reverso, etc.), 8-10 carabiners (mix of locking and non-locking), webbing or cordelette for anchor backup, and helmet. For potential snow/ice in early season, crampons and ice axes are necessary.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on the summit block is minimal given the Class 4 nature and loose rock quality. Climbers should be comfortable with exposure and loose rock; this is a scramble, not a protected rock climb. Most protection comes from careful foot/handhold placement and rappel anchors on descent. If parties wish to use protection, small cams or pitons can be placed in rock cracks for anchors on exposed traverses, but the rock quality is poor and may not hold well.

timing.recommendedStart: 7:00 AM from high camp to ensure adequate daylight for climb, rappel descent, and return to camp

climate.typical: Alpine summer conditions with cool temps (40-55F at elevation), afternoon wind common. Freeze-thaw cycles typical at high elevation.
climate.spring: Spring (May-early June) has high snow coverage, crevasse danger on glaciers, and avalanche risk in gullies. Not recommended.
climate.summer: Mid-June through August: generally most stable. Afternoon thunderstorms possible July-August. Snow at high camps persists until early July. Daytime temps 35-50F, nights near freezing.
climate.fall: September onwards: shortening daylight, increased precipitation, early snow at altitude. Not recommended.
climate.winter: Winter climbs possible but serious undertaking. First winter ascent via West Face was February 2024. Requires ice climbing skills and avalanche awareness.
climate.forecastZone: North Cascades alpine zone; check mountain-forecast.com for Boston Peak (2711m) and NWAC avalanche forecasts

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required year-round for overnight camping. Permits are free at Marblemount Ranger Station or $6 non-refundable reservation fee via Recreation.gov
access.passRequired: North Cascades National Park entrance pass or America the Beautiful annual pass (no fee charged separately for day use)
access.fees: Permit $6 reservation fee (if advance reservation via Recreation.gov); free if walk-up at ranger station. No park entrance fee for day use.
access.closures: Cascade River Road may close in spring/late summer due to flooding or rockslides. Road is gated at milepost 20 (3 miles before Cascade Pass) in winter (Nov-May typically).

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open June-October typically; winter closure at milepost 20
road.seasonalGate: Milepost 20 (closed Nov-Apr typically) - 3 miles before Cascade Pass trailhead; foot traffic permitted beyond gate
road.driveNote: Cascade River Road branches from SR-20 near Marblemount and is paved for first 10 miles, then gravel. Drive 23 miles from SR-20 junction to reach Cascade Pass trailhead. Boston Basin trailhead is accessed via Cascade River Road at Diamond Mine Road fork (22 miles from junction). Confirm road conditions before departure in shoulder seasons.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington WA (330 S Stillaguamish Ave): (360) 435-2133 - approximately 45 minutes from trailhead
emergency.notes: Cell reception in Boston Basin is unreliable; satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, Spot) strongly recommended. Report emergencies to 911 if cell service available, or via ranger station. SAR coordination for North Cascades is through Skagit County Sheriff.

routes.corrections: The route is listed as 'Southwest Face' in the database, but authoritative sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, trip reports) identify the standard route as the East/Southeast Face with Class 4 climbing. No named 'Southwest Face' route appears in primary climbing sources; this may be a variant name or misidentification. The grade (II, Class 4) and characteristics match the documented East/Southeast Face route. The Southwest Face designation should be verified against original source documentation if available.
routes.comms: Cell reception is unreliable in Boston Basin. Satellite communicator (InReach, Spot) strongly recommended. Ranger station can relay emergency messages if landline accessed at trailhead.

hierarchyNote: HIERARCHY CONCERN: Mount Johnson is in the Olympic Mountains (Jefferson County, Olympic National Park), NOT the North Cascades as indicated in the task description. The elevation (7,680 ft) and coordinates match the Olympic peak. Regional context and parent area in the database should reflect Olympic Mountains, not Cascades.
blurb: Mount Johnson is the fourth-highest peak in the Olympic Mountains at 7,680 feet, located in the Needles range within Olympic National Park in Jefferson County, Washington. Named after early climbers Elvin Johnson (though evidence attributes earlier ascents to Scott Osborn, Joe Halwax, and John King circa 1935), this peak features notorious loose and rotten rock typical of the Olympics but offers outstanding views from its exposed scramble summit.

routes.fa: Scott Osborn, Joe Halwax, John King, c. 1935
routes.overview: A steep, exposed class 4 scramble from Surprise Basin to the summit via loose scree and rotten rock terrain, requiring careful route-finding and rock quality management. The route approaches from the saddle between Mount Clark and Mount Johnson, ascending ramps and scrambling to the summit block.
routes.beta: The standard approach to Mount Johnson involves hiking to Royal Lake (7.2 miles, 2,650 ft gain), continuing into upper basin, then accessing Surprise Basin. From Surprise Basin, aim for the 6900-foot notch between Mount Johnson and Mount Clark. Mount Johnson sits on climber's left from this col. The route ascends via ramp systems, keeping to class 3 by skirting right of Gasp Pinnacle, then makes a final class 4 push to the summit block. Terrain is consistently loose and rotten, particularly in upper sections.
routes.approach: From US 101 near Sequim, take Louella Road, then Palo Alto Road west for 5.8 miles, turning right on Forest Road 2880 to Upper Dungeness Trailhead parking (47.8779, -123.1369). Follow Upper Dungeness trail through old-growth forest 1 mile to Royal Basin Trail junction at footlog bridge. Turn right on Royal Basin Trail through moss-carpeted forest with steep sections and switchbacks, gaining 2,650 feet in 7.2 miles to Royal Lake (5,700 ft). Continue off-trail past Royal Lake into upper basin (1 mile, 500 ft gain). Continue through increasingly rocky terrain past moraine into Surprise Basin. Loose talus and scree approach to Surprise Basin is the loosest section of entire climb. Aim for Surprise Col at approximately 7,000 feet.
routes.descent: Descend via same route with extreme caution on loose terrain. Due to exposed class 4 scrambling and poor rock quality, maintain careful footwork and strict spacing between party members. Use same ramp systems and route-finding to safely descend back to Surprise Col and basin.
routes.descentText: Retrace ascent line through Surprise Basin down to upper Royal Basin. Travel one person at a time through narrow gully sections to avoid rockfall hazard to team below. The loose scree apron descending from Surprise Col requires careful, controlled movement to prevent uncontrolled slides. Regain Royal Basin Trail at moraine and return via established trail system to Royal Lake and trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike Upper Dungeness to Royal Basin Trail (1 mile), continue on Royal Basin Trail to Royal Lake, establish camp at designated sites. Day 2: Early start from camp; continue off-trail through upper basin and access Surprise Basin. Reach col (4-5 hours from camp) and summit Mount Johnson (2-3 hours from col). Return to Surprise Col and descend to upper basin camp. Day 3: Descend Royal Basin Trail and return to trailhead (4-5 hours).
routes.bail: Retreat at any point back down to Royal Basin if conditions deteriorate or rockfall hazard becomes excessive. Safe bailout locations include returning to Surprise Col or upper Royal Basin camp. Do not push to summit in deteriorating weather or if rockfall activity increases. Descend immediately if lightning threatens.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if loose rock conditions become unmanageable, if weather deteriorates significantly, if party is not maintaining safe spacing to avoid rockfall injuries, or if fatigue compromises footwork on exposed scrambling. Conservative turnaround: if not at Surprise Col by mid-morning, consider day hike to col only. If summit push begins after 1 PM, retreat to col and plan summit attempt for next day.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer most favorable climbing conditions. Early July climbing may require ice axe and crampons; late June too early for safe travel. October temperatures drop rapidly with early storms common. Peak window is mid-July through mid-September. July offers driest weather but maximum rockfall exposure; August-early September offer more stable rock conditions.
routes.face: North face and northeast-facing gully system from Surprise Basin approach
routes.detailedRack: Standard alpine scrambling rack: helmet (required), ice axe (early season), crampons (early season), 1-2 locking carabiners, 1-2 quickdraws, minimal rock protection. Given loose terrain and poor rock quality, traditional protection unreliable. Route managed through route-finding and footwork, not protection.
routes.proNeeds: Helmets mandatory for all party members due to objective rockfall hazard and party-generated rockfall risk on steep, loose scree. No rock climbing protection practical; this is a route managed through exposure to loose terrain and careful footwork.
proTips: Scout the approach to Surprise Basin before darkness; terrain is confusing and scree slopes treacherous to descend in poor light.
Sweat Pinnacle and Gasp Pinnacle are useful landmark features for navigation in upper basin.
Start very early (5-6 AM from camp) to maximize daylight for scrambling and descent before afternoon weather deteriorates.
Small ice field in Surprise Col can be crossed without crampons if snow patches are firm, but always carry crampons as backup.
Helmet discipline is critical; this peak has genuine objective hazard from loose rock even with perfect weather.
watchOut: Rockfall hazard is serious and constant on Mount Johnson's loose terrain; helmet is not optional, it is essential.
Rotten rock means holds break unexpectedly; test all holds before committing full body weight, particularly near summit block.
Route-finding in Surprise Basin requires attention; poor visibility or fatigue can lead to wrong gullies and lost time.
Weather changes rapidly at 7,600+ feet; afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August.
Late-season snow and ice patches persist in Surprise Col into August; ice axe and crampons may be needed even in summer.
knownHazards: Objective rockfall hazard from sustained loose and rotten rock throughout upper mountain, with particular danger in gully systems leading to summit block.
Party-generated rockfall when multiple climbers on steep sections; maintain strict spacing with one person at a time through narrow passages.
Avalanche risk exists in early season (June-early July) with significant snow and ice in Surprise Col.
Exposure on class 4 scrambling with 500+ foot drops on exposed sections; errors in foot placement can result in falls.
Variable weather with rapid deterioration possible; exposure to afternoon thunderstorms, sudden temperature drops, and wind funneling through basins.

climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Seattle WFO: Olympic Mountains zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with cool, wet conditions. At 7,600+ feet on Mount Johnson, expect 10-15F cooler than sea level. Strong wind exposure on summit.
climate.spring: May-early June: Substantial snowpack, avalanche risk high, road access may be limited. Not recommended for first ascents.
climate.summer: July-September: Warmest period with daytime highs 45-55F at summit. July tends driest but has afternoon thunderstorms. August-early September more stable. Persistent snowfields in Surprise Col and north-facing aspects.
climate.fall: October: Rapid cooling, early storms likely, snow at higher elevations. Season effectively closes by early October.
climate.winter: November-April: Deep snow, avalanche hazard extreme. Only experienced winter mountaineers attempt.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Olympic National Park)
access.permit: Yes - Wilderness permit required year-round via Recreation.gov. Early reservations essential May 1 - September 30.
access.fees: Parking fee required (Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent)
access.closures: None typical for Mount Johnson specifically, though trail access may be gated seasonally. Park generally open year-round but backcountry camping typically May-October; winter access severely limited.

road.name: Forest Road 2880 (Upper Dungeness Road)
road.status: Passable late spring through fall; may be closed/blocked in winter.
road.seasonalGate: Typically open May through October; closed by snow or gates in winter months.
road.driveNote: From US 101 near Sequim, take Louella Road west, turn left on Palo Alto Road for 5.8 miles, then right onto Forest Road 2880. Approximately 15-20 minutes from Sequim to trailhead. Parking area has vault toilet and picnic table. Standard passenger cars acceptable; high-clearance recommended.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00-6:00 AM from Royal Lake camp to maximize daylight; 4:00 PM latest summit start

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Visitor Center: (360) 565-3130
emergency.nearestHospital: Olympic Medical Center (Port Angeles) Emergency: (360) 417-7000 (45 min by vehicle). Alternative: Jefferson Healthcare (Port Townsend).
emergency.notes: Cell phone reception unreliable in Royal Basin; satellite communication strongly recommended. For emergencies in wilderness, exit to Royal Lake and attempt signaling with mirror/whistle, then hike to trailhead for emergency services contact.

routes.corrections: Current database lists grade as 'Class 3-4' but sources consistently describe class 4 scramble. First ascent attribution in some sources to Elvin Johnson & George Martin (1940) is secondary ascent; primary ascent evidence points to Scott Osborn, Joe Halwax, John King (circa 1935).
routes.comms: Cell phone reception is unreliable and limited in Royal Basin area and upper approach. Satellite communication or personal locator beacon (PLB) strongly recommended for emergencies. No established communication infrastructure at the peak or access routes.

routes.overview: Alpine scramble ascending to the col between Magic Mountain and Hurry-Up Peak, gaining the south ridge with mixed Class 3-4 broken rock terrain leading to the true summit via moderate exposure.
routes.beta: A popular side trip from the Cascade Pass area or via the Ptarmigan Traverse. The south ridge route involves ascending from the col to mixed rock scrambling with moderate to significant exposure on the summit ridge. Route finding can be tricky; descend via the same route.
routes.approach: Start from Cascade Pass trailhead at end of Cascade River Road (23 miles from Marblemount, ~1 hour drive on mix of paved and gravel road). Hike 3.7 miles with 1,700 ft elevation gain to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). From Cascade Pass, scramble up the northwest slope of Hurry-Up Peak; the route reaches the false summit before dropping 80 feet into a dirty gully on the right side.
routes.descent: Reverse the route down Class 3-4 broken ledges that corkscrew the east side of the summit, back through the gully, and retrace to Cascade Pass.
routes.descentText: Descend via the same route. The Class 3-4 broken ledges require careful footwork on the downclimb. Watch for loose rock throughout the descent. Return to Cascade Pass via the northwest slope, then retrace the 3.7-mile trail back to the trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: July through August offer the most stable weather and minimal snow. June can have icy sections requiring an ice axe. September remains viable but weather becomes more unpredictable. Winter presents significant avalanche danger from wet, heavy snow and extensive snow coverage.
knownHazards: Loose rock is typical throughout the scramble
Moderate to significant exposure on summit ridge; falls could be fatal
Ice on approach slopes in spring and early summer; ice axe may be required
Avalanche danger in winter from wet, heavy snow on upper slopes
Route finding can be challenging; peaks and ridge names are not always clear
watchOut: Significant exposure on the summit ridge requires careful foot placement
Loose rock on upper pitches; test holds before committing
In winter, the west side experiences high precipitation and avalanche hazard
Ice axe essential in spring/early summer when snow is present
proTips: The peak makes an excellent viewpoint for Trapper Lake and the north-central Cascades
A good sidetrip during the Ptarmigan Traverse if approaching from the north
From Cascade Pass, the summit can be reached in approximately 1 hour roundtrip
Consider this route as one of several potential peaks from Cascade Pass (Magic Mountain, Mix-up Peak also nearby)
routes.turnaround: If approaching from Cascade Pass for a day trip, plan to be descending by mid-afternoon to have adequate daylight. Watch weather closely; the pass can deteriorate rapidly.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Seasonal; closed winter at milepost 20 (Eldorado). Paved for first 10 miles, then gravel with narrow and steep sections.
road.seasonalGate: Closed in winter; typically opens by end of June
road.driveNote: Drive from Marblemount on Cascade River Road (State Route 20): 23 miles to trailhead in approximately 1 hour. Confirm road status before driving as winter closure and seasonal conditions affect access.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning for alpine weather safety

climate.typical: Alpine weather dominates; summer (July-August) generally stable with afternoon cloud buildup. Frequent afternoon thunderstorms possible.
climate.spring: Snow present; icy conditions common; ice axe required. Avalanche danger peaks in spring from wet-snow cycles.
climate.summer: Mostly dry; occasional afternoon storms. July and August optimal for stable conditions.
climate.fall: Weather becomes increasingly variable; early storms possible. September remains generally viable but unstable.
climate.winter: High precipitation, especially snow. Wet, heavy snow creates extreme avalanche danger. Route inaccessible.
climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North Cascades (National Weather Service Zone WAZ567)

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park / Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: No day-use fee; Backcountry permit required for overnight camping ($10 per person + $6 reservation fee through Recreation.gov)
access.permit: Backcountry overnight permit required year-round if camping; must be picked up by 11 AM on start date
access.closures: Cascade River Road closed in winter at milepost 20; typically opens by end of June

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Headquarters: (360) 854-7200; Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount): (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: United General Hospital (Sedro-Woolley) or Cascade Valley Hospital (Arlington)
emergency.notes: Area lies on Skagit/Chelan County border; activate emergency response via 911 or park ranger station

routes.comms: Cell phone coverage unreliable at high elevation; satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, Spot) recommended for safety in this remote alpine area. Cascade Pass area has limited to no cell service.
gear: Rock climbing shoes or boots with good footwork
Map and compass or GPS device
Ice axe (required or highly recommended in spring through early summer)
routes.detailedRack: Minimal technical rack needed if climbing unroped. Optional: small selection of cam sizes (0.1-2) and a few nuts for emergency rappel anchors if the group ventures off-route and needs to retreat. Most parties climb unroped and rely on excellent route finding and favorable conditions.
routes.proNeeds: For the South Ridge, protection is minimal if climbing unroped (typical for this alpine scramble). A helmet is essential for rockfall. If roped teams are used, a light rack (2-3 cams per pitch, nuts 1-6) provides safety. Most parties manage without technical protection on this Grade II-PD route.

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach via northwest slope to col between Magic Mountain and Hurry-Up Peak
pitchDetail.notes: Scramble up loose scree and rock to reach the saddle. Moderate exposure as elevation increases. Route finding straightforward.

pitchDetail.pitch: South ridge from col to false summit
pitchDetail.notes: Climb mixed broken rock ledges up the south ridge. Moderate to significant exposure. Watch for loose rock; test holds. Height of terrain: ~300 feet of actual climbing.

pitchDetail.pitch: False summit to true summit (via gully descent and east-side corkscrew)
pitchDetail.notes: Drop 80 feet into dirty gully on right side (east face). Climb Class 3-4 broken ledges that corkscrew around the east side of summit block. Exposed scrambling requires careful foot placement. Final 50 feet to true summit.

routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate or group is struggling with the scramble, retreat to Cascade Pass and descend the trail. The route is escapable at most points—descend carefully the way you came up. Avoid the false summit if route-finding becomes difficult; drop back to col rather than pushing forward.

hierarchyNote: Peak location and hierarchy verified: Hurry-Up Peak (7,821 ft, 48.433793 N -121.038057 W) is correctly positioned at the northern terminus of the Ptarmigan Traverse in the North Cascades Range, on the Skagit/Chelan County border, southeast of Cascade Pass, on the border of North Cascades National Park and Glacier Peak Wilderness. All geographic references and elevations cross-checked against Wikipedia, PeakVisor, and USGS references. ELEVATION CORRECTION: Database lists 7,845 ft but all authoritative sources consistently show 7,821 ft."
blurb:
Hurry-Up Peak (7,821 ft) is a granite alpine summit at the northern terminus of the Ptarmigan Traverse in the North Cascades, straddling the Skagit-Chelan County border southeast of Cascade Pass. The peak is known for its Class 2-3 to Class 3-4 scrambling routes with moderate exposure on the summit ridge, offering excellent views of Trapper Lake and the surrounding Cascades. Popular as a sidetrip from the Cascade Pass trail or the multiday Ptarmigan Traverse, it features both a mellow northwestern approach and a more exposed South Ridge variant.">
</invoke>

Icy Peak is a 7,073-foot volcanic remnant of the Hannegan Caldera in North Cascades National Park, located at the head of Nooksack Cirque. The peak features small glaciers on all sides with an icefall occupying the northeast cirque, offering exceptional views of Mount Shuksan's north face and the Price Glacier. It is a popular introductory alpine and glacier mountaineering objective, with most climbers approaching via Ruth Mountain from the Hannegan Pass trailhead.

routes.overview: Popular glacier and snow route involving descent from Ruth Mountain across glacier terrain and moderate rock scrambling to the summit via the southwest gully. Primarily a snow, glacier, and Class 4 rock climb with excellent exposure and views.
routes.beta: Most common Icy Peak route: from Hannegan Pass trailhead, ascend to Ruth Mountain, traverse glacier and snow south and west to approximate 6500 ft, then ascend talus and snow through southwest gully to summit. Route involves navigating a horizontal crevasse near the top of the glacier, aiming for notch on southwest ridgeline at 6600 ft, then south through glacier's west face. Final summit push is a 100-foot Class 4 gully with mostly good rock.
routes.approach: Drive Mt. Baker Highway SR 542 east 12.5 miles past Glacier Public Service Center (46 miles from Bellingham). Turn left onto Hannegan Pass Road (FS Road 32) and continue 5.3 miles to Hannegan Trailhead parking at 3,100 ft elevation. Hike Hannegan Pass Trail 674 for 4 miles gaining 2,000 ft to Hannegan Pass (5,100 ft). Continue east on unmaintained mountaineering route ascending Ruth Glacier north slope to Ruth Mountain summit (7,115 ft), approximately 5-6 hours from trailhead. Establish camp on snow at 6,600 ft on opposite side of notch west of Ruth's summit.
routes.descent: From summit, downclimb or rappel the 100-foot Class 4 summit gully (rap slings anchor around a horn at top). Descend west-facing snow slope to south-facing ridge leading to Ruth Mountain-Icy Peak saddle area at 5,800 ft. Rather than descending loose scree gully directly, stay left and descend leftward-slanting slope to saddle. Reverse route back to Ruth Mountain summit and camp, then back to trailhead.
routes.descentText: The descent from the summit begins with a 100-foot rappel or downclimb of the Class 4 gully where anchor slings are in place around a horn. From the gully, traverse a west-facing snow slope to a south-facing ridge that leads down to the Ruth Mountain-Icy Peak saddle at approximately 5,800 feet. Rather than taking the steep, loose scree gully directly to the saddle, climbers should stay to the left and descend a leftward-slanting slope to reach the saddle area more safely. From the saddle, backtrack across the Icy Glacier, ascending back to Ruth Mountain's summit. Finally, descend the Ruth Glacier and reverse the approach trail back to Hannegan Trailhead. Total descent time from summit typically 3-5 hours.
routes.turnaround: Begin descent from summit by early afternoon to ensure safe passage of lower-angled slopes before conditions deteriorate. If on track to summit after early afternoon, strongly consider turning around to avoid navigation challenges in diminishing light. Stream crossings at Ruth Creek can swell significantly by afternoon as snowmelt increases.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with July, August, and September offering the most stable and clear weather. June can still have significant snow and increased avalanche hazard. Early season (June-early July) offers firmer snow and glacier conditions but requires excellent winter mountaineering skills and crevasse rescue capability. Late season (late August-September) features less snow, exposed rock hazards, and potential for wet conditions. July and August typically offer optimal conditions with established glacier routes, lower avalanche risk, and manageable snow.
routes.proNeeds: Firm understanding of glacier travel and crevasse rescue. All members of the party must be comfortable roping up and performing basic glacier travel. Ice axe and crampons essential. Conservative approach to crevasse hazards necessary as snow bridges can be deceiving.
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable in Nooksack Cirque area due to terrain and distance from towers. Carry a satellite communicator (InReach, Garmin, or similar) for emergency contact and location sharing. Weather can change rapidly; plan for clear skies to become obscured within hours. Check forecast before departure.
knownHazards: Crevasse danger: Typical North Cascades crevasses are 30-60 feet deep; horizontal crevasse near glacier top requires careful navigation
Avalanche hazard: West-facing slopes receive high precipitation and heavy, wet snow creates high avalanche danger, especially in early season
Exposed ridge: Class 4 knife-edge summit ridge is steep, loose, and wildly exposed with poor rock quality
Loose rock: Summit gully and approach have loose, poor quality rock; helmets mandatory
Icefall: Northeast cirque icefall is unstable; route avoids direct exposure but avalanche danger exists
Stream hazard: Ruth Creek crossings are dangerous in early season and afternoon due to rapid snowmelt swelling
Crevasse bridges: Snow bridges can fail suddenly; probe carefully and move as quickly as safely possible
objHaz: Crevasses in Ruth and Icy Glaciers (30-60 ft deep)
Loose rock on summit gully and towers
proTips: Strong climbers can link Ruth and Icy Peak as a continuous traverse for efficiency; establish camp on Ruth at 6,600 ft and summit Icy the next day
East side of Icy Peak (away from icefall) is safer; route naturally avoids northeast cirque
Early July often has excellent firm snow and established routes; by late August, rock is more exposed but crevasses better defined
Summit register documents approximately 1 ascent per year; expect solitude and self-reliance
If roping for glacier travel, use Z-pulley crevasse rescue setup and ensure all team members briefed before departure
watchOut: Turn around by early afternoon; afternoon ascents increase rockfall and avalanche risk
Check Ruth Creek conditions before crossing at trailhead; if water is above knees, consider waiting or aborting
Monitor snow bridge integrity constantly; areas with hollow sounds indicate weak bridges
Never cross the icefall in northeast cirque regardless of apparent stability
Loose rock is abundant; keep helmet on at all times in rocky sections

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park) and US Forest Service (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for overnight stays in North Cascades National Park. Available through recreation.gov or first-come-first-served at ranger stations. Reserve in advance during peak season.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or $30/year) required for parking at Hannegan Trailhead
access.fees: $5 per vehicle day use fee at Hannegan Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass accepted)
access.closures: None year-round for Hannegan Pass Trail, but flood damage may temporarily limit larger vehicle access. Call Glacier Public Service Center (360) 599-2714 for current conditions.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (FS Road 32)
road.status: Passable to passenger vehicles; occasional flood damage limits larger vehicles
road.seasonalGate: Not gated; open year-round but mountain conditions restrict actual access to June-September
road.driveNote: Road may have washouts or flooding, especially in spring. From Glacier, WA, drive 12.5 miles east on Mt. Baker Highway SR 542 past Glacier Public Service Center, turn left on FS Road 32, continue 5.3 miles to parking area. High-clearance vehicles recommended in spring/early summer.

timing.recommendedStart: 5-6 AM from trailhead to ensure sufficient daylight for camp setup at Ruth Mountain by evening

climate.forecastZone: Marine West Coast; Pacific storm systems dominate
climate.typical: Maritime climate with high precipitation, especially winter. Summer brings relatively stable conditions with Pacific high pressure systems reducing cloud cover July-August.
climate.spring: High avalanche hazard; heavy wet snow; stream crossings dangerous due to snowmelt; ice axe and crampons essential; frequent storms
climate.summer: Clearer skies; high pressure over Pacific reduces cloud cover especially July-August; temperatures well below freezing on summit; afternoon thunderstorms possible; slushy snow conditions by late August
climate.fall: Decreasing daylight; snow typically absent from lower elevations by October; glaciers still present but routes well-defined; cold nights; more stable weather than spring
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall; extreme avalanche danger; ice conditions; generally inaccessible to average parties; temperatures well below freezing

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (Prospect Dispatch coordinates Whatcom County emergency services)
emergency.rangerStation: Mt. Baker Ranger Station (Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest): 10091 Mt. Baker Highway, Glacier, WA 98244. Hours: 8 a.m.-4:30 p.m. daily. Phone: (360) 599-2714. For North Cascades National Park: Wilderness Information Center (summer) or Park Headquarters (year-round).
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth Saint Joseph Medical Center (Bellingham): approximately 40 miles from Hannegan Trailhead, 60-90 minute drive depending on location within park and rescue coordination.
emergency.notes: Bellingham Mountain Rescue Council available 24/7/365 through 911. Whatcom County Search & Rescue operates multiple specialized units (foot search, technical rope, swiftwater rescue). Cell service unreliable; satellite communicator strongly recommended. High-altitude rescue operations may require helicopter support from Seattle-area units.

routes.overview: The standard climbing route on Big Kangaroo ascends the west-facing approach gully system to reach the summit via a series of short pitches. The route features a dramatic exposed 5.6 slab on the summit block, a rugged and scenic alpine climb in Washington's North Cascades.
routes.beta: This Grade II alpine route climbs approximately 500 feet of rock with up to 4 pitches from the base of the summit block. The approach ascends parallel gullies on the west face of Kangaroo Ridge beneath Big Kangaroo. The route culminates in a steep, mostly unprotectable exposed 5.6 slab across the summit fin with a single rusty old bolt for protection.
routes.approach: From Highway 20 hairpin turn at Washington Pass (approximately 1 mile east of the pass, at milepost 163), follow a climber's trail heading south toward Early Winters Creek. Cross the creek (generally easy crossing, especially mid-to-late season) and bushwhack through slide alder and dense forest heading southeast. Ascend approximately 2,000 feet through open talus and scree before reaching a series of gullies on the west face. Follow parallel gullies upward; these gullies are often snow-filled into July. At 7,500 feet, bear left onto a ramp to another gully system and continue to the base of the rocky summit area. Locate the steep gully system leading to the ridge north of the summit block. Rope up at the base of the climbing terrain.
routes.descent: Downclimb the summit pitch or set up a rappel from the summit bolt (though the bolt is extremely old and questionable). Follow the northwest-facing gully down and east, descending steep terrain back to the meadows below. Class 3-4 downclimbing and a couple of rappels may be required. Some parties have successfully descended a northerly gully pointing toward the SR-20 hairpin. Alternatively, some parties have descended the south arete with class 3-4 down climbing.
routes.descentText: From the summit, climbers can downclimb or rappel via the single bolt on the summit block (though not recommended due to bolt condition). The typical descent involves negotiating a narrow catwalk with exposure around the summit horn to the north side, then descending gullies and talus. A northerly gully descent toward the SR-20 hairpin has proven effective. Loose rock (choss) is common throughout the descent, so careful foot placement is essential. Grade 3-4 scrambling with short rappels or rope-managed downclimbing sections required.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through September. July and August offer the best conditions with most snow melted from the approach gullies. June climbs may require crampons and ice axes for snow patches. Avoid early June when extensive snow coverage is present. September is viable but weather becomes more unpredictable and afternoon clouds frequent.
routes.comms: Cellular coverage is unreliable at Washington Pass; service from major carriers is spotty and not guaranteed. Satellite communication or InReach device recommended for safety communication.
gear: Rock protection (cams 0.3-3.0 inch range, doubles in 1-2 inch sizes)
routes.detailedRack: Single 60-meter rope. Cams ranging from 0.3 to 0.75 sizes sufficient for the route, with doubles in the 0.5-1 inch range for repeated features. A set of wired stoppers / nuts useful for small placements and gully climbing. Large cam (up to #3 or #4) useful for potential larger features. One or two quickdraws for setup, slings for rappel anchors and runner placement.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on Big Kangaroo is often difficult to place. Much of the gully climbing is unprotected or poorly protected, with loose rock common. The summit pitch is mostly unprotectable exposed 5.6 slab with only a single rusty bolt for protection. Skillful gear placement essential; consider bail options if unable to place adequate protection.
routes.rappels: 2 rappels maximum; typically negotiated via downclimbing or single-rope rappels from natural anchors or the questionable summit bolt.
objHaz: Loose rock throughout gullies and approach
Exposed summit pitch with minimal protection
Narrow catwalk with significant exposure around summit horn
Old questionable rappel bolt on summit
watchOut: Do not rely on the summit bolt for critical protection; it appears extremely old.
Summit pitch becomes unclimbable if wet—dry conditions essential.
Dense vegetation and choss on approach requires careful navigation.
Cornices may overhang ridges (early season); stay clear of corniced edges.
Thick vegetation and bushwhacking on lower approach can be disorienting; mark your exit route.
knownHazards: Loose, poor-quality granite throughout the gully system and approach requires helmet and careful rock protection.
The summit pitch is a mostly unprotectable exposed 5.6 slab with eye-popping exposure; serious fall potential.
Cornices present on Kangaroo Ridge during winter and spring; remain present into early summer.
Approach gullies collect significant snow into July, requiring crampons and ice axe.
Dense forest on lower approach with minimal visibility; route-finding can be challenging.
proTips: Establish route-finding landmarks before ascending; the approach has minimal cairns and can be disorienting.
Follow the right side of Early Winters Creek, then cut left toward the low point on the ridge between Big Kangaroo and Half Moon.
If multiple parties have established the route, follow their boot tracks or established paths to save time on approach.
Place protection liberally in gullies to account for poor quality and minimize risk on the exposed summit pitch.
Plan descent carefully before summit; scout options during the ascent to avoid confusion on descent.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time of 3:00 PM to allow adequate light for descent and exit from the dense forest on the approach. If not on summit by 2:00 PM, consider aborting the climb. Afternoon clouds and weather systems move in quickly; maintain awareness of changing conditions.
routes.bail: On the gully approach, retreat down the same gully system to the approach trails and exit via Early Winters Creek. From the base of the climbing section, continue downclimbing the gullies to the approach traverse. If above the climbing but below the summit block, descend to the south ridge and look for established descent routes downslope. If committed on the summit pitch, downclimbing may be the only option—rappel anchors can be constructed from slings if necessary.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (United States Forest Service)

road.name: Washington State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open year-round, but may be closed seasonally in winter; check current conditions
road.seasonalGate: Winter closure typical Nov-April
road.driveNote: The hairpin turn parking area is approximately 1 mile east of Washington Pass at milepost 163. Parking is limited (approximately 10 cars) in a pullout; other Highway 20 pullouts available nearby. Road is paved and well-maintained. High clearance not required.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with pacific weather systems. Wet, heavy snow common in winter; high avalanche risk. Alpine tundra environment above tree line.
climate.spring: Snow remains in gullies through late June; cornices present on ridges; unpredictable weather with possible storms
climate.summer: Most stable conditions Jun-Aug; afternoon clouds and local thunderstorms possible; low probability of snow above 8000 ft
climate.fall: Sep-Oct increasingly unstable weather; afternoon storms likely; snow returns to higher elevations by late September
climate.winter: Heavy snow, cornices, extreme avalanche risk; climbing not typically feasible. Only technical, well-equipped parties
climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) Cascade West and Cascade East zones

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (emergency). Non-emergency: Okanogan County Sheriff at 509-422-7232 or 800-572-6604
emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest Mount Baker Ranger District: 360-599-2714. North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount): 360-854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial Street, Leavenworth, WA 98826. Phone: 509-548-5815. 24-hour Emergency Department.
emergency.notes: Big Kangaroo is located in Okanogan County but the nearest hospital is in Chelan County (Leavenworth). Helicopter rescue is available but weather-dependent. Cell service unreliable; satellite communication device recommended.

waypoints.name: Base of Gully System / Rope-up Point

pitchDetail.notes: Initial gully approach climbing, mostly Class 4-5 scrambling through rock and choss. Gain ridge crest between Big Kangaroo and Half Moon. Some loose rock; place protection carefully.
Ascend steep gully to base of summit block. Mostly choss and unstable rock; route-finding critical. Navigate through nifty cannonhole at gully head, rappel station present on far side.
Narrow catwalk section with eye-popping exposure around the summit horn's right side. Traverse steep slab-face on smooth granite with minimal protection.

Final summit pitch: mostly unprotectable exposed slab moves across smooth granite, approximately 15-20 feet, with single rusty bolt for protection. Straddle ascent of summit fin. Crux of route; exposed and committing.

blurb: Big Kangaroo is an 8,326-foot granite peak on Kangaroo Ridge in Washington's North Cascades, located two miles east of Washington Pass in Okanogan County. First ascended by Fred Beckey, Helmy Beckey, and Walt Varney on June 21, 1942, the peak is composed of rapakivi granite and features dramatic alpine climbing with excellent scrambling and technical pitches. Big Kangaroo is known for its rugged terrain, loose rock, and a memorable exposed summit block.
hierarchyNote: Peak name and elevation verified. Elevation listed in database as 8,323 ft; Wikipedia and other sources cite 8,326 ft and 8,280 ft. Coordinates verified from Wikipedia (48°31′09″N 120°37′03″W). Route 'Southwest Rib / Standard' name not found explicitly in primary sources; closest match is 'West Route' (Grade II, 5.5) or the standard approach via west-facing gullies. The given route specifications (Grade II, 5.6, 4 pitches, 500 ft) are consistent with documented descent routes but the specific name 'Southwest Rib' may be a local or informal variant naming.

blurb: Mount Hinman (7,492 ft) is a remote, glaciated peak in Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, located 2 miles west of Mount Daniel on the border of King and Kittitas counties. Named in 1934 for Dr. Harry B. Hinman, a founder of The Mountaineers club, the mountain offers solitude and stunning alpine terrain via Class 2 scrambling, though the namesake Hinman Glacier has largely disappeared as of 2022 and route finding remains challenging with required off-trail travel over long approaches.

routes.face: North/Northeast slopes via La Bohn Gap
routes.overview: The standard approach to Mount Hinman via Necklace Valley climbs from La Bohn Lakes through mixed Class 2-3 talus to the north face, traversing a heavily fractured summit ridge with permanent snowfields to reach the 7,492 ft summit.
routes.beta: Most climbers approach from Necklace Valley (9 miles, 5,100 ft gain), the shortest and most common route. The ascent involves 4-9 miles of off-trail scrambling from La Bohn Lakes, gaining 2,500-3,000 ft to the summit via Class 2 talus and snow slopes. The Hinman Glacier (namesake feature) has largely disappeared as of August 2022, leaving only stranded patches of snow/ice. Standard gear includes ice axe, crampons, GPS, and map. Most climbers execute this as a 2-3 day backpacking trip.
routes.approach: From Skykomish, WA, drive east on US-2 for ~2 miles, turn right on Foss River Road (Forest Service Road #68) for 4.1 miles to the Necklace Valley trailhead parking area (permit board and bathroom present). Follow the East Fork Foss River trail 4-5 miles through dense forest (GPS useful, minimal signal) to Emerald Lake, gaining ~2,700 ft. Continue off-trail ~1 mile through the valley to the end of the cairned path at a dry lake bed. Branch left following faint paths/cairns up a steep waterfall-adjacent slope (Class 3 scrambling) ~1,000 ft to La Bohn Lakes at 6,000 ft. From La Bohn Lakes, ascend the ridgeline north toward La Bohn Gap (6,600 ft), then continue north up mixed talus, Class 2 scrambling, and loose rock through the 'bowl' area, finally traversing the heavily fractured, questionable summit ridge. Total off-trail distance from La Bohn Lakes: ~4-5 miles with 1,500 ft gain to summit.
routes.descent: Retrace the same ridgeline route south to La Bohn Lakes, then descend via the same waterfall bypass and trail back to the trailhead. Total descent time typically 3-4 hours from summit to La Bohn Lakes, then 3-4 hours to trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, carefully backtrack south along the heavily fractured ridge, descending to La Bohn Gap area. Rejoin the ascent route through talus and Class 2 terrain, descending the steep waterfall-adjacent slope (use caution; this slope is steep and loose). Follow the trail back through Emerald Lake valley to the trailhead, watching for GPS/map landmarks as forest is dense.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through late September offers the most stable conditions. Snow and avalanche risk increase dramatically before July, and early September can still have good conditions. August is considered the optimal climbing month with minimal snow on approach trails and manageable rockfall risk.
routes.bail: From La Bohn Lakes, retreat is straightforward via the ascent route. If severe weather develops on the ridge, climbers can descend from any point back to La Bohn Lakes. In emergencies, the approach valley provides multiple bailout points with water and shelter potential; call King County SAR (emergencies: 911).
routes.turnaround: Conservative parties should plan a mandatory turnaround time (e.g., 2-3 PM from the summit) to ensure safe descent before dark, given the remote location and loose scrambling terrain. Weather deterioration is common in afternoons; an early start (5-6 AM) from base camp is strongly advised.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Park at Necklace Valley trailhead, hike East Fork Foss trail to Emerald Lake base camp (4-5 hours, 2,700 ft gain). Day 2: Off-trail ascent to La Bohn Lakes (2 hours), then summit ridge traverse and peak (4-5 hours), descend to La Bohn Lakes (1.5 hours). Day 3: Descend Emerald Lake valley trail to trailhead (4 hours). Alternative (2-day push): Camp at La Bohn Lakes, summit Day 2, descend Day 2 afternoon.
proTips: Bring a GPS loaded with a preloaded route or track; off-trail navigation in dense forest is critical
Start early (5-6 AM) from base camp to minimize afternoon rockfall and ensure daylight descent
An ice axe and crampons are essential for the steep snow/ice slopes near the summit; many who skip them end up sliding or post-holing
Bring trekking poles for the approach to reduce knee strain on long off-trail miles
Mid-August conditions are typically optimal; visit in early August or late August if possible to avoid peak snow (early July) or deteriorating weather (late September)
The Hinman Glacier has largely disappeared; don't expect to see an extensive glacier feature
watchOut: Loose, fractured rock on the summit ridge can shift under boots; test all handholds
Steep snow/ice slopes near the true summit require ice axe and crampons; many accidents involve attempting bypass routes
Crevasses are unlikely given glacier retreat, but snowbridge collapses and hidden crevasses are possible on any glacier-fed snowfield
Rockfall risk increases significantly after 11 AM as the day warms; be off the exposed ridge by midday
The off-trail scramble section is exposed and loose; a slip can result in a significant fall
Very remote location: SAR response can take many hours; self-rescue knowledge is critical
knownHazards: Rockfall: Warming temperatures throughout the day increase rockfall from the summit ridge and surrounding cliffs; early starts mitigate this risk
Exposure: The scrambling sections include exposed terrain with 100-500 ft fall potential if a climber slips
Snow and ice: Late June through early August can have hard snow/ice on the upper slopes; crampons are often essential
Avalanche: Steep gullies and snowfields in the approach valley can slide in wet or heavy snow conditions; avoid after recent snow or warm rain
Off-trail navigation: Dense forest and lack of trails make getting lost or reversed out of route possible; GPS/map/compass required
Altitude exposure: This is a relatively high peak requiring good fitness and acclimatization; altitude sickness is unlikely at 7,500 ft but party fitness varies
Isolation: 18+ miles of travel from nearest road; any injury requires self-evacuation or SAR assistance

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM from base camp

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach (Necklace Valley trailhead to Emerald Lake)
pitchDetail.notes: 4-5 hours, 2,700 ft gain on maintained trail through dense forest, then off-trail through Emerald Lake valley for final mile

pitchDetail.pitch: Off-trail scramble (Emerald Lake to La Bohn Lakes)
pitchDetail.notes: 1-2 hours, steep waterfall bypass slope is Class 3; loose, broken rock and minimal switchbacks; steep and rooty

pitchDetail.pitch: Ridge traverse (La Bohn Lakes to summit)
pitchDetail.notes: 3-4 hours, 1,500 ft gain through talus, mixed Class 2-3 terrain, heavily fractured summit ridge with questionable footing and 100-500 ft exposure; loose rock throughout

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest / Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (shared boundary)
access.permit: Alpine Lakes Wilderness self-issue permit (free), required May 15 - Oct 31; deposit permit at trailhead box
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (NSA parking pass), $5 day / $30 annual, required at Necklace Valley trailhead
access.fees: $5 day or $30 annual for NSA parking pass (required)

road.name: Foss River Road (Forest Service Road #68)
road.status: Maintained seasonal access, typically open May-November depending on snow
road.driveNote: From US Highway 2 east of Skykomish, WA (near Skykomish Ranger Station), turn right on Foss River Road, continue 4.1 miles to Necklace Valley trailhead parking area on left. Road is well-maintained gravel/dirt suitable for standard vehicles in season. Parking area has ~15-20 car capacity, bathroom, and wilderness permit box.

climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service West Slopes North Cascades and Passes (WAZ567)
climate.typical: Cool, often changeable high-altitude alpine climate; afternoon thunderstorms common in summer; heavy precipitation from Pacific systems
climate.spring: Variable; snow possible through early June; cold nights below freezing
climate.summer: Clearer, warmer conditions Jul-Sep; daytime temps 40-60 F, nights near freezing; low pressure systems bring occasional rain
climate.fall: Early September can offer excellent weather; late September brings increasing instability and moisture; first freezing temps arrive late September
climate.winter: Heavy snow, frequent storms, and avalanche danger make winter climbing dangerous; not recommended

routes.comms: No cell phone coverage in the Necklace Valley approach; Verizon generally provides the strongest signal of major carriers in remote Cascade areas, though coverage is spotty even at trailhead. Satellite communicators (inReach, Garmin, Spot) are strongly recommended for emergencies in this remote wilderness area.

emergency.county: King County (southern approach), Kittitas County (northern peaks)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office (call 911 for emergencies); King County Search & Rescue Association operates 600+ volunteer rescuers
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288, (360) 677-2414, open Wed-Fri 9 AM-12 PM and 1 PM-4 PM
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, North Bend, WA (full emergency services, ~45 minutes from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Remote location means SAR response can take many hours; self-rescue knowledge and emergency supplies are essential; carry first aid, communication device, and know basic rescue techniques

gear: Ice axe (essential for steep snowfield near summit)
GPS unit with preloaded route or track
whatToBring: Rope (optional, for rappel-free descent via ascent route)
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier touring rack: ice axe, crampons, and gaiters for snow/ice. Bring 50-60 meter rope for emergencies and rappels if desired, though standard route can be descended without rappelling. Trekking poles for approach. GPS unit and map. First aid kit with extra-large blister packs and pain management. No rock climbing protection is used on standard route (Class 2-3 scramble), though scrambling gloves can help with loose rock handling.
routes.proNeeds: For a Class 2-3 scramble route, technical protection is minimal. Crampons and ice axe are the primary safety tools for snow/ice. A rope can be useful for group management on the exposed ridge, though not commonly used on standard route. Self-rescue skills and crevasse awareness are more important than specific gear.

waypoints.name: Necklace Valley Trailhead / East Fork Foss Parking

routes.corrections: The given database elevation of 7,498 ft differs slightly from the most commonly cited elevation of 7,492 ft per Wikipedia, PeakVisor, and USGS sources. The route name 'Hinman Glacier' is now somewhat misleading, as the Hinman Glacier largely disappeared by August 2022 per glaciologist research; the route is primarily a Class 2-3 scramble via north-facing slopes and La Bohn Gap rather than a true glacier climb. No specific first-ascent party information is available in sources; only the peak's first ascent year (1928) is documented.

blurb:
Tricouni Peak (8,105 ft) is an alpine summit in North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, Washington, located north of the North Klawatti Glacier and southeast of the Borealis Glacier on the edge of the Eldorado Ice Cap. Composed of Eldorado Orthogneiss metamorphic rock, the peak was first ascended in 1951 by Les Carlson, Elwyn Elerding, and Jeanne Elerding. The peak's name derives from the tricouni, a traction nail historically used on mountaineering boots for ice climbing.</blurb>
<parameter name="hierarchyNote">Elevation discrepancy: database lists 8,105 ft while Wikipedia cites 8,102 ft. Both sources verified; difference likely due to survey methods. Using database value (8,105 ft) as primary.

routes.overview: The standard alpine approach gains the summit via the southwest-facing snow chute and class 3-4 rock scramble after traversing from Lucky Pass (Primus-Tricouni col at 7,200 ft). This route combines glacier travel on Borealis Glacier with mixed snow and rock climbing on protected but exposed terrain.
routes.approach: From Thunder Creek Trailhead at Colonial Creek Campground (1,240 ft), hike the Thunder Creek Trail 6-6.5 miles to McAllister Camp (1,900 ft). Continue off-trail up McAllister Creek and through forest, gaining 2+ miles to the toe of Borealis Glacier at 6,000 ft where good base camps exist. Ascend the gentle lower slopes on Borealis Glacier's eastern edge, navigating around crevasses and icefall hazards, to gain the upper basin and traverse left toward Lucky Pass. Cross the Primus-Tricouni col (Lucky Pass, 7,200 ft). From the col, ascend a steep snow finger (200 ft) followed by solid ramps and ledges on exposed rock to reach the boulder-strewn summit. NOTE: Thunder Creek bridge washed out in 2017; use log crossing at logjam 0.25 mi upstream from original bridge location.
routes.descent: Reverse the approach, down-climbing the class 3-4 rock sections with care on loose blocks. Snow and scree slopes are prone to slides in afternoon sun. Downclimb carefully or use rappel anchors (8-30m rappels reported) from natural features. Some parties prefer to exit via an alternative lower-angled ramp on the north side of the ridge to avoid sustained downclimbing.
routes.descentText: Descend the class 4 rock and class 3 mixed terrain via the same route, or utilize a hidden ramp on the lower slopes that avoids the most exposed sections but requires careful scrambling. All downclimbing must be done deliberately to avoid dislodging rock on teammates below. Rappels of 8-30m may be set up on established anchors on the ridge; without these, some loose class 4 downclimbing is required.
routes.beta: A challenging alpine objective requiring solid class 4 rock scrambling ability and glacier travel experience. This is a summer-only route; snow and icefall hazards are significant. Early-season ascents (April-May) require ice climbing skills and knowledge of crevasse rescue. Mid-to-late summer (July-August ideal) offers better snow conditions with reduced avalanche risk. Early starts are essential to avoid afternoon sun warming and snow slide danger on south-facing slopes. Plan 2-3 days for the objective.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Thunder Creek Trailhead to Borealis Lake base camp (6-8 hours, 6-7 miles with 5,000 ft elevation gain). Day 2: Alpine start from base camp to summit and return to camp (8-12 hours depending on snow conditions and route finding). Some parties base camp lower and make an additional day of approach. Day 3 (if needed): Descend to trailhead (6-8 hours, 7 miles with 6,000 ft descent).
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate or team members experience difficulty on class 4 terrain, retreat back to Lucky Pass and the upper Borealis Glacier, then descend via the ascent route to base camp. Do not attempt to descend unfamiliar terrain. If the glaciers are heavily crevassed or snow bridges are unstable, abandon the summit attempt and return to base camp. Borealis Lake (6,000 ft) is a safe location to wait out weather.
routes.turnaround: If the team is not safely on the summit by mid-afternoon, retreat to preserve daylight for the exposed descent. If you encounter wide-open crevasses or signs of avalanche activity on the approach, turn back before committing to the exposed terrain near Lucky Pass. If weather deteriorates with low visibility on the technical scramble, descend immediately.
routes.bestSeason: Mid to late summer (July-August) offers optimal conditions with maximum snow melt and stable afternoon weather windows. June can work but may have more snow bridges and crevasse hazards. Early September is possible but weather becomes more unstable. April-May ascents are technically feasible but require ice climbing skills and crevasse rescue equipment; late-season descents can be dangerous with isothermal wet snow.
routes.rappels: Typically 1-2 rappels of 8-30m if using established anchors; downclimbing is possible without them on most sections
objHaz: Crevasses (active opening horizontal and vertical fissures as of June conditions)
Avalanche hazard on steep snow slopes from maritime-influenced wet snow
Icefall on upper Borealis Glacier cliff bands
Loose rock and slabs on scramble section
Exposure on exposed ridge sections during descent
watchOut: Start early to maximize daylight on exposed terrain; descend before afternoon sun warming
Rope team for glacier travel and establish anchor for rappels before committing to descent
Crevasse rescue skills required; do not travel on glaciers without proper rope and protection
Scout the terrain on approach to identify safe descent route before committing to summit push
Loose rock hazard on scramble—use helmets and manage party placement to prevent rockfall on teammates
Weather changes rapidly; retreat immediately if conditions deteriorate or visibility drops
knownHazards: The Borealis Glacier is split into upper and lower sections with cliffs and an icefall across much of the lower terminus; route-finding must stay on safe ice and avoid cliff bands
Snow bridges are most unstable in afternoon after solar warming; traverse glaciers in early morning when frozen
Maritime climate produces heavy, wet snow with high avalanche potential; clear skies in summer reduce risk but south and east-facing slopes can slide
Rock quality on the scramble section is generally solid but loose blocks are common; careful foot placement and helmet use essential
routes.proNeeds: Primary protection consists of ice axe and crampons for glacier travel. Rock protection (alpine rack with stoppers and cams) recommended for the exposed class 4 scramble but many parties climb unprotected. Rope (50m dynamic) essential for crevasse safety on glacier; 8-10mm ropes typical for alpine use.
routes.detailedRack: Glacier travel kit: ice axes (2), crampons (1-2 pair), mountaineering rope (50m single or twin 8-10mm), snow anchors (pickets or ice screws if rapping), belay/rappel device. Alpine rock rack: 1-2 sets of stoppers (wired nuts size 2-6), 1-2 cams (size 0.5-2), quickdraws (6-8), slings, carabiners. For pure alpine mode: rely on body belays and natural anchors; single rack of 8 stoppers and 4 cams is typical minimal rack.
gear: Mountaineering boots with good ankle support
Climbing shoes or sticky approach boots for scramble section
Pickets or ice screws for glacier anchors
Sunscreen and lip balm with SPF
Gaiters to keep snow out of boots
whatToBring: Water bladder or bottles (2-3L carry capacity)
High-calorie trail food and gels for 2-3 days
Map and compass or GPS device
Sunscreen, lip balm, insect repellent (for lower elevations)
Tent (4-season for winter, 3-season adequate for summer)
Stove and fuel, pot and utensils
proTips: Scout the descent route during the ascent; darkness makes finding the correct line extremely difficult. Note distinctive rock formations, snow patterns, and cairns (if any) on the way up.
Descend in the early morning after nighttime freeze has consolidated snow and ice; late-afternoon descent means climbing down soft, unstable slopes.
Crevasse rescue practice before the trip is essential. Know self-rescue and team-rescue techniques; one person cannot rescue a crevasse fall alone.
If you approach in early season with significant snow, snow climb the approach rather than hiking; it is faster and safer.
Plan to camp at Borealis Lake, not higher on the glacier; do not camp on glaciers due to avalanche and crevasse risk.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with cool summers and heavy winter precipitation. Summer high elevations experience variable conditions with afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August. Early mornings are most stable.
climate.spring: April-May: wet snow at lower elevations, deep snowpack at higher elevations, avalanche hazard elevated due to rapid warming cycles. Rain-on-snow events can trigger wet slab avalanches.
climate.summer: June-August: warmest, most stable weather window. July-August optimal with frequent clear skies and moderate temperatures (40-60°F at base camp, 20-35°F near summit). Afternoon thunderstorms possible but not common. High solar radiation means early starts essential.
climate.fall: September: weather becomes unstable with increased storm probability. First significant snow can fall any time after mid-September.
climate.winter: November-March: extreme avalanche hazard, deep snow, and cold temperatures (-10 to 20°F near base camp, -20 to -40°F on summit). Not typically attempted by parties without specialized winter mountaineering skills.
climate.forecastZone: North Cascades area; use regional weather forecasts from mountain-forecast.com, NWAC (Northwest Avalanche Center), or NPS North Cascades weather resources

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (National Park Service)
access.permit: Required year-round for overnight backcountry camping. Reserve online through Recreation.gov (60% of permits) or walk-in same-day (40% held for walk-ups). Permit must be picked up by 11am on start date or reservation is cancelled. Minimum 2 days advance reservation required for online.
access.closures: No general closures to Tricouni Peak, but weather and avalanche conditions may close access seasonally. Check with park ranger station before trip.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (dawn) from base camp on summit day to maximize daylight on exposed terrain and descend before afternoon conditions deteriorate.

waypoints.name: Thunder Creek Trailhead, Colonial Creek Campground

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to Colonial Creek Campground turnoff near Newhalem
road.status: Maintained year-round; seasonal gate closures possible in winter.
road.seasonalGate: May open late (spring snow/avalanche conditions); check North Cascades NP status.
road.driveNote: From Newhalem, head east on SR-20 for 9.5 miles to Colonial Creek Campground entrance. Park at the large lot above the boat ramp. Trailhead is near the amphitheater (hiker parking) or behind the trailer dump station (stock parties).

emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Parkway, Mount Vernon, WA 98273, (360) 424-4111 (open 24 hours)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Visitor Center / Ranger Station near Marblemount; contact NPS at (360) 856-5700 for backcountry permit and emergency planning
emergency.notes: Skagit County SAR has extensive mountaineering rescue experience in the North Cascades. Cell phone coverage is unreliable above 5,000 ft; consider satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, personal locator beacon) for safety.

blurb: Witches Tower is an 8,566-foot granite summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Leavenworth, Washington, part of the iconic Enchantments basin. This distinct rock pinnacle sits at the west end of the Enchantments, 0.4 miles east-southeast of Dragontail Peak, surrounded by remnants of Snow Creek Glacier. It offers several moderate rock routes and scramble options for climbers seeking alpine granite climbing.
hierarchyNote: Elevation correction: Given elevation 8546 ft conflicts with authoritative sources (Wikipedia, Peakbagger, Mountain Project, USGS references) which list 8566 ft. Route name "South Face / Standard Route" may conflate the Class 3 scramble (South Face) with the technical West Buttress route (2 pitches, 5.4) documented in climbing sources. Both routes exist on the peak.

routes.overview: The South Face presents a mixed scrambling and rock climbing approach to Witches Tower's summit, combining exposed Class 3-4 scrambling with short rock sections and a final 4-foot jump to reach the true summit block.
routes.beta: This route approaches from the south after descending Aasgard Pass into the Upper Enchantments. Navigate south and southwest toward the tower's base, ascending toward the white granitic wall. Find a ledge to traverse around the east side of the tower, then scramble large black granite blocks to reach the summit. A 4-foot slot requires a jump to access the true summit. The route is exposed and requires careful footwork, especially on descent.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive south on Icicle Creek Road for 8.5 miles to Bridge Creek Campground, then turn left on Eightmile Road (Forest Road 7601) for 4 miles to the Lake Stuart-Colchuck Lake trailhead (elevation 3,540 ft). Follow the Lake Stuart Trail for 2.5 miles to the Mountaineer Creek crossing, then turn left on the Colchuck Lake Trail. Continue 1.8 miles to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft). Work around the south end of Colchuck Lake and ascend steeply to Aasgard Pass (7,750 ft) - the steepest section gains 2,200 feet in approximately 0.75 miles of scree and rocky terrain. From Aasgard Pass, head south-southwest toward Witches Tower, ascending approximately 800 feet over roughly 1 mile to reach the tower's base.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route on the south and southwest sides, carefully downclimbing the scramble terrain. Care is needed on the exposed ledges and loose scree. The descent is Class 2-3 but requires attention due to exposure. The full descent from Witches Tower to Colchuck Lake trailhead typically takes 5-6 hours.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the same route used for ascent, carefully downclimbing the scramble sections on the south and southwest faces. The exposed ledge traverses require deliberate movement. Once clear of the technical terrain, descend steep scree and talus toward Aasgard Pass. From Aasgard Pass, the descent to Colchuck Lake follows the same steep scree and rocky path used for ascent. From Colchuck Lake, follow the trail northeast back to the Lake Stuart-Colchuck Lake trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September offers the most stable weather, with snow mostly melted by mid-July. August provides warm, dry conditions but expect mosquitoes. September-October offers clearings with fewer crowds and larch trees turning golden, though freezing mornings occur at higher elevations. June presents higher avalanche and crevasse danger on approach snowfields.
routes.bail: If above Aasgard Pass, the only descent is down the same route; no bail-outs exist. Turn around early if conditions deteriorate. If approaching the scramble section, descend before committing to the technical terrain. Below Aasgard Pass, retreat via the same trail to Colchuck Lake.
routes.turnaround: Maintain safety margins for your skill level on exposure and descending terrain. Turn around if weather deteriorates, if party members are exhausted, or if descending daylight appears insufficient. The exposed ledge traverse and descent scree present objective hazards that demand clear visibility and stable conditions. Never continue descending if visibility drops or afternoon storms develop.
routes.proNeeds: Protection for scrambling: small to medium cams for occasional belayed pitches, slings for natural protection, and a harness. Approach snowfield crossing may require ice axe and crampons (late June through July).
routes.detailedRack: Bring a rack of small to medium cams (0.5-1.5 size range) and wired protection. Include several slings for natural anchors and rock-to-crack placements. While much of the route is scrambling, two short pitches warrant a simple trad rack: cams up to about #2 Camalot size and a selection of wires. A single 50m rope is adequate for belay anchors and protection, though many parties move together on the scramble sections.
whatToBring: Sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support
Map and compass or GPS device
proTips: The exposed south-side ledge traverses are the route's crux sections - move deliberately and maintain three points of contact
