Approach snowfields early in the season (June-July) are stable when frozen from night; cross in early morning before warming softens the snow
The West Buttress (2-pitch technical route on the backside) offers an alternative technical climbing line for parties seeking more continuous rock climbing
Daylight is precious - start well before dawn to maximize summit time and safe descent; plan to be off the technical terrain well before dusk
The final 4-foot jump to the true summit is easier than it appears; approach with moderate speed rather than hesitation
watchOut: Exposure is continuous on the south-facing ledges - falls here are serious; maintain focus and use protection for less experienced climbers
Scree and talus on descent are loose and unstable; avoid kicking rocks onto other parties and downclimb carefully
Late afternoon thunderstorms develop quickly in the Enchantments during summer; descend aggressively if weather deteriorates
Snow Creek Glacier remnants may present crevasse hazards early season; stay aware of white patches indicating hidden crevasses
Darkness comes early in deep valleys; miss sunset and descent becomes extremely hazardous
knownHazards: Exposed scrambling on south face with significant drop-offs on either side of the ledges
Loose granite blocks and scree throughout, especially on descent
Snowfield crossing on approach (June-July) with crevasse hazard
Objective rockfall hazard from climbers on upper sections (peak hours in summer)
Rapidly developing mountain thunderstorms in afternoon during July-August
No escape terrain - commitment required once above Aasgard Pass

pitchDetail.notes: Lower scramble section navigates the south-facing ledges with exposure on both sides, traversing loose granite blocks and scrambling terrain
Upper pitches feature steeper rock scrambling, the 4-foot summit slot, and exposed moves to reach the true summit block on solid granite

climate.typical: Alpine climate with maritime influence brings wet snow in winter and variable summer weather. Daytime highs in August average 55-65F at summit elevation; nights drop below freezing.
climate.spring: Late May through June: Heavy snowmelt on approach, snowfield crossings common, avalanche hazard on steeper sections, weather unstable with frequent rain and new snow above 8000 ft.
climate.summer: July-August: Warmest months with daytime highs 55-65F at summit, nights near freezing. Clear mornings followed by afternoon thunderstorm development. Mosquitoes prolific near lake elevations. Approach snowfields mostly melted by mid-July.
climate.fall: September-October: Clear conditions, cool mornings (near freezing), stable weather before winter patterns establish. Occasional early season snow events possible in late October. Shorter daylight hours require early starts.
climate.winter: November-April: Deep snow coverage, extreme avalanche hazard on all approach terrain, road closures (Eightmile Road closed), route impossible for most parties.
climate.forecastZone: North Central Washington, Alpine Lakes area. Check mountain-forecast.com for Dragontail Peak area forecasts; weather conditions apply similarly to Witches Tower. Monitor National Weather Service Pendleton, OR forecast office for alpine forecasts.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, USDA Forest Service
access.permit: Day use: Free day-use permit available at trailhead. Overnight use: Requires advanced lottery permit through recreation.gov ($5/person/day, non-refundable lottery fee $6, valid May 15-Oct 31).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass ($5 day use or $30 annual) or America the Beautiful Annual Pass ($80/year)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass: $5 per day or $30 annual. Overnight permits (if camping): $5/person/day with $6 non-refundable lottery application fee.
access.closures: Eightmile Road (Forest Road 7601) closes mid-November through May for winter. Upper Enchantments typically inaccessible November-June due to snow. Day use available June-October; overnight camping lottery May 15-Oct 31.

emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Community Hospital, 1461 Gooch Street, Chelan, WA 98816 (approximately 45 minutes from trailhead)
emergency.rangerStation: Leavenworth Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest: (509) 548-6977
emergency.notes: No cell service in Enchantments; Chelan County Volunteer Search and Rescue and Chelan County Mountain Rescue respond. Evacuation via helicopter weather-dependent. Communicate itinerary to someone at home before departing.

routes.comms: No cellular service in the Enchantments basin or valleys. Cell coverage may be available at the Lake Stuart-Colchuck Lake trailhead. Satellite phones or personal locator beacons (PLBs) recommended for overnight trips.

road.name: Eightmile Road (Forest Road 7601)
road.status: Seasonal - open June through November, closed November-May for winter
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, drive south on Icicle Creek Road 8.5 miles to Bridge Creek Campground, turn left onto Eightmile Road (Forest Road 7601). Follow 4 miles to the Lake Stuart-Colchuck Lake trailhead. Graded dirt and gravel surface; high-clearance vehicle recommended. As of December 2025, Icicle Road beyond Eightmile Campground closed to local traffic only due to storm damage - verify current status before driving.
road.seasonalGate: Gate closes mid-November, opens May (dates vary by winter severity)

routes.itinerary: Day Trip: Depart trailhead at 04:00, reach Aasgard Pass by 08:00-08:30, summit Witches Tower by 10:30-11:00, begin descent by 12:00, exit technical terrain by 14:00, arrive trailhead by 19:00-19:30. Overnight Option: Many parties camp at Colchuck Lake (requires Enchantments permit) and climb as a day trip from there.
routes.corrections: Elevation: Current database elevation 8546 ft is incorrect; reliable sources consistently list 8566 ft (2611 m). Route classification: 'South Face / Standard Route' may conflate the Class 3 scramble (South Face) documented in most sources with the West Buttress technical route (2 pitches, 5.4); both exist on the peak. Pitches field: Most scramble descriptions do not reference 'pitches' - this data format may better describe the West Buttress route.

blurb: East McMillan Spire (7,992 ft) is a remote alpine peak in the Picket Range of North Cascades National Park. The peak has a tall north face of biotite gneiss and requires glacier access. First ascent occurred in 1940 by Fred Beckey, establishing the area as a significant alpine climbing destination in the remote southern Pickets.
hierarchyNote: Elevation sourced as 7,992 ft from Wikipedia, Peakbagger, and Mountain Project; database lists 7,989 ft (minor 3 ft discrepancy). First ascent attribution of 1940 appears problematic; sources indicate Fred and Helmy Beckey's 1940 ascent was of West McMillan Spire, not East McMillan Spire.

blurb: Little Tahoma Peak (11,138 ft) is a striking volcanic remnant rising from the eastern flank of Mount Rainier in Pierce County, Washington. Often considered the state's third-highest mountain despite its satellite status, it offers a challenging alpine mountaineering objective with demanding glacier travel and exposed rock scrambling. The peak's unstable andesite composition and proximity to multiple crevassed glaciers make it a serious undertaking best suited for experienced mountaineers.

routes.overview: The standard route to Little Tahoma's summit via the Fryingpan and Whitman glaciers, combining moderate glacier travel with exposed Class 4 rock scrambling on loose volcanic terrain. A technical mountaineering objective with significant crevasse and rockfall hazards.
routes.beta: Approach from Summerland alpine meadow via the Fryingpan Glacier, aiming southwest toward Whitman Crest notch at 9,000 feet. Cross the Whitman Glacier and ascend to the summit block, where a short but serious Class 4 scramble (30-40 feet) on loose andesite reaches the true summit. The route is graded as a moderate alpine climb, feasible for competent glacier climbers but not for beginners.
routes.approach: Begin at Fryingpan Creek Trailhead (3,800 ft), located west of White River Ranger Station on Sunrise Road. Follow Fryingpan Creek Trail for 4.1 miles and 2,100 feet of elevation gain to Summerland alpine meadow (5,800-6,000 ft). At Summerland, locate the stone shelter and toilet area, then proceed northwest across the meadow toward Meany Crest (7,500-7,600 ft). From Meany Crest, descend slightly to access the Fryingpan Glacier around 7,000 feet, then rope up for glacier travel. Traverse Fryingpan Glacier southwest toward Whitman Crest, cross through the notch at 9,000 feet, and descend onto Whitman Glacier for the final ascent.
routes.descent: Reverse the climbing route. Descend from the summit via the Class 4 scramble, re-enter the Whitman Glacier, retrace steps down to Whitman Crest notch, traverse Fryingpan Glacier back to Meany Crest, then descend the trail to Summerland and the trailhead. Afternoon sun softens snow, enabling plunge-stepping. Total descent time typically 3-4 hours from summit to high camp.
routes.descentText: Exit the summit block via short rappels or downclimbing the Class 4 scramble (typically protected with a handline for groups). Re-enter the Whitman Glacier and reverse the approach, carefully navigating crevasses and bergschrunds. At Whitman Crest notch, transition back to Fryingpan Glacier and continue descending to Meany Crest. From Meany Crest, descend the Meany Snowfield and trail system back through Summerland to the trailhead. Afternoon conditions typically soften snow on steeper sections.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Fryingpan Creek Trailhead and hike to Summerland (4-6 hours). Day 2: Acclimatization and rest at Summerland. Day 3: Hike to Meany Crest (1-2 hours) and establish camp. Day 4: Ascend to Little Tahoma summit and return to Meany Crest camp (6-8 hours round trip from camp). Day 5: Descend to Summerland and return to trailhead (4-5 hours total descent).
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates or altitude illness develops, retreat downward via the glacier. The route can be abandoned at any point before the final Class 4 scramble by descending to Whitman Glacier and retracing steps. Escape is straightforward via the same route to Meany Crest and Summerland. In emergencies, Meany Crest provides shelter and is accessible to rescue teams.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by mid-morning on summit day if you have not reached the bergschrund at 10,600 feet to ensure safe daylight descent. Do not attempt the Class 4 scramble if daylight hours are insufficient for safe retreat. Weather changes rapidly; retreat immediately if lightning, high winds, or sudden cloud cover develops.
routes.bestSeason: July through early August offers optimal conditions with better glacier consolidation, more stable snow, and frozen rock. Early July sees lingering snow and firm conditions. Late July and August provide moderate ice/snow angles and reasonable rock conditions. Late August through September remain feasible but see increased rockfall risk as volcanic rock dries. Early season (May-June) possible from Summerland when snow allows, but road often closed.
routes.fa: 1894 by J.B. Flett and Henry H. Garrison via the East Shoulder from Summerland (August 29, 1894)
objHaz: Crevasses on Fryingpan and Whitman Glaciers
Loose rock on final summit scramble
knownHazards: Loose volcanic talus and scree throughout upper mountain; rockfall is a significant objective hazard, particularly from the upper Fryingpan Glacier and gully system.
Fryingpan and Whitman Glaciers are crevassed, especially around 9,000-10,600 feet; crevasse rescue skills are mandatory.
The final Class 4 scramble is exposed with a 30-40 foot pitch over loose, unstable andesite before the true summit; many parties set a handline or belay for safety.
Afternoon warming can destabilize snow and trigger rockfall; early starts are critical.
Avalanche terrain exists on Meany Snowfield and approach slopes; assess conditions and travel during stable snow periods.
watchOut: Arrival at high camp puts you below freezing level; exposed ridge travel has 2,000+ feet of exposure to Emmons Glacier.
Water sources may remain frozen well into summer; carry sufficient water or melt snow.
The summit block is composed of exceptionally loose volcanic rock; handle all holds carefully and verify before committing weight.
Use steel crampons on ice and mixed ground; aluminum crampons will not cut hard ice.
Sudden afternoon thunderstorms and rapid cloud cover are common in mid-summer; delay for weather.
proTips: Plan an overnight at Meany Crest; summit day from low camp is feasible only for very fit parties and not recommended due to afternoon rockfall exposure.
Aim through the Whitman Crest notch at 9,000 feet on the climber's right (southeast) side to avoid the high point.
Traverse Fryingpan Glacier early, before afternoon sun softens the snow and increases crevasse danger.
Start summit day by 5:00-6:00 AM to reach the top by noon and descend before afternoon warming destabilizes rock.
Use a helmet on this peak; rockfall is real and frequent, especially late in the day.
Cell coverage is spotty; carry a satellite communicator for emergencies.
The views from the summit are exceptional; on clear days Mount Rainier's summit is at eye level to the south-southwest.
This is an ideal training peak for climbers preparing for higher alpine objectives.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel with crevasse rescue skills is mandatory. Parties must self-rescue from crevasses, assess avalanche and rockfall hazards, and move efficiently on mixed terrain. Class 4 scrambling skills required.
gear: Mountaineering boots (double if freezing level below 10,000 ft)
Full insulating and wind-resistant layering system
whatToBring: Water or ability to melt snow (minimum 2 liters)
Satellite messenger or personal locator beacon
Repair kit for crampons and boots
routes.detailedRack: Glacier travel: 50-60 m rope (half-ropes or single 8-9 mm rope), 2-3 pickets or snow anchors, 1 ice screw (anchor backup), 2 locking carabiners, 1 ice axe per person, crampons with anti-balling plates. Rock scramble: single locking carabiner for handline/belay on summit block (optional); most parties do not carry full rock rack as scrambling is brief and low-grade.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Mount Rainier National Park)
access.permit: Climbing permit required for all travel above 10,000 feet or on glaciers. $82 per person per calendar year.
access.fees: $82 climbing permit per person per year; $12 per person per night wilderness camping permit (May 1-September 15).
access.closures: Sunrise Road typically closed November through mid-June due to snow.

road.name: Sunrise Road (White River entrance to Fryingpan Creek parking)
road.status: Open summer only, typically May 22-September 30
road.seasonalGate: Yes
road.driveNote: Drive to White River Entrance (10 miles east of Enumclaw, WA), continue 3 miles to Fryingpan Creek Trailhead parking just west of White River Ranger Station. Parking is limited and fills early on sunny summer days; additional parking available 1 mile east at Wonderland Trail connection.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00-6:00 AM on summit day to avoid afternoon weather and rockfall

emergency.sheriffDispatch: (253) 287-4455; Mount Rainier Dispatch: 360-569-6600 (0700-2000 hrs); Puget Sound IAC: 425-783-6150 (24 hrs)
emergency.rangerStation: White River Ranger Station: 360-569-6650
emergency.nearestHospital: Tacoma General Hospital (approximately 60 miles, 90 minutes by helicopter)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is spotty; satellite communicator or personal locator beacon strongly recommended. All parties above 10,000 ft should carry emergency communication devices.

climate.typical: Cool alpine climate; summit temps 20-35F, nights below 0F. Winds 15-25 mph; severe afternoon storms possible.
climate.spring: May-June: Freezing level below 10,000 ft. Temps 15-30F. Snow stability varies; moderate avalanche danger. Road closures common early season.
climate.summer: July-August: Optimal conditions; freezing level at/above 10,000 ft. Daytime temps 25-35F; nights 0-15F. Afternoon thunderstorms common. Early starts critical.
climate.fall: September-October: Freezing level 10,000-10,500 ft. Temps dropping 15-25F. Rockfall increases as rock dries. Road closure imminent by late October.
climate.winter: November-April: Extreme conditions. Freezing level below 8,000 ft. Temps 0 to -20F. Avalanche danger high. Road closed; not recommended except expert parties.
climate.forecastZone: Mount Rainier/North Cascades (NWAC avalanche forecast zone)

routes.comms: Cell coverage is spotty throughout Mount Rainier National Park. Satellite communicators and personal locator beacons are essential for emergency communication in the backcountry. Do not rely on cell service for emergencies above Summerland.

blurb: The Rake (also known as "The Blob") is a 7,840-foot peak in the Southern Picket Range of North Cascades National Park, Washington. Located between Mount Terror and Twin Needles, it features steep gneiss and granite terrain with mixed alpine climbing. The peak was first ascended as part of the Complete Southern Pickets Traverse in 2003 and remains a challenging objective for alpine mountaineers.
hierarchyNote: Database elevation listed as 7,869 ft, but authoritative sources (Alpinist article, Steph Abegg's comprehensive Picket Range guide) confirm 7,840 ft. Peak location and hierarchy (Southern Picket Range, between Mt. Terror and Twin Needles) verified as accurate. Database route entry lists 'Grade III, 5.7' with '1951' FA, but AAC Publications documents the East Ridge as Grade IV 5.9 with July 2003 first ascent by Wayne Wallace, Colin Haley, and Mark Bunker.

routes.overview: Alpine ridge climbing on steep gneiss and granite terrain in the Southern Picket Range. The documented East Ridge route features vertical to overhanging rock climbing with established protection, followed by classic alpine ridge traversing.
routes.beta: The East Ridge route involves vertical to overhanging rock on pitons to avoid a loose gully below, followed by approximately half a mile of alpine ridge climbing. The route was established as a first ascent during the Complete Southern Pickets Traverse in July 2003. Access is gained via a snow and scree approach from the Mount Terror/Rake notch.
routes.approach: From the standard Mount Terror approach, ascend the loose, narrow gully between Mount Terror and The Rake to reach the notch between the two peaks. The gully contains hazardous loose rock and requires careful route-finding. From the notch, the East Ridge begins with rock climbing pitches featuring established belay/rappel anchors.
routes.descent: Rappel from the summit. Multiple anchor systems are in place on the ridge.
routes.descentText: Rappel the East Ridge back to the notch using established anchor systems spaced approximately 25 meters apart. Descend the gully between Mount Terror and The Rake back to the approach basin. Extreme caution required due to loose rock in the gully.
proTips: The easternmost tower (Turret) was climbed by John Roper in 1984
A summit log from 1961 was discovered in 2015, indicating earlier activity on the peak
Approach via Mount Terror/Rake notch is the standard line
Gully approach is loose and dangerous; carefully select the safest path
Most climbers encounter this peak as part of a multi-day Picket Range traverse
watchOut: Loose, dangerous gully approach between Mt. Terror and The Rake
Vertical to overhanging rock sections require solid climbing ability and protection
Alpine terrain subject to rapid weather changes
Remote location with limited escape or rescue options
knownHazards: Objective hazard from loose rock in the gully approach
Exposure on vertical to overhanging pitches
Weather deterioration common in afternoon hours
Long approach and descent - substantial time commitment
routes.turnaround: Ensure you reach the base of rock climbing by mid-morning to allow time for the technical sections and descent before afternoon weather deteriorates. Turn back if conditions are unsafe or if you lack the climbing grade to safely manage the vertical sections.
whatToBring: Food and water for 10+ hour day
gear: Pitons or rack for alpine climbing
routes.detailedRack: Alpine climbing rack suitable for Grade IV rock: selection of carabiners and quickdraws, 60m multi-pitch rope setup, pitons for protection on vertical sections and loose terrain, helmet for rockfall hazard in gully approach
routes.proNeeds: Grade IV rock climbing ability required. Protection essential on vertical to overhanging sections. Firm grounding in alpine rock climbing, loose terrain assessment, and objective hazard evaluation necessary.

pitchDetail.notes: Initial pitches from the notch feature vertical to overhanging rock on pitons, designed to avoid a loose gully below
Continued rock climbing on better rock quality, establishing alpine ridge approach
Approximately half-mile of alpine ridge climbing to summit; simulclimbing sections with established anchors

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (6:00 AM or earlier)

routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the most stable weather and minimal snow/ice. September can work but weather becomes increasingly unstable. Earlier in summer, snow in the approach gully may complicate route-finding.
routes.comms: No cell service in the Picket Range. Satellite communicator or emergency beacon recommended for this remote alpine terrain.
routes.itinerary: Most parties approach this as part of a multi-day Picket Range traverse rather than a single-day objective due to the remote approach. Standard approach from Glacier Lake trail takes 1-2 days of approach. Technical climbing and descent requires a full day. Retreat options are limited to the original gully descent.
routes.bail: Retreat via the same route down the gully to the Mount Terror/Rake notch. In emergencies, rappel the rock pitches and descend the gully to return to the approach basin. The remote location limits emergency evacuation options.

blurb: Austera Peak (8,339 ft) is the highest point of the remote Austera Towers ridge in the south unit of North Cascades National Park, located between Klawatti and Primus peaks. This rugged granite summit is approached via extensive glacier traversals across the Inspiration, McAllister, and Klawatti ice fields. First ascended on September 16, 1965 by Joe and Joan Firey, John and Irene Meulemans, and Anthony Hovey.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: Database lists 8,370 ft; authoritative sources (Wikipedia, USGS NAVD 88) list 8,339 ft. The peak is correctly placed in Skagit County, North Cascades National Park, south unit, between Klawatti Peak (south) and Primus Peak (north).

routes.overview: A classic alpine glacier approach combining extensive glacier traversals across the Inspiration, McAllister, and Klawatti Glaciers with short moderate alpine rock scrambling to summit. The final 30 feet feature exposed low 5th class climbing on the northeast face.
routes.beta: This is the standard route to Austera Peak, involving glacier travel through some of the most scenic terrain in the North Cascades. From Eldorado Camp, rope up early and traverse multiple glaciers, passing two major cols (one technical) before reaching the summit tower. Most of the route is scrambling and glacier travel with only minimal technical rock climbing.
routes.approach: From Cascade River Road parking at Mile 20 (2,100 ft elevation, 19 miles from Marblemount), cross the river via log or established ford/rope crossing to access the climber's trail on the north side. Hike approximately 7 miles gaining 6,000 feet through forest, open talus, and alpine terrain to Eldorado high camp at 7,600 ft near the east ridge base (1-2 days). From camp, rope up and traverse the upper Inspiration Glacier eastward in a massive contour toward Klawatti Col. Continue east on the upper McAllister Glacier below Klawatti's west face for 0.4 miles to reach the technical Klawatti-Austera Col at 7,900 ft. In summer/fall, receding snow embankments and moat issues make this crossing range from Class 5 downclimbing at the lowest notch to Class 4 terrain to the right (southeast).
routes.descent: Descend by reversing the approach route. From summit, downclimb 30 feet of exposed rock (Class 4) back to broken ridge terrain. Continue downclimbing to snow around 8,200 ft and descend snow to the Klawatti Glacier. At Klawatti-Austera Col, perform a 75-foot rappel using existing anchor system to access the glacier below. Navigate the moat and reverse traverse of the McAllister Glacier, then retraverse Klawatti Col and Inspiration Glacier back to camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb approximately 30 feet of exposed low 5th class terrain (potential 5.6 in descent conditions) on the northeast face back to the broken ridge. Continue downclimbing Class 3-4 scrambling to snow patches around 8,200 ft elevation. Descend the snow to the Klawatti Glacier proper. At the Klawatti-Austera Col, set up a 75-foot rappel using the existing anchor (evaluate anchor condition before use). Descend to the glacier and navigate the col moat, which may require Class 5 downclimbing in early season or Class 4 scrambling to the right (southeast) to bypass. Retraverse the McAllister Glacier westward under Klawatti's west face, then back across Klawatti Col and contour back across Inspiration Glacier to Eldorado Camp.
routes.fa: Peak first ascent: September 16, 1965 by Joe and Joan Firey, John and Irene Meulemans, and Anthony Hovey. Specific route first ascent unknown.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through late September, with August offering the most stable conditions. Early season offers better snow conditions but more crevasse exposure and technical moats. Late season features receding glaciers and more exposed rock but increased serac risk and difficult moat crossings. Winter ascents are possible via ski but require ski mountaineering experience and avalanche assessment.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC forecast zone includes this area; consult mountain-forecast.com for North Cascades specific data
climate.typical: Maritime alpine climate with high precipitation. Expect rapidly changing weather; afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.
climate.spring: Snow-dominated with high avalanche danger west side; glacier conditions variable
climate.summer: Generally stable but afternoon storms common; 50-70F typical at 8,000 ft; moats widen late season
climate.fall: Improving stability through September; temperatures dropping; early snow possible late month
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall, extreme avalanche danger on west-facing slopes; temperatures -10 to 20F at elevation

access.landManager: National Park Service
access.fees: $10 per person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee for backcountry permit
access.permit: Backcountry camping permit required year-round for North Cascades National Park, reservation via Recreation.gov or walk-in at Wilderness Information Center

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open seasonally, typically late spring through early fall
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated late fall through early spring; check current status with NPS before driving
road.driveNote: Drive Highway 20 to Marblemount, turn east on Cascade River Road and drive 19 miles to large parking lot on the right at Mile 20 (approximately 2,100 ft elevation). No parking fee. High-clearance vehicle recommended for rough portions of road.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from camp (5-7 AM) to avoid afternoon storms

routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack totaling 0.3 to 2 inches in cam sizes. Single rack to 1 inch for col work. Few long slings for rappel anchors and protection. Retrievable cordalette for building anchors. No nuts recommended; glacier travel is primary protection concern.
routes.proNeeds: Standard glacier travel protection via rope team setup and crevasse rescue capability. Final pitch is low 5th class can be soloed or simul-climbed with rope and light rack. Col crossing may require hauled/lowered gear if parties are not confident downclimbers.
proTips: Best to do as a 2-day trip with overnight at Eldorado Camp; single-day ascents from car require exceptional fitness and early alpine start
August offers the most reliable conditions; moats and snow embankments more stable than early or late season
The Klawatti-Austera Col moat crossing is the crux and most committing section scout carefully and establish retreat plan if conditions look dangerous
Existing anchors at col are there but verify condition; bring materials to build solid anchor if needed
Serac avalanches are visible from upper Klawatti icecap; watch upper slopes and avoid times of known instability
This area is remote with virtually no trail users; allow extra time for navigation and route finding
watchOut: Loose, unstable rock on summit tower and ridges second all climbing and downclimbing despite moderate grade
Receding glaciers create large moats; late-season crossings may require rappels or exposed downclimbing
Seracs visible from upper Klawatti icecap; time alpine start to minimize exposure if climbing in afternoon heat
Crevasses more visible in summer/fall but remain serious hazard; always rope up on glaciers
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer be off high terrain by 2-3 PM
Final descent is ropeable but some parties find it loose and exposed; consider protecting traverse
objHaz: Loose rock throughout summit block and ridges
Serac avalanches on upper Klawatti icecap (visible but indirect hazard)
Crevasse hazard on all glacier sections, especially Inspiration and McAllister
Moat crossing hazard at Klawatti-Austera Col in mid to late season
Exposed rock terrain on final approach to summit
knownHazards: Loose granite throughout the summit tower and lower ridge sections; rockfall hazard to parties below and to downclimbers
Large moats form at Klawatti-Austera Col in mid to late season, requiring Class 5 downclimbing or Class 4 scrambling to bypass
Serac avalanches visible from upper Klawatti icecap; indirect hazard but recommend early start to minimize afternoon heating
Crevasse hazard on Inspiration, McAllister, and Klawatti Glaciers; crevasses more visible in summer but remain dangerous year-round on glaciers
Rapid weather changes typical of maritime alpine environment; afternoon thunderstorms possible June through September
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Depart trailhead early (6-7 AM) from Cascade River Road parking. Hike and scramble 7 miles gaining 6,000 feet through mixed forest, open talus, and alpine terrain to Eldorado high camp at 7,600 ft (6-8 hours depending on fitness and snow conditions). Establish camp and rest. Day 2: Depart camp 5-6 AM with glacier kit. Rope up and traverse Inspiration Glacier in massive contour to Klawatti Col (straightforward, 1-1.5 hours). Continue east to Klawatti-Austera Col at 7,900 ft, navigating moat and steep snow sections as needed (1-2 hours depending on conditions). Traverse north onto Klawatti Glacier and climb snow/rock to summit ridge. Scramble to summit (1.5-2 hours from col). Spend 30 min to 1 hour on summit for photos and rest. Descend via reverse route, including 75-foot rappel at col (4-5 hours). Return to Eldorado Camp (4-6 hours total descent). Day 3: Return hike to trailhead (4-6 hours depending on conditions).
routes.bail: Primary bailout from high camp is to descend the approach route back to Eldorado Camp, then back to trailhead via same hiking route. If troubled high on the route, reverse back toward Klawatti Col; the Klawatti Glacier north slope offers descent options, though these are less established. If unable to descend Klawatti-Austera Col, parties can traverse south on McAllister Glacier to seek alternative descent routes via Klawatti Peak's west side (consult guidebooks), but this is committing and less recommended.
routes.turnaround: Conservative parties should turn around by 2-3 PM to ensure adequate daylight for descent, giving 6-7 hours daylight remaining for the technical sections and return to camp. If col crossing is more difficult than expected or weather is deteriorating, be prepared to turn around and descend without reaching summit.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Ranger Station (Wilderness Information Center), 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount, WA; (360) 856-5700 ext. 515 during business hours or (360) 873-4590 ext. 37 after hours
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Pkwy, Mount Vernon, WA 98273; (360) 424-4111 (Level III Emergency Department, approximately 30-45 min drive from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Remote location with no cell service in most of alpine areas; satellite communicator or InReach device strongly recommended. Helicopter evacuation possible but extremely expensive; ensure adequate insurance. Contact ranger station before trip to file trip plan.

routes.corrections: Database elevation listed as 8,370 ft; authoritative USGS NAVD 88 datum and Wikipedia sources confirm 8,339 ft elevation. Coordinates from Wikipedia DMS: 48 degrees 34 minutes 06 seconds N 121 degrees 06 minutes 05 seconds W. The peak was first ascended as a whole on September 16, 1965; the specific Southwest Ridge / McAllister Glacier route first ascent details are not clearly documented in available sources.

blurb: Chimney Rock is a distinctive 7,727-foot alpine peak in Washington's Alpine Lakes Wilderness, approximately 10 miles north-northeast of Snoqualmie Pass. The craggy central spire dominates the ridge dividing the Snoqualmie and Yakima drainages and represents the highest-quality alpine rock climb in the low-elevation Cascade range. First ascended on August 27, 1930, by Forest Farr, Art Winder, and Laurence Byington via the south and east faces after several days of exploration, the peak was long considered inaccessible before this breakthrough ascent.
hierarchyNote: Route name "West Face / South Summit (Standard)" does not match any explicitly documented route in primary sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Wikipedia, AAC publications, WTA). The 1930 first ascent was via "south and east face," which may correspond, but no primary source explicitly calls any route "West Face." Verify against local climbing guidebooks.

routes.overview: Alpine rock climb involving mixed rock and snow scrambling between the main (7,727 ft) and south (7,440 ft) peaks via a prominent couloir, likely representing the original 1930 first ascent line.
routes.beta: From the upper Chimney Glacier, ascend the prominent gully between the main summit and south peak. Follow Class 3-4 scrambling on debris and snow, traversing ledges through the couloir. Continue with Class 4-5 rock climbing on slabs and chimneys to gain the summit. The route involves both snow and rock sections requiring helmets and protection.
routes.approach: Hike Pete Lake Trail (No. 1323) for 4 miles to Pete Lake (2,980 ft). Continue around Pete Lake junction with Pacific Crest Trail, turning north at the second switchback ascending toward Waptus Lake pass. Leave trail and descend slightly to cross North Fork Lemah Creek via log crossing. Follow boot path marked with cairns and orange flagging ascending steeply toward Chimney Rock, trending leftward through brushy terrain. Bivouac or establish base camp at 4,650-5,400 feet near moraine deposits.
routes.descent: Descend via 5-6 rappels using 30-meter and 60-meter ropes with tag-team methodology between rappel stations. Final sections involve downclimbing lower-angle rock and traversing back to glacier. Total descent time approximately 5-6 hours from summit to base camp.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend initially via class 4 terrain toward the notch between main and south peaks. Establish the first rappel station and systematically work down the south face using two-rope rappels to 30-meter anchors. Navigate steep rock and chimney sections with 30-50 meter rappel distances. Upon reaching the gully, downclimb steeper sections and traverse leftward on ledges to regain the glacier. Complete final descent across Chimney Glacier back to base camp, watching for crevasses and moats.
routes.fa: 27 August 1930 by Forest Farr, Art Winder and Laurence Byington
routes.rappels: 5-6 rappels to 30m using tag-team approach with two ropes
knownHazards: Wet, heavy snow creates avalanche danger in spring and early summer
Chimney Glacier moats at rock base become problematic in late season
Loose blocks in chimney pitch require careful climbing and helmet protection
Large bergschrund requires careful navigation or may necessitate jumped crossing
Late-season crevassing on glacier increases hazard; early summer climbing recommended
watchOut: Early summer is better than late summer due to reduced crevassing and moat development
Brushy, steep trail-finding on approach requires careful navigation
Extended hiking distance (19+ miles total) requires excellent fitness and early start
Loose rock throughout route necessitates helmets and tight rope teams
Moat development at glacier base may limit safe crossing options late season
proTips: Plan for 2-day trip with overnight bivouac or high camp
Early alpine start (5:00 AM) from base camp is essential for safe descent
Class 4-5 climbing on generally sound rock is protectable with standard rack
Approach conditions improve daily as trail becomes established; start trip mid-week
The peak experiences exceptional weather stability during July and August
routes.bestSeason: Early to mid-summer (June through July) offers the optimal climbing window. Early June provides better glacier conditions but increased snow hazard; July offers stable weather and established camps. Avoid late summer when crevassing and moats become problematic and precipitation increases, making escape difficult.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM from base camp on day 2; day 1 approach takes 6.5 hours

climate.typical: Alpine Lakes Wilderness experiences marine west coast climate with high precipitation on western slopes. Summer brings stable weather with clear skies and minimal cloud cover.
climate.spring: Heavy precipitation and unstable conditions; lingering snow creates avalanche and crevasse hazard.
climate.summer: June-July offers best conditions: clear skies, stable high pressure, temperatures 40-65F at elevation. Early morning cold hardens snow for glacier travel.
climate.fall: Increasing precipitation and deteriorating weather; climbing becomes riskier. September marks transition to autumn storms.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and maritime climate create dangerous conditions. Winter ascents require winter mountaineering experience and specialized gear.
climate.forecastZone: Cascade Range, Washington central; see mountain-forecast.com and NWAC (Northwest Avalanche Center) for specific conditions

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (west) and Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (east); Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Day-use parking $5 (Northwest Forest Pass, single day) or $30 (annual)
access.permit: Free self-issued wilderness permit available at trailhead; fill out before entering wilderness
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for Pete Lake Trailhead parking

road.name: Cooper Lake Road to Pete Lake Trailhead
road.status: Open year-round to trailhead; access road may have seasonal restrictions based on snow
road.seasonalGate: Pete Lake Trailhead accessible year-round; parking lot plowed to trailhead
road.driveNote: Pete Lake Trailhead lies approximately 6 miles south-southeast of Chimney Rock at 2,800 feet elevation. Road is well-maintained but can be rough; high-clearance vehicle recommended, especially in spring. Allow 2-3 hours drive from Seattle area.

routes.detailedRack: Bring a comprehensive trad rack including stoppers (even and odd sizes), full set of single cams from BD #0 to #3 with emphasis on larger 1-2 inch sizes for parallel-sided cracks, and doubles of common sizes. Include slings (multiple lengths), quickdraws (8-10), and runners for expedient protection placement on loose terrain.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is generally achievable on sound rock. Most pitches offer adequate crack systems for nuts and cams. The crux pitch may have flaring or challenging placements; backup anchors recommended. Snow and ice sections require ice protection (screws, pitons).
whatToBring: Sturdy approach boots with good ankle support
Headlamp or light with extra batteries
Water bottles and hydration electrolytes (2-3 liters minimum)
High-calorie food: bars, trail mix, dried fruit
First aid kit including blister treatment

pitchDetail.notes: Scrambling on debris and snow through gully approaching main rock; mixed climbing on ledges; generally good rock with straightforward protection
Steep rock section with 5.6 crux moves; right-facing dihedral with crack; solid protection available
Chimney pitch with small overhang and loose blocks requiring helmet protection; flaring and grass/dirt V-shaped constrictions; delicate rightward traverse required

emergency.rangerStation: Cle Elum Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (manages Alpine Lakes eastern access)
emergency.nearestHospital: Kittitas Valley Hospital, Ellensburg, WA (approximately 45 miles south) or Cascade Medical Center, Snoqualmie, WA (approximately 30 miles west)
emergency.notes: Emergency evacuation from Alpine Lakes Wilderness typically requires helicopter or ground rescue via Pete Lake Trail. Satellite communicator (PLB or Garmin inReach) strongly recommended due to limited cell coverage.

routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is limited to nonexistent above Pete Lake. Verizon may have weak signal at lower elevations of approach. Satellite communication device (personal locator beacon, Garmin inReach, or satellite messengers) highly recommended for emergency contact.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Pete Lake Trailhead early morning. Hike 4 miles to Pete Lake (2.5 hours), continue 6+ miles off-trail to Sunrise Knob base camp (3.5 hours), establish camp and rest. Day 2: Alpine start at 5:00 AM from base camp, 8.5-hour summit push via Chimney Glacier and couloir route to 7,727 ft, 6-hour descent to base camp. Day 3: Break camp and hike out Pete Lake Trail to trailhead (4 hours). Total distance approximately 19 miles with 5,000 feet elevation gain and loss.
routes.bail: From high camp or glacier base, descend back down Pete Lake trail to trailhead. If above the main rock buttress before full commitment to climbing, retreat down gully and across glacier toward base camp. If committed to rock sections, retreat becomes fixed-anchor-dependent; carry emergency bivy gear. Poor weather on summit day warrants turnaround decision by 1:00 PM to ensure safe descent before darkness.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time of 1:00-2:00 PM on summit day. If summit is not reached by this time, descent must begin immediately to ensure arrival at base camp before full darkness. Weather deterioration, technical difficulty, or team member struggle are valid turnaround triggers regardless of proximity to summit.

blurb: Nooksack Tower (8,285 ft) is one of the most difficult mountains to climb in the Cascade Range, rising as a dramatic peak in the Mount Shuksan Massif northeast of Shuksan's main summit. Famous for loose rock, sustained ice and snow climbing, and complex alpine terrain requiring advanced mountaineering skills and ten or more rappels for descent. The peak's first ascent in 1946 by Fred Beckey and Cliff Schmidtke established it as a serious alpine objective with poor rock quality and objective hazards including rockfall.

routes.overview: The standard north face approach to Nooksack Tower, combining glacier travel, steep snow/ice climbing up a 50-degree couloir, and exposed fourth-class rock climbing along the north arête. A serious alpine objective demanding mountaineering versatility.
routes.beta: The route begins from camp at 3,000 ft on the North Fork Nooksack River and ascends via a reconnoitered route to reach the bergschrund at 6,500 ft. From there, climbers kick steps up an 800-foot, 50-degree ice couloir on the north face, then traverse westward into a rock trough before tackling the final 1,200 feet of fourth-class rock climbing along the north arête to the summit.
routes.fa: 1946 by Fred Beckey and Cliff Schmidtke
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the most stable conditions for this alpine objective. Early season (July) typically has more snow and ice; late summer (August-September) offers better rock conditions but increased rockfall hazard. Winter ascents are rare and extremely dangerous; the first winter ascent was in February 1983.
routes.approach: From Bellingham, exit Interstate 5 at Sunset Drive/Mount Baker exit. Drive Mount Baker Highway (Washington 542) east through the town of Glacier (last resupply for groceries) and continue 13 miles to the Nooksack River bridge. Immediately before the bridge, turn left on Nooksack River Road (USFS #32). Stay left at 0.5 miles, stay right at 1.4 miles from highway. Continue to the washout at 2.3 miles from the highway and park at Ruth Creek (elevation 2,156 feet). Hike the abandoned road beyond the washout for two miles to the Nooksack Cirque trailhead at 2,550 feet. River ford the Nooksack (use the log crossing at the original location noted at N48.87097 W121.61267; new logs are often swept away). Proceed up the forested mountainside east of the creek, starting up the west bank of Price Creek until finding a good log crossing. Continue to the moraine above Price Lake (3,911 feet), then hike the ridgeline between Price and East Nooksack Glaciers upward from the moraine to a rock slab bivouac spot at approximately 5,900 feet. From the bivouac, gain the glacier, traverse climber's right along the glacier to an exposed snow saddle below Nooksack Tower, then drop down and work onto the main lobe of the Price Glacier.
routes.descent: Descend by rappelling the north face. Approximately 10-12 rappels (typically to 30 meters) down the north face and couloir area bring climbers back to the glacier. Use existing anchors (pitons placed in rock, natural features with knife blades and stoppers, and deadman pickets) but be prepared to freshen many anchors with new tat and leaver nuts, as much of the existing anchor hardware is worn and tattered from previous ascents.
routes.descentText: The descent is technical and time-consuming. From the summit, retrace the ascent route along the north arête and begin rappelling. Most descents require 10-12 rappel stations, approximately 30 meters per rap, bringing you back to the base of the couloir and eventually to the glacier below. The Price Glacier route may be trivial or complex depending on conditions; current conditions often show no very technical terrain on Price Glacier, but this changes day-to-day with weather and season. Climbers should be proficient with rappel anchors and anchor evaluation, as some anchors are marginal. The descent to the glacier can be completed in several hours by experienced parties, but should not be rushed given the objective hazards and rappel complexity.
watchOut: Rock quality is poor and marginal throughout the route; protection opportunities are limited
Snow and ice conditions can change rapidly and are dangerous; assess couloir conditions carefully
Rockfall is a constant hazard; wear a helmet at all times
The descent requires 10+ rappels; ensure all party members are proficient with rappel systems and anchor evaluation
Weather can deteriorate quickly in the North Cascades; start early and have a clear turnaround time
The river ford can be dangerous in high water, especially in early summer; an afternoon crossing may be impossible if morning water was crossable
knownHazards: Loose, fractured rock throughout the route affords poor protection and breaks easily
50-degree ice couloir contains rockfall hazard from loose rock above
Bergschrund crossing is mandatory and can be difficult depending on snow bridge conditions
Price Glacier contains crevasses and routes vary year to year with glacial retreat
North face exposure and steep terrain leave little margin for error; falls are fatal
Descent anchors are often tattered and require refreshing; anchor failures could be catastrophic

timing.recommendedStart: Depart camp well before dawn (3-4 am) to reach bergschrund by 10:30 am and summit by mid-afternoon, allowing time for multiple rappels before dark

routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time based on your estimated time to reach the bergschrund and summit. If you have not reached the bergschrund by 11:00 am, seriously consider descending. If you have not summited by 2:00 pm with clear weather, plan to descend immediately. The descent is long and rappel-dependent; do not commit to the summit if daylight for multiple safe rappels is in doubt.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is unreliable or absent in this remote alpine area; carry a satellite communicator or personal locator beacon if expecting emergency rescue. The North Cascades is an area of heavy cloud cover and poor ground-to-sky visibility.
routes.detailedRack: For rock: bring a selection of stoppers and pitons from small (knife blades) to medium sizes for building anchors. Natural features and horn placements are possible but marginal. Plan for minimal protection on the rock pitches; much of the route relies on solid belays rather than running protection. For ice: ice screws (optional but bring 1-2 for marginal ice patches). For glacier: full glacier rescue kit with extra carabiners, prussiks, and webbing for crevasse rescue.
routes.proNeeds: Parties should consist of experienced alpine climbers proficient in rock climbing (Grade 4-5 leading experience required), ice climbing (snow and ice to 50+ degrees), rappel anchor construction and evaluation, and crevasse rescue. Self-rescue and rope handling competency is essential on this remote objective.
whatToBring: Water bottles or hydration reservoir (2-3 liters minimum)
High-calorie food (energy bars, nuts, dried fruit, gels)
Map and compass or GPS device
proTips: Start the climb very early (3-4 am from camp) to maximize daylight for the long rappel descent
Carefully evaluate snow bridge conditions at the bergschrund; your rope can be fixed here as a safety line for the descent
The ice couloir is the crux of the ice climbing; kick good steps and protect with solid belays even though it's simulable
The rock arête is loose and exposed; move together quickly on fourth-class terrain and place occasional pro
Mark rappel stations clearly (especially the first one) to avoid losing them during the descent in fading light
Test all rappel anchors carefully before committing your weight; tattered old tat can fail
The North Cascades weather is unpredictable; be prepared to descend and bail at any point without summiting

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.fees: $6 non-refundable permit application fee; $10 per person per night recreation fee (youth under 15 free)
access.permit: Backcountry Use Permit required for all overnight stays in North Cascades National Park. Reserve online via Recreation.gov at least 2 days in advance (60% of permits available online). Early-access lottery runs February 10-March 3 annually. Walk-up permits available first-come, first-served for remaining 40% (up to 1 day in advance).

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (Washington State Route 542) to Nooksack River Road (USFS #32)
road.status: Year-round access to highway; Nooksack River Road and parking area may be affected by washouts or seasonal closure. Check current conditions with USFS or NPS.
road.seasonalGate: Road access is weather-dependent; Road 32 washout at 2.3 miles is the standard parking point but can be affected by flooding.
road.driveNote: From Bellingham take I-5 north to Sunset Drive/Mount Baker exit. Drive WA-542 east 13 miles past Glacier to Nooksack River bridge. Potholes in parking area but generally passable. Last services (gas, food) in Glacier. The road beyond Ruth Creek washout is unmaintained and vehicle-dependent on current conditions.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park (Marblemount Ranger Station is nearest; www.nps.gov/noca)
emergency.nearestHospital: Bellingham Regional Medical Center (Bellingham, WA, approximately 45 miles and 1.5 hours from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Whatcom County Search and Rescue (24/7) maintains technical rope rescue and swiftwater rescue capabilities. Helicopter rescue is possible but weather-dependent. Rescue from this peak is extremely difficult and time-intensive due to remote location and steep terrain.

climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Forecast Zone: Western Washington Cascades, Mount Baker area
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation year-round. Summer weather is variable; expect frequent afternoon thunderstorms, rapid weather changes, and high winds. Morning clear conditions are common; afternoon deterioration is typical. Glaciers and snow persist through summer.
climate.spring: Heavy snow and wet avalanche conditions through May; not typically climbed. High water on river crossings.
climate.summer: July-August offer the most stable conditions. Temperatures range 30-45°F at altitude. Frequent afternoon clouds and thunderstorms. Glaciers are passable but crevassed. Snow/ice conditions are variable.
climate.fall: September still offers climbing days but weather deteriorates. Heavy precipitation begins. Not reliably climbable after late September.
climate.winter: The peak is rarely climbed in winter. Extreme avalanche danger, heavy snow loading, and very short daylight. First winter ascent was February 1983 by Jim Nelson's party. Exceptional mountaineering only.

waypoints.name: Ruth Creek Parking (Nooksack River Road, USFS #32)
Bergschrund (base of north face ice couloir)

routes.corrections: The given data lists 8 pitches; Mountain Project and other technical sources document 10 pitches for the Beckey-Schmidtke route. The route is commonly referred to as the 'NE Face' in climbing literature and reports (not 'East Ridge'), though the ridge terminology is not incorrect. Grade discrepancy: the given Grade IV and 5.6 match research; some older sources cite D+ 5.8 (likely older or variant grading systems). Mountain Project lists elevation as 8,532 ft; USGS, Wikipedia, PeakVisor, and most recent sources confirm 8,285 ft (2,525 m) is correct.

routes.overview: The rarely-climbed steep south face rises directly above the East Nooksack Glacier, presenting a much more technical alpine rock and ice route than the standard north face. A serious 12-pitch Grade V climb with poor rock quality and limited protection.
routes.beta: The South Face is the steeper 'back side' of the tower located directly above the East Nooksack Glacier, presenting as much vertical relief as the north aspect but steeper. The route starts on the right side of the south face with front-pointing up a short couloir before transitioning to rock climbing. Pitches angle slightly left and upward, passing a short overhanging bulge and entering a large depression. Simulclimbing leads up and left out of the depression to the left-center portion of the south face and eventually the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through early August offers the most stable conditions. Earlier in summer provides more snow and ice cover. Afternoon heat can accelerate rockfall hazard on this sun-exposed face.
routes.approach: Approach is similar to the Beckey route via Ruth Creek and Price Lake moraine, but instead of ascending toward Price Glacier, traverse to the East Nooksack Glacier. Climb to the base of the south face (elevation approximately 6,500-7,000 feet), which rises directly above the glacier. Multiple variations exist depending on snow/ice conditions; the climb starts on the right side of the face.
routes.descent: Descent routes vary; parties may reverse part of the ascent or traverse to the north face for descent via the standard Beckey-Schmidtke rappel line (10+ rappels to glacier).
objHaz: Extremely poor rock quality with limited protection
watchOut: Rock quality on this route is extremely poor and affords very limited protection opportunities; many pitches have minimal gear
Rockfall increases throughout the day as sun heats the face; start very early to minimize exposure
The overhanging bulge is committing and exposed
Complex climbing through the large depression requires solid 5.10 rock climbing ability
Limited escape options once committed to upper pitches
knownHazards: Extremely poor rock that breaks easily and offers minimal natural protection
Sun-exposed face increases rockfall hazard, especially in afternoon
Route-finding through the depression and upper face can be confusing
Limited bivy options on the face itself

timing.recommendedStart: Depart bivouac or camp by 3-4 am to get high on the face before afternoon heating and rockfall acceleration

routes.turnaround: Do not commit to this route unless committed to the summit push. Route-finding and complexity make retreat difficult. Establish a firm turnaround time (e.g., 2:00 pm) based on weather, condition assessment, and pace.
gear: Pitons or stoppers for marginal protection
routes.detailedRack: Bring a full trad rack with emphasis on small to medium stoppers and pitons. Protection is limited and marginal on much of the route due to poor rock quality. Ice screws are essential for the 55-degree ice sections. Plan for simulclimbing on lower-angled rock to move quickly.
routes.proNeeds: Expert alpine rock climbers (5.10 ground-up experience required), proficient in ice climbing to 55 degrees, experienced in route-finding on complex terrain, and comfortable with minimal protection. Parties should include rescue-competent members familiar with crevasse rescue and rope handling in alpine environments.
whatToBring: Waterproof shell (wind is significant at altitude)
Sunglasses and high-SPF sunscreen (critical on south face)
proTips: Start this climb extremely early (2-3 am) to minimize rockfall exposure on the sun-exposed face
The overhanging bulge is the likely crux; place solid protection and take time
Simulate whenever possible on lower-angled terrain to maintain speed
The depression is complex; have good route-finding and be prepared for route-finding delays
Consider descending via the north face rappel line to avoid retracing the steep south face

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry Use Permit required

climate.typical: South-facing aspect means earlier snow melt and rockfall hazard. Morning may have residual frost; afternoon heat can weaken snow/ice bonds and accelerate rockfall.

hierarchyNote: Peak verified: Nooksack Tower (8,285 ft / 2,525 m) is a real, identifiable mountain in the Mount Shuksan Massif of the North Cascades, located 0.75 miles northeast of Mount Shuksan's main summit in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington. Coordinates (48.836152°N, 121.586642°W per PeakVisor; 48.83102, -121.60283 per Mountain Project) are consistent across authoritative sources. Parent range (Cascade/Skagit Range) and regional hierarchy are geographically plausible and confirmed. No hierarchy concerns identified.

blurb: Vasiliki Tower is a distinctive granite spire standing at 7,940 feet just north of Burgundy Col in the Wine Spires of Washington's North Cascades. Named by Fred Beckey in 1952 for his friend Vasiliki Dwyer, the tower offers friendly alpine rock climbing on solid granite with excellent views across the Wine Spires. The peak is part of the three-mile Vasiliki Ridge, a prominent feature visible from Washington Pass and the North Cascades Highway.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy noted: Mountain Project lists 7,663 feet while ListsofJohn and StephAbegg sources list 7,940 feet. Used 7,940 feet as primary elevation. Geographic hierarchy verified: Wine Spires in North Cascades near Washington Pass, Okanogan County, Washington.

knownHazards: Exposure on vertical granite
Rock fall potential from upper sections
Weather hazards at elevation
proTips: Enjoy excellent sun exposure on south face
Link with Northwest Face for a full day
View of Wine Spires summit area is spectacular from top
watchOut: Afternoon thunderstorms possible
Rockfall from upper pitches
Exposure on technical pitches
routes.beta: Two-pitch technical rock climb on solid granite. The South Face receives consistent sun, making it a good choice early and late in the season. Approach via Burgundy Col from Highway 20. Descent via North Ridge scramble or rappel.
routes.overview: A friendly two-pitch alpine rock route on solid granite offering excellent views and moderate technical climbing with good protection opportunities.
routes.approach: Drive east on SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) to Washington Pass, continuing to mile marker 166 near the sign pointing to Silver Star. Park at the pull-off at approximately 4,300 feet. Follow the well-worn trail down and across Early Winters Creek, then ascend steeply up the climber's trail following Burgundy Creek. The trail climbs approximately 3,700 feet over three miles to Burgundy Col (7,800 ft), passing a bivy area known as The Ledge at the two-mile mark. Vasiliki Tower appears on the left upon entering Burgundy Col. Approach time from trailhead to Burgundy Col: 2-4 hours.
routes.descent: Down climb the North Ridge to return to Burgundy Col or rappel from the summit.
routes.descentText: From the summit, climbers typically descend via the North Ridge, a fun and easy Class 3 scramble back to Burgundy Col. The North Ridge descent takes approximately 1 hour round trip from summit to col. Alternatively, rappel stations may be used for a faster descent.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through late September. The South Face benefits from solar heating, making it suitable for early season (June-July) and late season climbs. Avoid winter months due to heavy snow and avalanche danger in approach terrain. Best conditions are typically late June through August.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is limited or absent in the Washington Pass/Burgundy Col area. Satellite messaging devices recommended for emergencies.

access.landManager: US Forest Service / Washington State Land
access.closures: SR-20 closes late November through late April due to winter snowfall. Day-use access; no overnight permit required outside North Cascades NP boundary.

road.name: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open mid-spring through fall
road.seasonalGate: Closed late November through late April
road.driveNote: Paved highway access to Washington Pass; parking pullout at mile marker 166, four miles east of Washington Pass summit.

climate.typical: High elevation alpine climate with strong winds and rapid weather changes. Summer is short; snow melt complete by mid-July.
climate.spring: May-early June: Snow at higher elevations, variable trail conditions. Cool mornings, warmer afternoons. Avalanche danger in approach.
climate.summer: Mid-June through August: Best season. Stable weather typical but afternoon thunderstorms common. Summit temps 40-60F, cooling significantly at night.
climate.fall: September: Shorter days, occasional early storms. Weather generally stable. Cooling nights.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snowfall, extreme avalanche danger. Not accessible.
climate.forecastZone: NWAC North Cascades Zone

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (5-6 AM) to avoid afternoon weather and maximize daylight.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Wilderness Information Center: (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Wenatchee Valley Hospital (approximately 90 miles) or Mid Valley Hospital in Omak, WA (approximately 50 miles). Flight evacuation possible for serious incidents.
emergency.notes: Remote location with significant rescue response time. Self-rescue important. Helicopter available June-September. Pre-trip communication recommended.

routes.detailedRack: Bring selection of nuts, cams (0.5 to 3 size range), and slings. Granite offers good protection opportunities. Two 30m ropes essential for rappel descent.
routes.proNeeds: The South Face offers numerous cracks for traditional protection. Good natural anchors at belay stations. Moderate climbing with good protection throughout.

pitchDetail.notes: Face climbing on solid granite with good protection features. Pleasant climbing to sandy bench. Establish belay at ledge.
Slab climbing from bench to summit. Good cracks on left side with finger-sized features. Final moves lead to rocky summit.

routes.corrections: Elevation discrepancy: Mountain Project lists 7,663 feet while other sources list 7,940 feet; 7,940 feet used as primary. First ascent (Al Murdoch and Ed Cooper) retained from database but could not be independently verified online; Ed Cooper confirmed as 1950s FA pioneer in Pacific Northwest.

blurb: Eagle Peak (5,958 feet) stands at the western end of the Tatoosh Range within Mount Rainier National Park. Originally called Sim-layshe by local Native Americans, the peak features Class 3 alpine scrambling on volcanic rock with dramatic Mount Rainier views and was first summited by a USGS crew in 1910.

routes.bestSeason: Mid-late summer to early fall when snow is absent and conditions are most stable. June through early October provides the best weather, with July and August offering the warmest and most snow-free conditions.
routes.overview: A scenic alpine scramble from Eagle Peak Saddle to the summit featuring Class 3 rock with an exposed section and optional Class 4 bypass. The route combines well-maintained trail approach with high-alpine scrambling on volcanic terrain with prominent Mount Rainier views.
routes.beta: From the Eagle Peak Saddle (5,700 ft), a path leads east to a rock step featuring loose holds (Class 4). The primary bypass route goes left 100-200 feet into a short Class 3 gully, climbs to a ledge, then rejoins the main route at the top of the rock step. More scrambling then leads to the summit. The entire ridge section is exposed on both sides with uneven terrain.
routes.approach: Drive via WA-706 through Elbe and Ashford to Mount Rainier National Park's Nisqually Entrance (National Park Pass required, $30 per vehicle). Continue to Longmire and park at the Longmire Lodge and Museum lot. Walk past staff housing and cross the historic Nisqually Suspension Bridge to reach the trailhead 300 feet beyond. Follow the Eagle Peak Trail switchbacks through old-growth forest for 3.6 miles, ascending gradually through forest (first 2 miles), then reaching a stream and small alpine meadow. The final 0.5 mile climbs steeply and rockily to the saddle between Eagle Peak and Chutla Peak (5,700 ft).
routes.descent: Reverse the route from the summit down to the saddle via Class 3 scrambling (slightly harder on descent—coach teammates through the crux rock step). Descend the trail back to Longmire via the same 3.6-mile switchback trail through forest and meadow.
routes.descentText: The descent from the summit to the saddle involves reversing the Class 3 scramble, with the crux rock step being slightly more difficult going down. Best technique is to face outward, pivot right to face into the rock wall, then step down with a blind foothold. Use 'veggie-belay' (roots and branches) as needed for security. Once back at the saddle, descend the well-graded trail switchbacks through alpine meadow (watch for exposure and uneven terrain in the saddle area—use extreme caution as falls could be deadly), then through old-growth forest. Stream crossing and gradual descent continues to the Nisqually Suspension Bridge and trailhead parking.
routes.turnaround: Plan to turn around by mid-afternoon (typical start time 6-7 AM) to ensure descent before dark. Winter travel requires avalanche skills and navigation expertise due to steep slopes and avalanche danger; not recommended for most parties.
knownHazards: Exposed terrain at saddle with fall consequences
Loose rock holds on Class 4 bypass section
Avalanche danger on steep meadow in winter (requires avalanche skills)
Rockfall hazard when multiple parties on route
Wet conditions increase scrambling difficulty
objHaz: Exposure: uneven exposed terrain on both sides of ridge
Loose rock: Class 4 step has loose holds
Winter avalanche hazard: steep upper meadow terrain
Weather exposure: subalpine peak subject to rapid weather changes
watchOut: The saddle is extremely exposed—a slip could be fatal. Exercise extreme caution when crossing.
The crux rock step has loose holds; test all holds carefully and ensure secure placement.
False summit on Eagle Peak sits directly above to the left; follow climber's path to true summit.
Do not climb in large parties due to rockfall hazard.
In winter, this route requires avalanche awareness and navigation skills; snow coverage masks terrain.
proTips: Start early (6-7 AM) to maximize daylight for descent.
The Class 3 gully bypass is easier than attempting the Class 4 rock step; most parties take this route.
Coordinate with partners on the crux descent—coach each other through the exposed step.
The false summit confusion is common; look for the climber's path veering right toward true Eagle Peak summit.
Summer heat and high sun exposure require sunscreen and bug spray; bring plenty of water.
Wildflowers peak in late July and August in the subalpine meadows.
whatToBring: Sturdy hiking boots with good grip
Map, compass, or GPS (trail is clear but saddle navigation can be confusing in fog)
Insulating layer and windbreaker (weather changes rapidly at elevation)
Headlamp or flashlight for contingency late descent
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection needed; route requires scrambling ability and comfort with exposure. Natural features (roots, branches) suffice for aid if needed.

pitchDetail.notes: Mix of loose and stable rock, well-defined climber's path. Stay right to avoid false summit on left.
Primary recommended route. Climb gully to ledge, traverse to rejoin main route at top of Class 4 step.

pitchDetail.pitch: Direct Class 4 step (not recommended for most)
pitchDetail.notes: Loose holds, exposed, harder on descent. Most parties avoid this and use the Class 3 bypass.
Steeper scramble with exposure on both sides. Take time to find secure foot and hand placements.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00 AM - 7:00 AM for optimal daylight on descent

road.name: WA-706 to Nisqually Entrance, then park roads to Longmire
road.status: Paved, open year-round though Nisqually Entrance may have seasonal closures during extreme weather or winter nights
road.seasonalGate: Nisqually Entrance typically open during daylight, may close at night during winter
road.driveNote: The Nisqually Suspension Bridge to trailhead has a 3-ton (6,000 lb) weight limit; no RVs, trailers, or heavy vehicles can cross. From Nisqually Entrance to Longmire is ~16 miles, approximately 30-40 minutes of driving.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Mount Rainier National Park)
access.fees: $30 per vehicle for National Park Pass (good for 7 days)
access.passRequired: National Park Pass (Nisqually Entrance)
access.closures: Trail may be hazardous or closed in winter due to snow and avalanche risk. Contact Longmire Wilderness Information Center for current conditions.

climate.typical: Subalpine transitional climate. Mornings cool and clear; afternoon thunderstorm potential, especially July-August. Nights drop to freezing even in summer.
climate.spring: May-early June: snow lingers in higher meadows, trail often wet from snowmelt, avalanche hazard high.
climate.summer: July-September: warmest, most stable season. Midday temps 50-65°F at saddle. High sun exposure. Afternoon thunderstorms possible. Mornings often foggy.
climate.fall: September-October: crisp mornings, clear days, fewer people. Snow can return by late October. Shortening daylight.
climate.winter: November-April: heavy snow, extreme avalanche danger on upper meadow, requires winter mountaineering skills and navigation in whiteout conditions.
climate.forecastZone: Mount Rainier Zone (NOAA/National Weather Service); check mountain-forecast.com for Eagle Peak specifically

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (primary emergency number); Pierce County Mountain Detachment non-emergency: (253) 287-4455
emergency.rangerStation: Longmire Wilderness Information Center, Mount Rainier National Park: (360) 569-6650 (summer hours vary)
emergency.nearestHospital: Good Samaritan Hospital Emergency Department, Puyallup, WA (401 15th Ave SE, Puyallup, WA 98372). Phone: (253) 697-4000. Approximately 45-60 minutes drive from Longmire.
emergency.notes: Cell service is spotty in the park; Verizon and T-Mobile may have limited coverage on high ridges but unreliable. Carry a satellite communicator for critical emergencies. Park ranger patrols are seasonal (heaviest in summer).

routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable throughout Mount Rainier National Park; coverage on Eagle Peak saddle and ridge is spotty depending on carrier (Verizon and T-Mobile have some ridge-top coverage but not guaranteed). Recommend carrying a satellite communicator (Garmin InReach or similar) for emergency communication. Most climbers have no cell signal on the route.
routes.corrections: Wikipedia lists Eagle Peak elevation as 5,908 ft and USGS data shows 5,899 ft on some records; Mountain Project and most climbing sources cite 5,958 ft as the official elevation. The 5,700 ft elevation mentioned in NPS materials refers to the Eagle Peak Saddle, not the summit. The trail description ending at the saddle is the day-hike standard route; the actual summit requires additional Class 3 scrambling beyond the official trail terminus.

routes.overview: The first ascent of Shark Rock's west face, a solid volcanic rock route on a seldom-visited formation in the Dark Divide.
routes.beta: EZ Way is a 5.6 trad climb on Shark Rock's west face. The rock is very solid but protection is extremely difficult due to minute cracks. A classic but serious alpine rock route requiring competent trad climbing skills and protection placement in difficult crack systems.
routes.approach: Drive ~2 hours via FR90, then FR25 south toward Elk Pass. Continue east on rough gravel road FR2551 for approximately 1 5/8 miles to an unmarked spur. Hike the Boundary Trail #1 for 2 1/4 miles (1,050 ft gain) to Badger Lake, then continue 2 3/4 miles with 700 ft additional elevation gain to the last switchback before the ridge crest. From the switchback, traverse nearly level ~200 feet through open timber (dropping ~25 feet) until breaking out on the open west slope. Total approach: ~5 miles, ~1,750 feet elevation gain.
routes.descent: Rappel or downclimb the route; descend back via the west slope to Boundary Trail junction.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September offers the best climbing weather. The area experiences significant precipitation and cloud cover most of the year; summer provides the best dry conditions for this high-altitude rock route.
objHaz: minute cracks make protection extremely difficult
limited protection options on west face
volcanic rock quality requires careful placement
watchOut: Protection is severely limited due to minute cracks throughout the route
West face has few crack systems; east face (vertical/overhanging) offers better protection options
Exposed position requires confidence in runout climbing
Weather can deteriorate rapidly in the Dark Divide
knownHazards: Difficult protection placement; expect run-outs on the west face
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer months
Exposure to weather on ridge approach
Route-finding in scrambling terrain below rock
gear: Trad rack (primarily small and micro cams and nuts)
proTips: Arrive early to maximize daylight on approach
Scout the final approach on a clear day before committing to the climb
East face has better protection if you want an easier trad experience
This is a small area with few routes; it remains pristine and rarely climbed

access.landManager: Gifford Pinchot National Forest (USFS)

road.name: Forest Road 25 to FR2551 (rough gravel access)
road.status: Passable in dry conditions; may be rough in summer
road.driveNote: Drive FR90, then FR25 toward Elk Pass area. Access rough gravel road FR2551 approximately 1 5/8 miles to unmarked spur for Shark Rock approach. Road can be washboarded; high-clearance vehicle recommended.

timing.recommendedStart: Dawn (5-6am) for full daylight on approach and climb

climate.forecastZone: Pacific Northwest Cascades (Skamania County, WA)
climate.typical: Alpine volcanic rock area subject to frequent cloud cover and precipitation. Summer afternoon thunderstorms are common. Wind can be significant on the ridge.
climate.spring: Wet and unpredictable; snow may linger at higher elevations through June
climate.summer: Best season with warmest, driest conditions June-September; still expect afternoon storms and cool nights above 5,000 ft
climate.fall: Conditions deteriorate rapidly September-October; increasing precipitation and cloud cover
climate.winter: Snow, ice, and avalanche hazard; route not typically climbed November-May

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skamania County Sheriff's Office (non-emergency dispatch available)
emergency.rangerStation: Gifford Pinchot National Forest Supervisor's Office: (360) 891-5000
emergency.nearestHospital: Skyline Medical Clinic (Emergency Department) in Stevenson, WA; ~50 miles south via FR25/FR90 and Highway 12
emergency.notes: Limited cell service in the Dark Divide; satellite communicator or personal locator beacon recommended. Emergency response times can exceed 1-2 hours from remote location.

blurb: Shark Rock is a distinctive volcanic formation in the Dark Divide, a remote and seldom-visited wilderness area between Mount St. Helens and Mount Adams in southwestern Washington's Gifford Pinchot National Forest. The peak features solid volcanic rock with limited crack systems and sharp protection challenges, making it a destination for experienced trad climbers seeking pristine, undeveloped terrain. The first ascent route, EZ Way (5.6), was completed by Don Nelsen and Kelly Wagner on August 18, 2017.

The Pleiades are a series of four jagged spires on Larrabee's east ridge in the North Cascades, located immediately southeast of American Border Peak within the Mount Baker Wilderness. Known for notoriously poor rock quality and exposure, these peaks offer exposed Class 3 scrambling with panoramic views of the North Cascades.

routes.overview: A challenging Class 3+ scramble accessing the Pleiades spires from High Pass, featuring loose rock terrain and exposed traverses along a goat ledge system to link the four peaks.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the most favorable weather with temperatures 45-70°F (7-21°C) and minimal precipitation. Snow lingers long in the area, so early season visits may encounter snow and avalanche hazard; August and early September are most reliable for dry conditions.
routes.approach: From Twin Lakes Trailhead: Drive Twin Lakes Road (Forest Service road 3065) from Glacier Public Service Center, 12.5 miles east. High-clearance vehicle essential; beyond Yellow Aster Butte trailhead (4.5 miles), road is rough. Alternatively park at Yellow Aster Butte trailhead and road-walk 2.5 miles to Twin Lakes with 1,600 ft elevation gain. From Twin Lakes, hike Winchester Mountain Trail 0.2 miles, fork right onto High Pass Trail, descend 300 ft beneath cliffs and wildflower slopes, reach High Pass (5,965 ft) in ~3.6 miles roundtrip, 765 ft gain from lakes.
