routes.descent: Return via the same route. Descend the gully systems and scree/snow slopes reversing the ascent; a black-lichen-covered rocky gully provides descent from peak 2. Maintain awareness of rockfall hazard and loose terrain throughout descent.
routes.descentText: From the Pleiades ridge, backtrack to the goat ledge and traverse it. Descend to the notch between peaks 1-2. Reverse the gully descent (use the descent gully to the east when returning). Scree and snow slopes lead back to High Pass basin. Descend the scree/snow slope to High Pass, then retrace the High Pass Trail back to Twin Lakes.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Reach Twin Lakes campground or car camp. Day 2: Hike to High Pass (3.6 miles roundtrip from Twin Lakes, 765 ft gain, ~2-3 hours); ascend scree/snow NNE to col south of Larrabee; traverse basin and climb gully to notch between peaks 1-2; scramble peaks 2, 3, and 4 via goat ledge traverse (total ~7.5 miles, 3,400 ft gain from Twin Lakes for all four peaks); return via descent gully and High Pass; ~8-10 hours round trip.
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate or loose rock becomes too hazardous, retreat is straightforward: return down the gully system to the basin, retrace to High Pass, and descend the High Pass Trail. The route offers no major exposure traps; loose rock is the primary hazard rather than poor escape routes.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if conditions prevent safe downclimbing or if group is not comfortable with Class 3 scrambling on poor-quality rock. Weather deterioration (thunderstorms common in afternoon) or fatigue should prompt retreat. Daylight and weather are the critical constraints; plan to summit peaks by early afternoon and return to High Pass by sunset.
routes.beta: All four Pleiades peaks are Class 3 scrambles, though loose rock makes them more challenging mentally. The 3rd peak from the west is the tallest. Most climbers scramble peaks 2, 3, and 4 via the goat ledge traverse; peak 1 (to the west) is steeper with exposure and slabby sections. Loose rock and poor-quality stone are the defining characteristics; this is not solid climbing terrain. A helmet is strongly recommended. Keep party size small (6 or fewer) to minimize rockfall hazard. Rock quality is among the worst in the Cascades. Most routes involve scrambling on exposed terrain with height but relatively stable handholds/footholds for Class 3 climbing.
objHaz: Exposure on peak 1 (knife-edge ridge, slabby sections)
knownHazards: Extremely poor-quality rock; helmet mandatory
Loose terrain creates objective hazard from upper parties
Peak 1 has exposure and slabby sections requiring careful footing
Avalanche chutes present on flanks during snow season
Snow and ice linger long; late June/early July still hazardous
watchOut: Rocks are described as 'some of the worst' in the Cascades; each climber must be extremely attentive to party placement to avoid rockfall
All four peaks are jagged spires with sharp, unstable edges; care required on narrow sections
Weather can deteriorate rapidly in afternoon (frequent thunderstorms in summer)
Cell coverage unreliable or absent in mountains; rely on satellite device if critical communication needed
proTips: Climb in early morning before afternoon thunderstorms develop
Use the descent gully on peak 2 rather than class 4 move; no rappel needed
Cairns mark the route but may be far apart; way-trail may disappear; stay alert to navigation on ridge
Rock quality improves slightly the higher you go; focus on solid placements
Approach on snow (early season) is faster than scree but requires care for avalanche and crevasse hazard
gear: Rock climbing shoes or sticky approach shoes
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock climbing gear required; this is a scramble. Bring a helmet and grippy footwear (rock shoes or sticky approach shoes). Trekking poles helpful for scree. Microspikes or crampons if snow present. No rope, protection, or belay gear needed.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection needed; this is a scramble, not a climb. Loose rock means falling rock from above is the hazard, not inadequate anchors. A helmet mitigates rockfall risk to yourself.
whatToBring: 3L+ water (no water on ridge; cache at col if planning to link all four peaks)
First aid kit with blister treatment (mandatory for loose terrain)

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach: Twin Lakes to High Pass
pitchDetail.notes: 3.6 miles roundtrip from Twin Lakes, 765 ft gain. Well-marked trail with switchbacks and cliff traverses. Wildflowers in summer. Water available at lakes and streams en route.

pitchDetail.pitch: High Pass to South Col (Larrabee Col)
pitchDetail.notes: Off-trail scree/snow slope climb NNE, gaining ~400-500 ft to col. In early season, may be steep snow requiring care for avalanche (crevasse unlikely but snow is wet and heavy). By August, usually dry scree.
Traverse basin ~0.5 mile, descend slightly, then climb gully system to notch. Gully is loose and narrow in places, Class 2-3 scrambling. ~30-45 minutes from col.

From notch, traverse ~50 ft east and ascend Class 2-3 scramble to summit. Excellent panoramic views. Return to notch and descend via descent gully (black lichen-covered rocky gully to the east, no rappel needed).

From notch, ascend to goat ledge and traverse the ledge to link peaks 3 and 4. Peak 1 (west) requires Class 3+ scramble with exposure, slabby sections, and knife-edge ridge. Peak 3 accessible via ledge; peak 4 at far end of ledge traverse. Extreme care on ledge due to loose rock.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00-6:30 AM to avoid afternoon thunderstorms and ensure return to High Pass by dusk

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Mount Baker Wilderness)
access.permit: Self-registered free wilderness permit at trailhead kiosk; optional but recommended for safety
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Recreation Pass for parking at Twin Lakes Trailhead or Yellow Aster Butte Trailhead
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass: $5/day or $30/year
access.closures: Twin Lakes Road may be gated or impassable after heavy snow/winter (typically November-May). Check with Glacier Public Service Center before attempting winter approach.

road.name: Twin Lakes Road (Forest Service Road 3065)
road.status: Rough, high-clearance vehicle only beyond Yellow Aster Butte trailhead (4.5 miles from public road). Unpaved, narrow with few pullouts, not maintained for passenger vehicles.
road.seasonalGate: Typically passable late May-early November depending on snow. Winter closure common.
road.driveNote: From I-5 exit 255, take State Route 542 east 33 miles to Glacier Public Service Center. Continue 12.5 miles on Twin Lakes Road. Road becomes increasingly rough; high-clearance vehicle essential. Beyond mile 4.5 (Yellow Aster Butte trailhead), only high-clearance vehicles recommended; standard passenger cars should park at Yellow Aster and add 2.5 miles road-walk (1,600 ft gain). Four-wheel drive not necessary but helpful.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; Pacific Ocean air masses bring frequent precipitation on west side of Cascades. Temperatures 45-70°F (7-21°C) in summer, dropping to freezing above 6,000 ft even in July. Wind common on exposed ridges.
climate.spring: Snow lingers long due to maritime influence; heavy, wet snow. Avalanche hazard high. Not recommended before mid-July.
climate.summer: Most favorable; July-August have lowest precipitation (1.7 in./43 mm per month) and warmest temps (55-75°F day, 40°F+ night at 7,000 ft). Afternoon thunderstorms common, especially August. Clear mornings typical.
climate.fall: September remains favorable; weather deteriorates late September. Snow may return late September. Days shortening; earlier darkness.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall (maritime influence creates wet, dense snow). Avalanche hazard extremely high. Climbing not recommended without mountaineering experience and avalanche gear. Route closed by winter conditions.
climate.forecastZone: NWAC (Northwest Avalanche Center) zone: North Cascades/Mount Baker area. Check NWAC website for current avalanche forecast, especially May-July.

emergency.rangerStation: Glacier Public Service Center: 10091 Mt. Baker Highway, Glacier, WA 98244. Phone: (360) 599-2714
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, 2901 Squalicum Parkway, Bellingham, WA 98225 (~45 minutes drive from trailhead). Emergency Room: 24 hours. Phone: (360) 454-5464
emergency.notes: Cell coverage unreliable or absent on peaks and in the basin. Verizon has better coverage than other carriers in the Cascades but do not rely on it above 6,000 ft. Satellite communicator (inReach, PLB) strongly recommended for emergency communication. Helicopter evacuation extremely difficult due to terrain and weather; self-rescue is assumed. Register trip with someone at trailhead if possible.

routes.comms: No cell phone coverage on the peaks or in the Pleiades basin is reliable. Verizon service is best in the Cascades but do not count on signal above 6,000 ft. At Twin Lakes and High Pass trailhead area, limited coverage may exist on ridges or certain locations. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, personal locator beacon) strongly recommended if you need reliable emergency communication. The area is remote and helicopter rescue is weather and terrain-dependent.

waypoints.name: Twin Lakes Trailhead (end of Twin Lakes Road)
High Pass Trail fork (right turn from Winchester Trail)
Basin south of Larrabee (scree/snow slopes approach to col)
South Col (Larrabee Col) - notch in south arm of Larrabee

blurb: Liberty Cap (14,112 ft) is a sub-peak on Mount Rainier's summit area in the North Cascades of Washington, composed of volcanic rock and glacial terrain. The peak marks the northwestern edge of Mount Rainier's summit plateau and serves as the objective of Liberty Ridge, considered one of North America's premier alpine climbs. First ascended via Liberty Ridge in 1935 by Ome Daiber, Arnie Campbell, and Jim Burrow, Liberty Cap has since become a destination for experienced alpine mountaineers seeking a serious test of snow, ice, and mountaineering technique.

routes.fa: Ome Daiber, Arnie Campbell, Jim Burrow, 1935
routes.overview: Liberty Ridge is a classic Grade IV-V alpine climb dividing the Willis and Liberty Walls on Mount Rainier's north face, featuring 5,700 feet of exposed steep snow and ice up to 60 degrees with significant objective hazards from rockfall and avalanche.
routes.beta: Liberty Ridge splits the North Face of Mount Rainier between Willis Wall and Liberty Wall, two of the most dangerous alpine walls in the lower 48 states. The route is considered the premier unguided climb on Mount Rainier and is included in Roper and Steck's Fifty Classic Climbs in North America. Approximately 100 climbers attempt Liberty Ridge annually; however, the route accounts for roughly 25% of Mount Rainier climbing fatalities due to persistent objective hazards and the committing nature of the climb. Fast, experienced parties can complete the climb in two days, while average parties typically require three to four days. Early-season timing (May to mid-June) is critical to minimize rockfall and avalanche hazard as temperatures warm and snow/ice conditions deteriorate.
routes.approach: From White River Campground (4,400 ft), hike the Glacier Basin Trail for 3.5 miles to Glacier Basin (5,935 ft). Continue ascending to St. Elmo Pass (7,400-7,600 ft), then descend to cross the Winthrop Glacier at 7,300 ft, traversing around the lower Curtis Ridge to establish camp on the flat area of Carbon Glacier around 7,400 ft. From camp, descend to and traverse directly up the Carbon Glacier, navigating crevasses and following the glacier as directly as conditions allow to the base of Liberty Ridge. Ascend a snow ramp from the east side of the ridge base to gain the ridge crest, then follow the west side of the ridge up snow slopes and rock outcrops to Thumb Rock (10,500-10,775 ft). The technical climbing begins in earnest above Thumb Rock.
routes.descent: Most parties descend the technically easier Emmons-Winthrop Glacier route rather than retracing Liberty Ridge. From Liberty Cap, descend to the upper Liberty Cap Glacier, navigate the bergschrund, and descend toward Camp Schurman (9,400 ft) via the Emmons Glacier. From Camp Schurman, follow the marked Emmons Glacier route down the Interglacier (combination of walking and glissading), descending approximately 2,000-3,000 vertical feet through steep sections via glissade to Glacier Basin, then descend the trail to White River Campground.
routes.descentText: Follow the Emmons-Winthrop descent standard route: from Liberty Cap, descend carefully through crevassed terrain on the Liberty Cap Glacier to the upper Emmons Glacier. Rope teams must manage crevasse exposure throughout. Descend the Emmons Glacier to Camp Schurman (9,400 ft) maintained by the NPS, then continue down the Interglacier using a combination of walking on moderate slopes and glissading on steeper sections (many parties rope up for this section). Upon reaching Glacier Basin Moraine, transition to the hiking trail and descend back to White River Campground via the marked Glacier Basin Trail (3.5 miles, ~2-3 hours of hiking). A car shuttle from White River to Moraine Park or back to the trailhead may be preferred by parties wanting to avoid the final trail descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Start at White River Campground early (pre-dawn). Hike to Glacier Basin, ascend to St. Elmo Pass, descend to Winthrop Glacier, and traverse to Carbon Glacier for camp around 7,400 ft (8-10 hours of hiking/skinning). Day 2: Ascend Carbon Glacier to base of Liberty Ridge. Begin technical climbing on the ridge, navigating steep snow and ice to Thumb Rock (10,500 ft) for bivy or camp (8-10 hours climbing). Day 3: Climb from Thumb Rock through the ice gully and upper ridge sections to Liberty Cap summit (14,112 ft), then descend to Camp Schurman for overnight (6-8 hours climbing, 3-4 hours descent). Day 4: Descend Emmons-Winthrop route via the Interglacier back to White River Campground (6-8 hours hiking/glissading/skiing). Optional: Fast parties in perfect conditions may summit on Day 2 and descend to Camp Schurman, completing the route in 3 days.
routes.bail: Early bailout: From lower Carbon Glacier, climbers can descend back down Winthrop Glacier to St. Elmo Pass and retreat via the approach route to White River Campground (full approach reversal takes 4-6 hours). Mid-route bailout: From Thumb Rock or lower Liberty Ridge, descend back to Carbon Glacier and retrace the approach. Upper route bailout: From upper Liberty Ridge or Liberty Cap Glacier, if conditions deteriorate or summit push becomes unsafe, descend carefully toward Camp Schurman via the Emmons Glacier route. The critical decision point is typically Thumb Rock; climbers must assess conditions and energy reserves carefully before committing to the final ice pitches to Liberty Cap Glacier.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time based on daylight hours and descent requirements. Early-season (May-June) offers longer daylight (~15-16 hours); climbers summiting after 2 PM risk inadequate light for the Emmons descent if conditions are poor or fatigue is high. A noon summit turnaround is conservative but appropriate for parties unfamiliar with the descent. Severe weather (wind >30 mph, heavy snow, poor visibility) should trigger immediate descent even if the summit is within reach.
routes.bestSeason: Liberty Ridge is best climbed from early May through mid-June, with a possible window into early July depending on snow and weather conditions. May and early June offer the most stable snow and ice conditions, minimizing rockfall and avalanche hazard as the snowpack remains consolidated. Late season (late June onward) brings increasing icefall from Willis Wall and Liberty Wall as warmer temperatures destabilize the couloirs and glacier surfaces; apple-sized chunks of ice dropped from 1,000 feet above are lethal. Freezing levels must remain below 14,000 feet to maintain firm glacier conditions. The route is rarely attempted outside May-July due to high risk and unreliable access; early season storms (May) can be severe but the snowpack is generally more stable than late season.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is spotty on Mount Rainier; Verizon and T-Mobile have limited coverage in the Paradise area and may extend signals into lower elevations, though coverage cannot be relied upon above 10,000 feet. Climbers should carry a satellite communicator (InReach, Spot, or similar) for emergency communication and trip reporting, especially for parties planning to camp on the glaciers. The isolated terrain on the remote north side of the mountain offers virtually no cellular signal; satellite communication is essential for safety.
knownHazards: Avalanche risk is persistent on the approach and lower ridge, with daily avalanche cycles on warm afternoons from Willis Wall and Liberty Wall. Early starts and completion of dangerous terrain by early afternoon are essential.

Rockfall from the volcanic rock of Thumb Rock and Gun Sight feature can be triggered by vibration, warming, or snow/ice melt. Rotten, crumbly volcanic rock creates additional hazard during rock traverses.

Icefall from Willis Wall (left of the ridge) and Liberty Wall (right of the ridge) occurs frequently during warm periods; the 200-300 foot wall of ice at the top of Willis Wall is particularly unstable.
Crevasses on Winthrop and Emmons glaciers are large and present significant hazard; climbers must be proficient with glacier rescue and crevasse self-rescue techniques.
Bergschrund crossings on the upper Liberty Cap Glacier require careful navigation; the 25-30 foot vertical ice section may require rappelling or technical climbing depending on snow conditions.
Steep slopes (40-70 degrees) throughout the route present slip and fall hazard; crampons must be worn continuously and climbers must maintain focus and balance on exposed terrain.

Weather is highly unpredictable in the Cascade Range; storms can develop rapidly with high winds, heavy snow, and poor visibility. High confidence weather forecasts with stable pressure systems are mandatory before launching.
watchOut: Do not climb Liberty Ridge in afternoon or late-season conditions when avalanche and rockfall risk is extreme.
Maintain constant awareness of conditions on Willis Wall and Liberty Wall; if ice/rock fall is observed, retreat immediately.
Establish a firm turnaround time based on daylight and energy; do not push the summit if you lack adequate time for the descent.
Rope teams must manage crevasse exposure throughout the approach glaciers and on the upper mountain; wear harnesses at all times on glaciated terrain.
Be prepared for rapid weather changes; carry adequate layers, including a down jacket and Gore-Tex shell, even if the forecast looks clear.
Helmet is mandatory; rockfall and ice fall injuries are common on this route.
routes.detailedRack: 4-6 ice screws (mix of sizes, 60-90mm), 2-3 snow pickets or deadman anchors, 30-meter glacier climbing rope (8-9mm) for two-person teams or doubled for larger parties, ice ax (modern shaft recommended for efficiency), crampons (12-point stainless steel or hybrid, well-maintained), two ice tools (technical ice climbing tools recommended for the upper pitches), helmet (helmet recommended for rock/ice fall protection), harness, slings, carabiners (5-6 total), prusik cord or ascenders for crevasse rescue, shovel for anchor placement and shelter.
routes.proNeeds: Climbers must be proficient in glacier travel, crevasse rescue, and steep snow and ice climbing. A minimum of 12 alpine climbs involving snow and ice climbing is required before attempting Liberty Ridge. Rescue skills must include: setting up belay anchors in snow/ice, crevasse rescue via Munter hitch and/or ascenders, self-rescue techniques, and efficient rope management in alpine terrain. Ice climbing proficiency on 70-degree ice is essential for the upper pitches.
gear: Chalk and duct tape for maintenance
whatToBring: Base layer top and bottom (merino wool or synthetic)
Water bottles or hydration bladder (insulated to prevent freezing)
High-calorie food (energy bars, nuts, dehydrated meals, butter, cheese)
Stove, fuel, and lighter for hot beverages
Maps and compass or GPS device
Sleeping bag rated to 0F or lower
Foam pad or inflatable sleeping pad
Bivy sack (optional but useful for emergency shelter)
proTips: Start before dawn to complete the approach and lower ridge technical climbing before afternoon warming and avalanche cycles.
Simul-climbing (continuous climbing on rope without belays) on the lower ridge sections significantly speeds progress and is acceptable for experienced, confident parties.
Mark the route with wands (bamboo sticks or flagging) on descent, especially on the approach glaciers in whiteout conditions.
Camp on the flat areas of Carbon Glacier or near Thumb Rock; avoid exposed ridge sections for overnight bivies.
Descend the Emmons-Winthrop route rather than rappelling Liberty Ridge; it is faster, safer, and avoids the hazards of the climb for a fresh party.
Pay close attention to conditions at Thumb Rock; this is the critical decision point for the final push to Liberty Cap.
Rope up for the entire Emmons-Winthrop descent on glaciated terrain; crevasses are large and hidden.
Carry high-calorie food and maintain hydration; the altitude and exertion combine to create significant energy demands.
Brief each party member on rescue procedures before starting; communication and efficient belaying are critical for safety.

pitchDetail.pitch: 1-2: Approach Glaciers (White River to Carbon Glacier Base)
pitchDetail.notes: Hiking and glacier travel on Glacier Basin, Winthrop Glacier, and Carbon Glacier. Navigation through crevassed terrain with potential for concealed crevasses. Route-finding is the main challenge; many parties navigate between known landmarks and marked camps. Simul-travel with loose rope is typical for experienced teams.

pitchDetail.pitch: 3-4: Lower Liberty Ridge to Thumb Rock
pitchDetail.notes: Ascend snow slopes and rock outcrops from Carbon Glacier base to Thumb Rock (10,500-10,775 ft). Terrain alternates between 30-40 degree snow/ice and scrambling on volcanic rock. Some sections require step-cutting or cramponing on blue ice. Bergschrund crossing may require short ice climbing or creative passage.

From Thumb Rock, ascend either a short vertical ice pitch (70-80 degrees) in the center of the rock band, or traverse left around the cliff. The ice pitch is a 10-20 foot technical section requiring confident ice climbing and secure placement of ice screws for protection. This represents the crux of Liberty Ridge and is where parties often spend significant time.

pitchDetail.pitch: 6: Upper Ridge to Liberty Cap Glacier
pitchDetail.notes: Climb increasingly steeper slopes (50-60 degrees) and navigate the upper ridge crest or its western flank to reach the Liberty Cap Glacier around 13,500 ft. Several short icy sections and potential bergschrund crossings require rope belays or simul-climbing. Final approach to Liberty Cap summit involves bergschrund navigation (25-30 feet of vertical ice) and final snow/ice approach to the summit area.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (4-5 AM) to maximize daylight for the approach and lower technical climbing.

road.name: Mount Rainier National Park Access Road (State Route 410 to White River Entrance)
road.status: Open seasonally; typically open July-September for passenger vehicles, may be closed by snow in winter and spring
road.seasonalGate: Sunrise to Sunset; special permits required for early access
road.driveNote: From Enumclaw, WA, drive SR-410 east toward Mount Rainier National Park. Enter at White River Entrance. The White River Road winds upward with several hairpin turns; high-clearance vehicles recommended but passenger cars can access White River Campground in summer. Road conditions are variable in spring and early summer; check NPS website for current status before travel. Parking is available at White River Campground.

climate.forecastZone: NOAA Mount Rainier Forecast Zone (West Slope)
climate.typical: Highly variable; Mount Rainier creates its own weather system. Conditions can change rapidly from clear to severe storms. Average summit temperature year-round is below freezing; wind speeds increase significantly with altitude and exposure on the north face.
climate.spring: May typically sees high snow levels (26 inches average), cold temperatures (freezing levels below 14,000 ft), and stable high-pressure systems. June sees rapid snowmelt and increasing instability as freezing levels rise. Average May highs: 45-50F at lower elevations; 0-10F at 14,000 ft.
climate.summer: June temperatures: highs ~55F at lower elevations; snowfall drops to ~6 inches. July conditions: minimal snow (0.3 inches average), firmer ice, but increased rockfall/icefall from warming. Average July highs: 60-65F at lower elevations; 15-25F at summit.
climate.fall: Late July and August see the most stable weather but also the highest avalanche and rockfall hazard from melting. September storms can be severe and unpredictable. Early September sees the onset of fall conditions; many climbers transition off the mountain by early August.
climate.winter: November-April: Mount Rainier is buried in snow; Liberty Ridge is not typically climbed due to extreme cold, unstable snow, and avalanche hazard. Deep winter powder and unconsolidated snow create dangerous climbing conditions.

access.landManager: National Park Service, Mount Rainier National Park
access.fees: Annual climbing permit fee: $82 per person (valid 1 calendar year). Recreation fee: $12 per person per night for wilderness camping. Vehicle entry fee: $30 per vehicle (included with NP annual pass). Walk-up permit fee: $6 non-refundable. Park entrance fee: $30 vehicle or $15 individual (included in climbing permit).
access.permit: Climbing permit required for all glacier and above-10,000-foot climbing. Permits available via Recreation.gov or in-person at park ranger station. Reservations recommended in advance (permits often sell out in May-June). Walk-up permits available at limited times (~1/3 of permits reserved for walk-ups).
access.passRequired: Mount Rainier National Park entrance pass or America the Beautiful annual pass. Climbers should have a valid climbing permit.
access.closures: Liberty Ridge access may be restricted during high avalanche hazard periods or due to extreme weather. Check NPS website and contact Mount Rainier Dispatch (360-569-6600) for current conditions before driving to the trailhead. Early-season closures due to snow on access roads are common in April and early May.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 or Mount Rainier Dispatch (360-569-6600, 0700-2000 hrs) or Puget Sound Interagency Coordinating Center (425-783-6150, 24 hrs). Alert and Warning Center coordinates Mount Rainier rescue operations.
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier National Park Ranger Station (multiple locations within park). White River Ranger Station is the closest to Liberty Ridge trailhead.
emergency.nearestHospital: Harborview Medical Center (Seattle, King County, ~100 miles): Level I trauma center
emergency.notes: Liberty Ridge is one of the most dangerous climbing objectives in North America; search and rescue operations are common during the climbing season. The park maintains a dedicated rescue helicopter for mountain evacuations. Cell phone coverage is minimal; sat communicators (InReach, Spot) are essential for emergency communication. All climbers must file a float plan with someone outside the expedition; notify them of summit success or descent completion.

blurb: Colchuck Balanced Rock is an 8,240-foot granite summit in the Stuart Range within the Enchantments (Alpine Lakes Wilderness), named for its distinctive pyramidal top-block that nests atop the peak. First ascended in 1958 by Gene Prater and Thomas Quin, the crag has become firmly established as one of Washington's premier alpine free-climbing zones with nearly 1000 feet of sustained high-quality granite routes featuring excellent crack systems and knobs.

routes.bestSeason: Early summer (June through August) offers the most stable rock conditions and favorable weather windows for the full alpine experience.
routes.beta: The West Face is one of the best sustained alpine rock routes in the North Cascades, offering nearly 1000 feet of climbing with memorable pitches including an enduro corner, wild roof traverse, protected crux, and the region's hardest 5.8 chimney. The route culminates in an exposed V1 boulder problem topping out the balanced rock. Traditional protection is required throughout with natural gear placements and in-situ slings for rappel anchors.
routes.overview: Nine-pitch alpine trad route on granite offering sustained 5.10 climbing with a 5.11+/12- crux and V1 boulder problem finish.
routes.approach: From the Stuart Lake Trailhead (via Icicle Road, 8.5 miles east of Leavenworth on Highway 2, then 3.7 miles on NF-7600), hike 4 miles and 1,100 feet of elevation gain on the Stuart Lake Trail to the Colchuck Lake junction. Continue left 4 miles and 1,100 feet more on the Colchuck Lake Trail to Colchuck Lake (5,570 ft elevation). From the east side of Colchuck Lake outlet, ascend the Aasgard Pass/gully system approximately 400 feet above the lake until reaching dense bushes. Traverse left through the bushes to an obvious gully leading directly to the base of the West Face (approximately 30-45 minutes from lake).
routes.descent: Descend via 60-foot rappel to the east from in-situ slings, then scramble down scree or talus on the right (clockwise) back toward the base. Alternative: rappel with two 60-meter ropes to avoid loose descent sections. From base, return via the same gully system and boot-pack trail back to Colchuck Lake.
routes.descentText: The primary descent uses a well-established 60-foot single rappel anchored from in-situ slings located on the east face of the summit block. Descend to a talus field, then traverse and scramble down scree slopes on the south and east aspects. The descent can be accomplished with two 60-meter ropes to minimize exposure on loose ground. Once back at the base of the obvious gully, return via the same approach route, taking care on talus and scree sections which can be slippery when wet or icy.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Depart trailhead in early morning (before 6am for optimal summit timing). Hike 8 miles gaining 2,200 feet to Colchuck Lake (2-3 hours). Continue up gully system 30-45 minutes to route base. Begin climbing by mid-to-late morning. Climb West Face (6-8 hours depending on speed). Rappel descent (1-2 hours). Complete hike out in daylight (1-2 hours). Return to vehicle by early evening (full day commitment of 12-14+ hours).
routes.bail: Early bailouts available at each belay station via rappel with 60-meter ropes. From pitches 1-4, can continue downclimbing scramble sections or rappel carefully using natural anchors. Upper pitches (5-8) are more committed but can be rappelled with in-situ anchors. The summit boulder problem (pitch 9) can be bypassed entirely by rappelling from the final stance prior to the technical boulder sequence.
routes.turnaround: Start descent by mid-afternoon to ensure adequate daylight for rappelling and trail descent. Strong alpine weather can develop rapidly; if not on summit by 2-3pm during shorter daylight months, consider rappelling from current position. Turnaround time depends on group speed and daylight hours but generally should target summit by 1-2pm for safe descent.

timing.recommendedStart: Before 6:00 AM

knownHazards: Always loose rock present throughout the route and approach gully
Rockfall hazard from upper pitches—maintain awareness and limit party to two climbers maximum
Exposed fifth-class boulder problem at summit requires high concentration and falling risk
Route is very solar-exposed creating intense heat gain on summit day (south/west-facing)
Alpine weather can change rapidly; be prepared for wind and lightning risk on exposed ridge
Snow verglas on pitches when wet; additional hazard in early season
watchOut: The flaring chimney on pitch 7 is described as 'the region's hardest 5.8'—do not underestimate this technical section despite the grade
Summit boulder problem (V1) requires solid climbing ability and confidence; many competent 5.11 climbers struggle with the technical topout moves
Dense bushes and brush on approach can obscure the gully entry—cairns mark the way; don't venture too high along the ridge
Descent is not bolted—ensure everyone in party is confident with rappel setup and anchor evaluation on in-situ slings
Mosquitoes are significant hazard during descent in peak summer
Bring adequate sun protection; intense reflected sun from white granite creates severe sunburn risk
The descent descent involves significant loose rock and scree—carefully manage rappel angles to avoid dislodging rock on partners below
objHaz: Weather exposure (lightning, wind, rapid conditions change)
proTips: Pitch 6 crux combines the two hardest features: powerful hand crack work into roof traverse into boulder problem—pace yourself and don't rush
Pitch 7 chimney is best climbed with aggressive wide-crack technique to manage the flaring geometry
The route has excellent natural belay ledges between each pitch—take time to set good anchors from slung horns or placed protection
Two-climber maximum recommended due to loose rock—three-person parties risk excessive rockfall hazard
Start extremely early to beat the heat and sun exposure on this south/west-facing crag
Bring extra water; alpine rock in full sun creates dehydrating environment
Download offline maps of the approach; trail markers can be obscured by heavy snow in early season
gear: Trad rack with doubles on all sizes up to 3 inches
Nuts in full range (especially smaller sizes for thin cracks)
Two 60-meter ropes (can be descended on one in some sections, but two recommended for safety)
Slings and webbing for anchor reinforcement
routes.detailedRack: Build a full traditional climbing rack with emphasis on smaller sizes (0.4-1.5 inch range) for the numerous thin crack pitches. Carry doubles of sizes 0.75 through 2.5 inches; triples of the 1-2 inch range are reasonable. Include a single 4-inch cam for potential wider sections. Bring a minimum of six slings and pieces of webbing for anchor reinforcement, especially for rappel anchor cordelette setup. Two 60-meter ropes are standard for this route; a single rope can work with careful management but two ropes provide much greater flexibility for descent and safety margin.
routes.proNeeds: Full traditional climbing protection with emphasis on crack-climbing gear. Climbers must be proficient at placing nuts and cams in granite cracks and understanding placement quality. Ability to construct solid natural anchors from slings, horns, and placed protection is essential—this route has no bolts. Rappel skills including multi-pitch rappel management, anchor assessment, and self-rescue are critical given the remote alpine location and no-bolt descent protocol.
whatToBring: Extra water bottles or hydration system (alpine sun and thin air increase dehydration)
Insulating layers even in summer (alpine temperatures drop significantly)
Headlamp or light (long day may extend past dark in winter months)
Emergency communication device (satellite communicator recommended—no cell service)
First aid kit with blister treatment
Extra rappel slings (in-situ anchors may be worn)
Knife or multi-tool for gear management and rappel setup verification

pitchDetail.notes: A few steep moves off broken ramp and past a tree; sets tone for route complexity and rock quality
Thin-hands splitter crack, then move left around a pillar; excellent protection available
Stem thin, slabby corner leading to a treed ledge; stemming technique required on delicate rock
Corner climbing transitions to sustained finger and thin-hand crack in a dihedral system; well-protected with good gear placements
Traverse left under the huge roof; exposed traverse with multiple protection placements; committing section
Hand crack in corner, climb past a roof, and crux boulder problem; this pitch combines the route's most technical challenges with roof work and powerful bouldering
Flares and jugs to flaring chimney; described as 'the region's hardest 5.8'—chimney flaring requires aggressive wide-crack technique and powerful leg work
Move right along ledge and then scramble up to summit area; easier terrain but exposed to weather and wind
Boulder problem to the very top of the balanced rock; exposed technical topout move over the summit block; many competent 5.11 climbers struggle with this short but committing crux

routes.rappels: 1x 60-foot rappel from slings; optional 2x 60-meter rope descent for additional safety margin

road.name: Icicle Road to NF-7600 Forest Service Road
road.status: Passable summer/early fall; may have snow closures in winter
road.seasonalGate: Check with Wenatchee River Ranger District for seasonal road status; forest service roads may close due to snow November-April
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, drive west on US Highway 2 for 2 miles, then turn left onto Icicle Road. Follow Icicle Road for 8.5 miles to the turnoff, then turn left onto NF-7600 and follow this forest service road for 3.7 miles to reach the Stuart Lake Trailhead parking area. Parking area fills early on weekends during peak season.

climate.typical: Alpine environment with rapid weather changes. Granite absorbs intense solar heat creating extreme temperature swings between sun and shade. Wind accelerates cooling at altitude. Early morning temperatures near freezing even in July/August.
climate.spring: Spring access may be limited by snow on Aasgard Pass and approach gully. Trail usually open by late May. Snow patches persist on north aspects into June. Verglas (thin ice glazing) creates hazard on scramble sections when wet.
climate.summer: June-August: best climbing season with reliable access and most stable weather windows. July/August offer longest days and warmest temperatures. Intense sun exposure creates dehydrating conditions and sunburn risk. Afternoon thunderstorms possible but not frequent.
climate.fall: September: still good climbing window with cooler temperatures reducing heat stress. Shorter days require early starts. Occasional early season snow higher up. Weather becomes more unpredictable with faster storm development.
climate.winter: November-April: winter ascents require specialized skills. Snow and ice conditions demand proper winter mountaineering training. 2012 winter ascent noted sugar snow and verglas creating hazards. Approach involves 4+ miles of skiing. Only for experienced winter alpine climbers.
climate.forecastZone: Wenatchee River Ranger District and Chelan County; check mountain-forecast.com and Northwest Avalanche Center for alpine conditions.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Forest Service)
access.fees: $5 day parking pass or $30 annual Northwest Forest Pass required at trailhead
access.permit: Wilderness Permit required May 15-October 31 (free; available at trailhead). The Enchantments area specifically requires an Overnight Wilderness Permit from June 15-October 15 with limited availability (separate application process required for overnight stays).
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (parking at Stuart Lake Trailhead)
access.closures: Aasgard Pass may have snow/ice hazards in early season. Forest service roads subject to seasonal closure November-April. Check current trail conditions via WTA or ranger district before attempting route.

emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, (509) 548-2550
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial St, Leavenworth, WA 98826, (509) 548-5815 (24-hour Emergency Department)
emergency.notes: No cell service in the alpine lakes area. Satellite communication device (InReach, Spot, or iPhone 14+ satellite messaging) is strongly recommended for emergency communication. Total evacuation time from summit is 3+ hours via helicopter, so prevention and self-rescue skills are critical. The nearest ranger station is approximately 45 miles away.

routes.comms: No cellular service available once entering Icicle Creek Canyon near Leavenworth. Alpine Lakes Wilderness is completely outside cell coverage zones. Satellite communicators (Garmin InReach, Spot devices, or iPhone 14/15/16 satellite message feature) are essential for any emergency communication. Download offline maps and carry paper backup.
routes.corrections: Elevation listed as 8,239 ft in original data; Wikipedia and multiple sources confirm 8,240 ft (1-foot difference). Mountain Project lists West Face with 9 pitches; some secondary sources mention 8+ pitches. The route details extracted show consistent 9-pitch descent from StephAbegg detailed topo. Given data shows 6 pitches; actual documented routes show 9 pitches. Given data shows 'Grade III, 5.11' but authoritative sources consistently document Grade IV 5.11+/12- commitment level.

routes.bestSeason: Best climbed when snowline is around 5,000 feet; summer is the season of choice, though spring has the advantage of snow-covered brush masking obstacles. April to October is the most popular time of year.
routes.overview: Straightforward scramble on the main peak via the northwest ridge, involving hiking through forested terrain with Class 3-4 scrambling to reach the summit.
routes.beta: A popular mountaineering route requiring moderate off-trail navigation skills. A fairly well-used climber's trail ascends approximately one mile to the ridge, then continues east to the notch between north and south peaks. Pink tape and cairns occasionally mark sections but are unreliable. An ice axe is recommended in spring and summer. Navigation can be challenging in bad weather; GPX/map recommended.
routes.approach: From Highway 2 near Baring, turn north onto Barclay Creek Road (FSR 6024) near Der Baring Store, crossing railroad tracks. Continue approximately 4.3 miles to the Barclay Lake trailhead. From the trailhead, follow an abandoned logging road about one-third mile to a creek crossing marked by a cairn. Ascend a steep boot path approximately 2,000 feet over a little more than a mile to reach the west ridge. Continue east along the ridge for about one-half mile (only 300 feet elevation gain), then traverse around cliff bands through wooded gully. Return to ridge crest and descend into talus basin. From the notch between north and south peaks, scramble north on heather and talus terrain to reach the main summit.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent route. Be careful to avoid cliffing out on descent as it's easy to get off-route, particularly on the right side with significant exposure. Descending the south-facing gully should be avoided as it is steep, wet, and difficult.
routes.descentText: Retrace the climbing route back down the scramble section to the talus basin. Exit the basin back through the ridge crest, then descend the wooded gully around cliff bands. Continue back down the west ridge, then descend the steep boot path through the forest, following cairns to the abandoned road and trailhead. Total descent time approximately 3-3.5 hours.
knownHazards: Rockfall from steep cliff bands throughout the route
Avalanche risk in the basin area, particularly from South Baring face
Snow and ice on upper sections in spring and early summer
Cornice danger in winter and spring at ridge crest
Significant exposure on the right side of upper route near notch
Loose rock and brush throughout approach
watchOut: Route finding required; easy to wander off in brush
Beware of steep, wet gully on south side—avoid on descent
Pink tape and cairns are unreliable for navigation
Check avalanche forecast for South Baring before climbing
Early season ascents require ice axe and crampons
objHaz: Poor rock quality in cliff bands
proTips: Bring a GPX or detailed map; navigation by cairns and ribbons alone is risky
Early morning start recommended for this long approach
Best done in mid to late summer when brush is most navigable
The trail is much easier to follow on ascent than descent
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate or party is moving slowly, return down the same route before dark. There are no bailout routes on the ridge; commitment is required.
routes.turnaround: This is a full-day objective. Establish a turnaround time in early afternoon to ensure descent before dark, especially on descents.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00 AM or earlier

road.name: Barclay Creek Road (FSR 6024)
road.status: Passable; may have potholes
road.driveNote: Rough in spots; 4WD vehicles recommended in poor weather. Approximately 4.3 miles from Highway 2 near Baring, Washington. Parking lot fits about 10-20 vehicles; arrive early on weekends.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

climate.forecastZone: Cascades Range - Central Cascade forecast zone
climate.typical: Highly variable; frequent rain, cool temperatures, high wind exposure at ridge
climate.spring: Snow/avalanche risk; afternoon sun softens snow
climate.summer: Warmest, most stable season; afternoon thunderstorms possible
climate.fall: Deteriorating weather; rain and early snow
climate.winter: Extreme snow and avalanche danger; route closed

emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Medical Center - Evergreen (approximately 30 miles)
emergency.notes: Limited cellular coverage in wilderness area. Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest manages the area. SAR response will take significant time due to remote location.

routes.fa: Don Gordon and Ed Cooper (with Fred Beckey as team member), July 9-13, 1960
routes.bestSeason: June through September when high-alpine conditions are most stable and approach gully is most accessible. Early June may have snow; late September weather becomes unstable.
routes.overview: A serious Grade IV-V alpine rock/mixed route on the imposing north face featuring four vertical steps (300, 400, 220, and 250 feet) separated by spacious ledges, requiring constant Grade V-VI climbing with minimal bolted protection. This is a committing, technical alpine climb with significant objective hazard and requires substantial experience.
routes.beta: The route involves four major vertical steps. The lower approach consists of approximately 2,300 feet of brush fighting up steep cliffs through dense vegetation (slide alder, vine maple, devil's club). Establish Dolomite Camp at the base of the first step. The climbing involves vertical, delicate mixed terrain with roof sections requiring stirrup aid climbing on black rhyolite with poor natural protection. The crux is on the second step (400 feet) which features 120-degree roof sections and extremely delicate vertical climbing. Fixed ropes are placed in 10-rope caches on lower sections to assist load hauling. Multiple attempts from 1951-1959 failed before the successful first ascent in 1960.
routes.approach: From the Barclay Lake trailhead, cut through the bush east toward the base of the north face. Ascend approximately 2,300 feet through dense brush, slide alder, vine maple, and devil's club via steep gullies and forested terrain to reach the base of the face. The first ascent party improved trails with yellow trail markers and installed fixed ropes on lower sections. Expect significant brush fighting with heavy loads.
routes.descent: After summiting, make long rappels down the upper rock sections using established anchor points on each of the four steps, descending ledge to ledge. The descent involves multi-rope rappels and careful anchor setup. After exiting the rock, scramble/rappel down the steep brush-filled gully and wooded terrain back to base camp. Total descent significantly longer and more complex than ascent.
routes.descentText: From the summit, move back onto the upper ice axe terrain and begin rappelling. Make long rappels from anchors on the fourth step (250 feet) to ledge. Continue rappelling from anchors on the third step (220 feet), second step (400 feet - most complex), and first step (300 feet). After the final rappel, scramble down the brush and gully terrain back through the steep wooded gullies, following the approximate line of ascent. Expect the entire descent to take 4-6 hours and require careful route finding.
knownHazards: Extremely unreliable and poor quality rock throughout
Minimal natural protection; climbers have reported poor piton placements
Rockfall from unstable faces and from climbers above
Dense brush and vegetation in lower approach gully
Extreme heat exposure from eastern-facing aspect (can cause heat exhaustion)
Weather instability; afternoon storms possible
Cornice formation on ridges in winter/spring
Fixed ropes may be in poor condition if not recently maintained
watchOut: This is a serious technical alpine climb with high objective and subjective hazard
Approach takes significant effort with heavy loads; move methodically
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; monitor forecast closely
Minimize time on exposed rock; move efficiently on pitches
Approach gully can have rockfall from climbers; communicate loudly
Heat exposure on approach requires extra water and frequent breaks
Beware of poor rock quality; test all holds and placements
objHaz: Poor rock quality throughout route creating climbing hazard
Altitude and weather exposure on 3500-foot face
Loose debris and rockfall from brush and approach gully

pitchDetail.notes: 300 feet; vertical step requiring Grade V climbing to first ledge

400 feet; most difficult section with vertical, delicate climbing on black rhyolite. Features 120-degree roof sections requiring stirrup aid climbing (C1-C2). Poor natural protection; climbers resort to bolts.
220 feet; Grade V climbing to upper ledge
250 feet; final vertical step to summit ridge

routes.detailedRack: Specialized aid climbing gear including stirrups, piton kit emphasizing chrome-moly knife-blade pitons (essential for poor rock), selection of bolts for anchor placement, crampons, ice axes for lower gully, belay anchors, rappel equipment (multiple 10-rope caches placed by established parties), water bags for heat exposure.
routes.proNeeds: This route demands substantial alpine climbing experience including advanced aid climbing skills (C1-C2), comfortable leading Grade V rock on marginal protection, and multi-day expedition competence. A party should include at least one climber with prior North Face alpine experience. Rope management and anchor skills are critical due to complex rappel descent.
gear: Fixed ropes (10-rope cache minimum; may be established by previous parties)
Aid climbing gear (stirrups, pitons, chrome-moly knife-blade pitons essential)
Bivouac equipment (tent or bivy sac, sleeping bag, pad)
Extra water bags (critical due to heat exposure)
Climbing protection (natural pro, slings, carabiners, belay anchors)
whatToBring: Full alpine climbing kit and technical gear
proTips: Consult Fred Beckey's 'Climbing and High Routes: Stevens Pass to Rainy Pass' for detailed historical information and first ascent details
Fixed ropes from previous parties may be present on lower sections; use caution as their condition is unknown
The approach brush is easier to navigate with trail markers; clear brush if possible to aid future parties
Start early in the season (June) to avoid afternoon thunderstorms; July weather less stable
routes.bail: Bail only by descending the same route. There are no alternative descent routes on this face. The only safe retreat is via rappelling back down the route of ascent.
routes.turnaround: Plan a turnaround time in early afternoon to ensure safe rappel descent before dark. Stopping early is critical given the complex multi-rope rappel descent required.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM or earlier

road.name: Barclay Creek Road (FSR 6024)
road.status: Passable; may have potholes
road.driveNote: Rough in spots; 4WD vehicles recommended in poor weather. Approximately 4.3 miles from Highway 2 near Baring, Washington. Parking lot limited; arrive early.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

climate.forecastZone: Cascades Range - Central Cascade forecast zone
climate.typical: Alpine; high exposure to weather. Cool temperatures, frequent wind, unpredictable conditions
climate.spring: Snow/avalanche risk on approach; potentially unstable weather
climate.summer: Best climbing season; warmest temperatures; afternoon thunderstorms possible
climate.fall: Deteriorating weather; increasing storms and early snow
climate.winter: Extreme snow, avalanche, and weather hazard; route not recommended

emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Medical Center - Evergreen (approximately 30 miles)
emergency.notes: Extremely remote location with minimal cell coverage. Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest manages the area. SAR response will take many hours given location and technical nature of rescue. Satellite communicator or PLB strongly recommended.

blurb: Baring Mountain is the 3rd steepest peak in Washington at 6,127 feet, located in the central Cascades near Highway 2 within the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. The peak features a distinctive double summit and imposing sheer north face. It is one of the highest peaks in the Wild Sky Wilderness and is accessible via the Barclay Lake trailhead. The mountain was first ascended on July 28, 1897 by Albert H. Sylvester and John Charlton. The peak is known for both popular mountaineering scrambles on the standard northwest ridge and serious technical alpine climbing on the north face, which was first successfully climbed in 1960 by Don Gordon and Ed Cooper after a decade of attempts.

Bonanza Peak (9,516 ft) is the highest non-volcanic peak in Washington, located in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades. The peak's meta-granitic slopes are flanked by three major glaciers (Company, Mary Green, and Isella) and are known for their technical complexity, loose rock, and remoteness. First ascended in 1937 by the Mazamas climbing club, it remains one of the most challenging county highpoints in the lower 48 states, demanding proficiency in glacier travel, rock scrambling, and alpine navigation.

routes.overview: The classic and most popular route to Bonanza Peak's summit, ascending the east-facing Mary Green Glacier and finishing via the northeast ridge. This intermediate alpine climb combines glacier travel with mixed rock and scrambling, offering a complete North Cascades alpine experience.
routes.beta: From Holden basecamp (Holden Lake or Holden Pass), approach via established trail to glacier base at ~7,300 ft. Rope up and ascend Mary Green Glacier bearing west along its north edge, then traverse southwest to the glacier's upper corner. Climb the steep upper snow finger to the right until reaching rock; transition to the northeast ridge with mostly Class 3-4 rock just right of a gully for several rope lengths, then traverse left to a prominent notch in the upper ridge north of summit. Scramble Class 4 loose ridge crest past large blocks to the summit horn.
routes.approach: From Chelan, take the Lady of the Lake ferry service to Lucerne (approximately 50 miles up Lake Chelan), then ride the connecting shuttle bus to Holden Village. From Holden Village (3,262 ft), hike west via trail to Holden Lake (5,278 ft) or Holden Pass (6,360 ft) for basecamp. Standard approach follows established trail through talus and slide alder terrain. Route to glacier begins with Class 3 terrain crossing talus slopes and traversing ledges toward waterfall area and slabs leading to Mary Green Glacier.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route. Rappel down the slabby terrain on the upper northeast ridge using 4 segments of rappelling (approximately 200-300 feet total). Descend glacier carefully managing crevasse hazards and moats. Return to basecamp via the approach terrain. Total descent time: 3-4 hours from summit.
routes.descentText: From the summit (9,516 ft), traverse down the exposed northeast ridge (Class 3-4, mostly loose rock) back to the upper snow finger. Rappel through a slabby dihedral section (Class 4) using existing anchor points or natural anchor systems in 4 segments. Once back on the glacier, conduct a careful methodical descent of Mary Green Glacier, testing snow bridges and managing bergschrunds and moats (crevasses). Use belay or simul-climbing for safety on steeper sections. Return to basecamp via the approach terrain.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Travel from Chelan to Holden Village via ferry and shuttle, establish basecamp at Holden Lake or near Holden Pass. Day 2: Acclimatization and rest day, or alpine start for fast parties. Day 3: Summit push—depart basecamp early (3-5 AM), 4.5-6 hours to summit, descend same day to basecamp by evening.
routes.bail: From the glacier, descend back toward Holden Lake/Pass camp. From upper rock sections, retreat down Class 3-4 terrain if conditions deteriorate. Abort conditions: sustained white-out weather, unstable snow, excessive crevassing, or rockfall. Holden Village can provide emergency assistance.
routes.turnaround: Ensure sufficient daylight to complete descent before dark. Aim for descending to glacier by 2 PM. Turn around if conditions degrade, weather worsens, or summit isn't achievable by early afternoon.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through late August, with peak conditions typically in July. Earlier in season (June) glacier heavily crevassed with unstable snow bridges. Later seasons (late August onward) feature more exposed rock transitions and increased rockfall hazard.
proTips: Climb mid-summer (July preferred) before glacier becomes heavily broken by meltwater; bergschrunds and moat crossings become harder in late August-September.
Early alpine start (2-3 AM) essential to maximize daylight and manage rockfall hazard from waterfall slabs during afternoon warming.
Most parties move together on glacier using simul-climbing or short-rope; rope on steeper upper sections and exposed northeast ridge.
Waterfall area (lower approach slabs) is hazardous; cross quickly in early morning before water flow increases from daytime melt.
Post-hole rate increases dramatically on hard snow; crampons save energy on sustained moderate angle glacier.
Loose rock on upper northeast ridge is significant descent hazard; move carefully and avoid knocking rocks on party members below.
watchOut: Loose meta-granitic rock on all rock pitches—test holds before weighting, especially on descent.
Rockfall from waterfall zone and upper cliff bands, particularly in afternoon when temperatures warm; time passage for early morning.
Crevasses and bergschrunds on Mary Green Glacier, especially in early-mid summer when snow bridges are fragile.
Moats (gap between glacier and rock) difficult or impossible to cross late in season; earlier attempts have better snow conditions.
Exposure on northeast ridge; several sections Class 4 with steep drop-offs; secure all anchors and manage group positioning.
Inclement weather extreme objective hazard; afternoon thunderstorms common in summer; descend or bail early if clouds build.
knownHazards: Loose, fractured meta-granitic rock throughout route, exacerbated by water percolation and freeze-thaw cycles.
Significant rockfall danger from waterfall slabs and upper cliff bands during warming periods.
Crevasse hazard on Mary Green Glacier, particularly early season when snow bridges concealed.
Bergschrund and moat crossings, difficult or impossible in late season.
Exposed Class 4 scrambling on northeast ridge with significant exposure.
Frequent alpine thunderstorms in summer; electrical hazard at exposed summit and ridge.
Route-finding hazard; poor visibility in clouds makes navigation difficult on glacier and upper mountain.
routes.comms: No cellular service on peak or approach terrain. Satellite communicator (InReach, Zoleo) essential for emergency communication. North Cascades typically have good satellite visibility.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Chelan Ranger District) and Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for vehicle parking at trailheads

road.name: No road access; ferry + shuttle required to Holden Village
road.status: Lady of the Lake ferry operates seasonal May-October; Holden Village shuttle subject to seasonal operation
road.seasonalGate: Ferry and shuttle typically seasonal; spring/fall availability limited
road.driveNote: No direct vehicle access. Ferry from Chelan to Lucerne approximately 4 hours, then shuttle 13 miles to Holden Village. Reservations strongly recommended in summer. NOTE: Holden Village experienced flooding in 2025; confirm current transport before booking.

climate.typical: North Cascades maritime climate, unpredictable and rapid changes. Summer daytime 50-70F (10-21C), nights below freezing. Frequent afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.spring: Poor conditions; deep snow, avalanche hazard, unstable snow bridges. Typically avoided.
climate.summer: Most stable (June-September). Daytime 50-70F (10-21C). Snow persists to July. Afternoon thunderstorms common. Best mid-July.
climate.fall: Increasing clouds, rain likely, early snow possible. Shortening daylight.
climate.winter: Extreme avalanche hazard, deep snow, corniced ridges, -10 to 20F typical. Essentially unclimbable.
climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center—East Slopes Central

emergency.nearestHospital: Chelan Hospital, Chelan, WA (~60 miles, ~2+ hours)
emergency.notes: No cell service. Satellite communicator essential. Holden Village has limited medical capacity; serious incidents require helicopter evacuation to distant hospital. Allow 2+ hours for rescue response time.

routes.detailedRack: Single rope setup: 60m dynamic 9-10mm rope, 2 locking carabiners, 6-8 non-locking carabiners (HMS/pear-shaped preferred), webbing (1-2 inch, 20-30 feet total), 1-2 accessory cord (8mm, 50-60 feet for rappel anchors), belay device (ATC/Grigri/tubular), rescue pulley. No rock protection needed on standard route; natural anchors (rock horns, ice screws in perpetual ice). Spare webbing for emergency rappel anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Anchors established through natural systems (rock horns on glacier-rock junction, ice screw placements in perpetual ice near upper ridge, buried axes/pickets). Rappel anchors on Class 3-4 descent terrain built from natural features or remnant fixed anchors. Most parties do not place trad protection; scrambling managed via exposure management and careful movement.
whatToBring: Warm layers (synthetic or down jacket, fleece, base layers)
Toiletries (sunscreen, lip balm, toilet paper, waste bag)

routes.beta: Route could not be verified in published climbing sources.
routes.corrections: Route unverified: The 'Northeast Face / Company Glacier' route could not be verified in any published climbing sources despite extensive research. After searching Mountain Project, SummitPost, American Alpine Club Publications, WTA, The Mountaineers, and numerous trip reports, no documented route combining these two features with the listed grades (IV, 5.7, AI2, AD) was found. The Company Glacier is confirmed as a geographic feature (north side of peak), but no climbing route on it is documented. Documented technical routes are: (1) Mary Green Glacier (Class 4, Alpine AD), (2) Northeast Buttress via three-summit traverse (V 5.7/5.8), (3) Soviet Route on SW Peak (V 5.10a), (4) Cascadian Route (V 5.10+), (5) Oregonian Route (V 5.9+). Requires verification with local climbing communities.

blurb: Cascade Peak is a 7,428-foot alpine summit in Skagit County's North Cascades, situated above Cascade Pass on the shared border of North Cascades National Park and Glacier Peak Wilderness. The peak features breccia rock and small glacier remnants on its north side. First ascended July 23, 1950 by Fred Beckey, Pete Schoening, and Phil Sharpe.
hierarchyNote: Database elevation 7,415 ft should be corrected to 7,428 ft per verified sources (Wikipedia, Peakbagger, etc.)

routes.fa: Unknown for this route; Cascade Peak FA: Fred Beckey, Pete Schoening, Phil Sharpe (July 23, 1950)
routes.overview: Technical alpine rock scramble accessed from the C-J Couloir col between Cascade Peak and Johannesburg Mountain, featuring low 5th class rock with typical alpine exposure and loose terrain to the summit.
routes.beta: Class 4 alpine rock scramble on the East Ridge of Cascade Peak. Approach via Cascade Pass trail, then cross-country scramble to the C-J Col. Route involves mixed scrambling and low fifth class rock climbing with alpine exposure and loose rock hazards throughout.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road 23 miles from Highway 20 (near Marblemount) to the Cascade Pass trailhead. Hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.6 miles with 1,800 ft elevation gain (approximately 2-3 hours). From Cascade Pass (~5,400 ft), cross-country scramble southeast to reach the C-J Couloir col between Cascade Peak and Johannesburg Mountain. Continue up the East Ridge from the col to the summit.
routes.descent: Class 3-4 down-climbing via the same route or alternate scramble lines on adjacent slopes. Exercise caution on loose rock and exposed terrain throughout descent.
routes.descentText: Carefully down-climb the same Class 3-4 rock terrain used for ascent, or descend via alternate scramble lines on the east face. Loose rock is common; verify every handhold and foothold. Microspikes may be beneficial in early season when snow patches remain. Exposure typical for alpine scramble; move deliberately and deliberately.
routes.bestSeason: July through August offers the best conditions with stable weather, minimal snow, and accessible camps at Cascade Pass. Late June may have snow requiring microspikes; September possible but increasingly unstable weather. Winter and spring dangerous due to avalanche hazard in approach couloirs.
proTips: Start early from Cascade Pass camps to maximize daylight on exposed terrain
Early season (June-July) offers best snow consolidation for glacier approach; avoid mid-summer rockfall after warming
The C-J Couloir between Cascade and Johannesburg is a natural landmark readily identified from Cascade Pass
Plan as multi-day outing; establish camp at Cascade Pass before attempting the peak
Crampons and ice axe useful early season for frozen rock and snow patches on approach
watchOut: Rockfall hazard increases significantly mid-day due to solar warming and glacial melt
Crevasses present on glaciers above Cascade Pass; rope teams essential on glacier approach
Frequent afternoon thunderstorms July-August; be off all exposed terrain by early afternoon
Loose rock throughout route; verify every handhold and foothold
Significant exposure on scramble sections; one climber at a time on narrow ridge
knownHazards: Objective rockfall from warming slopes above the route
Crevasse hazards on glacier approaches to Cascade Pass
Seasonal avalanches in approach gullies (primarily winter/spring)
Sudden weather changes typical of maritime climate including lightning and hail
Glacier recession creating new moats, loose terrain, and ice-fall hazards
routes.comms: Cellular coverage is unreliable at Cascade Pass and on approach. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach or Spot) strongly recommended for safe backcountry communication. Always verify coverage before relying on any device.
routes.proNeeds: Small alpine rock rack with cams 0.4-3 inches and offset nuts for Skagit gneiss irregular cracks. 60-70m dry-treated single rope. Crampons with horizontal front points and ice axe mandatory for glacier sections. Pickets for glacier anchors. Insulated mountaineering boots for all-day alpine terrain.
routes.detailedRack: One set of cams from 0.4 to 3 inches, offset nuts (essential for irregular Skagit gneiss cracks), alpine quickdraws with extendable runners to manage rope drag on mixed terrain. Single 60-70m dry-treated climbing rope (8.6-9.2mm). Glacier pickets and anchors for snow/ice protection. Full ice axe and crampons (semi or fully automatic steel) for glacier approach.
whatToBring: Warm mid-layers (wool or synthetic, avoid cotton)
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike Cascade Pass Trail (3.6 miles, 1,800 ft elevation gain, 2-3 hours) from trailhead to Cascade Pass camp area and establish camp. Day 2: Scramble to C-J Col and ascend East Ridge to summit (4-6 hours round trip from camp). Day 3: Descend to camp and pack out, or descend all the way to trailhead (3-4 hours to pass).
routes.turnaround: If parties have not reached the C-J Col by mid-morning, or if weather or visibility deteriorates, retreat to Cascade Pass camp. Afternoon lightning storms are common; all exposed terrain must be clear by mid-afternoon for safety.
routes.bail: From C-J Col or lower ridge sections, descend via alternate gullies back to Cascade Pass valley. In poor visibility, descend the same route used for ascent. If climber is injured on exposed terrain, retreat to Cascade Pass where satellite communication can summon ranger assistance.

timing.recommendedStart: 0600 from Cascade Pass camp

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open year-round; check NPS website for seasonal gates
road.seasonalGate: Cascade Pass parking opens late June to early July
road.driveNote: 23 miles from Highway 20 at Marblemount to Cascade Pass trailhead; paved initially then gravel; final miles rough and steep. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Northwest Forest Pass required for parking.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Cascade-West-South
climate.typical: Maritime climate with frequent storms, rain, and wind. High elevation snow and ice year-round in shaded terrain.
climate.spring: Heavy precipitation and avalanche hazard in couloirs; lingering snow at pass elevation into June
climate.summer: Stable weather July-August; frequent afternoon thunderstorms; rapid snowmelt increases rockfall and icefall. Early June may have microspikes-level snow.
climate.fall: Increasing storm frequency; snow above 6,000 ft by late September; cooling temperatures
climate.winter: Heavy snow, avalanche danger, extreme cold above pass; serious mountaineering undertaking

access.landManager: National Park Service / US Forest Service (North Cascades National Park and Glacier Peak Wilderness boundary)
access.fees: $26 per backcountry trip permit; day use free
access.permit: North Cascades National Park backcountry permit required for overnight stay. Reservations via Recreation.gov; walk-ups available.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for Cascade Pass trailhead parking
access.closures: Cascade Pass parking may be gated until late June or early July

emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon: 360-424-4111 (Level 3 trauma); United General, Sedro-Woolley: 360-856-6021
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 360-854-7245
emergency.notes: Satellite communicator mandatory. SAR response weather-dependent. Report emergencies via satellite device or by descending to Cascade Pass.

blurb: Chiwawa Mountain (8,459') is a prominent peak in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, straddling the Snohomish-Chelan county border on the Cascade crest. The peak is renowned for the Lyman Glacier on its northeast flank and offers multiple mountaineering routes at various difficulty levels. First ascent: 1921, led by Lorenz A. Nelson with The Mountaineers club.

routes.overview: The simplest and most popular mountaineering route on Chiwawa Mountain, ascending the southwest slopes via snow and scree to the summit with moderate scrambling on the approach ridge.
routes.fa: 1921, Lorenz A. Nelson with The Mountaineers
routes.beta: Ascend the southwest snowfield and scree slopes to reach the summit at 8,459 feet. The route follows the path of least resistance from Fortress-Chiwawa Col, traversing east-northeast aiming for the col, then ascending the final south ridge. Navigate snow or scree to within 100 feet of the summit, then traverse the west side to avoid steep rock sections on the final approach.
routes.approach: From Trinity Trailhead (2,800 ft) at the end of Chiwawa River Road, take the Buck Creek Trail for 1.4 miles, then turn right onto the Chiwawa River Trail (old road grade). At 5 miles, continue left on the Chiwawa River Trail. Ford the Chiwawa River and follow the meadow's right edge. Bushwhack straight uphill to reach the 5,500-foot Fortress-Chiwawa Col. Establish camp at a 6,300-foot bench with krummholz vegetation and water sources.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent route via the southwest snowfield and southwestern slopes. Aim for the scree slope near the right skyline (SW slope) and avoid the left skyline to prevent exposure to cliff bands. Descend to Fortress-Chiwawa Col and return via the established trail to Chiwawa Basin.
routes.descentText: Descend via the southwest snowfield and scree slopes, carefully navigating back to Fortress-Chiwawa Col. Use caution on loose talus and avoid steep terrain on the peak's north face. From the col, descend through meadows and forest, cross the Chiwawa River, and return via the Chiwawa River Trail to the trailhead.
routes.turnaround: The turnaround point from camp should be mid-afternoon to ensure safe descent in daylight. In early summer with longer daylight hours, routes should still descend before darkness, with particular attention to safe river crossing and trail navigation.
routes.bestSeason: June through October offers the most stable conditions. Early season (June-July) features snow and avalanche terrain; mid-summer (August) provides the most stable conditions with reduced avalanche risk and established routes. September and early October offer excellent weather and lower crowds.
routes.bail: From the southwest slopes below the summit, retreat downslope toward Fortress-Chiwawa Col. If caught in deteriorating weather on the ridge, descend immediately to lower elevations. In early season with significant snow, turn back if new avalanche evidence appears or instability is suspected.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is minimal to nonexistent in the Glacier Peak Wilderness. Satellite communicators such as personal locator beacons or satellite messengers are recommended for emergency communication and trip reporting.
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain on southwest slopes, especially early in season (June-July); late-season snowpack is generally more stable by August
Loose talus and scree sections prone to slides; unstable rock on descent requires careful footwork
Class 3-4 scrambling on steep ridge sections; exposure on approach ridge near Fortress-Chiwawa saddle
Creek crossings on Chiwawa River Trail; flow is highest in early summer
Rapid weather changes common in alpine environment; thunderstorms possible in afternoon
watchOut: Early season avalanche hazard is the most serious objective hazard; assess snowpack stability before committing to high-angle slopes
Navigation in poor visibility can be challenging in the upper basins; use map and compass or GPS
The peak is bordered by steep terrain on multiple sides; avoid venturing onto north-facing slopes in early season
Large biting insects at lower elevations and meadow areas; bring insect repellent
whatToBring: Microspikes or other traction device (alternative if snow coverage is minimal)
Backpack (50L for 2-day trip, 30L for day trip)
Tent and sleeping bag (for overnight trips)
Stove and fuel (for hot meals and water)
Maps (USGS Glacier Peak Wilderness map) and compass/GPS device
First aid kit with blister treatment
routes.detailedRack: Standard mountaineering rack: ice axe for self-arrest and belay anchor placement, crampons for snow and ice climbing, harness and dynamic rope for glacier crevasse rescue (if roping up), carabiners and slings for anchor construction, and belay device. Protection is minimal as the route is primarily scrambling and snow travel; rock protection not typically needed.
routes.proNeeds: No rock protection needed for the scrambling sections; parties typically ascend unroped. Rope and crevasse rescue equipment recommended if glacier travel is anticipated or if traversing crevassed terrain. Crampons and ice axe are essential for self-arrest capability in case of slips on snow or ice.
proTips: Ascend early in the day to maximize time and allow safe descent before dark; 5-6 hour approach from trailhead to camp is typical
Establish camp at 6,300 feet on the bench with krummholz; this position offers water access and good views for weather forecasting
Navigate to summit on a clear day; the route is not obvious in poor visibility, especially in snow conditions
If climbing in early season (June-July), assess avalanche risk carefully and consider staying on ridgelines rather than snow slopes
The southwest snowfield offers the most direct route; avoid the north-facing gully systems where avalanche activity is more common

pitchDetail.notes: From Fortress-Chiwawa Col, the terrain is moderate. Bushwhacking section from river to col can be steep and brushy. Establish camp at 6,300-foot bench.

Ascend snow and rock traverse northeast from col toward summit ridge. Route finding can be tricky in poor visibility. Snow provides good climbing conditions early season; transitions to scree by late summer.

Climb southeast along ridge, then traverse west side to avoid steep rock on final 100 feet. Shattered rock with loose sections; careful footwork required. Traverse around west side to reach summit block.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (5-6 AM) for 2-day trip to establish camp with time to explore and cook; dawn start (6-7 AM) for day trip attempt

waypoints.name: Buck Creek - Chiwawa River Trail Junction

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FS Road 6200)
road.status: CLOSED as of May 2026 due to storm damage; closure through December 31, 2027 unless rescinded sooner
road.seasonalGate: Seasonal; typically accessible June through October when snowpack permits
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, take US-2 West for 15 miles, turn right on WA-207 North for 4.4 miles, turn right on Chiwawa Loop Road, then take the second left onto Chiwawa River Road/FS Road 6200. The road becomes gravel at mile 11 and is very rough dirt for the final 12 miles; high clearance vehicles recommended. Total drive distance approximately 23 miles. IMPORTANT: Road is currently CLOSED due to storm damage—verify current conditions before planning trip.

climate.forecastZone: Glacier Peak Wilderness, North Cascades, Eastern Washington Cascades
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy precipitation driven by Pacific weather systems and orographic lift. High variability in conditions; afternoon thunderstorms common in summer.
climate.spring: May-June: Unstable weather with frequent precipitation; heavy snow above 6,000 feet; avalanche hazard high; route typically snowy and icy
climate.summer: July-August: Most stable season with clear skies typical; temperatures at summit around 40-50 degrees F; afternoon thunderstorms possible; snow mostly consolidated by August; best climbing conditions
climate.fall: September-October: Clear days with cooling temperatures; early storms possible; shorter daylight hours; lower crowds; reliable climbing conditions through September
climate.winter: November-March: Heavy snowfall, avalanche hazard extreme; not recommended for ascents; some winter mountaineering possible in stable conditions

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District)
