access.fees: $5 per day or $30 annual Northwest Forest Pass required for parking
access.permit: Free wilderness permit required at Trinity Trailhead for Glacier Peak Wilderness access
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (Recreation Pass, Annual Pass, or Digital Day Pass via recreation.gov app)
access.closures: Chiwawa River Road currently closed due to storm damage through December 31, 2027 (as of May 2026). Check Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest alerts for current status.

emergency.county: Snohomish County (western slope) and Chelan County (eastern slope)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County: 911; Chelan County: 911 or RiverCom (509) 663-9911
emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest: (509) 548-2550 (Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri 9am-12pm and 1pm-3:30pm, closed Wednesdays)
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (approximately 45 miles); Darrington Clinic provides closer medical services
emergency.notes: Helicopter evacuation possible via Snohomish County Helicopter Rescue Team or Chelan County Mountain Rescue. Satellite communicator (PLB or messenger) strongly recommended due to lack of cell coverage.

routes.fa: July 31, 1931 by William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg (Seattle Mountaineers) - first ascent of peak; July 5, 1969 by Dallas Kloke and Scott Masonholder - first ascent of Northeast Gully and East Ridge route variation
routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the best climbing conditions with most snow melted and stable weather. June still has significant snowcover and higher precipitation. September begins to see more unstable conditions.
routes.overview: The primary mountaineering route climbs the northeast side via Colonial Glacier to reach the summit. This is an alpine scramble combining glacier travel with exposed Class 3 rock scrambling, requiring ice axe and crampons.
routes.beta: Standard approach via Colonial Glacier from the 5,400 ft base camp. Ascend the glacier southeast to steeper slopes, then follow a wide snow finger (steep 150 ft section) to the summit ridge. Final approach involves exposed Class 3 scrambling on mixed rock, scree, and dirt terrain. Total climb from base camp approximately 1,600-1,700 feet.
routes.approach: Start at Pyramid Lake Trailhead on State Route 20 near milepost 127 (21 miles east of Marblemount). Park on the north side of the road. Hike the Pyramid Lake Trail 2.1 miles and 1,500 feet elevation gain to Pyramid Lake. From Pyramid Lake, continue on climber's trail ascending east ridge of Pyramid Peak area. Reach climber's camp at 5,400 feet elevation after approximately 4-5 miles total and 3,500+ feet of elevation gain. Water available from waterfalls in the basin.
routes.descent: Descend same route. Drop down from summit ridge notch to the snow (easier than ascent). Follow ridgeline back down through boulder fields and scree. Watch for lichen-covered rocks indicating off-route travel. Return to base camp, then descend Pyramid Lake Trail to trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descent mirrors the approach in reverse. From the summit ridge notch, descend to snowfields and traverse back down the wide snow finger to the glacier. The descent is generally easier than the ascent on the steeper sections. Return via the ridgeline, descending through boulder fields on dirt and scree terrain - stay on well-traveled sections with lichen removal. Expect approximately 2.5-3 hours descent from summit to base camp.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if: weather deteriorates with high winds or visibility drops below safe level; party energy is insufficient for safe descent before afternoon; crevasses become unstable or snow bridges show signs of failure; any team member exhibits altitude sickness or fatigue incompatible with safe descent.
routes.bail: From mid-route: descend back down the ridge system to base camp. From higher elevations: retreat via the snow finger and glacier to the base camp. If stranded high: bivy sites available on moraine and rock above the base camp lake; melted-out locations with water access.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, 3-4 AM from base camp to allow full daylight for technical sections

knownHazards: Crevasses on Colonial Glacier requiring rope team travel and crevasse rescue skills
Steep snowfield traverses at 45-55 degrees requiring crampons and self-arrest capability
Loose rock and scree on final summit pyramid
Rockfall from adjacent Pyramid Peak especially during warm afternoons
Exposure on narrow ridge sections with Class 3 scrambling
Marine west coast climate with rapid weather changes and heavy precipitation
watchOut: Rockfall hazard from Pyramid Peak particularly between 10am-3pm during warm weather - move quickly through traverse zones
Large moats (gaps between glacier and rock) may be present at glacier margins depending on season
High lichen coverage on rocks indicates off-route travel; main route is obvious and well-traveled
Wet avalanche danger in spring and early summer from heavy, wet snow
Crevasses may be hidden under snow bridges - always rope up on glacier travel
Steep moat crossing near the summit requires careful foot placement and self-belay consideration
routes.detailedRack: For rope team glacier travel: 50-60m dynamic rope, 1-2 locking carabiners for belay/rappel, 2-3 straight-gate carabiners, belay device (ATC or similar), prussik cord or personal anchor system for crevasse rescue. For rock scrambling: helmet, chalk bag. Glacier rescue kit should include pulley or carabiner for Z-pulley setup.
routes.proNeeds: Rope and harness mandatory for glacier crevasse protection. Crampons and ice axe essential for any snowfield sections. Self-rescue and basic crevasse rescue skills required for parties traveling on glacier. Ice axe used for self-arrest practice and moat/snow bridge crossing.
proTips: Start very early (3-4 AM) from camp to complete climb in daylight and avoid afternoon rockfall from Pyramid Peak
Scout the route on approach day if visibility is clear
The wide snow finger to the summit ridge is obvious - follow the main line
Crampons can be removed at the small flat area below the final rock notch
Many parties combine this with ascents of nearby peaks (Pyramid Peak, Paul Bunyan's Stump, Neve Peak) in multi-day trips
Water is readily available at base camp from waterfalls in the basin
Late June through July has substantial snow; August and September have more rock showing
The route-finding is straightforward with bootpath visible in most conditions
whatToBring: Sleeping bag (rated for below freezing temperatures)
Water bottles or hydration reservoir (3+ liters capacity)
First aid kit with blister treatment
Hat and balaclava for sun and cold protection
Trekking poles (helpful on approach with heavy pack)

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy winter precipitation. Summers mild and relatively dry. High variability in weather with rapid changes possible. Winter snowfall is heavy and wet.
climate.spring: High avalanche danger in March-May due to wet snow. Late May sees melting begin but snow still substantial at higher elevations. Frequent afternoon thunderstorms common.
climate.summer: July-August are the most stable months with warm daytime temperatures (60-70°F) but cold nights (30-40°F). June still has high precipitation and snowcover. September sees decreasing temperatures and increasing weather variability.
climate.fall: September sees cooler temperatures and more unstable weather. October brings significant snow at higher elevations and conditions approach winter. Not recommended for climbing after early October.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche danger make this peak unclimbable. High winds and severe weather typical.
climate.forecastZone: North Cascades area forecast zone; marine influence from Pacific coast creates maritime climate pattern

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.fees: No entrance fee to North Cascades National Park. Trailhead parking requires Northwest Forest Pass ($30/year or $5/day pass).
access.permit: Backcountry camping permit required: $10 per person plus $6 nonrefundable permit fee during summer season (mid-May to early October). No fee outside summer season. Permits available through Recreation.gov or at Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking at Pyramid Lake Trailhead on National Forest lands.
access.closures: Trail typically open late June through September. Pyramid Lake is closed to camping. Party size limited to 6 people maximum.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open year-round to Diablo Lake area, though winter chains required. Road frequently closed in winter at higher elevations eastward.
road.seasonalGate: Highway 20 may close at upper elevations in winter. Access to Pyramid Lake Trailhead is generally available year-round but trail conditions deteriorate in winter.
road.driveNote: Drive State Route 20 to milepost 127 (6.5 miles east of Newhalem, 80 miles east of Seattle, 3 hours drive). Park on north side of road at signed Pyramid Lake Trailhead near Gorge Lake. About 2.5 hours drive from Seattle.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount): 360-854-7245; Address: 7280 Ranger Station Road, Marblemount, WA 98267
emergency.nearestHospital: PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center, Bellingham, WA (approximately 60 minutes drive from trailhead); emergency room available 24/7
emergency.notes: Cell service very limited in the area. SAT communicator or personal locator beacon recommended for emergencies. Rescue helicopter access possible but weather-dependent.

routes.comms: Cell phone reception is very limited to nonexistent in the Colonial Peak area due to mountainous terrain. Satellite messenger device (Garmin inReach, Apple Emergency SOS, etc.) recommended for emergencies.

blurb: Colonial Peak is a prominent 7,771-foot granite summit in Washington's North Cascades, rising dramatically 6,560 feet above Thunder Arm of Diablo Lake. The standard West Ridge route via Colonial Glacier is a popular alpine mountaineering objective combining glacier travel with Class 3 rock scrambling. First ascended on July 31, 1931 by Seattle Mountaineers members William Degenhardt and Herbert Strandberg, the peak offers relatively moderate technical climbing compared to its southern neighbors but demands glacier skills and proper preparation.
hierarchyNote: Colonial Peak is correctly placed in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington. Coordinates 48°39'41"N 121°07'16"W (also verified as 48.661332°N, 121.122206°W) definitively identify this peak, which rises prominently above Diablo Lake's Thunder Arm. No hierarchy concerns detected.

blurb: Cutthroat Peak (8,066 ft) is a prominent granite mountain in the North Cascades near Washington Pass, straddling the Chelan/Skagit County boundary. It features accessible moderate alpine climbing ranging from Grade II to III, with the South Buttress being the classic route. The peak was first ascended July 22, 1937 by Kenneth Adam, Raffi Bedayn, and W. Kenneth Davis via the West Ridge.

routes.overview: The classic 850-foot alpine route on Cutthroat Peak, featuring moderate rock climbing through slabs, chimneys, and overhangs with a 5.8 crux near the summit.
routes.approach: From Highway 20 pullout 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass on north side, drop into meadow below peak and scramble up leftmost large gully (approximately 5th class scrambling) to ridge crest; approximately 1.5-2 hours to technical base.
routes.beta: The route wanders up the South Buttress crest through mostly easier climbing with several short 5.6-5.7 sections interspersed. Pitch 8 (crux) features a wide chimney with hand cracks; ascend via stemming then step right to face. Final pitches ascend a prominent offwidth crack described as a splitter, with scrambling to summit. Solid granite alternates with sections of loose blocks and kitty litter throughout.
routes.descent: Two primary options: (1) West Ridge descent via approximately three 30m rappels using bolted anchors with chains, descending northwest from summit, then downclimbing ridge while looking for cairn markers as terrain eases; or (2) South Buttress descent via 13+ rappels back down climbing route with fixed anchor chains.
routes.descentText: West Ridge descent is faster. From summit, three single-rope rappels using bolted anchors with chains; first rappel drops onto a large flat ledge. Second rappel on north side of ridge requires careful location of bolted stations when viewing from above. After rappels, descend sandy ribs and gullies to avoid loose rock below ridge crest. Alternatively, rappel South Buttress route itself using more than a dozen rappels, which allows gear and shoe caching at base of technical climbing.
routes.itinerary: Single-day push recommended. Arrive at trailhead early (parking fills quickly). Approach and scramble 1.5-2 hours to technical base. Technical climbing approximately 5-6 hours. Descent 2-3 hours. Total time base to base typically 9-10 hours.
routes.bail: From lower pitches, downclimb and descend gully. From mid-route (pitch 6-8), can descend east into adjacent gullies to base of South Face. From summit, commit to one of two descent options (West Ridge or South Buttress).
routes.turnaround: If weather deteriorates mid-climb, descend immediately via downclimbing or rappeling. Do not proceed to summit in afternoon thunderstorm development typical of July-August.
routes.bestSeason: June through September after snow retreat. July and August offer most stable conditions. June may have residual snow and running water. Early September still good before first autumn storms. Avoid October-May due to winter conditions, short days, and avalanche danger in gullies.
routes.detailedRack: Single rack from 0.3-3 inches for crux pitches; bring a few doubles in the 0.5-2 inch range for the full route. Nuts and slings recommended. One 60m rope sufficient for descent.
proTips: Park at pullout west of Washington Pass early; lot fills quickly
The crux (pitch 8) offers great protection in the hand crack chimney
Final offwidth crack is a splitter with solid protection; take time to rack it well
West Ridge descent is much faster but requires careful anchor location; mark the way with cairns on ascent
Loose rock and kitty litter prevalent; stay aware of protection below
watchOut: Loose rock and rockfall throughout route; watch for climbers below
High-angle slab sections (pitch 10 area) prone to rockfall
Offwidth crack on final pitches is committing with limited escape options
Afternoon thunderstorms common July-August; start early and descend by early afternoon
Limited cell reception at summit or on peak
knownHazards: Loose rock and kitty litter sections with high rockfall potential
Unprotectable scramble on West Ridge with significant exposure
Afternoon lightning and thunderstorms in summer months
Avalanche debris swath crossing on approach in early season
routes.comms: Cell phone reception very limited on peak and on approach ridge; plan for no reliable communication.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff Emergency Dispatch: (360) 428-3211; Chelan County Sheriff: (509) 667-6830
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon (30+ miles south); Chelan Valley Hospital, Chelan (40+ miles east)
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.notes: This is remote alpine terrain. Emergency services response time is 2+ hours. Self-rescue and party care are essential. Avalanche safety and rope rescue skills required.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20) to Highway 20 pullout 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass
road.status: Open June-October typically; verify seasonal closure status
road.seasonalGate: North Cascades Scenic Highway closes seasonally in winter (typically November-April)
road.driveNote: From Marblemount area, drive east on SR-20 approximately 55 miles to pullout on north side of highway 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass. From Winthrop, drive west on SR-20 approximately 32 miles. Parking is extremely limited and fills quickly; arrive early.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades alpine
climate.typical: Rapid weather changes common. Morning clear, afternoon thunderstorm development typical July-August. High winds above 30 mph common. Temperature drops 3-4 degrees per 1000 feet elevation gain.
climate.spring: Significant snowpack through June. Avalanche hazard in gullies. Running water and wet rock.
climate.summer: July-August best conditions but afternoon thunderstorms common and unpredictable. Warm mornings (50-65F at peak) but rapid afternoon cooling and wind. Brief window of stability.
climate.fall: September still good with less afternoon storm activity. Early frosts and first snow possible late September. Shorter days limit options.
climate.winter: Peak closes October-April. Extreme avalanche danger in gullies. Heavy snowfall (400+ inches annually in Cascades). Temperatures well below freezing, high wind.

waypoints.name: State Creek approach - start of gully scramble

pitchDetail.notes: Easier terrain. Mix of clean cracks and slab climbing interspersed with scrambling. Build to crux pitch.
Wide chimney with hand cracks. Stem up, then step right to face with great protection. Classic Cascade granite crack climbing.
Slab walking and easier climbing. Crosses big slab and approaches final offwidth section.
Final section with prominent offwidth crack (splitter). Ascend offwidth then scramble to summit. Great protection throughout.

routes.overview: The first-ascent route and alternate finish to Cutthroat Peak. Grade II alpine with mostly 5th class scrambling and short technical sections up to 5.6, totaling approximately 400 feet of technical climbing.
routes.fa: July 22, 1937 — Kenneth Adam, Raffi Bedayn, and W. Kenneth Davis (first ascent of the peak)
routes.approach: From Highway 20 pullout 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass, drop down to creek crossing (State Creek area), ascend open meadows into the bowl southwest of the summit, then ascend to saddle just west of West Ridge end. Approximately 1.5-2 hours approach.
routes.beta: From saddle, traverse east along South Face of West Ridge approximately 100 feet to a shallow blocky gully. Scramble first section (Class 4), then belay up a solid section with two in-place pitons (approximately 50-foot pitch of Class 5 scrambling). Easy scrambling follows along the ridge crest to summit block. Crux is an exposed, mostly unprotectable scramble over rock hump followed by a wide flat ramp with exposure on both sides. Final moves are hard vertical cracks (5.6-ish) to summit. Brief chimney (5.5) on approach.
routes.descent: Two 30m rappels down the northwest side using bolted anchors with chains, trending toward West Ridge end. Downclimb ridge while looking left for cairn markers above chains as terrain becomes easier to travel below ridge crest. From first chains, rappel west down a ramp to next set of chains. Third rappel from midway down face descends to the gully or up and over a bump to ledge that traverses west to West Ridge buttress foot.
routes.descentText: Descent via three rappels on northwest side using bolted anchors throughout. Downclimbing between rappels with careful rock placement. Finding bolted stations on descent can be difficult when viewing from above; mark the way on ascent with cairns. After reaching base of technical rock, follow gullies and sandy ribs to avoid loose rock.
routes.itinerary: Day climb. Early start essential due to parking availability. Approach 1.5-2 hours, technical climbing 3-4 hours, descent 1.5-2 hours. Total time typically 6-8 hours base to base.
routes.bail: From lower pitches, downclimb and retreat into gully. From mid-ridge, descend east into adjacent gullies to base. From summit, commit to the three-rappel descent on northwest side.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if weather deteriorates. Afternoon storms common July-August; descend immediately if lightning threat develops.
routes.bestSeason: June through September. July and August most stable. Less popular than South Buttress so fewer parties.
routes.detailedRack: Small rack focusing on medium to large protection. Slings for natural anchors. One 50-60m rope sufficient for descent with good communication.
proTips: First-ascent route and excellent early-season alternative to crowded South Buttress
Mark descent route carefully with cairns on ascent to aid in locating rappel stations
The final vertical cracks provide good protection with solid granite
Mostly a scramble with short technical sections, making it faster than South Buttress
watchOut: Mostly unprotectable exposed scramble over rock hump—significant exposure
Loose rock and rockfall potential on descent
Bolted anchor stations difficult to locate from above
Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer
knownHazards: Exposed scrambling with minimal protection
Loose rock on descent gullies
Afternoon lightning threat July-August
routes.comms: No reliable cell reception on ridge or at summit.

waypoints.name: West Ridge saddle - start of technical climbing

pitchDetail.notes: Short chimney section on approach to saddle. Accessed via narrow ramp with bear hug move at blind corner.
Main pitch. Solid pitons in place. Blocky gully then scramble to ridge crest. Two pitons provide fixed protection.
Final cracks to summit. Hard vertical moves with good granite and protection. Mostly scramble before final push.

routes.overview: A more direct variation of the South Buttress route, featuring higher overall difficulty and more sustained climbing than the wandering classic line.
routes.approach: From Highway 20 pullout 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass on north side, drop into meadow below peak and scramble up leftmost large gully to ridge crest; approximately 1.5-2 hours to technical base.
routes.beta: More direct variation of the South Buttress that takes a more continuous line rather than the standard wandering route. Generally steeper and more direct through the face rather than following the ridge crest as consistently. Increased difficulty due to steeper sections and fewer easy-terrain breaks.
routes.descent: West Ridge descent or South Buttress descent options as described in main South Buttress route.
routes.descentText: Use West Ridge descent (faster, bolted anchors) or South Buttress descent (more rappels, allows gear caching). See South Buttress route for detailed descent information.
routes.itinerary: Single-day push. Approach 1.5-2 hours, technical climbing 6-7 hours, descent 2-3 hours. Total time base to base typically 10-12 hours.
routes.bail: If route proving too difficult, descend to base via downclimbing or adjacent gullies.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if encountering sustained difficulty significantly above your ability. Afternoon weather deterioration common; descend immediately if storms threaten.
routes.bestSeason: July through September for most stable conditions.
routes.detailedRack: Complete rack from 0.3-3 inches; bring extra doubles in 0.5-2 range due to sustained climbing and less wandering than classic route. Full protection essential.
proTips: More sustained and direct than the classic route—better for climbers wanting continuous climbing
Expect more height without terrain easing
Still well-protected granite throughout
watchOut: Loose rock and rockfall throughout
More sustained difficulty than classic South Buttress—plan for longer climbing time
Afternoon thunderstorms common
knownHazards: Loose rock and rockfall
Sustained climbing exposure
Afternoon lightning threat July-August
routes.comms: No reliable cell reception at summit or on peak.

blurb: Gunsight Peak (8,198 ft) is a prominent alpine summit in the remote Glacier Peak Wilderness, surrounded by Blue and Chickamin glaciers. The peak is composed of white granite and represents the highest point in the Gunsight Range, offering some of the finest granite climbing in the Washington Cascades.
hierarchyNote: Elevation correction needed: database lists 8,185 ft but all major sources (Wikipedia, Summitpost, Peakbagger, topographic maps) confirm elevation as 8,198 feet.

routes.overview: Alpine Class 4 scramble with technical rock crux (5.6) leading to summit via the west face and northwest ridge. Route ascends broken granite terrain with significant exposure and requires ice ax and crampons for glacier approach.
routes.beta: The standard line ascends the west face via a broken snow ramp and rock terrain, featuring a 5.6 technical crux in a steep corner section. The climb involves mixed alpine scrambling (Class 3-4) with exposed ridge traverse on solid granite, followed by chimney climbing to reach the tiny horn-shaped summit.
routes.approach: Access via Downey Creek Trailhead (elev. 1,450 ft) on Suiattle River Road (FSR 26) east of Darrington, WA. Follow Downey Creek Trail 6.5 miles to Bachelor Creek, then Bachelor Creek trail approximately 9.5 miles to creek crossing at 3,700 ft. Ascend steeply via switchbacks to Cub Lake Pass (~6,000 ft), descend to Cub Lake (5,300 ft), traverse heather and scree to Itswoot Ridge (~6,200 ft). Continue east across Dome Glacier northwest side to Dome-Chickamin Col (~8,500 ft), then traverse east across crevassed Chickamin Glacier to peak base. Alternative approach via Stehekin and PCT: ferry to High Bridge, follow PCT south ~9.7 miles, then off-trail through dense brush to high camp (~6,760 ft). Total approach: 25-35 miles, 2-4 days, 6,000-7,000 ft elevation gain.
routes.descent: Multiple double-rope rappels down the west face (4-6 rappels, approximately 50m, 50m, 50m, and 20m). Downclimb from summit to first rappel anchor below summit chimney. After reaching snow/glacier, downclimb and scramble back to snow finger and return via Chickamin Glacier.
routes.descentText: Descent requires rappel equipment and careful navigation. From summit, downclimb to the top of the main chimney system to access the first rappel anchor. Rappel down the west face using 50m pitches (quad-rapped) to reach a ledge above the glacier. Make a final short rappel (20m) to reach snow/ice. Downclimb and scramble back to the snow finger between Gunsight and South Gunsight Peak. Return via Chickamin Glacier, being extremely careful with crevasse fields and moat crossings. Glacier traversal requires belayed protection and careful route-finding to avoid hidden crevasses and bergschrunds.
routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the most stable weather and reduced snow hazards, though crevasse fields remain significant throughout the climbing season. Late June and early September are possible but present increased rockfall risk from melting ice and less-consolidated snow. June sees higher snow coverage and avalanche risk. September can have early storms but longer daylight hours.
objHaz: Moat formation (gap between glacier and rock face)
knownHazards: Chickamin Glacier is heavily crevassed with multiple large crevasse systems requiring careful route-finding and potential rope team techniques
Bergschrunds at glacier base typically require rappel setup for safe descent crossing
Moats (gaps between glacier and rock walls) can form or widen during warm weather and may prevent access to climbing pitches
Loose, slabby granite throughout face requires helmets and careful protection placement
Afternoon thunderstorms are common in peak season; high elevation exposure makes summit area dangerous in electrical storms
Remote wilderness location (2+ day approach) requires total self-sufficiency; evacuation is extremely difficult
watchOut: Do not attempt glacier crossing in unfamiliar conditions or heavy cloud cover; crevasse visibility is critical
Rope up for all glacier travel; many climbers have been caught off-guard by hidden crevasses in seemingly benign terrain
Check moat clearance and bergschrund conditions before committing to the climb; some seasons may have impassable gaps
Loose rock is prevalent; wear helmets and place protection carefully in early pitches where gear may be tricky
Plan to summit early (often before 10am) to avoid afternoon thunderstorms; descend quickly if weather deteriorates
Navigation off-trail from PCT or via brush bash approach requires excellent map reading and GPS use

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM from base camp (summit typically 11:00 AM-12:00 PM with 2-3 hours climbing/scrambling from glacier base)

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Downey Creek Trailhead, hike 6-7 miles to Bachelor Creek area, establish camp. Day 2: Follow Bachelor Creek trail to Cub Lake Pass area, hike to Itswoot Ridge, establish high camp (~6,000-6,200 ft). Day 3: Cross Dome Glacier to Dome-Chickamin Col (~8,500 ft), traverse Chickamin Glacier to peak base, establish glacier camp. Day 4: Alpine start (3-4 AM), approach glacier and crevasse field, simul-climb and belay through technical crux, summit and begin rappel descent, return to camp. Day 5: Retrace approach via Chickamin Glacier and descent route.
routes.bail: If conditions are unfavorable on summit day, retreat from glacier base after assessing crevasse and moat conditions (5-6 hour descent). If moat crossing is impassable, rappel setup or wait for conditions to change. From anywhere on climbing route, downclimb and rappel to snow/glacier. For medical emergency on approach, self-evacuate to Bachelor Meadows or lower camp and send for rescue via satellite communicator.
routes.turnaround: If you have not reached the base of the climbing pitch by 10:00 AM, descend immediately to base camp due to afternoon thunderstorm risk. If weather turns bad at any point, descend and seek shelter. Do not attempt glacier crossing in whiteout conditions.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades/Glacier Peak Wilderness zone (covered by Northwest Avalanche Center general forecast but no dedicated peak forecast)
climate.typical: Alpine weather with cool temperatures, significant wind exposure above 8,000 ft. Afternoon thunderstorms are common July-August. Early morning conditions are typically stable.
climate.summer: July-August average high temperatures 40-50°F at summit elevation, overnight lows 30-40°F. Afternoon thunderstorms common. Early July may have significant snow; late August sees more bare rock.
climate.spring: May-early June: heavy snowpack, avalanche hazard on approach. Crevasse coverage may obscure hazards. Not recommended.
climate.fall: September: morning frost, occasional snow flurries possible at summit. Early storms possible. Days shorten rapidly.
climate.winter: November-March: extreme avalanche hazard, massive snowpack, technical ice climbing, extreme cold. Approach nearly impossible and not recommended for standard route.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Darrington Ranger District
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (Northwest Recreation Pass) required for vehicle parking at Downey Creek Trailhead
access.fees: Recreation pass fee (typically $30/day or annual pass options available)
access.closures: Suiattle River Road (FSR 26) closed April 2, 2026 through January 1, 2028 due to flood damage; impassable at milepost 4.5. Check with Darrington Ranger District for updates. Alternative access via Stehekin and PCT available but adds 1-2 days.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (FSR 26)
road.status: CLOSED April 2, 2026-January 1, 2028 (flood damage at MP 4.5)
road.seasonalGate: Typically open July-September when weather allows
road.driveNote: From Darrington, WA, drive north 8 miles on Suiattle River Road to FSR 26, then east 20 miles toward Downey Creek Trailhead. Road is currently impassable past milepost 4.5 due to 70-foot washout from December 2025 flooding. Contact Darrington Ranger District (360) 436-1155 for current status and repair timeline. Alternative: Access via Stehekin ferry and Pacific Crest Trail.

gear: Full alpine rock rack (cams #0.3-2, nuts, quickdraws)
Slings (at least 3 dyneema, for anchors and protection)
Pickets (ice screws for glacier travel and anchors)
Glacier travel kit (cord, carabiners, rescue pulleys)
Crampons for dual-traction (smooth bottoms on rock approach)
routes.detailedRack: Core alpine rack: Cams #0.3, #0.5, #0.75, #1, #2 (one of each), 8-10 wired nuts from #1-6, 12-15 quickdraws. Belay anchors: 4+ cordelettes or slings rated for multi-pitch belay setup. Glacier travel: ice screws (17cm, x6), pickets (x4), dynamic cord. Rappel: Two 60m x 9mm alpine ropes, carabiners for rope management, three rappel devices for simul-descent, belay plates.
routes.proNeeds: Traditional alpine protection with emphasis on crack climbing. The early pitches require solid gear placements in granite cracks and flakes. Later sections rely on knots and anchor improvisation. Expect some third/fourth-class scrambling where protection is limited. Rock quality is generally good on the face but loose blocks are common, so secure placements are important.
proTips: Start very early (3-4 AM) from glacier base to avoid afternoon thunderstorms and maximize daylight for rope work
Scout the moat crossing and glacier approach the day before if possible; conditions can change overnight
Use a GPS or detailed topo map to navigate glacier crevasse field; many climbers report getting off-route in flat light
Rope team of two is feasible but three makes glacier travel safer and crux climbing more secure
Simul-climb the early Class 3-4 terrain to save time; place solid protection and move quickly
The 5.6 crux is short but exposed; practice efficient lead climbing and protection placement before attempting
Rappel anchors may require augmentation from natural features; bring slings and knots for anchor rigging
Consider posting someone on belay while others downclimb loose terrain to manage rockfall hazard
Have a bailout plan for moat crossing; if the gap is larger than 1m+ with no obvious crossing, descend rather than jump

emergency.county: Snohomish County (also portions of Chelan and Skagit counties)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff: 911 (emergency), non-emergency dispatch not easily reachable from wilderness. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach) strongly recommended.
emergency.rangerStation: Darrington Ranger District: (360) 436-1155, located in Darrington, WA. Station hours: 8:00 AM - 4:30 PM weekdays. Glacier Public Service Center: (360) 599-2714 (located on Baker Highway in Glacier, WA; open 8 AM - 4:30 PM daily).
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital in Arlington, WA (approximately 40 miles west of trailhead, ~60-90 min drive). Skagit Valley Hospital in Mount Vernon, WA also nearby. Providence Regional Medical Center Everett, Colby Campus in Everett, WA for trauma center access.
emergency.notes: Remote wilderness location requires complete self-rescue capability. Satellite communicator (InReach, SOS) is essential as cell service is nonexistent in the area. Helicopter rescue is available but extremely expensive and weather-dependent. Start hikes with filed itinerary and expected return time. Contact Snohomish County Volunteer Search and Rescue (SCVSAR) for non-emergency coordination.

routes.comms: No cellular service in the Glacier Peak Wilderness area. Satellite messaging devices (Garmin InReach, Apple Emergency SOS via satellite) recommended for emergency communication. Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) also recommended for remote alpine climbing. Radio communication is unreliable due to terrain.
routes.corrections: Elevation: Database lists 8,185 feet; sources consistently report 8,198 feet (confirmed on Wikipedia, Summitpost, Peakbagger, topographic maps). The peak was incorrectly labeled as 'Blue Mountain' on USGS maps but is correctly identified as Gunsight Peak. First ascent history of the standard route is not definitively documented in available sources; Fred Beckey made first ascent of North Gunsight Peak in 1940, not the main summit.

hierarchyNote: Lane Peak is located in the Tatoosh Range within Mount Rainier National Park (southern Cascades), not the North Cascades. The provided hierarchy placement appears geographically incorrect. Elevation discrepancy: data lists 6,035 feet; authoritative sources (Wikipedia, Peakbagger) confirm 6,012 feet (1,832 m). Coordinates: 46°45'29"N 121°45'11"W.
blurb: Lane Peak (6,012 feet) sits on the crest of the Tatoosh Range south of Mount Rainier in Mount Rainier National Park, Washington. The peak is named after U.S. Secretary of the Interior Franklin Knight Lane, who established the National Park Service in 1917. Known for three iconic north-facing couloirs offering spring alpine climbing with elevation gains up to 1,500 feet and slopes reaching 45 degrees.

routes.overview: The most popular north-facing couloir on Lane Peak, with moderate ice and mixed climbing over three distinct steps. Left-leaning line in the center of the north face visible from the road.
routes.beta: WI2 M2 couloir with mostly snow or ice at 40-45 degrees. Three main steps: first step approximately 15 feet (second most difficult), second step approximately 8 feet (easiest), final step approximately 25 feet (crux). Couloir narrows from 40 feet wide at base to 20 feet near exit. Boot pack typically established by other parties in season.
routes.approach: From Reflection Lakes pullout on Stevens Canyon Road (1.5 miles before Snow Lakes Trailhead), hike 2 miles with approximately 2,000 feet elevation gain. Walk briefly along Stevens Canyon Road, then drop into basin toward Tatoosh Creek. Climb up large debris triangle feature and angle left into couloir base. Approach takes approximately 1-1.5 hours from parking to couloir base.
routes.descent: From couloir exit at notch in east ridge, descend via south-facing slope or return down same couloir. Alternatively, descend to saddle between Lane and Denman Peaks and drop into basin below.
routes.descentText: Most efficient descent is via the south-facing slope from the couloir exit, though this requires careful route-finding in poor visibility. In winter/spring conditions with adequate snow, groups often descend the same couloir used for ascent or traverse to adjacent descent routes. Total descent takes 1-1.5 hours.
routes.bestSeason: Spring and early summer (April-June) provide optimal conditions when snow fills the couloirs without creating excessive wet slide hazard. Winter ascents possible in stable snow conditions, but requires careful avalanche assessment. Late spring offers most reliable snow quality.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (6-8am)

routes.detailedRack: Single ice axe per climber (50cm+ technical or straight shaft aluminum). Crampons rated for steep ice (12-point recommended for crux sections). Climbing helmet mandatory for rockfall protection. Boot crampons compatibility essential.
routes.proNeeds: Groups of 2-3 recommended for simpler approach and psychological comfort. Short rope (30-50m) may be used in exposed sections but most parties move unroped. Two-per-party ice axe usage sufficient; no double tooling typically required.
proTips: Start early to establish boot pack and ensure descent in daylight
North-facing aspect means shade even in spring; temperature extremes possible
The 25-foot crux step is significantly steeper than lower steps; pace yourself
Excellent spring conditions typically mid-April through June; June has more melt-out but also mush
Check avalanche forecast (NWAC) before attempting; north face receives sufficient wind loading
Mount Rainier park officials track conditions; check road status for Stevens Canyon access
Ski descent possible in spring but boot skiing recommended for technical sections
watchOut: Rockfall: helmets mandatory, especially in afternoon sun when rocks loosen
Avalanche hazard: the run-out zone at base has significant slope angle; stable snow crucial
Weather: rapid wind and weather shifts common; whiteout possible even in spring
South-facing descent slope: sun exposure creates instability; descend early to avoid afternoon melt
knownHazards: Rockfall from upper slopes, particularly in afternoon sun when snow sublimates around rocks
Avalanche terrain: run-out zone at base and initial steep approach both slide-prone in unstable conditions
Exposure: upper sections of couloir have 15-20 foot walls, fall would be serious
Cornices: can form at ridge exit in winter; use caution breaking over top
routes.bail: Descent via same couloir is always an option in deteriorating conditions. Route offers safe turn-around at couloir exit notch without committing to summit.
routes.turnaround: Safe turnaround is at couloir exit notch in east ridge. Do not commit to summit traverse if visibility is poor or party is running behind schedule. Post-noon descent of south-facing slope becomes increasingly hazardous.
routes.comms: Cellular service unreliable in Mount Rainier National Park terrain; satellite communicator recommended. Park ranger radio coverage exists but not accessible to public. Plan to be self-sufficient.

pitchDetail.notes: Approach couloir, 40-45 degrees, mostly snow with some ice sections. Approximately 300 vertical feet.
First step, approximately 15 feet of ice or mixed climbing, second most difficult section. Good protection opportunities in ice.
Second step, approximately 8 feet, easiest of the three pitches. Bridge snow or thin ice.
Crux section, approximately 25 feet to couloir exit. Steepest angle and technical climbing required. Protection can be marginal; ice quality variable.
Exit snow slope to ridge notch, 150-200 feet. Angle decreases but angle still steep (30-35 degrees). Transition from couloir to open slope.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road / Paradise Valley Road
road.status: Seasonal - typically open Memorial Day to mid-October
road.seasonalGate: Closed mid-October to Memorial Day due to snow
road.driveNote: From Nisqually Entrance, drive to Longmire (15 miles), then east on Stevens Canyon Road toward Reflection Lakes area. Road well-maintained when open. No chain requirements noted. Check park website for current status.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy precipitation. Paradise receives 500+ inches of annual precipitation, primarily as snow in winter.
climate.spring: Cool and wet, 35-45°F at 5,000 feet. April-May: snow conditions optimal for climbing, firm morning snow softening afternoons. Expect 1-2 inches new snow possible.
climate.summer: Cooler than lowlands, 55-65°F at 5,000 feet. Late June onward, melt-out is rapid; couloirs become more mixed rock/ice and less reliable.
climate.fall: Cool and wet, 40-55°F. September sees occasional stable snow early month. October: first major snows arrive, roads close.
climate.winter: Cold and very snowy, 20-35°F at 5,000 feet. December-March: heavy snow (300+ inches total), high avalanche hazard, short daylight.

access.landManager: Mount Rainier National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: Standard park entrance: $30 per vehicle (7-day pass) or America the Beautiful pass $80 annual. No additional day-use fees for non-technical climbing.
access.permit: Climbing permits required only for climbs above 10,000 feet or on glaciers. Lane Peak (6,012 feet) does not require climbing permit. Wilderness permits required for overnight camping ($12/person/night).
access.passRequired: Valid America the Beautiful pass or standard entrance fee required. Payment by card only at Nisqually Entrance (no cash).
access.closures: Stevens Canyon Road seasonal closure mid-October to Memorial Day. Check nps.gov/mora for current conditions. Road may close earlier than scheduled in major storms.

emergency.nearestHospital: Lewis County General Hospital (24/7 Emergency Department), Chehalis, WA (approximately 45 minutes from park entrance). Arbor Health-Morton Hospital also serves area.
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Lewis County Sheriff's Office: 911 for emergencies. Non-emergency: contact park ranger stations.
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier National Park Ranger Stations at Nisqually Entrance and Paradise area. Wilderness Information Center provides permit information and conditions updates.
emergency.notes: Rescue response capability limited by terrain and weather. Self-rescue and self-sufficiency assumed. Park has dedicated Search and Rescue team but response times may be several hours. Satphone or personal locator beacon recommended for high-risk parties.

routes.overview: The easiest of three major north-facing couloirs on Lane Peak, offering moderate snow climbing in a broad, well-protected gully. Suitable for less technical parties and good introduction to steep couloir climbing.
routes.beta: Broad couloir approximately 40-50 feet wide, predominantly snow with 45-degree angle. Easiest objective on the north face with fewer technical crux sections compared to The Zipper. Runout zone at base can be avalanche-prone, so stable snow conditions essential. Most efficient ascent follows the obvious broad line, trending slightly right as elevation gains.
routes.approach: Same as The Zipper: From Reflection Lakes pullout on Stevens Canyon Road (1.5 miles before Snow Lakes Trailhead), hike 2 miles with approximately 2,000 feet elevation gain. Walk briefly along Stevens Canyon Road, then drop into basin toward Tatoosh Creek. Climb up debris triangle and continue to base of couloir system. Identify The Fly as the rightmost/broadest of the three couloirs. Approach takes approximately 1.5-2 hours to couloir base.
routes.descent: Descend via same couloir (skied conditions possible in spring) or traverse left to The Zipper descent route if preferred. South-facing slope descent from ridge saddle also viable with care.
routes.descentText: Descent is typically via the same couloir used for ascent. In spring conditions with good snow coverage, skiing or smooth glissading possible. In harder or mixed conditions, boot descent with careful self-belay recommended. Transition from couloir to ridge saddle requires careful route-finding. Total descent 1.5-2 hours.
routes.bestSeason: Late April through early June optimal. The Fly maintains snow longer than exposed routes due to broad north-facing aspect and shaded position. Earlier spring (March-April) possible but avalanche hazard typically higher.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (6-8am)

routes.detailedRack: Single ice axe per climber (50cm technical ice axe or straight shaft). 10-point crampons adequate for most conditions; 12-point recommended if icy sections anticipated. Climbing helmet mandatory. Boot-crampons compatibility essential.
routes.proNeeds: Route generally soloed or two-person parties. No rope typically required given moderate angles and broad gully. Self-arrest practice essential for this terrain.
proTips: The Fly is recommended for climbers new to alpine couloir climbing due to broad gully and forgiving angle
Best snow conditions typically mid-April through May; June softens significantly
Identify couloir carefully from base - rightmost of three main gullies on north face
North exposure means cool temperatures all day; dress warmly even on sunny days
Ski descent possible and popular in spring; approach skin up can be efficient
Less crowded than The Zipper but generally tracks established and visible from distance
Excellent views of Mount Rainier's south face and entire Tatoosh Range from ridge saddle
watchOut: Avalanche hazard: runout zone at base is genuine slide terrain; check forecast carefully
Rockfall: boots and helmets protect against falling ice/rock from above and surrounding slopes
Wide couloir can concentrate wind; cold temperatures develop quickly with wind chill
Whiteout in poor visibility makes couloir identification and route-finding difficult
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain: run-out zone at couloir base has avalanched historically; stable snow conditions essential
Exposure: couloir walls are lower than on Zipper but still 10-15 feet, fall consequence serious
Weather: afternoon thunderstorms possible in summer; get off ridge by early afternoon
Snow quality: late spring melt creates variable conditions; early morning ascent captures best snow
routes.bail: Broad couloir offers good bail option by descending back down same route. No committing sections; parties can turn around at any point.
routes.turnaround: Safe turnaround throughout route, but recommend turning around if not at ridge notch by mid-afternoon. South-facing slopes above saddle become hazardous to traverse in afternoon sun.
routes.comms: Cellular service unreliable in Mount Rainier National Park terrain; satellite communicator recommended. Plan to be self-sufficient; relay information through ranger station if possible.

pitchDetail.notes: Broad approach snow slopes, 40-45 degrees, approximately 400-500 vertical feet. Mostly snow, occasional ice beneath. Well-established boot pack in season.
Main couloir, 45 degrees, approximately 600-700 vertical feet. Broad gully 40-50 feet wide provides psychological comfort. Most challenging part of route but still moderate.
Upper couloir to ridge saddle transition, angle decreases to 30-35 degrees, approximately 200-300 vertical feet. Good snow consistency, straightforward exit to ridge.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road / Paradise Valley Road
road.status: Seasonal - typically open Memorial Day to mid-October
road.seasonalGate: Closed mid-October to Memorial Day due to snow
road.driveNote: From Nisqually Entrance, drive to Longmire (15 miles), then east on Stevens Canyon Road toward Reflection Lakes area. Road well-maintained when open. Check park website for current road status before driving.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy precipitation. Paradise receives 500+ inches of annual precipitation, primarily as snow in winter.
climate.spring: Cool and wet, 35-45°F at 5,000 feet. April-May: snow conditions optimal for climbing, firm morning snow softening afternoons. Expect 1-2 inches new snow possible.
climate.summer: Cooler than lowlands, 55-65°F at 5,000 feet. Late June onward, melt-out is rapid; couloirs become more mixed rock/ice and less reliable.
climate.fall: Cool and wet, 40-55°F. September sees occasional stable snow early month. October: first major snows arrive, roads close.
climate.winter: Cold and very snowy, 20-35°F at 5,000 feet. December-March: heavy snow (300+ inches total), high avalanche hazard, short daylight.

access.landManager: Mount Rainier National Park (National Park Service)
access.fees: Standard park entrance: $30 per vehicle (7-day pass) or America the Beautiful pass $80 annual. No additional day-use fees for non-technical climbing.
access.permit: Climbing permits required only for climbs above 10,000 feet or on glaciers. Lane Peak (6,012 feet) does not require climbing permit. Wilderness permits required for overnight camping ($12/person/night).
access.passRequired: Valid America the Beautiful pass or standard entrance fee required. Payment by card only at Nisqually Entrance (no cash).
access.closures: Stevens Canyon Road seasonal closure mid-October to Memorial Day. Check nps.gov/mora for current conditions.

emergency.nearestHospital: Lewis County General Hospital (24/7 Emergency Department), Chehalis, WA (approximately 45 minutes from park entrance). Arbor Health-Morton Hospital also serves area.
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Lewis County Sheriff's Office: 911 for emergencies. Non-emergency: contact park ranger stations.
emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier National Park Ranger Stations at Nisqually Entrance and Paradise area. Wilderness Information Center provides permit information and conditions updates.
emergency.notes: Rescue response capability limited by terrain and weather. Self-rescue and self-sufficiency assumed. Park has dedicated Search and Rescue team but response times may be several hours.

blurb: Mount Custer (8,653 ft) stands in the far northern North Cascades National Park, about 1 mile south of the Canadian border. The peak is composed of oxidized volcanic breccia and is notoriously loose, earning the reputation as the "king of choss" in the North Cascades. The summit offers commanding views of Silver Lake, Mt. Spickard, the Mox Peaks, and Mt. Redoubt. First ascended in 1958 by Frank Dawe, Roy Mason, and Karl Teichman.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy noted: task data lists 8,653 ft, but multiple authoritative sources (Peakbagger, WTA, topographic maps) list 8,630 ft (2,630 m). Both refer to the same peak. Peak location (48.9891, -121.2497) in North Cascades National Park near Canadian border is confirmed accurate across multiple sources.

routes.fa: 1958 Frank Dawe, Roy Mason, Karl Teichman
routes.overview: The standard route ascends Custer's west slopes from Ouzel Lake camp to a SW basin at 7,700 ft, then continues to the 8,260-ft notch south of the summit before traversing the chossy South Ridge to the summit. A 2-day trip is recommended, though a car-to-car day ascent is possible.
routes.beta: This is the most frequently ascended route. Start from Ouzel Lake (~5,700 ft) and head eastward up the west slopes, which are semi-open initially and transition to alpine terrain around 6,200 ft. Minor cliffbands require Class 3 scrambling to navigate around. Climb to the obvious SW basin (~7,700 ft), continue to the 8,260-ft notch immediately south of the summit, then traverse the final chossy South Ridge to the summit. Descent is more technical than ascent and may require roped sections. The peak is known for exceptionally loose, fractured volcanic rock throughout.
routes.approach: Drive from Highway 1 via Exit 119A (Chilliwack/Sardis exit) in British Columbia. Follow Vedder Road south to Chilliwack Lake Road and drive ~40.5 km. Turn left onto narrow dirt Depot Creek Road (~200 yards before the bridge over Depot Creek). High-clearance 4WD vehicles can drive an additional 2.5 miles up Depot Creek Road to a large clearing parking area. Hike up Depot Creek valley on the old trail (may require bushwhacking through recently logged sections initially) for 8-9 miles with 3,500-6,000 ft elevation gain to reach Ouzel Lake (~5,700 ft), taking 5-6 hours. The trail passes a dramatic waterfall with steep, wet rock sections that may require careful scrambling or a short rope. From Ouzel Lake, the direct route to Mount Custer's summit is 2-3 hours.
routes.descent: Descend via the South Ridge, reversing the ascent route. The descent is significantly more technical than the ascent, especially in the upper section near the 8,260-ft notch and along the South Ridge. Approximately 30 vertical meters near the summit require extreme caution. Bring 20 m of rope to belay sections or for additional safety. Experienced parties may be able to descend without rope, but loose rock hazard is high. Choose a better traversing line on return to bypass gendarmes and bypass exposed terrain. The rock quality on descent demands helmets and careful footwork.
routes.descentText: Reverse the approach route via the South Ridge and SW basin back to Ouzel Lake. The technical crux occurs in the upper sections where careful navigation and possibly roped sections are needed to manage loose rock and steep terrain. Lower elevation slopes back to Ouzel Lake are less exposed but remain loose throughout. Total descent time is 2-3 hours to Ouzel Lake, then 5-6 hours back to the trailhead.
routes.bestSeason: May through the first significant snowfall of autumn (typically September or October). Outside this window, high snow and avalanche risk make ascent dangerous, particularly on the glaciated north side and approach terrain.
routes.proNeeds: The route is a scramble (Class 3-4) with no technical ice climbing required, though loose rock rockfall hazard is extreme. Standard alpine scrambling protection (helmet, careful footwork, possibly light rope for descent). No ice tools or crampons needed for standard summer conditions.
routes.detailedRack: Helmet (essential for rockfall protection from loose debris). 20 m single rope for potential rappel/belay sections on descent or for assisted downclimbing. Approach rope (20-30 ft) for potentially wet slabs on the Depot Creek waterfall section. Lightweight harness and sling for belay anchors. Trekking poles for approach trail.
whatToBring: Water treatment or plenty of water capacity
proTips: Ouzel Lake offers the best camping with flat, sandy, treeless benches at 5,700 ft
Consider climbing from Ouzel Lake as a day ascent to keep base camp weight minimal
The loose rock hazard is greatest in upper sections; helmets are essential at all times
Bring extra caution on descent where rockfall risk is highest; use rope liberally if available
Many parties prefer to start the summit push at dawn (around 6:00 AM) from Ouzel Lake camp to allow maximum daylight
If combining with neighboring peaks, the Depot Creek-Silver Lake saddle (7,380 ft, 2 hours from Ouzel Lake) provides a higher camp for Mt. Rahm and Mt. Spickard
Volcanic breccia rock is particularly unstable when wet; plan accordingly for weather
knownHazards: Loose volcanic breccia rock throughout the ascent and especially descent—consistently reported as the most serious hazard
Rockfall from unstable terrain above, particularly in the upper South Ridge section
Avalanche hazard from Custer Glacier and Maselpanik Glacier on north side in heavy snow years
Steep, wet rock sections on the Depot Creek waterfall approach
Exposure to afternoon storms and lightning in exposed upper terrain
Difficult route finding in bad visibility on the SW basin approach
watchOut: Mount Custer is notably the 'king of choss' in the North Cascades—the rock quality is exceptionally poor and loose throughout
Rockfall is a serious hazard in upper sections; always wear helmet and consider climbing early or during stable weather
Descent is significantly more dangerous than ascent due to loose terrain and rockfall risk
The 30-meter section near the 8,260-ft notch demands extreme attention and possibly rope assistance
Wet conditions on the Depot Creek approach can make the waterfall crossing hazardous; exercise extra caution in rain or after heavy precipitation

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach: Depot Creek valley trail to Ouzel Lake
pitchDetail.notes: 8-9 miles, 3,500-6,000 ft elevation gain, 5-6 hours. Trail follows old valley, passes steep waterfall section with wet rock requiring careful scrambling or rope assist.

pitchDetail.pitch: Ouzel Lake to SW basin (7,700 ft)
pitchDetail.notes: Semi-open slopes transitioning to alpine around 6,200 ft. Class 2-3 scrambling with minor cliffbands to negotiate. Approximately 2,000 ft elevation gain, 1-2 hours. Rock is very loose throughout.
Continued Class 3 scrambling on unstable volcanic breccia. Route-finding is straightforward but loose rock is a constant hazard. Approximately 560 ft gain, 1 hour.

pitchDetail.pitch: 8,260-ft notch to summit via South Ridge
pitchDetail.notes: Final Class 3-4 scramble on notably chossy South Ridge. About 390 ft gain, 30-45 minutes. This section demands helmets and careful foot placement. Descent of this section is the crux and may require rope.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (5:30-6:00 AM) from Ouzel Lake camp to maximize daylight for the technical descent sections

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required from North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades (NWAC operates forecasts for this region)
climate.typical: Alpine tundra with exposed ridges. Highly exposed to afternoon storms and rapid weather changes.
climate.spring: High avalanche hazard from snowpack on glaciers and steep terrain. Snow extends to well above Ouzel Lake elevation.
climate.summer: May-September: most stable conditions. July-August typically driest. Afternoon thunderstorms common.
climate.fall: September-October: increasing snow. High avalanche risk as new snow loads accumulate. First significant snowfall typically ends climbing season.
climate.winter: November-April: Extremely high avalanche hazard. Peak buried under 10+ feet of snow. Route is not recommended.

routes.comms: Cell service is extremely limited to nonexistent in the Depot Creek area. Satellite communication devices (Garmin InReach, PLB) recommended for emergency communication from the backcountry.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (WA) / British Columbia (approach via Canada)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park (main): (360) 856-5700 ext 515. After-hours emergency: (360) 873-4590 ext 37. Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount): (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (approximately 2.5 hours drive from Highway 1 exit). Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA also available. Canadian hospitals in Chilliwack available closer to trailhead but would require helicopter evacuation likely from the peak.
emergency.notes: Emergency evacuation from the peak is very difficult due to remote location and no road access to trailhead. Helicopter rescue would likely involve evacuation to Skagit Valley Hospital. Self-rescue or evacuation to Ouzel Lake then walking out is most realistic scenario. Satellite communication is essential.

road.name: Chilliwack Lake Forest Service Road and Depot Creek Road
road.status: Passable for high-clearance 4WD vehicles only. Depot Creek Road is rough and overgrown in sections.
road.driveNote: From Highway 1 in British Columbia: Take Exit 119A (Chilliwack/Sardis exit). Drive south ~40.5 km on Chilliwack Lake Road. Turn left onto narrow dirt Depot Creek Road (unsigned, located ~200 yards before bridge over Depot Creek). Continue 2.5 miles to large clearing parking area. Total driving time from Highway 1: ~1.5 hours. Road is in British Columbia.

routes.corrections: Elevation discrepancy: USGS and task data list 8,653 ft; multiple sources (Peakbagger, WTA, topographic maps) list 8,630 ft (2,630 m). Both refer to identical peak location. The true elevation likely falls between these values. Coordinates remain consistent (48.9891, -121.2497).

routes.routeName: Southeast Route via Hurricane Ridge / Cat Basin
routes.bestSeason: July through September offer the best climbing conditions. Earlier than mid-July, the route experiences heavy snow coverage, particularly on the Catwalk ridge, making travel difficult and requiring ice axe and crampons. By mid-July through mid-September, snow conditions improve significantly and the route is generally in good shape.
routes.overview: The Southeast Route ascends Mount Carrie via the High Divide Trail and the exposed Catwalk ridge (Class 3) to reach the southwest ridge summit approach, combining alpine scrambling with significant exposure on a narrow ridge.
routes.beta: This route gains the High Divide crest from Heart Lake, then traverses south along the High Divide for 3 miles before ascending the Catwalk—an infamous, exposed ridge-top connecting Cat Peak to Mount Carrie. The Catwalk features high Class 2 to low Class 3 scrambling with substantial exposure; a fall here can be fatal. Beyond the Catwalk, the route follows the southwest ridge of Mount Carrie, initially grassy but becoming a mixture of grass and rock outcroppings at higher elevations, traversing scree and possible snow to the true summit at the far right of the summit basin.
routes.approach: From Port Angeles, drive 28 miles west on US 101 to Sol Duc Hot Springs Road. Continue 12.2 miles to Sol Duc Trailhead road-end. Hike the Sol Duc River Trail 7.85 miles to Heart Lake (4,744 ft). From Heart Lake, ascend steep, open slopes to High Divide crest (5,042 ft) at 8.3 miles total. Continue south along High Divide 3 miles to the Catwalk—the exposed ridge-top section connecting Cat Peak to Mount Carrie. Boston Charlie's Camp is located roughly 14 miles into the approach.
routes.descent: Reverse the route back across the Catwalk and High Divide trail to the trailhead. The descent follows the same scrambling terrain in reverse; extreme care required on the exposed Catwalk ridge.
routes.descentText: From the summit basin, descend southwest and traverse scree back to the Catwalk. The exposed ridge requires hands for stability and careful foot placement due to loose rock and exposure. Once across the Catwalk, descend the High Divide Trail northward back to Heart Lake, then follow the Sol Duc River Trail downstream to the trailhead.
knownHazards: Catwalk ridge: high Class 2 to low Class 3 exposure—a fall is potentially fatal
Loose rock and rockfall from crowded sections; helmet essential
Snow and ice conditions early season (before mid-July) make ridge travel very hazardous
Slushy, unstable snow on steep slopes when conditions are marginal
Mosquitoes can be surprisingly aggressive at sunrise and sunset
Trail disappears mid-route in one section (mile 6.6–6.8) due to earthquake damage
objHaz: Exposure and fall risk on Catwalk ridge
watchOut: The Catwalk is the crux of the climb—treat it with respect and expect high exposure.
Helmet is mandatory; rockfall is frequent from loose, unstable rock.
Before mid-July, expect deep snow on the Catwalk; ice axe and crampons required.
Wildlife encounter risk; black bears present in the area.
Cell phone coverage is unreliable; carry satellite communication device for emergencies.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning from camp to ensure daylight for Catwalk crossing

gear: Ice axe (essential June–July, advisable through August)
Crampons (essential June–July, may be optional August–September)
routes.detailedRack: A lightweight alpine rack for scrambling: ice axe, crampons, helmet, rope (30–50m rope optional for group safety on Catwalk), and self-rescue equipment. Harness and sling recommended if roping. For water-crossing sections, consider trekking poles for stream fords.
routes.proNeeds: Grade Class 2–3 scrambling protection: helmet mandatory for rockfall hazard, ice axe essential for early-season snow and self-arrest, crampons for frozen surfaces June–July. Rope carries psychological security on the Catwalk but is not strictly required for individuals with solid scrambling skills and comfort with exposure; recommended for groups.
whatToBring: Layers (high-altitude, wet Pacific weather is common)
High-calorie food and electrolyte drink mix
First aid kit with blister treatment
proTips: Start early from Boston Charlie's Camp to ensure you cross the Catwalk in daylight and have time to retreat if weather deteriorates.
Scout the Catwalk carefully in marginal light; the exposure is real and requires full attention.
The loose rock (choss) is typical of the Olympics—expect to pick your way carefully and wear a helmet.
Descending is often more hazardous than ascending on scrambles; move methodically on the way down.
Cache water bottles at Boston Charlie's to avoid carrying excessive weight; melt snow or find streams at higher elevations.
Wildlife is active at dawn and dusk; make noise while traveling.
routes.bail: If weather or snow conditions deteriorate before the Catwalk, descend back to High Divide and retreat to Heart Lake. If caught on the Catwalk in poor visibility, descend carefully and deliberately—do not rush. Retreat is always an option; the mountain will be there another day.
routes.turnaround: If you haven't summited by mid-afternoon, descend immediately. The Catwalk is dangerous in low light or deteriorating weather, and darkness falling during descent is a real hazard. Safe turnaround time depends on seasonal daylight and weather forecast.

climate.typical: Maritime west coast climate; frequent precipitation, especially autumn and winter. Summers are relatively dry but still prone to afternoon thunderstorms. Wind is common at ridge elevation.
climate.spring: High avalanche risk from heavy spring snowfall; route often impassable until late June.
climate.summer: July–August is the dry season with mild daytime temps (50–60°F at summit), but cold at night. September can bring early storms.
climate.fall: Rapid weather deterioration; frequent storms and early snow.
climate.winter: Deep snow and severe conditions make the route extremely hazardous; generally not climbed.

routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is unreliable throughout Olympic National Park. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach or similar) is strongly recommended. Emergency contact via sat messenger: dial 911 or local ranger station once you return to coverage area.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Olympic National Park)
access.closures: Backcountry areas subject to seasonal closures and weather-related restrictions. Check NPS website and ranger station before departure.

road.name: Sol Duc Hot Springs Road
road.status: Open seasonally; winter closures possible
road.seasonalGate: Generally open April–November; check NPS conditions
road.driveNote: From Port Angeles, drive west on US 101 approximately 28 miles. Turn south on Sol Duc Hot Springs Road and continue 12.2 miles to trailhead road-end. Road can be narrow and winding; allow 1–1.5 hours from Port Angeles.

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Visitor Center (Port Angeles) or ranger patrols; contact NPS Dispatch
emergency.nearestHospital: Olympic Medical Center, Port Angeles (939 Caroline St, 360-417-7000); ~45 minutes drive from trailhead
emergency.notes: Olympic National Park has active SAR; US Coast Guard MH-65 helicopter based at Port Angeles Air Station can respond to emergencies. Satellite communicator essential due to poor cell coverage.

routes.bestSeason: July through September are optimal. Carrie Glacier is accessible from mid-July onward when early-season conditions stabilize. July requires full glacier travel gear (crampons, ice axe); August and September may see reduced snow cover but demand vigilance for crevasse hazards.
routes.overview: The Standard Route ascends Mount Carrie via glacier travel on Carrie Glacier (northeast cirque) in conjunction with high-divide approach, combining alpine scrambling with glacier negotiation and crevasse rescue capability.
routes.beta: Approach via High Divide Trail to Boston Charlie's Camp. From camp, this glacier variation gains the northeast cirque and Carrie Glacier (6,800 ft) rather than the direct southwest ridge scramble. Carrie Glacier descends from ~6,800 ft to ~5,000 ft, flowing north-northeast in a chute confined by an arête and Mount Fairchild. The route requires rope team travel with crevasse rescue equipment, probing for hidden crevasses, and awareness of seracs and icefall hazards. Summit approach similar to the SE route, but entry via glacier instead of exposed Catwalk.
routes.approach: Same as Southeast Route to Boston Charlie's Camp (14 miles, ~8.2 hrs). From camp, ascend northeast toward Carrie Glacier cirque. Glacier ice begins around 6,000 ft. Rope up and probe carefully; crevasses can be concealed by snow bridges. Ascend toward the summit of Mount Carrie via the glacier system, moving as a roped team.
routes.descent: Reverse rope-team glacier descent, using ice axe arrests and anchor placement for security. Once off glacier, follow the high-divide approach back downstream to Heart Lake and Sol Duc Trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descend the glacier as a rope team, moving deliberately with probing and ice axe management. Exiting the glacier, descend to High Divide Trail and reverse the approach via Heart Lake back to Sol Duc Trailhead. Glacier descent is often slower and more technically demanding than ascent; allow adequate daylight.
knownHazards: Crevasses on Carrie Glacier (often concealed by snow bridges)
Seracs and icefall from steep upper glacier sections
Glacier retreat and changing route conditions year to year
Avalanche potential on glacier flanks (steep walls)
Early-season snow cover (before mid-July) obscures crevasse hazards
Frost and rime ice make cramponing difficult; ice axe essential
watchOut: Rope team travel is mandatory; never solo glacier travel.
Probe constantly for hidden crevasses, especially where the glacier appears continuous.
Listen for serac activity; move through avalanche terrain quickly.
Crampons should be sharp and well-maintained; carry ice tools (ice axe and crampons).
Watch for changing route conditions; crevasses enlarge and shift as summer progresses.

timing.recommendedStart: Very early morning from camp to allow time for rope-team deliberate movement and potential crevasse rescue.

gear: Rope (50 m minimum, 60 m recommended for glacier travel)
routes.detailedRack: Full crevasse-rescue rack: 50–60 m rope (static or dynamic), harness, belay/rappel device (ATC Guide or similar), minimum 4 locking carabiners per person, slings (4–5 per person), Prusik cords (3 per person), glacier probe, ice axe, crampons, and helmet. Anchor hardware: minimal pitons or ice screws for emergency rope anchors. Mechanical-advantage pulleys for crevasse extraction.
routes.proNeeds: Glacier travel requires: ice axe proficiency (self-arrest practice mandatory), crampons technique, rope management, crevasse rescue skills (raising systems, z-pulley, mechanical advantage). All team members must be competent in these skills or trained by a guide. Helmet protects against falling ice and impacts.
whatToBring: Mountaineering boots (insulated, Vibram sole for crampons)
Insulated hat, gloves (avoid mittens for rope work), and sunglasses
proTips: Carry a lightweight probe pole or avalanche probe for constant crevasse checking; time spent probing is safety invested.
Practice self-rescue and crevasse extraction before the climb; know how to set anchors and execute mechanical-advantage raises.
Move as a tight rope team on the glacier; space of 2–3 meters between climbers optimizes crevasse-rescue ability if someone breaks through.
Place ice-axe and crampons anchors solidly; test them before trusting body weight.
Glacier conditions vary dramatically year to year; get current beta from climbers who went recently.
In June and early July, plan for full rope-team movement; late August/September may allow shorter pitches or short-roping.
routes.bail: If crevasses are too extensive or concealed, or if a team member feels unsafe, descend back to High Divide Trail and exit via Heart Lake. Glacier crevasse rescue is demanding; if a team member falls and rescue is unsuccessful, descend immediately and call for professional rescue.
routes.turnaround: Mandatory turnaround time: early afternoon. Glacier descent is slower and more hazardous than ascent; you must have daylight and clear visibility for safe rope management and crevasse detection. If you haven't summited by 2 PM, descend.

climate.typical: Maritime Pacific climate with frequent weather changes. Glaciers experience daily freeze-thaw cycles in summer, creating rime and firm snow.
climate.spring: Spring snowfall masks crevasses; route closure until mid-July is typical.
climate.summer: Daytime temps 40–55°F at glacier elevation; sunny days soften glacier surface and increase avalanche hazard on flanks. Early mornings (before 9 AM) are safest for glacier travel.
climate.fall: Early storms and snow increase crevasse hazard and avalanche risk.
climate.winter: Route not recommended; deep snow, poor visibility, and severe conditions.

routes.comms: Satellite communicator is mandatory on glacier travel. Cell coverage is nonexistent in the backcountry. Carry personal locator beacon (PLB) for serious emergencies.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Olympic National Park)
access.closures: Backcountry subject to seasonal closures; check NPS for current conditions.

road.name: Sol Duc Hot Springs Road
road.status: Open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Generally open April–November
road.driveNote: From Port Angeles, 28 miles west on US 101; then 12.2 miles south on Sol Duc Hot Springs Road to trailhead.

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Visitor Center, Port Angeles
emergency.nearestHospital: Olympic Medical Center, Port Angeles (939 Caroline St, 360-417-7000)
emergency.notes: NPS SAR and US Coast Guard helicopter rescue available. Sat communicator mandatory for emergency notification. Crevasse rescue is specialized; helicopter extraction is likely only option for serious injury.

blurb: Mount Carrie (6,995 ft) is the highest peak in the Bailey Range subrange of the Olympic Mountains. Named in 1899 by surveyor Theodore Rixon for his wife Caroline "Carrie" Jones Rixon, the peak is characterized by significant glaciation with Carrie Glacier in the northeast cirque and Fairchild Glacier on the east slope. Both glaciers descend from ~6,800 ft to ~5,000 ft. The peak remains remote and rarely visited, requiring multi-day backpacking through Olympic National Park wilderness.
hierarchyNote: Minor elevation discrepancy: given data lists 6,999 ft; USGS/multiple sources confirm 6,995 ft (2,132 m). Peak identity and geographic hierarchy are correct: Bailey Range subrange of Olympic Mountains, Olympic National Park, Clallam County, Washington.

routes.fa: 1963, first ascent by Denny Pruitt, Dan Baker, Jack Christiansen, and Roy Etten
routes.bestSeason: July through September when alpine meadows are snow-free, days are longest, and weather windows are most reliable. Avoid earlier when steep snow traverses remain hazardous.
routes.overview: The standard mountaineering approach to Mount Fairchild involves glacier travel on the Carrie or Fairchild Glacier combined with moderate Class 2-3 rock scrambling on the southwest ridge. This is a remote alpine traverse requiring solid glacier travel skills and crevasse rescue capability.
routes.beta: Mount Fairchild is the second-highest peak in the Bailey Range and is accessed via two primary routes: (1) the longer High Divide approach via Sol Duc, or (2) via Mount Carrie. Both routes involve significant glacier travel with crevasse hazards and require proper alpine mountaineering skills. The peak stands at the head of Fairchild Creek with the Fairchild Glacier on its east slope.
routes.approach: Via Mount Carrie: Ascend from Sol Duc trailhead via High Divide loop to Mount Carrie summit (6,995 ft). From Mount Carrie, descend northeast down the Carrie Glacier, circumnavigate to the north and west of Mount Fairchild, and traverse to the base of the southwest ridge. Alternatively, approach via Mount Fitzhenry using the Elwha River drainage, ascending 5,000 feet through brush, forest, and rocky ridges to Fitzhenry summit, then traverse to Fairchild Glacier base.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent down the southwest ridge to glacier base. Descend the Carrie or Fairchild Glacier, exercising extreme caution with crevasse hazards. Return via same approach.
routes.descentText: Carefully retrace your ascent path down the southwest ridge using careful footwork on Class 2-3 terrain. The glacier descent is the crux due to crevasse hazards—use proper rope team techniques and maintain constant awareness of hidden crevasse dangers. Late summer conditions show more exposed blue ice but less hidden snow bridges. Descend via the same route back to camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Sol Duc trailhead to High Divide area (8-10 hours from parking). Day 2: High Divide to Mount Carrie summit via Catwalk and alpine ridge (4-6 hours). Day 3: Mount Carrie to Mount Fairchild via Carrie Glacier traverse and southwest ridge ascent (4-5 hours). Day 4: Descend to base camp (4-5 hours). Day 5: Return to Sol Duc trailhead via High Divide (8-10 hours).
routes.turnaround: Evaluate conditions throughout. Turn around if crevasse rescue is not within your group's capability, if weather deteriorates beyond the ability to navigate safely, if any team member shows signs of altitude stress or exhaustion, or if daylight will not permit safe descent before dark.
