routes.bail: Crevasse fall rescue by trained team members is the primary self-rescue method. If rescue is not possible, descend to known cairned areas and signal for helicopter rescue via satellite communicator (no cell service). Retreat to Mount Carrie and descend via known High Divide route if unable to proceed on Fairchild.
routes.proNeeds: Rope team glacier travel: minimum 30m rope per two-person team, ice screws (minimal use—crevasse rescue is primary concern), slings, carabiners, belay devices. Crevasse rescue kit: mechanical advantage system (tibloc, microtraxion, pulley), multiple slings, cordelette.
routes.detailedRack: Single 30m 6mm static rope per rope team. Glacier rack: 2-3 ice screws (rarely needed), 4-6 slings (6mm and 1-inch tubular), 2 locking carabiners, crevasse rescue kit components (tibloc, microtraxion, pulley). Approach: approach shoes or trail runners work better than heavy boots for 24+ mile approach.
whatToBring: Approach shoes or trail runners (lighter than mountaineering boots)
Water bottles or hydration reservoir (3-4 liters capacity)
Food and energy gels (high calorie for 8-10 hour approach days)
Sun protection: goggles, sunscreen, lip balm
Insulating and waterproof layers (microfleece, hard shell)
First aid kit with blister treatment
Personal locator beacon (PLB) or satellite communicator
Ziplock bags for waste (pack it out)
watchOut: Crevasse hazards on Carrie and Fairchild Glaciers—hidden crevasses near cliff bands, especially between 6,200-6,400 ft elevation
Exposure to steep gullies and rockfall on loose sandstone and pillow basalt typical of the Olympics
Weather deterioration is rapid—afternoon thunderstorms and wind can create whiteout conditions in minutes
Late-season cornices on ridges may create dangerous collapse zones
The Catwalk on the High Divide route between Cat Peak and Mount Carrie has exposure and loose rock
knownHazards: Glacier travel in the Bailey Range requires ice axe, crampons, and rope skills; crevasses up to 45 meters deep are common
The Carrie Glacier splits at a cliff band with part plunging over a rockwall—route-finding is critical to avoid steep ice fields
Rockfall is endemic to Olympic peaks due to fractured, chossy sandstone and pillow basalt; helmets are essential
Loose terrain and post-glacial scree dominate approach routes; careful footwork is needed throughout
No emergency services within reasonable response time; self-sufficiency is mandatory
Glacier retreat has exposed bergschrunds and steepened the final ice slopes; conditions change annually
proTips: Start before dawn from Sol Duc to maximize daylight on the alpine section; 4-5 hour descent in daylight is safer
Carrie Glacier splits at a cliff; stay left and contour well below the cliff band to avoid a dead end
Fairchild Glacier is smaller and steeper than Carrie; mid-summer often has exposed blue ice but fewer hidden crevasses—negotiate carefully
Camps near Bear Pass offer views of Mount Fairchild and are preferred for Mount Carrie/Fairchild summits
Strap-on crampons work better than climbing boots on the 24+ mile approach; trail runners + strap crampons are faster and lighter than full mountaineering setups
Bring a sat communicator; cell service is nonexistent in the Bailey Range
June approaches are significantly more dangerous due to steep snow slopes; mid-July onwards glacier travel is safer
routes.comms: No cell service anywhere in the Bailey Range or Olympic alpine backcountry. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach or similar) essential for emergency communication. Contact Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center at (360) 565-3100 before trip to file itinerary.

timing.recommendedStart: Depart Sol Duc trailhead before dawn for High Divide approach; at-altitude camp preferred

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy Pacific precipitation. Alpine zone (6,000+ ft) is cooler and windier.
climate.spring: Snow depths 6-15 feet at alpine elevations. Steep snow traverses dangerous. Crevasses often hidden. Not recommended.
climate.summer: July-September optimal. Highs near 60-75F at base, 40-60F at alpine elevations. Lows 45-55F. Minimal precipitation (0.8 in. July). Long daylight (16+ hours in July). Glaciers show more exposed blue ice and fewer snow bridges late summer.
climate.fall: September transitions rapidly to winter conditions. Snow can fall at alpine elevations as early as late August. Weather windows narrow after Labor Day.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and extreme cold. Not viable for mountaineers without specialized winter skills and equipment. Avalanche hazard significant.
climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Puget Sound/Olympic Region; Olympic National Park microclimates vary by elevation and drainage

access.landManager: National Park Service, Olympic National Park
access.permit: Wilderness permit required for all overnight stays
access.fees: $8 per adult (16+) per night; youth 15 and under free; $6 non-refundable reservation fee
access.closures: No year-round closures for mountaineering; contact ranger station for current conditions

road.name: Sol Duc Hot Springs Road (Forest Road 6010)
road.status: Open late spring through early fall; winter closures possible
road.seasonalGate: Sol Duc Road gate typically opens late April, closes by November depending on snow
road.driveNote: Drive US-101 west from Lake Crescent 2 miles to Sol Duc Hot Springs Road; turn left and drive 14.2 miles to road-end at Sol Duc Ranger Station and parking area (elevation 2,000 ft). Parking lot often full in summer; arrive early.

waypoints.name: Sol Duc Trailhead / Sol Duc Ranger Station

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Clallam County Sheriff (911 or satellite communicator from wilderness)
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Park Wilderness Information Center (360) 565-3100; Hoh Ranger Station (360) 962-2283
emergency.nearestHospital: Olympic Medical Center, Port Angeles (45+ miles; helicopter evacuation likely required)
emergency.notes: No ranger stations on the Bailey Range. Emergency response time is 12+ hours minimum via helicopter. Self-rescue or satellite PLB activation is essential.

blurb: Mount Fairchild is a 6,900-foot glaciated peak and the second-highest summit in the Bailey Range of Olympic National Park's Olympic Mountains. The peak is notable as the head of Fairchild Creek and home to the Fairchild Glacier; it was named for pilot William R. Fairchild (1926–1969), who flew hundreds of photographic missions documenting glaciers for scientific research. The first ascent was achieved in 1963 by Denny Pruitt, Dan Baker, Jack Christiansen, and Roy Etten.

routes.overview: The West Ridge traverses from Mount Fury's East Peak across exposed terrain with three prominent towers to reach one of Washington's most remote alpine summits, involving hours of technical scrambling and Class 5 rock climbing on granite.
routes.beta: The West Peak route starts from the East Peak via the Southeast Glacier approach. The traverse between summits is a wild and fun scramble with decent exposure and more technical difficulty than the East Peak ascent. Three distinct towers require climbing up and over; the rock quality on these sections is generally sound granite. The route involves low Class 5 rock climbing up to 5.4, though many climbers solo the final sections. A 50-60 meter rope is recommended for rappelling 4th and 5th class terrain.
routes.approach: Start at Ross Dam with a water taxi to Big Beaver Campground. Follow the Big Beaver Trail approximately 11 miles to near Big Beaver Camp, then leave the trail and cross Big Beaver Creek via log jam. Scramble up through Access Creek Basin following flagged climber's paths through semi-open forest with heather slope sections. Continue cross-country approximately 1-2 hours through reasonable hiking to reach Luna Col Camp at approximately 7,200 feet. Allow Day 1 for approach and camp, Day 2 for East Fury climb and West Peak attempt, Day 3 for descent. Total round-trip distance approximately 40 miles over 3-4 days.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route, reversing the East Peak scramble and glacier traverse. Rappel 50-60 meter rope down 4th and low 5th class sections encountered during descent. The descent involves exposed 4th class slabs; several descent variations exist but most involve working down loose Class 2-3 boulders to the saddle between peaks, then reversing glacier and scramble terrain to base camp.
routes.descentText: From West Peak summit, carefully reverse the traverse across the three towers, which can be more difficult descending than ascending due to exposed nature of terrain. Downclimb low 5th class granite sections to reach the saddle between East and West peaks. Use rope protection and rappels on exposed 4th class slabs as needed (typically 1-2 rappels required). Traverse back across the southeast glacier to reach the col, using ice axe and crampons. Follow the flagged climber's paths back through Access Creek Basin to Big Beaver Trail, taking care on loose sections. Allow approximately 4-5 hours for the descent from West Peak to camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Ross Dam water taxi to Big Beaver Campground, hike Big Beaver Trail 11 miles to Access Creek area, scramble cross-country to Luna Col Camp at 7,200 feet. Day 2: Climb East Peak via Southeast Glacier (approximately 5 hours from camp), traverse to West Peak summit (approximately 2.5 hours), return to East Peak and descend toward camp (approximately 4 hours from West Peak). Day 3: Return to Big Beaver Campground, arrange water taxi back to Ross Dam (hike approximately 7 hours).
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate or climber fitness is insufficient, retreat to East Peak summit and retrace route back to camp. The peak is remote with limited escape options; weather changes rapidly and climbers should be prepared to descend in poor visibility. Crevasse rescue capability is essential; if crevasse accident occurs, retreat via Access Creek to seek ranger assistance.
routes.turnaround: Climbers should aim for an early morning departure (6-7 AM) from camp to ensure sufficient daylight for the approach to West Peak. Plan to begin descent from West Peak no later than 2-3 PM to allow adequate time for technical downclimbing before darkness. Summer provides extended daylight (dawn ~5 AM, dusk ~10 PM in August) but weather deterioration can occur rapidly; monitor conditions continuously.
routes.bestSeason: The climbing season for Mount Fury West Peak is extremely limited, typically July through early September when snow melt permits glacier travel. Mid-July through late August represents the optimal window with longest daylight hours (16+ hours) and most stable weather patterns. Early July typically has excess snow requiring skis or microspikes; September faces increasing storm probability and early season snow. Most successful ascents occur in July or August during stable high-pressure systems.
routes.comms: Cell phone reception is unreliable or nonexistent in the North Cascades backcountry at Mount Fury's elevation. Satellite messengers (Garmin InReach or similar) are strongly recommended for this remote location. No fixed communication infrastructure exists. Emergency situations require hiking out to Big Beaver area or signaling to park rangers.
proTips: Three towers on the traverse can be bypassed on the left (east) side if the direct route proves too exposed; scout your line carefully
Bring extra rap slings and quicklinks in case existing anchor systems are missing or damaged
The granite rock quality is generally very good; protection placements are usually straightforward on technical sections
Most parties solo the final sections above the third tower; move quickly and confidently on the low 5th class to minimize time exposure
Late July and August offer the longest daylight hours; July starts may encounter more snow and avalanche hazard
watchOut: The route involves significant exposure throughout the traverse; a fall would likely be fatal; full protective systems and self-rescue capability essential
Loose rock is common on the descent; alert parties below before dislodging stones
Crevasses exist on the Southeast Glacier approach; never travel alone and always maintain rope team integrity
The hanging ice cliff above the lower glacier section requires quick movement; do not linger in hazard zone
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; thunderstorms are common in afternoon; descend early to avoid lightning exposure
knownHazards: One of Washington's 'Difficult 10' hardest peaks with fewer than 20 successful ascents as of 2016
Extreme remoteness: 40+ miles from trailhead with multi-day commitment makes rescue extremely difficult
Three prominent towers with exposure create navigation challenges and add psychological difficulty
Complex terrain and scrambling require advanced alpine skills and good judgment
High avalanche potential on glacier sections during wet spring or after new snow
gear: 50-60 meter climbing rope (double preferred for rappels)
Rock climbing rack: nuts, cams, pitons
whatToBring: Winter hat and gloves (essential even in July)
First aid kit with blister treatment
Stove and fuel for camp cooking
Lightweight bivy sack or emergency shelter
Map and compass (GPS unreliable at times)
routes.detailedRack: For the Class 5 sections on the West Peak traverse, bring a pared-down rack: 2-3 carabiners of nuts (brass and aluminum small to medium), 3-4 cams in 0.5 inch to 2 inch range (emphasize smaller sizes), a few pitons for solid aid and anchor backup, 2-3 slings for equalization and protection anchors. Most of the route is scrambling-protected terrain; only the three tower cruxes require full protection. The upper sections alternate between free climbing and scrambling, making a minimal approach essential for speed and weight management.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are moderate for the Class 5 pitches; most experienced climbers can protect themselves with basic anchors in the granite. Pitons are primarily useful for backup anchors on sketchy sections, though modern climbers typically use cams and nuts. The low angle and moderate protection requirements mean self-rescue capability is more critical than full pro redundancy; if ground-up climbing is necessary, climbers must have experience with trad anchors in granite.

climate.typical: High alpine climate with marine west coast influences; extremely wet with high precipitation on western slopes; frequent fog and low clouds
climate.spring: May-June: Significant snow remaining above 6,000 feet; avalanche danger elevated; poor route visibility due to snow cover
climate.summer: July-August: Warmest season with highs 45-70F at valley level (summit temperatures 20-40F lower); minimal precipitation (1.7-2 inches); maximum daylight (16+ hours); glacier travel feasible
climate.fall: September: Cooling trend with first snow possible above 8,000 feet; increasing storm frequency; daylight decreasing rapidly; September ascents risky
climate.winter: October-June: Heavy snowfall and ice common; avalanche danger very high; mountaineering route not practical except for specialized winter expeditions
climate.forecastZone: North Cascades National Park marine west coast climate zone; weather systems from Pacific force westerly winds up Cascades producing high precipitation on windward slopes

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Required: Backcountry camping permit from NPS; reservable up to 60 days in advance via Recreation.gov or available 40% first-come-first-served
access.fees: Backcountry permit: no fee but reservation required; Water taxi (Ross Lake Resort): approximately $60 per person round trip
access.closures: Backcountry travel generally unrestricted but avalanche and snow conditions may close access seasonally; check with NPS Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount before trip

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (State Route 20) to North Cascades Highway
road.status: Paved to Ross Dam Trailhead; open year-round but subject to winter weather closures near pass
road.seasonalGate: North Cascades Highway occasionally closes due to winter snow; typically open July-September; check WSDOT for current conditions
road.driveNote: Drive to Ross Lake Resort (off SR-20 near Concrete, WA); from Concrete approximately 12 miles east on SR-20 to trailhead parking. Facilities at Ross Lake include water taxi dispatch and basic supplies.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00 AM from camp on summit day to ensure adequate daylight for descent

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Visitor Center, Marblemount (360-873-4500); Backcountry permits and emergency coordination
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Mount Vernon, WA) or United General Hospital (Sedro Woolley, WA); approximately 1-2 hours from trailhead
emergency.notes: Mount Fury is extremely remote; helicopter rescue is difficult and weather-dependent. Satellite communication (InReach, PLB) strongly recommended. Skagit County Sheriff coordinates mountain rescue; Skagit Mountain Rescue Unit operates in this area. Self-rescue capability is essential due to extreme remoteness.

routes.corrections: The specific West Ridge / Northwest Route with 1938 first ascent attribution is difficult to verify in detail from modern climbing sources. Primary documentation references Mongo Ridge (VI 5.10, 1938 via Ptarmigan Climbing Club activity though Mongo Ridge FA is credited to Wayne Wallace solo in August 2006). The provided Grade III-IV rating for the West Ridge is consistent with early alpine traverse routes. Elevation given as 8303 ft is consistent with 2022 theodolite survey result of 8305 ft +/- 10 feet for West Fury summit. The 5.6 rock grade appears conservative for the actual Class 5.4 scrambling typically encountered.

routes.rockGrade: Class 4, with final band low Class 5
routes.overview: Success Cleaver is the only route on Mount Rainier avoiding significant glacier travel, featuring exposed rock scrambling and loose scree terrain on a 3.3-mile ridge rising over 8,000 feet on the mountain's southwest side.
routes.beta: A remote alpine scramble on volcanic rock featuring short sections of Class 3 scrambling in lower sections, several short but steep Class 3-4 rock bands, and a final challenging low Class 5 rock band directly below Point Success. Early season ascents encounter steep snow; late summer ascents traverse steep, exposed, unstable scree slopes.
routes.approach: From Longmire trailhead (2,760 ft), ascend the Wonderland Trail approximately 6.3 miles with steep switchbacks over Rampart to Indian Henry's Hunting Ground (5,300 ft). Continue via Mirror Lakes trail to Pyramid Peak area, then descend to Success Cleaver base at approximately 6,500 ft. Total approach: approximately 4,100 feet of elevation gain over 10 miles to reach Indian Henry's Hunting Ground.
routes.descent: Most parties carry over the summit and descend Disappointment Cleaver route, which is well-flagged and easier to navigate. Direct descent of Success Cleaver is difficult and not recommended after a long summit day.
routes.descentText: Direct descent of Success Cleaver involves managing steep, exposed scree traverses and loose boulder terrain, particularly in the 11,000-9,400 foot range. Early season snow-covered terrain is generally safer than late-summer scree. Most expeditions avoid direct descent by continuing over summit to Columbia Crest and descending the Disappointment Cleaver route instead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Longmire to Indian Henry's Hunting Ground and establish camp (6-7 hours). Day 2: Ascend Success Cleaver to Point Success or Columbia Crest summit and return to high camp (8-10+ hours). Day 3: Descend to Longmire or via Disappointment Cleaver (6-8 hours).
routes.bail: Descend back down Success Cleaver to Indian Henry's Hunting Ground or continue over summit to Columbia Crest and descend Disappointment Cleaver, which is more established and easier to navigate in poor visibility.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if weather deteriorates, exhaustion sets in, or the final Class 5 rock band appears unsafe. Do not press on if afternoon thunderstorms are developing.
routes.bestSeason: June and July are optimal for Success Cleaver, with June offering better snow consolidation and July providing the most stable weather. June snow conditions enable confident travel; July-August heat increases rockfall hazard. May offers quiet conditions but less consolidated snow; September brings deteriorating weather patterns.
routes.comms: Limited cell service in remote areas; satellite communication via personal locator beacon or satellite communicator is strongly recommended. Some cellular coverage may be available at higher elevations depending on provider, but cannot be relied upon.
knownHazards: Rockfall from upper slopes and unstable rock bands
Avalanche hazard in chutes and headwall sections
Steep, exposed scree traverses with fall potential
Rock instability and loose boulder terrain
Icefall from glaciers flanking the route
Volcanic rock weakness due to hydrothermal alteration
Sudden weather deterioration with limited shelter
proTips: Early-season ascents on consolidated snow are significantly safer and faster than late-summer scree travel
Identify descent route during approach for safety in poor visibility
Start early (3-4 AM from camp) to minimize exposure to afternoon rockfall
The final Class 5 rock band below Point Success requires careful route-finding
Consider carrying over to Columbia Crest and descending Disappointment Cleaver for safer descent
watchOut: Rockfall significantly increases in afternoon heat; descend before midday when possible
Loose, unstable scree can lead to major falls; move deliberately and test holds
The chutes and headwall below the ridge are prone to avalanches under certain snow conditions
Cell service is unreliable; ensure emergency communication device is fully charged
Fatigue increases accident risk on steep terrain; bivouacking is safer than descending while exhausted
routes.detailedRack: A light alpine rack including a selection of passive protection (nuts, hexes) for low Class 5 sections, quickdraws, and slings. Most parties scramble Class 3-4 terrain unroped, carrying a 30-meter glacier rope for potential team belay on steepest sections. Trekking poles helpful for scree travel.
routes.proNeeds: Light protection for the final Class 5 rock band; climbing is mostly unprotected scrambling. A 30-meter rope and basic belay anchors suffice for most of the route.
whatToBring: Personal locator beacon or satellite communicator

climate.forecastZone: Pacific maritime (wet, mild winters; cool, dry summers)
climate.typical: Highly variable; typical alpine mountain weather with rapid changes. Summer afternoon thunderstorms common.
climate.spring: May: Cooler, wetter conditions with lingering spring storms; good snow consolidation.
climate.summer: June-July: Optimal climbing window with stable weather, moderate temps. July-August: Warmer, increased rockfall hazard.
climate.fall: September: Rougher weather patterns and more frequent storms.
climate.winter: October-April: Route snow-covered and dangerous; committing alpine climbing.

access.landManager: National Park Service (Mount Rainier National Park)
access.fees: $82 per person climbing permit (annual, calendar year); Mount Rainier National Park entrance fee applies
access.permit: Climbing permit required for anyone ascending above 10,000 feet. Must be obtained in person at ranger station.
access.passRequired: Valid photo ID required for climbing permit registration
access.closures: Route generally accessible May-September. Longmire-Paradise road closes nightly November 1-March but reopens daily.

road.name: Nisqually Entrance Road to Longmire
road.status: Open year-round
road.seasonalGate: Longmire-Paradise road may close during severe winter weather
road.driveNote: Longmire is 6 miles north of Nisqually Entrance via paved road. Road to Paradise closes nightly in winter but reopens next morning after snow removal. Check NPS website for current status.

timing.recommendedStart: 3-4 AM from high camp to minimize rockfall exposure

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 or Mount Rainier National Park Dispatch: 360-569-2211
emergency.rangerStation: Jackson Visitor Center at Paradise; ranger stations at Sunrise and SE entrance
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence St. Peter Hospital, Olympia, WA (approximately 70 miles southwest)
emergency.notes: Helicopter rescue available. Satellite communication strongly recommended. Report emergencies to park dispatch immediately.

routes.corrections: Multiple elevation sources cite Point Success at 14,147 ft (given data), 14,104 ft (USGS), and 14,158 ft (SummitPost). Point Success is not a separate peak but a named point on Mount Rainier's summit ridge.

blurb: Point Success is a named point on Mount Rainier's summit ridge at 14,158 feet in the Cascade Range. The Success Cleaver route is one of only two Mount Rainier climbs avoiding significant glacier travel, offering a remote alpine scramble on volcanic andesite with Class 3-4 rock scrambling, loose scree traverses, and a final low Class 5 pitch.
hierarchyNote: Point Success at 14,158 ft is not a separate peak but a named geographic point on Mount Rainier's summit ridge, part of the Success Cleaver formation. Hierarchically correct as a sub-feature of Mount Rainier in the Cascade Range, Pierce County, Washington. No hierarchy conflict detected."

blurb: West Peak of Mount Fury in the Northern Picket Range is the higher summit of Mount Fury's massif and one of Washington's most remote and technically demanding alpine objectives. The peak rises above 8,300 feet and requires exposed scrambling with 5th class rock climbing, dangerous loose/chossy rock, and significant psychological commitment. Fewer than 20 parties have summited this peak as of 2016, making it a true wilderness mountaineering challenge in North Cascades National Park.

routes.overview: A wild and exposed scramble traverse connecting East Fury (8,280 ft) to West Fury (8,305 ft), involving three main towers with Class 2-4 descent to saddle and Class 4-5 climbing on towers. This is the standard method of traversing between the two peaks and involves significant exposure, loose rock, and dangerous rappels.
routes.beta: Once you have reached the summit of East Fury, you may continue to West Fury via this traverse. The traverse is a series of gendarmes and towers with a combination of scrambling and low 5th class rock. The descent off East Fury to the low saddle is on very loose Class 2-3 boulders. There are three towers you must climb up and over before making the final ascent to the summit of West Fury. A 50-60 meter rope is recommended at the very least for rappelling the 4th and low 5th class areas you have climbed.
routes.approach: Approach via the East Fury route first, summiting East Fury via the standard Southeast Glacier and Class 3 scramble routes. The traverse begins immediately from East Fury's summit.
routes.descent: Traverse back to East Fury summit via the same route (downclimbing towers and using rappels), then descend East Fury via the Southeast Glacier and Class 3 terrain back to Luna Camp and Luna Col, then descend the Access Creek basin to the Big Beaver Trail.
routes.descentText: Descend the towers you climbed by reversing the route and setting rappels off poor-quality anchors on chossy rock. Each rappel down makes retreat progressively more difficult. Once off the traverse, descend the east side of East Fury to the saddle on loose Class 2-3 talus, then descend the Southeast Glacier back to Luna Col and the main Access Creek drainage.
routes.rappels: 6 raps to 40-50m (rope may need to be repositioned due to poor anchor quality)
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July to late August presents the most stable conditions with minimal remaining snow. Early season (May-June) may have significant snow covering terrain and requiring different techniques. September is possible in stable weather years, but approaching winter storms are a concern.

timing.recommendedStart: 0600 from Luna Camp

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Water taxi from Ross Dam to Big Beaver, hike 9.4 miles to Luna Camp (7,200 ft). Day 2: Ascend to East Fury summit, traverse to West Fury, return to East Fury summit. Day 3: Descend to Luna Camp, begin exit. Day 4: Complete descent to Ross Lake and water taxi return.
routes.comms: No cellular service available on the peak. Satellite communication device (e.g., Garmin InReach, SPOT) recommended for emergency communication in this remote area. Verizon occasionally has coverage at Ross Lake trailhead (8,000+ ft).
gear: Helmet (essential for rockfall and loose rock)
routes.detailedRack: A modest rock climbing rack is essential due to poor anchor placements throughout. Bring 1-2 sets of small cams (0.4-1.5 inches) and a set of nuts for protection and rappel anchors. Many parties report difficulty finding solid rock for gear placement; natural features (trees, rocks) may be used for anchors where possible.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is difficult to place due to chossy, loose granite. Many climbing parties find it nearly impossible to establish solid anchor points and often rely on natural features or accept marginal protection. Rappels are typically established using multiple strands and directional placements to increase security.
proTips: This traverse is almost always soloed due to the difficulty of protecting on chossy rock
Bring extra cordage for redundant rappel anchors
Scout the descent route carefully before committing; retreat becomes progressively difficult
Most experienced parties can complete the traverse in under an hour, but allow a full day if rope work is needed
Excellent scrambling skills and comfort with exposure are essential
watchOut: Extremely loose and chossy granite throughout - rockfall hazard
Very difficult to place protection or establish secure rappel anchors
Three exposed towers with substantial drop-offs - exposure and fall hazard
Poor-quality rock can crumble when weighted - test handholds carefully
High altitude (8,300 ft) and remote location - limited escape options
Retreat becomes increasingly difficult once you begin rappelling
knownHazards: Chossy, unconsolidated granite prone to failure
Limited solid anchor placements for protection or rappels
Exposure on multiple tower sections with 100+ ft drops
Poor-quality tat on descent - unreliable fixed anchors
Avalanche potential in early season on approaches
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate or party is uncomfortable, downclimb the first tower and descend east side of East Fury. Early retreat to the tower areas is still possible, but continued rappelling becomes increasingly committing. There are no bailout routes once committed to the middle towers.
routes.turnaround: Turnaround at 2:00 PM from West Fury summit to ensure sufficient daylight for descent. If significant snow is encountered on the approach glacier, reassess the route. Party consensus and comfort level with loose rock should determine go/no-go decision before committing to first tower.

access.landManager: National Park Service - North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for all overnight travel
access.fees: Recreation fee of $10 per person, per night; youth 15 and under free
access.closures: Access is always on foot/water taxi; vehicle access limited to Ross Dam trailhead

road.name: North Cascades Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Seasonal closure mid-November through mid-April due to snow
road.driveNote: Drive to Ross Dam Trailhead near Concrete, Washington (milepost 134 on SR 20). Limited parking at trailhead.
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 closed November-April; backcountry access limited by snow

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades marine west coast
climate.typical: Cold and wet with frequent storms. Marine influence from Pacific Ocean brings persistent moisture, especially west-facing slopes. Wind can be severe at altitude.
climate.spring: May-June: Rapid snowmelt creates hazardous avalanche conditions and stream crossings. Temperatures still below freezing above 7,000 ft at night.
climate.summer: Jul-Aug: Most stable conditions with 40-50F daytime highs at altitude, occasional clear days between weather systems. Wet and heavy snow if it falls.
climate.fall: September: Earlier storms bring significant precipitation. Temperatures dropping rapidly. October approaches winter conditions.
climate.winter: Nov-Apr: Extreme avalanche danger, frequent white-out conditions, deep snow. Access restricted by road closure. Only winter mountaineers with specialized skills should attempt.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 for emergency; non-emergency North Cascades Communications Center: (360) 873-4590 ext. 37
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades Visitor Center (Newhalem): (206) 386-4495 ext. 11; Wilderness Information Center (Marblemount): (360) 854-7245; Park Headquarters: (360) 854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Arlington, WA): ~60 miles west. Skagit Valley Hospital (Mount Vernon): ~55 miles west
emergency.notes: Rescue from West Peak is extremely difficult and may take 12+ hours due to remote location. Evacuation by helicopter is weather-dependent and often impossible. Self-rescue mindset essential. Sat phone/InReach strongly recommended.

whatToBring: Water and high-calorie food for summit day
Sat communication device (Garmin InReach or SPOT)
routes.corrections: This is the standard or commonly-used route for traversing between East and West Fury peaks. No single first-ascent party is documented for this specific traverse, as it is a natural line between the two summits. Some sources indicate it may be soloed more often than attempted with rope protection due to loose rock.

hierarchyNote: Hierarchy confirmed: West Peak is the west summit of Mount Fury (elevation 8,305 ft per theodolite survey), located in the Northern Picket Range, Picket Range, Cascade Range, in North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, Washington. Coordinates 48°48'43"N 121°19'24"W align with published peak data. No discrepancies with known geography.

blurb: Poster Peak (7,565 ft) is a striking alpine peak one mile southeast of the Early Winters Spires in the North Cascades near Washington Pass. Named after appearing on a ski poster by local climbers and skiers, the peak offers moderate alpine rock routes on its northeast buttresses and gentle ski-accessible slopes on its western aspects. The peak is well-known for the Blue's Buttress route, a classic Grade III alpine climb established in 2003.

routes.overview: The leftmost of two prominent northeast buttresses on Poster Peak offering moderate alpine rock climbing with a mix of scrambling and technical pitches. Known for delightful rock quality on the ridge crest and a choose-your-own-adventure feel with manageable grade and short approach.
routes.beta: Blue's Buttress is a classic single-day alpine route featuring approximately 9-10 pitches when simul-climbing. The route offers mostly easy 3rd and low 5th class terrain with occasional 5.6-5.7 crux pitches. Starting with easy ledges above the drainage, climbers follow the path of least resistance up the buttress crest to the summit. Rock quality improves significantly on the ridge crest after the initial gully section. The route is described as high-quality, fun scrambling and moderate climbing without continuous hard sections.
routes.approach: From Highway 20, park at the large turnout at the hairpin turn one mile east of Washington Pass. Follow the trail toward Kangaroo Pass for approximately one mile, then split off right up the drainage that descends from Poster Peak. Follow this drainage uphill (south) keeping right for some cairns that cross the drainage. Traverse east following treeline and cairns below the east-northeast buttresses. House Buttress is the first (rightmost) buttress you encounter; continue left around its base to reach Blue's Buttress. Reach easy ledges at the base of Blue's Buttress after approximately one hour of approach. Total approach distance approximately 1.5-2 miles.
routes.descent: Walk southeast off the summit toward the col along the southeast ridge (at approximately 7,130 feet). Descend the steep loose gully back toward the base of the buttresses. Take care on loose terrain and snow-covered sections if present.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend southeast toward Poster Col (the saddle at approximately 7,130 feet) via easy 3rd class terrain. Continue into the steep scree gully below the col, carefully down-climbing loose terrain. If snow is present, boot-ski or carefully descend snowy sections. Contour back to the buttress base and retrace the approach via cairn-marked terrain. Descent typically takes 30 minutes to over an hour depending on conditions and party ability. Use caution on all loose rock and scree.
routes.bestSeason: The best climbing season is mid-June through September, with peak conditions in July and August when temperatures are warmest and snow has fully melted from the approach and lower pitches. Early season (June) can have continuous snow near the base of the climb. Late season (September) offers crisp weather but shorter days. Spring (April-May) can offer good skiing conditions on the southwest and southeast slopes.
routes.comms: Cell phone reception is not reliable at Poster Peak. Satellite communication devices (personal locator beacons or satellite messengers) are recommended for safety in this remote alpine area.
routes.detailedRack: Bring a double set of cams from #0.5 to #3 (emphasis on #1-2.5), a set of nuts covering small to large sizes, and large hexes. Two 70-meter ropes are sufficient for simul-climbing most of the route. Approach shoes or light boots work well; approach is mostly 3rd class scrambling on talus.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is generally moderate throughout the route. Rock quality in the initial gully is poor with limited reliable placements; two fixed pins are reportedly on the first pitch but are often missed by parties. The crux section features downsloping, awkward terrain with limited protection. Rock quality improves substantially on the ridge crest where solid placements become more available. Most climbers simul-climb the middle and upper sections, requiring efficient rope management rather than multiple belayed pitches.
whatToBring: Three season or light four-season jacket
proTips: Stay on the ridge following the line of least resistance rather than deviating into adjacent terrain; once you find the ridge crest, navigation is straightforward.
Look for the obvious roof (large cave-like feature) as a navigation aid; traverse left around the base of this feature to access the easy ledges.
Two fixed pins are often present on the first pitch but can be missed; bring small pitons as backup protection for the crux gully section.
The route has a choose-your-own-adventure feel; multiple lines work; pick the path with the best rock quality and protection opportunities.
Climb during cooler hours (early morning) to minimize rockfall risk; afternoon heating can loosen rock.
This is an ideal route for alpine rock practice or returning from injury, offering substantial rock climbing sections in a relatively short climb.
Most efficient parties simul-climb continuously above the initial gully; stopping frequently for belays slows down the climbing significantly.
watchOut: Loose rock throughout the route, especially in the lower gully section—test holds before committing weight.
Poor rock quality in the lower sections before reaching the ridge crest requires careful climbing and protection placement.
Objective hazard of falling rock from climbers higher on the route—position yourself safely and communicate with other parties.
Downsloping, awkward terrain in the crux section of the initial gully makes protection placement difficult and climbing exposed.
Steep descent gully on the return features loose rock and scree; descend with caution and be aware of rockfall risk below.
knownHazards: Loose rock is prevalent throughout the climb, particularly in lower gully sections and on the descent terrain.
Rock quality deteriorates in lower sections but improves significantly on the ridge crest; route-finding should prioritize solid rock.
The initial gully approach to the ridge has limited protection and difficult, awkward climbing with downsloping holds.
Objective rockfall hazard exists on the descent gully, especially if other parties are climbing higher on the peak.
Snow and ice may be present on lower sections during early season (before mid-June), requiring additional protection and care.

pitchDetail.notes: Approach to the base of the buttress and initial climbing. Easy ledges lead upward with occasional 5.6 crux moves. Two fixed pins often present but easy to miss. Navigate toward the obvious roof/cave feature.
Gain the ridge crest through the crux gully section. Downsloping, awkward terrain with limited protection. Once above this section, rock quality and route-finding improve significantly.

Fun scrambling and simul-climbing along the ridge crest to the summit. Multiple options exist; follow the line of least resistance with the best rock. Delightful rock quality and enjoyable movement on the upper ridge.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:30 AM from Hairpin parking to ensure summit by mid-afternoon and complete descent in daylight. Earlier starts provide additional margin for safety and navigation.

road.name: Washington State Highway 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open generally May-November; winter closures possible east of the pass due to snow
road.seasonalGate: Highway 20 can close in winter; check WSDOT for current status. Hairpin parking accessible during open season.
road.driveNote: Large turnout parking at the hairpin turn one mile east of Washington Pass. No parking fees; no Northwest Forest Pass required for parking at Hairpin.

climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Eastern Washington (Okanogan County)
climate.typical: Alpine environment with rapid weather changes. Afternoon thunderstorms common in summer; afternoon winds increase. Temperature variability from freezing at dawn to 50-60°F on south aspects at midday.
climate.spring: Snow likely on approaches and lower pitches through May; corn snow conditions develop in late April-May; cooler and shorter days.
climate.summer: Stable weather generally July-August; mornings clear, afternoon thunderstorms possible. Daytime highs 50-65°F; nights near freezing. Sunrise near 4:45 AM, sunset near 8:45 PM.
climate.fall: September offers crisp, stable weather; less likely for afternoon convection. Shorter days; sunrise near 6:30 AM, sunset near 7:00 PM.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche danger; Highway 20 often closed east of pass; not typically climbed.

access.landManager: Wenatchee National Forest (USDA Forest Service)
access.fees: No fees for parking at Hairpin turnout
access.permit: No permit required for day climbing
access.passRequired: No parking pass required for Hairpin turnout
access.closures: Highway 20 seasonally closed November-May in winter; check WSDOT before planning trips in shoulder seasons

emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Parkway, Mount Vernon, WA 98273; approximately 75 miles west (90 minute drive)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Ranger Station (Marblemount); Wenatchee National Forest ranger contacts available through USFS website
emergency.notes: Skagit County Sheriff SAR covers this area. Cell service unreliable; satellite communicators recommended. Helicopter evacuation possible in emergencies; landing zones may be limited on the peak.

routes.corrections: Mountain Project lists elevation as 7,840 ft; however, the Alpine Institute, multiple climbing sources, and topographic references consistently cite 7,565 feet. The corrected elevation (7,565 ft) is used throughout this description. The pitch count varies in sources from 3 pitches (simul-climbed continuously) to 12 pitches (if stopping to belay each section); 9-10 pitches represents typical climbing with strategic simul-climbing sections.

routes.overview: Gato Negro is a newer alpine rock route on the East Face of Silver Star Mountain's sub-peak Whine Spire. This big, sustained 1,500-foot climb features impressive granite pitches with mostly solid rock, though loose sections and tricky route-finding require careful attention.
routes.beta: Gato Negro is a full-day alpine rock climb with fairly sustained 5.10a/b climbing spread across 11 long pitches. Most pitches span 180-190 feet with none shorter than 150 feet. The route features a spectacular hand-crack first pitch, bolted 5.10a face climbing on fragile flakes, rising traverses under roofs, and splitter cracks on the final headwall. Route-finding is somewhat challenging; avoid a clean blocky chimney to the right of the route that looks good but is not as good as it appears. Cruxes are mostly well-protected, but a few slightly runout 5.8 sections on loose granite and a 5.9+ offwidth chimney section present additional challenges.
routes.approach: Drive SR-20 east past the hairpin turn at Washington Pass to the next major pullout on the right side near Milepost 166. Park in the broad shoulder area. A marked climbers trail drops from the highway, crosses the creek, and climbs steeply upward toward the bench camp at 6,500 ft. The trail branches right toward Whine Spire, approaching the middle of three rock masses to the right of the pass. Water can be found at the bench camp but may be limited or non-existent late summer.
routes.descent: Steep gully requiring 4th-class down-climbing with multiple rappels and loose rock throughout.
routes.descentText: The descent is committing and loose: a steep, extremely loose gully requiring 4th-class down-climbing with multiple rappels (4-6 depending on party decisions and rope management). Loose rock hazard throughout. Plan for 2.5 to 3 hours to complete the descent in good conditions, longer if darkness falls during the descent.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is the primary climbing season. Late June through early July is particularly recommended to avoid late-summer snow and to ensure adequate daylight for the long descent. Avoid early season (June) when snow patches and ice may still be present at the base.
routes.detailedRack: Bring a full set of cams ranging from sizes 0 through 5, with an additional half set of small cams. Stoppers and nuts have limited utility on this route but one small nut may be useful to avoid plugging a lock on the crux. Standard alpine rock gear package.
routes.proNeeds: Cruxes are mostly well-protected with good placements. Some slightly runout 5.8 sections on loose granite require careful lead climbing. The 5.9+ offwidth chimney section requires solid technique and protection skills.
proTips: Bring a good topo or use the SuperTopo guidebook
Avoid the blocky chimney to the right that looks appealing but is suboptimal
Be prepared for loose rock and poor rock quality on some pitches
Start early to ensure daylight for the descent
Party of 4 can efficiently manage rope arrangements
Experienced parties have completed camp-to-camp in 9.5 hours
watchOut: Beware of bad tat on the first chockstone on the crux pitch
Loose, 5.8 quality kitty litter granite presents rockfall and gear placement challenges
The gully descent is steep and extremely loose with continuous rockfall hazard
Limited daylight in September can turn descent into a night affair
Committing moves on fragile flakes require solid footwork
knownHazards: Loose rock throughout, especially on descent
Fragile granite features requiring careful body placement
Runout sections on poor rock quality
Route-finding challenges despite relatively straightforward path
Steep gully descent with loose rock and rockfall hazard

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center - Washington Pass Zone
climate.typical: Cool mountain weather with afternoon thunderstorms common
climate.spring: Snow and ice present, route typically inaccessible
climate.summer: Warm days, cool nights; afternoon thunderstorms possible. Snow patches may linger early season
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures, longer shadows reduce daylight; September brings shorter days
climate.winter: Route closed due to snow and avalanche hazard

emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest - Marblemount Ranger District
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (about 60 miles)
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable in the Washington Pass area. Satellite communication device recommended. Emergency helicopter access possible via NWSRU.

routes.comms: Cell service is spotty to nonexistent throughout the Washington Pass climbing area. Satellite communicators (InReach, Spot) are highly recommended.

blurb: Viviane Campsite is a small alpine rock climbing area in the Enchantments basin of Washington's Central Cascades near Leavenworth, featuring polished granite formations on the north shore of Lake Viviane. The area sits at 6,802 feet in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness and serves as a base camp for climbers accessing the surrounding alpine granite of Prusik Peak and the Enchantments.
hierarchyNote: Viviane Campsite is technically a climbing area/campsite at Lake Viviane rather than a distinct peak. The location was added to Mountain Project in July 2020 by Joe Sexton and is classified as a climbing destination within the Stuart-Enchantments area. Elevation data (6,802 ft) represents the campsite location, not a summit.

routes.overview: A single-pitch alpine granite route featuring smearing and jug climbing on solid granite near Lake Viviane, accessible as top-rope or traditional lead.
routes.beta: The route starts with a layback and smears before mantling onto a ledge. The crux is a 5.10ish move through a thin crack. Short climbers may have a harder time due to the length and reach required. Can be set as top-rope using a boulder at the top.
routes.approach: From Snow Lakes Trailhead, hike the Snow Lake Trail approximately 9.5 miles with 6,400 feet elevation gain to reach Lake Viviane. Once at Lake Viviane, the climbing location is immediately to the right of the beach/campsite area when arriving from the trail. The climbing is right alongside the trail at the lake shore.
routes.descent: Downclimb or rappel from the anchors/boulder at the top (typically short rappel to ground level given the 30 ft length).
routes.descentText: Since this is a short 30-foot route, climbers can either carefully downclimb the same line or set up a short rappel from the boulder anchor at the summit of the route. A single 30-60m rope is sufficient. Alternatively, walk off if setting up as a top-rope via the boulder.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Snow Lakes Trailhead to Lake Viviane (6-8 hours with full pack). Establish camp at Viviane Campsite. Day 2: Acclimatization day; scout the route and set up top-rope if desired. Day 3: Climb Smears, Jugs, and Rock & Roll (1-2 hours of climbing). Day 4: Descent to trailhead (5-6 hours).
routes.bail: From the crux position, downclimbers can retreat by downclimbing through the thin crack section and mantling back to the ledge, then retracing the initial layback/smear section. The route is short enough that bail is straightforward. In emergency, rappel from the top anchor.
routes.turnaround: Given the alpine location and weather exposure, climbers should plan to start climbing early (dawn) and aim to be off the rock by early afternoon to avoid afternoon thunderstorms. If weather deteriorates or parties are delayed, be prepared to bivy near the route or descend via the trail.
routes.bestSeason: July through August offers the most stable alpine weather in the Enchantments, with minimal snow at lake level. September and early October are viable shoulder months but expect cooler temperatures and potential early season snow higher up. May-June and late October are not recommended due to significant snow accumulation, avalanche hazard on approaches (Aasgard Pass), and permit restrictions.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the Enchantments basin due to remote alpine location and surrounding granite. No reliable cell coverage should be assumed. Satellite messengers or personal locator beacons (PLBs) are recommended for emergency communication. The nearest ranger station is at Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest headquarters in Wenatchee.
routes.detailedRack: #2 carabiners, #2 and small stoppers for lead protection. For top-rope setup: two 60m ropes or two shorter ropes (single rope climbing via natural anchor). Quickdraws for leading. Helmet required.
routes.proNeeds: Light traditional pro: #2 carabiners and small stoppers are sufficient for the thin crack crux. The route does not require extensive rack; a single set of small to mid-sized cams could supplement protection on the crux if desired. Most climbers use top-rope given the short length and natural anchor at top.
whatToBring: sleeping bag (rated for potential sub-freezing nights)
fire starter or lighter (for stove use only)
knownHazards: Afternoon thunderstorms common; descent and exposure risk if caught above treeline in lightning
Loose/exposed granite rock; test all holds and anchors carefully before committing weight
Weather exposure at 6,802 feet; temperature swings from hot sun to near-freezing at night
Hidden cracks in granite can trap limbs if not careful; use solid footwork and body positioning
Thin crack crux requires precise technique; higher risk of hand/finger injury if poorly protected or climbed
proTips: Start climbing at first light to minimize afternoon thunderstorm exposure; be off the rock by 1-2 PM
Short climbers may find success using better footwork on the smear section rather than relying on reach; work the techniques rather than power
The boulder anchor at top is solid granite; test all holds before weighting
Set up top-rope early in the day to allow multiple ascents without leading repeatedly
The route is short but compact; the crux is well-protected if solid rock is used for placements
watchOut: Do not attempt to climb late in the day due to thunderstorm risk and fading light at alpine elevation
Descending in darkness on the trail from Viviane is hazardous; headlamps and navigation skills essential
Early season (May-June): Snow and avalanche danger on approach (Aasgard Pass) make route inaccessible; wait for conditions to stabilize
Afternoon weather deterioration can be rapid; if weather changes suddenly, descend immediately rather than waiting out conditions on the route

pitchDetail.notes: Single 30-foot pitch starting with a layback and smearing sequence, transitioning to a mantle onto a ledge. Crux is located in the upper third: a 5.10ish move through a thin crack requiring precise footwork and hand placement. Well-protected with small traditional gear (#2 cams, stoppers). Finish on solid ground near a large boulder suitable for anchoring.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (USDA Forest Service)
access.fees: $5 per person per day hiking permit fee (if successful in obtaining daily lottery); $6 non-refundable application fee for advanced lottery permit (Feb 15-Mar 1). Northwest Forest Pass required for parking ($30/year or $5/day).
access.permit: Overnight Wilderness Permit required May 15-Oct 31 for all camping in Enchantments Permit Area. Highly competitive lottery system (less than 10% success rate). Day-use permits are free but self-issued at trailhead. Maximum group size: 8 people including stock.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for vehicle parking at Snow Lakes Trailhead
access.closures: Icicle Creek Road (access to Snow Lakes Trailhead) closes seasonally in late fall/winter, typically around November through March or April, restricting access to the area.

timing.recommendedStart: Dawn (5-6 AM) to maximize daylight and minimize afternoon thunderstorm exposure; aim to be off the rock by 1-2 PM

road.name: Icicle Creek Road (FS-7601 and extension to Snow Lakes Trailhead)
road.status: Seasonally closed in late fall/winter; open typically May-October
road.seasonalGate: Late fall/winter closure (November-March/April depending on snow conditions)
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, WA, take Icicle Creek Road south approximately 15 miles to the Snow Lakes Trailhead parking area. The road is paved for the first several miles, then transitions to maintained gravel. No high-clearance vehicle required in good conditions. When closed, the road is impassable; check Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest website for current status before driving.

climate.forecastZone: NOAA Forecast Zone: Central Washington Cascades (Leavenworth area)
climate.typical: Alpine environment with rapid weather changes. Temperatures range from 40-65 F during summer days, dropping to 20-40 F at night. Wind is common at altitude. Afternoon thunderstorms develop frequently June-September.
climate.spring: Snow present at lake level through mid-June in most years. Cold temperatures (freezing at night, 30-40 F during day). Avalanche danger on Aasgard Pass approaches. Not recommended for climbing.
climate.summer: Best and most stable season (July-August). Daytime temperatures 55-70 F, nights 35-45 F. Low precipitation, clear skies common. Occasional afternoon thunderstorms. Ideal climbing window.
climate.fall: September-early October: Temperatures dropping (45-60 F day, 25-35 F night). Morning frost common. Increasing storm frequency. Late October: Significant weather deterioration, early snow possible at higher elevations above 7,000 feet.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Chelan County Sheriff (911 for emergencies). Ranger Station: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee Ranger District
emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee Ranger District (main office in Wenatchee; patrol rangers may contact via emergency channels)
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, 817 Commercial Street, Leavenworth, WA 98826. Phone: (509) 548-5815. 24-hour Emergency Department. Approximate drive time from Snow Lakes Trailhead: 45-60 minutes (hike down plus drive).
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable in Enchantments; sat phones or PLBs recommended. Rescue from alpine location will be helicopter-assisted given remote terrain and steep approach. Self-rescue and preventive measures are essential. Nearest Ranger Patrol typically based out of Wenatchee; response times can be several hours.

blurb: Chablis Spire is the southernmost of the four Wine Spires, granite spires perched at the eastern fringe of Washington Pass in the North Cascades. The peak is distinguished by its iconic "bunny ears" double summit visible from western approaches. The East Face offers a rarely-climbed moderate 4-pitch alpine route with high-quality granite and noteworthy runout sections requiring careful route-finding.
hierarchyNote: Chablis Spire verified as part of Wine Spires group east of Washington Pass in North Cascades. Coordinates 48.55259, -120.59032 confirm location near Okanogan County Washington. Elevation 8,350 ft consistent with Cascades alpine spires of this prominence. Hierarchy is correct.

routes.overview: A 4-pitch alpine climb on high-quality granite featuring moderate climbing with runout sections and an unprotectable summit block. The East Face is rarely climbed but provides direct access to the distinctive horned summit. This is a worthwhile alpine rock objective for parties comfortable with runout climbing and willing to accept the inherent risks.
routes.beta: Traverse right from start toward the large hanging 'split block'. Pitch 2 features a finger crack option (5.8) or easier terrain (5.4) with a critical 25-30 foot unprotectable section on a dyke. Pitch 3 climbs through a chimney and dirty corner. The final pitch offers laid-back climbing in an obvious corner approach to summit, though the final moves to the horned summit are unprotectable. Light rack to 3 inches adequate. The route requires careful rock quality assessment and awareness of unprotected sections.
routes.approach: Access via SR-20 north of Cascade Loop; drive past the hairpin turn just east of Washington Pass to the pullout near Milepost 166 on the north side of the highway. A climbers trail drops from the pullout, immediately crosses Burgundy Creek, and ascends steeply through timbered terrain to a bench camp around 6,500 feet. From camp, continue east toward the Wine Spires. From the bench camp, traverse to approach Chablis Spire via Burgundy Col approach (recommended spring-fall; 2-5 hours total from trailhead) or via Silver Star Creek drainage (recommended for winter; approach in snow). Chablis is reached after traversing past or over Burgundy Col area.
routes.descent: Downclimb carefully to locate the large horn anchor with existing slings. Execute two double-rope rappels with 60m ropes: first rappel to intermediate anchor, second rappel completes descent to the base of the East Face.
routes.descentText: From the summit area, downclimb via loose blocks and corners to locate the large granite horn with established sling anchor. This is a critical waypoint. Thread both 60m ropes through the horn slings. First double-rope rappel descends approximately 120 feet to an intermediate anchor (located on the face). Reanchor ropes for second rappel to complete descent to scree base of East Face. Exercise caution for rockfall and loose rock throughout the descent process.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September. Peak season is July through late August when the approach is snow-free and weather most stable. June approach may encounter residual snow but is feasible. September becomes viable with warmer days but increasing afternoon thunderstorm potential. Winter ascents possible via Silver Star Creek drainage approach for experienced parties; avalanche risk requires careful assessment via NWAC forecasts.
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Richard Berge, Wesley Grand
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable in the Washington Pass area. Satellite communication devices (Garmin InReach, PLB, or satellite-capable phones) recommended for emergency communication. Okanogan County has excellent mountain communication infrastructure for dispatch but personal backup communication critical for remote alpine location.
routes.proNeeds: Light traditional rock rack through 3 inches. Natural protection abundant from granite flakes and crack systems, but Pitch 2 includes a 25-30 foot dyke section with limited pro. Two 60m ropes required for two-rappel descent. Sling and carabiner anchor kit essential. No ice tools required on standard East Face route.
routes.detailedRack: Light rack to 3 inches: approach shoes and medium pack for approach, 1-2 sets of quickdraws for potential fixed anchors, single set of traditional cams (0.5-3"), supplemented with small wired nuts (RZA or similar), 4-6 slings of various lengths, two 60m dynamic ropes (single strand rated), rescue pulley and friction device for rappel safety, belay device, locking carabiners.
whatToBring: Water bottles or hydration pack (3L minimum)
watchOut: Runout climbing with limited pro on Pitch 2 (25-30 feet unprotected dyke section)
Unprotectable summit block requires careful movement and route-finding
Steep approach trail can cause heat exhaustion in summer conditions and loose terrain
Rockfall hazard from loose rock throughout climb and descent
Early season snow/ice on approach creates slipping hazard
Afternoon thunderstorms common July-August; descend early
knownHazards: Unprotected 25-30 foot section on Pitch 2 dyke
Summit block unprotectable final moves
Runout climbing R-rated protection throughout route
Loose rock hazard on descent
Exposure to lightning in afternoon hours
proTips: Start very early (5-6am departure from trailhead) to maximize daylight hours for descent
Chablis is rarely climbed compared to nearby Burgundy or Chianti; expect solitude
The distinctive 'bunny ears' summit provides excellent navigation reference for the Wine Spires area
Carry significantly more water than typical as the approach is steep and exposed to sun
Consider camping at bench camp to acclimatize and reduce altitude-related stress; summit push can be done next morning
Check NWAC avalanche forecast if approaching via Silver Star Creek drainage
Have detailed descent plan reviewed before starting climb; rappel anchor locations critical
routes.turnaround: Ensure descent light remains adequate (minimum 1.5-2 hours before full darkness). Establish mandatory turnaround time no later than 2:00pm from summit to allow safe daylight descent and rappel setup. Descending in darkness increases risk of rappel anchor errors and rockfall hazard.
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate during ascent, return to base of climb via same route and rappel descent. If unable to complete climbing, downclimbing Pitch 1 is safest bailout. Once committed above Pitch 1, summit push is safer than complex retreat via side terrain. Helicopter rescue possible via Okanogan County Sheriff (509-422-7200), though challenging in steep granite terrain.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Early departure from trailhead at Milepost 166 (~6am). Hike to bench camp at 6,500 feet (2-4 hours). Establish camp, rest, hydrate, prepare for next day. Day 2: Early alpine start from camp toward Chablis Spire via Burgundy Col or direct approach (1.5-2 hours to base). 4-pitch climb and summit (3-4 hours). Descent via 2 rappels (1-1.5 hours). Return to camp. Day 3: Pack out, return to trailhead via hiking trail (2-4 hours downhill).

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00-6:00am from trailhead to secure daylight for descent

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades, Washington Pass region (Okanogan County)
climate.typical: Alpine climate with cool temperatures and highly variable precipitation. Western-facing slopes receive significant orographic precipitation. Weather can change rapidly. Afternoon thunderstorm potential June-September.
climate.spring: Late May-June: Temperatures 50-65°F, highly variable, significant snow on higher elevations and north-facing slopes. Approach may require snow skills.
climate.summer: July-August: Temperatures 65-75°F, minimal precipitation (1.5-2" monthly), snow-free approach, afternoon thunderstorm risk 20-40% on summer afternoons.
climate.fall: September: Temperatures 55-65°F, increasing precipitation (~2.5"), mostly clear first week, increasing cloud cover and rain latter month.
climate.winter: November-March: Severe weather, high avalanche risk on approach terrain. Highway SR-20 closed.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass $30 annual or $5 day-use pass required for trailhead parking
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (annual $30) or Day-Use Pass ($5)
access.closures: SR-20 typically closed November-April; access via winter approaches requires avalanche safety training and current snow conditions assessment

road.name: North Cascades Highway, State Route 20
road.status: Open late April through mid-November; seasonal closure November-April
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) typically gated/closed between Milepost 160-171 from November through April due to heavy snow accumulation
road.driveNote: From Seattle: Take I-5 north to Mount Vernon, head east on SR-20 through Sedro-Woolley. Continue on SR-20 east toward Washington Pass. Pass the famous Washington Pass Overlook (pullout on right), continue past hairpin turn and locate climbers pullout near Milepost 166. Pullout is on north (right) side of highway. 2.5-3 hour drive from Seattle area. High-altitude mountain pass; chain/tire requirements possible in shoulder season.

emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District (Okanogan-Wenatchee NF); limited weekend/seasonal staffing
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth WA (~60 miles southwest, 1.5-2 hours drive); Elevation: 1,300 feet; 24-hour emergency department
emergency.notes: Washington Pass is one of four critical mountain passes that Okanogan County must maintain communication coverage for. Cell service unreliable; satellite communication strongly recommended. Helicopter rescue possible but challenging in steep granite terrain. Leave trip itinerary with emergency contact before departure.

pitchDetail.notes: Traverse right toward the large hanging 'split block'. Establish climbing on solid granite with good natural pro from flakes and cracks. Build anchor for belay.

Finger crack option climbs at 5.8 or easier terrain available at 5.4. Critical 25-30 foot unprotectable section on dyke with minimal gear placements. Requires confident movement on moderate terrain with limited protection.
Climb chimney feature and progress up dirty corner. Moderate climbing with adequate natural protection from granite features. Build anchor.

Fun, laid-back climbing in obvious corner approach to summit. Final moves to the horned summit block are unprotectable and require careful route-finding. 40-foot section with no viable pro. Downclimb to horn anchor with slings established for rappel.

routes.corrections: First ascent of Chablis Spire peak (via western couloir approach) was in 1952 by Fred Beckey, Dick Berge (also listed as Richard Berge), and Wes Grande (also listed as Wesley Grand) per AAC publications. The specific first ascent date and first ascensionists for the East Face route are not explicitly documented in primary sources; the FA listed (Beckey, Berge, Grand) is attributed to the route on Mountain Project but 1952 date is for the peak's first ascent via a different approach. Coordinates and elevation from Mountain Project verified as accurate for summit location.

blurb: Full Montey is a climbing area within Tyler Peak Crags in Washington's Olympic National Forest, featuring pillow basalt outcroppings near the upper Dungeness River. The area comprises multiple climbing walls (Mossness, Tumblestone, Yew Wall) with alpine-character sport routes ranging from 5.6 to 5.10d. Accessed via forest roads in Clallam County's Buckhorn Wilderness, it offers quality alpine climbing during the dry months of July through September.
hierarchyNote: Full Montey, The is classified as a climbing area, not a standalone peak. It is a sub-area of Tyler Peak Crags located on the slopes of Tyler Peak (6,364 ft). The climbing area base sits at approximately 4,217 feet elevation. Consider clarifying the data model: this may represent the climbing area's base location rather than a distinct geographic peak.

routes.rappels: Multiple 60m+ rappels from upper pitches (6-8 raps from grassy ledge area)
routes.overview: Junior's Farm is an eight-pitch alpine climbing route on the Mossness Wall featuring a mix of sport and traditional climbing with pitches ranging from 5.6 to 5.8. The route includes traverse and scramble sections between pitches and concludes with scrambling above the eighth pitch to reach the summit plateau.
routes.beta: Start on the Bad-Tempered Prom Queen pitch (5.8+/FA: John Goodrum) located on a low-angle bulge just left of vine maple, serving as the approach to the upper pitches. Longer pitches include approximately 10-11 clips. Pitches 1 and 6 are notably stiff 5.8s. Fixed lines assist with the traverse between pitches 4-5. Plan for 6+ hours total for ascent and descent. The route is hot in summer—climb early in the day. Communication between climber and belayer may be difficult on upper pitches in wind.
routes.approach: From US Highway 101 near Sequim Bay State Park, turn west onto Louella Road or east onto Palo Alto Road. Follow Palo Alto Road to Forest Road 2880, then turn right and continue to FR 2870. Follow 2870 continuing to 2860. Turn right at spur sign marked 270 and travel approximately 1.2 miles to parking at sweeping left turn with boulder field visible uphill. Unmarked trail ascends boulder field; Mossness Wall visible through trees above. Base of climb is 30-45 minutes from parking.
routes.descent: Rappel descent from grassy ledge area above pitch 8 via multiple anchors. A single 60-70m rope is insufficient for complete descent—ensure team carries adequate rappel stations and backup anchors.
routes.descentText: After completing pitch 8 and scrambling to the upper summit plateau area, descend via rappelling from the grassy ledge area at the base of the upper pitches. This area has established rappel anchors. Due to the terrain and pitch length, multiple rappels are required—at least 6-8 raps to 30m or longer. A single 60m rope is insufficient; ensure adequate redundant anchors at each station. Do not rely solely on fixed lines if present, as they may be unreliable. Verify all anchor points independently.
routes.bestSeason: June through September offer the best climbing conditions. July is the driest month (0.74 in precipitation) with reliable sunshine and highs around 73-74F. August sees coastal fog but continued stable weather (highs 74F). September is pleasant with slightly increased rainfall as transition to fall begins. Snow and ice conditions preclude winter climbing.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is spotty in the upper Dungeness area. Verizon has the most reliable coverage among carriers, though even Verizon coverage is inconsistent. No satellite communication capability verified for the climbing area. Plan accordingly for emergencies.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning to avoid afternoon heat and ensure adequate daylight for descent

gear: Single rope 60-70m (consider double rope for rappels)
whatToBring: Rain jacket (even in summer—weather changes quickly)
Base layer (top temps can cool rapidly)
routes.detailedRack: A standard rock climbing rack suitable for 5.8-5.9 sport and trad climbing. Primary protection should be quickdraws (12-15 recommended for longer pitches). For trad sections, carry a full set of climbing nuts (BD.3 through BD.3 carabiners or equivalent), slings (60cm and 120cm), and 1-2 passive cams if available. Many pitches have fixed bolts, but fixed gear should not be the sole reliance. Ensure rappel anchors are verified before weighting—carry redundant sling and carabiner systems.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on this route is mixed sport and trad. Most pitches have bolted anchors, but bolts should be supplemented with traditional gear on traverse and scramble sections. The upper pitches (6-8) benefit from redundant protection. Ensure adequate slings and carabiners for building solid anchor systems on grassy ledge areas where rock quality may be variable.
proTips: Start early to avoid afternoon heat and maximize daylight for the 6+ hour round trip
Do not rely on fixed lines for safety—verify all anchor systems independently
Communicate clearly between climber and belayer, especially on pitches 6-8 where wind can make verbal contact difficult
Longer pitches require 10-11 clips—ensure adequate quickdraw supply
The first pitch (Bad-Tempered Prom Queen) is a stiff 5.8—warm up adequately
Rock quality varies—inspect placements carefully on traverse sections
Scramble sections between pitches can be deceptively technical—move deliberately
Establish rescue anchors at the grassy ledge before beginning descent
watchOut: Rockfall hazard from other climbing parties on Mossness and nearby walls
Variable rock quality requiring careful inspection of all placements
Wind on upper pitches can make communication and climbing more difficult
Inadequate rope length for complete descent—multiple rappels required
Terrain around Mossness Wall comprises loose rock and talus
Summer heat makes midday climbing dangerous—plan early starts
Fixed lines on pitch 4-5 traverse may be unreliable for sole protection
knownHazards: The rock is pillow basalt with variable quality throughout the wall; test all handholds and footholds carefully, especially on traverse and scramble sections.
Rockfall is an ongoing hazard from climbers on adjacent routes (Awkward is an Awkward Word, Shart Attack, etc.) on the same wall.
The descent from pitches 6-8 requires multiple long rappels; inadequate rope length is a common error—ensure team is equipped with at least 60-70m of rope and multiple anchor systems.
The grassy ledge area above pitch 8 where rappel anchors are located may be loose or unstable; establish secure anchors independently rather than relying on existing fixed gear.
Cell phone reception is unreliable in this area; emergency communication should not depend on cellular networks.
The approach involves boulder scrambling in an unmarked area; route-finding in poor visibility or darkness presents hazard.

pitchDetail.notes: Low-angle bulge with vine maple landmark. Approximately 95 feet (29m). First ascent: John Goodrum. Sport anchor with lower and upper anchor positions. 5-8 clips. Leads to grassy ledge base of pitch 2.
Mixed grades with approximately 10-11 clips on longer pitches. Includes traverse and scramble sections between pitches 4-5 with fixed line assistance.
Stiff pitch similar to pitch 1 in difficulty. Longer pitch with 10-11 clips. Traverse/scramble to pitch 7.
Upper pitches leading to final grassy ledge. Scramble section above pitch 8 to summit plateau.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service (Olympic National Forest)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking

road.name: Forest Road 2880, Forest Road 2870, Forest Road 2860 via Palo Alto Road / Louella Road
road.status: Open seasonally; typically May-November
road.seasonalGate: No winter gate, but snow/ice may render roads impassable December-March
road.driveNote: From US 101 near Sequim Bay State Park, take Palo Alto Road (east) or Louella Road (west). FR 2880 turns right and descends; continue to FR 2870. Follow to 2860. Turn right at spur 270 sign and travel 1.2 miles to parking. Gravel roads may be rough; high-clearance vehicle recommended after recent weather.

climate.forecastZone: Olympic Mountains / North Olympic Peninsula (NOAA Zone WAZ328)
climate.typical: Cool maritime climate; summers dry with marine fog possibility. Winters wet with frequent precipitation. Lower elevation climbing area sees less snow than higher peaks.
climate.spring: May-June sees increasing temperatures and lower snowpack. Rainfall decreases as summer approaches. Expect 60-65F highs.
climate.summer: July-September best for climbing. July driest (0.74 in precip), highs 73-74F. August warmer but coastal fog (foggust). September still stable, highs 68F.
climate.fall: September-October sees transition; increasing rainfall and cooler temps. October typically gets 3+ inches precipitation.
climate.winter: November-April: wet, cold, snowy at elevation. Unsafe for sport climbing due to ice, snow, and frequent storms.

emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Forest Recreation Office (360) 956-2400
emergency.nearestHospital: Olympic Medical Center, Port Angeles (60+ minute drive from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable in the upper Dungeness area. Verizon is the most reliable carrier in the region. Evacuations from the climbing area require helicopter rescue from nearby ridges due to terrain. Nearest aid post or ranger contact is the Upper Dungeness Trailhead facility (basic first aid supplies, no staff). Sequim Fire District and Clallam County SAR coordinate mountain rescues.

waypoints.name: Bad-Tempered Prom Queen / Junior's Farm Start

hierarchyNote: CRITICAL: The given peak name "Main Peak" does not match the real peak. All research sources confirm Hourglass Gully is on Mt.
