Index (Mount Index), a well-documented 5,991 ft peak in the North Cascades near Skykomish, WA. The peak should be named "Mount Index" or "Mt. Index" not "Main Peak". Coordinates (47.77453, -121.58094 per Mountain Project) and elevation (5,991 ft per Wikipedia) correctly identify Mt. Index Main Peak.
blurb: Mount Index is a dramatic trio of granodiorite spires rising steeply above the western Cascades near US Route 2, composed of 29-35 million-year-old Index granite from the Index Batholith. The Main Peak (southernmost of the three spires) at 5,991 feet features the Hourglass Gully winter route, first climbed in 1963 by Jim Pritchard, Stan Jensen, and Cecil Bailey.

routes.overview: A classic winter alpine ascent of Mount Index's Main Peak via a steep gully and exposed ridge, offering 1,900 feet of technical snow/ice climbing with significant avalanche and rockfall hazard.
routes.beta: Begin at Lake Serene trailhead, ascend 2,000 feet through forest to the lake (4.1 miles), then climb steeply southeast toward the summit. The Hourglass Gully presents 40-60 degree snow and ice, with a crux 60-degree pitch on the East Ridge. The gully constricts dramatically near the exit with near-vertical sections. Timing is critical; the window for this route is narrow (early March through early May). After May, melted conditions expose loose rock and create severe bushwhacking challenges.
routes.approach: From US Route 2 near Gold Bar (milepost 35.2), turn south onto Mount Index Road and drive 0.3 miles to the Lake Serene trailhead parking area (600 ft elevation). The Lake Serene trail climbs 2,000 feet over 4.1 miles of well-maintained forest trail, reaching the north end of the lake. From Lake Serene, the route diverges right (west) around the lake through avalanche debris and old snow toward a saddle between Lake Serene and the East Ridge of Mount Index. This approach section gains approximately 3,500-4,000 additional feet over rough terrain.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route. The East Ridge section is often rappelled on descent, requiring two 30-meter ropes to navigate the steep, forested terrain. Climbers typically boot-ski or downclimb the Hourglass Gully in spring snow conditions, with small wet slides common. The final descent returns via the Lake Serene trail.
routes.descentText: The descent mirrors the ascent route but is significantly more technical. On the upper East Ridge, climbers set up two 30-meter rappels using existing rap slings in trees. Descending the Hourglass Gully in spring conditions often involves boot-skiing down 40-50 degree slopes with small wet slides. The steep constriction requires careful down-climbing or short rappels. The ridge traverse back to the lake saddle is exposed with 1,000-foot drops to the northeast side. Return to Lake Serene via the approach slope and descend the 4.1-mile trail by headlamp if a very early start was not made.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive from Puget Sound region (1-2 hours) to Lake Serene trailhead. Hike Lake Serene trail (2-3 hours to lake). Camp at Lake Serene if doing this as a two-day climb. Day 2: Early pre-dawn start (2:30 AM typical) from trailhead or 4:30 AM from lake camp. Approach saddle (2-3 hours), climb Hourglass Gully (2-3 hours), traverse East Ridge (1-2 hours), reach summit by mid-morning (8-10 hours from car). Descend via rappels and gully (2-3 hours). Return to Lake Serene (2-3 hours). Hike out (2-3 hours), reaching car by dark. Total single-day push: 15+ hours; two-day with camp: more manageable timing.
routes.bail: Retreat is possible at any point before committing to the Hourglass Gully constriction. If conditions are icy rather than snowy at the gully entrance, turn around and descend via the approach route. If caught in avalanche terrain with unstable snow, descend immediately to the lake saddle. The crux 60-degree pitch on the upper ridge has limited retreat options—do not commit unless conditions and team ability are certain. In whiteout conditions, reverse course before reaching the gully. Avoid descending the gully in deteriorating conditions; retreat to saddle instead.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time based on daylight hours and team fitness. For winter ascents starting at 2:30 AM from the car, a noon turnaround from the summit area (giving 8-10 hours for approach and climb) allows 4-6 hours for descent and the 4.1-mile trail before dark. If the turnaround time is reached before gaining the upper Hourglass Gully constriction, descend immediately. Do not attempt the technical upper gully in fading light.
routes.bestSeason: The optimal window is early March through early May, with March-April being ideal for stable snow conditions and solid ice. By mid-May, the gully and upper snow slopes become wet, slushy, and prone to loose rock. Summer (June onward) is not recommended due to exposed wet rock, extensive bushwhacking on the ridge, and extreme difficulty; the gully becomes a steep waterfall with hazardous wet rock. Winter ascents (December-February) are possible but require extreme caution; early-season snows are deep and stable, but temperatures are cold and storms frequent. Spring ascents benefit from consolidated corn snow that hardens overnight, providing the most stable climbing conditions.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage in the Index/Gold Bar area is generally good (71-93% coverage depending on carrier, with T-Mobile 5G covering ~72%). However, once on the peak and in high alpine terrain, cell coverage becomes unreliable or nonexistent, especially on the ridge or in the gully. Satellite communicators (PLBs, inReach) are strongly recommended for emergencies; traditional cell phones cannot be relied upon for rescue coordination from the peak.
routes.fa: Jim Pritchard, Stan Jensen & Cecil Bailey (1963)
routes.rappels: 2 × 30m (30-meter ropes used for descent through forested ridge sections)
objHaz: avalanche: massive paths, especially upper gully
routes.detailedRack: Two 30-meter ropes (single-rope rappels, doubled for security), dynamic belay anchors or pickets at rap stations. Ice axe (technical climbing tool essential for step-cutting and self-arrest). Crampons (rigid or semi-rigid, microspikes insufficient). Rock protection: slings for trees (abundant), quickdraws for anchors. Rappel device (ATC-style or equivalent). Helmet (mandatory due to rockfall and ice fall hazard). Ice screws (optional, for belayed pitches if team prefers; not typical for this route).
routes.proNeeds: Protection is tree-based and natural feature slings; climbers typically clip existing rap slings on descent or place slings around large trees. Rock is granodiorite; belay anchors should be bombproof trees or solid rock features. No bolts; this is entirely natural protection and tree anchors.
whatToBring: Insulated jacket (down or synthetic for winter)
Insulated gloves and inner glove liners
Pack (60-70L for two-day, or 35-40L if dayhike push)
Food and water (2-3 liters carrying capacity)
Map and compass (USGS Mount Index quad)
GPS device or smartphone with offline maps
First aid kit (blister treatment, pain relief)
Avalanche beacon, probe, shovel (for group climbing)
proTips: Start at 2:30-3:00 AM from the parking lot to maximize daylight on technical sections and descent
Scout the Hourglass Gully entrance from the saddle before committing; if it looks icy and thin, consider bail
On the upper East Ridge, use boot-pack steps for downclimbing if conditions allow; this avoids the need for rappels if snow is stable
Bring a small stove and water bottles; staying hydrated in dry winter air is essential even in cold conditions
Coordinate with a partner or register with local ranger station; rescue from this terrain is expensive and complicated
The Lake Serene trail can be snowy even when lower elevations are clear; microspikes or snowshoes may be necessary for the 4.1-mile approach
Avoid the Hourglass Gully if the upper section shows heavy icefall or hanging ice hazard; rock fall is common from the right (south) side
Practice rappelling before this route; cold hands and gloves make rappel setup slow and error-prone
watchOut: Massive avalanche terrain: the gully and surrounding slopes run thousands of feet; check NWAC avalanche forecast and look for recent slide activity
Icefall: rock and ice regularly shed from the gully's right side; wear helmet always
Exposure: the upper East Ridge has 1,000-foot drops; a fall is fatal; move carefully and use rope for self-belay if nervous
Weather: a clear dawn can turn to storm by afternoon; this area is subject to rapid fog and wind; start early and be willing to bail
Rockfall during descent: loose rocks are common as snow melts; communicate constantly and keep eye on climbers below
knownHazards: Active avalanche paths dominate the Hourglass Gully; the gully is a natural funnel for snow and debris from the upper peak
Icefall and rockfall on the right side of the Hourglass Gully are frequent; this section is the objective hazard pinch-point
Exposure on the upper East Ridge is extreme; a slip or rope failure is likely fatal; this is a serious mountaineering undertaking
Dense brush on the ridge between the lake saddle and the summit is a major objective hazard in summer; winter and spring ascents avoid this via snow
Cornices form on the summit ridge; avoid stepping on cornice edges, especially on the north side

pitchDetail.notes: 4.1 miles, 2,000 ft gain, well-maintained trail through forest, may be snow-covered in early season requiring microspikes

pitchDetail.pitch: Approach slope to East Ridge saddle
pitchDetail.notes: 3,500-4,000 ft gain over rough terrain, ascend right (west) around Lake Serene, cross avalanche debris and old snow, navigate through trees and brush to reach saddle
Steep brushy ridge with intermittent cliffs, approximately 500-600 ft; crux of ridge; dense brush in summer, snow-covered in winter/spring
Short steep rock/snow section, approximately 40-60 feet, sometimes snow-covered, sometimes wet rock depending on season; rappel station at top
Approximately 300-400 ft gain, consistent 40-45 degree snow slope; avalanche terrain; wide and open
The route narrows dramatically and steepens to 45-60 degrees; sections approach near-vertical ice exit; technical climbing or chopping/step-cutting; approximately 200-300 ft; crux of the entire route

pitchDetail.pitch: Hourglass Gully upper section to ridge
pitchDetail.notes: Exit gully onto upper ridge, 150-200 ft of moderate snow slope, less technical than constriction but still steep and exposed
Final 100-150 ft to summit, knife-edged on exposed ridge with 1,000-foot drops to NE; cornices possible; grand exposure
First rappel from anchors at top of crux pitch, 30m × 2 ropes; descent of steep snow/ice
Descend gully via boot-skiing or careful downclimbing; small wet slides common in spring; 400-600 ft descent
Final rappel(s) or downclimbing through forested ridge section back to saddle; use existing slings on trees for anchors; 500-600 ft

pitchDetail.pitch: Descent: Approach slope to Lake Serene
pitchDetail.notes: Descend rocky/snowy slope back to lake, 3,500-4,000 ft; may be icy; caution for avalanche terrain

road.name: Mount Index Road (from US Route 2)
road.status: Open year-round
road.driveNote: From US Route 2 at milepost 35.2 near Gold Bar, turn south onto Mount Index Road (well-signed). Drive 0.3 miles to the Lake Serene trailhead parking lot. Road is paved and maintained. In winter, expect occasional snow and ice; drive carefully. Parking is free and typically available except on popular weekends.

climate.forecastZone: NWAC Cascade Crest (CAS), specifically lower west-slope Cascades near Stevens Pass Zone
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate. Orographic lift forces Pacific weather systems upward, creating high precipitation. Temperatures on the peak typically range from 20-35°F in winter, 25-40°F in spring. Wind is frequent and can be extreme, especially on the summit ridge.
climate.spring: Spring (March-May) is the best climbing season. Snow consolidates into corn, and stable avalanche conditions prevail. Daytime temperatures rise to 25-35°F. May sees increasingly wet conditions and rain-on-snow avalanche hazard. Late spring brings loose rock and wet gully sections.
climate.summer: Summer (June-September) features melted gully sections, exposed wet rock, and severe brush. Avalanche hazard decreases but rockfall increases. Temperatures rise to 45-60°F. Afternoon thunderstorms are common and dangerous on exposed ridge.
climate.fall: Fall (September-November) sees increasing snow, wind, and storms. Not typically climbed due to short daylight, unpredictable weather, and early seasonal storms. Possible light snow cover.
climate.winter: Winter (December-February) features deep, stable snow, but extreme cold, high avalanche hazard from new snow, and frequent storms. Temperatures drop to 10-25°F. Days are short (8 hours daylight). Early-season (December-January) snow depth can exceed 10 feet at higher elevations.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District, U.S. Forest Service
access.closures: No formal closures, but avalanche hazard may close access informally during active unstable conditions. Check NWAC forecasts and Skykomish Ranger District updates before travel.

timing.recommendedStart: 2:30-3:00 AM from trailhead for day push; 4:30 AM from lake if camping

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911 (Valley Communications Center for King County coordinated dispatch). Snohomish County also responds. Cell coverage unreliable on peak; use satellite communicator if possible.
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger District Station, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288. Phone (360) 677-2414. Hours: Wed-Fri 9 AM-12 PM and 1-4 PM (closed 12-1 lunch). Offers permits, maps, and trail information.
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe (primary for Skykomish Valley), Phone (360) 794-7497. Cascade Valley Hospital in Arlington, Phone (360) 435-2133. Providence Hospital in Everett (Snohomish County), Phone (425) 261-2000. Helicopter evacuation is possible but weather-dependent; rescue is expensive and should be considered as absolute last resort.
emergency.notes: This peak is a serious alpine undertaking with difficult terrain and high objective hazard. Rescue is slow and expensive; self-rescue capability and decision-making to bail are essential. Register your climb plans with someone reliable, and carry a satellite communicator or PLB. Cell coverage fails above ~3,000 feet on peak.

blurb: Mount Deception is the second-highest peak of the Olympic Mountains at 7,788 feet, located in the northeastern Olympics within Olympic National Park. Composed of Eocene basaltic pillow lava, the peak is notorious for extremely loose, rotten rock and steep, exposed terrain requiring solid mountaineering skills. Originally named Mount Holmes by early explorers, it was renamed Mount Deception due to the frequent cloud cover that confused climbers attempting to locate routes on the peak.
hierarchyNote: Peak location and hierarchy verified as accurate: Mount Deception is in Royal Basin, northeastern Olympics, Olympic National Park, Jefferson County, Washington. Geographic coordinates and elevation verified against multiple authoritative sources.

objHaz: Extremely loose and rotten basaltic pillow lava rock
Rockfall and rock slides (especially afternoon and late season)
Poor rock quality requiring constant vigilance
Fatalities have occurred from rockfall and exposure
routes.overview: The standard route via Royal Basin approaches Mount Deception from the northeast, scrambling over loose moraine and extremely rotten basalt terrain to the summit. This is Class 2-3 scrambling that is steep, exposed, and notorious for the worst rockfall hazard in the Washington Alps.
routes.beta: Approach via Upper Dungeness Trailhead. Follow Upper Dungeness Trail 1 mile to Royal Basin Trail junction. Continue 7.2 miles to Royal Lake (5,100 ft) and 0.8 miles more to Upper Royal Basin (5,600 ft). From Upper Basin camp, climb southwest over glacial moraine ~1 mile to base of northeast face. Ascend steep slope (30-50 degrees) to ridge crest, then follow ridge to summit. Class 2-3 scrambling with extreme loose rock. 5+ hours from camp. Helmet mandatory. Descent reverses route with rockfall watch.
routes.approach: Trailhead: Upper Dungeness Trailhead (47.8776N, 123.1369W, 2,500 ft). From Highway 101 near Sequim, take Luella Road east and follow Forest Service Road 2870 for 8.7 miles to parking. Hike Upper Dungeness Trail 1 mile through forest to Royal Basin Trail junction. Follow Royal Basin Trail 7.2 miles up Royal Creek through forest and open terrain, gaining 2,500 feet to Royal Lake (5,100 ft) with established campsites. Continue 0.8 miles through alpine meadows past Shelter Rock to Upper Royal Basin camp (5,600 ft). Total approach: 7-8 miles, 3,000 feet, 5-7 hours.
routes.descent: Descend carefully from summit via northeast ridge and moraine route, exercising extreme caution for rockfall. Use rising traverse to minimize exposure. Reach Upper Royal Basin after ~2 hours. Continue down Royal Basin Trail to Royal Lake (1-1.5 hours), then descend to Upper Dungeness Trail junction (2-2.5 hours), and finish to trailhead (15-20 minutes). Total descent: 5-6 hours.
routes.descentText: Descent is the most dangerous section due to extreme rockfall hazard on rotten pillow lava slopes. From summit, descend carefully northwest following ascent route, alert for rock displacement from above. Many climbers report rockslides triggered by minimal contact. Use rising traverse pattern to reduce exposure to falling rock. Place feet deliberately and do not dislodge rocks. Maintain 50+ feet spacing between partners to avoid simultaneous exposure. Helmets remain on throughout descent. Reach Upper Basin after 2 hours, then descend 3-4 hours to trailhead via established trail.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Upper Dungeness Trailhead. Hike 7.2 miles to Royal Lake camp (5-7 hours, 2,500 ft gain). Establish camp, refill water, rest. Day 2: Continue 0.8 miles to Upper Royal Basin camp (5,600 ft) or push for summit. If summiting: depart pre-dawn 4:00am, climb 1 mile over moraine to northeast face base, scramble 5+ hours to summit, return to camp (4-5 hours descent). Total 10-11 hours. Day 3: Descend via Royal Basin Trail to trailhead (5-6 hours, 10+ miles).
routes.bail: From Upper Royal Basin: descend to Royal Lake via same route, then follow Royal Basin Trail to trailhead. This is the safe bailout for all situations. From higher on northeast face: if weather worsens or rockfall intensifies, descend immediately to Upper Basin via safest available route. Rope essential if crevasse self-rescue needed. Emergency: Call Wilderness Information Center (360-565-3100) via satellite communicator.
routes.turnaround: For camp-based summit attempt, parties not reaching summit by 11:00am should abandon attempt and descend immediately. This ensures adequate daylight and energy for safe descent. Afternoon rockfall hazard increases significantly from sun-loosened rock, making descent increasingly dangerous after mid-afternoon. If weather deteriorates at any point, descend immediately regardless of summit proximity.
routes.bestSeason: Best months: June through early July, before peak rockfall season. Snow/ice coverage stabilizes the otherwise extremely loose terrain. Early-season ascents strongly preferred. Climbers attempting August-September consistently report nightmarishly loose rock and intense rockfall hazard. Plan ascents before July 15 if possible. Late-season climbers should expect worst conditions and extreme rockfall hazard.
routes.comms: Cell phone reception is unreliable or absent in Royal Basin and above. No reliable cellular coverage. Satellite communicators (Garmin InReach, SPOT) strongly recommended for emergency communication. Report route plans to Wilderness Information Center (360-565-3100) before departure with expected return time.
routes.detailedRack: Helmet is absolutely mandatory and non-negotiable. Glacier crossing requires ice axe and crampons. Bring 2-3 ice screws or snow pickets for emergency anchor points. Mountaineering backpack (50-60L) for multi-day Alpine camp. Climbing boots with aggressive tread or rock shoes for secure footwork on loose terrain. Trekking poles useful for approach but abandoned at camp for summit push. Most climbers bring lightweight rope (30-50m) for emergency situations though standard route does not require roped climbing.
routes.proNeeds: Solid mountaineering skills and experience essential. Must be competent with self-arrest and emergency self-rescue. Excellent rock climbing experience (Class 3-4 level) required due to exposure and loose rock. Comfort scrambling on extremely rotten rock and navigating exposure is critical. Superior route-finding skills needed as path varies with snow conditions. Falls on Class 2-3 exposed terrain frequently result in serious injury or death.

pitchDetail.notes: Upper Dungeness and Royal Basin trails: well-maintained, 7.2 miles, 2,500 ft to Royal Lake
Royal Lake to Upper Royal Basin: 0.8 miles through meadows, 500 ft gain
From camp southwest over glacial moraine ~1 mile to northeast face base, loose boulders, 1-1.5 hours
Snow or scree slope, 30-50 degrees, prone to rockfall, 1.5-2 hours to ridge
From ridge to summit: scrambling on rotten rock, narrow ridge, exposed, loose terrain, 30-45 min

road.name: Forest Road 2870
road.status: Passable
road.seasonalGate: Generally open April-December; check USFS for winter closures
road.driveNote: From Highway 101 near Sequim, take Luella Road east 1 mile to Palo Alto Road, left 1.1 miles to forest boundary. Turn right on FSR 2880, past Dungeness Forks 1 mile, stay left on FSR 2870 for 8.7 miles to trailhead. Narrow forest service road; high-clearance vehicle recommended.

climate.forecastZone: Olympic Mountains (WAZ513)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate, mild temperatures, frequent Pacific moisture. Rain shadow location, less precipitation than western Olympics.
climate.spring: April-May: Snowmelt, high creek water, snow above 6,000 ft possible. Temperatures 40-50F at base, freezing summit. Frequent rain.
climate.summer: June-September: Mild (60-70F base, 30-40F summit). Early: snow/ice present. Mid: mostly snow-free. Late: dry conditions, extreme rockfall hazard.
climate.fall: September-October: Cooling (45-55F), increasing precipitation. Snow above 6,000-6,500 ft by late September.
climate.winter: November-March: Heavy snow at elevation, below-freezing temperatures. Route unclimbable without winter mountaineering skills.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park / NPS and Olympic National Forest / USFS
access.fees: $5/day or $30/year Northwest Forest Pass for parking. $5/person/night backcountry permit (children under 15 free).
access.permit: Overnight permit required for Olympic National Park camping. May 1-Sept 30: quota system with 30-day advance reservations (360-565-3100). Outside this period: first-come, first-served.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required at trailhead.

emergency.rangerStation: OLP Wilderness Information Center (360-565-3100); Visitor Center (360-565-3130)
emergency.nearestHospital: Olympic Medical Center, Port Angeles (360-385-5700), 60 miles from trailhead
emergency.notes: Route in Olympic National Park with established rescue capabilities. Cell reception unreliable. Satellite communicators recommended. Jefferson Search and Rescue coordinates with NPS. Helicopter evacuation possible from Upper Royal Basin. Report plans to Wilderness Center before departure.

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00am from camp

proTips: Climb early June-July when snow stabilizes loose terrain
Start pre-dawn to minimize rockfall exposure
Use rising traverse routes rather than direct descent
Maintain helmet throughout climb
Keep partners 50+ feet apart for rockfall safety
Summit turnaround time 11:00am absolute
Crampons mandatory if snow present
watchOut: Helmet absolutely essential - worst scramble for rockfall
Loose rock cascades easily from minimal disturbance
Falls result in serious injury or death
Fatalities have occurred from rockfall and exposure
Never use direct descent due to rockfall
Crevasses on glacier - step lightly when crossing
knownHazards: Fatalities occurred from rockfall and exposure
Pillow lava: poor quality, constant loose rock
Falls without arrest difficult to stop on loose terrain
Afternoon rockfall increases significantly
Crevasse hazards on glacier
Snow slopes up to 50 degrees - avalanche risk
Route-finding difficulty in poor visibility
routes.corrections: Database peak elevation listed as 7,786 feet; authoritative sources consistently report 7,788 feet. Route grade listed as 'Class 2 (steep and exposed)' but actual conditions are Class 2-3 scrambling with exposure comparable to Class 3 terrain due to extremely loose rock. This is one of the most dangerous Class 2-3 scrambles in Pacific Northwest.

blurb: Castle Peak (8,343 ft) is a remote granite summit in the Hozameen Range of the North Cascades, located one mile south of the Canada border in the Pasayten Wilderness. The peak was first ascended by a USGS survey party in 1904 and features an imposing north face with multiple technical rock routes.
hierarchyNote: CRITICAL DATA INTEGRITY FLAG: The route 'Southeast Face' (id: wa_southeast_face_2) does not exist as a documented climbing route. After comprehensive research of Mountain Project, SummitPost, Wikipedia, and AAC publications, no such route is recorded. Castle Peak itself is confirmed real and correctly located (8,343 ft, Hozameen Range, Pasayten Wilderness). The route appears to be mislabeled, undocumented, or a data entry error. Documented routes include Herrington-Hirst (IV 5.10d, 2009), The Drawbridge (IV 5.10+ A1, 2017), and North/South Face routes, but no Southeast Face technical route exists.

routes.beta: Route not documented in any climbing database after comprehensive research across Mountain Project, SummitPost, Wikipedia, and American Alpine Club sources. Castle Peak does feature a non-technical Southeast Ridge scramble (Class 2-3) and East Ridge Couloir approach, but no technical Southeast Face route is recorded.
routes.bestSeason: Jun-Sep

blurb: Mount Torment is a glaciated 8,120-foot alpine peak in the North Cascades' Boston Basin, renowned for the famous Torment-Forbidden Traverse. First ascended on August 23, 1946, its name reflects the first ascent party's ordeal climbing in intense heat with minimal water. The peak sits above pristine talus meadows within North Cascades National Park.
hierarchyNote: Mount Torment is correctly identified at 8,120 ft (2,472 m) in Boston Basin, North Cascades National Park, Skagit County, Washington. Coordinates 48.5123, -121.0768 verified across multiple authoritative sources (Mountain Project, Peakbagger, climbing guides).

routes.overview: A classic alpine rock route on the southeast face and south ridge of Mount Torment, combining quality alpine rock with glacier approach and descent. The climbing is moderate with excellent exposure.
routes.beta: The South Ridge begins with an approach via the Taboo Glacier from Boston Basin high camp. After a short glacier crossing, climbers traverse under a spur ridge and ascend slabby terrain to reach the south ridge notch. The route follows the left side of the ridge via short dihedrals, ledges, and chimneys for roughly 1,000 feet to the summit. The final sections circumnavigate the peak via the southeast face with brief class 4 scrambling before reaching the airy northeast ridge summit.
routes.approach: Approach to Boston Basin trailhead: From Marblemount, drive 22 miles on Cascade River Road (first 10 miles paved) to parking pullout at mile 22 (elevation 3,200 ft). Follow abandoned road 3/4 mile to old mine site, then locate climber's path ascending steeply through rainforest with multiple stream crossings. Reach lower camp in ~2 hours with 2,500 ft elevation gain. Continue northwest on climber's path to upper/high camp at ~6,400 ft (another 1-1.5 hours, ~1,000 ft gain). From high camp, short scramble of roughly 1 hour takes you to the notch at the base of the South Ridge.
routes.descent: Downclimb and rappel the south ridge using multiple short, traverse-style rappels (5-6 double-rope rappels typical). Alternatively, traverse southeast to the SE face, downclimb slabs, then cross via class 2 saddle into Torment Basin. Watch for moat at cliff base in mid/late season—crossing can be the hardest crux of descent. Use remaining webbing to supplement fixed rappel stations.
routes.descentText: From the summit, carefully downclimb the northeast ridge (exposed, class 3-4). At the ridge notch, traverse left across exposed ledges before reaching rappel stations on the southwest face. Make 5-6 double-rope rappels over steep slabs, descending approximately 800-1,000 feet. Before the final rappel over the lowest cliff, look left for the last station. The moat between the cliff base and glacier can be difficult to cross in late season; scout this hazard carefully during descent. Once on the glacier, walk down slabs along the east side of the south ridge to class 2 saddle crossing back to Torment Basin or down toward the glacier.
routes.bestSeason: July through September offers the best conditions with stable weather and minimal snow/ice. Late June is possible but may have more snow. Early October occasionally viable in dry years.
routes.detailedRack: Light trad rack with small to medium cams (#0.5-2), wires for thin seams, and abundant slings (shoulder and full-length runners). Bring 2-3 foot extensions for rappel anchor setup. Carry several extra carabiners for anchor flexibility. Ice screws not typically needed on this route unless climbing through snow in early season.
routes.proNeeds: Climbers must be comfortable with alpine rock scrambling and 5th class protection placement. Ability to lead or follow 5.4-5.5 trad pitches in an alpine setting with a pack is essential. Solid belay and rappel technique required; fixed anchors may be absent.
proTips: Scout the approach and moat crossing during ascent to plan fastest descent route
Bring light approach shoes for the scree/talus sections; swapping out at base saves weight
In mid-late season, chimney climbs along the ridge may be filled with snow requiring ice axe work
The route has good natural anchors but bring quickdraws and slings to set up efficient belays
Respect the exposed sections and moving quickly is safer than dawdling
watchOut: Bergschrunds and moats at the base of the southeast face can be impassable in mid/late season and may present the hardest climbing of the entire route
Loose rock is common; test all holds and be cautious of dislodging material onto partners below
The notch traverse is exposed; helmets mandatory to prevent rockfall injuries
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; monitor conditions from Boston Basin and retreat if necessary
knownHazards: Loose alpine rock throughout the route, especially on the SE face
Significant moat/bergschrund at cliff base in mid/late season
Exposed scrambling sections with significant drop-offs
Changing conditions on the Taboo Glacier approach require crevasse awareness

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park / U.S. Forest Service
access.permit: Permit required year-round for overnight camping in North Cascades National Park
access.closures: Cascade River Road may close seasonally due to flooding, rockslides, or snow; verify current conditions at NPS visitor center before driving.

routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable or absent in Boston Basin. Satellite communicators (InReach, Spot) are recommended for emergency communication.

climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapidly changing weather. Expect temperature swings of 20+ degrees F between day/night. Strong winds common on exposed ridge. Afternoon thunderstorms possible July-August.
climate.spring: Longer snow approaches; bergschrund/moat hazards increase; avalanche risk on approach; typically not recommended before late June
climate.summer: Most stable conditions July-August. Cooler temps in early July. Increasing moat/bergschrund difficulties by late August. September weather more variable.
climate.fall: September offers stable conditions early month; increasingly unreliable by October. Early snow possible mid-late September.
climate.winter: Not recommended; extreme avalanche and crevasse hazards; poor visibility; sub-zero temperatures; limited daylight

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Ranger Station, Marblemount area
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (360-424-4111, ~50 miles southeast); Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (360-436-7000, ~40 miles south)
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable; use satellite communicator for emergencies. Coast Guard helicopter rescue possible from Boston Basin in good weather. Evacuation may require ground team assistance for evacuating injured climber to trailhead.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00 AM from high camp for day climb

waypoints.name: Boston Basin Trailhead / Cascade River Road Pullout
Boston Basin High Camp (under Unnamed Glacier)

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Arrive at Boston Basin trailhead (mile 22 Cascade River Road, parking at 3,200 ft). Hike climber's trail with full pack to lower camp (~2 hours, 2,500 ft gain). Establish camp and rest. Day 2: Optional acclimatization day or move to high camp. Ascend high camp (~1.5 hours, 1,000 ft gain). Scout approach and relax. Day 3 (Climb Day): Start pre-dawn from high camp, scramble 1 hour to south ridge notch, climb 3-3.5 hours to summit via South Ridge route, descend 2.5-3 hours via rappels back to high camp. Day 4: Break camp and descend to trailhead via climbing trail.
routes.turnaround: If summit is not reached by early afternoon from the notch, retreat to high camp and attempt again the following day or exit the area. Do not push into darkness on the exposed ridge.
routes.bail: From the south ridge notch, retreat down-glacier via the Taboo Glacier approach. From any point on the ridge, descend southeast to the SE face and work down to the main glacier. All retreat options require snow travel and crevasse awareness.
routes.corrections: The given data indicated 5.6 grade and 6 pitches for the South Ridge route. Authoritative Mountain Project data confirms this route is 5.4 with 4 pitches. Additionally, the given data showed 'Grade III' which aligns with Mountain Project Grade III classification (confirmed).

routes.overview: One of the most sought-after alpine traverses in North America, connecting two glaciated 8000+ foot peaks via an exposed ridge. The crenellated ridge features knife-edge scrambling, technical rock, snow/ice traverses, and significant exposure. A complete alpine experience requiring full mountaineering skills.
routes.beta: The Torment-Forbidden Traverse is a multi-day alpine adventure combining Mount Torment's South Ridge approach with a mile-long exposed ridge traverse to neighboring Forbidden Peak (8,815 ft), finishing via the famous Forbidden West Ridge (5.7, 50 classic). The route crosses the Taboo Glacier, climbs to Mount Torment's summit via the south ridge, then descends and traverses the Forbidden Glacier before ascending the knife-edge ridge with exposure on both sides. Climbers must move quickly, efficiently protect themselves, and be capable of 5.6 rock climbing with a medium pack over 12+ continuous hours.
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive 22 miles via Cascade River Road to Boston Basin trailhead parking at mile 22 (3,200 ft elevation). Follow climber's path up Boston Creek to lower camp (2 hours, 2,500 ft), then upper/high camp at ~6,400 ft (1.5 hours, 1,000 ft). From high camp on day 1 of climb sequence, traverse west under spur ridge to reach Taboo Glacier, then ascend glacier to south ridge notch (~1 hour from high camp).
routes.descent: From Forbidden Peak's summit, descend the West Ridge Couloir (exposed rappel sections) via fixed anchors and rappels (typically 6-8 double-rope rappels over ~1,000 ft) back to the saddle between the peaks. Continue traversing and descending the ridge/glacier systems back to Boston Basin high camp via the Forbidden Glacier crossing. Total descent 2.5-3.5 hours of active climbing/rappelling, then walk back to high camp.
routes.descentText: Rappel and downclimb the West Ridge Couloir from Forbidden Peak's summit via approximately 6-8 double-rope rappels (rope retrieval required between stations). Descend approximately 1,000 feet of steep terrain to the saddle. From the saddle, traverse back across the Forbidden Glacier (watch for moats and crevasses in late season—rope team recommended). Descend via the known glacier route back to Boston Basin high camp (~1.5-2 hours of glacier travel). Use ice axes for glissading where safe; watch for hidden crevasses and weak snow bridges.
routes.iceGrade: AI2-AI3 (variable depending on seasonal snow/ice conditions)
routes.rappels: 6-8 double-rope rappels on Forbidden West Ridge descent, plus multiple rappels on traverse
routes.fa: Ed Cooper and Walt Sellers, July 26, 1958
routes.face: South Ridge of Torment, Forbidden Glacier, Crenellated Ridge traverse
routes.bestSeason: July and early August offer the most stable weather and reliable snow conditions. Late June is possible but may have heavier snow. September becomes increasingly unpredictable with earlier storms. Avoid March-May and October-May for high avalanche risk.
gear: Pickets or deadman for snow anchors
Medium trad rack (cams #0.5-3, wires) for technical rock sections
Ascender for rope management on steep snow
routes.detailedRack: Dual 60m ropes strongly recommended for efficient pitch climbing and rappelling. Alpine rack includes: 6-8 ice screws (for snow anchors and crevasse rescue), 2-3 pickets or snow deadman for glacier travel, medium trad rack (cams through #3, wires), 8-10 locking carabiners, multiple slings (especially shoulder runners for quick protection). Bring cordelette (6-7mm nylon, 5-6 meters) for master-point anchors. Extra webbing for rappel station repairs.
routes.proNeeds: Climbers must have prior alpine experience in the Cascades, solid 5.6 rock-climbing ability, and comfort with steep snow/ice travel. Self-rescue capability essential: ability to set anchors, rappel efficiently, manage rope systems, and assess crevasse hazard. Must be able to move quickly and safely on exposed terrain for 12+ hours without significant rest.
proTips: Tag-line one rope for efficiency on rappels to avoid losing gear in crevasses
Simul-climb the lower-grade ridge sections where safe to save time
Bring wrist crampons or microspikes for easier approach travel
Scout bergschrunds during ascent to identify safest crossing for descent
Bivy gear essential if you fall behind schedule; don't push exhausted onto technical terrain at night
Pre-rig rappel anchors on descent where possible to save time
Communication breakdown is common on long pushes; establish hand signals and turnaround times in advance
watchOut: Exposed ridge sections (Sidewalk in the Sky) have 1000+ foot drops on both sides; protection is sparse and exposure is extreme
Waterfalls below the glacier can be deadly during high-water periods or warm afternoons—monitor and plan retreat if hazard is increasing
Late-season crevasses and moats are difficult and dangerous; early-morning glacier crossing recommended
Fatigue on day 2-3 increases decision-making errors on exposed terrain; consider tactical bivouac over pushing dangerous hours
Loose rock is prevalent on the ridge; helmet essential to protect from dislodged material
knownHazards: Waterfalls and slabs below glacier can produce fatal rockfall during afternoon melt or high water
Crevasses on Taboo and Forbidden Glaciers vary seasonally; rope-team essential
Ridge exposure (1000+ foot drops both sides on 'Sidewalk in the Sky' section) allows minimal protection
Fatigue-induced errors increase dramatically on day 2-3 of continuous alpine movement
Weather deterioration rapid; afternoon thunderstorms likely mid-summer

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park
access.permit: Permit required year-round for overnight camping in Boston Basin and Torment Basin; reserve in advance through park system
access.closures: Cascade River Road may close seasonally; verify access before trip. Park may restrict climbing for bear safety during certain periods (typically June 1-November 15 require bear canister food storage).

routes.comms: Cell service is absent in Boston Basin. Satellite communicator (InReach or Spot) strongly recommended for emergency communication; two-way radios useful for party coordination across ridge.

climate.typical: High alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Expect 15-25 degree F temperature swings day-to-night. Strong wind on exposed ridge sections (20+ mph gusts common). Afternoon thunderstorm development likely July-August.
climate.spring: Poor conditions; heavy snow, avalanche hazard, longer approaches, cold temperatures, limited visibility, crevasse hazards extreme
climate.summer: Best season. July offers most stable weather. Early August still good but moat/bergschrund hazards increase. Late August more variable; increasing storm probability.
climate.fall: Early September possible in dry years; weather increasingly unstable. October typically too dangerous (early snow, avalanches, cold).
climate.winter: Extreme hazards; not recommended. Avalanche danger, crevasse hazards at maximum, sub-zero temps, limited daylight.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Ranger Station (Marblemount area ranger contact)
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (360-424-4111, ~50 miles); Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (360-436-7000, ~40 miles)
emergency.notes: Helicopter rescue possible from Boston Basin on clear-weather windows. Cell service absent; satellite communicator mandatory. Evacuation of injured climber likely requires multi-party effort or extended waiting for rescue services.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn from high camp (3:00-4:00 AM), Day 2 of climb sequence

waypoints.name: Boston Basin Trailhead / Cascade River Road Pullout
Boston Basin High Camp (launch for TFT)

routes.itinerary: Day 1: Arrive Boston Basin trailhead. Hike 2-3 hours to lower camp (2,500 ft gain). Establish camp, explore, and rest. Day 2: Move to high camp (1.5 hours, 1,000 ft). Scout glacier approach and orient to ridge. Rest and prepare gear. Day 3 (Summit Push): Pre-dawn start from high camp, 1 hour to notch, 3-4 hours climbing Mount Torment via South Ridge, summit by mid-morning. Descend/traverse Mount Torment (1-1.5 hours), cross Forbidden Glacier (1-1.5 hours), ascend knife-edge ridge (2-3 hours), reach Forbidden Peak summit late afternoon or evening (tactical bivy if needed). Total 12-16 hours active movement. Day 4 (Descent): Descend Forbidden West Ridge via rappels (3+ hours), return to high camp, rest at camp or break camp and hike down. Day 5: Descend trailhead (2-3 hours).
routes.turnaround: Establish turnaround time (e.g., 2:00 PM from Mount Torment summit) and stick to it rigidly. If not on Forbidden Peak by late afternoon of day 3, prepare for legitimate bivy or retreat. Do not push exhausted onto exposed terrain at night.
routes.bail: From Mount Torment summit, descend to saddle and return via Forbidden Glacier to high camp (manageable in daylight). From the ridge proper, only safe descent options lead back to saddle and glacier. From Forbidden Peak, descent via West Ridge is mandatory and time-consuming; plan accordingly. All bail options require rope and glacier travel expertise.
routes.corrections: The given data indicated a 5.7 rock grade for the Torment-Forbidden Traverse. Mountain Project (authoritative) and multiple climbing guides confirm 5.6 as the technical rock grade. The alpine grade 'D' and overall 'IV' commitment match sources (Alpine Grade D confirmed). Note: Grade descriptions vary; some sources list 'IV-V' reflecting the committing, exposed nature and overall route complexity, but the rock technical grade is 5.6 not 5.7.

hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy noted: given data states 8,748 ft; verified sources (Wikipedia, PeakVisor, USGS) consistently report 8,745 ft. Peak is correctly placed in Okanogan Range, Pasayten Wilderness, Okanogan County - hierarchy verified. Named after Lage Wernstedt (1878-1959), Forest Service surveyor. Second-highest peak in Pasayten Wilderness after Jack Mountain (9,075 ft); #1 in Okanogan Range by elevation.
blurb: Mount Lago is the highest summit of the Okanogan Range and second-highest peak in Washington's Pasayten Wilderness at 8,745 feet. Located on the eastern Cascade crest, this complex alpine peak features pink porphyry-scarred slopes, rocky ribs, headwalls, and cradles the easternmost glacier in the Cascade Range. First ascended by Hermann Ulrichs and Dick Alt in 1933.

routes.overview: The standard scramble route to Mount Lago's summit via Shellrock Pass. A Class 2 alpine scramble with loose rock requiring constant attention, featuring approximately two hours of scrambling from Shellrock Pass to the 8,745-foot summit.
routes.beta: From Shellrock Pass camp (7,500 ft), look for a red talus gully east of the Carru-Lago saddle. The route ascends the gully roughly 400 feet, then traverses right staying below the buttress and above treeline to the southwest face, then follows the west ridge staying on the south side to the summit. Loose talus and orange scree dominate; stay left on firm rock sections. Descend via the same route or glissade down the north side via snowfields when available (typically June-August). The mountain is a pile of crumbly choss with complex slab sections and numerous rocky ribs.
routes.approach: From Slate Pass Trailhead (48.7380, -120.6787; 6,900 ft), take Robinson Creek Trail / Buckskin Ridge Trail #498 down the Middle Fork Pasayten River valley for approximately 16 miles total. The approach passes Fred's Lake (good camping at ~6,800 ft), then proceeds into the Eureka Creek valley where the main challenge is a creek crossing requiring balance on slick logs (especially during snowmelt). Continue ascending from Eureka Creek through talus fields to reach Shellrock Pass (~7,500 ft), where a half-acre campsite bench exists just southwest of the pass. From trailhead to Shellrock Pass typically requires 1-2 days of backpacking depending on fitness and creek conditions.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route or, when snow exists (typically June-August), glissade down the north side via snowfields. Total descent takes approximately 2-3 hours to Shellrock Pass. Avoid the north cliffs which are enormous and dangerous.
routes.descentText: Descend via the ascent route, reversing the talus gully and ridge traverse back to the Carru-Lago saddle meadow (~7,100 ft). The descent requires careful footing on loose rock. When snow coverage is adequate (typically June-August), descend via glissade down north-facing snowfields; this can significantly reduce descent time but requires ice axe and self-rescue skills. Total descent from summit to Shellrock Pass camp: approximately 2-3 hours. Return to trail and descend to camp via the lower ascent gully to complete the round trip (8-10 hours total climbing and descending from Shellrock Pass camp).
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Slate Pass Trailhead to Fred's Lake camp (approximately 6-8 hours, 6-8 miles, 1,500 ft elevation gain). Day 2: Fred's Lake to Shellrock Pass camp (approximately 4-6 hours, 4-5 miles, 1,000 ft gain). Day 3: Shellrock Pass to Mount Lago summit and return to Shellrock Pass camp (approximately 8-10 hours). Day 4: Shellrock Pass to Slate Pass Trailhead (approximately 10-12 hours return descent). Total trip: 3-4 days minimum; 4-5 days recommended for acclimatization and weather flexibility.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward: descend the ascent route back to Shellrock Pass camp, then continue down to Fred's Lake or return directly to Slate Pass Trailhead (approximately 8-10 hours from Shellrock Pass). No technical crux requires special rescue ability; loose rock and creek crossings are the main hazards.
routes.turnaround: Plan a turnaround time that allows 4-5 hours from Shellrock Pass camp to summit and back, ensuring adequate daylight for the approach descent. If higher camps are not reached by mid-afternoon on approach day, descend to established campsites (Fred's Lake, Shellrock Pass bench) rather than attempting summit on marginal daylight.
routes.bestSeason: July through September is optimal. June can still have significant snowmelt making creek crossings dangerous; August is typically excellent with stable weather and manageable snow; September offers longer views with autumnal clarity but increasing weather instability. While the peak could technically be climbed in other seasons, Harts Pass Road and Slate Pass Trailhead are typically accessible July-September only, with snow closures December-June. Winter ascents are possible but require alpine ski/mountaineering skills and current avalanche forecasting.
routes.proNeeds: No protection is needed for this scramble route. Belay equipment is not required. Traction and stability are the primary concerns.
routes.detailedRack: Not applicable for Class 2 alpine scramble. Climbers on talus typically carry minimal gear: ice axe (for self-arrest when snow/ice is present), microspikes or crampons for early-season icy sections, and a lightweight rope (~30-50m) for parties uncomfortable on steep talus or for rappelling if glissading on the descent (though not essential).
gear: Ice axe (optional; essential if snow present)
Microspikes or crampons (for icy sections, especially early season)
Sturdy scrambling boots with good ankle support
Gaiters (to keep talus out of boots)
Lightweight trekking poles (optional; helpful on talus)
Rope 30-50m (optional; for self-rescue or belay if uncomfortable)
whatToBring: Tent with good ventilation (talus doesn't stake well; ground pad critical)
Sleeping bag (rated to at least 15F; August can still bring frost)
Lightweight food (dehydrated meals, energy bars, nuts)
Sun protection: sunscreen SPF 50+, hat, sunglasses (altitude + talus reflection)
Insulating layers: base layers, fleece, windproof jacket
First aid kit with blister treatment, pain reliever, anti-diarrheal
Navigation: map, compass, GPS (offline map critical; cell service unreliable)
Toilet paper and trowel (pack out waste per wilderness protocol)
Repair kit: duct tape, patches, cordage
proTips: Scout the red talus gully approach during daylight; the lower ascent gully can be hard to identify from above
Plan creek crossing during early morning when water levels are lowest; snowmelt is highest midday
Shellrock Pass camp bench fills quickly on popular weekends; arrive mid-morning on Day 2 to secure the best spot
Glissading requires ice axe proficiency; if uncomfortable, reverse the ascent route
Combining Lago with nearby Mount Carru (8,595 ft) via the Carru-Lago saddle is popular and feasible in one day from Shellrock Pass (Class 2-3, adds 3-4 hours)
Carry extra food; weather delays are common in this remote area, and early season snowmelt can swell creeks unexpectedly
watchOut: Loose talus throughout: every step requires attention; rockfall risk to lower parties if climbing in groups
Creek crossing during snowmelt (June-July): swollen Eureka Creek has dangerous crossing on slick logs; consider waiting for lower flows or bringing lightweight boots for creek crossing
Enormous north cliff face: do not traverse beyond the west ridge to the north; the descent potential is lethal
Early season: light snow observed at 7,500 ft even in August; bring microspikes and ice axe
Weather: rapid afternoon thunderstorms are common; plan summit push for early morning departure from camp
Remote location: cell coverage is nonexistent; satellite messenger recommended for solo climbing or if group exceeds 4-5 climbers
knownHazards: Constant talus/scree exposure requiring vigilant footwork and stable handholds
Creek crossings with slick logs during high water (June-July snowmelt)
Exposure to fall on the west ridge approach (Class 2-3 terrain)
Altitude and thin air: 8,745 ft elevation may cause mild AMS symptoms in unacclimatized climbers
Weather: isolated peak in Pasayten Wilderness receives variable conditions; wind, rain, snow can develop rapidly
Loose rock hazard: pink porphyry scarring indicates fractured geology; rockfall is possible from upper sections
routes.comms: No cell phone coverage in Pasayten Wilderness. Satellite communication device (Garmin inReach, PLB) strongly recommended for solo or small groups. Emergency contact via satellite 24/7.

road.name: Harts Pass Road / Forest Road 5400
road.status: Gravel, variable conditions; recently reopened after 2026 storm damage
road.seasonalGate: Typically accessible July-September; closed December-June due to snow. Gate at Slate Peak (7,488 ft) prevents vehicle access beyond that point.
road.driveNote: From Winthrop, take State Route 20 east 14 miles to Mazama junction. Turn right to Mazama, then left onto Lost River Road (becomes Harts Pass Road). Drive 13.5 miles to Harts Pass Guard Station, turn right on FR 5400-600 toward Slate Peak. Slate Pass Trailhead is approximately 3.5 miles from this turn, at the second switchback past the horse loading area. Road conditions: pavement to Mazama, gravel beyond; rough/rocky in places; high-clearance vehicle recommended but not required for standard passenger cars (take slowly). Methow Valley Ranger District: (509) 996-4000.

climate.typical: Alpine/subalpine climate with short summers and long winters. Eastern slopes receive significantly less precipitation than western Cascades due to rain shadow effect. Extreme weather variability: thunderstorms common in afternoon during summer; night temperatures can drop below freezing even in August. Wind is persistent at altitude.
climate.spring: Road typically still closed due to snow. Spring climbing not practical due to avalanche hazard, stream crossings at peak snowmelt volume.
climate.summer: Best season. July: Variable weather, significant snowmelt making creek crossings challenging. August: Most stable; daytime temps 50-65F at summit, night freezing. September: Excellent visibility, cooler nights, but increasing storm frequency.
climate.fall: Late August-September optimal. October: Possible early snow, shortening season. Harts Pass Road may close by late October.
climate.winter: Harts Pass Road closed. Peak receives heavy snow. Winter ascents possible but require full alpine mountaineering skills, avalanche rescue capability, and ski descent proficiency.

access.landManager: U.S. Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.permit: Free, non-quota backcountry permit required for overnight use in Pasayten Wilderness. Self-issue at Slate Pass Trailhead.
access.fees: None for wilderness use. Northwest Forest Pass recommended ($5/day or $30 annual) for parking at Harts Pass area trailheads; check current requirements.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass may be required for parking at Harts Pass area; verify with Methow Valley Ranger District
access.closures: Harts Pass Road typically closed December through June due to snow. Pasayten Wilderness permits self-issued year-round, but road access limits practical climbing season to July-September.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00 AM from Shellrock Pass camp to summit (allows 2 hours up, 2-3 hours down with safety margin before dark)

emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District: (509) 996-4000 (Mon-Fri 8am-4pm, closed 12:30-1:30pm). After-hours: reach through county dispatch.
emergency.nearestHospital: Three Rivers Hospital in Brewster, WA (~45 miles northwest): (509) 689-2517. Mid Valley Hospital in Okanogan, WA (~50 miles northwest): (509) 422-3500. North Valley Hospital in Tonasket, WA (~35 miles northeast): (509) 486-2112
emergency.notes: Pasayten Wilderness is extremely remote; helicopter rescue would require specialized request through county emergency services. Evacuation from Shellrock Pass to road is 12+ miles on foot/stretcher. Satellite rescue communication strongly recommended. Cell phones have zero coverage in wilderness.

routes.corrections: Elevation discrepancy: Given database lists 8,748 ft; verified USGS, Wikipedia, PeakVisor, and other authoritative sources list 8,745 ft. Corrected to 8,745 ft. First ascent date (1933 by Ulrichs and Alt) is confirmed by Wikipedia; an earlier possible ascent by Lage Wernstedt in 1926 is mentioned in summitpost.org but is unconfirmed. FA attribution to Ulrichs/Alt/1933 is accepted as most reliable. 'South Slope - South Face' naming: sources typically refer to this as 'Standard Route,' 'South Slope approach,' or simply 'Class 2 scramble'; formal route name may vary. Coordinates: 48°49'46"N 120°32'15"W verified against PeakVisor (48.829075°N, 120.537491°W) and found to be consistent.

blurb: Lexington Tower is a 7,560-foot granite pinnacle in the North Cascades approximately one mile south of Washington Pass. Part of the iconic Liberty Bell Group, it features excellent golden granite climbing on all aspects with routes ranging 5.7-5.12. First ascended July 5, 1954, by Tim Kelley and Dick McGowan via the North Face.
hierarchyNote: Peak elevation listed as 7621 ft in database but authoritative sources (Wikipedia, Mountain Project, SummitPost) show 7560 ft. Corrected. Peak correctly positioned in Liberty Bell Group within Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest.

routes.fa: Steve Marts and Don McPherson, June 1966
routes.rappels: 7 double-rope rappels or walk-off west side
knownHazards: Pitch 5: exposed 30-foot traverse with marginal protection
Wide crack offwidth sections require specialized technique
Fixed 2x4 wedge protection on pitch 6
routes.overview: An 800-foot alpine rock route on excellent granite with sustained 5.9+ climbing following a crack and chimney system with significant exposure.
routes.beta: Start on low-angle dihedral left of Lexington-Minuteman gully. Ascend via cracks and chimneys; crux is pitch 5 with exposed traverse. Upper pitches are off-width and chimney climbing. Exit via notch, descend west gully. Standard trad rack essential; doubles to #3, single #5-6 cams critical.
routes.approach: Park 1.2 miles east of Washington Pass on Highway 20. Follow climbers trail 30-45 min through timber to boulder/scree field. Approximately 1.2 miles and 1000 ft gain to base. Two approach options depending on snow.
routes.descent: From exit notch, traverse west into gully on skiers left. Short 20-foot 3rd-class scramble (no rappel needed). Traverse right to cattle trail connecting to Blue Lake Trail. Faint climbers trail shortcuts to Highway 20.
routes.descentText: Exit via notch (route does not summit). Head west into gully on skiers left for short 3rd-class scramble. Traverse right along cattle trail to Blue Lake Trail system. Where trail turns downward, locate faint climbers trail shortcutting directly to Highway 20 pulloff, saving approximately one mile.
routes.itinerary: Early start essential. Arrive parking 5-6 am, hike to base 6:30-7:00 am, climb 4-5 hours, begin descent by noon-1 pm, return to car 2:00-3:00 pm.
routes.bail: Most exposed on pitch 5. Options: (1) Lower from anchors if unsafe. (2) Rappel down route using existing anchors. (3) Descend west gully to cattle trail. Safest option once above mid-route is to descend rather than continue.
routes.turnaround: Sustained exposed alpine climb. Turn around if: weather deteriorates, you cannot climb 5.9+ fluidly, you will not descend by daylight, wind or temperature unsafe. Safe window roughly 6-8 hours.
routes.bestSeason: June-September. Early June has snowmelt and wet rock. July-August optimal with most stable weather despite afternoon thunderstorms. September reliable but increasingly marginal. Before late May or after early October, snow/ice at base plus short daylight makes climb significantly more committing.
routes.comms: Cell service unreliable. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, PLB) recommended.
routes.detailedRack: Standard rack to 6 inches: doubles to #3 cams, single #5 cam, single #6 cam (critical for off-widths), one or two #4 cams (frequent use pitches 5-6). Bring 10+ slings. Optional #7-8 for experienced climbers. 60-meter double ropes recommended.
routes.proNeeds: Solid trad climbing with emphasis on crack climbing and offwidth technique. Pitch 5 has marginal protection. Wide sections require comfort with less pro and proper stemming/chimneying technique.
proTips: Start very early (5-6 am) for daylight
Pitch 5 is crux and most exposed; scout beforehand
Off-width is technique not strength; use stemming
Bring approach shoes, leave at base
Walk-off descent saves time
Afternoon thunderstorms common Jul-Aug
watchOut: Pitch 5 traverse requires focus with marginal protection
Weather can deteriorate rapidly
Approach crosses rockfall paths
Descent is exposed and technical

road.name: North Cascades Highway State Route 20
road.status: Seasonally open
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed November-April
road.driveNote: Park 1.2 miles east of Washington Pass on south side. Northwest Forest Pass needed for Blue Lake Trailhead alternative.

climate.forecastZone: Washington Cascades North Central
climate.typical: Maritime alpine with significant precipitation
climate.spring: May-June: snowmelt, wet rock, variable weather
climate.summer: July-August: optimal with occasional afternoon thunderstorms
climate.fall: September: generally reliable but increasingly unstable
climate.winter: October-April: heavy snow, avalanche danger, highway closed

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass for Blue Lake Trailhead
access.closures: Highway 20 closed November-April

emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Community Hospital (1.5+ hours); PeaceHealth Sedro-Woolley (2+ hours)
emergency.notes: Remote location with SAR response time 1-2+ hours. Cell unreliable. Satellite communicator recommended.

pitchDetail.notes: Low-angle dihedral with class-3 ledge traverse
Crack and dihedral with significant runouts
Dihedral with roof, undercling through
Crux: 30-foot exposed traverse then difficult lieback
Off-width with fixed 2x4, two bolts
Chimney to off-width section
Lower-mid 5th class chunky rock
4th class catwalk traverse to exit notch

blurb: Unicorn Peak at 6,971 feet is the highest summit in the Tatoosh Range, a sub-range of the Cascade Mountains in Mount Rainier National Park. The peak's distinctive summit horn resembles a unicorn's head and horn (officially named in 1913), and offers commanding views of Mount Rainier and much of southwest Washington. The 6-mile round-trip day climb combines hiking, snow travel (early season), and low 5th-class rock climbing on solid granite.
hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: the input elevation (6940 ft) is close to but lower than the official USGS elevation of 6971 ft, confirmed by multiple authoritative sources (Wikipedia, USGS GeoNames, SummitPost, WTA). Consider updating to 6971 ft. Peak location in Tatoosh Range near Mount Rainier is correct.

routes.overview: The classic approach to Unicorn Peak via Snow Lake, combining hiking and snow travel through an upper basin to the summit horn, finishing with Class 3-4 scrambling and a 40-50 foot Class 4-5.4 rock pitch on the summit pinnacle.
routes.beta: From Snow Lake, traverse west on a climbers trail and ascend a steep chute with talus (summer) or good snow (spring). Aim for the saddle on the west side above the basin. From there, hike east just south of the ridge crest to a sub-summit dome, then approach the summit pinnacle from the south side. Multiple route variations exist on the 50-70 foot summit tower, ranging from Class 4 (east side, exposed) to 5.6 (direct south face).
routes.approach: Start at the Bench and Snow Lake Trailhead on Stevens Canyon Road, 1.5 miles east of Reflection Lakes. Follow the maintained Snow Lake Trail 1 mile to Snow Lake (1 mile, ~350 ft gain). Continue 0.2 miles where maintained trail ends, then follow a path through talus and tree-covered terrain to an obvious gully. Ascend the gully (Class 3, steep slope 40 degrees) onto a heather bench path. Traverse the moraine on the left side of small Unicorn Glacier using a path below cliffs. Total approach: ~3 miles, ~2500 ft gain to the basin.
routes.descent: Downclimb Class 3 rock to the two rappel stations on the summit. Use a 30-meter rope for the first rappel to the notch on the south side (some parties prefer 60m to avoid mid-rope concerns). Second station allows rappel down the ascent route to Class 3 terrain. Some parties have rappelled directly down Class 3 rock after first rappel, but downclimbing on east side is exposed to the north face and not recommended.
routes.descentText: The standard descent involves rappelling the summit climbing route via established slings/anchors. A 30m rope reaches from the summit to the top of Class 3 terrain via the south side rappel station; a 60m rope provides more security and reduces potential rope-stretch concerns. After rappelling to Class 3, downclimb the gully and return via the ascent route through the basin and back down the Snow Lake Trail. Return distance: ~3 miles from basin to trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from Snow Lake Trailhead to Snow Lake (1 mile, 1-2 hours). Continue up gully and traverse to basin (2 miles, 2-3 hours from lake). Climb summit horn (1-2 hours). Descend via rappel and Class 3 downclimbing (1-2 hours). Return down to trailhead (2-3 hours).
routes.bail: Early season: if snow conditions deteriorate or visibility becomes poor, retreat via the ascent gully before committing to the final climb. Mid-summer: if talus/scree becomes too difficult, consider bailing at the basin saddle and avoiding the final rock pitch. Late season: wet talus hazard may force retreat before reaching the basin.
routes.turnaround: In early/mid-season, a reasonable turnaround time would be early afternoon (1-2 PM) to ensure adequate daylight for descent and rappel. Mid-summer conditions with significant talus may require even earlier turnaround to complete the descent before dark.
routes.bestSeason: May through September for general accessibility. May-June is optimal for snow travel on the approach (much faster than scree-bashing). June is excellent for a mix of manageable snow and good weather windows. July-early September are viable but involve tedious talus hopping on the approach; the ascent becomes an abysmal steep, chossy scree exercise once snow has melted. Late May and June offer the most enjoyable climbing conditions with firm snow. Avoid mid-summer scree if possible.
routes.face: South face of summit horn; main ascent from south side
routes.rockGrade: 5.0-5.6 (depending on route variation; classic south face is 5.4)
routes.fa: USGS party, 1910 (first recorded ascent of Tatoosh Range peaks; specific route FA not documented)
knownHazards: Loose talus fields dominate mid-summer approach, making scree descent dangerous; early season with snow cover is much safer
East side Class 4 route has significant exposure to the north face (climb-thru not recommended for descent)
Maritime influenced snow tends to be wet and heavy; avalanche danger exists in winter and early spring on steep slopes
Late season (July+) snow melt creates dangerous wet talus and slide paths near Snow Lake
routes.proNeeds: Single 30m rope minimum (60m recommended to reduce rope-stretch concerns on double-rappel descent); harness, rappel device, slings/runners for anchors, rock shoes recommended for leader on 5.4 pitch.
routes.detailedRack: Light rack suggested: A few runners/slings for anchors at summit, single 30-60m rope, harness, rappel device (ATC or similar), locking carabiner. Ice axe and crampons recommended mid-summer for steep gully. Helmet strongly advised given objective rockfall hazard.
proTips: Early season (May-June) is vastly superior to mid/late summer; the snow-covered gully on the approach is easy and enjoyable, while mid-summer scree is a grind
Bring rock shoes or sticky boots if leading the technical pitches; the granite is solid and easier with proper footwear
The classic south face route (5.4) is better rock quality and more enjoyable than other variations
Consider the Tatoosh Traverse if weather and time permit; the traverse to Foss Peak and The Castle offers great alpine scenery
Rappel slings have been moved in recent years; check current anchors carefully before committing to rappel
Firm, good-quality granite makes this an excellent rock climbing objective when approached via other Tatoosh peaks (Foss, The Castle)
watchOut: Do not climb the steep headwalls to the left of the main gully in the approach basin; stay in the main gully
Watch for moat development late season; may require extra time/technical bridging near the saddle
Loose, chossy rock on the approach and descent makes mid-summer travel dangerous; good protection of party members during exposed sections essential
Rockfall hazard is real; helmet use non-negotiable on summit tower and gully sections

access.landManager: National Park Service (Mount Rainier National Park)
access.fees: $30 per vehicle, $25 motorcycle, $15 individual (America the Beautiful annual pass $80)
access.permit: No permit required for day climbing; climbers above 10,000 feet must register (Unicorn does not trigger this as it is below 10,000 feet)
access.passRequired: Mount Rainier National Park entrance pass required
access.closures: Stevens Canyon Road typically closed November-May; check nps.gov for current seasonal status

timing.recommendedStart: 7:00 AM (early start critical for safe descent and to avoid afternoon weather)

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road
road.status: Seasonal, typically open June-October
road.seasonalGate: Road closes in late October through May due to snow; check nps.gov before planning trip
road.driveNote: Paved road with good access to trailhead parking (15-20 vehicles). Located 1.5 miles east of Reflection Lakes on Steven's Canyon Road. From west via Paradise: drive 2.7 miles after turning onto Stevens Canyon Road. From east: drive 16.1 miles to Snow Lake sign. Road is passable only during dry season.

climate.forecastZone: Pierce/Lewis County Cascades (NWS forecast area)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate; cool, wet, maritime-influenced. Significant precipitation on west side of Cascades, especially winter. Rarely below 0F or above 80F.
climate.spring: Snow cover dominates; approach gully may have consolidated snow and ice; avalanche risk elevated on steep slopes; frequent weather changes
climate.summer: June-July: mix of snow patches and talus; thunderstorms common mid-afternoon. July-Sep: mostly clear of snow; hot days but cool nights; afternoon thunderstorm risk still present
climate.fall: Increasingly unsettled weather; snow possible at higher elevations by late September; window closing for safe conditions
climate.winter: Heavy snow, avalanche danger, ice axe/crampons essential; Stevens Canyon Road closed

emergency.rangerStation: Mount Rainier National Park Rangers: (360) 569-2211 (5:30 AM - 11 PM, 8 PM in winter)
emergency.nearestHospital: Tacoma General Hospital (MultiCare), 315 MLK Jr Way, Tacoma, WA 98405. Level II Trauma Center, 24-hour emergency department. ~90 minutes drive from Snow Lake trailhead.
emergency.notes: If overdue from listed return time, rangers will initiate automatic search. Call 911 for life-threatening emergencies. Sat-phone or personal locator beacon recommended given minimal cell coverage.

routes.comms: No cell service expected on trail; service may exist in immediate Paradise developed area only (very limited extent into wilderness). Do not depend on cell coverage for safety; consider satellite messenger or GPS device for emergency communication.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep chute gaining 2500 ft from Snow Lake to upper basin. In summer, talus travel; in spring/early summer, consolidated snow provides faster ascent. Main gully clearly visible; avoid steep headwalls to left.

40-50 feet of rock climbing on solid granite. Rock shoes recommended for leader. Right-side approach (adjacent variation) is easier Class 3-4 with generally solid rock and good handholds. Direct south face (5.4-5.6) is longer and more exposed. East side Class 4 option is quite exposed to north face (descent not recommended).

routes.corrections: Elevation: Given input 6940 ft is close but lower than official USGS elevation of 6971 ft; all major sources (Wikipedia via USGS GeoNames, WTA, SummitPost, PeakBagger) consistently report 6971 ft. Grade terminology: Original route data said 'Class 3-4 summit block' and 'rockGrade: 5.0-5.2'; research confirms route has Class 4-5.6 rock pitch, with classic south face route being 5.4 and variations ranging 5.0-5.6. Approach is Class 3. These represent substantial rock climbing, not a simple scramble.

routes.routeName: South Route via Lake of the Angels
routes.overview: The South Route approaches via the scenic Putvin Trail to Lake of the Angels basin (~3.9 miles), then scrambles north via the West Ridge to the main summit. This is the standard and most popular ascent of Mount Stone.
routes.beta: From Hamma Hamma River Road, hike the steep Putvin Trail (FST 813) through Olympic National Forest gaining nearly 3,000 feet in less than 4 miles. Emerge in an alpine basin at Lake of the Angels (~4,900 feet), then scramble Class 3 terrain north of a rock rib, ascending the West Ridge with some exposed class 4 moves to reach the South Peak summit.
routes.approach: From Hoodsport, WA, drive US Highway 101 north 13.5 miles to signed Hamma Hamma River Road (FS Road 25) turnoff. Follow FS Road 25 for ~11.5 miles (paved initially, then well-maintained dirt) to Putvin Trail trailhead marked near mile marker 12 and Putvin's grave historical marker. Parking is available in a small roadside area.
routes.descent: Retrace the ascent via the West Ridge back to Lake of the Angels, then return via Putvin Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the West Ridge by reversing the approach, carefully managing the steep terrain and unstable rock common to the Olympics. The descent retraces the ascent back through the Class 3 scramble to the basin, then descends the steep Putvin Trail. Route-finding is generally straightforward in descent if weather is clear; in poor visibility or early season snow, navigation can be challenging and an altimeter/GPS is advised.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September is optimal. By late June, most snow has melted at lake elevation (5,000 feet), though patches may persist into July. Wildflowers peak mid-June through mid-August. The route is generally snow-free by July but can retain patches in shaded gullies and couloirs through August. October presents clear skies and fewer crowds but deteriorating weather patterns.
routes.proNeeds: Rock scrambling experience, comfortable with exposure, navigation skills (map, compass, GPS). Helmets recommended due to rockfall hazard and loose rock common to Olympic basalt.
routes.detailedRack: Helmets are essential due to loose Olympic basalt and rockfall hazard. Ice axes recommended for travel June-July when snow and ice patches are common. Microspikes useful for frozen snow. Trekking poles recommended for steep descent on Putvin Trail.
whatToBring: 10 essentials (map, compass, sunscreen, first aid, knife, fire starter, shelter, extra clothes, extra food, extra water)
Backpack (20-25L for day hike, 40L+ for overnight)
proTips: Start early to avoid afternoon thunderstorms common in Pacific Northwest summers.
The Putvin Trail is extremely steep; break into short segments with rest stops to manage fatigue.
Lake of the Angels is the last reliable water source and popular camping spot; arrive early for campsites in peak season.
Navigation is straightforward in clear weather but challenging in fog or snow; mark route mentally as you ascend.
The South Peak (true summit at 6,612 ft) is distinct from the Middle Peak to the west; do not mistake the Middle Peak for the main summit.
Olympic basalt rock is notoriously unstable; test handholds and footholds before committing weight.
Combine with Mount Skokomish (nearby) for a two-peak day or overnight.
Avoid the South Couloir in late spring when snow/ice may obscure instability.
watchOut: Unstable Olympic basalt rock—much will become scree if touched; treat all holds as suspect until tested.
Exposure on West Ridge—some class 4 moves with air below; helmets essential.
Rockfall hazard from other climbers and loose rock; wear a helmet and avoid congestion at narrow sections.
Early season snow and ice; late snow patches may hide unstable terrain.
Navigation challenges in poor visibility; the terrain is steep and complex.
Afternoon thunderstorms common June-September; plan to descend by early afternoon.
Tight time constraints for day ascents; the round trip is 9-10 miles with 5,400 feet of gain—allow 10-14 hours total.
Water sources are sparse after leaving Lake of the Angels; carry enough water for the climb.
knownHazards: Unstable Olympic basalt rock common throughout the route; pillow lava can be slippery when wet, and much loose talus becomes scree if disturbed.
Exposure on West Ridge scramble; several sections have air below and require secure holds.
Snow and ice persist into July and can hide unstable rock or trap climbers.
Thunderstorm activity is common in summer afternoons, bringing lightning and rapid weather deterioration.
Steep Putvin Trail descent strain; injuries on descent are most common due to fatigue.
Wildlife: Black bear and mountain goat encounters documented; make noise while hiking.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates during the climb, retreat immediately back to Lake of the Angels and bivouac if necessary. The lake basin provides shelter and water. If descent is unsafe due to snow or icing, carefully descend to the basin to wait out conditions; if stranded overnight, the Lake of the Angels camping area is suitable. If injury occurs on the West Ridge, climbers can descend via the approach used to ascend; assist the injured party and call for rescue if needed (satellite messenger recommended).
routes.turnaround: Experienced parties should set a firm turnaround time no later than 1:00 PM to avoid descending in darkness or afternoon thunderstorms. If the summit is not reached by early afternoon, descend to ensure a safe exit before weather deterioration and darkness.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is unreliable or absent in the alpine basin and summit area; do not depend on cellular service. A satellite messenger (Garmin InReach, SPOT) is strongly recommended for emergency communication. Alert someone of your plan and expected return time before starting the hike.

timing.recommendedStart: Dawn (5:00-6:00 AM) to complete descent before afternoon storms

road.name: Hamma Hamma River Road (FS Road 25)
road.status: Open summer/fall; subject to seasonal winter closure
road.seasonalGate: Typically open May-November, but verify current status with Olympic National Forest before traveling.
road.driveNote: Paved to mile marker 7, then well-maintained dirt road to Putvin Trailhead at ~11.5 miles from US 101. Standard passenger vehicles can pass, but high-clearance preferred after rain. No fuel or services at trailhead; nearest gas/supplies in Hoodsport (20 miles).

climate.forecastZone: Olympic Mountains, Mason County, WA
