climate.typical: Wet maritime climate with frequent cloud cover. Summer is the dry season, but afternoon thunderstorms are common. Annual precipitation is high (100+ inches on windward slopes).
climate.spring: Snowmelt and flooding common; trails can be muddy and impassable with creek crossings. Snow persists on upper elevations through May.
climate.summer: Warmest and driest season. Daytime temps at summit: 40-55°F. Afternoon thunderstorms frequent; morning ascents essential.
climate.fall: Clear, stable weather through September; deteriorating conditions October onward. Frost possible above 5,000 feet by mid-October.
climate.winter: Hamma Hamma Road closed by snow; peaks buried under 10+ feet of snow. Route completely inaccessible.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (summit), Olympic National Forest (approach via Putvin Trail), Mount Skokomish Wilderness (lower basin)
access.fees: Parking at Putvin Trailhead requires a recreation pass; Northwest Forest Pass ($5/day or annual). Camping at Lake of the Angels is free in wilderness.
access.permit: No climbing permit required. Group size limited to 12 people + 8 livestock. Campfires prohibited above 3,500 feet.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking (https://www.fs.usda.gov/main/olympic/passes-permits)
access.closures: Hamma Hamma Road subject to seasonal closure by snow (typically November-April). Check Olympic National Forest alerts before visiting.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Mason County Sheriff non-emergency: (360) 426-4441; emergency: 911. Dispatch center: MACECOM (24/7) handles search and rescue coordination.
emergency.nearestHospital: Mason General Hospital, 2100 Sheridan Lane, Shelton, WA 98584. Phone: (360) 426-1611. ~30 miles from trailhead.
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Forest Quilcene Ranger District Office: (360) 765-2200 (Tue-Wed, 9 AM–3 PM). For emergencies, call 911.
emergency.notes: Cell service unavailable in alpine areas; satellite messenger essential for backcountry rescue coordination. Closest help: Hoodsport Fire & EMS (240 N Standstill Dr., Hoodsport, WA 98548; (360) 877-9882). Evacuation from the basin would require helicopter; climbers should self-rescue to lower elevations if possible.

routes.routeName: Putvin Trail / Lake of the Angels Scramble
routes.overview: Direct ascent via the very steep Putvin Trail (FST 813) to Lake of the Angels, then scramble to the summit via standard Class 3 rock. This is essentially the same destination as the South Route but emphasizes the trailhead-to-base approach via Putvin Trail.
routes.beta: Putvin Trail is notorious for its steep pitch, gaining nearly 3,000 feet in less than 4 miles. The trail negotiates several short sections of Class 3 scrambling over wet rocks and tree roots before reaching the Lake of the Angels basin. This route is more direct than any traverse and is the most commonly used summer climbing route on Mount Stone.
routes.approach: From Hoodsport, WA, drive US Highway 101 north for 13.5 miles to the signed Hamma Hamma River Road (FS Road 25). Follow FS Road 25 for approximately 11.5 miles to the Putvin Trail trailhead parking area (marked near mile marker 12, with Putvin's grave historical marker nearby). The trail (FST 813) begins at roughly 1,200 feet elevation and ascends very steeply along Boulder Creek through dense Olympic forest. Gain nearly 3,000 feet of elevation in less than 4 miles, passing an old road crossing at 1.3 miles and emerging into the Mount Skokomish Wilderness. Reach the Lake of the Angels basin (4,900 feet) at approximately 3.9 miles. From the lake, climb north via the West Ridge to the summit (another ~1,700 feet of scrambling ascent).
routes.descent: Retrace the route: descend the West Ridge and Class 3 scramble back to Lake of the Angels, then descend the steep Putvin Trail to the trailhead. The descent of Putvin Trail is arduous and technically demanding due to steepness and loose terrain; take frequent breaks and exercise extreme caution on wet or icy sections.
routes.descentText: From the summit, reverse the scramble descent via the West Ridge, carefully managing unstable rock and exposure. Reach Lake of the Angels basin. The Putvin Trail descent is the crux of this route: the steep pitches and loose terrain make it easy to slip or to dislodge rocks. Travel slowly and deliberately, testing footing constantly. In wet conditions or with afternoon fatigue, the descent becomes significantly more hazardous. An ice axe (early season) can be useful for self-arrest in steep snow sections. Reach the trailhead approximately 4-5 hours after leaving the lake.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September. By late June, Putvin Trail is generally snow-free, though stream crossings may be swollen with snowmelt. July-September is most stable. October is possible but weather deteriorates rapidly.
routes.proNeeds: Strong scrambling ability; experience on steep rocky terrain. Comfortable with Class 3 moves and light exposure. Good fitness essential due to relentless steep terrain on Putvin Trail.
gear: Sturdy hiking boots with aggressive tread
routes.detailedRack: Helmets essential—the steep Putvin Trail corridor is narrow and rock hazard is real from other parties and from natural rockfall. Trekking poles dramatically improve safety and reduce knee strain on the brutal descent. Ice axe recommended for travel through lingering snow (June-July). Good boots with deep lugs are critical for traction on wet, loose terrain.
whatToBring: 3+ liters water (trail is dry above 3,000 feet until Lake of the Angels)
proTips: Use trekking poles on descent to save knees and improve stability on loose terrain.
Break the Putvin Trail into segments; it is mentally and physically taxing at the relentless steepness.
Start before dawn to summit by early afternoon and avoid afternoon thunderstorms.
The trail can be treacherous when wet; rain in the morning can make midday travel dangerous.
Scout loose rocks before stepping; the trail is narrow and dislodged rocks are a hazard to others.
If attempting to day-hike, plan a 10-14 hour outing and consider a headlamp for the lower trail in case of late descent.
Overnight at Lake of the Angels to split the effort and enjoy the alpine environment.
watchOut: Putvin Trail is extremely steep, especially in the first 2 miles; pace descent carefully to avoid injury.
Wet rocks and tree roots create slipping hazards; assume all ground is slippery, especially in the forest.
Rockfall from above is possible if other parties are on the trail; announce your presence.
Loose scree on lower sections can trigger a slide if you step incorrectly.
The trail is narrow and winding; poor visibility in fog makes navigation difficult.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common; descend off exposed ridge by early afternoon.
Fatigue increases accident risk on descent; stop frequently to rest.
knownHazards: Steep Putvin Trail with loose rock and wet conditions; ankle injuries and falls are common on descent.
Unstable Olympic basalt throughout the route.
Rock fall hazard from other climbers and natural rockfall.
Creek crossings can be dangerous during high-water conditions (snowmelt, heavy rain).
Narrow trail corridor provides limited bailout options in emergency.
routes.bail: If the Putvin Trail conditions are unsafe (icy, flooded, or weather deteriorating), turn back immediately and return to the trailhead. If climbers become exhausted on the descent, rest at Lake of the Angels and climb down carefully, using trekking poles and deliberate footing. If injury occurs on the steep trail, self-rescue to lower elevations if possible; cell service is unreliable; a satellite messenger (SPOT/InReach) is essential for summoning rescue.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time of 1:00 PM at the latest to ensure the summit is reached and a safe descent begun in daylight. If turnaround time passes before reaching the summit, descend to Lake of the Angels, rest, and descend Putvin Trail carefully.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is unreliable; do not depend on cellular service for emergencies. A satellite messenger (Garmin InReach, SPOT device) is strongly recommended. Inform someone of your expected return time before departing.

timing.recommendedStart: Before dawn (4:30-5:30 AM) for day hike; overnight recommended

road.name: Hamma Hamma River Road (FS Road 25)
road.status: Open summer/fall; winter closure by snow
road.seasonalGate: Typically open May-November. Check Olympic National Forest alerts for seasonal closures and road status.
road.driveNote: Paved to mile marker 7; well-maintained dirt from there to Putvin Trailhead (~11.5 miles from US 101). Passable by standard vehicles but high-clearance preferred after rain. No services at trailhead; nearest gas/food in Hoodsport (20 miles away).

climate.forecastZone: Olympic Mountains, Mason County, WA
climate.typical: Maritime climate with high precipitation. Summer is relatively dry but thunderstorms are common. Fog and low clouds are frequent.
climate.spring: Cold and wet; trails muddy and stream crossings dangerous. Snow persists above 4,000 feet through May.
climate.summer: Warmest season; daytime temps 45-60°F at summit. Afternoon thunderstorms frequent; mornings are more stable.
climate.fall: Clear weather through mid-September; deteriorating rapidly in October. Frost likely above 5,000 feet by mid-October.
climate.winter: Road closed; peak buried in snow (10+ feet). Route completely inaccessible.

access.landManager: Olympic National Park (summit area), Olympic National Forest (Putvin Trail approach), Mount Skokomish Wilderness (lower basin)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking ($5/day or annual). Camping at Lake of the Angels is free.
access.permit: No climbing permit required. Group size limit: 12 people + 8 livestock. No campfires above 3,500 feet.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required at Putvin Trailhead
access.closures: Hamma Hamma Road subject to seasonal closure by snow (Nov-Apr). Check Olympic National Forest alerts.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Mason County Sheriff: (360) 426-4441 (non-emergency); 911 (emergency). MACECOM dispatch center coordinates rescue.
emergency.nearestHospital: Mason General Hospital, 2100 Sheridan Lane, Shelton, WA 98584. Phone: (360) 426-1611. ~30 miles from trailhead.
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Forest Quilcene Ranger District: (360) 765-2200 (Tue-Wed, 9 AM–3 PM).
emergency.notes: Cell service absent in alpine areas; satellite messenger recommended. Hoodsport Fire & EMS: (360) 877-9882. Helicopter evacuation from Putvin Trail or upper basin may be necessary for serious injury.

blurb: Mount Stone is the highest point in Mason County, Washington, standing at 6,612 feet in the Olympic Mountains. This accessible Class 3 scramble offers one of the most rewarding peak experiences in the Olympics, with trail through old-growth forest and alpine scenery, excellent camping at Lake of the Angels, and views of The Brothers, Mount Washington, Mount Olympus, and surrounding peaks. The 5,000+ foot elevation gain and steep Putvin Trail approach make it a challenging but popular day climb or overnight backpack destination from June through September.
hierarchyNote: CRITICAL GEOGRAPHIC DISCREPANCY: Mount Stone (6,612 ft) is located in the Olympic Mountains in Mason County, Washington—NOT in the North Cascades as the database context suggests. The peak lies within Olympic National Park at coordinates 47°36′23″N 123°16′00″W, with much of the approach passing through Olympic National Forest and Mount Skokomish Wilderness. The USGS topo quad is Mount Skokomish (not a North Cascades quad). If this peak is intended to be classified in a North Cascades hierarchy in the database, the hierarchy parent is incorrect and should be corrected to 'Olympic Mountains.' Additionally, the provided elevation of 6,618 feet should be corrected to 6,612 feet per multiple authoritative sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Wikipedia, Peakbagger, USGS data).

blurb: Mount Washington is a 6,260-foot peak in the southeastern Olympic Mountains, rising steeply above Lake Cushman with 2,615 feet of prominence. Known for excellent access despite challenging terrain, the peak offers multiple routes ranging from the popular standard Class 3+ scramble to technical alpine climbs on the SE Ridge, featuring exposed ridge walks, loose rock, and commanding views of Puget Sound and Washington's major mountains.

routes.overview: The Standard South Route is a popular Class 3+ alpine scramble on Mount Washington's south side, the least technical but still steep and exposed route to the summit featuring extensive ridge walking, loose scree, and significant exposure.
routes.beta: A strenuous alpine scramble featuring Class 3 and 4 ridge walks, scree fields, talus slopes, and short rock faces. The route ascends approximately 3,200 feet in 4 miles roundtrip with continuous exposure and required scrambling skills throughout. Notable features include the 'catwalk' crux and 'Dog Walk Ledge.'
routes.approach: From Hoodsport, WA on US-101, drive north toward Lake Cushman on State Route 119 for 9.4 miles to a T-intersection. Turn right on Forest Road 24 and drive 1.5 miles, then turn left on Forest Road 2419. Continue 6.6 miles to road's end at the Mount Washington Trailhead (elevation approximately 3,050 ft). The trailhead is marked by a large boulder near a parking area.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route as ascent. Use extreme caution on steep, loose terrain—the descent requires as much time as the ascent. A safer alternative for those comfortable with Class 3/4 scrambling is the gully to skier's right of the scree funnel, which has less unpredictable loose rock than the main funnel.
routes.descentText: Descend via the ascent route, moving deliberately on steep, loose terrain. The descent is as demanding as the ascent due to stability concerns on unstable scree and talus. Experienced scramblers can traverse the gully to the right (skier's right) of the main scree funnel for a safer downclimb with less chaotic loose rock than the central funnel. Special attention required descending from Dog Walk Ledge and the Catwalk due to exposure.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Pre-dawn start from parking area. Hike/scramble through forested sections for 1 mile (approx 0.75 hrs). Enter open scramble terrain and ascend via exposed ridges and scree fields (3,000 ft elevation gain over 2-3 miles, 2.5-5 hrs). Reach summit by late morning to early afternoon. Descend same route (2.5-5 hrs) to return to parking before dark.
routes.bail: If weather deteriorates, snow/ice conditions appear unsafe, or visibility drops below safe scrambling levels, retreat immediately via the ascent route. Do not push to the summit in marginal conditions. Weather exposure on the upper ridge is significant and can change rapidly.
routes.turnaround: Turn around by mid-afternoon to ensure sufficient daylight for a safe descent. Do not continue above 5,500 ft if weather is deteriorating or snow/ice conditions are unstable. The upper ridge is fully exposed to weather and wind.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed June through September when snow is minimal and conditions are most stable. July, August, and September provide optimal conditions with minimal avalanche danger. Avoid December through May when significant snow cover creates substantial avalanche hazard. Not recommended during or immediately after wet weather.
routes.comms: Cell phone service is spotty to nonexistent in the Lake Cushman area. Limited service may be available at the trailhead parking area but none on the upper mountain. Satellite communicator strongly recommended for emergency communication.
proTips: Start very early to maximize daylight for descent
Bring at least 3 liters of water—exposure makes dehydration a risk
Helmet is essential for rockfall protection on exposed scrambling
Consider trekking poles for stability on loose scree
Route-finding can be challenging; careful navigation needed on ridge
In early season (May-June) or late season (Sept-Oct), an ice axe is essential if snow is present
watchOut: Loose scree on descent is hazardous and can shift unexpectedly
Avalanche danger significant December-May with snow present
Weather exposure on upper ridge can deteriorate rapidly
Route-finding required—multiple false summits and confusing terrain
Exposed Class 3/4 ridge walks with significant drop-offs
Wet weather makes loose rock exceptionally dangerous
knownHazards: Scree and talus loose rock hazard throughout descent
Avalanche terrain present December-May with snowpack; avoid route
Exposed ridge traverses with significant exposure
Steep loose downclimb hazards requiring focused footwork
Poor rock quality throughout with unstable holds
Weather exposure on upper ridge with minimal shelter
routes.detailedRack: No technical climbing rack required. Ice axe (60-70cm) essential for early season (May-June) or late season (Sept-Oct) when snow may be present; recommended even in summer for self-arrest capability. Helmet strongly recommended for rockfall and scrambling accidents. Trekking poles provide stability on loose terrain.
routes.proNeeds: Not a roped climbing route. Helmet mandatory for protection from rockfall and loose rock hazards. Trekking poles recommended for stability on scree and talus. Self-rescue skills and avalanche awareness essential.
whatToBring: headlamp with extra batteries (for descent if running late)
rope (30m) for rappeling sketchy sections if needed in bad conditions

access.landManager: US Forest Service (Olympic National Forest)
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required
access.closures: Subject to seasonal closures due to snow, weather, and fire-related restrictions. Forest Road 2419 periodically closed for fire mitigation. Check with Olympic National Forest before visiting.

road.name: Forest Road 2419
road.status: Gravel road, passable summer/fall, may close seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Winter closures possible for snow/weather; check Forest Service website
road.driveNote: Gravel road requiring high-clearance vehicle. Total distance from Highway 101 to trailhead is approximately 16.4 miles via Forest Roads 24 and 2419. Drive time approximately 45 minutes from Hoodsport.

climate.forecastZone: Mount Washington (Olympic), WA
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with Pacific weather fronts. High precipitation year-round, especially winter. Snow tends to be wet and heavy due to maritime influence, creating significant avalanche danger.
climate.spring: Avalanche hazard significant from snowmelt and temperature instability. Wet, heavy snow common. Temperatures: 25-45°F. Frequent weather changes.
climate.summer: Stable conditions, low avalanche risk, reduced cloud cover. Hot, muggy weather possible. Temperatures: 50-75°F. Best climbing window July-August.
climate.fall: Stable conditions, low avalanche risk. September typically excellent. October may see early snow. Temperatures: 40-65°F.
climate.winter: Cloudy, wet conditions typical. Heavy wet snow common (15-30 ft seasonal). Significant avalanche danger. Route not recommended. Temperatures: 20-40°F.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn (4-5 AM) to maximize daylight for descent

emergency.nearestHospital: Mason Health (Mason General Hospital), 901 Mountain View Dr, Shelton, WA 98584, approximately 30 miles south
emergency.rangerStation: Olympic National Forest - Hoodsport Ranger Station area
emergency.notes: Response time can be 30+ minutes or longer due to remote location and limited deputy coverage in Mason County (5-7 deputies covering 1,051 sq miles). Satellite communicator strongly recommended. Self-rescue and wilderness first aid skills essential.

blurb: Sahale Mountain (8,680 ft) is a prominent jagged, glaciated peak in North Cascades National Park offering accessible alpine climbing with two popular glacier routes. First ascended in August 1897 by John Charlton and Albert H. Sylvester, it stands 0.3 miles south of the higher Boston Peak and features characteristic granite formations sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation, making it a popular destination for mountaineers seeking moderate alpine experience with airy exposure and panoramic views of the North Cascades.

routes.overview: The standard and most popular route to Sahale Mountain via Cascade Pass and Sahale Arm, climbing moderate glacier terrain with a brief scramble to the airy summit pyramid featuring a short Class 3-4 chimney feature.
routes.fa: August 1897 (peak first ascent); John Charlton and Albert H. Sylvester
routes.rappels: 1 double-rope rappel (30m) on summit pyramid; 5 total rappels for full descent
routes.approach: From Cascade Pass parking (3,600 ft), follow maintained Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 miles and 1,700 ft gain to Cascade Pass (5,400 ft). From pass, branch north and switchback steeply through alpine meadows 2.2 miles and 2,200 ft gain to Sahale Glacier Camp (7,600 ft). From high camp, traverse onto Sahale Glacier with careful crevasse routefinding toward center to base of summit pyramid.
routes.beta: Begin at Cascade Pass trailhead off Cascade River Road. The first 2.5 miles feature 35 gradual switchbacks gaining 1,500 ft. Transition from forest to alpine meadows. Sahale Glacier is small and not very active. Route weaves SW to N side of summit block. The short chimney feature (5.0) on pyramid's south side serves as main crux with exposure. Few pieces of rock pro useful for final moves. Most groups don't rope for scramble but protection is reasonable. Descend via rappel (or 5-rappel sequence for full descent) and traverse SE to loose rocks, then steep snow downclimb (40-45 degrees) or face-in descent, followed by scree traverse and plunge-stepping down gentle snowfield to camp.
routes.descent: Rappel 30m from anchor block on summit pyramid to loose terrain below. Face-in downclimb or cautious plunge-step down steep firm snow section (40-45 degrees). Traverse scree field, then gentle snowfield with plunge-stepping back to Sahale Glacier Camp. Entire descent 2-3 hours. Route-find carefully to avoid larger crevasses.
routes.descentText: After summit rappel to loose rocks, descend steep firm snow section (40-45 degree slope) either by face-in downclimbing with kicked-in steps or plunge-stepping for more experienced mountaineers. Many climbers face-in with ice axe holes for security. Transition to loose scree traverse, then plunge-step down gentler snowfield to regain Sahale Glacier camp. Early season may have larger crevasses requiring more careful routefinding. Total descent time 2-3 hours depending on conditions.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Cascade Pass Trailhead (23.1 miles from Marblemount, 1 hour). Hike 5.9 miles and 3,940 ft gain via Cascade Pass Trail and Sahale Arm Trail to high camp at 7,600 ft (4-5 hours). Establish camp, review route, prepare gear. Day 2: Alpine start 6-7am from camp. 2-3 hours to summit pyramid base, 1-1.5 hours climbing pyramid via Sahale Glacier to summit. 2-3 hours descent. Total camp-to-camp ~7-10 hours.
routes.bail: From Sahale Glacier Camp, descend back via Sahale Arm Trail to Cascade Pass (2-3 hours), then to trailhead (2 hours). If bad weather above camp, retreat immediately. If caught on summit pyramid in poor conditions, rappel to camp and await improvement. Full abort to trailhead from high camp achievable in 4-5 hours with good fitness.
routes.turnaround: An early alpine start (6-7am) is essential. Most climbers should be back at high camp by 5-6pm. Aim to begin descent by 2-3pm. If not at or near summit by 1pm, consider descending to avoid darkness.
routes.bestSeason: July-August offers the most reliable conditions. Late July is best (though crowded) with stable weather and summit block mostly snow-free. August brings reliable sunshine and dry trails but more open crevasses. June has avalanche danger, cornices, and often road closures to trailhead. September is usually dry but massive crevasses may split glacier, complicating navigation. June-early July may see summit block snow-covered.
routes.comms: No cell service in North Cascades backcountry. Satellite communicator (InReach, Spot) recommended. Emergency contact: Skagit County Sheriff Dispatch (360) 336-1234 or 911 where coverage exists.
routes.detailedRack: A few small rock protection pieces (0.5-1 inch cracks) for final 5.0 chimney crux useful but most ascents unroped. Sling for rappel anchor at summit. Full alpine rack not needed.
routes.proNeeds: Rock protection minimal (a few small pieces for final pitch). Climbing helmet strongly recommended for rockfall risk and rappelling. Crevasse rescue kit (pickets, T-slot anchors, slings) if roped. Most parties not roped on glacier.
proTips: Start hike by 6-7am for best snow conditions and safety margin
Crevasse field is straightforward and few truly dangerous crevasses exist
Sahale Glacier is small and 'not very active' - routefinding not typically difficult
Stash ice axes and crampons at base of summit block to scramble unweighted
In August, crevasses open up more; September may have massive splits across glacier
Cascade Pass area sees high crowds—mid-week visits and earlier season offer more solitude
Road to trailhead often snow-blocked; confirm opening with NPS before going (typically June 25-July 1)
Double-check rappel anchor block is solid before committing full weight
watchOut: Avalanche danger: June climbs can face active slide risk on approach; avoid if uncertain
Cornices may persist into July on summit block; stay well back from ridge edges
Crevasses open significantly in September—expert routefinding required
Steep snow section (40-45 degrees) on descent is serious; poor technique = slide risk
Loose rock on summit pyramid; helmet essential; minimize time under exposed rock
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; retreat quickly if conditions worsen
Approach road frequently closed by avalanche snow into late June/early July
knownHazards: Crevasse hazard: Multiple crevasses on Sahale Glacier, especially in September when field may split across multiple paths; careful routefinding via center of glacier recommended
Avalanche hazard: Entire approach and upper route crosses potential avalanche terrain; early season (June, early July) carries active risk; road approach may be snow-blocked by avalanche deposits
Cornices: Wind-loaded snow features persist into July on summit ridge; stay well back from cornice edges to prevent breakthrough and fall
Exposure: Final summit scramble Class 3-4 with 1,000+ ft exposure on all sides; protection anchors marginal; loose rock common
Steep snow descent: 40-45 degree slope on descent between summit and glacier; slip = self-arrest unlikely or ineffective; requires secure crampon technique or ice axe self-arrest skill

climate.typical: Alpine climate with marine west coast influence; high precipitation on western slopes due to orographic lift from Pacific weather systems; cooler than adjacent ranges due to glacier effect and elevation; frequent wind.
climate.spring: April-May: Approach roads snow-blocked; high avalanche danger on route; frequent storms. Snow lingers above 6,000 ft.
climate.summer: July-August: Most stable and dry months; frequent sunshine; crevasses open; night temperatures near freezing or above; possible afternoon thunderstorms. Warmest, shortest climbing season.
climate.fall: September-October: Early September mostly dry; later September massive crevasse opening; early frost mornings; days shorten rapidly; first major storms arrive late Sept.
climate.winter: November-March: Deep snow; extreme avalanche hazard; short daylight; temperatures well below freezing; approach impossible; recommended for expert mountaineers only.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry wilderness permit required for overnight camping. $6 reservation fee + $10 per-person per-night recreation fee (ages 15 and under free).
access.fees: $6 permit reservation fee; $10 per-person per-night camping fee
access.closures: Cascade River Road typically closed Dec-May; opens end of June (sometimes July in heavy snow years). Permits sometimes difficult to obtain during peak season (July-Aug).

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open (seasonal)
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens late June; closes for winter (Dec-May). Heavy snow years may delay opening to July. Early-season avalanche deposits may cut road at mileposts 18-20.
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from Marblemount, WA (off State Route 20). First 10 miles paved; remaining 13.1 miles rough gravel with potholes. No RVs/trailers over 22 ft; vehicles over 8 ft width prohibited past milepost 18. Steep narrow section with drop-offs in final 3 miles. Approx 1 hour drive. Eldorado parking (mile 20) available for larger vehicles.

timing.recommendedStart: 6-7am from high camp for alpine start

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount WA 98267; (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (operated by Skagit Regional Health), Arlington, WA—Level IV Emergency Department, 48 beds, ~45 minutes from trailhead. Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA—Level III ED.
emergency.notes: No cell service in backcountry. Satellite communicator (InReach/Spot) strongly recommended. Skagit Mountain Rescue Unit (SMRU) operates in region. Evacuation by helicopter possible but weather-dependent and expensive.

routes.overview: A steeper glacier alpine route ascending from Boston Basin via the left (north) side of Quien Sabe Glacier to the Boston-Sahale Col, then traversing a final exposed snow and rock ridge to the summit. More technical crevasse work and objective hazard than the standard Sahale Glacier route.
routes.rappels: 3-5 rappels for descent; some may be short
routes.approach: From Marblemount, drive Cascade River Road 21.7 miles to Boston Basin Trailhead (3,200 ft). Follow abandoned Diamond Mine road ~0.75 miles, then trail up steeply across 4 stream crossings over 0.5 miles to old mine site. Continue steep climbers' trail 3-4 hours and 3,300 ft gain through forest to Boston Basin high camp (6,500 ft) on flats below glacier. From camp, approach Quien Sabe Glacier directly.
routes.beta: Boston Basin approach is steeper and rougher than Cascade Pass route. Trail is eroded and steep. Boston Basin sees less traffic and wider crevasse field on Quien Sabe Glacier compared to Sahale Glacier. Ascend left (north) side of Quien Sabe Glacier, watching for crevasses and selecting reasonable line—usually near center in steeper sections. Route between Sahale and Boston Peak summits (Sharkfin Tower is visible reference). Traverse south via col below the two peaks to reach Boston-Sahale Col. From col, traverse exposed snow to reach loose Class 3 summit scramble with persistent cornice into early summer. Final scramble is brief and loose.
routes.descent: Retrace ascent route. Rappel via established anchors on rock walls along steep couloir (3-5 rappels to Quien Sabe Glacier). Descend Quien Sabe Glacier carefully avoiding crevasses. Retrace Boston Basin approach trail downhill (2-3 hours from camp).
routes.descentText: Descend back to Boston-Sahale Col via exposed snow ridge (simpler going down). Rappel anchors on rock walls along steep couloir provide relatively straightforward descent via 3-5 rappels back to Quien Sabe Glacier field. Navigate crevasses carefully avoiding larger splits, particularly late-season when field may have massive crevasses splitting across. Retrace Boston Basin trail downhill, watching footing on steep eroded sections. Total descent 3-4 hours camp-to-camp.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Boston Basin Trailhead (21.7 miles from Marblemount, ~45 min). Hike steep trail 3-4 hours and 3,300 ft to Boston Basin high camp (6,500 ft). Prepare gear and rest. Day 2: Early start from camp (5-6am). 1-2 hours to glacier base, 2-3 hours ascending glacier and Quien Sabe route to summit. 3-4 hours descent camp-to-camp. Total summit-day commitment 8-10 hours.
routes.bail: From Boston Basin camp, full descent to trailhead achievable in 3-4 hours. Steep eroded trail is challenging downhill. If weather deteriorates above camp, descend to camp and retreat via trail. Rappel anchors should allow safe bail from couloir at any point.
routes.turnaround: Alpine start by 5-6am essential. Most climbers should reach col by 9-10am to maintain turnaround. If not at col by 11am, consider descending. Aim to begin descent by 1-2pm to avoid darkness on exposed terrain.
routes.bestSeason: July-August: Most stable and dry. Late July crowded but proven. August reliable sunshine but crevasses open. June: avalanche danger, road may be blocked, cornices present. September: usually dry but massive crevasse openings create navigation challenge; early September more reasonable than late Sept.
routes.comms: No cell service in North Cascades backcountry. Satellite communicator (InReach, Spot) essential for Boston Basin due to remoteness. Emergency contact: Skagit County Sheriff (360) 336-1234.
routes.detailedRack: Pickets for snow anchors (2-4). A few small rock cams (0.4-0.75 inch) for couloir anchors. Slings for anchor backup. This route requires more anchor placement skill than Sahale Glacier route due to steeper terrain and need to establish descent rappel anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Crevasse rescue setup essential: pickets, T-slot anchors, slings, pulleys. Most parties rope on glacier. Snow protection skills required for anchoring.
proTips: Alpine start 5-6am from camp critical for safety margin
Boston Basin high camp is lower (6,500 vs 7,600 ft) requiring 1,000+ ft morning gain
Crevasse field is significantly more complex than Sahale Glacier—rope team recommended
Route-finding up north side of glacier most reliable; center of glacier steeper
Cornice persists into July; summit approach may require ice-tool clearing
Early (June) climbs face high avalanche risk on approach; confirm conditions
Boston Basin is less-traveled; fewer tracks to follow; navigation more complex
Establish and test rappel anchors thoroughly before committing full weight
watchOut: Crevasse hazard: Quien Sabe Glacier has a larger, steeper crevasse field than Sahale—multiple large splits across glacier, especially late season; mandatory rope teams
Cornice hazard: Wind-loaded cornice on Boston-Sahale Col may extend into early summer; can collapse without warning; approach cautiously and stay back from edge
Avalanche: Entire approach and route cross avalanche terrain; June-early July present active hazard; steep eroded Boston Basin trail prone to loose rock and slides
Exposed ridge: Final ridge from col to summit is exposed with serious fall consequence on both sides (100+ ft); good footing essential; loose rock hazard
Navigation: Fewer ascents than Sahale Glacier route; fewer tracks; steeper approach makes retreat more committing; thunderstorm hazard
Steeper terrain: Route is Grade II (vs Grade I equivalent for Sahale); steeper slopes = more objective hazard and technical demand
knownHazards: Crevasse hazard: Quien Sabe Glacier features multiple large crevasses with steep sidewalls, especially in September when field may split with massive gaps across climbing direction; mandatory use of rope teams and picket anchors for crevasse rescue
Cornice hazard: Boston-Sahale Col features a persistent wind-loaded cornice in early season that can be 6+ ft thick; collapse risk extreme; approach ridge from below col, stay well back from edge
Avalanche hazard: Boston Basin approach crosses avalanche paths; upper route crosses hanging glacier terrain; June-early July active risk; radar recommended
Steep snow and ice: Upper route has sections of 35-45 degree snow requiring secure crampon technique; falls on steep slopes difficult to self-arrest on; ice axe technique essential

Route-finding complexity: Steeper terrain and crevasse field make navigation more difficult than Sahale Glacier; poor visibility or whiteout conditions can lead to route-finding errors and exposure to larger crevasses

climate.typical: Alpine climate with marine west coast characteristics; high precipitation on western slopes; cooler due to glacier and elevation; windier at col; frequent storm cycles.
climate.spring: April-May: Avalanche danger high; road may be blocked; approach snow-covered; frequent storms; cornice hazard peak.
climate.summer: July-August: Most stable months; crevasses open; possible afternoon thunderstorms; nights near freezing. Best climbing window.
climate.fall: September-October: Early September mostly dry; later September massive crevasse opening; early frosts; storms increase.
climate.winter: November-March: Extreme avalanche hazard; deep snow; short daylight; expert-only with specialized gear.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park)
access.permit: Backcountry wilderness permit required. $6 reservation fee + $10 per-person per-night recreation fee (ages 15 and under free).
access.fees: $6 permit reservation fee; $10 per-person per-night camping fee
access.closures: Cascade River Road typically closed Dec-May; opens end of June (sometimes July). Permits can be difficult to obtain in peak season.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open (seasonal)
road.seasonalGate: Opens late June, closes for winter (Dec-May). Heavy snow years delay opening to early July.
road.driveNote: 21.7 miles from Marblemount to Boston Basin Trailhead via Cascade River Road (off State Route 20). Road conditions: first 10 miles paved, remaining 13+ miles rough gravel. No RVs/trailers over 22 ft; vehicles over 8 ft width prohibited past milepost 18. Drive time ~45 minutes. Limited parking (6-8 cars).

timing.recommendedStart: 5-6am alpine start from Boston Basin high camp

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades NP Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount: 7280 Ranger Station Rd, Marblemount WA 98267; (360) 854-7245
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA—Level IV ED, ~1 hour from trailhead. Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA—Level III ED.
emergency.notes: Boston Basin is remote; no cell service. Satellite communicator (InReach/Spot) essential. Helicopter evacuation possible but expensive and weather-limited. Skagit Mountain Rescue Unit covers region.

blurb: Molar Tooth is a prominent 7,547-foot granite pillar located on the ridge between Cutthroat Peak and Cutthroat Pass in the North Cascades, composed of granite from the Golden Horn Batholith. The peak offers a variety of technical climbing routes ranging from Class 4 scrambling to Class 5, with well-developed ridge and face routes featuring solid granite and vertical jointing. First climbed on September 19, 1971 by Alex Bertiulis with Charles and Patricia Raymond via the NE Arete.

routes.rappels: 3 rappels on north ridge, or 80-foot rappel southwest
routes.fa: Armstrong, Sandbo, Woodmansee, Apter, Gibson - July 1977
routes.overview: A moderate alpine route on Molar Tooth's north ridge featuring 2-3 low to moderate 5th class pitches up the west rib to the summit, with excellent granite climbing punctuated by loose rock hazards and quality variations.
routes.beta: The North Ridge - West Side is one of the primary technical routes on Molar Tooth. The route ascends via the west rib with multiple short 5th class pitches (5.4-5.8), solid granite with vertical jointing providing good protection opportunities, and enjoyable alpine climbing despite loose rock concerns. The descent via north ridge rappels requires careful anchor placement and rope management due to potential loose rock.
routes.approach: Two primary approaches: (1) From the Cutthroat Lake trailhead on Highway 20, hike 1.8 miles with 500 feet elevation gain to Cutthroat Lake (4,800 ft). Continue another 3.7 miles with 1,800 feet elevation gain to Cutthroat Pass (6,800 ft), then follow the ridge crest south approximately 1 mile to the base of the North Ridge. (2) From Rainy Pass North trailhead (4,875 ft) on the PCT, hike about 1.5 miles north to just before Porcupine Creek (around 5,250 ft elevation), then ascend the ridge northeast toward Molar Tooth—approximately 2 miles with 2,300 feet elevation gain. The second gully provides a cleaner approach to the base of the north ridge and west rib.
routes.descent: Descend via 3 rappels on the north ridge, or work southwest from the summit for an 80-foot rappel. Ensure solid anchor points as granite can be coarse and loose rock is prevalent. Exercise caution with rope management on descent.
routes.descentText: The standard descent involves 3 rappels down the north ridge from the summit, or alternatively descend southwest with an 80-foot rappel. The granite offers good anchor possibilities through cracks and features, but careful placement is essential—test all anchors before committing weight. Be vigilant for loose blocks and flakes. Rope management is critical in low light. Once below technical terrain, descend gullies back toward the pass or PCT.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Cutthroat Lake trailhead or Rainy Pass. Hike to approach base (3-4 hours via Cutthroat Lake route, or 2-3 hours via PCT). Scout route and camp if needed. Day 2: Climb North Ridge - West Side (6-8 hours total from base for approach, climb, and descent). Conclude descent by mid-afternoon to avoid scrambling in dark.
routes.bail: From base of technical climbing, retreat via gullies back toward pass or PCT. If difficulties encountered, downclimb low-angle terrain or rappel technical pitches. Route is straightforward to descend via the same north ridge system.
routes.turnaround: Be off technical terrain by 2:00 PM to ensure adequate daylight for descent via rappels and gully terrain. Alpine starts (4:00-5:00 AM) are standard. Do not commit to climbing if poor weather is approaching.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September offers optimal climbing window, with July and August providing most stable conditions. By late June, snow melt clears approach trails. July-August are dry with minimal precipitation (1.7 inches), comfortable alpine temperatures (55-75°F), and light winds. September conditions deteriorate with cooler temperatures and increasing snow chance.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable in the North Cascades. Carry a satellite communicator or personal locator beacon for emergency communication. Methow Valley Ranger District operates Monday-Friday 8:00 AM to 4:00 PM.
proTips: Loose rock is the biggest hazard—constant vigilance essential
Bring full trad rack with emphasis on 0.5-3 inch protection for vertical jointing
The west rib approach gully has two options; take the cleaner second gully
Start before dawn to maximize daylight for descent rappels
Rock on technical pitches is generally solid but test holds before weighting
Post-sunrise rockfall is a concern; alpine start strongly recommended
watchOut: Loose rock and rockfall hazard—constant vigilance on gully approaches and between pitches
Coarse granite with poor quality sections between technical pitches
Limited protection on some traverse sections; place pieces conservatively
Descending in low light with loose rock creates serious hazard—plan descent before dark
Vertical granite can be slick when damp or icy early season
knownHazards: Loose rock is the biggest climbing problem on all terrain
Rockfall hazard from loose blocks and flakes on gullies
Granite steep with vertical jointing but quality varies significantly
Approach gullies contain most loose material
whatToBring: Approach shoes or light hiking boots
routes.detailedRack: Bring complete trad rack with cams 0.5-3 inches, stoppers/nuts, and hexes. Vertical granite jointing provides excellent protection throughout technical pitches. Consider extra quickdraws (10-12 total) and locking carabiners for belays. Multiple moderate-length pitches with good crack systems allow solid piece placement.
routes.proNeeds: Natural rock protection via cracks and vertical jointing. Granite features provide ample protection opportunities. Test all anchors before committing weight and avoid placing protection in weathered sections. Most parties find good solid placements throughout the climb.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
access.fees: $5 per vehicle per day
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass or $5 day-use pass via Recreation.gov
access.closures: Highway 20 may be closed in winter; typically open late April to November

road.name: North Cascades Scenic Highway (State Route 20)
road.status: Seasonally open, typically late April to November
road.seasonalGate: Highway 20 closed east of Washington Pass in winter
road.driveNote: From Winthrop, drive west approximately 28 miles on SR 20 to Forest Road 400 turnoff for Cutthroat Lake trailhead. For Rainy Pass trailhead, continue to Rainy Pass parking area (35 miles west of Winthrop). Road plowed in winter for some access but becomes impassable east of Washington Pass.

climate.forecastZone: West Slopes North Cascades and Passes (WAZ567)
climate.typical: Maritime climate with heavy precipitation October-April. Summers relatively dry. Alpine temperatures 55-75°F in July-August with overnight freezing common (40-50°F).
climate.spring: Snowmelt dominates; trails muddy; avalanche danger on slopes; not ideal for climbing.
climate.summer: July-August optimal: highs 70°F, lows 45°F. Alpine 55-75°F. Minimal precipitation (1.7 inches), light winds, 24% rain chance. Clear skies with 40-50°F overnight freezing for early-morning frozen gully conditions.
climate.fall: September cooling, increasing snow chance. Clouds increase, visibility decreases. October brings first significant snow at high elevation.
climate.winter: Heavy precipitation, significant snowpack, avalanche danger. Routes inaccessible; highway often closed east of Washington Pass.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Okanogan County Sheriff: 509-422-7232 or 800-572-6604; Skagit County Sheriff: 360-428-3211. For emergencies dial 911.
emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, 24 West Chewuch Road, Winthrop, WA 98862. (509) 996-4000. Monday-Friday 8:00 AM to 4:00 PM.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (360-424-4111). Three Rivers Hospital, Brewster, WA (509-689-2517) with 24/7 Emergency Room.
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable in North Cascades; carry satellite communicator or personal locator beacon. Area is remote with 2-4 hour minimum rescue response times. Self-rescue planning essential. Communicate route plan with someone outside party.

pitchDetail.notes: Initial west rib pitches with good granite and vertical jointing providing solid protection. Loose blocks in approach gully; careful foot placement required.
Upper pitches to summit with steeper, more exposed climbing. Coarse granite with vertical jointing provides adequate protection. Quality varies; test holds. Final pitches near summit.

hierarchyNote: WARNING: This peak is located in the Olympic Mountains/Olympic National Forest, NOT the North Cascades or Cascades Range. The geography appears misclassified in the current hierarchy. The Brothers are on the Olympic Peninsula, west of Seattle, distinct from the North Cascades (northeast of Seattle) and the Cascades proper (central WA).
blurb: The Brothers is a twin-peaked mountain in the Olympic Range featuring Edward Peak (south, 6,866 ft) and Arthur Peak (north, 6,674 ft), prominent landmarks visible from the Puget Sound. The south peak was first summited in 1912 via the popular Lena Lake approach; the formations offer accessible alpine scrambling and technical snow/rock traverses on glaciated terrain with crumbly volcanic rock.

routes.overview: The standard snow-filled couloir ascent of the south face; a popular mountaineering route combining snow gully climbing with scramble terrain.
routes.beta: Ascend the couloir bearing generally north-northwest toward the headwall. Work right and upward until able to easily traverse left; if climbing exceeds 3rd class consistently, keep traversing to easier ground.
routes.approach: From Lena Lake Trailhead (685 ft) at the end of Hamma Hamma River Road (7.5 miles paved from Hoodsport), hike the main trail 3 miles gaining 1,550 ft to Lower Lena Lake. Continue 3 miles through the Valley of the Silent Men to Upper Lena Lake and climbers camp (~5,500 ft, 3,000 ft gain from trailhead). Continue up the east fork of Lena Creek valley. Enter the south couloir base via avalanche meadow at ~5,800 ft.
routes.descent: Descend the couloir following the same line down, exiting into the lower meadow section.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent couloir, moving carefully over exposed terrain. If snow is thin or wet, exercise caution on scree sections. Exit to the meadow and return via the Lena Creek valley and upper Lena Lake trail.
routes.itinerary: Full day or overnight option: Day 1 - hike to climbers camp (6-7 hours). Day 2 - couloir ascent, summit, and return to camp (5-7 hours), or push to trailhead same day (additional 4-5 hours descent).
routes.bail: If conditions deteriorate or climbing exceeds 3rd class consistently, descend the couloir early. Escape left to adjacent gullies or ridges is generally feasible. Return to climbers camp via the approach valley.
routes.turnaround: If the climbing grade exceeds 3rd class for sustained terrain, if snow becomes unstable or wet-slushy, or if weather deteriorates (afternoon thunderstorms common), turn around and descend to safe ground.
routes.bestSeason: June through September, with June-July offering consolidated snow-climbing conditions; August-September may present bare rock and scree with less objective avalanche hazard.
proTips: Route-finding is critical—stay in the main couloir line and avoid smaller gullies to the left which lead to steeper terrain
Start before dawn (5am) to minimize afternoon weather impact and rockfall hazard
Scout the lower couloir section before committing to the upper pitches
The route changes drastically with snow coverage; early June offers more consolidated snow than late summer
watchOut: Avalanche hazard—particularly in early season or after fresh snow; the meadow approach is the primary risk zone
Loose and crumbly volcanic rock when exposed; use caution on mixed sections
Water flow can be substantial during snowmelt (July-August); route may change
Exposure on narrower sections of the gully
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain through approach meadow and lower couloir
Falling ice/rime from upper headwall in cold conditions
Rapid weather deterioration and afternoon thunderstorms common
Poor rock quality and falling rock from other parties
routes.detailedRack: No rock protection needed; some parties bring 1-2 slings to sling rock horns if desired for psychological protection on the narrowest scramble sections, but this is neither standard nor necessary.

road.name: Hamma Hamma River Road
road.status: Open summer/fall; may be gated seasonally
road.driveNote: 7.5 miles paved Forest Service road from US 101 north of Hoodsport. From Hoodsport, drive US 101 north 14 miles to FR-25 turnoff at milepost 318, then 7.5 miles to Lena Lake Trailhead. Large parking for ~30 vehicles; privy available.

access.landManager: Olympic National Forest (lower) / Olympic National Park (upper)
access.fees: $5 daily Northwest Forest Pass or annual pass
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (day-use); no backcountry permit required for National Forest but check NPS regulations for park portion

climate.forecastZone: WAZ513 - Southwest Olympics
climate.typical: Maritime temperate climate; significant seasonal precipitation, frequent cloud cover, rapid weather changes above 4,000 ft
climate.spring: March-May: Frequent rain and snow; avalanche hazard high; clearing days increasingly common by late May
climate.summer: June-August: Warmest, clearest period but also windiest; afternoon thunderstorm development common; freezing line typically 7,000-8,500 ft
climate.fall: September-October: Rain increasing, early snow possible above 6,000 ft by October
climate.winter: November-March: Heavy snow, avalanche hazard severe, technical winter climbing only

emergency.rangerStation: Staircase Ranger Station (Olympic NP, seasonal summer hours)
emergency.nearestHospital: Mason County Hospital (Shelton, ~30 miles south via Hoodsport)
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable/absent in Hamma Hamma Recreation Area; plan accordingly and file float plan. SAR coordination through Mason County Sheriff and NPS rangers.

routes.overview: An exciting and technical alpine traverse connecting North Peak (Arthur) and South Peak (Edward) via the ridge crest; tests comprehensive alpine skills including rock scrambling, snow climbing, and rope management on exposed terrain.
routes.beta: From base camp (~5,500 ft), ascend north over occasionally 4th-class rock terrain to a notch at 6,100 ft separating the peaks. From the notch, traverse the ridge crest ascending mixed snow and rock pitches to North Peak (6,674 ft), then retrace to South Peak (6,866 ft) or descend south. Pitches involve moderate snow anchoring with pickets and sparse rock protection; simulclimbing possible on moderate sections.
routes.approach: From Lena Lake Trailhead (685 ft), hike 6 miles via Lena Lake and climbers camp approach (4-5 hours, 3,000+ ft gain) to base camp at ~5,500 ft. From climbers camp, cross Lena Creek westward around 6 miles into the climb, navigate through the slide meadow and burn scar heading north until reaching flat terrain suitable for bivy or day-climbing base.
routes.descent: Retrace the ridge traverse south from North Peak to the saddle. Descend the ridge on the south side via the standard gully approach, or descend east to locate the upper Lena Creek valley and return via the approach route.
routes.descentText: Reverse the ridge traverse carefully, managing rope carefully on exposed sections. Once south of the saddle, descend the east-facing gully to the 5,500 ft base camp area, then retrace the trail descent to Lena Lake Trailhead (additional 4-5 hours).
routes.itinerary: Recommended overnight push: Day 1 - Hike from trailhead to climbers camp (6-7 hours, establish bivy). Day 2 - Traverse ridge, summit both peaks, return to camp (10-12 hours), or retreat to trailhead same day (add 4-5 hours). Alternatively, 2-day push from trailhead with overnight at higher camp.
routes.bail: If snow becomes unstable, if protection placements are inadequate, or if weather deteriorates significantly, descend off the ridge crest via the east gully (non-technical, 3rd class) back to the saddle area and retreat south to base camp.
routes.turnaround: If snow coverage is minimal (thin snowpack, many moat gaps), protection sparse, or if party speed is below 1 pitch per hour, consider the easier South Couloir route instead. Turnaround time should be called by 2pm to ensure safe descent before dark.
routes.bestSeason: June, when consolidated seasonal snow eases rock protection and improves ice/snow climbing quality; July possible if snow remains; early June offers thin snow that may ease rock protection on exposed sections.
proTips: Early June climbing with thin snow can make rock protection easier than later season
Simulclimb moderate snow pitches to save time and manage exposure
Two-day approach with overnight bivy spreads effort and allows acclimatization; recommended for parties unfamiliar with alpine rock/snow
Establish solid snow anchors with pickets in 45-60 degree slopes; rock protection is sparse and crumbly
watchOut: Pitches 2-3 involve moderate snow angles (45-50 degrees) requiring solid placements in consolidated snow
Rock sections have sparse pro and crumbly volcanic material; protect 5th class scramble sections with slings on rock horns if needed
Exposure on the ridge crest; winds can be strong and sudden
Moat formation around couloirs in early-season melt; may require boulder hopping or snow/ice climbing to bridge
knownHazards: Avalanche terrain on ridge approach and lower slopes
Moat formation at the base of snow couloirs in early melt season
Thin/sparse protection on mixed rock terrain
Exposure to sudden weather changes and afternoon thunderstorms
Rockfall from other parties or loose material on ridge
gear: Alpine rock rack (0.5-2 inch cams and stoppers)
Triple-length slings for snow horn anchors
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rock rack: 0.5-2 inch range (3-4 cams, 4-6 stoppers, 3-4 slings); 4-6 snow pickets for 45-60 degree slopes; triple-length slings for rock horn anchors. Most anchors are snow-based on the ridge; rock anchors are backup on sparsely protected rock sections.
routes.proNeeds: Light alpine trad rack with emphasis on snow anchors (pickets) rather than rock pro; most climbing is snow or 4th/easy-5th class scramble with minimal rock protection.

road.name: Hamma Hamma River Road
road.status: Open summer/fall; may be gated seasonally
road.driveNote: 7.5 miles paved Forest Service road from US 101 north of Hoodsport. From Hoodsport, drive US 101 north 14 miles to FR-25 turnoff at milepost 318, then 7.5 miles to Lena Lake Trailhead. Large parking for ~30 vehicles; privy available.

access.landManager: Olympic National Forest (lower) / Olympic National Park (upper)
access.fees: $5 daily Northwest Forest Pass or annual pass
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (day-use); no backcountry permit required for National Forest but check NPS regulations for park portion

climate.forecastZone: WAZ513 - Southwest Olympics
climate.typical: Maritime temperate climate; significant seasonal precipitation, frequent cloud cover, rapid weather changes above 4,000 ft
climate.spring: March-May: Frequent rain and snow; avalanche hazard high; clearing days increasingly common by late May
climate.summer: June-August: Warmest, clearest period but also windiest; afternoon thunderstorm development common; freezing line typically 7,000-8,500 ft
climate.fall: September-October: Rain increasing, early snow possible above 6,000 ft by October
climate.winter: November-March: Heavy snow, avalanche hazard severe, technical winter climbing only

emergency.rangerStation: Staircase Ranger Station (Olympic NP, seasonal summer hours)
emergency.nearestHospital: Mason County Hospital (Shelton, ~30 miles south via Hoodsport)
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable/absent in Hamma Hamma Recreation Area; plan accordingly and file float plan. SAR coordination through Mason County Sheriff and NPS rangers.

objHaz: Offwidth climbing (crux sections P2 and P5)
Steep snow or loose rocks near col early season
routes.overview: A classic 7-pitch alpine rock climb featuring striking splitter cracks and varied climbing techniques. All pitches progress through layback fingers, perfect hands, fists, chimneys, and offwidth sections on exceptional granite with significant exposure.
routes.beta: Rebel Yell is a well-established North Cascades classic featuring 7 pitches of alpine trad climbing (5.10 overall with crux pitches P2 and P5 at 5.10b). The route climbs the clean east face via splitter cracks with varied techniques. All pitches are led on gear. Descent via 4 double-rope rappels on fixed anchors. Extra gear can be cached at base. Typically done as 2-day expedition.
routes.approach: From Highway 20 pullout approximately 2 miles east of the hairpin turn near milepost 166: descend to creek over talus and rock, pick up climber's trail switchbacking steeply upward to Burgundy Col bench camp (6,500-6,600 ft elevation). Total elevation gain from trailhead (4,250 ft) to base camp is approximately 2,250-2,350 ft. From camp to route base via upper Silver Star Glacier area: approximately 1 hour, traversing across lower slopes to reach the east side of Chianti Spire and base of East Face.
routes.descentText: Descend via 4 double-rope rappels using two 60-meter ropes on fixed anchors. Rappel down and to the rappeller's right following established anchor stations. Typical descent time approximately 40 minutes.
routes.descent: 4 double-rope rappels with fixed anchors
routes.bestSeason: Mid-June through late September offers the best climbing conditions. Peak climbing season is July through September with most reliable weather and stable snow conditions. Early season (mid-June through June) may have lingering snow in couloirs and approach gullies requiring crampons. Highway 20 typically closes mid-October through May.
routes.comms: No cell service at Washington Pass. Satellite communication or personal locator beacon recommended. Closest services in Marblemount or North Bend.
routes.bail: Early retreat possible from Burgundy Col via descent of approach. From route, rappel descent provides straightforward exit via established anchors.
routes.turnaround: Plan turnaround by mid-afternoon to complete descent before dark. Typical full day: 1 hour approach from camp, 6 hours climbing, 40 minutes descent. Start climbing by 8:00 AM for safe daylight margin.
routes.detailedRack: Rack to 4 inches with doubles recommended. Essential: doubles of #0.75, #1, #2, #3 Camalots or equivalent; doubles of yellow alien and #0 camalots for small cracks; standard selection up to #4 Camalot for larger cracks and offwidths. Multiple slings and quickdraws (12-15 minimum). Ice axe and crampons for approach.
routes.proNeeds: Complete rack to 4 inches with emphasis on doubles for crack climbing. Route climbs continuous crack systems with varied widths requiring flexible gear choices. Offwidth sections (P2 and P5) demand larger pieces and secure placements.
proTips: All seven pitches are unique and memorable; quality rock and climbing justify the approach effort
Offwidth sections on P2 and P5 are the crux pitches; bring doubles and solid off-width technique
Extra gear can be cached at route base reducing load on rappel descent
Start early from camp (6:00-7:00 AM) for safe descent before dark
Ian Nicholson (local guidebook author) rates this 'one of the best alpine rock climbs in the Cascades'
watchOut: P2 and P5 are 5.10b offwidth crux sections requiring solid technique
Loose talus and scree on approach gullies; careful foot placement essential
Steep snow or loose rocks on approach to col early in season
Area experiences more thunderstorm activity than other Cascades regions; monitor weather
Fixed anchors should be backed up with own anchor if quality questionable
knownHazards: Offwidth climbing sections demand experience; poor technique leads to rapid fatigue and long falls
Approach terrain is avalanche-prone in spring/early season; current avalanche danger assessment recommended
Freeze-thaw cycles create icy, hard snow conditions with slippery surfaces and difficult step-kicking
Exposed ridge location receives lightning during thunderstorms; descent if storms develop
Rockfall potential from freeze-thaw activity; helmet mandatory

pitchDetail.notes: 140 ft. Flakes and cracks leading to offwidth base. Initial pitch sets tone; solid rock.
80 ft. Fist crack transitioning to wide offwidth. First crux pitch; technical offwidth climbing required.
100 ft. Lower-angle dihedral climbing. Recovery pitch with slightly easier climbing.
70 ft. Slabs, arete traverse, then hand crack approach. Varied climbing style.
100 ft. Thin hands crack transitioning to cupped hands then widening to offwidth. Second crux; sustained offwidth.
140 ft. Extended offwidth section with ramp exit. Long offwidth pitch; technical but moderate grade relative to width.
20 ft. Summit block final moves. Short summit pitch.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00-7:00 AM from Burgundy Col base camp

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park / US Forest Service (National Forests)
access.permit: No permit required for backcountry access
access.closures: Highway 20 (SR-20) typically closed mid-October to May due to snow. Backcountry access via foot travel beyond closure gates is permitted year-round but avalanche and snow hazard awareness required.

road.name: SR 20 North Cascades Highway
road.status: Seasonal - closed mid-October to May
road.seasonalGate: Silver Star gate at milepost 171 (east side), Rainy Pass gate (west side); gates moved to lower elevations (Early Winters gate milepost 178, Newhalem gate milepost 120) later in season as snow conditions allow
road.driveNote: Drive SR-20 to pullout approximately 2 miles east of hairpin turn at Washington Pass near milepost 166. Small pullout with limited parking. Road well-maintained but narrow with tight hairpin turn near pass.

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Visitor Center (Newhalem) or Marblemount District Ranger Station
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Mount Vernon, ~60 miles); Island Hospital (Anacortes, ~50 miles); United General Hospital (Sedro-Woolley, ~45 miles)
emergency.notes: No cell service at Washington Pass. Sat phone or PLB recommended. Response time from ranger/rescue likely 2+ hours for serious incident. Helicopter evacuation possible in good weather.

climate.forecastZone: NOAA WAZ567 (West Slopes North Cascades and Passes)
climate.typical: Maritime climate with cool summers and heavy winter snow. Precipitation concentrated in fall/winter months. Summer is dry with low precipitation.
climate.spring: Highly variable. Snow melt creates avalanche hazard and loose rock. Freeze-thaw cycles produce icy, hard snow. Lingering snow on approach.
climate.summer: Optimal season. July-August: high 70F, low 45F, driest months with ~1.7 inches rainfall, peak sunshine 9 hours/day. High UV index (6-7).
climate.fall: Conditions deteriorate rapidly late September onward. Snow increases; storms more frequent. Highway closure likely by mid-October.
climate.winter: Highway closed. Avalanche and snow hazard extreme. Snowfall 300-400+ inches annual at pass.

waypoints.name: Base of Chianti Spire East Face

routes.corrections: Grade corrected from 5.9 to 5.10 (all major sources: Mountain Project, SummitPost, North Cascades Mountain Guides, Steph Abegg consistently report 5.10). Pitches corrected from 6 to 7 based on detailed pitch breakdowns from Steph Abegg and North Cascades Mountain Guides. Route length corrected from 700 ft to 800 ft per Mountain Project and Steph Abegg pitch breakdown. First ascent corrected: given 1952 Hieb/Maki was peak's north face FA, not East Face/Rebel Yell route-specific. Rebel Yell/East Face route FA is 1986 by Mark Bebie and Jim Nelson per Mountain Project, SummitPost, and North Cascades Mountain Guides. Peak elevation discrepancy noted: given 8459 ft, Mountain Project lists 8400 ft (likely USGS map resolution).

blurb: Thunder Mountain and Slippery Slab Tower comprise a granite alpine rock climbing area in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Stevens Pass, Washington. Slippery Slab Tower (6,356 ft) is a popular alpine rock climbing objective accessed from the PCT via Trap Pass, offering short but well-protected routes on quality granite with excellent summit views. The area is part of the Wenatchee Mountains, managed by the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, and features numerous scrambling and climbing options.

routes.bestSeason: July through September provide the most favorable weather. July can have intense mosquito pressure. August-September offer the most reliable conditions with lower precipitation.
routes.beta: This is a two-pitch alpine rock climb that functions as a half-day objective after the long approach. Pitch 1 involves a low 5th class move followed by class 3 scrambling to a large tree belay (40 feet). Pitch 2 features face climbing transitioning to a gully, then exposed slab to the summit (50+ feet). The route can be climbed as one full 60m pitch or broken into two for better communication. A climbing register is present at the summit. Despite its name, this route is neither slippery nor a slab.
routes.approach: Start at Tunnel Creek Trailhead (3,100 ft). Follow Tunnel Creek Trail #1061 for 1.6 miles to Hope Lake (4,400 ft), gaining 1,141 feet over 2-2.5 hours. From Hope Lake, head south on the Pacific Crest Trail for 3.8-4 miles to Trap Pass (5,800 ft), gaining an additional 400 feet. From Trap Pass, follow an obvious climber's trail south along the east side of the ridge for 0.5 mile to the base of Slippery Slab Tower.
routes.descent: Rappel the climbing route using two rappels (60m double-strand or multiple singles). Follow boot path back to Trap Pass and reverse the approach via PCT and Tunnel Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, make two rappels down the climbing route to return to the base of the tower. Climbers typically use a 60m rope doubled for each rappel, or make multiple 30-50m single-strand rappels. Be mindful of loose, pointy rocks during the descent. Once back at the base, scramble back through the pass terrain to Trap Pass, then reverse the PCT and Tunnel Creek Trail descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (if overnight): Depart early morning from Tunnel Creek Trailhead, reach Hope Lake (2-2.5 hrs), continue to Trap Pass area and camp at one of the pleasant camping spots in the Thunder Mountain Lakes region (3-4 additional hours of approach). Day 2: Climb Slippery Slab Tower and return to camp (6-8 hrs). Day 3: Return to trailhead (4-5 hrs). For day trip, depart before dawn and allow 10-12 hours total.
routes.bail: From Trap Pass, retreat back down PCT and Tunnel Creek Trail to Hope Lake. From the tower base, retreat is straightforward by reversing the approach.
routes.turnaround: If climbing as a day trip, plan turnaround times conservatively given the long approach. The actual climbing portion is brief (2-3 hours), but the approach from Trap Pass requires 4-5 hours. Start early from the trailhead and plan to begin the climb by mid-morning.
knownHazards: Loose, deteriorating black rock on upper sections of pitch 2
Significant exposure on summit slab section during descent
Weather variability and rapid condition changes in the Cascades
Mosquitoes in early summer, especially July, can be severe in lake basins
Snow persistence on approach trail into July despite summer season
watchOut: Despite its name, this route is neither slippery nor a slab—expect solid granite
Loose rock on upper pitches requires careful gear placement and foot work
Early July mosquitoes can be unbearable; bring effective repellent or plan for August-September
The approach is long (6+ miles one-way); don't underestimate energy and time requirements
proTips: Break this into two pitches for better communication even though it can be done in one 60m pitch
There is a climbing register at the summit dating back to 1987-1988
An overnight trip is often more pleasant than a full day trip given the 6+ mile approach
The view of ranges south of Stevens Pass from the summit is exceptional and rarely seen by casual hikers
gear: Ice axe (if snow present on approach)
routes.detailedRack: Nuts and light rack of small to medium cams (BD#0.4-#2). Include a .3-3 size range of nuts. Bring slings and extra webbing for anchor building. Rap rings and extra cordage recommended for backup rappel anchors.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is readily available on this route with good placements in cracks and corners. Nuts and small to medium cams are the primary protection. Slings around natural features (trees, horns) provide secure anchors.

pitchDetail.notes: Begins with one low 5th class move from the initial belay. The remaining 40 feet is class 3 scrambling to a large evergreen tree with abundant webbing and slings. Straightforward ground once initial move is overcome.

Starts with 20 feet of face climbing leading to an open book move. Do not ascend the book but traverse diagonally left over an arete into a class 3 gully. Climb the gully 50 feet to a large boulder with quality anchor materials. From the boulder, climb 50 feet of exposed slab on better rock to the summit. Trending climber's left is generally easier.

routes.fa: Dave Harrah, William Herston, Keith Rankin, Ruth Rankin, May 1947
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable at Trap Pass and the tower base due to terrain and location within Alpine Lakes Wilderness. Satellite communication devices recommended for emergencies.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (primary); Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (eastern side of watershed)
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required
access.passRequired: Alpine Lakes Wilderness Permit required May 15 - October 31

road.name: Forest Service Road to Tunnel Creek Trailhead
road.status: Maintained seasonal road
road.seasonalGate: Road generally open June-September; verify conditions before travel
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, Washington: Head northeast on Highway 2, then follow Forest Service roads to Tunnel Creek Trailhead parking area. Road can be rough in spots; high-clearance vehicle recommended for early season travel.

climate.typical: Alpine climate with rapid weather changes. Exposed ridge terrain can be windy and subject to afternoon thunderstorms. Snow persists into July in some years.
climate.spring: May-June: Trail may have snow, especially above 5,000 feet. Mosquitoes begin in late May-early June. Unpredictable weather with temperature swings.
climate.summer: July: Warm days (68°F average high, 47°F low), 24% chance of rain daily. Mosquitoes severe, especially July. August: Warmest month (70°F high, 48°F low), rare rain (1.7 inches/month), mosquitoes decrease. September: Cooling to 61°F, increasing rain (2.5 inches), weather reliable.
climate.fall: September-October: Best overall weather. Temperatures 45-61°F. Lower precipitation mid-September, increasing by late October. Western larch turns gold.
climate.winter: October-May: Heavy snow in high terrain. Approach routes impassable due to snow and avalanche hazard. Not recommended.

timing.recommendedStart: Depart Tunnel Creek Trailhead by 5-6 AM for day trip; earlier departure preferred given 10-12 hour round-trip time

emergency.rangerStation: Leavenworth Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Leavenworth office)
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth (~60 miles from trailhead)
emergency.notes: Cell service unreliable; satellite communication recommended. Helicopter rescue possible in emergencies but weather-dependent. Self-rescue capability essential for this objective.

routes.bestSeason: June through early July offers the most stable weather and water availability. September conditions are drier but snow risk increases toward October. Spring (May) and late fall have significant snow hazard on approach gullies.
routes.overview: A nearly 1,500-foot overhanging prow of very sustained alpine climbing at 5.11+/5.12, featuring multiple roof systems, technical arete climbing, and serious exposure throughout. One of the most demanding alpine climbs in the Cascades.
routes.beta: Vanishing Point is approximately as steep a route as one could find on the mountain—nearly every pitch involves relentless overhanging terrain at 5.11–5.12. The route was established over several years and fully modernized in 2020 with new stainless-steel anchors and improved protection. Notable features include pitch 5 (crux, 5.12+), a collapsed roof section, and several intentionally runout pitches. The approach involves 800 feet of mostly roped-up slab climbing with fixed lines and bolts.
routes.approach: Start at Barclay Lake trailhead (74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288). Follow the pleasant level main trail for about 20 minutes to a bridge crossing a large creek (~1 mile). Before the bridge, break right into forest around a large boulder, then walk through relatively open forest or along the creek bed until reaching an open boulder field. Continue through the boulder field to reach steep gullies. Ascend a 60–70° forested rib with fixed lines and natural protection. Climb around the right side of a chossy waterfall (exposed scrambling). Continue up the wash to a ramp with hand lines, then ascend approximately 800 feet of mostly roped-up slab via bolted sections and natural features. Total approach time: 2.5–3.5 hours for competent parties.
routes.descent: Walk off via the heather descent to a BASE jumper trail system, then follow well-worn switchbacks through forested ridge system back to the Barclay Lake trailhead parking area. Approximately 3 hours car-to-car from summit.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend via the established BASE jumper trail system (marked on climbing area maps). The descent is non-technical but lengthy. Follow the forested ridge and switchback trail through old-growth forest back down to the Barclay Lake trailhead. The descent can be muddy and root-strewn; allow 3 hours total for descent and return to parking.
routes.bail: Retreat is possible via rappel using anchor bolts throughout the route. The approach anchors added in 2020 are now reversible with a single 70-meter rope, allowing full retreat from the approach slab if weather or other factors necessitate turning back.
routes.turnaround: If not making good time by mid-afternoon (typically by 12–1 pm depending on start time and climbing speed), seriously consider rappelling off rather than pushing through darkness. The descent is long and can be hazardous with exposure in poor light.
routes.rappels: Multi-stage descent via rap anchors spaced 30–35 meters apart; reversible with single 70-meter rope per 2020 modernization
gear: Single rack of nuts and cams to #1 (finger to mid-hand)
routes.detailedRack: Single rack to #1 with a few duplicates in the finger sizes. Recommended: 2–3 nuts in fingers, 2–3 mid-hand nuts, 2–3 hand-sized cams, 1–2 #1 cams. Approach climbing may require ice axe (early season). Small to medium nuts and slings optional but not required. 15 draws (mix of singles and doubles) recommended; 6 of these can be single runners.
routes.proNeeds: Primarily bolted sport climbing with some trad pitches. Anchors are bolted throughout; the bolts were replaced with stainless steel and upgraded during 2020 modernization. Climbers must be comfortable with self-rescue, anchor building, and transitioning between bolted and trad protection. The route involves high commitment and serious altitude—turnaround decisions should be made early.
proTips: Pitch 5 is the crux at 5.12+ (graded 5.11c on FA, but climbers report it as significantly harder). Scout the line on approach if possible.
The collapsed roof section on pitch 9/10 (rerouted in 2020) demonstrates route changes since FA—the route may still undergo evolution as weathering continues.
Pitch 8 is intentionally left with significant runout as a mental challenge; this is by design. Know your comfort level.
Start very early (4–5 am from trailhead) to maximize light on the climb and descent.
Water is usually available in the approach gully until early August; carry extra capacity if climbing later in season.
Bring a satellite communicator or PLB—cell service is unreliable in the Skykomish drainage.
The 2020 modernization improved the approach significantly; older trip reports may reference less secure sections.
Ben Gilkison's 2012 onsight of most pitches is impressive but not representative of typical climbing here—this is a serious, committing alpine route.
watchOut: Rockfall risk is real, especially on pitch 5 area where the roof has partially collapsed since 1999 FA.
Loose rock throughout approach and on tower—wear helmet at all times.
Multiple runout sections—not all are bolted; know the line before committing.
Serious exposure on most pitches—not a beginner alpine climb.
Approach is technical and hard to navigate even with beta; parties have gotten lost.
Weather can change rapidly in the Cascades—be prepared to descend or bivy.
Fixed lines on approach may be weathered or absent in some years—do not rely solely on them.
