knownHazards: Objective rockfall hazard on upper pitches and roof sections, exacerbated by weathering and freeze–thaw cycles.
Runout sections on pitch 8 (intentional) create exposure and mental difficulty.
Approach gully waterfall is chossy and exposed; loose rock is common.
The 60–70° forested rib with fixed lines presents scrambling hazard if weather or darkness arrives.
Summer thunderstorms can develop rapidly; lightning exposure is significant on the tower.
Lichen and moisture on bolts and slab sections create slipping hazard after rain.

pitchDetail.notes: Slab approach, bolted
Slab; follows bolts leftward, finger gear optional
Slab; 2 bolts added in 2020 to reduce runout
Face climbing; mix of bolts and natural gear
CRUX: Roof pitch, graded 5.11c FA but often felt as 5.12+; roof section has partially collapsed since 1999
Arete climbing; technical, exposed
Face; mix of bolts and cams
Runout section intentionally left with minimal protection as mental challenge
Rerouted in 2020 to eliminate 'death flake'; now avoids the collapsed section
Easier terrain; good rest
Arete; likely second crux of route
Sustained overhanging terrain
Roof; technical
Sustained
Face climbing
Overhanging terrain
Sustained pitch near summit
Top out terrain
Final pitches to summit block
Summit terrain; walk-off

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00 AM–5:00 AM from trailhead

whatToBring: Water or hydration system (2–3L for dry/late-season)
High-calorie food (energy bars, nuts, nut butter)
First aid kit (blood stoppers, pain relief, blister treatment)
Map and compass (GPS unreliable in dense forest)
Insulation bivy layer (in case of unplanned bivy; caves reported near route start)

access.landManager: US Forest Service, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required at Barclay Lake Trailhead

routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is unreliable or absent in the Skykomish drainage and on the tower. A satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, PLB, or similar) is strongly recommended for emergency communication and trip reporting. Notify the Skykomish Ranger Station before your climb if possible.

road.name: FS Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road)
road.status: Open (conditions vary seasonally)
road.driveNote: From US Highway 2 near the town of Baring, WA, turn north onto Barclay Creek Road (FS 6024), which is directly across from Der Baring Store. Cross railroad tracks and continue 4.3 miles on FS 6024 to Barclay Lake Trailhead parking area (~20 parking spaces). Road is usually passable June–October; spring runoff and winter snow may close access.

climate.typical: The Central-West Cascades are maritime-influenced with mild summers and wet winters. Expect rapidly changing weather, afternoon thunderstorms (especially July–August), and cool nights. Afternoon clouds and rain are common even in summer.
climate.spring: April–May: Snow lingers on approach gullies above 3,500 ft through May; ice axe often needed. Spring runoff swells creeks. Approach is technical and avalanche-hazardous early in season.
climate.summer: June–August: Most reliable climbing window. June is typically drier than July–August. Expect afternoon clouds and occasional thunderstorms. July–August can be humid and buggy. Water is abundant in approach gullies.
climate.fall: September: Cooling nights, shorter days, lower crowds. Early September resembles summer; late September sees increasing snow risk. October can have early snow on high terrain.
climate.winter: November–March: Route is snow and ice-covered; winter alpine climbing required. Approach is avalanche-hazardous. Not recommended for most climbers.

emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288. Hours: Wed–Fri 9:00 AM–12:00 PM, 1:00 PM–4:00 PM. Phone: (360) 677-2414.
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Health (Snoqualmie, WA), approximately 30+ miles southwest. Airlift via helicopter (SAR) likely required from route for serious injury.
emergency.notes: Rescue response time is long due to remote location and steep approach. Self-rescue skills and party discipline are essential. Notify ranger station before climbing. Carry a satellite communicator.

waypoints.name: Barclay Lake (creek crossing before main bridge)

blurb: North Face Tiffany Mountain is a 650-foot tall climbing area on the northeast side of 8,245-foot Tiffany Mountain in the Okanogan Range of Washington. The face features rough granite with several couloirs, arêtes, and vertical cliffs, making it an excellent alpine training ground for beginning climbers seeking early-season snow climbing and moderate rock routes.

routes.bestSeason: June through early July when snow is continuous but before it retreats entirely; snow melts fairly early in the summer once roads are accessible.
routes.overview: The longest and most continuous narrow snow gully on the left side of Tiffany's north face, averaging 40 degrees with steeper sections to 55 degrees.
routes.beta: A sustained snow climb with a crux at the upper narrow section. Approximately 5-hour round trip from trailhead depending on conditions. Route requires good snow conditions and avalanche awareness.
routes.approach: Drive to Tiffany Springs Campground (6,760 feet); follow trail #373 east past Tiffany Lake to Honeymoon Pass (7,150 feet, 45 minutes). From Honeymoon Pass, traverse southwest along the east slope below the ridge crest to the small cirque at the base of the north face (7,360 feet).
routes.descent: Walk off the northwest summit ridge to access the northeast ridge above Little Tiffany Lake. Follow the unmaintained trail down to the main trail at Honeymoon Pass.
routes.proNeeds: An ice axe is essential. If the snow is continuous, bring a rope and medium nuts and pickets for protection of the crux section.
watchOut: Hazardous emergency descent due to sparse anchor placements near the crux section
Route subject to avalanche risk on a north-facing aspect during heavy snow years
Early-season steepness and cornices on the upper section
knownHazards: Sustained snow slopes averaging 40 degrees
Crux at narrow upper section (up to 55 degrees)
Limited protection options for emergency self-rescue
Early melt-out reduces climbing window significantly

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning to avoid afternoon snow instability

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest

climate.forecastZone: WAZ049 (Western Okanogan County)
climate.typical: High alpine environment with persistent snow into June-July; dry in August-September
climate.spring: Heavy snow coverage; roads may not be accessible until May
climate.summer: Snow field conditions June-July; bare by late August; wildflowers peak mid-June through July
climate.fall: Rapidly clearing conditions; window closes by late September as new snow arrives
climate.winter: Heavy snow accumulation; not typically attempted

road.name: Forest Service Road #39 to Tiffany Springs Campground
road.status: Usually snowfree mid-June through September

road.driveNote: Rough roads require high-clearance vehicle; 8 miles north from FS Road #37 junction. Road conditions variable spring through early summer.

routes.bestSeason: June through early September when snow has cleared from lower sections but good anchor conditions remain; crux pitches are rock-dependent and best when dry.
routes.overview: An excellent six-pitch alpine climb on the North Tower ascending mostly quality granite rock on the right side of Tiffany's north face, with crack climbing, chimneys, and ridge scrambling.
routes.beta: A wonderful alpine rock climb with great exposure and views. The route offers generally solid protection with the crux featuring a wide flaring chimney and off-width section requiring careful gear placement. First three pitches establish the ridge; crux pitch (P4) is 5.7-5.8 in a chimney; final pitches are 4th class scrambling to the tower summit.
routes.approach: Park at Tiffany Springs Campground (6,760 feet) and follow trail #373 east past Tiffany Lake to Honeymoon Pass (7,150 feet, approximately 45 minutes). From Honeymoon Pass, traverse south on the grassy slope to reach the climb's base at approximately 7,600 feet (additional 30-45 minutes).
routes.descent: Walk off the northwest ridge of Tiffany and then head northeast above Little Tiffany Lake to rejoin the main trail at Honeymoon Pass.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the northwest ridge (mostly 3rd class scrambling) to approximately 8,000 feet, then contour northeast toward Little Tiffany Lake, picking up the northeast ridge trail that descends to Honeymoon Pass. Total descent time approximately 1.5-2 hours depending on snow coverage and group speed.
routes.detailedRack: Medium traditional rack: small stoppers from sizes 0-2, full range of cams from #00 to #4 (especially #2, #3, #4), extra-long slings (2-3) for chimney protection, 60-meter rope for single-pitch belays or simul-climbing on 4th class terrain.
routes.proNeeds: The route features reasonable protection on most pitches with solid rock quality. The crux pitch requires careful gear placement in a wide chimney and off-width section with tricky placements. Standard rack management and knowledge of crack climbing placement essential.

pitchDetail.notes: Two pitches climbing lower rock to join the ridge, scrambling terrain with easy climbing
Ridge scramble to the base of the North Tower
Climb up the tower with excellent climbing for first 75 feet, featuring a wide flaring chimney and off-width section with tricky gear placements; this is the technical crux of the route
4th class terrain to the top of the North Tower
3rd class walking along the ridge to reach the main slope of Tiffany and the summit

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (6-7am) to complete the climb before afternoon weather

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest

climate.forecastZone: WAZ049 (Western Okanogan County)
climate.typical: High alpine environment; afternoon thunderstorms common in summer; wind exposure on ridge
climate.spring: Lower sections may have snow; roads may not be accessible until mid-May
climate.summer: Generally dry rock conditions; occasional afternoon storms; best climbing conditions June-August
climate.fall: Dry, stable weather early fall (September); rapidly deteriorating by late September
climate.winter: Snow and ice on route; not typically attempted

proTips: The rock quality on this route is excellent and generally solid, making this a great beginner alpine rock climb
Early start is critical to avoid afternoon thunderstorms common in the Okanogan Highlands
The chimney crux (P4) can be managed by experienced crack climbers; wrist locks and chimney technique essential
Simul-climbing the easier pitches (4th class sections) can save significant time
watchOut: Wide flaring chimney on P4 crux requires precise gear placement; loose or unsafe placements are common
Off-width section on crux pitch is physically demanding; know your off-width climbing
Summit ridge experiences significant wind exposure; be prepared with extra layers
Afternoon thunderstorms typical in summer; lightning risk on exposed ridge
knownHazards: Steep approach gully with loose rock and fall hazard
Crux pitch (P4) with wide flaring chimney and tricky off-width section
Granite quality varies; some sections loose, particularly on P1-3
Exposure on ridge scrambles (P3, P5, P6) increases with altitude
Afternoon lightning storms common June-August

routes.bestSeason: June through early September, when snow has cleared from lower sections; provides alternative to the more technical North Tower Ridge Route with excellent exposure.
routes.overview: A five-pitch alpine ridge route on the north face that follows the same ridge as the North Tower Ridge Route while avoiding its steeper, more technical pitches. Features excellent granite arete climbing with great exposure and views.
routes.beta: A more moderate alpine alternative to the North Tower Ridge Route, following an exciting arete with great exposure but lower technical difficulty. Route can be split into two parts: approach on easier terrain, then a more dramatic arete section. Requires careful attention to loose rock and boulders throughout. Approximately 4-5 hour total time from campground.
routes.approach: From Honeymoon Pass (7,150 feet), ascend the northeast ridge trail toward the col above Little Tiffany Lake. Traverse south to reach the Tower Arete base at approximately 7,500 feet (approximately 30 minutes from pass).
routes.descent: Descend via Tiffany's northwest ridge to approximately 8,000 feet, then down the old northeast ridge trail to rejoin the main path at Honeymoon Pass.
routes.descentText: From the top of the Tower Arete, descend the northwest ridge (mostly 3rd class) to approximately 7,950 feet. Contour northeast and locate the old northeast ridge trail (may be faint). Follow it down to rejoin the main Tiffany Lake trail at Honeymoon Pass. Total descent time 1.5-2 hours.
routes.detailedRack: 60-meter rope essential for belays on steeper sections and rappel anchors if needed. Multiple extra-long slings for arete-top anchors and natural features. Light rack of cams and nuts (optional but recommended for self-rescue options).
routes.proNeeds: Route can be managed with 60m rope and slings for natural-feature belays. Limited artificial protection available due to arete terrain; strong ropework skills and comfort on exposed terrain essential.

pitchDetail.notes: Rocky ridge ascent (100 feet); scrambling terrain leading to the arete
Low-angled ridge travel (200 feet) along the arete with excellent exposure
Left-side bypass of vertical terrain (175 feet); avoiding the steeper sections of the North Tower Ridge Route
Tower summit approach (90 feet); final approach to top of North Tower
Final crest traverse (75 feet); walking along the ridge to reach Tiffany's main slope

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning to avoid afternoon thunderstorms

blurb: Gunn Peak is a 6,244-foot granite summit in Washington's Wild Sky Wilderness, the highest point in the range rising dramatically just east of Index in the Skykomish Valley. The peak was first ascended in 1915 by Dr. Harry B. Hinman, Walter Eriksen, and Louis Lesh on their fifth attempt.
hierarchyNote: Database elevation listed as 6,238 ft, but NAVD 88 official elevation is 6,244 ft. Corrected in structured output. Peak location and hierarchy confirmed accurate.

routes.fa: July 18, 1915 by Dr. Harry B. Hinman, Walter Eriksen, and Louis Lesh
routes.overview: The standard scramble on Gunn Peak accessed from Barclay Lake featuring technical class 3-4 granite scrambling with exposure, gaining 4,300-4,900 feet over 7.5 miles roundtrip with a famous hidden ramp crux and exposed ledge near the summit.
routes.beta: Serious alpine scramble requiring climbing experience and high mountain skills. Follows overgrown logging roads and climbers' trails from Barclay Lake, ascending steeply through forest to boulder field approach. Technical crux is an exposed class 3-4 hidden ramp with loose rock and significant exposure. A narrow ledge on north side of summit requires careful foot placement, especially in wet or icy conditions. Multiple search and rescue incidents have occurred on this peak.
routes.approach: From US Highway 2 at milepost 41-42 near Baring, Washington, turn north onto Barclay Creek Road (Forest Road 6024) and continue 4.3 miles to the Barclay Lake Trailhead parking area (20 car spaces). Follow old forest road approximately 100 yards to climbers' trail. Cross Barclay Creek via rock-hopping. Climb steep switchbacks on grippy dirt for approximately 1,500 feet, gaining elevation to about 3,800 feet. At opening below large rock face, bear right and traverse under cliff bands. Navigate boulder field approach to summit. Total distance: 7.5 miles.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route via down-climbing. Requires careful route-finding through cliff bands and boulder fields. Rappel station exists above main scramble section for optional rappel instead of down-climbing hidden ramp. Download offline maps before departing, as cell service is unreliable.
routes.descentText: Descending requires retracing your ascent route carefully. From summit, descend granite catwalk and scrambling sections back to top of hidden ramp. Some parties rappel using fixed anchors; others down-climb the exposed class 3 scramble. Descent requires checking GPS multiple times to maintain correct bearing through cliff bands and boulder fields. Exercise caution on wet or icy rock. Once through boulder fields and below cliffs, descend switchback trails back to forest. Down-climb on lower switchbacks is manageable but slippery when wet. Total descent time typically 2-3 hours.
routes.itinerary: Typical day-climb: Depart trailhead 6-7 AM for 10-hour round trip. Climb steadily on switchbacks for 2-3 hours to reach 4,500 feet elevation. Continue scrambling through open terrain for 1-2 hours to boulder field. Spend 1-1.5 hours navigating boulder fields and cliff bands. Climb hidden ramp and upper sections for 30-45 minutes to summit. Enjoy 15-30 minutes on summit. Return descent via same route taking 2-3 hours. Depart summit by early afternoon to ensure daylight for entire descent.
routes.bail: Bail points: Lower switchback section (descend back to trailhead at any point); Boulder field (descend back to trailhead by returning to forest edge); Hidden ramp (critical juncture - consider rappel descent or delay to better conditions rather than forcing technical down-climb in poor visibility).
routes.turnaround: Start descent by 3-4 PM at latest to ensure full daylight return to trailhead. Be especially conservative in autumn (shorter days) and spring (weather instability).
routes.bestSeason: July through September optimal, with August-September offering most stable weather. Late June and early October feasible during good snow years. Waterfall crossing less hazardous and water levels lower in late summer. Winter and spring not recommended due to avalanche-prone terrain and icy conditions on exposed sections. Most ascents occur after late July.
routes.comms: Cell phone service unreliable or absent in Barclay Lake area and Wild Sky Wilderness. Download offline topographic maps (Gaia GPS, Caltopo, or similar) before departing. Carry satellite messenger or personal locator beacon (PLB) if possible, as multiple search and rescue operations conducted on this peak.
objHaz: Loose rock on hidden ramp section
Narrow ledge near summit with exposure
Ice and snow on upper sections in early season
Stream crossing hazard during high water runoff
knownHazards: Hidden ramp is steep gully with loose rock and dirt; ascend right side toward hemlock trees rather than gully which is often wet and unstable.
Famous narrow rock ledge on north side of peak near summit gives many climbers pause due to exposure and drop-off; becomes particularly dangerous if snow-covered or icy.
Multiple search and rescue operations required on this peak; cell service unreliable.
Route is not a trail and should only be attempted by experienced climbers with scrambling and alpine experience.
Slippery dirt on upper switchback sections, especially when wet; gaiters and good boots essential.
Fall danger is significant; protection not in place except for optional rappel anchors on hidden ramp.
watchOut: Do not attempt without experience on class 3-4 terrain and scrambling skills.
Climbing helmet is essential, not optional, on this route.
Download offline maps before departing; do not rely on cell service.
Check weather forecast before departure and turn back if conditions deteriorate.
Be aware of hidden ramp crux and its loose rock; take time to secure good foot placement.
On summit ledge, move deliberately and avoid looking down if prone to exposure.
Start early to ensure full daylight for descent.
Descent route-finding is more difficult than ascent; maintain clear navigation.
proTips: Start approach around 6-7 AM to maximize daylight and minimize risk.
Hidden ramp not visible from below and is counter-intuitive; look for ramp on east side of main gully rather than climbing entire rockslide.
Handline or fixed rope sometimes installed on ledge traverse near summit by earlier parties; if present, use it carefully.
Reverse exact path on descent; do not attempt to cut alternate lines, as route-finding errors are common on descent.
Consider rappel station on hidden ramp if parties include nervous climbers or if down-climb would be excessively exposed.
Broader terrain is a massif rather than single peak; remain oriented to Gunn Peak specifically and avoid wandering to nearby East Gunn Peak.
Take breaks in shaded areas as upper sections offer minimal shade.
gear: Rope (50-60 meters, optional for rappel descents)
whatToBring: Plenty of water (3-4 liters minimum for 4,900 ft gain)
Layers for temperature changes at elevation
First aid kit with blister treatment
routes.detailedRack: No rock protection needed as this is a scramble, not a rock climbing route. However, carry 1-2 runners/slings and carabiners if planning to use rappel station above hidden ramp section. A 50-60 meter rope is optional but recommended for groups with inexperienced members or for emergency descents.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection required for ascent (scramble), but some parties install slings and anchors at hidden ramp crux to facilitate safe descent via rappel. If descending by rappel, ensure anchor integrity before committing weight.
routes.rappels: Optional rappel station exists above hidden ramp section (approximately one 30-40 meter rappel to bypass crux down-climb). Most experienced parties down-climb instead.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep forest trail and overgrown logging road, stream crossing, initial switchbacks on grippy dirt
Steep switchbacks on dirt trail gaining 1,500 feet to 3,800 feet elevation; slippery when wet

pitchDetail.pitch: Mid-section boulder field and cliff bands (Class 2-3)
pitchDetail.notes: Navigate under cliff bands, traverse talus and boulder field; route-finding critical
Steep exposed gully with loose rock; alternate lines available (left: chockstone move; right: exposed climb); exposure significant
Heather ledge and granite catwalk on north side, exposed class 2 traverse with 1000+ foot drop-offs; narrow and requires careful foot placement
Final scramble over boulders to summit register; brief but exposed

timing.recommendedStart: 6-7 AM for 10+ hour round trip to ensure daylight descent

road.name: Forest Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road)

blurb: Black Peak is a prominent 8,975-foot granite peak in the Cascade Range's North Cascades region, straddling the border between North Cascades National Park and Okanogan National Forest. The peak is renowned for its position near Highway 20 and its exposed, aesthetically stunning alpine ridges. First ascended in 1926 by USFS surveyor Lage Wernstedt, Black Peak offers multiple accessible alpine climbing routes with excellent views of the North Cascades' craggy spires and glacial valleys.

routes.fa: Roger Jackson and Michael Kennedy, September 1, 1973
routes.overview: A quintessential North Cascades alpine ridge combining low 5th-class rock climbing with 3rd and 4th class scrambling along an exposed, scenic ridge crest. The route features outstanding position and exposure, making it a popular training objective for climbers preparing for higher-altitude alpine objectives.
routes.beta: Most of the climbing is 3rd and 4th class with the low 5th class crux (approximately 5.2) occurring about halfway to the summit. Lower sections of the ridge feature looser rock, but quality improves with elevation. The route tends slightly left on the ridge crest where possible. Rock quality is poor to mediocre on the lower sections but becomes more solid higher up. Weather and snow conditions strongly affect difficulty and time requirements. Early-season ascents require crampons and ice axe; late-summer ascents may be snow-free. A rope and small alpine rack recommended for protection though many parties simul-climb sections.
routes.approach: Drive Highway 20 to Rainy Pass, approximately mile marker 157. Park at the Lake Ann/Heather Pass trailhead (requires Northwest Forest Pass). Hike the Lake Ann trail for approximately 1-1.5 miles to the Heather Pass junction. Follow trail toward Heather Pass then drop approximately 200 feet on a well-defined high traverse above Lewis Lake. Bypass Lewis Lake on the south side and continue through a series of larch meadows to reach Wing Lake camp (6,905 feet). From Wing Lake, ascend the obvious moraine on the west side to reach the low col between the northeast ridge and Point 8,995. This col marks the base of the technical climbing. Approach is approximately 4-6 hours from trailhead to Wing Lake camp.
routes.descent: Descend via the South Ridge route. From the summit, traverse south and downclimb easy class 4 rock on the summit ridge to reach a saddle. Continue down a steep scree gully to the south col, then reverse the ascent route back to Wing Lake through larch meadows. Alternatively, some parties reverse the ascent route down the northeast ridge itself. Descent is approximately 4-7 hours to Wing Lake, longer in snow conditions.
routes.descentText: The South Ridge descent is the preferred route off the summit. Traverse south from the summit to a class 4 downclimb section of approximately 30 feet that accesses a saddle immediately below the summit. Descend south through increasingly loose terrain toward a prominent col in the south ridge. Drop into a steep scree gully (with loose moraine on descent) that leads southwestward to the south col. From the col, traverse northwest through the larch meadow terrain past Lewis Lake, then follow the high traverse back to Heather Pass and descend the Lake Ann trail to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Rainy Pass trailhead (4,875 ft) to Wing Lake camp (6,905 ft), 4-6 hours, 5,000+ feet elevation gain. Day 2: Wing Lake camp to Northeast Ridge base (7,600 ft) to summit (8,975 ft) and return to Wing Lake camp. 4-7 hours summit; daytrip from camp is feasible for fit parties starting early. Descent via South Ridge can be made same day for 12-15 hour total from trailhead, or a two-day trip with overnight at Wing Lake is recommended for most parties.
routes.bail: From Wing Lake camp, retreat is straightforward via the approach route in bad weather or high avalanche conditions. On the ridge itself, the primary bailout points are the low col between the NE ridge and Point 8,995 (can descend southwest into the basin below), or retreat back down the route to Wing Lake. The northeast ridge does not have established alternate escape routes; full return via ascent route is the primary bail option.
routes.turnaround: Most parties summiting the Northeast Ridge do so in 4-7 hours from camp. A conservative turnaround time of 2-2.5 hours from the summit ridge base ensures sufficient daylight to return safely to Wing Lake camp even with slower descent times. If summit is not reached by early afternoon, descent should be considered due to rapidly deteriorating afternoon weather patterns common to the North Cascades.
routes.bestSeason: June through September offers the most stable weather and generally good rock conditions. June and early July require proficiency with crampons and ice axe due to sustained snow on approach. Late July through September provides snow-free or minimal-snow conditions on most ascent routes, with the most predictable weather typically occurring in August and early September. The peak is occasionally climbed in May and October during stable weather windows, though early and late season require careful weather assessment.
proTips: The crux pitch (5.2) occurs approximately halfway up the ridge; most parties can identify this section by scrambling left around loose sections then up the west face with significant exposure. Testing every hold is essential due to variable rock quality.
Rope-up before the first Class 5 section rather than simulclimbing early sections to minimize rockfall hazard from the party above.
Consider descending via the South Ridge route rather than retracing the ascent; it offers better routefinding and avoids the loose moraine on the northwest approach.
A small rack (wired nuts, a few cams) is sufficient; many climbers place only a few pieces of protection and simul-climb most of the route.
Wind exposure on the ridge is significant; bring a wind-resistant outer layer even in stable conditions.
watchOut: Loose rock throughout the lower sections of the ridge; test all holds and climb carefully to avoid dislodging rocks onto party members below.
High exposure on the ridge crest with sections featuring 2,000+ foot drops; maintain focus on balance and footwork, especially on downclimbing during descent.
Cornices may form on the summit block and near the ridge crest in winter and spring; approach the summit carefully and test cornices before proceeding.
Avalanche terrain on the approach: practice avalanche awareness and consider stability assessment of snowfields early in the season.
Weather deterioration is rapid; summit early and begin descent by early afternoon to avoid afternoon thunderstorms and lightning exposure on the ridge.
The knife-edge traverse near the summit involves 100+ feet of narrow scrambling; exposure is significant on both sides and requires confident scrambling ability.
knownHazards: Loose and variable rock quality lower on route; rock improves higher but remains moderately friable throughout the ridge.
Objective hazard from rockfall, especially when multiple parties are on the route; helmets are highly recommended.
Exposure of 2,000+ feet in places with narrow scrambling sections requiring careful footwork and balance.
Afternoon thunderstorm development common in summer; lightning exposure on the high alpine ridge is significant.
Avalanche potential on approach slopes and snow-filled gullies, particularly early and late season.
Cornice formation on summit area and upper ridge sections during winter and spring months.
Verglas (thin ice coating) on rock from clear winter nights and early-season freezing can make scrambling sections hazardous.
gear: Climbing shoes or boots suitable for scrambling (approach boots acceptable, sticky-soled climbing shoes preferred for rock sections)
Rope (50-60m dynamic climbing rope recommended for protection and belay stations)
Small alpine rack (approximately 8-10 wired nuts, 2-4 cams in 0.75-2 inch range, sling anchors)
Helmet (strongly recommended due to rockfall and exposure hazards)
Ice axe and crampons (essential early season; optional late season depending on conditions)
Backpack (20-30L day pack if climbing from camp; larger if approaching from trailhead in one day)
Water bottles or bladder (1.5-2L capacity; refill at Wing Lake or Lewis Lake)
whatToBring: Layered clothing system (base layer, insulating mid-layer, wind-resistant outer layer)
Hat and gloves (essential for morning approach and high elevation)
Headlamp or flashlight with extra batteries (for early starts and potential late return)
First aid kit with bandages, pain relief, and blister management
Navigation: map and compass or GPS device; print the route on a map given limited cell service
Snacks and lunch for sustained energy (energy bars, nuts, dried fruit, trail mix)
High-calorie energy drinks or electrolyte supplements
Emergency bivouac items (emergency shelter, extra layer, fire-starting materials)
Repair kit (cord, duct tape, carabiners for emergency fixing)
Toilet paper and cathole shovel (pack out all waste, especially in pristine alpine areas)
Medications (personal prescriptions, allergy medications if applicable)
Insect repellent (minimal insects at elevation but useful at lower elevations)
routes.proNeeds: A small alpine rack of wired nuts, cams, and slings is sufficient for protection on the Northeast Ridge. Many parties simul-climb 3rd and 4th class sections and only place a few pieces of protection on the crux 5th class pitch. Rock protection placements are generally straightforward on good rock sections higher on the route, though lower sections may offer fewer clean placements due to loose, fractured rock. A 50-60m dynamic rope is standard for establishing belays and rapping if needed, though full rappel descents are rarely necessary on this route—most parties downclimb or simul-rappel with the rope tied off to natural anchors.
routes.detailedRack: Light alpine rack: approximately 8-10 wired nuts (sizes 0-3, covering 0.5 to 2 inches), 2-4 cams (0.75-2 inch range such as a flexible four-cam C4 or x4), several cordelette slings (8mm or 9mm) for building anchors on horn features and rock pillars, one or two 24-inch Spectra slings, and a few quickdraws for efficient transitions. Additional options include a small nut tool for removal. Bring minimal gear to save weight given the long approach; most pieces serve primarily for safety on the crux 5th class sections rather than continuous protection coverage.

timing.recommendedStart: 4:00-5:00 AM for trailhead departure to ensure summit by early afternoon

climate.forecastZone: Puget Sound/North Cascades (NOAA Western Washington)
climate.typical: Highly variable; afternoon thunderstorms common in summer. Morning starts are typically clear before afternoon instability develops. Rapid weather changes typical of the North Cascades. Wind exposure on ridges is consistent.
climate.spring: Snow-covered at elevation with avalanche hazard on approach slopes. Freezing temperatures typical at summit. High wind and cornices common. Visibility variable. Generally considered marginal season due to objective hazards.
climate.summer: Most stable season. Morning starts typically clear and calm, with afternoon thunderstorm development common (peaks forming their own weather). Temperatures at summit 40-50 F. Snowpack persists in early summer; becomes minimal by August. High UV exposure at altitude.
climate.fall: Similar to mid-summer with decreasing storm frequency. Temperatures at summit 35-45 F. Generally more stable than summer with fewer afternoon storms. Earlier storm development possible in October. Clear mornings typical.
climate.winter: Extremely cold and windy. Heavy snow with significant avalanche hazard. Frequent storms and limited visibility. Route is rarely climbed December-March. Ice and verglas common on rock.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Wenatchee River Ranger District) and North Cascades National Park
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass ($5 per day or $30 annual) required for parking at Rainy Pass trailhead. Golden Eagle Pass also valid.
access.closures: Highway 20 (North Cascades Highway) is seasonally closed from November through April (typically opens mid-April). No winter access via Rainy Pass during road closure.

emergency.rangerStation: Wenatchee River Ranger District, Wenatchee, WA (administrative); no ranger station at Rainy Pass. Emergency response via Skagit County SAR.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (approximately 1+ hour by helicopter; 1.5-2 hours by ground ambulance from Rainy Pass). Level III emergency department. Alternate: Cascade Valley Hospital, Arlington, WA (Level IV emergency department).
emergency.notes: Cell phone service is unreliable in the Rainy Pass area and absent at higher elevations. No rescue infrastructure at the peak; all emergencies require helicopter evacuation from high altitude or self-rescue/assisted descent to trailhead. Satellite communicator recommended for solo climbers or remote climbing.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (Washington State Route 20)
road.status: Open mid-April through November; seasonally closed November through mid-April (winter weather).
road.seasonalGate: North Cascades Highway closed at both ends (east of Marblemount and west of Okanogan) during winter months; gate closure typically November 15 - April 15 depending on conditions.
road.driveNote: Rainy Pass is approximately 60 miles east of I-5 near Marblemount via Route 20. The pass is at 4,875 feet elevation and experiences heavy snow in winter. Drive time from Seattle area: approximately 2-2.5 hours. Parking area at Rainy Pass has 2 trailheads on the south side of Highway 20; arrive early on weekends as lot fills quickly (no reservation system). Gas and services available at Concrete before heading east; no services between Concrete and Winthrop.

hierarchyNote:
Peak verification passed: Black Peak is a real, identifiable mountain at 8,975 ft (2,736 m) in Washington State's Cascade Range, specifically in the North Cascades region on the border between North Cascades National Park and Okanogan National Forest. Coordinates 48°31′25″N 120°48′58″W confirmed across multiple authoritative sources. The provided elevation of 8,991 ft is 16 feet higher than consensus sources (8,975 ft); this appears to be a local survey variant or rounding—the peak is correctly identified. Parent hierarchy (Cascade Range/North Cascades area) is geographically plausible and confirmed."/>
</invoke>

Jötunheim is an alpine cirque/climbing area (not a peak itself) located at the base of Mount Index; the cirque base elevation is 3,765 feet, but Mount Index summit is 5,916-6,002 feet. This area/cirque distinction is important for hierarchy organization.
blurb: Jötunheim is an alpine cirque in Washington's Central-West Cascades near Skykomish, comprising granite Norwegian Buttresses that offer one of the few opportunities for multi-pitch big wall climbing in the Pacific Northwest. The area is distinguished by steep alpine granite rising 2,000 feet from Lake Serene, featuring both free and aid routes in a remote high-elevation cirque setting.

routes.rappels: Full route descent by rappel (pitches vary in anchor requirements)
objHaz: Water seepage and seasonal waterfalls on pitches 5-6
routes.overview: Jötnar is the most sustained aid route in Washington State, ascending the left side of Mount Index's North Norwegian Buttress via a continuous crack system located approximately 100 yards left of the Doorish line. The route features 2,000 feet of mixed free and aid climbing across 16 pitches on granite with strong alpine ambiance.
routes.beta: This is the only repeatable Grade VI big wall in the Pacific Northwest with bolted stainless steel anchors and portaledge bivy opportunities achievable by experienced wall climbers in 3-4 days. Climbing follows a continuous crack system featuring varied aid pitches including thin pins, rivets, hooks, and roof passages. The Fang Roof (Pitch 8) stands out as a classic pitch with hand crack free climbing through an overhang. Plan for 7 days of capsule-style climbing with portaledge camps to complete the first ascent.
routes.approach: From Mount Index Road trailhead, hike the well-maintained Lake Serene trail (4.1 miles, 2,000 ft elevation gain, 1.5-2 hours) to the north end of Lake Serene. Traverse around the right side of the lake on an unofficial trail descending granite slabs to talus (~15 minutes). Fill water at the lake, then hike up talus to the base of the North Norwegian Buttress (~15 minutes). Total approach time approximately 2-2.5 hours from trailhead.
routes.descent: Rappel the entire route. Many pitches overhang significantly; use directional gear placements for anchor setup. Pitch 7 requires either fixed lines or down-aiding (pitch overhangs 40+ feet). First ascensionists used one climber riding haul bags down approximately 1,000-1,200 feet before final team double-rope rappels off route anchors. Minimize environmental impact by removing all fixed lines.
routes.descentText: The descent is accomplished by rappelling the route in full. Due to the sustained overhanging terrain throughout most pitches, careful anchor placement is critical, particularly on pitches with significant roofs. Climbers should plan to use at least two ropes for rappelling. The first ascent team used an unconventional technique where one climber managed haul bags on a very long rappel before the remaining climbers rappelled in pairs off stainless steel anchors. Plan to remove fixed lines during descent to preserve the rock.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through early September is the optimal climbing window, chosen specifically to avoid the spring and early summer snowmelt that creates seasonal waterfalls on pitches 5-6. The marine west coast climate brings wet, heavy snow in winter with high avalanche danger, making winter climbing inadvisable. Summer offers the most stable weather and lowest objective hazards.
routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is limited and unreliable in the cirque and on the wall due to terrain and lack of tower infrastructure. Satellite communication options (satellite messengers, InReach, or Globalstar devices) are strongly recommended for emergency contact and trip coordination on multiday climbs.
gear: Double rack of nuts and cams (micronuts to #4 Camalot, single #5)
10 rivet hangers (including #1 size for deep rivets)
2 sets of hooks (bat hooks and other variants)
Portaledge (required; no natural ledges exist on route)
Portaledge fly and rain shell (afternoon seepage common)
routes.detailedRack: Climbers should bring a double rack of nuts and cams ranging from micronuts to #4 Camalots with one #5 Camalot. Include a single set of offset cams, 5 each of beaks in various sizes, 2 lost arrows for aid placement, and 10 rivet hangers (some in #1 size for deeper rivets). Two sets of hooks are essential given the blank sections requiring creative aid placements. A quality portaledge with fly and rain shell is non-negotiable, as afternoon seepage creates waterfall conditions on pitches 5-6. The route features bolted anchors, but climbers should verify anchor condition before committing full body weight and be prepared for occasional loose bolts.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is a mix of aid placements and bolted anchors. Lower pitches rely on crack climbing with natural protection (nuts and cams), while upper pitches require rivet placements, hooks, and beaks on blank sections. The route features some bolted anchors already in place from the first ascent, but climbers must assess anchor condition, bring spares for critical bivy sites, and be prepared for creative placements on sections lacking traditional cracks.
whatToBring: Helmet (mandatory for rockfall and rappel hazards)
Carabiners and lockers (large selection for rivet placements)
Slings and cord for anchor backup
Chalk bag and tape for hands
First aid kit including blister management and wound care
Headlamps with extra batteries for early starts and late climbs
Water bottles and hydration system (1-2 liters per climber per day minimum)
High-calorie food for 6-7 days of climbing (focus on high-energy, lightweight options)
Sunscreen and lip balm with SPF
Sleeping bag rated to 20 degrees F (insulation required on portaledge)
proTips: Pitch 7 is notably sustained with a 40+ foot overhang; consider leaving a fixed line here for the descent or mastering down-aiding technique before the climb
The Fang Roof (Pitch 8) is a classic section featuring beautiful hand cracks and excellent free climbing; savor this pitch if you're comfortable with 5.9 free climbing
Plan portaledge bivy sites carefully; only sheltered slabby belay ledges exist on Pitches 3 and 9, so other nights require suspended sleeping
Arrive at Lake Serene early to allow ample time for approach and to scout the talus section before committing to the wall
Late July and August offer best conditions; September window closes quickly as cooler nights and additional moisture increase difficulty
Remove all fixed lines and care for anchors; this route is special because of bolted anchors, so future climbers depend on your stewardship
watchOut: Rockfall is a significant hazard, especially in lower pitches where loose rock and chossy conditions exist; wear helmet at all times
Afternoon seepage on Pitches 5-6 creates waterfall conditions; start early and try to pass these pitches before midday if possible
Water and wet rock mean marginal protection on some aid pitches; test rivets and hooks carefully before committing full weight
Overhanging terrain on Pitch 7 and the Fang Roof (Pitch 8) requires solid aid climbing skills and careful anchor setup
Dehydration is a real risk on multiday climbs due to sustained effort and limited water access; drink consistently and plan water management carefully
Winter climbing is dangerous here due to wet avalanche danger and waterfall ice; avoid November through June
The descent demands serious rope management and anchor skill; an error here is far more dangerous than climbing
knownHazards: High avalanche danger in winter due to maritime-influenced wet, heavy snow accumulation
Seasonal waterfalls on Pitches 5-6 create significant water flow, loose rock, and marginal protection in spring and early summer
Loose and chossy sections in lower pitches (below Pitch 6) require careful climbing and helmet use
Rockfall hazard from both loosely-attached blocks and dislodged gear from above; keep time at the wall base minimal
Sustained overhanging terrain throughout requires solid aid climbing technique and proper anchor placement to avoid ground falls
routes.fa: Sam Boyce, Lani Chapko, Kyle Willis, August 2020
routes.turnaround: Alpine big wall climbing requires deliberate early turnaround decisions. If bivy sites are not established by mid-afternoon on each climbing day, or if weather deteriorates significantly, retreat using existing rappel anchors. Descent takes 12+ hours and must be completed safely; do not push to summit under marginal conditions.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike Lake Serene trail (4.1 miles, 2000 ft gain, 2-2.5 hours), approach cirque (30 min), scout wall and bivy location (1-2 hours). Day 2-5: Climbing days with portaledge camps. First ascensionists climbed Pitches 1-3 with bivy on Pitch 3. Pitch 4 (crux A3+) and Pitch 8 (Fang Roof) are highlights. Days 6-7: Finish upper pitches to summit, then full rappel descent (10-12 hours). Multiday success requires disciplined pacing and weather monitoring.

pitchDetail.notes: Approach pitch; moderate free climbing to establish first belay on the wall
130 feet; easy free climbing up small ramp to left, steep cracks to large ledge, then thin aid on face to stance below splitter crack
80 feet; splitter cracks to sheltered slabby belay; excellent portaledge bivy site
120 feet; crux aid pitch - A3 up left-facing crack with roof halfway up, hook doom block, exit corner right past hooks to stance below mud corner
Varied climbing with water seepage risk; afternoon waterfall conditions common
Continued aid climbing with water seepage; often running water in spring/early summer
130 feet; left-facing corner on rivets to steep crack series overhanging 40+ feet; fixed line recommended for descent
150 feet; A2 from belay, C1 up beautiful splitter hand crack through overhang (5.10), 5.9 free climbing to belay - classic pitch
140 feet; second aid crux - right of bush quest on thin pins, rivets, hooks; bat hooks bypass chossy corner; long reach to beaks near top at bolt (tension traverse or reach)
Mixed climbing transitioning to upper wall pitches
Continued aid with improving rock quality
Easier aid climbing as route approaches summit section
Further aid climbing with reducing difficulty
Upper pitches include traverse left to large right-facing corner with 5.10 or C1 root aiding, and a 5.7 mossy dihedral and gully to summit - deteriorating rock quality near finish

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (before 6 AM) for multiday climbs to maximize daylight

road.name: Mount Index Road (from US Route 2 at Milepost 35.2)
road.status: Open year-round to trailhead; can be snow-covered in winter
road.driveNote: From US-2 eastbound near Index, turn south at Milepost 35.2 onto Mount Index Road and drive 0.3 miles to Lake Serene and Bridal Veil Falls Trailhead parking (at 600 feet elevation). Road surface is paved and typically well-maintained.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades/Skykomish Valley
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation
climate.spring: Snowmelt and frequent rain create waterfall conditions on Pitches 5-6; wet, unstable snow; avalanche danger; not recommended for climbing
climate.summer: Most stable season (Late July-September) with lower precipitation; cool nights (50s F typical); afternoon heating can trigger seepage on steep sections
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures and increasing precipitation; climbing window closes by late September as cloud cover and moisture increase
climate.winter: Heavy wet snow, high avalanche danger, waterfall ice, and cloudy conditions; winter climbing inadvisable

access.landManager: U.S. Forest Service (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest)

emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park visitor services; local Forest Service office
emergency.nearestHospital: Providence Regional Medical Center Everett (Pacific Campus), approximately 1-1.5 hours from Index; Skagit Valley Hospital also serves the region
emergency.notes: Index is a small mountain town with limited services. Evacuation from the wall requires helicopter rescue (King County SAR). Satellite communication strongly recommended for multiday climbs.

routes.corrections: First ascent timing reported variously as August 2020 (AAC) or June-July 2021 (Climbing Guides article); authoritative AAC publication lists August 2020. Route base elevation 3,765 feet refers to cirque, not summit. Jötunheim is an alpine cirque/climbing area, not a peak itself.

waypoints.name: Lake Serene & Bridal Veil Falls Trailhead

routes.overview: The East Face is an improbably steep-looking face that yields solid alpine climbing with good rock quality. The route ascends easy gray-colored terrain on the right-hand side of the peak, featuring the distinctive White Staircase section—exposed Class 3 scrambling near the summit ridge.
routes.bestSeason: July through early September provide the most stable weather and longest daylight hours for this technical alpine climb. The Cascade River Road typically opens by late June, though heavy snow years may delay access to early July. September remains excellent but weather becomes more variable as the month progresses.
routes.approach: Drive from Marblemount up Cascade River Road 23.1 miles to the Cascade Pass Trailhead. Hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 miles to Cascade Pass (5,400 ft elevation). From the pass, go left (south) toward the start of the Ptarmigan Traverse and ascend Mixup Arm. An unofficial but distinct climbers trail leads up the arm at approximately 5,900 feet, where it leaves the ridge crest going left toward the Cache Glacier. Pay careful attention crossing steep snow gullies on the traverse—a slip results in a 400-foot fall. Arrive at the north edge of the Cache Glacier at approximately 6,300 feet. Aim for the V-Notch Gully on the right side of the glacier. The climb goes up the gully and down the south side approximately 100 feet, then gains the Southeast face via Class 4 ramps. Proceed up the gully to the saddle at the start of the White Staircase.
routes.descent: 5 rappels total. A single 60-meter rope is adequate for the complete descent.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the White Staircase and Southeast face via five rappels. The route follows the path of ascent. A 60-meter single rope is sufficient length for the complete descent. Some rappel anchors may require maintenance or repair—bring extra slings and carabiners to reinforce existing anchors as needed.
routes.itinerary: Full day alpine climb: Leave Marblemount by 5:00 AM to maximize approach daylight. Drive approximately 1 hour to trailhead. Hike Cascade Pass Trail (3.7 miles, 1,700 ft gain) in 2-3 hours, reaching the pass by mid-morning. Scramble and traverse to Cache Glacier (1-1.5 hours). Technical climbing on East Face (2-3 hours). Descend by five rappels (1-1.5 hours). Return to trailhead by late afternoon or early evening depending on group speed. Plan for 7-9 hours total from parking lot to summit and back.
routes.bail: From the approach terrain below the Cache Glacier, retreat directly back toward Mixup Arm if weather deteriorates or the party tires. From the technical climbing section, downclimbing Class 4 terrain is possible but dangerous; if in doubt, continue through to summit and descend via established rappels. There is no safe mid-route bail from the White Staircase—commit fully to the climb or retreat before reaching the saddle.
routes.turnaround: Plan to turn around no later than 3:00 PM to allow sufficient daylight for the five-rappel descent. Weather commonly deteriorates in afternoon hours; avoid being caught on exposed terrain after dark.
objHaz: rockfall from loose terrain on Southeast Face and surrounding walls
loose and crumbly rock on SE-facing slopes
high exposure on White Staircase section with 100+ foot drops
steep snow gullies with 400-foot fall consequences
cornice hazards near Cascade Pass and Cache Glacier
knownHazards: Rockfall is a serious objective hazard on both ascent and descent, particularly from the SE Face and surrounding cliffs
Steep snow gullies on the Mixup Arm traverse require confident self-arrest skills and careful foot placement
The White Staircase is exposed Class 3 scrambling; a fall on this section is likely fatal given the terrain below
Loose rock is endemic to the Southeast-facing slope; exercise caution and move efficiently to minimize exposure time
Weather windows are brief in the North Cascades; afternoon storms can develop rapidly with little warning
watchOut: Do not downclimb the White Staircase under any circumstances—the exposure and loose rock make descent incredibly dangerous
The East Face looks harder than it climbs, but solid mid-5th class climbing near the summit requires focus and good footwork
If you feel unsafe on any section, retreat immediately; the exposure consequences are severe
Cross snow gullies during stable temperature windows when snow is most consolidated
proTips: Arrive at Cascade Pass trailhead before 8:00 AM to secure parking; the lot fills quickly mid-July through early October
Start climbing by mid-morning to complete the descent before 5:00 PM and avoid getting caught in afternoon weather
The White Staircase may look overly exposed, but stick to the right-hand line and trust the rock quality
Bring extra slings and carabiners; some rappel anchors benefit from reinforcement and backup
Download offline maps before cell service drops a few miles up Cascade River Road
gear: Trad rack: nuts, cams, and slings (light-to-moderate protection)
Crampons and ice axe for approach snow/glacier sections
Approach shoes with good grip for Class 4 scrambling
routes.detailedRack: A light-to-moderate rack of traditional protection serves the 2-pitch climb. Bring a selection of nuts (micronutrients through 2-3 inches), a few small to medium cams (0.5-2 inches), and multiple slings for anchors and protection. The White Staircase section offers limited pro placement despite its exposure—much of the climbing involves secure down-climbing and scrambling on good rock rather than continuous belayed pitching.
routes.proNeeds: The White Staircase and surrounding terrain offer minimal protection opportunities despite Class 3 and 5.0 difficulty. Climbers often treat exposed sections as simul-climbing or down-climbing unprotected. Bring your rack for sections where solid anchors exist, but understand that many sections will be unprotected due to terrain and rock quality.
whatToBring: 2-3 liter water bladder/bottles (water scarce above Cache Glacier)
Sunscreen and sunglasses (high UV at altitude)
Headlamp with extra batteries (for early start/late return)
Repair kit with tape and cord
Toilet paper and trowel (leave no trace)
Map and compass (download offline before losing cell service)

pitchDetail.notes: Approach pitch from Cache Glacier via V-Notch Gully to the Southeast face ramps. Navigate Class 4 scrambling via ramps and gullies to reach the saddle at the base of the White Staircase. Variable rock quality; loose holds common. Gain approximately 600-800 feet to reach the White Staircase saddle.

Exposed Class 3 scrambling on the distinctive White Staircase, a gray-colored high-angle terrain feature. The route follows the right-hand side of the staircase. Short mid-5th class section (approximately 15-20 feet) near the summit ridge to a notch in the exposed summit ridge. Follow the ridge south a short distance to the true summit. Extreme exposure with 100+ foot drops possible. Rock quality generally good but maintain three-point contact throughout.

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00-6:00 AM from Marblemount to maximize daylight; reach Cascade Pass by mid-morning

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Seasonal gravel road; typically open late June through October
road.seasonalGate: Road opens by late June or early July depending on snowpack; closes in fall due to weather. First 10 miles paved, remaining 13 miles rough gravel with steep narrow switchbacks.
road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from Marblemount (State Route 20) to Cascade Pass Trailhead. Drive time approximately 1 hour on gravel. Cell service drops within a few miles of Marblemount. Trailers and RVs over 22 feet long or 8 feet wide prohibited past milepost 18. High-clearance vehicles recommended. Download maps before departing. Parking fills quickly mid-July through early October; arrive before 8:00 AM for best parking availability.

climate.forecastZone: NOAA Pacific Northwest coastal mountains
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with high precipitation. Weather is highly variable. Afternoon thunderstorms are common mid-July through August.
climate.spring: Heavy snow at elevation; approach usually not feasible until late June
climate.summer: July and August offer the most stable weather windows. Afternoon storms frequent; mornings generally clear. Average high near 60°F at pass level. Snow and ice persist above 6,000 feet into July.
climate.fall: September is excellent with crisp, stable conditions and long daylight. October weather becomes variable. Early snow possible by late September.
climate.winter: Peak closed; typically inaccessible November through May due to snow and avalanche danger

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (peak) and Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (approach corridor)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for overnight trips within North Cascades National Park. Permits are limited and must be obtained in advance, particularly for mid-summer dates.
access.closures: The peak is in the North Cascades National Park and Glacier Peak Wilderness area. Road access via Cascade River Road is seasonal, typically closed late November through April or May depending on conditions. Fires not allowed at backcountry camps.

emergency.county: Skagit County (primary); Chelan County (secondary, shares border)
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Call 911 for emergency services. Skagit County Sheriff Search and Rescue provides primary rescue response. RiverCom 911 Dispatch Center (Chelan County) may assist depending on exact incident location.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Visitor Center: (360) 854-7200. Park rangers provide emergency support and rescue coordination.
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, 300 Hospital Parkway, Mount Vernon, WA 98273. Phone (360) 424-4111. Located approximately 40 miles southwest, 45-60 minute drive plus helicopter evacuation time if flight rescue required.
emergency.notes: Cell service is unreliable in the Cascade Pass area and drops within a few miles of Marblemount. Satellite communicator or personal locator beacon strongly recommended. Rescue response times depend on weather and visibility; helicopter evacuation from this elevation and terrain is challenging.

blurb: Hook Creek Drainage is a climbing and access area in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Leavenworth, Washington, offering approach routes to The Mole (Edward Peak, 7,280 ft) and other peaks in the Enchantments region. The area features quality granite climbing accessed via Hook Creek and Rat Creek drainages. The Mole was first ascended in May 1948 by Fred Beckey, Wesley Grande, and Ralph Widrig via the south face.
hierarchyNote: No hierarchy concern identified. Hook Creek Drainage is a valid climbing area within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness, and The Mole (Edward Peak, 7,280 ft) with GPS coordinates 47.5427, -120.7106 is geographically located within this drainage system.

routes.fa: Don Gordon, Pat Callis, Eric Bjornstad, Dan Davis 1962
routes.overview: The North Face of The Mole (Edward Peak, 7,280 ft) is an amazing alpine rock route offering 500 feet of easy, adventurous 5.7 climbing on the tallest of a quartet of granite spires at the northern fringe of the Enchantments, with unique views of the Icicle River valley.
routes.beta: Follow the obvious large crack system, which splits into two variations around 60 meters, both rated 5.7. The climbing throughout both pitches is consistent and enjoyable with no stand-out crux. Mossy cracks lead up and to the left, then back right onto the summit. An easy sandy gully (or snow) leads to the base.
routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive up Icicle Creek Road (Forest Road 76, 4.2 miles from SR-2) and park in the alphabet rock parking lot. Cross the bridge and head to Hook Creek Boulder. Pass the boulder and proceed up the drainage on an established trail toward Yellow Jacket Tower. Near Yellow Jacket Tower, branch right into Hook Creek Drainage through boulder fields to Edward Mesa. Approximate time from campsite to climb base: 40 minutes.
routes.descent: A three-rappel descent heads east, ending near a prominent chockstone chimney. Rappel stations are single-anchor with additional cordage. Alternative: scramble down the gully.
routes.descentText: Descend via three rappels to the east side of the peak, with each rappel approximately 30 meters using standard 60m ropes. Anchor stations use single bolts with supplemental cordage. End of rappels occurs near a prominent chockstone chimney. Alternative descent: scramble back down the gully to the base.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Hook Creek Boulder parking area, hike to Edward Mesa or alpine camp (5,000+ ft elevation gain, 6-8 hours). Day 2: Approach The Mole base (40 minutes), climb North Face (3-4 hours), descend. Day 3: Descend to vehicles (2-3 hours).
routes.bail: Retreat via the approach gully at any point. If above the first pitch, rappel or downclimb to base and traverse out via the gully.
routes.turnaround: Turn around if any team member is uncomfortable with the technical rock climbing, rappelling, or if weather deteriorates. The exposure and rappel descent warrant confident skills. Consider party fitness and conditions carefully.
routes.bestSeason: June through September offer the best climbing conditions. June and early July may have lingering snow. Late June through August typically provides the most stable weather. September can experience early storms and temperature drops.
routes.comms: No specific cell phone coverage documented for this remote alpine peak. Bring satellite communicator or personal locator beacon for emergencies.
proTips: The Mole offers excellent granite quality despite some mossy sections on the North Face
Bring extra slings for rock protection and anchor building at this remote location
The three-rappel descent is straightforward but requires careful anchor setup on single bolts
The approach via Hook Creek requires good route-finding and scrambling skills
Golden eagles nest nearby seasonally - check seasonal closures
This peak is rarely visited, offering solitude and unique views of the Enchantments
watchOut: Golden eagles nest on Bridge Creek Wall with closure Jan 1-Aug 15 (1/2 mile buffer)
Mountain goats frequent the approach areas and can cause rockfall
Moss and lichen on cracks can obscure rock quality and make protection placement tricky
The single-bolt rappel anchors require solid setup and backup anchors
Exposure on upper pitches may warrant hand lines or belay for less confident climbers
Icicle Creek Road experiences seasonal repairs and closures
knownHazards: Raptor nesting closure (Golden eagles, Jan 1-Aug 15, 1/2 mi radius from Bridge Creek Wall)
Mountain goat presence may cause rockfall during approach
Exposed rappel anchors on single bolts with significant exposure
Moss and lichen on North Face can reduce friction
Remote location with extended rescue response times

pitchDetail.notes: Follow obvious large crack system going up and to the left. Crack climbing with mossy sections. Approximately 200-250 feet. Gear to 3 inches.
Route splits into two variations, both rated 5.7. Trends back right and upward toward summit with exposure. Approximately 250 feet to summit. Hand line recommended for less experienced climbers.

routes.proNeeds: Trad rack with gear up to 3 inches. Bring full complement of nuts and cams: multiple pieces 0.5-3 inch, at least 2 of each size. Supplemental slings and cordage for backup anchors. 60m rope standard. Helmet mandatory.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: None, but Northwest Forest Pass required for parking ($5/day or $30/year)
access.permit: Free self-issued Wilderness Permit required May 15-Oct 31
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass required for parking
access.closures: Golden eagle closure: January 1-August 15, no activity within 1/2 mile of Bridge Creek Wall. Check Icicle Creek Road conditions with Forest Service.

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00-7:00 AM for alpine start

road.name: Icicle Creek Road (Forest Road 76)
road.status: Generally open Jun-Sep; winter closures possible
road.seasonalGate: None, but road conditions vary seasonally
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth at SR-2, head south on Icicle Creek Road 4.2 miles to Hook Creek Boulder parking area. Road is paved initially, then forest service road. Verify current access conditions with Forest Service (509-996-4000).

climate.forecastZone: Alpine Lakes Wilderness Zone, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
climate.typical: Alpine climate with significant variability. Summer highs 60-70F, lows 40-50F. Afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August.
climate.spring: May-early June: lingering snowpack, possible avalanche hazard, cold nights (30-40F)
climate.summer: June-August: best conditions. Highs 63-70F, lows 41-45F. Mornings clear, afternoon thunderstorms possible. Precipitation 1.7-2.2 inches monthly.
climate.fall: September: Highs 61F decreasing to 50s, lows 39F to 30s. Increasing precipitation. October onwards: heavy rain, early snow possible.
climate.winter: November-April: heavy snowpack, freezing temperatures. Not typically climbed.

emergency.rangerStation: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District: 509-996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Medical Center, Leavenworth (~20 miles, 30-40 min drive)
emergency.notes: Remote alpine location with extended rescue response times. Satellite communicator or PLB strongly recommended. Cell service unreliable.

objHaz: Raptor nesting closure affecting access timing
Exposure on technical pitches and rappel descent
Remote location with extended rescue times

blurb: Red Mountain is a distinctive 5,890-foot peak in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness near Snoqualmie Pass, known for its characteristic red volcanic rock. Located just 3 miles north of the pass in the Cascade Range, it is one of the most frequently climbed summits in the Snoqualmie area, offering straightforward Class 2-3 scramble routes with excellent views and accessible alpine terrain. Despite loose rock quality, the mountain is popular for quick alpine ascents and combinations with nearby peaks.

routes.overview: The standard and most popular route on Red Mountain, ascending loose red rock on the west slope via a well-traveled boot path and scramble. Class 2-3 terrain with alternating stretches requiring hands and sections of easy fourth-class scrambling, featuring no significant exposure but considerable loose rock.
routes.beta: Approach via Pacific Crest Trail north from Exit 52, following Commonwealth Creek trail to the base of the mountain. The final 1,000-1,100 feet ascends the west-facing slope at 30-40 degrees inclination. Most loose rock has been removed by frequent traffic. South-facing sections feature more solid red volcanic slabs. Winter ascents are possible with snow coverage on the southwest ridge when avalanche conditions are stable.
routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass West), turn north and cross under the freeway. Turn right onto the paved Pacific Crest Trail trailhead road and park at the PCT lot. Hike north on the well-maintained PCT for approximately 3 miles, following Commonwealth Creek Trail. The trail passes through old-growth forest with areas of blueberries and salmonberries. Reach the base of the mountain where the steep scramble begins, marked by cairns.
routes.descent: Descend via the same west-slope route. Exercise caution on loose rock sections, especially if other parties are climbing above you. Experienced parties can move together on the easier lower slopes to save time.
routes.descentText: Retrace your ascent route down the west slope of Red Mountain. The descent generally takes 1-1.5 hours. Use caution on loose terrain, particularly the Class 3 sections higher on the mountain where rockfall is a concern if other climbers are present above. The lower portions of the west slope are Class 2 and travel faster. The well-worn boot path is generally easy to follow in descent.
proTips: Bring a helmet even on the scramble—loose rock is common and rockfall hazard is significant when other parties are present
The solid south slabs offer better rock quality than the east and west ridges; consider them for descent if comfortable with exposure
Start early in the day to allow time for the long approach and to avoid being on the mountain during afternoon weather changes
Red Mountain makes an excellent combination with nearby Lundin Peak or Kendall Peak to maximize elevation gain in a single day
Winter ascents are feasible but require avalanche awareness; the SW ridge is the preferred winter line and should only be climbed with stable avalanche conditions
watchOut: Loose red volcanic rock throughout, especially on the east and west ridges near the summit
Significant rockfall hazard when climbing with other parties; helmet is essential
40-degree slopes become dangerously slippery when wet
Winter avalanche danger on steep flanks and the southwest ridge line
Exposed scrambling sections have no protection despite being low-exposure terrain
knownHazards: Loose red volcanic rock with multiple intersecting fracture lines
Rockfall from parties above
Wet conditions create slippery slopes
Winter avalanche danger from wet maritime snow
Limited exposure but consequences of falls are serious on loose terrain
whatToBring: Map and compass or GPS device
Gaiters to keep gravel out of boots
routes.detailedRack: No protection or rack required for standard scramble route. Winter ascents of southwest ridge may benefit from running belays on the lower ridge but are not technical climbing.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection needed. Winter ascents benefit from knowledge of crampon and ice axe technique for steep snow, but the angle is insufficient to require belay protection on most years.
routes.bestSeason: The optimal climbing window is June through October, with peak conditions typically June through September. July through September offer the most stable weather and lowest avalanche danger. June and October may encounter lingering snow or early storms. Winter ascents are possible but require careful avalanche assessment and stable, cold conditions to consolidate the snow.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, 6-7 AM

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass: $30 annual or $5 day pass
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful Interagency Pass)

routes.comms: T-Mobile provides the best cellular coverage in the Snoqualmie Pass area at 92%+ availability. Other carriers (AT&T, Verizon) also available but with variable coverage. Terrain and weather can degrade signal. Sat communication devices recommended for emergencies on longer trips into the wilderness.

climate.forecastZone: Eastern Washington Cascades Crest (NWAC)
climate.typical: Maritime mountain climate with wet, heavy snow in winter and mild summers. High precipitation year-round.
climate.spring: May-June brings rapidly changing conditions with snow melt and increasing rockfall. Not ideal for climbing.
climate.summer: July-September: warmest period with highs 65-72°F, most stable weather, lowest precipitation. Ideal climbing window.
climate.fall: September-October: weather becomes unstable with increasing precipitation and the first serious storms. Still climbable but requires caution.
climate.winter: December-February: extremely heavy maritime snow (27+ inches per month), high avalanche danger, temperatures 20-25°F. Suitable only for experienced parties in stable conditions.

emergency.rangerStation: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest offices in North Bend
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA 98065 (~15 miles)
emergency.notes: King County Search and Rescue (volunteers and professionals) operates in the Snoqualmie Pass area. Pre-assembled SAR teams visit trailheads May-September on weekends and holidays. Snoqualmie Pass Fire and Rescue provides immediate emergency response (avg 550 calls/year for area).

road.name: I-90 to Exit 52, then paved road to PCT trailhead
road.status: Open year-round; Exit 52 accessible in most conditions
road.driveNote: From Seattle, drive I-90 east to Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass West). Turn north at the bottom of ramp and cross under the freeway. Turn right onto paved spur road in approximately 100 yards to reach the PCT North trailhead parking area. Lot can fill on weekends in summer. No fuel or services at trailhead; last services at Snoqualmie Pass, 3 miles south.

pitchDetail.pitch: Lower west slope to boulder field
pitchDetail.notes: Well-traveled boot path through scattered trees and meadow. Gentle to moderate incline, loose gravel and scree.
Steeper terrain at 30-40 degrees. Frequent loose rock requiring careful foot placement. Hands needed frequently. Rock quality poor on ridges, better on south-facing slabs.
Exposed scramble over loose slabs. Most technical section. Good hand holds available. Exposure is minimal despite terrain steepness. Solid rock on south-side alternative.

routes.bail: Parties can exit the route at any point by descending the ascent route. The west slope offers consistent descent options and no commitment once above the base. For winter ascents, bail from lower ridge to avoid cornices.
routes.turnaround: For safety, most parties should plan to be off the mountain and descending by early afternoon to avoid weather deterioration. Turn around by early morning at the latest to ensure daylight for the entire descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Start from PCT parking lot early morning. Hike 3 miles on PCT/Commonwealth Creek trail (1.5-2 hours) to base of Red Mountain (2,900 ft elev). Scramble 1,000-1,100 feet on west slope (1.25-1.5 hours) to summit (5,890 ft). Enjoy views of Snoqualmie Peak, Kendall Peak, and the Alpine Lakes. Descend via same route (1.25-1.5 hours). Total time 4-5 hours from parking lot. Most parties complete as a day trip.
routes.corrections: Elevation listed as both 5,890 feet and 5,349 feet in different sources. WTA and Wikipedia confirm 5,890 feet (1,795 m) as correct. Mountain Project indicates 5,349 feet, which is incorrect and contradicts USGS and multiple authoritative sources.

hierarchyNote: Peak data verified: Red Mountain at 5,890 ft near Snoqualmie Pass (3 miles north) in King County, Washington is correctly identified. Located in Alpine Lakes Wilderness, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. Coordinates (47.4365, -121.4285) confirm position. Hierarchy is appropriate—peak is a standalone summit on the E-W ridge between Lundin Peak and Kendall Katwalk, with parent peak Snoqualmie Mountain. No conflicts detected."

blurb: Mount Triumph (7,277 ft) is a distinctive alpine peak in the North Cascades approximately 5.5 miles west-northwest of Newhalem, characterized as "a rock thumb with near-vertical to overhanging faces on three sides." Composed of ancient orthogneiss, the peak was first summited on July 31, 1938 by Lloyd Anderson, Lyman Boyer, Dave Lind, Sig Hall, and Louis Smith, and commands one of the most striking positions in the range above the Pickets and Skagit Valley.

routes.overview: The Northeast Ridge is a classic alpine rock route on sustained exposure featuring mixed fourth and low fifth class climbing culminating in a memorable 5.7 offwidth crux. The route traverses a sharp, propeller-bladed ridge with excellent rock and commanding positions above the Pickets and Skagit.
routes.beta: The Northeast Ridge, first climbed in 1965 by pioneering alpinists Joan and Joe Firey, follows good rock on a long ridge with mostly low 5th class climbing. Mostly Class 4 scrambling can be simulclimbed where the terrain allows, with several steep sections over gendarme towers and a knife-edge ridge section requiring belay. The route encompasses approximately 10 pitches and requires significant alpine rock experience.
routes.rappels: 6-11 rappels (typically 7-9 double-rope rappels plus 2 single-rope rappels); downclimbing between stations
routes.bestSeason: July through September when the access road and trail are open, the approach gully has largely melted out, and the rock is dry. July and August offer the most stable conditions. Earlier in the season, the approach is a snow climb with real avalanche hazard on the maritime snowpack; later, the gully can melt awkwardly and first storms arrive. A settled forecast, firm morning snow in approach gully, and dry rock are what make Triumph reasonable.
routes.approach: From Highway 20 near Newhalem, turn north onto Thornton Creek Road (FR 3745) and drive 5.3 miles to the Thornton Lakes Trailhead at 2,600 ft. Follow the Thornton Lakes Trail (4.5 miles, 2,200 ft gain) to Lower Thornton Lake at 4,400 ft. Traverse west around the lake to Middle Thornton Lake and ascend a steep ravine/gully (1,200 ft, Class 3) northwestward on faint climber's trail to reach the col campsite at 5,760 ft. Total approach time from trailhead: approximately 5-5.5 hours. The approach gully contains loose and steep talus and grass with a climber's path visible approximately one-third of the way up.
routes.descent: Descend via 6-11 rappels (typically 7-9 double-rope rappels plus 2 single-rope rappels with 60m ropes) interspersed with strategic downclimbing along the ridge crest. Anchors are generally solid and abundant. Return to col campsite requires approximately 6-7 hours.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb or rappel Class 4 terrain to the base of the crux pitch. Downclimb and rappel past the crux 5.7 offwidth (double rope rappel). Continue with 5-7 more rappels and downclimbing along exposed ridge sections, moving between anchor stations. Most rappels are 30-40 meters; some sections allow downclimbing. Existing anchor stations are generally reliable (sling anchors, occasionally with abandoned gear). Take care on the knife-edge ridge sections where both sides drop steeply.
