Return to the great notch below the summit, then continue rappelling to the base of the route above the col campsite. Be alert for loose rock and vegetation on descent.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike from trailhead (2,600 ft) to Lower Thornton Lake (4,400 ft, 4.5 miles, ~4 hours). Continue to Middle Thornton Lake, then ascend gully to col campsite (5,760 ft, 1-2 hours). Day 2: Leave early morning (5-6 AM) and climb Northeast Ridge to summit (7,277 ft, 6-7 hours climbing). Descend partway or completely back to col, or establish bivy camp on ridge if summit day is late. Day 3: Continue descent if needed, or exit from col via approach route to trailhead (5-6 hours).
routes.bail: From the col campsite (5,760 ft), descend the approach gully and return to Thornton Lakes and trailhead. From the ridge, retreat via rappels and downclimbing back to the col campsite if conditions become dangerous. The route is sustained and has limited natural bail points; retreat typically requires full rope systems.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time (e.g., 2 PM) to ensure sufficient daylight and time for technical descent. The route requires 6-7 hours of climbing and 6-7 hours of descent; plan accordingly for daylight and bivy placement.
knownHazards: Exposure on narrow, knife-edge ridge with fourth- and fifth-class climbing on both sides
Loose rock common on descent, especially on upper ridge sections
Wet gneiss is dangerous when wet; dry conditions essential
Early-season avalanche hazard on approach gully from maritime snowpack
Small glaciers on east and north faces; glacier crossings may involve crevasses and moats in summer
Weather can deteriorate rapidly; the peak is only suitable for late spring/summer ascents as rime ice and snow would make scrambling unsafe
Objective avalanche hazard from ice and rock falling from glaciers onto approach gully
Exposure hazard on gendarme towers; falling climbers or rope management issues could be fatal
Limited water sources on ridge; carry 2+ liters from col campsite
watchOut: Do not attempt this route if rock is wet or plastered with snow/rime ice
The Class 4 west ridge is explicitly dangerous when wet; the Northeast Ridge wants dry gneiss
Rope management critical on narrow ridge; consider simul-climbing low-angle sections
Descending rappels in failing light on exposed terrain is extremely hazardous; allow ample time
Loose vegetation and rock common on approach gully; test holds before committing weight
Watch for abandoned gear and old slings at anchor stations; verify anchor integrity
Early-season conditions: confirm road and trail status before committing; assume significant snow
Glacier crossing may require crevasse rescue capability; parties should be equipped and trained
objHaz: Avalanche hazard (early season on approach gully)
proTips: Simulclimb Class 4 sections where possible to save time; the ridge allows movement together on easier terrain
Scout anchor stations during ascent to optimize rappel efficiency on descent
Bring extra slings for anchor extension and rappel setup; existing anchors are sometimes complex
Start very early (5-6 AM) to maximize daylight for technical descent
The crux 5.7 offwidth can be climbed via a slab traverse alternative on the left, though it is less protected; the crack is the better option
Comfortable climbing shoes are ideal for the crux pitch; boots can work but loose feet make the vertical step harder
Establish bivy camp on the ridge (great notch below summit or on middle ridge section) if summit day arrives late to avoid rappelling in darkness
Use two 60m ropes for efficient rappelling; one rope may require linking rappels or rope skimming in places
The knife-edge ridge section is the crux of exposure; belay this section for safety and confidence
Check weather forecast and NWAC avalanche forecast before committing; conditions can change rapidly in maritime snowpack
routes.comms: No cell service in the North Cascades backcountry. Verizon has spotty coverage in developed areas near Newhalem but is unreliable or absent above treeline. Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach, PLB) strongly recommended for emergency communication from remote ridges.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park
access.fees: $6 reservation fee + $10 per person per night (youth 15 and under free)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required year-round for overnight stays; reserve online via Recreation.gov or obtain in-person at ranger station
access.passRequired: No day-use fee; permit required for overnight
access.closures: Access road and trail typically open June-October; inquire with NPS for current status, especially early/late season

road.name: Thornton Creek Road (FR 3745)
road.status: Open late spring through fall, exact dates weather-dependent
road.seasonalGate: Road may be gated or closed in winter; confirm status before driving
road.driveNote: From Highway 20 near Newhalem/Marblemount, turn north at Thornton Creek Road sign (watch for turnoff on north side of highway 11 miles east of Marblemount, 2 miles west of Newhalem). Dirt road climbs approximately 5 miles to Thornton Lakes Trailhead. Passenger vehicles can typically access this road in summer.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with significant Pacific moisture. Temperatures range 40-60F in summer at elevation. Morning temperatures near freezing; afternoon winds common. Rapid weather deterioration possible.
climate.spring: Significant snow in approach gully; avalanche hazard on maritime snowpack; rock may be plastered with rime ice; approach technically difficult
climate.summer: Best conditions; dry rock, stable weather windows, lower avalanche hazard. Mid-July through August most reliable. Small glaciers present but typically traversable.
climate.fall: Improving conditions in early September, but first storms arrive later in season; conditions deteriorate by late September
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall, wet dense snow, extreme avalanche hazard; route essentially impossible due to rime ice and snow plastering the rock; not recommended

timing.recommendedStart: 5:00-6:00 AM from col campsite to maximize daylight for technical descent

waypoints.name: Col Campsite (Northwest of Middle Thornton Lake)

pitchDetail.notes: Introductory pitches with blocky features and good jugs; easy but exposed climbing on ridge crest; first bivy site accessible after P2
Gullies and steep rises following ridge; gym-style climbing on Skagit gneiss with small holds; mostly moveable together

First gendarme tower; traverse around first gendarme on right; second gendarme climbed directly with enjoyable fifth class climbing; excellent exposure; knife-edge sections with terrific exposure on both sides

Memorable 5.7 offwidth crack in right-facing corner leading to a small crux tower. The upper pitch features a vertical step with minimal feet placements requiring upper body strength. Slab traverse alternative on left is less protected. Crux is actually quite fun when climbing well; approximately 30 meters with solid rock.

Easier climbing and scrambling above crux; follow ridge crest with continued exposure but lower angled terrain; small towers and knife-edge sections; final 10ft of Class 4 to summit; approximately 200 feet of terrain

gear: Helmet (highly recommended; loose rock on descent)
Bivy sack or lightweight bivy gear
routes.detailedRack: A moderate alpine rack is appropriate: 4-6 small cams (Metolius or Black Diamond #0.75-1 sizes), 4-6 medium cams (#2-3), micro stoppers (0.5-0.75), and a full set of standard stoppers. Carry 12+ slings (8 or more runners, 4 runners of 16-inch or 24-inch slings for anchors). One 60m single rope allows linking rappels, but two 60m ropes are strongly preferred for efficiency and safety. If bringing one rope, be prepared for some 60ft rappels and potential rope skimming.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is required on the crux 5.7 offwidth pitch (place cams and nuts in the crack for safe lead climbing). Lower-angle sections are mostly Class 4 and do not always require protection if simulclimbing, but placing anchors for belayed climbing increases safety. Anchor stations exist throughout the route (mostly slings); verify anchor integrity and supplement as needed.
whatToBring: 10 essentials (map, compass, headlamp, knife, lighter, first aid, repair kit, sun protection, nutrition, hydration)
2+ liters water capacity (unreliable water sources on ridge; refill at col campsite)
Insulating layers (fleece or down jacket for bivy)
Gloves and hat (early morning on ridge is cold)
Repair kit with tape, cord, spare sling
Bivy sack or lightweight emergency shelter
Optional: climbing guidebook, smartphone with offline maps
Optional: GPS unit (InReach or similar for emergency communication)
routes.corrections: The given data lists alpineGrade as II-III; most authoritative sources (Timberline Mountain Guides, havetent.com, SpokalPine) consistently rate this route as Grade III alpine, not II-III. The rock grade of 5.7 is confirmed. The peak elevation of 7,277 ft matches data from USGS sources and multiple climbing references.

hierarchyNote: Peak hierarchy verified: Mount Triumph (7,277 ft) is correctly located in the North Cascades range of Washington State, Whatcom County, approximately 5.5 miles west-northwest of Newhalem. Coordinates 48.7065°N, -121.3550°W are consistent across multiple authoritative sources (USGS, latitude.to, peakbagger-derived listings). Elevation matches USGS topographic data. No hierarchy conflicts identified. Peak characteristics (orthogneiss composition, three ridges, glaciers, position above Pickets) are consistent across guidebooks and trip reports.

HIERARCHY CONFLICT: "The Obelisk" cannot be verified as a real peak in Washington state after exhaustive research across primary sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, PeakBagger, NPS, CalTopo). All documented "Obelisk" peaks found are either: (1) The Obelisk in California's Sierra Nevada (Kings Canyon NP), (2) Bouldering areas in Washington's lower Cascades, or (3) Forbidden Peak (North Cascades), sometimes described as obelisk-like. No peak formally named "The Obelisk" exists in Washington per major climbing databases. The route "Lone Wolf" and first ascent by Wylie Moreshead (2020) could not be verified in any source. The "wa_" ID prefix conflicts with research findings. Recommend verifying coordinates and elevation or confirming whether this peak should be reassigned to California.

routes.corrections: Route data could not be verified from reputable sources. No climbing route 'Lone Wolf' on an alpine peak in Washington state could be found in Mountain Project, SummitPost, PeakBagger, or other authoritative databases. First ascent information provided in seed data could not be independently verified.

hierarchyNote: VERIFICATION FAILURE: Peak "Mossy Loaf" could not be verified through any authoritative primary sources (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, Peakery, or other major climbing databases). No routes named "Moss Out For Harambe" or "You Moss Be Joking" appear in any searchable climbing database. While similar-named peaks exist (Mossy Slab 5,480 ft, Mossy Ridge, Mossy Hill), this specific peak name does not appear on any major climbing resource. Extensive searches for first-ascent parties Josh Glantz and Amber Michaelis with September 5, 2020 date also returned no results. This peak data may be a new addition not yet indexed, or may require verification. Recommend confirming peak existence and location with local climbing community before considering this fully sourced.

routes.corrections: Cannot verify this route exists through any authoritative climbing database source. Route name not found on Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, or other major platforms.
Cannot verify this route exists through any authoritative climbing database source. Route name not found on Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger, or other major platforms.

hierarchyNote: Elevation discrepancy: database lists 8348 ft; authoritative sources (Wikipedia, USGS) cite 8343-8343.4 ft. Both refer to same peak (coordinates 48.9822, -120.8607); difference is within survey tolerance. Hierarchy placement (Hozameen Range, Pasayten Wilderness, North Cascades) confirmed correct.
blurb: Castle Peak (8,343 ft) is a remote granite summit in the Hozameen Range of the North Cascades, located one mile south of the Canada–US border in Washington's Pasayten Wilderness. The peak's iconic north face is visible from Manning Park in BC; first surveyed by USGS in 1904. Its solid granite slopes and ridges offer both accessible non-technical scrambling and technical rock climbing up to Grade IV 5.10+.

routes.overview: Non-technical route combining Class 2 and easy Class 3 scrambling with solid granite rock. The majority of terrain is straightforward with a few interesting Class 3+ steps and excellent rock quality.
routes.beta: Castle Peak's standard ascent is a granite scramble featuring mostly Class 2 terrain with sections of easy Class 3. Rock quality is excellent and reminiscent of the Stuart Range. Route-finding is required in micro-terrain and sections are steep with krummholz. The peak can be approached from three main directions, all requiring significant approach commitment.
routes.approach: Freezeout Creek approach (most commonly used): Access via Lightning Creek trailhead at 1,500 ft. Hike Lightning Creek Trail, then follow abandoned Freezeout Creek Trail to Freezeout Cabin at 3,600 ft (11.3 miles). Continue ascending Freezeout Creek drainage to glacier/base of peak. Alternatively, Manning Park approach: Start at Monument 78 trailhead in Manning Provincial Park, BC. Follow rolling Monument 78 trail for 7 miles along Castle Creek (gaining 600 ft) to US border. Continue cross-country through brushy terrain another 5 miles to south face. Third option (expensive): Ross Lake water taxi to Hozomeen Campground, then Freezeout Creek approach.
routes.descent: The walk-off is via the south side and east ridge. Descent is straightforward but care needed on loose rock and through krummholz in alpine meadows.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the south side via moderate terrain. Follow the east ridge back toward camp through alpine meadows. Watch for loose, vegetation-covered rock. Route-finding can be non-obvious in places. Retrace approach to base.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 3-4 day trip for Freezeout Creek approach: Day 1 - Drive to Lightning Creek trailhead, hike to Willow Lake (5 miles, 1,000 ft gain). Day 2 - Hike to Freezeout Cabin area (6.3 miles, 1,800 ft gain). Day 3 - Hike to glacier/base camp, ascend to summit and return to camp (full day scramble, ~6-8 hours climbing). Day 4 - Descend to established camp or trailhead. Manning Park approach adds complexity due to international border crossing and brush travel.
routes.bail: Steep bluffs with krummholz on upper terrain—be prepared to downclimb if route-finding becomes compromised. Early descent from alpine meadows is feasible to lower elevations. In snow season, avalanche hazard on certain aspects.
routes.turnaround: Establish a firm turnaround time: if conditions are poor or route-finding becomes difficult, descend immediately. Snow melting can accelerate rockfall risk. Reversing any difficult Class 3+ step is safer than pushing higher in deteriorating conditions.
routes.bestSeason: June through August. June is ideal with snow coverage on certain sections—snow can facilitate easier travel on slabs but increases avalanche risk on certain aspects. Late June/July offers best alpine conditions with minimal snow. August is typically snow-free but has greater rockfall danger from warming and potential thunderstorms.
routes.fa: 1904 USGS Survey party (first ascent of peak)
knownHazards: Significant rockfall from upper slopes, especially after sunrise or during warm afternoons
Steep bluffs with krummholz restricting escape routes
Vegetation-covered rock requires careful foot placement
Remote location requires 3-4 day commitment; rescue difficult
Route-finding non-obvious in several micro-terrain sections
Winter/spring avalanche danger on northeast aspects near glaciers
watchOut: Loose rock hazard increases throughout the day—aim for early alpine travel
Stay alert to weather changes; thunderstorms can develop quickly in summer
Snow-covered boulders on traverses can hide hazards
Teetering blocks near summit require care; don't dislodge unstable rock
proTips: June ascents with moderate snow cover can provide better slab friction than bare rock
Solid granite throughout—rock quality is surprisingly good for central North Cascades
Glacier at foot of north face is mellow enough for straightforward crossing
Alpine meadows at north face base are sublime camping areas
Carry extra food/water for multi-day approach; resupply not available past trailheads
gear: 30-meter rope (if any Class 4 encountered or for emergency rappel)
Small traditional rack (if class 4 climbing needed; often left at base)
Approach shoes suitable for long trail hiking
routes.detailedRack: Small rack (cams/nuts) optional for Class 3+ crux sections. Most ascents done without protection on the standard route. Natural ledges and rock features can serve as emergency belay points if needed, but protection is rarely deployed on ascent.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is minimal on standard route (Class 2-3 scramble). Natural ledges and rock features can serve as emergency belay points if needed, but protection is rarely deployed on ascent. Rescue/emergency rappel capability via 30-meter rope recommended.
whatToBring: Tent and sleep system rated to 30-40°F
Water treatment or bottles (resupply limited to established camps)
First aid kit (emphasis on blister treatment, pain relief)
Trekking poles (assist on approach and descent)
Sat communicator (Garmin inReach or similar—highly recommended for this remote peak)

timing.recommendedStart: 6:00 AM (alpine start) for summit day

climate.typical: Highly variable; alpine location near border experiences frequent weather systems. Expect cool temps, high winds.
climate.spring: Snow present at high elevations through June; avalanche hazard. Freeze-thaw cycles daily.
climate.summer: Warmest season, 40-60°F at 8000 ft. Afternoon thunderstorms common July-August. Clear mornings typical.
climate.fall: September snow possible; rapid onset of poor conditions. Not recommended after Labor Day.
climate.winter: Deep snow, extreme cold, avalanche danger. Only for experienced winter alpinists.
climate.forecastZone: North Central Washington/Cascade-East zone (NWAC); consult NWAC avalanche forecasts and mountain-forecast.com for Castle Peak's microclimate.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (US side); Manning Provincial Park (BC side for approach)
access.fees: None for wilderness use (self-issue permit at trailhead), but Northwest Forest Pass recommended if parking at Freezeout Creek trailhead area.
access.permit: Self-issue wilderness permit (no fee) at trailhead. International border crossing required for Manning Park approach—have valid passport.
access.passRequired: Valid passport for Manning Park approach (crosses US-Canada border). Not required for Freezeout Creek (all-US) approach.
access.closures: Pasayten Wilderness has no closures, but weather and snow can make approaches impassable before June or after September. Castle Pass trail (alternate route) not maintained since 2006.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County: 911 (What-Comm dispatch, Bellingham); non-emergency 360-676-6911. Okanogan County: 911. International border crossing may delay response; sat communicator essential.
emergency.rangerStation: Tonasket Ranger District (Okanogan side) or Forest Service offices at Marblemount area (Whatcom side). Pasayten Wilderness is remote; ranger patrol is infrequent.
emergency.nearestHospital: Bellingham (Whatcom County side): PeakView Hospital, Bellingham. Okanogan (Okanogan County side): Cascade Medical Center. Both 60-80+ miles from trailhead. Rescue via helicopter from summit area is possible but expensive; carry travel insurance/SOS annual plan.
emergency.notes: This is one of the most remote peaks in the Cascades with 3-4 day approach. Satellite communicator (Garmin inReach, Spot) strongly recommended. Cell service is nonexistent on the peak and sparse at trailheads. Hozomeen Ranger Station on BC side can be contacted via Manning Park visitor center.

routes.comms: No cell service on peak or most of approach. Sat communicator (Garmin inReach, Spot) essential. Limited service at trailheads (Hozomeen, Manning Park). Emergency broadcasts via NWAC.
routes.corrections: Elevation: Sources consistently report 8,343 ft (USGS) or 8,343.4 ft (precise surveys). Database value of 8,348 ft is within ±5 ft (0.06% variance), typical of survey variations and acceptable. No correction needed but note discrepancy.

pitchDetail.notes: Initial alpine terrain from glacier/camp area; mostly straightforward scrambling on solid granite. Good rock quality, solid.
Transitional section with some slab scrambling. Rock is clean granite slabs interspersed with blocky sections. Route-finding begins.
Steeper section with krummholz and bluffs. Requires careful foot placement on loose, vegetation-covered rock. Crux of standard route.
East ridge section with moderate slopes. Rock quality remains good. Teetering blocks near summit require caution.
Final push to summit across moderate slopes. Exposed windy section with excellent views of Pasayten peaks and Canadian ranges.

blurb: The Ptarmigan Traverse is a legendary multi-peak alpine route in Washington's North Cascades, crossing a dramatic 30+ mile ridge system at the convergence of the Cascade Range crest. This remote, unmarked high route traverses extensive glaciers, exposed scrambling, and pristine alpine terrain between numerous 8,000+ foot summits, requiring advanced mountaineering skills and representing one of the Pacific Northwest's most challenging alpine traverses.
hierarchyNote: Critical issue: "Alpine and Technical Traverses" is not a single peak but a multi-peak alpine traverse system. The route crosses at least 13 summits from Cascade Pass (3,660 ft) to Downey Creek (1,480 ft), including Buckner Mountain, Sahale Peak, Mount Formidable, Spider Mountain, Le Conte Mountain, Sentinel Peak, Old Guard Peak, The Lizard, German Helmet, Spire Point, Gunsight Peak, Dome Peak (8,924 ft), and Sinister Peak (8,440 ft). The peak record should likely reference a specific high point (Dome Peak) rather than attempting to represent an entire traverse system as a single peak.

routes.fa: Bill Cox, Calder Bressler, Ray W. Clough, and Tom Myers in July 1938
routes.bestSeason: July and August offer the most stable weather and optimal snow conditions for efficient glacier travel, though temperatures can range from warm in day to near freezing at night at altitude. Early July can provide firm morning snow but late season (mid-August onward) exposes more bare glacier ice and increases rockfall hazard. Winter ski traverses occur April through June requiring advanced backcountry skiing skills.
routes.aspect: Variable (N, NE, E, SE, W on different segments)
routes.face: Alpine ridge traverse along the Cascade crest; no single face designation applies to this multi-peak route
routes.overview: The Ptarmigan Traverse is a legendary 30+ mile, multi-day alpine ridge traverse in Washington's North Cascades that weaves between a dozen glaciated peaks, connecting Cascade Pass in the north to the Suiattle River drainage in the south. This remote, unmarked high route requires advanced glacier travel skills, self-reliance, and mountaineering experience to navigate exposed ridges, extensive crevasse fields, and complex route-finding across pristine alpine terrain.
routes.beta: This is a serious alpine mountaineering objective requiring off-trail wilderness navigation, extensive glacier travel, crevasse rescue skills, and the ability to self-rescue. The traverse crosses multiple glacier systems (Cache, Middle Cascade, Le Conte, Dana, South Cascade, Chikamin, and Garden glaciers) with significant crevasse hazards and variable snow conditions. Parties are limited to 6 people maximum. Most ascents take 4-8 days, with 6-7 days typical for guided trips. Route-finding is essential; a faint use trail exists but GPS and navigation skills are required. Snow conditions vary dramatically year to year, and late-season options may require Class 4 scrambles to bypass exposed ice.
routes.approach: Northern start: Drive State Route 20 to Marblemount, turn onto Cascade River Road and follow it 22 miles to the dead-end parking area at Cascade Pass Trailhead (elevation 3,660 ft, coordinates 48.4683, -121.0600). Hike the established 3.7-mile trail to Cascade Pass (5,351 ft), then continue off-trail south-southeast toward Mixup Peak and Cache Col, descending into the Kool-Aid Lake basin for the first bivouac. Navigation via map/GPS becomes critical beyond the pass.
routes.descent: The traverse terminates at Downey Creek drainage in the south (elevation ~1,480 ft, coordinates 48.25875, -121.22311). From Itswoot Ridge or Cub Lake area, descend via Bachelor Creek canyon following use trail through burned forest (2020 wildfire recovery) with log-hopping sections to reach Downey Creek Trailhead. An established trail (Trail 768) provides exit through maintained forest. Note: the Suiattle River Road approach (accessed via Darrington-Suiattle Road) was severely damaged by winter storms of 2025/2026; verify access conditions before planning a full north-to-south traverse.
routes.descentText: From White Rock Lakes or Itswoot Ridge area, descend steeply through gullies and snowfields over Dana Glacier toward Bachelor Creek. Follow Bachelor Creek north down the canyon, navigating log-crossing sections through recovering burned forest. The maintained Downey Creek Trail eventually becomes a clear 4-5 mile path dropping 1,600+ feet through timber to the trailhead. Total descent time: 8-12 hours depending on route and conditions. Alternative: consider an out-and-back option from Cascade Pass heading south to White Rock Lakes and returning, due to 2025/2026 winter damage to southern road access.
routes.itinerary: Day 1 (Cascade Pass to Kool-Aid Lake): 4-6 hours, 1,700 ft gain. Hike Cascade Pass Trail to the pass (5.7 miles), then off-trail south to Cache Col crossing on Cache Glacier, descend over red ledges and across Middle Cascade Glacier to Kool-Aid Lake bivouac (elevation ~7,000 ft). Day 2 (Kool-Aid to Yang-Yang Lakes): 8-11 hours, 2,500 ft gain/loss. Traverse Spider-Formidable Col via Middle Cascade Glacier, cross exposed red ledges (Class 3 scrambling), descend to Yang-Yang Lakes bivouac. Day 3 (Yang-Yang to White Rock Lakes): 7-11 hours, 2,000 ft gain/loss. Traverse Le Conte Ridge, cross Le Conte Glacier with crevasse hazards, descend to White Rock Lakes bivouac (visual highlight of entire route). Day 4 (White Rock rest day or peak day): Optional day to summit Dome Peak (8,924 ft) via Dome Glacier route (moderate ice climbing, 35-degree terrain) or rest for acclimatization. Day 5 (White Rock to Itswoot Ridge): 8-10 hours, 3,000 ft gain. Ascend Dana Glacier crossing bergschrund, summit Spire Point (8,264 ft) or traverse below on east face, descend to Itswoot Ridge bivouac. Day 6 (Itswoot to Downey Creek TH): 8-12 hours, 2,500 ft loss. Descend Bachelor Creek through forest, reach Downey Creek Trailhead. Alternative faster options: out-and-back from Cascade Pass (4-5 days), or extend to Dome Peak summit and add Sentinel Peak/Old Guard (7-8 days).
routes.bail: Downclimb option exists to Cache Col area allowing retreat to Cascade Pass via Mixup Peak on Day 1. Early in route (Cache Col through Red Ledges), if weather worsens or party is struggling, cache gear and return to Cascade Pass via established Cascade Pass Trail. At Yang-Yang Lakes, a reverse traverse option allows exit back north to Cascade Pass; this effectively turns the traverse into an out-and-back via the same route. At White Rock Lakes (Day 3), established use trail descends south via Downey Creek to the trailhead in 1-2 days if weather requires retreat—this is a good psychological bailout point. After Itswoot Ridge, few options remain other than completing the descent to Downey Creek.
routes.turnaround: Climbers should establish realistic turnaround times for each day based on party fitness and weather windows. An 8 AM departure each morning ensures sufficient daylight to reach the next bivouac (typically 6-10 hour days in alpine terrain). If daylight is fading and you haven't reached the next established camp by 3-4 PM, begin constructing an emergency bivouac rather than navigating in darkness. Weather windows change rapidly; if fog/whiteout conditions develop that prevent safe route-finding on glaciers, establish camp and wait for clearing rather than pushing through poor visibility (high avalanche/crevasse risk). Water is generally available except at high cols; ensure adequate hydration to maintain decision-making ability. If a party member requires evacuation (injury, altitude sickness), contact via satellite messenger and plan for possible helicopter extraction from a high glacier or meadow area; ranger station contacts available at Marblemount NPS Complex.
objHaz: Crevasses on Cache, Middle Cascade, Le Conte, Dana, South Cascade, Chikamin, and Garden glaciers
Bergschrunds at glacier heads; early season potential moat formations
Avalanche terrain on steep snowfields and couloirs, particularly Spider-Formidable area and Dana Glacier approach
Exposed scrambling on red ledges (Class 3) with fall hazard and rockfall from above
Route-finding hazard in fog or whiteout conditions on unmarked alpine terrain
Rockfall danger on steep scree slopes and talus fields, especially in late season when rock is loose
Weather exposure at altitude with rapid thunderstorm development possible in summer
Loose or unstable snow steps in early season when direct sun causes surface degradation
Cornice collapses at high passes when ice conditions weaken
Dehydration and heat stress at altitude on clear days with strong UV exposure
knownHazards: Crevasse fields on Cache Glacier and Middle Cascade Glacier are significant; early season requires careful assessment and belayed travel; late season exposes bare glacier ice and increased visual hazard
Bergschrunds at glacier heads (particularly Le Conte and Dana glaciers) require careful assessment or bypass via alternate rock routes
Avalanche danger is highest in winter/early spring and after heavy snowfall; mid-summer offers reduced but non-zero risk on south-facing slopes
Red Ledges section features narrow exposed Class 3 scrambling below Arts Knoll with 1,000+ foot exposure; loose rock and lichen hazard when wet
Route-finding complexity increases dramatically in late season when snow cover diminishes; GPS essential for backup navigation
Moats forming under cornice overhangs at high passes in mid-season; crossing requires careful assessment and may necessitate climbing cornices or detours
Loose scree and talus on slopes above ravines; rocks dislodged by one party member endanger climbers below
Afternoon thunderstorms common in July-August; electrical storm hazard requires early morning starts and descent planning
watchOut: Crevasse rescue skills are non-negotiable; travel roped up on all glacier sections, especially Cache Glacier, Middle Cascade Glacier, and Le Conte Glacier—do not skip rope travel 'to save time'
Red Ledges in winter/early spring conditions with snow on lichen-covered rocks are extremely slippery; Class 4 scrambles may become necessary to bypass
Parties exceeding 6 people violate North Cascades National Park regulations and increase hazard via increased rockfall hazard and route-finding delays
Never attempt this route without glacier travel and crevasse rescue experience—at minimum, practice self-arrest with ice axe and perform crevasse rescue drills before the trip
Water is scarce at high cols; dehydration impairs judgment and increases avalanche/rockfall awareness failures; pack adequate capacity and filter water immediately upon finding sources
Route-finding failures and route-confusion delays accumulate in fog; carry detailed maps and GPS device, not just guidebook descriptions
Solo climbing is possible (FKT 16:48) but extremely high-risk; weather, injury, or navigation mistakes become critical without a partner for support
Cell/satellite communication coverage is unreliable; assume you are on your own for rescue—carry satellite communicator (Garmin InReach or PLB) if possible
Fastest known time (16:48 solo, 24 hours guided fast-pack) is not representative of safe climbing; plan for 5-7 days minimum and do not attempt to match elite times
gear: Crevasse rescue kit (pulleys, carabiners, webbing, rescue cordage)
Emergency bivy sack or emergency tarp
First aid kit with blister treatment
Tent suitable for exposed alpine bivouacs (3-4 season)
Stove and fuel (liquid fuel or canister)
routes.detailedRack: For rock scrambles (Red Ledges, peak options): a light traditional rack including nuts (#1-10, nuts in #1-4 size range for thin cracks), stoppers, and hexes; approximately 3-5 cams in .1-1.5 inch range for deltas and off-width features. Total rock rack weight under 3 lbs. Slings (6-8 total mixed lengths) and quickdraws (6-8) for anchors and rappel backup. For glacier travel: per 2-3 climber rope team, include a minimum 30m or 60m glacier rope (single rope team), 2-4 ice screws (per team), 1-2 snow pickets (per team), 2-3 rock pitons for anchor backup. Crevasse rescue components: 1-2 pulleys per team, extra carabiners (locking), webbing slings (for mechanical advantage hauling), and 50+ feet of rescue/utility cordage.
routes.proNeeds: Protection needs are primarily for glacier anchors and crevasse rescue, not rock climbing protection. Snow pickets and ice screws provide primary belay anchors on glaciers; these should be backed up with rock anchors (pitons or deadmen) when available. The Red Ledges section is the only significant rock scramble and typically does not require protection for experienced parties (Class 3). However, nervous climbers or wet conditions may warrant light rock protection (small cams, slings) at a few narrow exposure points. Most parties self-belay via rope connection and rely on collective party awareness rather than technical protection installations.
proTips: Start before dawn to maximize daylight hours and catch firm morning snow for efficient glacier travel; afternoon sun degrades snow and increases crevasse hazard
Use a GPS device as backup navigation; the route is generally defined by terrain (glacier passes, ridgelines) but faint or absent trail markings make GPS essential in fog

Late July often provides better conditions than early July; early July may feature wet snow from recent melt and unstable avalanche conditions; mid-late July typically offers firm snow and clear skies
Cache heavy food/gear at established bivy sites if doing an out-and-back option from Cascade Pass, significantly reducing load and increasing speed on return
Establish camp by 4 PM even if you have daylight remaining; alpine weather changes rapidly and setting up in gathering darkness is risky and demoralizing
The red ledges section (below Arts Knoll) is one of the cruxes; scout the line in advance if visibility is good; loose rock and lichen make this section hazardous in wet conditions
Drink water continuously (eat/drink every 1-2 hours) at altitude to maintain altitude acclimatization and decision-making ability; dehydration significantly increases mistakes
If you summit optional peaks (Dome, Sentinel, Spire), plan for this taking 4-6 extra hours per peak; do not commit to peak summits unless time/weather window is clear
Downclimbing (retreating) is always safer than pushing forward into deteriorating weather; the traverse does not have a 'point of no return' until well south of White Rock Lakes

Satellite communicator (Garmin InReach or personal locator beacon) is highly recommended given remote terrain and unreliable cell coverage; contact Marblemount ranger station if you plan to file a float plan

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (5-7 AM) from Cascade Pass to maximize daylight for glacier travel and route-finding

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open to passenger vehicles. Note: 2025/2026 winter storms significantly damaged Suiattle River Road (southern access); the Downey Creek Trailhead approach road was washed out. Verify current road conditions with Marblemount ranger station (360-854-7245) before planning a traverse.
road.seasonalGate: Cascade River Road is typically open year-round to Cascade Pass Trailhead, though winter snow/ice may require high-clearance vehicles. Suiattle River Road (southern access) often closes seasonally due to snow; current status uncertain due to 2025/2026 damage.
road.driveNote: From Marblemount (on State Route 20): turn onto Cascade River Road immediately upon seeing the sharp right-angle bend in SR 20. Follow Cascade River Road 22 miles to the dead-end parking lot at Cascade Pass Trailhead (elevation 3,660 ft). Road is narrow but paved for most of the distance. Parking lot fills on popular weekends; arrive early (before 7 AM) or plan a weekday visit.

climate.forecastZone: Northwest Avalanche Center (NWAC) Cascades zone; weather forecasts available via mountain-forecast.com or NOAA forecast for North Cascades region
climate.typical: Highly variable, rapidly changing weather is the norm. Summer (July-August) features warm daytime temperatures (50-70°F at lower elevations, 30-50°F at altitude), cold nights (freezing to 20°F), and strong UV exposure. Afternoon thunderstorms are common; mornings are typically clearer. Humidity is high, creating fog and mist frequent in upper elevations.
climate.spring: May-June: Winter-like conditions persist; significant snow coverage aids glacier travel but avalanche hazard is elevated and icefalls are common. Extreme variability with potential for warm sunny days followed by storms. Bergschrunds are wide and difficult to cross.
climate.summer: July-August: Warmest season with daytime temperatures 50-70°F at lower elevations; above 8,000 ft expect 30-50°F days and 20-30°F nights. Morning sun creates firm snow (especially valuable for glacier travel); afternoon heating increases rockfall and avalanche hazard. Afternoon thunderstorms common; rain possible even mid-summer.
climate.fall: September-early October: Shorter daylight and increasing freeze-thaw cycles create unstable snow/ice conditions. First snow possible. Days are cooler (40-60°F); nights regularly below freezing. Weather windows are shorter and less predictable.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snow adds distance and complexity; avalanche hazard is significant. Requires backcountry skiing skills. Temperature inversions create extreme cold at altitude (well below 0°F at night). Winter traverse option via ski requires specialized skills and is rarely attempted.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park (northern half from Cascade Pass to approximately White Rock Lakes); Glacier Peak Wilderness and Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (southern half)
access.fees: Cascade Pass Trailhead: day use parking free. Backcountry overnight camping: Backcountry permit required; as of 2026, permits cost $10 per person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee (verify current pricing with Marblemount ranger station)
access.permit: Backcountry permit required for overnight stays. Obtain via North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (360-854-7245). Reservations recommended 2+ weeks in advance during peak season (July-August); about 60% of sites available for advance reservation. Groups limited to maximum 6 people. Register in person or by phone.
access.passRequired: No pass required for day-use access to Cascade Pass Trailhead. Annual America the Beautiful Pass ($80) or North Cascades Pass ($35) accepted if charged for parking (currently free).
access.closures: Cascade Pass area typically accessible July-October depending on snow conditions. Winter closures (November-June) possible due to avalanche hazard and snow accumulation. Verify current trail status via nps.gov/noca or call Marblemount ranger station. 2025/2026 winter storms damaged Suiattle River Road access; Downey Creek Trailhead may be inaccessible; consider out-and-back options from Cascade Pass.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Skagit County Sheriff (non-emergency): 360-873-4590; for backcountry rescue coordination contact via satellite communicator (Garmin InReach) or cell if coverage available
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center: 7283 Ranger Station Road, Marblemount, WA 98267; Phone: 360-854-7245 (general info); Emergency line: 360-854-7249 or 911
emergency.nearestHospital: United General Hospital, 2000 Hospital Drive, Sedro-Woolley, WA 98284; phone 360-856-6021; approximately 30-40 miles south of Marblemount via SR 20
emergency.notes: Cell phone coverage is unreliable or absent throughout most of the traverse; assume emergency communication is not available. Carry a satellite communicator (Garmin InReach or personal locator beacon) if rescue might be required. Helicopter extraction from high alpine terrain is possible but weather-dependent and expensive (have evacuation insurance). Ranger station contacts should be provided to a trusted emergency contact before departure. Average helicopter rescue costs $5,000-15,000; evacuation insurance recommended.

routes.comms: Cell phone coverage is extremely unreliable; expect no coverage in the Cascade Pass area and throughout most of the alpine traverse. Verizon and T-Mobile occasionally show marginal coverage near Cascade Pass trailhead (1-2 bars) but this is not dependable. Satellite communication is strongly recommended: Garmin InReach devices provide messaging capability and location sharing via satellite (requires subscription); personal locator beacons (PLBs) allow emergency activation only. Consider filing a float plan with a trusted contact providing expected return time and action plan if you do not check in within 12 hours of expected return.

pitchDetail.notes: Established trail from trailhead to Cascade Pass (3.7 miles, 1,691 ft gain); well-maintained; typical hiking. Beyond Cascade Pass, route-finding required.

Navigate Cache Glacier and cross Cache Col; glacier travel with rope skills required. Potential cornices and moat hazard. Historic steep snow step has degraded; current conditions typically easier but variable.

Narrow, exposed scrambling below Arts Knoll on lichen-covered rock ledges; significant fall exposure (1,000+ feet). Lichen and loose rock hazard, especially when wet. Original 1938 party onsighted this section. Can be bypassed via Class 4 terrain in poor conditions.

pitchDetail.pitch: Spider-Formidable Col via Middle Cascade Glacier
pitchDetail.notes: Traverse Middle Cascade Glacier crossing crevasse fields. Significant glacier hazard; rope travel required. Bergschrund at glacier head may require belayed crossing or alternate route.

pitchDetail.pitch: Le Conte Ridge traverse to White Rock Lakes
pitchDetail.notes: Cross Le Conte Glacier and descend complex terrain via gullies and snowfields. Le Conte Glacier is noted as potentially the most crevassed section; exercise caution and rope up. Descent steep (Class 3 scrambling).

pitchDetail.pitch: Dana Glacier ascent to Dome Peak base
pitchDetail.notes: Ascend Dana Glacier with 35-degree slope; crevasse hazard and bergschrund crossing. Self-arrest skills essential. Spire Point option via east face Class 3 scramble.

Dome Glacier route features 35-degree glacier approach with moderate ice climbing; final summit block involves Class 3 rock on knife-edged ridge. Crampons/axe needed for glacier; rock climbing gear may be used for belay on rock pitches. Full ascent/descent ~4-6 hours from base camp.

Optional traverse over Spire Point (8,264 ft) involves Class 3-4 scrambling on rock with exposure. Direct climbing possible via short rock pitches (5.4-5.6 range) but typically descended. Complex route-finding in this area.

Steep descent through forest with significant elevation loss (2,500+ feet). Numerous log-crossing sections; route-finding required. 2020 fire impact has improved visibility but fallen trees may block path. Total descent 8-12 hours.

blurb: North Peak is the northernmost of three dramatic spires on Mount Index (5,357 ft) in the North Cascades of King County, Washington. Rising more than 4,250 feet above lower slopes, this steep rock and ice face was first ascended in July 1929 by Lionel Chute and Victor Kaartinen via the North Face route. The peak offers serious alpine rock, mixed, and ice climbing with committing multi-pitch routes best tackled in early season.

routes.overview: A long, moderately technical alpine climb following a prominent gully system on the north face. Classic 12-pitch route alternating rock scramble and climbing sections, with exceptional upper pitches on the true north rib.
routes.beta: Start from Lake Serene and ascend talus to the base of the face. Lower pitches are brushy but negotiable when dry; middle section involves jungle-tree climbing through dense vegetation; upper three pitches on the true north rib are exposed and excellent rock. Route performs better in winter when snow covers thick brush. Requires good judgment, solid weather, and a competent team. Rain triggers stonefall; no quick descent in bad weather.
routes.approach: From Lake Serene trailhead (4.1 miles, 2000 ft gain to lake at 2200 ft elevation), continue around west side of lake over talus and boulders to the talus field below the north face. Allow 2-3 hours from trailhead to base. Navigate rockslides and boulder fields to reach the start of the route.
routes.descent: Approximately 1000 feet of exposed soloing and 1000 feet of rappels using existing anchor systems. Bushes and small trees are generally reliable for anchors and often more useful than pitons or chocks. Difficult route-finding on descent; bring headlamp and consider bivy sack. Winter descent is particularly hazardous.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend via exposed terrain and established rappel anchors. Total of 4-5 major rappels to 30-50 feet. Loose rock throughout descent area. Bushes and trees are primary anchor points—these are more reliable than metal protection in this location. Allow 5 hours for descent to Lake Serene from summit. Route-finding can be challenging; mark your uphill route mentally or with cairns.
routes.bestSeason: Early season (May-June) recommended when snow covers the 600 vertical feet of thick brush on lower sections, making the approach and initial pitches more straightforward. Early season also provides stable snow in gullies.
gear: small to medium alpine rock rack
routes.detailedRack: Small to medium alpine rock rack with comprehensive coverage; 12-14 slings/runners of various lengths; two full-length ropes (60-70m) for descent; ice axes; crampons; helmet essential; pitons and hammer helpful for establishing anchors in brush.
proTips: Lower pitches are negotiable when dry; upper three pitches offer excellent exposed rock climbing.
Route is significantly better in winter when snow covers thick brush vegetation.
Carry pitons and hammer—bushes and trees are more reliable anchors than traditional protection.
Reasonable team completed in 5 hours up, 30 min summit, 5 hours down.
watchOut: No quick descent option if weather deteriorates; rain immediately triggers stonefall.
Difficult route-finding on descent—mental note-taking of uphill route is critical.
Loose and rotten rock throughout, especially lower sections.
Thick brush makes lower pitches unpleasant; much better in winter.
knownHazards: Persistent rockfall hazard, especially when wet
Loose and rotten rock on many pitches
Vegetation-choked lower section requires route-finding skill
Exposed upper pitches with limited escape options
Water and soft snow can make descent treacherous

timing.recommendedStart: Very early dawn (4-5am)

routes.rappels: 4-5 rappels to 30-50 feet via established anchors
routes.turnaround: If still climbing upper pitches after 2pm, or if weather deteriorates, consider bivy or retreat via rappels. Weather windows are narrow.

road.name: Stevens Pass Highway (US-2) to Mount Index Road
road.status: Open year-round except during winter storms
road.driveNote: From Gold Bar, follow US-2 east 7 miles to Index; turn on Mount Index Road. Lake Serene trailhead 35 miles east of Everett, 65 miles east of Seattle.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest / Weyerhauser timberlands
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required at Lake Serene trailhead
access.permit: Weyerhauser recreational permit may be required
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

climate.typical: Maritime west coast climate with abundant precipitation and wet heavy snow. Avalanche danger present.
climate.spring: May-June: melting snow, variable conditions, snow covers brush. Avalanche danger.
climate.summer: July-August: Driest season, less snow, rock better exposed but brush problematic. Summer rockfall risk high.
climate.fall: September-October: Early season storms increasing; nights below freezing. Generally climbable.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snow, wet conditions, high avalanche danger. Better for rock (snow covers brush) but serious overhead rockfall hazard.
climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Seattle - Puget Sound and Hood Canal zone

emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288, (360) 677-2414
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe, Monroe, WA
emergency.notes: Limited cell coverage. Satellite communicator strongly recommended. Rescue from Mount Index is challenging and may require helicopter.

routes.comms: Limited cellular signal. AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile, and Sprint have minimal coverage. Skykomish area ranks 399th in Washington for cell reception. Satellite communicator (InReach, Garmin, etc.) strongly recommended.

pitchDetail.notes: Brushy rock scramble, start at base of gully
Exposed corner pitch, traverse left to gully
Steep gully and rock bowl
Slanting ramp system, may have snow mid-season
Right-curving steep gully, 3-4 pitches, terrific rock
Final pitch to summit

routes.overview: The first recorded traverse of all three summits of Mount Index (North, Middle, and Main peaks), linking them via exposed scrambles and rock climbing along the skyline. Classic alpine route first done in 1950 by Schoening and Beckey, now completed by modern parties in one night.
routes.beta: Start at Lake Serene and climb the skyline from right to left. Ascend talus and grass to NE spur, scramble through brush to first slab. Climb exposed corner and traverse left to a gully. Scramble up rock bowl, cut right around skyline via hidden ledge. Ledge traverse becomes exposed slab. Round corner and climb two leads to slanting ramp trending right approximately 400 feet to large open basin. Ascend 3-4 leads up right-curving gully. From North Peak summit, traverse to Middle and Main peaks via scrambling.
routes.approach: From Lake Serene trailhead, hike 4.1 miles and 2000 feet of elevation gain to Lake Serene at 2200 feet. Continue around west side via talus and boulders to base of route. Allow 2-3 hours from trailhead to start climbing.
routes.descent: Rappel from Middle and Main peaks back to Lake Serene. Multiple short rappels along the traverse line via established anchors.
routes.descentText: Rappel from three peaks back down the route. Established anchors on traverse line use trees and bushes. Several 30-50 foot rappels; pack adequate sling material. Accomplish 2-3 hour descent from Main Peak to Lake Serene via rappels.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Hike to Lake Serene (2.5-3 hours). Begin climbing North Face section (6-7 hours to North Peak summit). Camp or bivy on North Peak. Day 2: Traverse to Middle Peak (1-2 hours), continue to Main Peak (1-2 hours). Descend via rappels to Lake Serene (2-3 hours). Exit to trailhead (1 hour).
routes.bestSeason: Mid to late season (July-September) for water availability and less snow. Early season (May-June) offers better route definition with snow but requires avalanche understanding.
routes.bail: From North Peak: rappel down North Face route. From Middle Peak: descend to North Peak then rappel. From Main Peak: rappel toward Middle Peak and traverse back. Plan for emergency bivy on one of the peaks.
routes.turnaround: Commit to bivy if unable to complete all three summits and return to Lake Serene by darkness. Weather deterioration is retreat trigger.
routes.detailedRack: Single alpine rock rack with medium focus; 10-12 slings and rappel cord; one to two ropes depending on party size; ice axes; crampons; helmet.
whatToBring: bivy sack and emergency sleep system
water bottles and iodine tabs or water filter
warm layers and emergency down jacket
first aid kit with blister treatment
proTips: Water and conditions are the main cruxes—scout water availability before committing.
Schoening and Beckey climbed in sneakers over two nights; modern teams complete in one night.
Climb the skyline consistently from right to left—shortcuts can lead to dead ends.
Rope team of two is preferable for better belay placement and safety.
watchOut: Serious commitment—no easy escape once past North Peak. Weather windows are narrow.
Loose and rotten rock throughout; solid rock climbing judgment essential.
Overhead rockfall hazard from above on multiple sections.
Water becomes critical crux in late season.
knownHazards: Persistent rockfall hazard, especially on exposed ridge sections
Very loose rock on all three peaks, particularly Middle and Main
Water scarcity in late season; snow/water in early season
Avalanche danger in early season with wet heavy snow
Severe route-finding challenges if visibility compromised
Mental fatigue from sustained climbing over 12+ hours

timing.recommendedStart: 5-6am to ensure adequate daylight

road.name: Stevens Pass Highway (US-2) to Mount Index Road
road.status: Open year-round except during winter storms
road.driveNote: From Gold Bar, follow US-2 east 7 miles to Index; turn on Mount Index Road. Lake Serene trailhead 35 miles east of Everett.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest / Weyerhauser timberlands
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass required at Lake Serene trailhead
access.permit: Weyerhauser recreational permit required for traverse route
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass

climate.typical: Maritime west coast with abundant precipitation. Snow tends to be wet and heavy, creating avalanche hazard.
climate.spring: May-June: Melting snow, variable conditions, avalanche danger with wet snow. Snow covers lower brush.
climate.summer: July-August: Driest season. Less snow, rock climbing more exposed, but summer rockfall hazard increases. Water scarcity potential.
climate.fall: September-October: Early season storms; generally climbable with shorter daylight.
climate.winter: November-April: Heavy snow, wet conditions, avalanche danger. Traverse more complex with snow routing.
climate.forecastZone: National Weather Service Seattle - Puget Sound and Hood Canal zone

emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288, (360) 677-2414
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe, Monroe, WA (approximately 35 miles west)
emergency.notes: Rescue from traverse route is extremely difficult. Helicopter rescue may be only option from exposed terrain. Satellite communicator highly recommended.

routes.comms: Limited cellular signal. AT&T, Verizon, T-Mobile, and Sprint have minimal coverage. Satellite communicator (InReach, Garmin, etc.) is essential given the remote nature.

pitchDetail.notes: Multiple leads up gullies and exposed terrain to North Peak
Exposed scrambling and climbing along ridge
Steep gully and rock pitches
Traverse and descent to Main Peak along skyline

blurb: Elephant Butte is a remote 7,380-foot orthogneiss peak in the Picket Range of Washington's North Cascades, notable for its dramatic vertical rise above local terrain despite moderate absolute elevation. Though more of a lump on a ridge than a dramatic rock spire, it's a classic alpine mountaineering destination accessed via Stetattle Ridge, visited by only a handful of climbers annually due to its remote location and challenging approach through North Cascades National Park.

routes.overview: Alpine mountaineering route following Sourdough Mountain trail to ridge crest, then traversing scenic Stetattle Ridge with Class 2-3 scrambling and one complex Class 5 crux to the remote summit. A multi-day backpacking objective with exceptional wildflowers and panoramic views of the Picket Range.
routes.bestSeason: July through October offers the most reliable conditions with snow clears from lower elevations by mid-July. June can have significant snow; early September is ideal for stable weather and minimal postholing. Avoid April-May due to avalanche hazard in Sourdough Creek basin.
routes.face: Stetattle Ridge approach; south and east sides of peak present bypass terrain due to Class 5 crux over summit.
routes.approach: Drive Washington State Route 20 to mile 126 near Diablo and Gorge Lake. Park near the Sourdough Mountain trailhead behind the pool building at Diablo (elevation 890 ft). Ascend the steep Sourdough Mountain trail approximately 4.2 miles and 4,000 feet elevation to Sourdough Camp (designated camping area). Continue on-trail another 0.7 miles to the lookout summit area, then veer left onto the ridge trail traversing alpine meadows and tarns along Stetattle Ridge crest for approximately 5-6 miles to reach Elephant Butte base. Total approach from trailhead is roughly 10 miles one-way with 7,000+ feet elevation gain.
routes.descent: Descend via the same route, retracing Stetattle Ridge back to Sourdough Mountain trail and down to Diablo. Most parties descend on Day 3 after summit push, descending approximately 6,000 feet to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Retrace the approach exactly. Descending Stetattle Ridge, stay on the established route as conditions can be sloppy with loose rock and mossy terrain. Avoid attempting direct descent down creek valleys (particularly Sourdough Creek), which present serious hazard of avalanches in early season and dangerous waterfalls. Once on the Sourdough trail proper, the descent is straightforward but steep and can be hard on knees. Some groups choose to skip the final descent of 3+ miles in a single day and camp at Pierce Mountain Camp or Sourdough Camp on the way down.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: From Diablo trailhead, ascend Sourdough Mountain trail to Sourdough Camp (4.2 miles, 4,000 ft gain, 4-5 hours). Alternatively, experienced parties with light packs may reach higher camps on Stetattle Ridge in a single very long day. Day 2: From camp, continue to Stetattle Ridge crest and traverse ridge to Elephant Butte. Navigate Class 2-3 terrain with snowfields and scree; avoid the Class 5 overhung direct route by traversing cliff bands to the left (north). Approach base of peak (5-6 miles from camp, 3,000-4,000 ft gain, 6-8 hours). Day 3: Summit push and descent, or establish high camp on ridge and summit on Day 3. Descent via same ridge route back to Sourdough Camp and then to trailhead on Day 4 for parties doing 4-day trip.
routes.beta: Class 2-3 ridge scrambling with significant exposure. Elephant Butte presents a major obstacle and an overhung fifth class move over the summit, which is impractical for parties with overnight packs. Most experienced parties bypass left (north) around cliff bands using brush, mossy terrain, and careful route-finding. Groups unfamiliar with terrain should allow extra time for routefinding on ridge. Early season requires snowshoe/ski to Sourdough Camp area. Tents required for camping; two small designated camps (max 4 people each) are Sourdough Camp (4.2 mi from Diablo) and Pierce Mountain Camp (1 mi east of Sourdough lookout). Stoves mandatory; campfires prohibited.
routes.turnaround: Because this is a remote peak with difficult terrain and logistical complexity, establish clear turnaround times based on weather, snow conditions, and party fitness. Typical turnaround is mid-day on route to allow descent in daylight. Do not approach Elephant Butte base later than early afternoon on summit day.
routes.bail: From Sourdough Camp or along Sourdough Mountain trail: descend immediately via the steep Sourdough Mountain trail to trailhead (4.2 miles, 4,000 ft loss). This is straightforward though arduous. From Stetattle Ridge proper: descend via established ridge trail back to Sourdough Camp if conditions degrade; do not attempt direct descents down side creeks. From Elephant Butte base: retreat via same ridge route back to established trail.
routes.proNeeds: Route-finding skills essential on Stetattle Ridge; experience with exposure and scrambling on wet, loose rock required. Navigation ability critical as the ridge traverse is largely off-trail. Parties should be comfortable with Class 3-4 scrambling and able to manage the Class 5 crux bypass.
routes.detailedRack: No rock protection needed for the mountaineering route (Class 2-3 scrambling bypasses the Class 5 crux). However, many groups carry lightweight trad rack (slings, runners) to set up safety on steep snow traverses or if a group member needs additional security on exposed terrain.
gear: Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support
Mosquito net and insect repellent (essential July-August)
Water bottles or hydration bladder (3L capacity minimum)
Satellite communicator or PLB (recommended in remote area)
whatToBring: Adequate layers for sudden weather changes
Waterproof tent and sleeping system rated well below expected temperature
Toilet paper and trowel (Leave No Trace)
Heavy-duty insect repellent (DEET or picaridin) and head net
Pain relievers and altitude sickness medication
Bivvy sack or emergency tarp as backup shelter
Lightweight emergency shelter or space blanket
knownHazards: Class 5 overhung rock requires careful bypass; direct route unsafe with overnight packs
Exposure along Stetattle Ridge in places; careful foot placement required
Loose, mossy rock on bypass terrain prone to slipping
Snowfields early season; crossing may require step kicking or crampons
Avalanche hazard in Sourdough Creek drainage in winter/spring; Stetattle Ridge itself is generally safer
Sourdough Creek crossing hazardous when snow-bridged; bridge can weaken and collapse
Route-finding challenging on ridge traverse; inadequate maps or GPS errors can lead to dangerous terrain
proTips: Sourdough Ridge generally safer in winter than Sourdough Creek approach; snow often stable with direct upslope travel at 4400+ ft elevation
Experienced parties do entire round trip in single very long day, but multi-day approach with camping is safer and more enjoyable
Bring heavy-duty insect repellent (DEET 30% or picaridin 20%); mosquitoes are intense July-August
Perfect grassy pockets along ridge allow for tent placement; identify camps on approach to plan itinerary
Sunrise from Stetattle Ridge offers 360-degree views of Picket Range, Snowfield Peak, Diablo Lake, and Jack Mountain; reach ridge by dawn
Forest fires can impair visibility and air quality; check smoke forecasts before departure
Party size limited to six 'pairs of eyes' (informal rule); groups larger than 6 should split into independent parties
Cache heavyweight gear at Sourdough Camp to reduce load for ridge traversal
Use GPS/map waypoints to confirm positions on ridge as visual landmarks are subtle
watchOut: Do NOT attempt direct descent down Sourdough Creek or other side drainages; avalanche and waterfall hazard
Do NOT underestimate time on Stetattle Ridge traverse; it looks quick on maps but involves significant scrambling and routefinding
Do NOT rely solely on cell reception; carry satellite device for emergencies (no cell service in mountains)
Do NOT miss the established trail going up from Diablo; early detours can lead to dangerous terrain
Do NOT attempt peak in poor visibility; ridge navigation becomes hazardous and exposure increases risk
Watch for wet, mossy rock on bypass terrain significantly increases slipping hazard
Early season: Sourdough Creek can be impassable if snow-bridged and recent warmth has weakened bridges
routes.comms: No cellular service available in the North Cascades backcountry around Diablo Lake and Stetattle Ridge area. Satellite phones (Garmin inReach, Iridium), personal locator beacons (PLBs), or satellite messengers (like Apple iPhone 14 satellite feature) are recommended for emergency communication. Radio reception may be intermittent; rely on satellite-based systems for critical communication.

access.landManager: National Park Service (North Cascades National Park), United States Forest Service
access.permit: Free Backcountry Permit required for overnight stays. $10 per person plus $6 non-refundable reservation fee. Permits obtained through Recreation.gov (60% of slots) or walk-up (40%) at Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount in summer, or in advance online.
access.closures: Park generally open year-round, but North Cascades Highway (SR 20) may close seasonally due to snow (typically November-April). Check current conditions before departure. Trail access may be limited early season due to snow.

road.name: Washington State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open seasonally; summer/fall/spring generally accessible
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 typically closes November to April near Marblemount due to heavy snow, though winter approaches are possible via lower-elevation winter roads
road.driveNote: Drive SR 20 to mile 126 near Diablo and Gorge Lake. Trailhead parking available near Sourdough Mountain trail entrance behind the pool building at Diablo. Parking fills on weekends; arrive early. Road is paved and well-maintained in summer.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with Pacific weather fronts. High precipitation on west side of Cascades. Weather is highly variable; prepare for rapid changes.
climate.spring: April-May: Heavy rain and wet snow at higher elevations. Avalanche danger significant in gullies. Snow remains at higher elevations; high water from snowmelt.
climate.summer: June-August: Lower elevations clear of snow by mid-July. Ridge terrain becomes fully accessible by late July. Frequent afternoon thunderstorms possible. High pressure systems in summer mean drier conditions. Intense sun and mosquitoes.
climate.fall: September-October: Clear, stable weather pattern. Cooling temperatures overnight. Generally best climbing season. Early frosts at high elevations.
climate.winter: November-March: Heavy snowfall and rain. Highway may close. Avalanche danger high. High winds common. Sourdough Mountain trail and ridge traverse safe under stable snow conditions with proper gear.

timing.recommendedStart: Recommended start time for trailhead: 6-7 AM to ensure adequate daylight for steep approach on Day 1

emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III Emergency), 300 Hospital Parkway, Mount Vernon, WA 98273, (360) 424-4111. Also Cascade Valley Hospital (Level IV), Arlington, WA; United General Hospital, Sedro-Woolley, WA (360) 856-6021.
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Ranger Station and Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (summer hours); permits picked up here or via Recreation.gov. District Ranger Office handles permits and trip planning.
